directions: Plant rooted divisions in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, loamy to sandy soil in full sun to partial shade — southern dewberry is the dominant wild trailing blackberry of the Gulf Coast and Deep South, carpeting open woodlands, roadsides, fencerows, and disturbed ground from Texas to Florida with trailing, thorny canes that root at the tips wherever they contact soil. In early spring the canes are blanketed with small white five-petaled flowers excellent for native bees, followed by clusters of large, juicy, glossy black berries in April–May that are sweet, aromatic, and superb for eating fresh, making jam, or freezing. Unlike erect blackberries, dewberries trail flat along the ground rather than arching upright, making them a natural mulching ground cover under fruit trees and along the edges of food forest plantings. Harvest before full ripeness for jam; eat fresh at peak ripeness. Remove oldest canes after fruiting to keep the planting productive.

plant_note: Plant in early spring in very acidic, well-drained soil; low-chill cultivars such as 'Sunshine Blue' or 'O'Neal' are essential in mild-winter climates.
directions: Southern highbush blueberries are interspecific hybrids bred for low chill-hour requirements (150–500 hours below 45°F), making them the only highbush-type blueberry suitable for Gulf Coast, Florida, and mild-winter California climates. Maintain soil pH at 4.5–5.5 with pine bark mulch and acidifying fertilizers; do not use lime. Plant two or more cultivars for cross-pollination and improved fruit size. Prune lightly for the first three years, then begin removing the oldest, least productive canes each late winter to stimulate vigorous new wood.

plant_note: Set crowns in early spring; everbearing types can yield a partial crop the first fall if planted promptly.
directions: Plant in spring with crowns at soil level; remove only the spring flush of blossoms and allow the summer and fall crops to set. Everbearing types produce two distinct flushes — early summer and late summer into fall — with fewer runners than june-bearing types, making them well suited to hill culture or raised beds. Harvest when berries are fully red and fragrant. Replace plants every 3–4 years as productivity declines.

plant_note: Set bare-root crowns in early spring as soon as soil can be worked, keeping the crown level with the soil surface.
directions: Plant bare-root crowns in spring with the crown at soil level, setting runners into a matted-row system 12–18 in apart. Pinch all blossoms the first year to build root vigor; harvest begins the following June when berries are uniformly red and pull easily from the stem. Renovate beds immediately after harvest by mowing foliage to 3–4 in and thinning plants to 4–6 per square foot. Mulch crowns with straw in fall in zones 4 and colder.

plant_note: Plant container stock in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil; tolerates drought, salt spray, and wind — excellent for coastal gardens in zones 7–10.
directions: The strawberry tree is an attractive Mediterranean evergreen grown as a large shrub or small tree prized for the remarkable phenomenon of simultaneously bearing flowers and ripe fruit in autumn and winter — fruit from the previous year's flowers ripens just as the new season's flowers open. The round, warty, red-orange fruits are mildly sweet and grainy when fully ripe, high in sugar and suitable for jams, liqueurs, and fermented beverages (the species name 'unedo' is said to derive from the Latin 'I eat only one'). It tolerates poor, shallow, well-drained soils and thrives in Mediterranean-climate gardens with minimal summer irrigation once established. Little pruning is required; remove dead wood and shape lightly to maintain structure.

plant_note: Plant dormant bare-root canes in early spring as soon as soil can be worked.
directions: Summer raspberries fruit on floricanes — canes produced in year one that overwinter and bear fruit the following summer. After harvest, cut all fruited floricanes to the ground and thin new primocanes to 4–6 per linear foot for best airflow. Support canes on a two-wire trellis at 3 and 5 ft. Harvest when berries pull cleanly from the plug with no resistance, typically over 3–4 weeks in early summer.

plant_note: Plant in spring; well suited to containers and mild-winter areas.
directions: 'Sunshine Blue' is a compact, semi-dwarf southern highbush blueberry that is self-fertile and needs only about 150 chill hours, making it ideal for warm regions and pots. It tolerates a slightly higher soil pH than most blueberries but still performs best in acidic, well-drained media. Grow in full sun, water consistently, and feed with an acid-formulated fertilizer during the growing season. Its tidy habit and showy pink-to-white flowers make it a productive ornamental for patios and small gardens.

plant_note: Plant dormant bare-root vines in early spring; install a sturdy trellis or arbor before planting.
directions: Table grapes require a robust trellis capable of supporting 15–30 lbs of fruit per vine; the two-wire Kniffin system or a pergola are common choices. Train a permanent trunk and 2–4 fruiting arms during the first 3 years; thereafter, annual dormant pruning should remove 90% of the previous year's growth, leaving spurs or canes with 2–4 buds each. Thin clusters after fruit set to improve berry size and airflow. Harvest when berries reach full color, the tendrils nearest the cluster have dried, and sugar is confirmed by taste.

plant_note: Plant rooted tip cuttings or bare-root canes in early spring and install a two-wire trellis at 3 and 5 ft at the same time.
directions: Tayberry is a Scottish-bred hybrid (blackberry x raspberry) with long, conical, deep purple-red berries that ripen in mid-summer; the flavor is richer and sweeter than loganberry. Train the long trailing canes fan-style along wires, keeping current-year primocanes separate from fruiting floricanes to simplify post-harvest cleanup. After harvest, remove all fruited canes to the ground and tie in the new primocanes. In zone 5–6, protect canes over winter by detaching them from wires and covering with straw mulch or a frost-cloth tunnel.

plant_note: Plant root divisions or container-grown plants in early spring in partial shade with moist, well-drained soil.
directions: Thimbleberry is a large-leafed, thornless native Rubus that thrives along woodland edges and mountain slopes from Alaska to Mexico, tolerating more shade than almost any other fruiting cane. The soft, bright red berries resemble a wide, shallow thimble and have a delicate, sweet flavor but are extremely fragile — they must be eaten or processed within hours of harvest. Propagate by dividing root clumps in spring; seed germination is erratic and requires cold stratification. Allow the plant to naturalize in a semi-wild planting rather than managing it as a formal fruiting cane.

plant_note: Set out dormant plants in early spring once soil can be worked.
directions: Prime-Ark Freedom is a thornless, primocane-fruiting blackberry that bears large, sweet berries on first-year canes, giving a summer crop that can extend into fall. Plant in full sun in fertile, well-drained soil and provide a trellis or support for the vigorous canes. Because it fruits on new wood, you can mow floricanes to the ground in late winter for a single heavy late-season crop, or leave them for two harvests. Keep soil evenly moist during fruit development and mulch to suppress weeds. Feed in early spring with a balanced fertilizer.

plant_note: Plant dormant bushes autumn to early spring.
directions: 'Titania' is a vigorous, disease-resistant black currant valued for strong resistance to white pine blister rust and mildew. It produces long strigs of large, glossy berries over an extended ripening window, making harvest easier. Grow in full sun to light shade in fertile, moisture-retentive soil enriched with compost. Feed with a high-potassium fertilizer in spring and prune out the oldest quarter of the wood each dormant season to keep the bush productive.

plant_note: Plant after last hard frost in spring; provide a strong trellis.
directions: 'Triple Crown' is a thornless semi-erect blackberry famed for high yields of large, sweet, glossy-black fruit in mid to late summer. Train the vigorous canes onto a sturdy T-trellis and tip the primocanes at about four feet to promote fruiting laterals. Grow in full sun in deep, well-drained soil with consistent summer moisture during ripening. Remove spent floricanes to the ground after harvest to make room for next year's fruiting wood.

plant_note: Plant bare-root canes in early spring on a trellis; tummelberry is more cold-hardy than tayberry and better suited to short-season northern gardens.
directions: The tummelberry is a Scottish-bred Rubus hybrid developed at the Scottish Crop Research Institute from a tayberry cross, selected specifically for greater cold hardiness and easier mechanical harvest than its parent. It produces large, dark red to maroon conical berries slightly smaller than tayberries in midsummer with a rich, aromatic flavor well suited to preserves, juices, and desserts. Train the long, semi-trailing canes on a two-wire trellis at 3 and 5 ft, fruiting on second-year floricanes; cut all fruited canes to the ground after harvest and train new primocanes for the following year. It is substantially hardier than tayberry and performs reliably in zones 4–5 with minimal winter protection.

plant_note: Plant hardwood cuttings or bare-root stock in early spring; white currants perform well trained as a cordon against a north- or east-facing wall.
directions: White currant is a yellow-fruited mutation of red currant sharing identical cultivation requirements: it fruits most heavily on spurs of two-year-old and older wood. The translucent, ivory-white berries are sweeter and milder than red currants and are prized for elegant fresh-eating and jellies. Prune each late winter to maintain 8–12 canes of mixed ages, removing canes older than 5 years. Harvest entire strigs when berries have turned fully translucent white-golden and taste sweet; check local Ribes restrictions before purchasing.

plant_note: Transplant rooted runners in spring or fall; pin runner tips to moist soil and cut free when rooted. Easiest to establish from rooted plugs or runners rather than seed.
directions: Plant rooted runners in moderately moist, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; this native species is one parent of the cultivated strawberry and produces intensely flavorful small red fruits that surpass commercial strawberries in taste, though not in size. The plant spreads vigorously by runners to form a dense groundcover that suppresses weeds and tolerates moderate foot traffic. Fruits are borne in late spring through early summer; birds, chipmunks, and turtles compete for the harvest so a loose barrier net may be needed. Divide or thin the planting every 2–3 years by removing older central crowns and retaining the vigorous outer runners.

plant_note: Plant grafted or own-rooted dormant vines in early spring on a well-drained slope with good air drainage.
directions: Wine grapes thrive in lean, well-drained soils with a pH of 5.5–6.5 and perform best on slopes with excellent air circulation to reduce disease pressure. Establish a vertical shoot positioning (VSP) trellis with catch wires at 4 and 5 ft above the cordon wire. Rigorous dormant pruning — leaving only 30–60 buds per vine depending on vigor — is essential for fruit quality. Harvest timing is cultivar- and use-specific; measure Brix (sugar) with a refractometer and monitor seed color and berry flavor.

plant_note: Plant rooted cuttings or transplants in early spring; note that wineberry is invasive in much of the eastern U.S., so check local regulations before planting.
directions: Wineberry, native to Japan and China, produces small, brilliant red, jewel-like drupes with a sweet, delicate flavor; it is grown for fruit production in Asia and has naturalized extensively in the eastern U.S. and should be grown only in contained settings or where legal. The canes are covered in red glandular hairs and tip-root readily, so remove spent canes promptly and clear any rooted tips. Harvest when berries are uniformly bright red and detach easily from the sticky calyx — they do not ripen after picking and must be used within 1–2 days. Pruning follows the same floricane/primocane cycle as standard raspberries.

plant_note: Plant hardwood cuttings or bare-root stock in early spring in well-drained, fertile soil; worcesterberry is very thorny — wear heavy gloves when handling.
directions: The worcesterberry is a North American species of gooseberry (Ribes divaricatum) sometimes grown as a productive alternative to European gooseberries because it shows good resistance to powdery mildew and American gooseberry mildew. The small, dark purple-black berries resemble currants more than typical gooseberries and ripen in midsummer with a tart, rich flavor excellent for jams and pies. Prune to an open-vase structure each late winter, keeping 8–10 canes of mixed ages. Verify state restrictions on Ribes planting before ordering, as some states still restrict the genus.

scientific_name: Rubus caesius x idaeus x ursinus hybrid
plant_note: Plant bare-root canes in spring on a sturdy two- or three-wire trellis; youngberry is a trailing type requiring support and grows best in warm, humid climates.
directions: The youngberry is a dewberry hybrid developed in Louisiana in the early 1900s by B.M. Young, producing very large, dark maroon-purple berries with a high juice content and sweet-tart flavor excellent for wine, juice, and pies. Culture is similar to boysenberry: train long, trailing canes on a T-trellis at 3 and 5 ft, fruiting on second-year floricanes; after harvest, cut all fruited canes to the ground and tie new primocanes in their place. The plants are less cold-hardy than standard blackberries and benefit from cane protection or mulching in zone 6 winters. Harvest when berries turn uniformly dark purple-black and release easily from the plug.

plant_note: Sow in early spring around the last frost date, or in late summer 6–8 weeks before first frost to allow fall establishment.
directions: Inoculate seed with Medicago-specific rhizobium and broadcast at 15–20 lb per acre (about 1/4 oz per 100 sq ft), raking seed to 1/4 in depth into a firm, well-prepared seedbed with pH 6.5–7.0. Alfalfa fixes 100–200 lb of nitrogen per acre per year once established and deep taproots (up to 6 ft) break hardpan and mine subsoil minerals. Terminate by mowing at early bloom stage — typically every 30–40 days in summer — before the first killing frost; turning it under releases a large pulse of nitrogen. In perennial plantings, allow one cutting to go to seed to rejuvenate the stand every two or three years.

plant_note: Sow in early spring or late summer; thrives on wet, acidic, or cold soils where red and white clover struggle.
directions: Broadcast inoculated seed at 6–8 lb/acre into a firm seedbed; alsike clover is a short-lived perennial that tolerates more acid and waterlogged conditions than red clover and requires no liming on pH 5.5–6.5 soils. It will fix nitrogen reliably for 2–3 seasons without reseeding and pairs well with timothy or orchardgrass in mixed stands. Mow to prevent flowering from exhausting the plant, or allow it to bloom and incorporate as green manure in the second year. Avoid using as sole forage for horses, as alsike can cause photosensitization in equines.

plant_note: Sow 2–6 weeks before first fall frost; germinates and establishes faster than perennial ryegrass and can be interseeded into standing late-season crops.
directions: Broadcast seed at 20–25 lb per acre (about 1/2 oz per 100 sq ft) onto a prepared surface and rake to 1/4 in depth; annual ryegrass has one of the fastest establishment rates of all cool-season grasses, providing rapid erosion control and weed suppression. The dense, fibrous root system scavenges residual nitrogen effectively, locking it up over winter to prevent leaching. Terminate in early spring before it joints and sets seed — once headed, it becomes very difficult to kill without herbicide or repeated tillage. Mow to 2–3 in and till, or use a roller-crimper at boot stage for no-till termination; allow residue 2–3 weeks to break down before planting.

plant_note: Sow in fall in mild-winter regions for late-season biomass and nitrogen.
directions: Inoculate with clover rhizobia and broadcast at about 0.2-0.3 lb per 1,000 sq ft onto a firm seedbed, covering no deeper than 0.25 inch. Arrowleaf clover is a reseeding annual legume of the southern United States that produces high biomass and fixes nitrogen well into late spring. It is later maturing than crimson clover, extending the window of living cover and pollinator bloom. Terminate by mowing at early to mid bloom, then incorporate or leave residue as mulch. Allowing a portion to set seed lets the stand reseed naturally for the following season.

plant_note: Sow in early fall, often mixed with a cereal, so vines establish before frost.
directions: Inoculate seed with pea-vetch rhizobia and drill at 1.5-2 inches deep or broadcast at about 2-4 lb per 1,000 sq ft into a firm seedbed. Austrian winter pea is a viny legume that fixes substantial nitrogen and is frequently sown with cereal rye or oats for support and biomass. It overwinters in mild climates and resumes vigorous vining growth in spring. Terminate by mowing or crimping at early flowering when nitrogen fixation peaks, then incorporate or leave as mulch. The residue releases nitrogen quickly, benefiting a following heavy-feeding vegetable crop.

plant_note: Sow 4–8 weeks before frost; balansa clover is exceptionally flood- and waterlogging-tolerant, making it ideal for wet, low-lying spots.
directions: Broadcast tiny seed at 2–4 lb/acre (due to its small seed size) onto a firm, moist seedbed; balansa clover establishes quickly and tolerates prolonged waterlogging that would kill crimson or Persian clover. It hollows out a rapid canopy that suppresses winter weeds while fixing nitrogen throughout the cool season. Terminate at early bloom by mowing or rolling for the best nitrogen contribution and easiest residue breakdown. Excellent choice for poorly drained field margins or orchard middles that receive runoff.

plant_note: Sow in cool soil in early spring or fall; tolerates light frost.
directions: Barley is a fast-establishing cereal grain used as a soil-building cover crop, sprouting grain, and livestock feed. Drill or broadcast the seed into cool, firm soil in early spring or fall and rake it in for good contact. It germinates quickly, tolerates light frost, and produces abundant biomass that suppresses weeds and protects bare ground. Mow or turn under the growth before it heads out to return organic matter, or let it mature if harvesting grain.

plant_note: Sow in early fall 4–8 weeks before first frost, or in early spring; survives light frost but not hard freezes, so it winter-kills in zones 6 and colder.
directions: Broadcast inoculated seed at 15–20 lb per acre, raking seed lightly to 1/4 in depth; berseem germinates in near-freezing soil and establishes faster than crimson clover. Unlike most clovers, berseem regrows vigorously after each mowing, allowing two to three cuts before termination, with each cut releasing a pulse of nitrogen. It tolerates moderate salinity and poorly drained soils better than most cool-season legumes, making it useful in difficult garden sites. Terminate by incorporating the last flush of growth 3–4 weeks before planting the next crop.

plant_note: Sow in early spring; establishes slowly but becomes a persistent, non-bloating perennial legume ideal for slopes and orchard floors.
directions: Inoculate seed with Mesorhizobium loti and broadcast at 5–8 lb/acre onto a firm, weed-free seedbed; seedling vigor is slow, so suppress weed competition aggressively in the establishment year. Once established, birdsfoot trefoil is drought-tolerant and persists on shallow, acidic, or gravelly soils where alfalfa fails. It fixes moderate nitrogen, flowers bright yellow, and does not cause bloat in livestock — a key advantage over true clovers. Mow or graze periodically to prevent it from going rank and woody but allow it to flower at least once per season to feed pollinators.

plant_note: Sow 4–8 weeks before frost; black oat produces more biomass and stronger allelopathy than common oats and is the preferred cover in no-till systems.
directions: Broadcast seed at 40–60 lb/acre and cover to 1 in depth in fall; black oat (Avena strigosa) is widely used in Brazilian no-till systems for its exceptional allelopathic residue that suppresses weeds well into the following season. It winter-kills in zones colder than 5, creating a ready-made mulch mat without any tillage needed. In milder climates it overwinters and can be roll-crimped in spring when fully headed out to create a thick residue layer for direct transplanting through. Combine with a legume such as hairy vetch or crimson clover to add a nitrogen component to the mulch.

plant_note: Sow in late summer or early fall for maximum biofumigant biomass before frost.
directions: Broadcast at about 0.25-0.5 lb per 1,000 sq ft and rake lightly to cover 0.25 inch deep in a moist seedbed. Brown mustard is a high-glucosinolate brassica valued as a biofumigant that suppresses soilborne pests and weeds. For best biofumigation, mow or flail at early flowering when biomass peaks and immediately incorporate the chopped tissue into moist soil, then cover or roll to trap released compounds. Wait two to three weeks before planting sensitive crops to allow volatile compounds to dissipate. In cold winters the plants will winterkill, easing spring termination.

plant_note: Direct sow at 20–25 lbs per acre (or 1/2 lb per 1,000 sq ft) on prepared soil in warm weather; brown top millet is one of the fastest-establishing summer annuals for warm soils — germination and early cover are often visible within 5–7 days.
directions: Brown top millet is the most popular warm-season cover crop grain for wildlife food plots and dove fields across the southeastern United States, producing abundant, small-seeded seed heads in 50–70 days that are consumed avidly by mourning doves, bobwhite quail, mourning doves, and more than 30 other species of wild birds — it is essentially the standard seed for managed dove hunting fields in the Gulf South. As a summer annual, it establishes extremely rapidly in warm soil, provides quick weed suppression through its rapid canopy closure, and produces excellent biomass for soil organic matter after incorporation; it is also used as an emergency crop cover on eroded or disturbed soils where quick establishment is critical. Sow in June or early July for peak seed production by August–September for dove season; for cover crop biomass, incorporate into the soil after seed set when stalks are still green for maximum nitrogen and organic matter contribution. Extremely heat and drought tolerant once established — among the most reliable summer cover crops for the hot, dry conditions of the Gulf Coast in July–August.

plant_note: Sow anytime in warm frost-free weather; a fast summer smother crop that flowers in ~5 weeks.
directions: Broadcast seed about 1/2 in deep into warm soil; it sprouts within days and shades out weeds quickly. Cut or turn it under at first bloom, before it sets seed, or it will reseed. It also draws pollinators and helps make soil phosphorus available.

plant_note: Sow after frost into warm soil; killed by frost.
directions: Buckwheat is a fast-growing summer annual used as a smother cover crop, quick green manure, and edible grain. Broadcast the seed onto warm, worked soil after the last frost and rake it in, watering to ensure a fast, even stand. It germinates in days and quickly shades out weeds while its white blooms feed pollinators. Mow or turn it under before seed sets, roughly five to six weeks in, to add organic matter and prepare for the next planting.

plant_note: Sow after all danger of frost has passed; grows quickly in warm soil.
directions: Broadcast buckwheat seed onto worked soil once frost danger has passed and rake in lightly for good contact. It germinates within a week and races to flower in about a month, smothering weeds and drawing in bees. Mow or turn it under at first bloom to add green matter and free up phosphorus for the next crop. Succession-sow every few weeks through summer for continuous soil coverage, but never let a stand fully set seed unless you want volunteers.

plant_note: Broadcast seed on warm soil after frost; it grows fast and is easily killed by cold.
directions: Buckwheat is a fast-growing warm-season cover crop that smothers weeds, attracts pollinators, and improves soil when turned under. Broadcast seed densely onto raked soil after the last frost and rake in lightly; it germinates within a few days. Mow or till it in at flowering, before seeds set, to prevent unwanted reseeding and to add organic matter. It thrives in poor soils and needs little water, making it an easy short-term summer cover between crops.

plant_note: Fall or early spring sowing suits this cool-season native grass; benefits from cold stratification.
directions: Sow Canada wild rye in fall or early spring, pressing seed into contact with bare soil no more than a quarter inch deep. This fast-establishing native cool-season grass is a favorite nurse crop, holding slopes against erosion while slower prairie species take hold. Its arching, feathery seed heads feed birds and add graceful movement to naturalized plantings. Water during establishment, then let it fend for itself as a hardy, largely maintenance-free perennial.

plant_note: Sow 4–8 weeks before frost in fall, or in earliest spring; extremely drought- and cold-tolerant legume for challenging sites.
directions: Sow inoculated seed 1–2 in deep at 40–60 lb/acre; chickling vetch is one of the most drought-tolerant annual legumes, surviving on less than 10 in of annual rainfall and fixing substantial nitrogen even on marginal soils. The vining tendrils latch onto cereal companions like rye or triticale for support. Terminate at full bloom before pods set, as mature seed contains ODAP, a neurotoxin that is harmless in soil but hazardous if consumed in large quantities. Do not grow where children or livestock might graze the ripe seeds.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost; a fast summer cover crop terminated before it sets seed.
directions: A fast-growing warm-season cover crop that smothers weeds, scavenges phosphorus, and improves soil health even in poor ground. Broadcast or drill seed after the last frost onto a firm, moist seedbed and rake in lightly. It grows quickly and blooms within a month, attracting pollinators and beneficial insects. Mow or cut it down at first flower before seed sets to prevent volunteers, then leave the residue as a nutrient-rich mulch.

plant_note: Sow 4–8 weeks before first fall frost; common vetch is slightly more cold-tolerant than crimson clover and grows in poorer soils than hairy vetch.
directions: Sow inoculated seed at 40–50 lb per acre (about 1 oz per 20 sq ft) in fall, raking to 1/2 in depth; common vetch tolerates sandy, low-fertility soils where hairy vetch may struggle and establishes a climbing, vining habit that benefits from a grass companion for support. It fixes 80–120 lb of nitrogen per acre over winter and spring and is easier to terminate than hairy vetch because the vines are thinner and dry down faster. Mow at early bloom before pods swell to prevent reseeding; the narrow tendriled stems break down readily and release nitrogen within 2–3 weeks.

plant_note: Sow 4–8 weeks before first fall frost, or in early spring; fixes nitrogen and blooms crimson.
directions: Broadcast seed onto a raked bed at about 1/4 in depth and keep moist until established. Let it grow through winter, then mow and turn it under a few weeks before planting to release its fixed nitrogen. Terminate at early bloom for the best nitrogen return.

plant_note: Sow late summer to early fall, or early spring in cold climates.
directions: Broadcast crimson clover onto firmed soil and rake in shallowly, timing sowing for late summer or early autumn so plants establish before winter. This annual legume fixes atmospheric nitrogen while its deep red, elongated blooms feed early pollinators the following spring. Turn the stand under at peak bloom for maximum nitrogen return before it sets seed. It also makes a striking, low-maintenance living mulch between rows.

plant_note: Sow in late summer to early fall, or in early spring, for nitrogen fixation and cover.
directions: This annual clover fixes nitrogen while its deep-red, elongated flower heads attract bees, ladybugs, and other beneficial insects. Broadcast seed onto a firm, moist seedbed and rake in lightly for good soil contact. Sow in fall in mild climates to overwinter, or in early spring in colder regions. Terminate by mowing or tilling at early bloom to return the most nitrogen to the soil. It also makes an attractive, low-maintenance living mulch.

plant_note: Sow in late summer to fall for overwintering, or early spring in cold regions.
directions: Crimson clover is a nitrogen-fixing legume cover crop that enriches soil and produces showy crimson flowers loved by bees. Broadcast seed onto prepared soil in fall for overwintering or in early spring, raking in lightly for good contact. Let it grow to bloom, then mow and turn it under a few weeks before planting to release nitrogen and add organic matter. Distinct from perennial red clover, it grows quickly and is easy to incorporate into rotations.

plant_note: Sow in spring or early fall; a hardy perennial ground cover.
directions: Scatter Dutch white clover thinly over raked soil in spring or early fall and press in for good seed-to-soil contact, keeping the surface moist until it sprouts. This low-growing perennial legume spreads by creeping stems to form a durable living ground cover that fixes nitrogen and crowds out weeds. It thrives as a lawn companion, orchard understory, or pathway cover and tolerates light foot traffic. Mow occasionally to keep it tidy and encourage dense growth.

plant_note: Sow in late spring or summer; it is more heat tolerant than other mustards.
directions: Broadcast at about 0.25-0.5 lb per 1,000 sq ft and rake to cover roughly 0.25 inch deep in warm soil. Ethiopian mustard is more heat and drought tolerant than white or brown mustard, making it useful for summer biofumigation windows. Its high glucosinolate content suppresses nematodes and soilborne pathogens when the biomass is chopped and incorporated. Mow or flail at early flowering and immediately work the residue into moist soil, then irrigate or roll to seal the surface. Wait two to three weeks before planting a sensitive crop into the treated bed.

plant_note: Sow in early spring or fall in mild climates; cold hardy to the low twenties.
directions: Inoculate with faba-bean rhizobia and sow large seed 2-3 inches deep at about 4-6 inches apart in rows or broadcast heavily. Faba beans are upright legumes that fix large amounts of nitrogen and improve soil structure with their sturdy taproots. They tolerate cool weather and wet soils, making them a strong early-spring green manure in temperate gardens. Terminate by mowing or cutting at early flowering when nitrogen fixation is greatest, then incorporate the succulent stems into the soil. Allow two to three weeks for decomposition before planting a following crop.

plant_note: Direct sow 1–2 inches deep in fall 6–10 weeks before first frost, or in early spring 4–6 weeks before last frost; field pea requires cool soil (below 65°F) to germinate well and performs best as a fall-sown winter annual in the Gulf South.
directions: Field pea (Austrian winter pea) is the premier cool-season legume cover crop for the Gulf Coast and southeastern United States, providing rapid establishment, heavy nitrogen fixation, and excellent biomass production during the mild Gulf South winter when most cover crops struggle. Planted in fall, it overwinters as a cold-hardy vine, fixes 50–150 pounds of atmospheric nitrogen per acre, and produces 2–4 tons of organic matter per acre by the time it is terminated in spring — a nitrogen contribution equivalent to a full pre-plant fertilizer application that is released slowly as the biomass decomposes. The trailing vines suppress winter weeds effectively and the purple-pink flowers attract native bees in late winter and spring when few other plants are blooming; the tender vine tips are edible by humans, livestock, and wildlife. In zone 8–10, field peas can be planted as early as September for maximum winter biomass; terminate by mowing or rolling in spring 2–4 weeks before planting summer crops to allow nitrogen release.

plant_note: Sow 4–8 weeks before frost in fall, or in earliest spring; nitrogen-fixing and edible tender shoots.
directions: Sow inoculated seed about 1 in deep, often mixed with oats or rye for support. The vines fix nitrogen and produce edible pea shoots you can harvest before turning the crop under. Terminate at flowering for the best nitrogen contribution.

plant_note: Sow in spring or late summer; the deep taproot loosens compacted soil.
directions: Broadcast at about 0.1-0.2 lb per 1,000 sq ft onto a firm, weed-free seedbed and rake to a shallow 0.25 inch depth. Forage chicory is a deep-rooted perennial that mines nutrients from the subsoil, breaks up compaction, and provides mineral-rich forage and pollinator bloom when allowed to flower. It establishes as a rosette the first year and bolts in following seasons. Manage as a living cover by mowing to prevent flowering, or terminate by tillage or cutting the crown below the soil surface. Because it is persistent, mow or till before seed set to keep it from spreading beyond the intended area.

plant_note: Sow in late summer so plants bulk up before cold weather slows growth.
directions: Broadcast at about 0.2-0.4 lb per 1,000 sq ft and rake to cover 0.25 inch deep in a firm, fertile seedbed. Forage kale produces large amounts of leafy biomass and a fibrous root system that scavenges nitrogen and improves soil structure. It is very cold hardy and stays green well into winter, providing living cover and weed suppression during the cool season. Terminate by mowing or grazing before it bolts in spring, then incorporate the residue with light tillage. In colder zones repeated hard freezes may winterkill the crop, easing termination.

plant_note: Sow 6–10 weeks before first frost; produces a large, soil-penetrating taproot that winter-kills in cold climates, leaving a decomposed channel.
directions: Broadcast seed at 8–10 lb/acre or drill at 6–7 lb/acre and cover to 1/4 in depth; forage radish emerges quickly and develops a taproot up to 18 in deep that fractures compacted subsoil and scavenges nitrates before they leach. In USDA zones 5 and colder the taproot reliably winter-kills, leaving a hollow channel that improves spring drainage and air porosity. Where winters are mild, mow the rosette before it bolts in spring to avoid a weedy seed bank. The decomposing roots have a pungent sulfur smell for 1–2 weeks after freeze-kill — this is normal.

plant_note: Sow 6–10 weeks before first frost; forage turnip produces heavy leaf canopy that smothers weeds and a fleshy taproot that winterkills to release nutrients.
directions: Broadcast or drill seed at 1–2 lb/acre and cover to 1/4 in depth; forage turnip is faster-maturing and less prone to overwintering than oilseed radish, making it a reliable fall cover where a quick canopy is needed. It scavenges residual nitrogen and suppresses winter annual weeds under its broad leaves. The bulbous root winter-kills in hard frost zones and decomposes rapidly, releasing stored nutrients at planting time. In mild climates it may overwinter and bolt in spring — mow before flowering to prevent volunteer seedlings in subsequent crops.

plant_note: Wait until soil is thoroughly warm before sowing this heat-loving annual.
directions: Broadcast at roughly 0.5-1 lb per 1,000 sq ft and rake to cover about 0.5 inch deep in warm, firm soil. Foxtail millet is exceptionally drought tolerant and matures quickly, making it ideal for filling short summer windows between cash crops. Its fibrous roots improve soil tilth while the dense canopy shades out weeds. Mow before the seed heads harden to prevent reseeding, then incorporate or leave the residue as surface mulch. In cold regions frost will terminate any remaining plants at season's end.

plant_note: Sow after the last frost when soil is warm; amaranth loves heat.
directions: Direct sow green garnet amaranth after the last frost into warm soil, barely covering the tiny seeds. Thin plants for grain heads or grow densely and cut young leaves as a spinach-like green or microgreen. This heat- and drought-tolerant heirloom shrugs off poor soil, producing tall stalks topped with feathery green seed heads that mature to gluten-free grain. Harvest seed when heads shatter easily and dry thoroughly before storing.

plant_note: Sow after last frost in hot, dry climates; exceptional drought tolerance makes this the best legume cover for arid Southwest summers.
directions: Direct-sow inoculated seed 1 in deep at 20–30 lb/acre into warm, well-drained soil after all frost danger has passed; guar is native to arid Asia and thrives in sandy, alkaline soils with limited irrigation. It fixes atmospheric nitrogen through root nodules and produces significant biomass even in drought conditions, making it invaluable in the semi-arid Southwest and high plains. Terminate by mowing at early pod formation before seed fully hardens; the dense residue builds organic matter in soils chronically short of it. Avoid waterlogged sites — standing water causes rapid root rot.

plant_note: Direct sow scarified seeds after last frost once soil warms above 70°F; hairy indigo establishes best on sandy, well-drained soil in full sun and benefits from inoculation with appropriate Indigofera rhizobia before planting.
directions: Hairy indigo is a tropical legume that has been widely used as a summer cover crop and green manure in Florida and the Gulf Coast region since the early 20th century, when it was introduced as a low-input nitrogen source for sandy, infertile soils typical of the Gulf Coastal Plain where cool-season legumes cannot grow. It fixes atmospheric nitrogen at rates of 60–100 pounds per acre, produces dense biomass that suppresses summer weeds, and tolerates the heat, drought, and low-fertility sandy soils of Gulf South gardens and fields that challenge other cover crops in the summer months. The small, pink to purple flowers attract native bees throughout the growing season, and established plants are very drought resistant once rooted, requiring no irrigation on well-drained sands. In organic vegetable gardens and orchards in zone 8–11, use hairy indigo as a summer understory cover crop between rows or in fallow beds July–September to build organic matter and nitrogen before fall vegetable planting; terminate by mowing or tilling 3–4 weeks before fall planting.

plant_note: Sow 4–8 weeks before first frost, often mixed with winter rye which gives it something to climb.
directions: Sow seed about 1/2 in deep, ideally inoculated with the correct rhizobia and paired with a grass like rye for support. It overwinters and fixes a large amount of nitrogen by spring bloom. Terminate at early flowering for maximum nitrogen and easy breakdown.

plant_note: Sow in late summer or early fall for overwintering; a cold-hardy legume.
directions: Broadcast hairy vetch in late summer or early fall and cover about an inch deep so it can root before winter. This vigorous, cold-hardy legume sprawls across the soil, fixing large amounts of nitrogen and forming a weed-suppressing mat. It pairs especially well with cereal rye, which gives the vine something to climb. Terminate it at early bloom the following spring for a rich nitrogen boost before planting summer crops.

plant_note: Sow in late spring after soil warms; slow to establish but long-lived.
directions: Sow Indiangrass on prepared bare soil in late spring, pressing the fluffy seed in shallowly and keeping it moist during the slow germination period. This warm-season prairie perennial forms tall, upright clumps topped with shimmering golden seed heads that glow in autumn light. It is deeply rooted and drought-tolerant once established, excellent for erosion control and wildlife cover. Patience pays off, as stands strengthen considerably in their second and third seasons.

plant_note: Sow after last frost when soil reaches 65°F; one of the most heat- and drought-tolerant warm-season cover-crop legumes.
directions: Broadcast or drill inoculated seed at 40–50 lb per acre (about 1 oz per 25 sq ft), 1/2 in deep in warm, well-drained soil; cowpeas germinate and grow rapidly, fixing 100–150 lb of nitrogen per acre while suppressing summer weeds with their spreading vines. The crop tolerates sandy, infertile, and slightly acidic soils where most legumes struggle. Terminate by mowing at early pod stage before seeds mature to prevent volunteer plants; large amounts of vine biomass decompose quickly in warm weather. Pairs well with sorghum-sudangrass for a combined legume-grass summer cover.

plant_note: Direct sow after last frost in any well-drained to moderately moist, poor to moderately fertile soil in full sun; jack bean is an excellent green manure crop for tropical and subtropical gardens — it fixes large quantities of nitrogen and improves soil structure. Do not eat raw seeds — canavanine must be destroyed by thorough boiling.
directions: Direct sow after last frost in any well-drained soil in full sun — jack bean is a robust tropical annual legume widely grown as a green manure, cover crop, and nitrogen fixer throughout tropical and subtropical climates. The large, vigorous vines produce clusters of small, purple-pink pea flowers followed by enormous, flat, straight bean pods (10–14 inches long) containing large, white to ivory seeds. As a cover crop, it fixes substantial quantities of atmospheric nitrogen through root nodule bacteria, improving soil fertility for subsequent crops. The immature pods can be eaten as a green bean and the seeds can be eaten when thoroughly cooked (raw seeds and undercooked beans contain canavanine, a toxic amino acid analog that causes serious illness — always boil seeds in multiple changes of water). Very drought tolerant once established; excellent for rehabilitating poor, compacted, or depleted soils in the Gulf Coast vegetable garden rotation.

plant_note: Sow into warm soil after the last frost when soil reaches at least 60 F.
directions: Broadcast at about 0.5-1 lb per 1,000 sq ft onto a moist seedbed and rake lightly to cover 0.25-0.5 inch deep. Japanese millet germinates fast in warm soil and tolerates temporarily wet or flooded ground better than most summer cover crops. It produces abundant biomass in as little as six to eight weeks and can be used to smother summer weeds in fallow beds. Terminate by mowing before seed set to avoid volunteers, then incorporate residue or leave as mulch. Frost will kill the crop, providing free termination ahead of fall or spring plantings.

plant_note: Sow in spring; slow to establish but forms a long-lived rhizomatous stand.
directions: Inoculate with the specific kura clover rhizobia strain and broadcast at about 0.2-0.4 lb per 1,000 sq ft into a firm, weed-free seedbed no more than 0.25 inch deep. Kura clover establishes slowly during its first year because it invests heavily in rhizomes, but once established it forms a persistent living mulch. Its spreading roots fix nitrogen and provide erosion control and pollinator forage for many years. Manage rather than fully terminate it by mowing or suppressing with strip tillage so it regrows between cash crops. For complete removal, repeated tillage or cultivation is required because of its vigorous rhizomes.

plant_note: Sow after last frost in warm soil; an exceptionally heat- and drought-tolerant warm-season legume cover from Africa.
directions: Plant inoculated seed 1 in deep after soil temperatures exceed 65°F; lablab vines grow vigorously and can climb trellises or sprawl as a smothering ground cover. It fixes up to 200 lb N/acre equivalent under good conditions and tolerates low-fertility and mildly saline soils where cowpea struggles. Terminate by mowing or chopping at early pod set; raw mature pods contain toxic lectins, so the foliage should not be eaten without cooking. In frost-free zones it can be managed as a short-lived perennial ground cover.

plant_note: Broadcast sow in spring or fall; seeds germinate best in darkness and cool soil.
directions: Broadcast lacy phacelia seed in spring or fall and rake it in lightly, as the seed needs darkness to germinate. This fast-growing cover crop improves soil structure, reduces erosion, and produces bell-shaped violet flowers on curled fiddlehead clusters that are magnets for bees and beneficial insects. Mow or turn it under before it sets seed if you want to prevent self-sowing, or let a patch bloom to feed pollinators. It tolerates poor soils and needs little water once established.

plant_note: Sow late spring into warm soil; a slow-to-establish native bunchgrass.
directions: Sow little bluestem on bare, firmed soil in late spring, barely covering the fluffy seed and keeping it lightly moist while it germinates. This warm-season native bunchgrass forms tidy clumps of fine blue-green blades that turn brilliant copper-red in fall and persist through winter. It thrives in lean, well-drained soil and needs little water once rooted, making it ideal for erosion control and butterfly habitat. Cut old growth back in late winter to make way for fresh spring blades.

plant_note: Sow early spring or late summer for a quick cool-season cover.
directions: Broadcast oat seed over worked soil in early spring or six to eight weeks before your first fall frost and rake in about half an inch. Fast, dense growth shades out weeds and its fibrous roots hold soil against erosion and winter rains. In cold zones the crop reliably winter-kills, leaving a soft mulch that is easy to plant into come spring. It also builds plenty of organic matter when turned under while still green.

plant_note: Sow in early spring or late summer/fall for a winter-killed cover crop in cold zones.
directions: Broadcast oats densely over prepared ground in early spring or six to eight weeks before your first fall frost and rake in about half an inch deep. They establish fast in cool weather, forming a thick mat that outcompetes weeds and holds soil in place. In cold climates a hard freeze winter-kills the stand, leaving an easy-to-plant mulch by spring. You can also cut young growth for cat grass or graze it before it heads out.

plant_note: Sow 4–8 weeks before first fall frost; oats winter-kill below about 10°F, leaving a ready-to-plant mat of residue in spring without tillage.
directions: Broadcast seed at 50–80 lb per acre (about 1 oz per 30 sq ft) and rake to 1/2 in depth; oats establish quickly and produce substantial biomass before being killed by hard freezes. Their winter-killed residue lies flat as a weed-suppressing mulch that is easy to plant or transplant through in spring without additional termination work. Often mixed with field peas or hairy vetch to add nitrogen to the primarily carbonaceous grass residue. In mild-winter regions (zones 8–9), oats may overwinter and require mowing to terminate before spring planting.

plant_note: Sow six to eight weeks before frost so plants form a strong rosette to overwinter.
directions: Broadcast at about 0.25-0.5 lb per 1,000 sq ft and rake to cover 0.25-0.5 inch deep in a firm seedbed. Winter-type oilseed rape develops a deep taproot that breaks up compacted subsoil and scavenges nitrogen and sulfur. Overwintering plants bolt and flower in spring, providing early forage for pollinators if allowed to bloom. Terminate by mowing at early flowering before pods set, then incorporate the residue with tillage. Allow two to three weeks of breakdown before planting the following crop to avoid nitrogen tie-up.

plant_note: Sow after last frost in warm soil; the most heat- and drought-tolerant warm-season cover grass, thriving in Gulf Coast summers.
directions: Drill or broadcast seed 1/2 in deep into warm soil; pearl millet germinates and establishes faster than sorghum-sudangrass in sandy, low-fertility soils. It produces large amounts of biomass for smothering weeds and building organic matter, and tolerate short dry spells without wilting like corn or sorghum can. Mow or terminate before seed heads mature to prevent unwanted reseeding. Mix with cowpeas for a combined nitrogen-fixing and biomass cover.

plant_note: Plant rhizome sprigs in late spring once soil is warm; establishment is slow the first season but the stand fills in by year 2.
directions: Plant rhizome sections 2–3 in deep in well-drained, sandy or loamy soil; perennial peanut tolerates poor, acidic soils typical of Gulf Coast landscapes. Establishment is slow the first year but the low, dense mat fixes nitrogen and crowds out weeds without mowing once established. It can be used as a lawn alternative, orchard under-planting, or living mulch in vegetable corridors. In zones 8–9 the top may freeze back in hard winters but the rhizomes resprout reliably from below ground.

plant_note: Sow in late summer to early fall; perennial ryegrass establishes fastest of all cool-season grasses and forms dense sod for erosion control.
directions: Broadcast seed at 20–30 lb/acre and rake lightly to 1/4 in depth; perennial ryegrass germinates in 5–7 days at optimal temperatures and establishes the fastest ground cover of any cool-season grass, making it excellent for emergency erosion control or orchard middles. It can be used as a short-rotation annual cover in cold winters (zones 3–5) or managed as a persistent sod in mild climates. Terminate before planting vegetables by aggressive mowing followed by solarization or herbicide; perennial ryegrass regrows readily from crowns and requires thorough kill. Mix with red or white clover for a nitrogen-fixing living mulch under fruit trees.

plant_note: Broadcast seed at 8–12 pounds per acre in late summer or early fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in full sun; Persian clover is winter-hardy to about 20°F, making it a useful winter annual in zones 7–9 where it overwinters and provides early spring nitrogen to following crops.
directions: Broadcast seed in late summer or early fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in full sun; Persian clover is a Mediterranean annual clover widely used as a winter cover crop in mild-winter zones 7–9 where it establishes in fall, overwinters successfully, and produces a significant nitrogen-fixing spring flush before being incorporated as a green manure ahead of warm-season transplants. The distinctive pink-to-purple flowers open in the distinctive 'upside down' (resupinate) orientation that gives the species its name and common name. It is considered one of the best annual clovers for the winter-cover-crop niche in Gulf Coast and southeastern gardens — more productive and earlier-maturing than many other clovers in mild winters. Mow or till under at early flowering to maximize nitrogen release to following crops. Excellent as a nurse crop for cool-season vegetables.

plant_note: Sow 2–6 weeks before last spring frost, or in early fall in mild climates; flowers arrive in about 6 weeks and are among the best for attracting bees.
directions: Broadcast tiny seed at 3–5 lb per acre (a light dusting per 100 sq ft) on the surface of a firm, moist seedbed without covering, as seed needs light to germinate; rake in very lightly if birds are a problem. Phacelia establishes quickly in cool soil, outcompeting many weeds with dense, ferny foliage that also conserves soil moisture. The blue-purple flowers are exceptional for beneficial insects and bees — allow partial bloom before mowing to maximize pollinator benefit. Terminate by mowing just as the last flowers fade; it decomposes rapidly and leaves no allelopathic residue.

plant_note: Direct sow seeds in warm soil after last frost in well-drained, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; phasey bean germinates readily without scarification and establishes quickly in warm, humid conditions — ideal for summer cover cropping in the Gulf South.
directions: Phasey bean is a fast-growing tropical American legume that has become one of the most widely used summer cover crops in Hawaii and the Gulf Coast, valued for its rapid establishment, vigorous nitrogen fixation, heat and humidity tolerance, and ability to grow as a self-reseeding annual in frost-free areas of zone 9–11. The plant produces small, attractive red-purple flowers on erect, trifoliate-leaved stems throughout summer, with long, slender seed pods containing multiple seeds; it naturally reseeds in zone 9 and south, making it a long-term self-perpetuating cover crop in orchards, landscape beds, and permanent native plantings. As a legume, it inoculates easily with cowpea rhizobia and fixes 50–80 pounds of nitrogen per acre; its moderate height keeps it manageable as an understory cover beneath small fruit trees and shrub rows. Used extensively in no-till and reduced-tillage systems as a living mulch terminated by mowing in fall before cool-season crops are established.

plant_note: Sow in fall in mild-winter regions; it is less cold hardy than hairy vetch.
directions: Inoculate with vetch-pea rhizobia and broadcast at about 1-2 lb per 1,000 sq ft, raking to cover 1 inch deep. Purple vetch is a fast-growing annual legume best suited to mild-winter climates where it fixes nitrogen through the cool season. It climbs readily, so pairing it with a cereal companion improves standability and biomass. Terminate by mowing or crimping at early bloom before viable seed forms to prevent weedy volunteers. Incorporate the succulent residue or leave it as a nitrogen-rich mulch two to three weeks before planting.

plant_note: Sow in late summer to early fall for quick fall cover that scavenges nutrients.
directions: Broadcast at about 0.25-0.5 lb per 1,000 sq ft and rake to cover 0.25 inch deep in a moist seedbed. This fast-growing brassica establishes quickly, forming a fibrous root mat that takes up excess nitrogen and reduces leaching over winter. It is less cold hardy than oilseed rape and often winterkills in colder regions, providing free termination. Where it survives, mow at early flowering and incorporate the residue two to three weeks before planting. The rapid canopy closure also smothers fall-germinating weeds.

plant_note: Sow after the last frost in warm soil.
directions: Sow red amaranth directly once soil has warmed, scattering the fine seed on the surface and pressing in lightly. Plants climb up to six feet, crowned with dramatic burgundy seed heads that double as an ornamental accent and a nutritious grain harvest. Young leaves make an excellent cooked green, so pinch some early for the kitchen. Give it full sun and lean, well-drained soil, and it will thrive with little water.

plant_note: Sow 4–8 weeks before first fall frost, or frost-seed in late winter; short-lived perennial that fixes nitrogen and blooms its second year.
directions: Broadcast inoculated seed onto a prepared bed at 8–10 lb per acre, raking to 1/4 in depth; red clover tolerates slightly acidic soils and moderate shade better than most legume cover crops. It overwinters in zones 4 and warmer and puts on vigorous growth in early spring, often topping 2 ft before bloom. Terminate at early flower stage — before the first florets open to brown — to capture maximum fixed nitrogen of 80–150 lb per acre. A second flush of growth often follows mowing, allowing a second cut of biomass before final incorporation.

plant_note: Broadcast into cool moist soil in spring or late summer.
directions: Red clover is a nitrogen-fixing legume that builds fertility, protects bare soil, and feeds pollinators with its pink blooms. Broadcast the seed onto a firm, moist seedbed in early spring or late summer and rake lightly, keeping it damp until the fast-growing seedlings cover the ground. It performs best in full sun with average moisture and can be mowed to prevent seed set. Turn the green growth under before it flowers heavily to release nitrogen into the soil.

plant_note: Sow in early spring; the deep taproot exploits subsoil moisture and breaks compaction.
directions: Sow seed 1-1.5 inches deep at about 0.5-1 lb per 1,000 sq ft into warm, well-drained soil after the ground can be worked. Safflower develops a strong taproot that penetrates several feet, breaking up compacted subsoil and drawing up moisture and nutrients from depth. It is highly drought tolerant, making it a useful soil-building cover for dry sites and rotations. Terminate by mowing before the spiny flower heads mature, since the bracts become sharp and seed can volunteer. Incorporate the residue or leave it as surface mulch to protect the soil and improve organic matter.

plant_note: Sow in spring into well-drained, calcareous soil; not tolerant of wet ground.
directions: Inoculate with the specific sainfoin rhizobia and drill or broadcast at about 1-2 lb per 1,000 sq ft, covering 0.5-1 inch deep in well-drained soil. Sainfoin is a deep-rooted perennial legume that fixes nitrogen, thrives on dry alkaline soils, and produces showy pink blooms that are excellent for pollinators. Its non-bloating forage and deep taproot make it a durable soil-building crop where drainage is good. Manage by mowing after the first flush of bloom to encourage regrowth, or terminate mature stands with tillage before planting a following crop. It performs poorly on acidic or waterlogged sites, so match it to suitable ground.

common_name: Soil Builder Peas and Oats Cover Crop
plant_note: Broadcast in spring or late summer to build and protect soil.
directions: Broadcast this field pea and oat mix densely over prepared soil in spring or late summer, then rake in and water. The fast-growing combination adds organic matter and nutrients while shading out weeds. Pea plants fix nitrogen and the oats provide structure and biomass, together conditioning even rough ground such as former lawns and vacant lots. Let the stand grow until flowering, then cut and turn it under a few weeks before planting your main crop. The oats often winterkill in cold regions, leaving an easy-to-manage mulch.

scientific_name: Sorghum bicolor × Sorghum bicolor var. sudanense
plant_note: Sow after last frost when soil is above 65°F; produces massive biomass for weed suppression and soil building.
directions: Broadcast or drill seed 3/4 in deep into warm soil; sorghum-sudangrass hybrids germinate and grow aggressively, reaching 6–8 feet before termination. The dense canopy suppresses even aggressive perennial weeds, and allelopathic root exudates further inhibit weed germination. Mow to 4–6 in to encourage regrowth and additional tillering before final termination; allow residue 2–3 weeks to partially break down before transplanting into it. Avoid planting in soybean rotations immediately after, as the sorghum-family residue can briefly stunt legume establishment.

plant_note: Direct sow 1/2 inch deep at 4–6 pounds per 1,000 square feet in warm (65°F+) soil in full sun; sorghum-sudangrass is extremely aggressive in good conditions and can grow 1–2 inches per day in peak summer heat. Terminate with a roller-crimper, tillage, or mowing at or before anthesis.
directions: Direct sow in warm soil (65°F minimum) in full sun after last frost; sorghum-sudangrass is the premier summer cover crop for the South and Gulf Coast, producing more biomass per day than any other cover crop in hot weather — 6–12 feet of lush growth that smothers weeds, suppresses nematodes, builds organic matter rapidly, and breaks up compacted soil with its fibrous root system. The allelopathic root exudates suppress germination of subsequent crops if incorporated when immature; allow residue to fully decompose before planting a following crop. Mow or roll at boot stage (before flowering) to maximize biomass and prevent unwanted reseeding. An outstanding choice for filling the fallow summer season in zone 8–11 gardens, breaking pest and disease cycles between cash crops, and adding large quantities of organic matter to difficult clay or compacted soils.

plant_note: Sow in early spring as soon as soil can be worked for a fast summer smother crop.
directions: Broadcast or drill at roughly 2-3 lb per 1,000 sq ft into a firm seedbed and rake to cover about 1 inch deep. Spring oats germinate quickly in cool soil and produce dense biomass that outcompetes weeds and captures nitrogen. Unlike winter oats, spring oats will not survive hard freezes, which makes winterkill a convenient no-till termination in cold climates. In mild areas terminate by mowing before seed heads form, then incorporate or leave residue as surface mulch. Follow with a nitrogen-fixing legume to balance the carbon added by the grass residue.

plant_note: Sow 6–10 weeks before first frost in mild-winter climates; uniquely reseeds itself by burying its own seed pods underground.
directions: Broadcast inoculated seed at 15–20 lb/acre onto a well-prepared seedbed in late summer to early fall; subterranean clover tolerates poorly drained and acidic soils better than most annual clovers. The prostrate stems root at nodes and the spent flower heads push seed capsules into the soil surface, allowing the stand to self-reseed year after year without replanting. It fixes moderate nitrogen and forms a dense mat that suppresses winter and spring weeds. Mow or graze lightly in late winter to stimulate pod development, then allow to dry before any tillage.

plant_note: Sow after last frost once soil is consistently warm; a standout warm-season nitrogen fixer and nematode suppressor for zone 9–11.
directions: Broadcast or drill seed 1/2 in deep into warm, well-drained soil after danger of frost has passed. Sunn hemp grows rapidly — up to 6 feet in two months — and fixes 100–200 lb of nitrogen per acre while suppressing root-knot nematodes through root exudates. Terminate by mowing at first bloom (roughly 60–70 days) before seed set, since mature plants become woody and slow to decompose. Do not plant where livestock can graze, as the foliage contains alkaloids toxic to most grazing animals.

plant_note: Sow late spring in warm soil; a deep-rooted native perennial grass.
directions: Sow switchgrass on bare soil in late spring once it has warmed, pressing the seed in shallowly and keeping it moist during its unhurried germination. This warm-season perennial forms upright clumps three to six feet tall with airy red-purple seed heads that catch the light and persist into winter. Its deep roots make it exceptionally drought-tolerant and superb for erosion control and wildlife cover. Leave standing stems through winter for interest, then cut back in early spring.

plant_note: Sow 6–10 weeks before first frost; the deep taproot breaks up compacted soil and winter-kills in cold zones.
directions: Broadcast seed about 1/2 in deep in late summer; the thick taproot drills deep and shatters compaction. In cold-winter areas it dies and rots in place, leaving channels and released nutrients. Where it survives, mow before it flowers to prevent seeding.

plant_note: Sow 4–6 weeks before first frost in fall; combines the cold-hardiness of rye with the palatability and residue quality of wheat.
directions: Drill or broadcast seed at 60–90 lb/acre and cover to 1 in depth in fall; triticale is a wheat-rye hybrid that is nearly as cold-hardy as cereal rye but produces softer, lower-lignin residue that breaks down faster in spring. It scavenges soil nitrogen over winter and produces abundant biomass for weed suppression and organic matter. Terminate in early spring at the jointing stage (before the seed head emerges from the boot) by rolling, flail-mowing, or tillage; residue breaks down within 3–4 weeks in warm spring conditions. Pairs well with hairy vetch to combine nitrogen fixation with grass biomass.

plant_note: Sow after last frost; velvet bean is the most aggressive warm-season smothering cover legume, capable of blanketing 100+ sq ft per plant.
directions: Plant large seeds 1–2 in deep at 40–60 lb/acre (or 12 in apart in garden rows) after the soil is consistently warm; velvet bean vines scramble aggressively over weeds, fixing up to 400 lb N/acre equivalent while out-competing even nutsedge and Bermuda grass. All plant parts carry stinging trichomes — wear long sleeves and gloves when handling. Terminate by mowing or machete-chopping at flowering, then incorporate for a massive nitrogen and organic-matter boost. The hard, shiny seeds require scarification or a 24-hour soak before planting to improve germination.

plant_note: Direct sow 1 inch deep at 6–12 inch spacing after soil warms to 70°F in full sun; velvet bean is extremely vigorous and will climb aggressively over other plants — plant where aggressive spread and dense shading are acceptable. The seed pods are covered with irritating hairs; handle with gloves.
directions: Direct sow after soil warms in full sun — velvet bean is one of the most powerful warm-season cover crops and green manures in the tropics and subtropics, producing prodigious quantities of nitrogen-rich biomass in hot, humid conditions while smothering weeds under its dense, vining canopy. It fixes 150–300 pounds of nitrogen per acre and adds enormous quantities of organic matter when turned in. In zone 9–11 it can be used as a summer cover crop between spring and fall garden rotations. The seed pods and leaves are covered with fine silica hairs that cause severe itching on contact; handle all plant parts with gloves. The seeds (roasted and processed to remove L-DOPA) are eaten as a pulse in tropical regions. L-DOPA from mucuna seeds is also studied as a source of the Parkinson's disease medication. An extremely effective but aggressive cover crop suited to tropical and subtropical climates.

plant_note: Sow winter wheat in fall for overwintering or spring wheat in early spring.
directions: Sow wheat by broadcasting or drilling into prepared soil about one inch deep, either in autumn for an overwintering cover or in early spring. Winter wheat establishes roots before dormancy, protecting soil through the cold months and greening up quickly when temperatures rise. As a cover crop, turn it under before it heads out to add organic matter, or let it ripen to a golden head for grain, wheatgrass, or microgreens. Keep evenly moist during establishment for a uniform stand.

plant_note: Frost-seed onto bare or thin ground in late winter to early spring; the freeze-thaw cycle works seed into the soil surface naturally.
directions: Broadcast inoculated seed at 1/4–1/2 lb per 1,000 sq ft onto a lightly raked surface; the tiny seed needs good soil contact but should not be covered more than 1/4 in. White clover spreads by stolons to form a dense living mulch that fixes nitrogen and feeds beneficial insects with nectar-rich flowers. Mow periodically to prevent flowering from shading out adjacent crops, or allow it to bloom freely when used as a pollinator corridor. In annual vegetable beds, turn the stand under in early spring and wait 2–3 weeks before planting so the nitrogen flush is available to the next crop.

plant_note: Broadcast seed in early spring or late summer over prepared or existing turf.
directions: White clover is a low-growing perennial legume used as a lawn alternative or living cover crop, fixing nitrogen to enrich the soil while resisting drought. Broadcast the tiny seed over prepared soil or thin turf in early spring or late summer, then rake in lightly and keep evenly moist until established. Its white, globe-shaped flower clusters bloom from spring into summer, drawing bees and other pollinators. Mow occasionally to keep it tidy, or leave it to bloom in a low-maintenance eco-lawn. It thrives in full sun to part shade and well-drained soil.

plant_note: Plant in late summer for fast fall biomass; it winterkills in most climates.
directions: Broadcast at roughly 0.25-0.5 lb per 1,000 sq ft onto a firm, moist seedbed and rake to a shallow 0.25 inch depth. White mustard germinates rapidly in cool soil and is often grown to suppress cyst nematodes and weeds. It grows quickly but is less winter hardy than brown mustard, so it typically winterkills once temperatures drop into the low twenties. Terminate by mowing at early bloom and incorporating the residue, or simply let frost kill it and leave the residue as protective mulch. Follow with a spring vegetable once residue has broken down.

plant_note: Sow 4–8 weeks before first fall frost, or in early spring as soon as soil can be worked; a fast-growing brassica smother crop with biofumigant properties.
directions: Broadcast seed at 8–10 lb per acre (about 1/4 oz per 100 sq ft) onto a firm seedbed, raking to 1/4 in depth; mustard germinates in cold soil within days and establishes an aggressive canopy that smothers weeds. When chopped and incorporated at early flower stage, the glucosinolates in the tissue break down into biofumigant compounds that suppress soil-borne pathogens and weed seeds — a technique called biofumigation. Mow and immediately till to a 4–6 in depth, then water and tarp the bed for 2 weeks to trap fumigant gases. Terminate before it sets seed, as mustard reseeds abundantly and can become a weed.

plant_note: Sow in early spring as soon as soil can be worked, or in late summer 6–8 weeks before frost; a biennial that fixes up to 150 lb N/acre in its second year.
directions: Scarify or inoculate seed with Rhizobium meliloti, then broadcast at 10–15 lb/acre and cover lightly; white sweet clover tolerates alkaline, poor soils where other clovers fail. The plant forms a taproot in year one and overwinters to bloom prolifically in year two, fixing enormous nitrogen and feeding pollinators. Terminate in year two at early bud stage before stems become woody; chop and incorporate so the high-coumarin stems have several weeks to break down before planting. Avoid using freshly mowed green residue as mulch around brassicas, as coumarin can temporarily inhibit germination.

plant_note: Plant in early to mid fall so plants establish crowns before winter dormancy.
directions: Drill or broadcast at 2-3 lb per 1,000 sq ft about 1 inch deep into a firm, well-drained seedbed. Winter barley tolerates drought and saline soils better than other cereals and releases allelopathic compounds that suppress weeds. It is less cold hardy than rye or wheat, so it performs best in USDA zones 6 and warmer. Terminate in spring by mowing or crimping at the boot stage before pollen shed, then incorporate or leave as mulch. Barley residue breaks down faster than rye, allowing earlier planting of the succeeding crop.

plant_note: Sow from 6 weeks before to just after first frost; the most cold-hardy cover crop, germinating in near-freezing soil.
directions: Broadcast seed at about 1/2 in depth in fall; it germinates in cold soil when little else will. It scavenges leftover nitrogen and smothers weeds over winter, then puts on fast spring growth. Mow and turn under 2–3 weeks before planting, as its residue briefly suppresses small seeds.

plant_note: Drill or broadcast 4-6 weeks before hard freeze so plants tiller before dormancy.
directions: Sow at 60-120 lb per acre or about 2 lb per 1,000 sq ft, drilling 1-1.5 inches deep or broadcasting onto a firm, moist seedbed. Winter wheat overwinters as a rosette, resumes growth in spring, and scavenges residual soil nitrogen while suppressing weeds. It is easier to terminate than cereal rye, making it a good choice for gardeners. Terminate in spring by mowing at the boot to early-flowering stage, then incorporate with tillage or crimp and leave residue as mulch. Allow two to three weeks of decomposition before planting the following crop.

plant_note: Sow in early fall; it matures earlier than common hairy vetch.
directions: Inoculate seed and drill or broadcast at about 1-2 lb per 1,000 sq ft, covering to 1 inch deep in a firm seedbed. Woolly pod vetch is an early-maturing relative of hairy vetch that fixes nitrogen and produces dense sprawling growth. Its earlier flowering makes it easier to terminate ahead of spring planting than standard hairy vetch. Mow or roller-crimp at early bloom for effective no-till kill, then plant into the residue mat. Because it can reseed, terminate before pods harden to limit volunteers in future crops.

plant_note: Direct-sow in early spring or late summer; behaves like white sweet clover but is slightly shorter and prefers drier soils.
directions: Scarify hard-coated seed before sowing to improve germination, and inoculate with Sinorhizobium meliloti for reliable nitrogen fixation in new plantings. Broadcast at 10–12 lb/acre and press lightly into a firm seedbed; yellow sweet clover is more drought-tolerant than most clovers and establishes well on thin, calcareous soils. It overwinters as a rosette and bolts to bloom in spring of the second year; terminate at early bud for maximum N contribution. Allow 3–4 weeks between incorporation and planting to let allelopathic coumarin compounds dissipate.

plant_note: Plant nursery transplants in spring after all frost danger has passed, in rich, moist, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade. In zones 8–9, Abyssinian Banana must be treated as a large annual or overwintered indoors; dig and pot the plant before frost and store in a frost-free location.
directions: Abyssinian Banana is the most dramatic of the ornamental bananas, native to the highland forests of Ethiopia and East Africa and grown worldwide as a bold tropical accent plant for its colossal size and the extraordinary visual impact of its enormous leaves — reaching up to 10 feet long with striking red midribs on deep green blades — that create an instant tropical atmosphere wherever they are planted. Unlike true bananas (Musa species), Abyssinian Banana is monocarpic, meaning each trunk (pseudostem) flowers once after 3–10 years and then dies; unlike rhizomatous bananas it does not produce pups from the base, and in Ethiopia the starchy corm and pseudostem are fermented to produce enset (a culturally crucial food for over 20 million people) rather than relying on the fruit for food. In zones 9b–11 it persists as a perennial; in zones 8–9 it is typically grown as an impressive annual or dug and stored frost-free for the winter. Provide the richest, most moisture-retentive soil available, regular fertilization, and full sun for maximum leaf size.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring; dislikes root disturbance from transplanting.
directions: 'African Bride' bears dramatic white blooms with contrasting near-black centers atop airy, threadlike foliage. Direct sow in place because love-in-a-mist resents transplanting once its taproot forms. Grow in full sun in average, well-drained soil and thin seedlings for good airflow. After flowering, papery, dark-tinged seed pods develop that are excellent in fresh and dried arrangements. It self-sows readily, returning year after year in the garden.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant 2–4 weeks before last frost as plants tolerate light frost.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep in well-drained germination mix; seedlings grow quickly and should be potted on before becoming root-bound. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil as soon as the risk of hard frost has passed; osteospermum thrives in cool weather and slows during peak summer heat. Deadhead spent flowers to encourage continuous bloom and pinch stem tips to promote branching early in the season. In zones 9–11 it may survive as a short-lived perennial; take stem cuttings in late summer to perpetuate favorite selections.

plant_note: Sow after the last frost in a warm, sunny spot.
directions: Sow African flake daisy directly after the last frost, barely covering the seed in a sunny, well-drained bed. These cheerful yellow blooms with purple centers open on compact, fine-foliaged plants that shrug off heat and drought. Deadhead spent flowers to keep the display going through the warm months. Perfect for water-wise borders and containers, it asks for little more than sun and sharp drainage.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring or fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; African iris blooms most profusely in full sun but tolerates partial shade well. Avoid deadheading spent flower stalks — the branching stems continue to produce new flower buds from the same stalk for months.
directions: Plant in well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; African iris is one of the most practical and reliable perennials for zone 8–11 landscapes, earning its common name 'fortnight lily' because it blooms in cycles roughly every two weeks throughout the warm season. The elegant white flowers with yellow and purple markings closely resemble true iris flowers but are produced on branching stalks that continue reblooming without deadheading — the spent blooms drop cleanly without the need for any grooming. The clumping, strap-like, evergreen leaves provide bold, attractive foliage year-round. It is exceptionally drought, heat, salt, and poor-soil tolerant once established, performing equally well in coastal and inland gardens. Divide congested clumps every 4–5 years in spring. A closely related species, Dietes bicolor (yellow African iris), produces pale yellow flowers with purple spots and is similarly adaptable. An outstanding low-maintenance perennial for mass plantings, borders, and water-wise landscapes.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after nights stay reliably above 50°F.
directions: Sow seed 1/4 in deep in a warm starting mix and keep evenly moist until germination. Transplant after all frost risk is gone, spacing larger varieties 12–18 in apart. Deadhead large pompom blooms as they fade to redirect energy to new buds. Tall varieties may need staking in exposed, windy sites.

plant_note: Root leaf-stem cuttings in moistened propagating mix or water; African violets propagate easily from leaf cuttings with petiole attached, inserted into moist mix at 45 degrees — new plantlets emerge from the petiole base in 4–8 weeks. In zone 11–12 they can grow outdoors in deep shade and high humidity.
directions: Root leaf cuttings in moist propagating mix and grow as a houseplant in bright indirect light — never direct sun — in slightly acidic, well-drained, slightly moist potting mix: African violet is the world's most popular flowering houseplant, grown by millions indoors for its rosettes of soft, velvety, dark green leaves and year-round clusters of flowers in purple, violet, lavender, pink, white, and bicolor combinations. The keys to success are bright indirect light (east or north window in summer; south or west in winter), bottom-watering or careful top-watering that avoids wetting the crown and leaves, and consistently moist but not waterlogged, slightly acidic (pH 5.5–6.5) potting mix with good aeration.
