Feed every 4 weeks with a balanced fertilizer formulated for African violets. The genus was recently reclassified from Saintpaulia to Streptocarpus; thousands of named cultivars are available through the African Violet Society of America.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost risk has passed.
directions: Sow dust-fine seed on the surface of moist starting mix without covering — light is essential for germination. Ageratum produces fluffy, powder-puff blooms that attract butterflies and beneficial insects. Shear plants back lightly when flowering slows or plants become leggy to promote a fresh flush of bloom. Consistent moisture is important, as dry conditions cause the plants to stop flowering.

plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs in fall in moist, acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), well-drained, humus-rich, sandy or loamy soil in partial shade; Alabama azalea is a shallow-rooted woodland plant that requires well-drained, acidic soil and cannot tolerate alkaline, compacted, or waterlogged conditions.
directions: Alabama azalea is a rare native deciduous azalea endemic to the limestone cedar glades and sandy, acidic woodlands of central Alabama and adjacent states — one of the most sweetly fragrant of all native azaleas, its white to creamy-white flowers with yellow-orange blotches emit a delicate, spicy-sweet fragrance reminiscent of lemony honey that fills the woodland in spring. It blooms in April after the piedmont azalea but before leaves emerge, producing large, showy clusters of flowers at a time when the garden's background is still relatively bare. Alabama azalea is adapted to the warm, humid climate of the upper Gulf Coastal Plain, tolerating zone 9 summers better than most native azaleas of eastern North America — an important advantage for Gulf Coast native gardens where heat and humidity defeat many Rhododendron species. In acidic woodland gardens and under tall pines, plant in combination with native groundcovers like Carex, Pachysandra procumbens, and native ferns for an authentic Alabama woodland garden.

plant_note: Plant nursery-propagated transplants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to alkaline, rocky or loamy soil in partial shade. Alabama Snow-Wreath is a critically rare Alabama endemic and should only be sourced from nurseries that propagate their own cuttings.
directions: Alabama Snow-Wreath is one of the rarest native shrubs in North America, a monotypic genus (the only species in its genus) restricted in the wild to a handful of limestone bluffs and rocky river slopes in northwestern Alabama and a very few widely scattered natural populations in other southeastern states. In April and May it produces a cloud of unusual, feathery, white, petal-less flowers — the 'petals' are entirely absent, and the display comes entirely from numerous long white stamens — giving the shrub an airy, ethereal appearance reminiscent of its common name. An adaptable plant in cultivation that grows well in partial shade in a wide range of soils from dry and rocky to moderately moist, Alabama Snow-Wreath is easy to propagate from cuttings and is increasingly available from specialty native plant nurseries. Never collect from wild populations — purchase only from nurseries that propagate their own plants from cultivated stock.

plant_note: Start indoors; cold-moist stratification improves germination.
directions: 'Alan's Pride' is a first-year-flowering coneflower with unusual zesty lime-green blooms on graceful 2-foot stems. Start seed indoors and give it a period of cold-moist stratification to break dormancy and improve germination. Grow in full sun in average, well-drained soil where it becomes drought tolerant once established. The blooms draw bees and butterflies, and leaving seed heads standing feeds finches into fall and winter. Divide clumps every few years to keep them vigorous.

plant_note: Start indoors or direct sow after frost; needs light to germinate.
directions: 'Alaska' is a classic, long-lived Shasta daisy bearing large 3-inch white flowers with sunny yellow centers from summer into fall. Press seed onto the surface since it needs light to germinate, and keep it evenly moist until sprouts appear. Grow in full sun in well-drained soil for the sturdiest, most floriferous plants. Deadhead spent blooms to prolong flowering, and the long stems make excellent cut flowers. Divide clumps every two to three years to maintain vigor.

plant_note: Direct sow after danger of frost; resents transplanting.
directions: 'Alaska' nasturtium stands out for its cream-and-green marbled foliage that stays showy even between the bright, edible flowers. Direct sow the large seeds where they are to grow, as nasturtiums dislike having their roots disturbed. Grow in full sun to part shade in lean soil, since overly rich ground produces leaves at the expense of blooms. Both the peppery flowers and leaves are edible and make a colorful garnish. This compact, bushy type is well suited to containers and bed edges.

common_name: Algerian Ivy (Canary Island Ivy, Canariensis Ivy)
plant_note: Root cuttings or plant container transplants in fall or spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; Algerian ivy is more heat and drought tolerant than English ivy and a better choice for zone 8–10 Gulf Coast shade gardens. It can also be grown as a trailing vine for containers.
directions: Plant cuttings in moist, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; Algerian ivy is the superior ivy for Gulf Coast zone 7–10 gardens — larger-leaved, more heat tolerant, and more drought tolerant than English ivy, making it better suited to the summer conditions of the Gulf South. The leaves are 3–8 inches across, rich glossy green (sometimes with a reddish tint in winter), and distinctly larger than the familiar English ivy. The variegated form 'Gloire de Marengo' has silvery gray-green leaves with creamy white margins and is one of the most beautiful of all shade vines. Like English ivy it can climb by aerial roots and spreads vigorously as a groundcover, but has a less aggressive spreading habit than English ivy and is less likely to invade natural areas. Still, plant with care and keep away from forest edges.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; slower to establish than Japanese pachysandra but more ecologically valuable and better adapted to southern heat.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; Allegheny spurge is the native North American alternative to the commonly used Japanese pachysandra (P. terminalis), naturally found on the wooded slopes and ravines of the Appalachian mountains and Southeast. The large, scallop-edged leaves are semi-evergreen to evergreen and often show attractive silver marbling; fragrant white flower spikes in early spring attract early native bees. It performs better in zone 8–9 summer heat than Japanese pachysandra, is less prone to disease, and supports native insects — making it a superior choice in every way for southern shade gardens. Slower to spread than Japanese pachysandra but ultimately forms a dense, attractive groundcover. Never seen spreading invasively.

plant_note: Plant bulbs 4–6 inches deep in wet, acidic, mucky to sandy, humus-rich soil in full sun to light shade near a water feature or in a rain garden; Alligator Lily requires consistently wet to waterlogged soil and will not tolerate drought. Divide bulb clumps every 3–4 years to maintain vigor.
directions: Alligator Lily is a Florida endemic spider-lily found in wet flatwoods, freshwater marshes, and wet prairie margins of south and central Florida, producing elegant white flowers with reflexed petals and a distinctive fringed central cup (corona) characteristic of the Hymenocallis genus. Rare in cultivation despite being well suited to wet gardens, pond margins, and water features, Alligator Lily is a true South Florida native restricted to the peninsula and not found naturally anywhere else in the world. Plant bulbs 4–6 inches deep in wet, acidic, mucky to sandy, humus-rich soil in full sun to light shade near a water feature or rain garden; established clumps multiply steadily through bulb offsets to form impressive colonies in appropriate wet conditions. Alligator Lily is intolerant of drought and requires consistently moist to wet soil — it performs best in natural pond margins, wet flatwood restoration plantings, and South Florida water gardens.

plant_note: Surface sow; needs light to germinate.
directions: This pastel blend of sweet alyssum forms low, spreading mounds smothered in tiny, honey-scented flowers. Sow on the soil surface and press in gently, since the seed needs light to sprout, and keep it moist until established. Grow in full sun to part shade along borders, in rock gardens, or cascading from containers. The fragrant blooms attract beneficial pollinators throughout the cool season. Shear plants back lightly if they slow in summer heat to encourage a fresh flush of bloom.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall with the neck and top half of the bulb above soil level in well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; in zone 8–10 amaryllis naturalize in the ground and bloom reliably each winter-spring without the 6–8 week dry rest period required for potted indoor forcing.
directions: Plant bulbs with the top half exposed in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; amaryllis (correctly Hippeastrum) is one of the most spectacularly beautiful of all bulbous plants, producing massive trumpet-shaped flowers 6–8 inches across in brilliant red, pink, white, coral, salmon, or bicolor striped patterns on hollow, leafless stalks 18–24 inches tall. In zone 8–10 amaryllis naturalizes beautifully in the garden, blooming each January through April without any special treatment — simply plant the bulbs in fall, fertilize lightly in fall and early spring, and allow the foliage to die back naturally each summer. Gulf Coast gardens are famous for spectacular outdoor amaryllis displays along entry walks and in foundation plantings. The large clumps multiply year by year to create a more impressive display. Indoor forcing in pots requires a 6–8 week dry rest period at cool temperatures before returning to warmth and water.

plant_note: Surface sow in early spring or fall; needs light and cool soil.
directions: 'Amazing Grey' produces one-of-a-kind crepe-paper blooms in smoky lavender-grey tones, bred over many years from the Mother of Pearl blend. Scatter the tiny seed on the surface in early spring or fall, as it needs light and cool soil to germinate, and do not cover it. Grow in full sun in average, well-drained soil where it is quite drought tolerant. Because it resents transplanting, always direct sow where it is to bloom. Let some flowers form seed pods and it will happily self-sow for future seasons.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial shade; American alumroot is the most widespread native Heuchera species and the parent of many ornamental cultivars, though H. villosa outperforms it in hot, humid climates of zone 7–9.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial shade; American alumroot is the most widely distributed native Heuchera of eastern North America, found on rocky woodland slopes, cliff faces, and shaded stream banks from Canada to Georgia. The attractive, rounded, shallowly lobed leaves are often marked with attractive metallic silver marbling and remain semi-evergreen in mild winters, providing year-round ground-level interest in shaded borders. Small greenish-white flowers on 18–24 inch stems in late spring are modest but attract native bees. The species is one of the important parents in the development of the hugely popular hybrid coral bells (H. × hybrida) industry, contributing foliage coloring genes. Grows in average to poor soils with good drainage — excessive fertility and moisture promote crown rot. Divide every 3–4 years when the crown heaves from soil.

plant_note: Direct sow seed in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, dry to moderately moist, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; American basketflower germinates in cool soil and is best sown in fall in Gulf Coast gardens, where it overwinters as a basal rosette and blooms profusely in late spring before summer heat.
directions: Direct sow in fall in well-drained, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun — American basketflower is a spectacular, large-flowered native annual of the Texas and Gulf Coast prairies, producing showy, 3–4 inch, thistle-like flower heads in shades of rose-pink to lavender-pink (and rarely white) with an intricate woven 'basket' of straw-colored bracts beneath the blooms — giving rise to the common name. Despite its thistle-like appearance, it is entirely spineless and easy to handle. The large, bright flower heads are superb for cutting and drying, and are heavily visited by bumblebees, honeybees, and monarch butterflies. It is one of the showiest and most easily grown native annuals for the southern prairie garden, blooming from May to July in a single, spectacular season and then self-sowing to return the following year. Outstanding in masses in wildflower meadows alongside Indian blanket, black-eyed Susan, and coneflowers.

plant_note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped trees in late winter or spring in moist, slightly acidic to slightly alkaline, deep, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; basswood performs best in rich, moist, slightly alkaline conditions and is more tolerant of chalk soils than most native trees.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic to slightly alkaline, deep, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; American basswood is one of the most important honey trees in North America — when the intensely fragrant cream-yellow flowers open in June and July, beekeepers consider it the single most important nectar flow of the year, producing linden honey renowned for its delicate, flowery flavor. The fragrance of a flowering basswood in a neighborhood can be detected at a distance of several blocks. The large, heart-shaped leaves, distinctive bract-winged fruiting clusters, and tall, straight trunk make it a notable landscape specimen. Young leaves are edible in spring salads; the inner bark (bast) is one of the best natural fibers for cordage in North America and was extensively used by Native Americans. Tolerates partial shade well, making it suitable for understory planting in very large gardens.

plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring or fall; beautyberry establishes quickly from container stock and begins producing berries the first or second year after planting.
directions: Plant in well-drained to average soil in full sun to partial shade; American beautyberry is a native Gulf Coast shrub with large, coarse leaves and absolutely stunning clusters of metallic magenta-purple berries in fall that persist after leaf drop and create a brilliant late-season display. It is native to the forests, clearings, and forest edges of the Southeast and is uniquely tolerant of heat, drought, and sandy soil. The berries are eaten by 40+ species of birds and the crushed leaves contain callicarpenal, which has documented mosquito-repellent properties. Prune hard (to 12 inches) in late winter every few years to rejuvenate; beautyberry fruits on new wood, so hard pruning stimulates vigorous growth and heavy fruiting. The white-berried cultivar 'Lactea' is also striking.

plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring after frost; established plants tolerate periodic drought once roots are established.
directions: Plant in average, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; this southeastern native's spectacular clusters of vivid magenta-purple berries appear in fall when little else is fruiting and provide a critical food source for mockingbirds, robins, and other wildlife. Cut plants back by one-third to one-half in early spring, as flowers and berries form on current-season wood. In zones 6–7 it may die back to the roots in hard winters but rebounds vigorously in spring; mulch the root zone for winter protection. The berries are edible and have been used to make jelly, though they are quite astringent raw.

plant_note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in moist to moist-well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade. American Bladdernut is excellent for naturalizing moist, shaded areas where few other large shrubs will grow.
directions: American Bladdernut is a large, multi-stemmed native shrub, native to moist, rich deciduous forest understories, streambanks, and floodplain edges throughout eastern North America from Quebec to Florida and west to Kansas, forming thickets in the shade of oaks, maples, and ashes where few other ornamental shrubs survive. In April and May it produces drooping clusters of small, white, bell-shaped flowers attractive to native bees; these are followed in summer by the plant's most distinctive feature — inflated, papery, three-chambered seed pods resembling small green Chinese lanterns that persist into fall and rattle audibly in the breeze, turning tan and remaining decorative into winter. The opposite, trifoliate leaves resemble those of box elder and turn yellow in fall. An underutilized native shrub for difficult shaded, moist sites — excellent for naturalizing under large trees where maintaining lawn is impossible.

plant_note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to slightly alkaline, deep soil in full sun to partial shade. Select disease-resistant cultivars ('Valley Forge', 'Princeton', 'New Harmony') to reduce susceptibility to Dutch Elm Disease; choose open sites with good air circulation.
directions: American Elm was for much of the 19th and early 20th centuries the most important street and shade tree in eastern North America, the classic 'cathedral' tree whose interlocking vase-shaped canopies arched over village streets from New England to the Great Plains in an iconic display of American civic beauty — until Dutch Elm Disease (Ophiostoma ulmi), introduced from Europe in the 1930s, devastated the species throughout most of its natural range and removed it from the American streetscape within a few decades. Today, disease-resistant cultivars ('Valley Forge', 'Princeton', 'New Harmony') have restored American Elm to viability in the landscape and are the preferred planting choice for new trees; these cultivars maintain the original graceful, vase-shaped habit that made the species so beloved, with resistance to DED ranging from moderate to strong. American Elm is adaptable to a wide range of soils from wet bottomlands to dry uplands and tolerates urban conditions, road salt, compaction, and air pollution better than most native trees. Plant disease-resistant cultivars in full sun to partial shade in moist, well-drained soil; provide adequate space for the wide-spreading mature canopy.

plant_note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped trees in late winter or spring in moist, slightly acidic (pH 4.5–6.0), well-drained to moderately moist soil in full sun to partial shade; American holly is dioecious — plant at least one male within 50 feet of female trees for berry production. Most named female cultivars are sold with a recommended male pollinizer.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic (pH 4.5–6.0), well-drained to moderately moist soil in full sun to partial shade; American holly is the native evergreen tree holly of the eastern US, producing the classic red-berried, spiny-leaved holly that symbolizes the Christmas season. It grows naturally from Massachusetts to Florida and west to Texas, often in moist, slightly shaded woodland conditions along the coastal plain and Piedmont. The red berries (persistent through winter on female trees when a male pollinizer is present) are consumed by dozens of bird species, providing critical winter food for robins, cedar waxwings, and mockingbirds. It tolerates partial shade and performs remarkably well in zone 8–9 heat, making it one of the best native evergreen screening or specimen trees for the Gulf Coast garden. Hundreds of named cultivars exist — look for 'Fosteri' (compact), 'Savannah' (heavy fruiting, zone 7–9), and 'East Palatka' (Gulf Coast adapted).

plant_note: Plant small container-grown or balled-and-burlapped trees in fall in well-drained, moist to dry soil; hop hornbeam establishes slowly and resents transplanting of large specimens — start with young nursery stock.
directions: American hop hornbeam is a small to medium understory tree of exceptional adaptability, tolerating everything from deep dry shade under mature oaks to moist bottomland edges — one of the finest native trees for difficult shaded sites where larger trees struggle. Its common name refers to the distinctive hop-like fruiting clusters that hang from branches in summer and fall, providing important food for ruffed grouse, wild turkey, northern bobwhite, and numerous songbirds and small mammals. The wood is extraordinarily hard and dense — historically used for tool handles and fence posts and known as 'ironwood' — and the pale, finely ridged gray bark provides year-round ornamental interest. Grow as a specimen or in groupings beneath taller oaks, hickories, and maples to create a multi-layered native woodland garden; it thrives with minimal care once established and is largely pest and disease free.

plant_note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped trees in early spring or late fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade; transplants well from container stock but resents root disturbance once established.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade; American hornbeam (also called musclewood or blue beech) is one of the finest small native understory trees, growing naturally in the deep shade of bottomland forests and stream banks across eastern North America. The distinctive smooth, gray, sinewy bark with ridge-like muscle-like sinews makes it an outstanding specimen year-round. Delicate hop-like seed clusters in fall provide food for many finch and songbird species. Outstanding golden-orange to orange-red fall color. It grows slowly — 10–12 inches per year — but is very long lived. One of the few small trees genuinely suited to planting in deep shade under large oaks or maples. Excellent for naturalistic woodland gardens and native plant landscapes.

plant_note: Surface sow in early spring or fall; needs light and cool soil.
directions: 'American Legion', the classic Flanders or corn poppy, opens brilliant scarlet-red single blooms that famously symbolize remembrance. Scatter the fine seed over prepared soil in early spring or fall and leave it uncovered, since light aids germination. Grow in full sun in lean, well-drained soil where it thrives with little care. Direct sow where it is to grow because the seedlings dislike being moved. Allow spent flowers to set seed and this heirloom will naturalize and reseed for years.

plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring in moist, acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial shade; American snowbell grows naturally along stream banks and in moist woodland borders and requires consistently moist, acidic soil.
directions: Plant in moist, acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial shade; American snowbell is a beautiful and underused native shrub of moist woodland edges and stream banks throughout the Southeast, producing masses of nodding, pure white, bell-shaped flowers with bright yellow anthers in spring — a spectacular display in a shaded garden setting. The blooms are mildly fragrant and attract long-tongued native bees. It grows naturally along stream banks and moist forest edges in the coastal plain from Virginia to Florida and west to Arkansas. Much less commonly cultivated than its Japanese relative (Styrax japonicus), but equally ornamental and far more wildlife-friendly. The gray-brown bark and elegant branching pattern provide winter interest. An outstanding choice for a moist, shaded woodland garden or stream bank planting in the Southeast.

plant_note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in moist, deep, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral alluvial or bottomland soil in full sun. American Sycamore grows best in deep, moist, fertile soils near water and becomes a massive tree that requires ample space; do not plant near structures, sewer lines, or small yards.
directions: American Sycamore is one of the largest deciduous trees in North America — with trunk diameters commonly reaching 6–8 feet and old specimens exceeding 10 feet in diameter — native to the moist, rich alluvial soils of river bottoms, floodplains, and stream terraces throughout eastern North America from Maine to Florida and west to Kansas and Texas, where its massive, gleaming white upper trunk (revealed as the outer gray-brown bark sheds in large patches) makes it visible from great distances and impossible to mistake for any other native tree. The distinctive mottled, ivory-white, cream, and gray trunk bark is among the most ornamental features of any North American tree, and the large, maple-like leaves create deep, cooling shade in summer; the spherical, golf-ball-sized seed heads hang through winter and are consumed by a variety of birds including American goldfinches. Wood ducks, chimney swifts, and other cavity-nesting species prize the large hollow cavities that develop in old sycamores. Plant in deep, moist, fertile, slightly acidic soil near water features, streams, or in large park settings; it is not appropriate for typical residential lots given its ultimately massive size.

plant_note: Divide established clumps in spring and plant divisions in shallow water (0–6 inches deep) or at the water's edge in full sun to partial shade. American Water-Willow spreads by rhizomes to form dense colonies and is best established from divisions of existing clumps in wet, acidic, sandy or mucky streamside soil.
directions: American Water-Willow is a native aquatic and emergent perennial of clean streams, river margins, and shallow lake edges across eastern North America, where its rhizomatous colonies form dense mats of willow-like leaves in the current, catching and filtering silt while stabilizing stream banks. The bicolored white and violet flowers are produced prolifically from late spring through summer and are outstanding nectar sources for native bumblebees, hummingbirds, and sphinx moths visiting the shallow water's edge. Plant divisions in spring in shallow water (0–6 inches) or at the water's edge in full sun to partial shade; American Water-Willow spreads readily by rhizomes to form dense, erosion-controlling colonies and is an exceptional native plant for water garden edges, stream bank stabilization, and rain garden overflow zones where periodic flooding occurs. The dense underwater stems also provide important spawning and refuge habitat for small fish.

plant_note: Plant container vines in spring near a very strong support; purchase named cultivars like 'Amethyst Falls' or 'Blue Moon' for reliable blooming from an early age rather than seed-grown plants.
directions: Plant container-grown named cultivars near a very strong trellis, pergola, or fence in full sun to partial shade; unlike the invasive Asian wisterias (W. sinensis, W. floribunda), native American wisteria is well-behaved, will not escape into natural areas, and responds to pruning. The fragrant lilac-purple flower clusters appear in late spring, with good repeat bloom through summer on named cultivars. Prune twice yearly — once after the main spring bloom and again in late winter — to maintain size and encourage blooming spurs. American wisteria is naturally found in moist lowlands and swamps and tolerates wetter conditions than Asian species; do not confuse with the highly invasive Japanese and Chinese wisterias.

plant_note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, deep, fertile loamy soil in full sun to partial shade. Yellowwood is a long-lived, low-maintenance native shade tree that does not bloom every year — heavy flowering is often biennial or triggered by summer drought stress.
directions: American Yellowwood is a superb native shade tree, native to rich, well-drained limestone bluffs, rocky slopes, and bottomland forest edges in the mid-South from North Carolina and Kentucky south to Alabama and west to Oklahoma, producing in May and June the most spectacular flowering display of any native shade tree — long, pendulous clusters of fragrant, white, pea-like flowers resembling wisteria that drape the wide-spreading canopy in a breathtaking display. The smooth, light gray bark resembles beech bark and is ornamental year-round; the bright green, pinnately compound leaves turn yellow in fall; and the wood at the heartwood core is bright yellow — hence the common name. Yellowwood blooms on alternate years or following stress, but the wait is always worth it. Grow in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; it adapts to a wide range of conditions once established and is a long-lived, virtually pest-free tree.

plant_note: Plant rooted hardwood cuttings in spring in moist, fertile, well-drained, humus-rich soil in full sun to partial shade; angel's trumpets are heavy feeders and heavy drinkers — they require frequent watering and regular fertilization to produce the large, lush growth and abundant blooms they are capable of. All parts of the plant are highly toxic — do not grow where children or pets may ingest any part.
directions: Plant in moist, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; angel's trumpet is among the most spectacular flowering plants in temperate-tropical horticulture, producing enormous (10–20 inch) pendulous trumpet-shaped flowers in white, yellow, peach, pink, or salmon that hang gracefully downward and fill the air with an intoxicating fragrance, particularly in the evening and night. With adequate water and nutrition, a well-established plant can produce 50–100 blooms simultaneously at peak season. Brugmansia is distinguished from Datura by its pendulous (not erect) flowers and woody, tree-like habit. All parts of the plant are extremely toxic — containing tropane alkaloids including scopolamine, atropine, and hyoscyamine — and should never be grown where children or pets may contact or ingest any part. In zone 8 it may die back in hard winters but regrows from established roots; in zone 9–11 it is evergreen and can be trained as a small tree. Propagate from 12-inch hardwood cuttings placed directly in moist soil.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed and soil has warmed.
directions: Start seeds on the surface of a moist, fine-textured germination mix at 70–75°F; seeds need light to germinate so do not cover. Transplant into full sun after last frost, spacing 12–18 in apart in well-drained soil enriched with compost. Angelonia is heat- and drought-tolerant once established and rarely needs deadheading, as spent florets drop cleanly. Pinch stem tips early to encourage branching and a fuller plant shape.

common_name: Anise Sage (Black and Blue Sage, Brazilian Sage)
plant_note: Plant divisions or container-grown transplants in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; Salvia guaranitica is reliably perennial in zone 7–11 from tuberous roots that overwinter underground — established clumps grow larger and more floriferous each year.
directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; anise sage is one of the finest hummingbird plants for Gulf Coast gardens — the cultivar 'Black and Blue' in particular is prized for its deep, vivid cobalt-blue flowers held in glossy black calyces, producing a stunning color combination that is a magnet for rubythroated hummingbirds from summer through frost. The large, heart-shaped leaves are aromatic when crushed. In zone 7–11 it grows as a perennial from tuberous roots, with top growth dying back after frost and resprouts from established tubers in spring, often growing 4–5 feet tall and wide by midsummer. It spreads by underground stolons to form a substantial colony over time. Cut plants to the ground in late winter to encourage vigorous new growth. Other cultivars include 'Costa Rica Blue' (deeper blue, cooler tones) and 'Argentine Skies' (pale sky-blue).

plant_note: Succession sow every few weeks for continuous bloom.
directions: Sow annual candytuft directly in early spring, making successive sowings every few weeks for a long season of bloom. The flat clusters of white, pink, and purple flowers are fragrant and attract bees and butterflies. Grow in full sun and average well-drained soil, thinning seedlings to prevent crowding. Shear plants after flowering to keep them tidy and encourage a second flush.

plant_note: Sow directly; resents transplanting due to taproot.
directions: Direct sow annual mallow after the last frost, as it forms a taproot and resents transplanting. The large satiny hibiscus-like blooms in shades of pink and white appear all summer on bushy plants. Provide full sun and moderately fertile, well-drained soil, spacing generously for good air circulation. Pinch young plants to encourage branching and deadhead to keep the display fresh.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost; transplant is also possible starting 6–8 weeks early indoors.
directions: Sow seed 1/8 in deep and cover lightly, as darkness can aid germination. Annual phlox prefers cool growing conditions and puts on its best show in spring and early summer. Deadhead spent clusters regularly to prevent seed set and keep flowers coming. In hot climates, plants may slow in midsummer heat; cut back and they will often rebloom as temperatures cool in fall.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger has completely passed.
directions: Sow seed on the surface of moist starting mix, barely pressing in, as light aids germination. Grow on under bright light to prevent tall, weak seedlings before transplanting. Remove spent spikes at their base to encourage a continuous succession of new flower stems. Annual salvia blooms from early summer until the first hard frost.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed.
directions: Sow seed 1/8 in deep in moist starting mix; refrigerate seeds for 5–7 days before sowing to improve germination rates. Transplant after the last frost date into well-drained soil, as verbena is very susceptible to root rot in wet conditions. Shear plants back by one-third in midsummer if they become leggy to stimulate a fresh flush of bloom. Verbena thrives in heat and is drought tolerant once established.

plant_note: Start indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost; transplant only when nights are consistently above 55°F.
directions: Sow seed 1/4 in deep in warm, moist starting mix and maintain soil temperature above 75°F for best germination. Annual vinca is self-cleaning and requires no deadheading, shedding spent blooms on its own. It excels in summer heat and humidity and is highly drought tolerant. Avoid planting in cold or wet soil, as chilling causes the foliage to yellow and growth to stall.

plant_note: Direct sow seed in fall in very well-drained, rocky to sandy, alkaline to neutral, dry soil in full sun; antelope horns has a large taproot that makes it drought tolerant once established but very difficult to transplant — direct sowing in its permanent location is strongly preferred.
directions: Direct sow in fall in rocky, alkaline, or sandy, well-drained, dry soil in full sun — antelope horns is the iconic native milkweed of the Texas Hill Country, southern prairies, and Chihuahuan Desert, producing clusters of unusual, intricate flowers with green-white petals reflexed back to expose greenish-maroon hoods, giving the flower cluster an otherworldly appearance. The large, broad, horizontally spreading leaves are gray-green and waxy; the paired seed pods curve upward like antelope horns, full of silky-plumed seeds in late summer. It is one of the most important early-season monarch butterfly host and nectar plants in Texas, as it emerges and blooms in spring before other milkweeds are available during the critical spring migration period. Antelope horns is a deep-rooted perennial of great drought tolerance and longevity — once established in adapted caliche, limestone gravel, or dry clay it requires virtually no care or supplemental water and lives for decades.

plant_note: Plant in very well-drained, chunky, humus-rich, acidic epiphytic mix (orchid bark, perlite, and peat) in bright indirect light or deep shade; anthurium roots require excellent air circulation and will rot in dense, waterlogged soil. Keep roots slightly elevated if possible for best drainage.
directions: Plant in chunky, well-drained epiphytic mix in bright indirect light to deep shade — anthurium produces some of the most striking and long-lasting of all tropical flowers, with waxy, heart-shaped spathes in red, pink, white, coral, or burgundy surrounding a contrasting spadix, each spathe remaining attractive for 2–3 months. It is one of the most popular tropical houseplants worldwide and the most important cut flower in Hawaii's floriculture industry. As a garden plant in zone 10–12, it thrives in the deep shade of trees and covered patios, exactly where little else grows. The glossy, dark green leaves are themselves ornamental. It is sensitive to direct sun (which burns the leaves), overwatering, and temperatures below 55°F. In zone 9a it is an outstanding container plant for shaded patios, wintering indoors. Keep soil moderately moist and fertilize lightly every 6 weeks.

plant_note: Sow around the last frost; prefers cool growing conditions.
directions: Sow Apricot Beauty calendula a quarter inch deep around the last frost, as cool soil suits germination. This heirloom pot marigold bears soft semi-double blooms in apricot, peach, and pale-gold tones on tidy plants. The edible, medicinal petals are lovely in salads and herbal preparations. Deadhead often to extend bloom, and expect a fresh flush as autumn cools.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost or start indoors 4 weeks early.
directions: 'Apricotta' is an award-winning cosmos with soft apricot-pink blooms centered in yellow on tall, sturdy stems ideal for cutting. Direct sow after the last frost, or start indoors about a month early, barely covering the seed. Grow in full sun in average to lean soil, as rich ground produces foliage rather than flowers. It is drought tolerant once established and blooms prolifically from midsummer to frost. Deadhead or cut regularly to keep the plants flowering and to attract bees and butterflies.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring or early fall in full sun and lean, well-drained soil; avoid fertilizing, which produces tall floppy growth instead of compact flowering plants.
directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean to average soil in full sun; aromatic aster is one of the best fall-blooming native asters for difficult dry conditions, covering itself with lavender-blue flowers from September through hard frost and attracting migrating monarch butterflies and specialist aster bees. Pinch tips once in late spring to promote bushy, self-supporting growth; plants grown in rich soil or shade become floppy and bloom poorly. The leaves have a pleasant sticky, resinous aroma when brushed. Divide every 3 years in spring to keep clumps vigorous; excellent at the front of a drought-tolerant native plant garden or in a parking strip.

plant_note: Plant divisions or container plants in shallow water (2–6 inches deep) or in consistently wet to mucky, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; arrow arum is an emergent aquatic that thrives in the margins of ponds, streams, and wetlands and tolerates more shade than most aquatic plants.
directions: Plant divisions in shallow water (2–6 inches) or saturated mud at pond and stream margins in partial shade to full sun — arrow arum is a beautiful native aquatic perennial of eastern wetlands, producing bold, glossy, deep-green, arrow-shaped leaves on long petioles that emerge from the water in dramatic clumps. The arum-family flowers appear in late spring — a pale green, spathe-enclosed spadix — followed by clusters of dark green berries on a drooping stalk in summer that are relished by wood ducks, which specifically seek them out in late summer and fall. Arrow arum tolerates more shade than most aquatic plants and grows naturally in the partial shade of swamp forests, shaded stream banks, and the shaded margins of cypress bays and bottomland ponds. It is an important structural element of natural wetland plant communities, providing cover and food for numerous wildlife species. The large corm (tuckahoe) was an important food source for indigenous peoples who prepared it to remove irritating calcium oxalate crystals.

plant_note: Root stem cuttings in water or moist potting mix at any time in warm weather; in zone 10–11 plant out after nighttime temperatures are consistently above 55°F. In zone 9 plant in a sheltered, frost-protected spot or use as a container plant brought inside before frost.
directions: Root stem cuttings in water and plant in moist, well-drained, fertile soil in partial to deep shade — arrowhead plant is one of the most adaptable and beautiful shade foliage plants in the world, naturalizing as a groundcover or scrambling vine in zone 10–12 and widely used as a container or houseplant in cooler zones. Young plants produce distinctive arrowhead-shaped leaves that gradually shift to deeply lobed, multi-part leaves as the plant matures and begins to climb or vine. The many cultivars offer an extraordinary range of leaf patterns: solid green, pink, cream-variegated, pale green-silver, and deep burgundy forms are all widely grown. Syngonium thrives in the deep shade of buildings, porches, and mature tree canopies where it provides lush, jungle-like texture. It roots readily wherever stems contact moist soil, spreading to colonize shaded areas in tropical climates; it is considered invasive in Florida and Hawaii when grown outdoors.

plant_note: Plant container shrubs in spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; plant two or more different clones together for better berry set, as cross-pollination improves fruit production.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained to moderately moist soil in full sun to partial shade; arrowwood viburnum is one of the most ecologically valuable native shrubs in eastern North America, supporting over 100 species of Lepidoptera larvae and providing abundant flat-topped white flower clusters in late spring that supply nectar to specialist native bees, followed by clusters of dark blue-black berries in late summer and fall that provide critical pre-migration food for dozens of songbird species. The straight, flexible stems were historically used by Native peoples for arrow shafts. Outstanding fall color ranging from yellow to glossy red-purple. Extremely adaptable to soil types, pH ranges, and light conditions. A foundational native shrub for any wildlife-supporting landscape.

plant_note: Start indoors; cold-moist stratification improves germination.
directions: 'Artisan Red Ombre' is a breakthrough F1 hybrid coneflower, the first individual-color Echinacea from seed, blooming its first year on full, well-branched plants. Start seed indoors and provide cold-moist stratification to encourage even germination. Grow in full sun in well-drained soil where it becomes drought tolerant once rooted. The richly saturated red blooms are excellent for cutting and draw pollinators all summer. Leave some seed heads standing to feed birds and add winter interest.

plant_note: Start indoors; cold-moist stratification improves germination.
directions: 'Artisan Yellow Ombre' is an F1 hybrid coneflower that flowers the first year from seed, opening golden yellow and softening to buttery tones for a sun-drenched ombre effect. Start seed indoors and give it cold-moist stratification to improve germination. Grow in full sun in well-drained soil where it tolerates drought once established. The cheerful blooms are long-lasting in the vase and beloved by bees and butterflies. Leave spent flower heads for winter interest and to feed finches.

plant_note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade. Ashe Magnolia blooms within 3–5 years from seed — much faster than most magnolias — and is appropriate for smaller gardens where Bigleaf Magnolia (Magnolia macrophylla) would be too large.
directions: Ashe Magnolia is one of the rarest magnolias in North America, a critically imperiled native shrub or small tree endemic to the steep, moist, shaded ravines and bluffs of the Florida Panhandle in a handful of counties in far northwest Florida and adjacent Alabama, where fewer than 20 wild populations survive. Despite its rarity in the wild, Ashe Magnolia is relatively easy to grow in cultivation and is widely offered by specialty native plant nurseries; it is often treated as a dwarf or subspecies of the closely related Bigleaf Magnolia (Magnolia macrophylla) but is considered a distinct species by most authorities. The enormous, white, bowl-shaped flowers (12–14 inches across) with purple-spotted centers appear in April and May and are among the most spectacular of any magnolia — attracting native bees and beetles as pollinators. Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade and protect from drying winds that damage the enormous leaves; it is significantly smaller and faster-blooming than M. macrophylla, making it more suitable for the typical home garden.

plant_note: Plant container-grown plants or rooted cuttings in spring or fall in any well-drained soil in full shade to full sun; asiatic jasmine is extremely adaptable and one of the easiest groundcovers to establish in the South. Space 12–18 inches apart and mulch heavily until the ground is covered — the canopy fills in relatively quickly in warm weather.
directions: Plant in any well-drained soil in full shade to full sun; asiatic jasmine is the premier shade-tolerant groundcover for zone 7–11 landscapes — a dense, weed-suppressing mat of small, oval, glossy, dark-green leaves on wiry twining stems that fills in rapidly and maintains its deep color year-round. Unlike its fragrant relative confederate jasmine (T. jasminoides), asiatic jasmine rarely flowers in the landscape and is grown purely for its exceptional foliage ground cover performance. It is the go-to solution for the problem spots in Southern landscapes: dry shade under large oaks and magnolias, slopes that are difficult to mow, areas around tree roots where nothing else grows. Once established, it requires almost no maintenance — shear or mow to 3–4 inches in early spring every few years to remove the older, woody stems and stimulate fresh, dense regrowth. Remarkably drought tolerant, heat tolerant, and deer resistant. Variegated forms ('Ogon Nishiki') have yellow-green leaves that brighten shaded areas.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in spring as soon as soil is workable, or in fall for early establishment.
directions: Plant bulbs scales-down at a depth of 3 times the bulb's height, typically 6–8 in deep, in well-drained, humus-rich soil. Lilies prefer cool roots but sunny tops; mulch the soil surface and underplant with low perennials to shade the root zone. Remove only spent flowers, never the stem and leaves, so the plant can photosynthesize and recharge the bulb. Red lily beetle is a serious pest in many regions; inspect plants regularly and remove beetles and larvae by hand.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring or early fall in moist, organically rich soil with adequate moisture.
directions: Plant crowns so the top of the rhizome sits 1–2 in below the soil surface in consistently moist, humus-rich soil — astilbe does not tolerate drought and will brown and decline quickly without adequate moisture. The feathery plumes in shades from white through pink to deep red are spectacular in shade and semi-shade gardens from late spring through summer. Leave the russet dried plumes standing through fall and winter for ornamental interest. Divide every 3–4 years in spring when flowering declines or the crown heaves above the soil surface.

plant_note: Plant bulbs 2–3 inches deep and 4–6 inches apart in groups in fall in moist, slightly acidic soil; avoid planting in areas that dry out in summer.
directions: Plant bulbs 2–3 inches deep in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade; atamasco lily is a native zephyr lily of the Southeast, blooming in earliest spring (often around Easter, hence the common name) with large, pristine white funnel-shaped flowers that emerge from wet, grassy meadows, wet pinelands, and moist woodland openings from Virginia to Florida. Unlike most bulbs, it tolerates and actually prefers seasonally wet soil in spring followed by summer moisture (not drought). Plant in a low spot, under deciduous trees, or at a rain garden margin where it will receive extra moisture. The bulbs are toxic. The plant naturalizes readily in Gulf Coast gardens and may bloom sporadically in fall as well after rain events.

plant_note: Direct sow seeds in fall in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun at woodland edges and stream banks. Atlantic Goldenrod tolerates more shade than most goldenrods, making it useful for shaded or semi-shaded native plant gardens.
directions: Atlantic Goldenrod is a native perennial of moist, open woodlands, forest margins, and stream banks across the eastern United States, valuable as one of the few goldenrods that tolerates significant shade and moist conditions — making it a useful bridge between the sunny meadow and shaded woodland garden. The distinctive, open, arching inflorescence with well-separated clusters of bright yellow flowers differs from the more tightly packed plume of common goldenrod and is produced from August through October, providing late-season nectar for native bees, moths, and migrating monarch butterflies. A versatile native that thrives along shaded stream banks and moist woodland edges, Atlantic Goldenrod fills an ecological niche not covered by most other goldenrod species. Direct sow seeds in fall in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; established plants spread modestly by rhizomes and are long-lived in appropriate moist, semi-shaded conditions.

plant_note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in wet to moist, highly acidic (pH 3.5–5.5), sandy or peaty soil in full sun. Atlantic White Cedar is a bog and pocosin specialist that requires constantly wet, highly acidic, low-nutrient soil to thrive; it will not prosper in ordinary garden soils or in neutral to alkaline conditions.
directions: Atlantic White Cedar is a graceful, narrow-crowned native conifer, native to the freshwater wetlands, pocosins, and Atlantic white cedar swamps of the eastern coastal plain from Maine south to Florida and along the Gulf Coast to Mississippi, growing in pure stands in permanently wet, highly acidic, peat-based soils where the combination of constant moisture and extreme acidity prevents most other tree species from competing. Its scaly, aromatic, blue-green foliage on a stately, upright, narrow crown makes it one of the most elegant native conifers, and the wood — naturally resistant to decay and reputed to last for centuries — was historically one of the most valuable woods in colonial America, used for ship building, shingles, and water pipes. The dense, humid microclimate within a cedar swamp supports an extraordinary community of rare and specialized plants and animals, and Atlantic white cedar restoration is a major conservation priority along the coastal plain. Grow in full sun in constantly wet, highly acidic, low-nutrient, sandy or peaty soil; it will not adapt to ordinary garden conditions without mimicking the bog environment it requires.

common_name: Aucuba (Japanese Aucuba, Spotted Laurel, Gold Dust Plant)
plant_note: Plant container-grown specimens in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; aucuba is one of the most shade-tolerant broad-leaved evergreen shrubs available and tolerates dense, dry shade under trees where most plants fail. It is somewhat drought tolerant once established.
directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; aucuba is an outstanding broad-leaved evergreen shrub for zone 7–9 shade, producing large, leathery, glossy leaves in solid dark green or in the popular spotted 'Variegata' form with irregular yellow speckles that light up deep shade like scattered gold dust. The spotted forms are called 'gold dust plant' and are among the most striking variegated foliage plants for shade. Female plants bear large, glossy red berries in fall and winter that persist attractively; a single male plant is required for fruit production. Aucuba tolerates dense, dry shade under trees better than virtually any other broad-leaved evergreen shrub, surviving in conditions that defeat hostas, ferns, and most other shade plants. It is the ultimate plant for the dry, root-filled shade under large oaks and pines in the Gulf South.

plant_note: Sow after the last frost when soil is warm.
directions: Direct sow Autumn Beauty sunflowers an inch deep after the last frost, spacing them a foot or more apart for their branching habit. Each plant sends up multiple stems glowing in gold, bronze, orange, and burgundy, reaching five to seven feet tall from midsummer into fall. Their pollen-rich faces draw bees and butterflies, and the blooms make long-lasting cut flowers. Stake in windy sites and leave a few heads to ripen for the birds.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring through early summer; can also be set out in early fall in warm climates.
directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, humus-enriched soil in partial to full shade; the coppery-bronze new fronds that emerge in spring and again after stress give this fern its common name. Once established in zones 7 and warmer it remains semi-evergreen, holding most of its attractive glossy deep-green fronds through winter. It tolerates heat and moderate drought better than many ferns, making it a strong choice for southern shade gardens. Divide clumps in early spring every 4–5 years; each division should retain a central crown with attached roots.

plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring in very well-drained, alkaline to neutral, lean soil in full sun; excellent drainage is critical — this plant rots in heavy, wet clay soils.
directions: Plant in very well-drained, alkaline to neutral, lean to average soil in full sun; autumn sage is one of the most reliably blooming native shrubs in zone 7–9, producing bright red (or pink, white, coral, or burgundy in named cultivars) flowers from spring through hard frost — as close to continuous bloom as any woody plant achieves. It grows naturally on dry, rocky, limestone bluffs and canyon slopes in the Texas Hill Country and Chihuahuan Desert and is outstanding in zone 8–9 heat with excellent drainage. Ruby-throated and black-chinned hummingbirds are primary pollinators. Shear back by one-third in midsummer to encourage fresh growth and extended fall bloom. Named cultivars include 'Dark Dancer' (deep red), 'Furman's Red' (brilliant scarlet), and 'Big Pink' (soft pink). Deer resistant.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring after danger of hard frost has passed, or start indoors 4 weeks earlier; can also divide established clumps in spring.
directions: Sow seed shallowly in well-drained, gritty soil in full sun; this Great Plains native is exceptionally drought tolerant once established and thrives on neglect in lean soils where more pampered plants struggle. The brilliant sky-blue flower spikes in late summer and fall are spectacular when planted in mass and represent some of the purest blue available in the late-season garden. Stake plants loosely or install grow-through supports in spring, as the tall stems tend to flop after rain without support. Cut back to 6 inches in late winter; avoid dividing established plants frequently as they resent disturbance.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring or fall in mild-winter regions.
directions: Sow this California native directly where it is to grow, as it dislikes root disturbance. Press seed lightly into cool, moist soil and expect germination within a few weeks. The low mounding plants bear sky-blue flowers with white centers and thrive in the cool weather of spring. Provide light afternoon shade in hot climates and keep soil consistently moist to prolong bloom.

plant_note: Direct sow 4–6 weeks before last frost; seeds germinate in cool soil and young plants are frost hardy. For fall bloom, sow again in late summer.
directions: Direct sow seed 1/4 in deep in early spring as soon as the soil can be worked. Bachelor buttons resent transplanting due to a taproot and grow best when started in place. Deadhead spent flowers to prolong blooming, or allow a few to set seed for natural reseeding. Plants decline in summer heat; a second sowing in late summer yields a flush of fall bloom in cool climates.

plant_note: Start indoors in darkness; seed needs darkness and cool to germinate.
directions: 'Back to Black' bears compact plants covered in tiny three-quarter-inch flowers of the deepest purple, so dark they appear jet black. Start seed indoors in darkness at cool temperatures, since viola seed germinates best without light. Grow in full sun to part shade, taking advantage of the cool weather of spring and fall when violas perform best. Use it as a spring bulb cover or for fall color once summer annuals fade. Deadhead to prolong bloom, and the flowers are edible for garnishing.

plant_note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial shade. Bahama Wild Coffee is a frost-tender native shade shrub suited to zones 9b–11 and an excellent understory plant under live oaks and tropical hardwoods in south Florida landscapes.
directions: Bahama Wild Coffee is a compact native evergreen shrub, native to the coastal hammocks and tropical hardwood forests of south Florida, the Keys, the Bahamas, and the Caribbean, where it grows as a common understory shrub beneath larger canopy trees in partial to full shade. Similar to Wild Coffee (Psychotria nervosa) but with smaller, shinier, privet-like leaves (hence the species epithet ligustrifolia, meaning privet-leaved), it produces small white flowers year-round that attract native bees and butterflies, followed by clusters of red berries that turn black and are avidly consumed by migratory warblers, thrushes, and robins in fall and winter. An outstanding native shade shrub for zone 9b–11 landscapes where its shade tolerance, modest size, year-round bloom, and exceptional wildlife value make it a versatile foundation plant, hedge component, or understory specimen. Grow in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; established plants tolerate brief drought but prefer consistent moisture.

plant_note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped trees in late winter or spring; despite being a wetland tree, bald cypress performs well in ordinary, well-drained soil — it does not require wet conditions to thrive. Plant 6–10 feet from structures to allow for its natural buttressed base.
directions: Plant in full sun in any moist to wet or even ordinary well-drained soil; bald cypress is the defining tree of the Deep South and Gulf Coast swamps, but contrary to popular belief it grows and thrives in ordinary garden soil without standing water. The feathery, pale green foliage on horizontal branches turns brilliant copper-orange in fall before dropping — making this an unexpectedly beautiful deciduous conifer for the landscape. In wet soil the trees produce distinctive 'knees' (woody projections from the root system) that emerge from the water; in normal garden soil, knees are usually minimal or absent. The tree is extremely long-lived (centuries to millennia in the wild), remarkably storm-resistant, and grows 1–2 feet per year when young. An excellent large-scale specimen or street tree in the South.

plant_note: Attach individual rosettes or clumps to tree branches or mounts using monofilament; Ball Moss naturally seeds itself and colonizes tree bark, utility wires, and fences through wind-dispersed seeds. No soil or watering required once established — it is a true atmospheric bromeliad.
directions: Ball Moss is a native atmospheric bromeliad, native to the southeastern United States, Central America, South America, and the Caribbean, growing as an epiphyte on tree branches, utility wires, and even bare rock and fences in zones 8–11 — the silver-gray, ball-shaped clusters of recurved, scale-covered leaves are a familiar sight on live oaks and other trees throughout the Gulf Coast, Texas, and Florida. Often confused with Spanish moss (Tillandsia usneoides), Ball Moss grows in compact, round clusters rather than draping curtains, and is similarly harmless to its host tree — it uses the tree only for support and draws no nutrients from it, absorbing water and minerals entirely from the air and rain through its silvery trichome-covered leaves. Tiny, inconspicuous lavender flowers appear on slender stalks in late summer, followed by elongated seed capsules that split to release seeds attached to silky hairs (like dandelion seeds) that carry them on the wind to new hosts. Ball Moss requires no care, no soil, and no watering — simply attach a clump to rough bark in a bright location and allow it to grow and self-seed.

plant_note: Sow around the last frost; calendula prefers cool weather.
directions: Sow Ball's Orange calendula a quarter inch deep around your last frost date, as it germinates best in cool soil. This heirloom pot marigold bears fully double, brilliant orange blooms on sturdy stems, both edible and prized for herbal salves. Deadhead regularly to prolong flowering, and pick petals for teas or garnishes. It reseeds happily and often gives a second flush as temperatures cool in fall.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost or direct sow outdoors in early spring; blooms appear the second year from seed.
directions: Sow seeds shallowly, just barely covering them, since light aids germination; thin or transplant to 12–18 in spacing. Balloon flowers are late to emerge in spring — mark their location to avoid accidentally digging the fleshy taproot. Deadhead spent flowers to extend the blooming period through summer, and avoid dividing since the taproot resents disturbance. Plants are long-lived and improve in beauty over many years with minimal care.

plant_note: Plant divisions or container transplants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to alkaline, lean to moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; bamboo muhly is native to Arizona, New Mexico, and Mexico and tolerates both drought and light shade, making it versatile for difficult spots.
directions: Plant in well-drained, lean soil in full sun to partial shade; bamboo muhly is a large, feathery ornamental grass that is often mistaken for young bamboo at first glance — the numerous, fine, arching stems bear tiny delicate leaves and produce an airy, light-filtering visual effect remarkably similar to a small bamboo clump but without the invasive spreading habit. It grows naturally in rocky canyon bottoms and streamside slopes in the Sonoran and Chihuahuan Desert region, where it tolerates both drought and periodic flooding. Unlike most ornamental grasses that are grown primarily for fall plumes, bamboo muhly is grown mainly for its outstanding texture and motion — a billowing cloud of delicate stems that moves with the slightest breeze. It tolerates light shade and makes a superb screen or specimen. Cut back to 12 inches in late winter.

plant_note: Plant divisions in fall in moist to moderately dry, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; epimediums tolerate dry shade under established trees better than almost any other perennial groundcover. Do not disturb established plantings unnecessarily — they improve dramatically over years.
directions: Plant divisions in moist to moderately dry, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; epimedium (barrenwort, bishop's hat) is arguably the most underused and underrated of all shade groundcovers — a slowly spreading, low-maintenance perennial that tolerates dry shade under established tree roots better than any other herbaceous perennial. The attractive, heart-shaped leaflets are often mottled with red or bronze when young, and the dainty four-petaled flowers resembling tiny hats or butterflies in yellow, pink, white, red, or bicolor appear in spring. E. x versicolor 'Sulphureum' has pale yellow flowers and reliably semi-evergreen foliage in mild winters. Once established, epimediums are nearly drought proof and essentially pest-free. Cut old foliage to the ground in late winter before new growth begins to showcase the spring flowers. A plant that rewards patience — increasingly impressive each year after establishment.

plant_note: Plant container specimens in fall in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, rocky to clay soil in full sun to partial shade; bastard oak is adapted to the limestone soils and rocky, alkaline, well-drained upland sites of the Edwards Plateau and Gulf Coast prairies, where it grows in conditions that exclude most other oaks.
directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, rocky to clay, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun — bastard oak (Quercus sinuata) is a medium to large deciduous white oak of the limestone-dominated Edwards Plateau, Cross Timbers, and Gulf Coast prairies of Texas and adjacent states, notable for its distinctive leaves with deep, rounded, scalloped lobes unlike any other Texas oak. It grows on rocky limestone hillsides and in deep clay draws, tolerating both the thin, alkaline, rocky soils of the Hill Country and the heavy black clay soils of the Blackland Prairie with equal adaptability. The tree is moderately fast-growing for an oak, long-lived, and provides outstanding shade and wildlife value (acorns in the white oak group ripen in one season and are highly palatable to deer and wildlife). An underutilized oak for the challenging alkaline, clay, and limestone soils of central and south Texas garden sites where other oaks perform poorly.

common_name: Beach Morning Glory (Railroad Vine, Bay Hops)
plant_note: Sow scarified seeds directly in sandy, well-drained, infertile soil in full sun; nick the hard seed coat or soak in water 24 hours before planting. Beach morning glory requires pure beach sand or sandy coastal soil — it will not tolerate clay or fertile soil, and actually grows best in the poorest, driest, most exposed coastal conditions.
directions: Sow scarified seeds in sandy, infertile coastal soil in full sun; beach morning glory (railroad vine) is one of the most important native plants of tropical and subtropical beaches and coastal dunes worldwide — a prostrate, creeping vine that can spread 60 feet or more along the sand from a single plant, rooting as it goes, stabilizing shifting dunes against erosion with a mat of large, distinctive bilobed hoofprint-shaped leaves. The large, rose-purple morning glory flowers are produced throughout the growing season and are a beautiful ornamental bonus. Each stem can extend 100 feet, rooting at nodes wherever moisture is available. It is resistant to salt spray, storm surge flooding, and hurricane-force winds in ways that few other plants can match. An outstanding native for coastal restoration, living stabilization of beach dunes, and coastal ornamental planting. It is not invasive in its native coastal range.

plant_note: Direct sow seed after last frost in very well-drained, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic to slightly alkaline, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun; beach sunflower is a sprawling, fast-growing annual that self-sows prolifically to naturalize in open, sunny, sandy soils along the Gulf Coast.
directions: Direct sow after last frost in well-drained, sandy to loamy, dry soil in full sun — beach sunflower is a fast-growing, sprawling native annual of Gulf Coast beaches, dunes, sandy roadsides, and open flatwoods, producing cheerful, 2–3 inch yellow sunflowers with dark centers from early summer through frost on prostrate, branching stems that spread to cover large areas with a carpet of flowers. In zone 9–11 it blooms nearly year-round in sunny open spots. Unlike most sunflowers, this species is extremely heat and salt tolerant, thriving in sandy, nutritionally poor soils and handling reflected heat from pavement and buildings without complaint. It is an outstanding, zero-irrigation wildflower for difficult dry, sunny, sandy sites in the coastal South — slopes, parking lot edges, dry medians, and hot foundation exposures. Self-sows prolifically to maintain a persistent colony. The seeds are an excellent food source for goldfinches and sparrows in winter.

plant_note: Direct sow fresh acorns in fall at 1–2 inch depth in dry, sandy or rocky, strongly acidic, extremely well-drained, infertile soil in full sun. Bear Oak is highly specific to acid barrens and pitch pine-scrub oak habitats and will not compete in more productive or shaded soils. It spreads by rhizomes to form dense thickets.
directions: Bear Oak is a low, colony-forming native scrub oak of the acid pine barrens, serpentine outcrops, dry sandy ridges, and coastal heathlands of the northeastern United States, where it is a dominant species of the globally rare pitch pine-scrub oak barrens community from Massachusetts south to North Carolina. The small, irregularly multi-lobed leaves have a distinctive whitish, velvety underside; the abundant small acorns are a critical food source for black bears (the origin of the common name), ruffed grouse, white-tailed deer, and numerous songbirds in the barrens communities where Bear Oak's dense, fire-adapted thickets provide both food and cover. Highly fire-adapted, Bear Oak resprouts vigorously from deep rhizomes after the frequent, low-intensity fires that historically maintained pitch pine barrens and oak-scrub habitats, making it nearly impossible to eliminate once established in appropriate conditions. Direct sow fresh acorns in fall in strongly acidic, infertile, well-drained, sandy or rocky soil in full sun; Bear Oak requires the same fire-maintained, nutrient-poor conditions as its native barrens habitat.

plant_note: Plant rhizomes in late summer to early fall so roots establish before frost; divisions planted after midsummer bloom by the following spring.
directions: Set rhizomes horizontally with the top surface exposed to the sun — never bury them, as iris borers and rot thrive under covered rhizomes. Plant in late summer after bloom, with the fan of leaves pointing in the direction you want growth to spread. Divide every 3–4 years when clumps become overcrowded, discarding the woody center and replanting healthy outer fans. Good drainage is essential; soggy soil causes rhizome rot.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring in cool soil; soak seed first.
directions: 'Beaujolais' is a Spencer-type sweet pea with deep, wine-red blooms on long, sturdy stems perfect for bouquets. Soak the seed overnight and direct sow in early spring in cool soil, since sweet peas thrive in chilly weather. Provide a trellis or netting for the climbing vines and grow in full sun to part shade in rich, well-drained soil. Keep plants evenly watered and cut flowers often to prolong blooming, which can extend into fall in mild climates. The large ruffled flowers are lightly fragrant.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring or fall in moist, well-drained soil with good air circulation.
directions: Plant in moist but well-drained soil rich in organic matter; good air circulation around plants is essential to minimize powdery mildew, their primary disease issue. The shaggy, two-lipped flowers are magnets for hummingbirds, bees, and butterflies from midsummer into fall. Deadhead spent blooms to extend flowering and cut plants back by one-third after the first flush to encourage a second wave. Divide every 2–3 years in spring because the spreading rhizomes exhaust the soil in the clump's center.

plant_note: Direct sow around last frost date; seeds need cool temperatures to germinate and benefit from cold stratification.
directions: Refrigerate seeds for one week before sowing; then direct sow on the soil surface in early spring since seeds need light and cool temperatures to germinate. Thin to 9–12 in apart — crowded plants are prone to powdery mildew. The tall, architectural stems bear small white flowers nestled inside large, shell-shaped green calyces that remain ornamental for months and dry beautifully. Cut stems for fresh or dried arrangements before calyces begin to brown.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil.
directions: 'Benary's Giant' is a florist favorite with fully double, densely petaled blooms up to 6 inches across in a rainbow of colors on tall, strong stems. Direct sow after the last frost once soil is warm, or start indoors a few weeks early. Grow in full sun in rich, well-drained soil and space generously for airflow, which helps prevent powdery mildew. The long-lasting flowers hold up to heat and rain and are outstanding for cutting. Cut or deadhead frequently to keep butterflies visiting and blooms coming until frost.

common_name: Bengal Clock Vine (Sky Vine, Blue Trumpet Vine)
plant_note: Plant rooted cuttings in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil in full sun; bengal clock vine grows extremely rapidly and requires a very sturdy, large trellis, pergola, or fence — it can cover a large structure in a single season and is considered invasive in Florida and Hawaii. Do not plant near natural areas.
directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun on a very large, sturdy support structure; the Bengal clock vine (sky vine) is one of the most spectacular flowering vines available for warm climates, producing pendant clusters of enormous (3–4 inch), lavender-blue to pale blue funnel-shaped flowers with white throats continuously from spring through frost in zone 9–11. The huge, heart-shaped leaves and rampant growth create a dense, cooling shade canopy over pergolas and arbors very quickly. It is an outstanding choice for covering large unsightly structures rapidly. However, this vine is INVASIVE in Florida, Hawaii, and similar warm climates — plant only where you can control its spread and never near waterways or natural vegetation. In zone 9a it often dies back to established roots in hard winters and recovers rapidly; in true zone 10+ it is a permanent, massive vine.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall in moist to wet, acidic, sandy or peaty soil in full sun; bent-awn plumegrass naturally occurs in moist longleaf pine savannas, wet roadside ditches, and boggy depressions — requires consistently moist, acidic soil and full sun.
directions: Bent-awn plumegrass is a spectacular native grass of Gulf Coastal Plain wet longleaf pine savannas and roadside swales, producing large, open, silky plumes of copper-tinted to silvery-white spikelets in fall that shimmer in the breeze and rank among the most beautiful of all native grass seed heads in the Southeast. Each spikelet has a distinctive twisted, bent awn (bristle) that catches the light and gives the plume a textured, almost sparkling appearance — the 'bent-awn' common name accurately describes this key feature. Like its close relative sugarcane (in the same genus), bent-awn plumegrass has reed-like stems with prominent nodes and broad, corn-like leaves that give it a bold, architectural quality during the growing season; the tall, plumed form makes it an excellent vertical accent in wet native meadows and rain garden borders. It grows naturally alongside other savanna species including bladderworts, sundews, orchids, and pitcher plants in the fire-maintained wet pine flatwoods of the Gulf Coast, where it is an indicator of high-quality natural habitat.

plant_note: Sow in spring; seeds benefit from cold stratification, or fall sow.
directions: Surface-sow wild bergamot in spring after a period of cold, moist stratification, or sow outdoors in fall to let winter do the chilling. This native perennial in the mint family spreads into clumps of aromatic foliage topped by lavender, tufted blooms that hum with bees and hummingbirds. Give it good airflow to reduce powdery mildew, and deadhead to encourage repeat flowering. Deer and drought both leave it largely alone once established.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring, just below soil level, with leaves pointing upright; in warm climates fall planting is equally successful.
directions: Plant in well-drained, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full shade; the large, leathery, glossy leaves — which turn attractive shades of red and bronze in winter where temperatures drop below freezing — earn this plant a spot as one of the best evergreen shade groundcovers. The clusters of pink or magenta flowers appear in early spring on stout red stems, often before the last frost. Remove tattered older leaves by pulling them sharply downward to snap them free from the rhizome. Divide crowded plants every 4–5 years in spring by cutting through the woody, surface-creeping rhizomes.

plant_note: Direct sow on the surface of prepared, weed-free soil in spring; big bluestem seed germinates best with good soil contact. Alternatively, cold-stratify seed for 30 days before spring sowing to improve germination rate.
directions: Plant in well-drained to average, dry soil in full sun; big bluestem is the dominant grass of the tallgrass prairie, once covering hundreds of millions of acres from Manitoba to Texas, and is a cornerstone native grass for prairie restorations, meadow gardens, and large-scale naturalized plantings. The blue-green foliage turns brilliant orange-copper-burgundy in fall and holds through winter as attractive standing tan straw. The distinctive three-parted seed heads — resembling turkey feet in silhouette — are architectural and wildlife-valuable. Deep roots (up to 10 feet) make it exceptional for erosion control and sequestering carbon. Extremely drought-tolerant once established; never needs irrigation or fertilizer in average to dry soil. Cut back to 6 inches in early spring before new growth begins.
