plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring or fall in well-drained, amended soil; in zones 8–10, fall planting is preferred. Dig a wide, shallow hole — 2x the width of the root ball — and set the plant at the same depth it grew in the container.
directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in morning sun with afternoon shade; bigleaf hydrangeas are the classic summer-blooming shrubs with large mophead or lacecap flower clusters in blue, pink, or white. Flower color depends on soil pH — blue in acidic soil (pH below 5.5) and pink in alkaline soil (pH above 6.5); to maintain blue, add aluminum sulfate and maintain pH below 6.0. In the South (zones 8–9) afternoon shade is essential to prevent wilting and maintain flower quality. Old-wood bloomers (most classic varieties) should be pruned only immediately after flowering — never in fall, as this removes next year's buds. Reblooming types such as 'Endless Summer' bloom on both old and new wood, making them more reliable after hard winters.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring in consistently moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, fertile soil in partial to full shade; ligularia wilts dramatically in dry conditions or afternoon heat — it requires consistent moisture and shade from afternoon sun.
directions: Plant in consistently moist to wet, humus-rich, fertile soil in partial to full shade; ligularia is a bold-textured Asian shade perennial valued for its very large, rounded, kidney-shaped leaves and clusters of bright orange-yellow daisy-like flowers on tall stems in midsummer — providing rare, vibrant color in the shaded garden at a time when most shade plants have finished blooming. The large, purple-backed leaves on named cultivars like 'Desdemona' and 'Othello' are dramatically ornamental even out of bloom. However, ligularia is extremely sensitive to heat and dry air — the large leaves wilt badly in full afternoon sun or dry conditions, recovering at night but looking stressed through the day. It requires consistently moist to boggy soil and cool shade to look its best. Best suited to cool-summer climates (zone 4–7) or consistently moist, shaded stream banks in zone 8. An outstanding plant for boggy shade gardens.

plant_note: Plant container-grown trees in spring in moist, acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade; bigleaf magnolia requires protection from strong winds, which shred the enormous leaves. Partial afternoon shade is important in zones 7–8.
directions: Plant in moist, acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil sheltered from strong winds in partial shade; bigleaf magnolia holds the distinction of producing the largest simple leaves of any native tree in North America — up to 32 inches long and 12 inches wide — and the combination of these extraordinary leaves with the enormous, intensely fragrant white flowers (up to 16 inches across) makes this one of the most spectacular ornamental trees in the world. The cream-white flowers in late spring to early summer have a distinctive sweet fragrance detectable from considerable distance. It grows naturally as a rare understory tree in moist, protected ravines and hollows of the South Appalachians and Gulf States. Site it in a protected, partly shaded location away from wind — the huge leaves shred easily in exposed sites. The rose-pink fruiting cones in fall are ornamental and provide food for birds.

plant_note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in moist, acidic, well-drained to moist soil in partial shade. Bigleaf Snowbell is most spectacular when planted where its pendant white flowers can be viewed from below, such as on a slope above a path or at the edge of a raised bed.
directions: Bigleaf Snowbell is a beautiful native understory shrub or small tree, native to the moist, rich deciduous forests of the southeastern United States from Virginia and Tennessee south to Florida and west to Louisiana, where it grows as a multi-stemmed shrub or small tree 6–12 feet tall in the shade of larger hardwoods and pines along stream terraces and ravine slopes. In May and June it produces cascading racemes of fragrant, pure white, bell-shaped flowers with conspicuous yellow stamens that cover the branches in a spectacular display and attract native bees; the flowers are best appreciated from below, where the full pendant clusters are visible against the dark canopy. The large, soft-textured leaves (up to 5 inches long) give the plant its common name and create an attractive bold texture in the shade garden. Grow in moist, acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade and protect from drought; it is more adaptable to heat and humidity than Japanese snowbell (Styrax japonicus) and is a superior choice for southeastern gardens.

plant_note: Plant container-grown divisions in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; bird of paradise blooms most prolifically when the roots are slightly restricted — avoid overpotting or overly large planting holes. Plants from seed take 3–5 years to bloom; divisions flower within 1–2 years.
directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; bird of paradise is one of the most iconic and recognizable flowering plants in the world, producing dramatic flowers of vivid orange and electric blue that resemble the head of a tropical bird emerging from a boat-shaped green spathe — each flower head lasts several weeks and established clumps produce flowers nearly year-round in frost-free gardens. A well-established clump of 5–7 crowns in a sunny location in zone 9–12 is one of the most spectacular things in any garden. The broad, paddle-shaped leaves on long petioles provide bold architectural foliage between bloom cycles. Slightly root-restricted plants bloom far more freely than those planted in oversized holes — this is one plant that thrives in a somewhat confined root zone. Water deeply but infrequently once established and apply a slow-release balanced fertilizer in spring and again in midsummer. Divide large clumps in spring for propagation, taking care to minimize root damage.

plant_note: Plant container-grown plants or divisions in fall in very well-drained, strongly acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), sandy or rocky, dry, poor soil in full sun to partial shade; birdfoot violet will not thrive in clay, wet, or fertile soils — it requires the conditions of the sandy pine barrens and dry rocky glades in which it is native.
directions: Plant in very well-drained, strongly acidic, sandy or rocky, dry, poor soil in full sun to partial shade — birdfoot violet is one of the most beautiful of all native violets, with the largest and most vividly colored flowers of the eastern species — 1 inch lavender to pale violet or bicolored (the classic bicolor form has dark upper petals and pale lower petals with an orange eye). The deeply divided, multi-lobed leaves closely resemble a bird's foot, giving the plant its common name. Unlike most violets that thrive in moist, rich soil, birdfoot violet is strictly a plant of dry, poor, acidic, sandy, or rocky soils — pine barrens, sandstone outcrops, and dry, open glades where soil fertility is minimal. It will languish and die in clay or rich soil. An outstanding and distinctive native for sandy, acidic rock gardens and dry native plant gardens in zones 3–8; a specialist larval host for several native fritillary butterflies.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil, or start indoors 3-4 weeks early.
directions: 'Birdhouse' produces hard-shelled gourds that, once cured, can last for decades and be carved, painted, or drilled like wood for countless craft projects. Direct sow after frost in warm soil or start indoors a few weeks early, giving the vigorous vines plenty of room to sprawl or a sturdy trellis to climb. Grow in full sun in rich, well-drained soil with steady moisture through the long season. Allow the gourds to fully mature and dry on the vine, then cure them in a dry, airy spot for several months before crafting.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring 2–4 weeks before last frost; does not transplant well — sow in situ.
directions: Sow seeds directly where they are to grow, pressing them onto moist soil — they need light to germinate; thin to 9–12 in apart. Ammi majus produces flat, lacy white umbels similar to Queen Anne's Lace but with cleaner, more refined flowers that are highly valued by florists. The feathery foliage and long stems make it an ideal filler in bouquets with a vase life of 7–10 days. Successive sowings 3–4 weeks apart extend the harvest period; plants bolt in summer heat so succession-sow in spring only.

plant_note: Sow in spring; press seed onto surface as light aids germination.
directions: Press black eyed Susan seed onto the soil surface in spring without covering, since light aids germination. This tough native produces golden petals around a chocolate-brown cone, blooming for months and drawing bees and butterflies. It tolerates heat, drought, and poor soil, and is generally left alone by deer. Deadhead for tidiness or leave late seed heads for goldfinches and self-seeding.

plant_note: Plant container specimens (ball-and-burlap or container-grown) in fall in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist, loamy soil in full sun to partial shade; black gum develops a deep taproot and transplants best when young — avoid moving established specimens.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist, loamy soil in full sun to partial shade — black gum consistently ranks among the top native trees for fall foliage color: its leaves turn brilliant shades of scarlet, orange, purple, and wine-red as early as late August in the South, among the first of all trees to change, providing exceptionally early and long-lasting fall color in the landscape. The tree is pyramidal when young and becomes more irregular and picturesque with age; horizontal branching and somewhat layered architecture make it attractive year-round. Small, dark blue to black berries in clusters ripen in September–October and are consumed by over 30 species of songbirds and mammals. Black gum is one of the most important wildlife trees in the eastern US due to its early-ripening, high-fat fruits. Adaptable to a wide range of moisture conditions from dry upland slopes to wet bottomlands — an outstanding landscape tree for native plantings.

plant_note: Plant container specimens in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun to partial shade; black haw is more drought tolerant than most viburnums and succeeds in drier, shadier conditions that defeat other viburnums.
directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade — black haw is a native multi-stem large shrub or small tree of open woodlands, roadsides, and fence rows throughout the eastern US, valued for four seasons of ornament: flat-topped clusters of white flowers in April, lush summer foliage, brilliant scarlet to burgundy fall color, and clusters of ripening fruit that progress from green to pink to blue-black as fall advances. The dark blue-black berries are edible and have a sweet, raisin-like flavor when fully ripe, used traditionally to make preserves, wine, and fruit leather; they are also heavily consumed by migrating birds in fall. Black haw is one of the most drought-tolerant and shade-tolerant native viburnums — it thrives where other viburnums struggle — and brings exceptional wildlife value to naturalistic plantings and food forest understories.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost or start indoors 6 weeks early.
directions: 'Black Knight' bears almost-black, deep maroon pincushion blooms on long stems, at home in cottage and formal gardens alike. Direct sow after frost or start indoors about six weeks early, barely covering the seed. Grow in full sun to part shade in well-drained soil where it is fairly drought tolerant. It blooms over a very long season and is a magnet for bees and butterflies. Cut flowers freely for arrangements, and let some seed heads dry on the plant for added texture.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring or fall; needs darkness to germinate.
directions: 'Black Magic' is a deep, near-black bachelor's button, also called cornflower, prized for cut and dried flowers with edible petals for garnishing. Direct sow in early spring or fall, covering the seed since it needs darkness to germinate. Grow in full sun in average, well-drained soil where it tolerates drought. It naturalizes readily, self-sowing for a fresh crop the following season. Deadhead to prolong bloom and to keep the plants tidy and productive.

plant_note: Plant nursery-grown stock in spring in estuarine, tidal, or high-marsh substrate in full sun; Black Mangrove is the most cold-tolerant of the Florida mangroves and has been expanding its range north along both Gulf and Atlantic coasts. The characteristic pencil-like pneumatophores (air roots) project upward from the mud — do not damage or bury them. Never plant outside of the natural range.
directions: Black Mangrove is the most cold-tolerant and widespread of the three mangrove species native to Florida and the Gulf Coast, colonizing the mid- to upper intertidal zone above the Red Mangrove and tolerating periodic freezes that kill its more frost-sensitive relatives — a cold-hardiness that has allowed it to expand dramatically northward along Texas and Florida coasts as winters have warmed. The shallow, horizontally spreading root system produces thousands of pencil-shaped pneumatophores (air-breathing roots) that project vertically from the mud in dramatic, spiked mats, aerating the root system in oxygen-depleted estuarine sediments. Black Mangrove leaves excrete salt through special glands on their undersides, enabling survival in highly saline tidal mudflats; the flowers are an important nectar source for bees and produce the prized black mangrove honey marketed along the Gulf Coast. Plant in estuarine, tidal, or high-marsh substrate in full sun in Zones 9–11; Black Mangrove is protected in Florida and most coastal states and should only be established from nursery stock within its natural range.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; black mondo grass spreads slowly and is best spaced 6–8 inches apart for a groundcover effect — it takes 2–3 seasons to fill in. The near-black foliage color intensifies in partial sun; deep shade reduces color intensity.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade; black mondo grass is one of the most dramatic and unique foliage plants available for shaded landscapes, producing very narrow, arching, strappy leaves that are such a dark purple-black that they appear nearly jet black — an almost unparalleled foliage color in horticulture. Small, pale pink flowers on short stalks appear in summer, followed by jet-black berries that provide additional ornamental interest through winter. It spreads slowly by underground rhizomes, forming low, slow-expanding clumps that work beautifully as a groundcover, edging plant, or textural accent in shade gardens. The black foliage creates extraordinary contrast with silver-leaved plants (Japanese painted fern, brunnera) or with the bright lime-green of golden creeping Jenny. It provides the most intense color in partial shade with some morning sun. Divide congested clumps every 3–4 years in spring to maintain vigor and appearance.

plant_note: Sow after the last frost in warm soil.
directions: Sow Black Russian sunflower seeds an inch deep after the last frost in a spot with full sun and room to grow tall. This heirloom giant towers eight to twelve feet with a single large golden face and dark center, producing abundant oil-rich seeds for roasting or bird feed. Give young plants steady water and stake in exposed sites to support the heavy heads. Let heads dry on the stalk, then cut and cure them before harvesting the seeds.

plant_note: Surface sow in early spring or fall; needs light and cool soil.
directions: 'Black Swan' bears intricate, finely cut semi-double blossoms as dark as cabernet on graceful, curved stems. Scatter the tiny seed on the soil surface in early spring or fall, leaving it uncovered since light and cool temperatures aid germination. Grow in full sun in well-drained soil where it is drought tolerant. Sow it along a frequented path or in a large container where its 3-to-5-inch blooms can be admired up close. Direct sow only, as poppies resent root disturbance, and let it self-sow for future years.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost; resents transplanting.
directions: 'Black Velvet' bears glamorous, ruby-black edible flowers that add drama to salads, cheese plates, and garden beds alike. Direct sow the large seeds where they are to grow, since nasturtiums dislike root disturbance. Grow in full sun to part shade in lean soil, as overly rich ground yields foliage instead of flowers. Both the peppery flowers and leaves are edible. Its compact, bushy form suits containers, borders, and bed edges, blooming from summer to frost.

plant_note: Plant nursery transplants or root hardwood cuttings (12–18 inch stem sections planted directly in moist soil) in early spring. Black Willow establishes exceptionally rapidly and begins producing root anchorage within weeks; it is the preferred native species for immediate streambank and erosion control in moist to wet sites.
directions: Black Willow is the largest native willow in North America and the most important native tree for the immediate stabilization of eroding streambanks, native to the moist to wet, sandy and gravelly alluvial soils of rivers, streams, and floodplains throughout eastern North America from New Brunswick to Florida and west to Montana and New Mexico. Its aggressive, fast-establishing root system grips unstable bank soil within weeks of planting, and hardwood cuttings root readily when simply pushed into moist streambank soil in early spring — making it the fastest and cheapest native solution for stream restoration and bank protection. The pendulous, narrow, lance-shaped leaves create a graceful, weeping appearance over water; catkins appear in early spring, providing one of the season's earliest pollen sources for native bees emerging from winter dormancy. Black Willow is short-lived (40–60 years) and prone to branch breakage in ice and wind storms, but its rapid establishment and wildlife value (woodpecker habitat, caterpillar host plant) make it an invaluable native pioneer for wet, disturbed sites where quick establishment is the priority.

plant_note: Direct sow on or near last frost date; seeds need light to germinate so press onto soil surface.
directions: Press seeds onto the soil surface — they need light to germinate and should not be covered. Plants often bloom their first year from an early start, flowering freely into fall. Deadhead to prolong blooming or allow seeds to self-sow for a naturalized planting. Rudbeckia fulgida varieties are longer-lived perennials; divide every 3 years to refresh the planting.

plant_note: Start indoors 6-8 weeks early for earlier bloom.
directions: Start black-eyed Susan vine indoors six to eight weeks before the last frost for the longest season of bloom. The twining stems carry cheerful orange, yellow, or white flowers each with a dark central eye. Provide a trellis, fence, or container support in full sun to partial shade with rich, evenly moist soil. Feed lightly and keep well watered, and the vine will flower nonstop until frost.

plant_note: Start indoors; stratification aids germination.
directions: Blackberry lily is actually a member of the iris family, bearing orange, red-spotted flowers followed by clusters of glossy black seeds resembling blackberries. Start seed indoors, as a period of cold stratification improves germination. Grow in full sun to part shade in well-drained soil with its fanned, sword-like foliage adding structure to the border. The tall, clean stems make excellent cut flowers, and the seed heads are prized for dried arrangements. It self-sows and returns reliably as a short-lived perennial.

plant_note: Plant only young, nursery-grown container plants in fall in dry, sandy, rocky, or acidic, very well-drained soil in full sun; blackjack oak is extremely difficult to transplant from the wild due to its deep taproot and thrives in the poorest, most droughty soils available.
directions: Blackjack oak is the signature tree of dry, sandy, impoverished 'blackjack' soils across the southeastern United States — its distinctive club-shaped, three-lobed leaves with a broad, rounded top and narrow base are immediately recognizable, and the tree is virtually impossible to establish in rich or moist soils, thriving only in the driest, poorest, most acidic conditions that prevent most trees from growing. Often found alongside post oak in the 'Cross Timbers' forest of Oklahoma and Texas, blackjack oak commonly forms dense, almost impenetrable thickets on sandy, infertile ridges that are habitat for some of the most specialized dry-land wildlife communities in the South. Despite its demanding site requirements, blackjack oak is a highly valuable wildlife tree: its small acorns are consumed by wild turkey, whitetail deer, and at least 30 species of birds, and the crooked, gnarled dead snags are prime nesting habitat for cavity-nesting birds. In landscapes with genuinely poor, dry, acidic, sandy soils, it is one of the most reliable native trees for creating a self-sustaining, wildlife-rich oak woodland.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; do not cover seeds, as light aids germination.
directions: Press seeds onto the surface of moist growing medium and provide warmth (70–75°F) and bright light without covering; transplant after frost into sharply drained, even poor soil since gaillardia thrives on neglect. Deadhead spent blooms continuously to keep the bold red and yellow flowers coming from early summer through frost. Avoid clay soils and overwatering — wet conditions in winter are the primary cause of plant loss. Divide or replace plants every 2–3 years, as they tend to be short-lived perennials.

plant_note: Press seed onto the surface after frost; light aids germination.
directions: Press blanket flower seed onto firmed soil around the last frost, leaving it uncovered since light speeds germination. This heat-loving wildflower blazes with red, orange, and yellow daisy-like blooms all summer, thriving in sandy, lean soils where other plants sulk. It is exceptionally drought-tolerant and a magnet for bees and butterflies. Deadhead for continuous color, or leave a few heads to self-sow next season.

plant_note: Plant bare-root crowns in early spring as soon as the soil can be worked; dormant division in fall is also successful.
directions: Plant crowns 1–2 in deep in cool, humus-rich, consistently moist but well-drained soil in partial shade. Bleeding heart goes dormant by midsummer in warm climates — interplant with hostas, ferns, or other shade-lovers to fill the gap left when the foliage yellows and dies back. Divide clumps every 4–5 years in early spring just as new growth emerges; the fleshy roots are brittle, so handle with care. All parts of the plant are mildly toxic if ingested.

plant_note: Plant rooted cuttings or container plants in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; bleeding heart vine performs best in bright filtered shade and struggles in hot, direct afternoon sun in zone 9. It is particularly effective as a patio container plant that can be moved to protection in colder zones.
directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; bleeding heart vine is one of the most exquisitely beautiful flowering vines available for shaded tropical and subtropical gardens, producing pendant clusters of flowers that are truly bicolored — the white, inflated, papery calyx (the balloon-like outer part) encloses a brilliant red corolla with protruding red stamens, creating a striking red-and-white contrast that gives the plant its common name. After the red corollas drop, the white calyces persist for weeks and gradually turn pinkish-purple, extending the display. This semi-twining vine blooms most heavily in spring and again in fall and prefers the bright, filtered shade of a pergola or porch where the flowers can be enjoyed up close. In zone 9a it needs winter protection or overwintering indoors; it thrives outdoors year-round only in frost-free areas. Keep it slightly root-bound in containers for best bloom.

plant_note: Plant rhizomes just 1 inch deep in fall with the growing tip pointing upward; or sow fresh seed immediately after collection in June — seed viability is very brief and must be planted within days of harvest. Disturb rhizomes minimally.
directions: Plant rhizomes 1 inch deep in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade beneath deciduous trees; bloodroot is one of the earliest and most beautiful spring woodland wildflowers, producing brilliant white, poppy-like flowers (the double-flowered form 'Multiplex' looks like a white peony blossom) that emerge wrapped in the lobed, glaucous-blue leaf before it unfurls. Flowers last only 1–2 days in warm weather but are intensely striking. The common name refers to the bright orange-red sap from the rhizome, which was used by many Indigenous peoples as a dye, insect repellent, and medicine — note that this sap is caustic and toxic in quantity. Plants go dormant by midsummer; mass planting with hostas or ferns hides the bare spots.

plant_note: Plant offsets in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, rocky to sandy, dry soil in full sun; blue agave is cold hardy to approximately 23–28°F when established in sharply-drained soil and performs well in zone 9 with a degree of winter protection for young plants.
directions: Plant offsets in well-drained, dry, rocky to sandy soil in full sun — blue agave is the world-famous Mexican highland succulent that is the sole legal source of tequila, cultivated in vast plantations in the Jalisco region of Mexico where it requires 7–10 years to mature before the piña (heart) is harvested for distillation. As an ornamental landscape plant it is spectacularly bold: rigid, channeled, blue-gray leaves up to 5 feet long with very sharp terminal spines form enormous symmetrical rosettes that make striking architectural focal points. After 7–12 years of growth, a single 20-foot flower spike is produced; the plant dies afterward but produces numerous offsets. Outstanding for dramatic, statement-making specimens in dry, hot, zone 9–11 landscapes, tropical-style gardens, and as a large container plant. The 'Weber Azul' variety is the tequila industry standard and the most commonly available cultivar in nursery trade.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring or fall; needs darkness to germinate.
directions: 'Blue Boy' dazzles with true sky-blue flowers that are wonderful massed and practically care-free thanks to their drought tolerance. Direct sow in early spring or fall, covering the seed since bachelor's button needs darkness to germinate. Grow in full sun in average, well-drained soil. The flowers can be used fresh or dried, and the edible petals make a colorful garnish. Deadhead to extend bloom, and it will self-sow to return the following season.

common_name: Blue Cathedral Bells Cup and Saucer Vine
plant_note: Start indoors 6-8 weeks early; needs warmth to germinate.
directions: Also called monastery bells, this fast-growing vine bears 2-inch cup-shaped flowers that open pale green and mature to violet purple. Start seed indoors six to eight weeks before the last frost in warmth, standing the flat seeds on edge in the soil. Grow in full sun to part shade near arbors, fences, or trellises where it quickly forms a fragrant privacy screen. Pinch back the tips to encourage branching and more blooms. It flowers from summer until frost and is grown as an annual outside frost-free zones.

plant_note: Set out shrubs in spring after soil warms.
directions: 'Blue Chiffon' Rose of Sharon is a deciduous flowering shrub with large, double, lacy blue flowers borne from summer through fall. Plant in spring in full sun to light shade in average, well-drained soil, giving it room to reach its mature size. Water regularly the first year to establish, after which it becomes fairly low-maintenance and tolerant of heat. Prune in late winter or early spring to shape the shrub and encourage vigorous new flowering wood. The long bloom season and hardiness make it a reliable summer backbone for borders and hedges.

plant_note: Sow in spring or fall; seeds benefit from cold stratification.
directions: Sow blue columbine in fall or after cold-stratifying the seed, pressing it onto the surface since light helps it sprout. This hardy perennial wildflower nods with delicate blue-and-white spurred blooms that lure hummingbirds and bees in late spring. It appreciates partial shade and consistent moisture, making it a graceful choice for woodland edges and containers. Leave seed heads to self-sow, as plants are relatively short-lived but readily replace themselves.

plant_note: Direct sow on dry, sandy, or gravelly soil in full sun after the last frost; blue curls germinates readily in warm soil but requires excellent drainage and low soil fertility — it thrives in barren, open patches where it has little competition from grass.
directions: Blue curls is a native annual wildflower of pine barrens, dry sandy clearings, and open woodland paths across the eastern United States — tiny but dazzling when in bloom, bearing intricate blue to violet flowers with dramatically long, arching stamens (the 'curls') that give the plant its evocative name and distinguish it from all other wildflowers in the landscape. The strongly aromatic foliage has a pungent, camphor-like scent similar to basil and other members of the mint family; blue curls has historically been used as an insect repellent and minor herbal remedy. It is one of the most important native annual wildflowers for supporting specialist bees in dry, sandy, barren habitats — bombylius flies and small native bees are primary pollinators. Direct sow on disturbed sandy soil in full sun; it thrives in poor, dry conditions and competition-free open ground where few other plants grow well, self-seeding from late summer through fall to return the following year.

plant_note: Plant rooted cuttings after last frost in very well-drained, lean soil in full sun; excellent drainage is essential — blue daze rots in moist, heavy soil. In zone 9–11 it acts as a perennial.
directions: Plant rooted cuttings in very well-drained, lean to average soil in full sun after last frost; blue daze is one of the most beloved summer annuals in Gulf Coast gardens, producing a continuous cascade of brilliant sky-blue flowers from spring through frost over trailing stems of soft silver-green leaves. It behaves as a perennial groundcover in zone 9–10, spreading to form a low, drought-tolerant mat of near-constant bloom through the summer months when most plants struggle with heat. The blue flower color is exceptionally vivid under southern summer sun. Excellent in containers, hanging baskets, rock gardens, or as a front-of-border annual where summer heat and excellent drainage are provided. Propagate by rooted stem cuttings in fall to maintain plants through winter in zone 9+.

plant_note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring or fall; division every 2–3 years maintains the intense blue color.
directions: Plant in full sun and very well-drained, lean to average soil; blue fescue is a cool-season grass that goes semi-dormant in the heat of summer and performs best in cool, dry climates. The tight, spiky mounds of intensely silvery-blue foliage are striking as edging or mass plantings; tan flower spikes appear in early summer. Divide every 2–3 years in early spring, discarding the dead center and replanting the vigorous outer portions — this maintains the best blue coloration. Avoid heavy, wet clay soils and humid conditions which promote crown rot.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in moist to wet, slightly acidic soil at pond or stream margins; keep the rhizome at the soil surface, not buried. Blooms best in full sun but tolerates partial shade.
directions: Plant rhizome divisions at the soil surface in moist to wet, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; blue flag iris is the quintessential native wetland iris of the northeastern and central US and Canada, growing in marshes, bogs, wet meadows, and streambanks with bold violet-blue flowers veined with white and yellow in late spring. It is native across a vast range from Labrador to Virginia, making it more cold-hardy than the similar southern blue flag (I. virginica). The flowers provide nectar for specialist long-tongued native bees and bumblebees. Plant at the margin of a water garden, in a rain garden, or in any consistently moist location. All parts of the plant are toxic if ingested. The rhizomes spread slowly to form handsome colonies; divide every 4–5 years in fall to maintain vigor.

plant_note: Plant divisions or stem cuttings in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade; blue ginger requires warm temperatures and high humidity to thrive — it does not tolerate cold, dry, or windy conditions. In zone 9 it dies back to the ground after frost but reliably regrows from rhizomes in spring.
directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; blue ginger is one of the most treasured and sought-after shade plants for tropical and subtropical gardens, producing dense terminal clusters of intensely deep royal-blue flowers — the rich, saturated blue is among the most vivid blues in tropical horticulture — on upright, bamboo-like stems with spirally arranged, glossy, broad leaves. The flowers appear in late summer and fall, at exactly the time when blue is most welcome in the garden palette. Despite the name, it is not a true ginger (Zingiberaceae) but belongs to the spiderwort family. It grows most luxuriantly in warm, sheltered, brightly shaded spots — beneath a pergola, porch, or canopy tree — in zones 9–12. In zone 9a it dies back completely after frost, but established rhizomes regrow reliably each spring and the plant typically blooms in its first year of regrowth. A true collector's plant and one of the best shade perennials available for the Gulf Coast garden.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost once soil warms.
directions: Blue grama is a native prairie grass and the most heat- and drought-tolerant of the native bunchgrasses. Direct sow after the last frost once soil has warmed, in full sun and well-drained soil. Its distinctive seed heads project horizontally like eyelashes, turning golden brown and sometimes red as they curl and dry in fall. The feathery foliage forms a tidy clump ideal for xeriscapes, meadows, and naturalized plantings. Once established it needs little water and thrives with minimal care.

plant_note: Plant container specimens in spring in very well-drained, sandy to loamy, alkaline to slightly acidic, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun; blue hesper palm is cold-hardy to approximately 12–15°F when established and requires excellent drainage — it will not tolerate wet or waterlogged soils.
directions: Plant in very well-drained, dry to moderately moist, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun — blue hesper palm is arguably the most ornamentally striking palm in the world for dry climates, producing enormous, rigid, deeply divided, silvery powder-blue fan fronds up to 6 feet across on a stout, solitary trunk that grows very slowly to 30–40 feet. Native to Baja California and northwest Mexico, it is one of the most cold-tolerant fan palms for dry-climate gardens, hardy to approximately 15°F once established, and thriving in the hot, dry conditions of zone 9–11 Southwest and Gulf Coast. The spectacular 15–20 foot long creamy-yellow flower stalks that extend dramatically beyond the crown are among the most dramatic flowering events of any palm. Extremely drought tolerant once established — planted in well-drained soil, it requires virtually no supplemental irrigation in warm, dry regions. Growth is very slow (6–12 inches per year), so purchase the largest specimen the budget allows.

plant_note: Plant divisions in fall or spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade; blue hosta is notably more heat tolerant than most blue-leafed hosta cultivars and seeds relatively true — seedlings often produce good, deep-green to slightly glaucous plants.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to deep shade — blue hosta (Hosta ventricosa) is a large, vigorous species from eastern Asia notable for producing some of the deepest, most attractive purple-lavender flowers in the genus — tall, attractive scapes of deep violet bell-shaped flowers in midsummer that are significantly more ornamental than the pale flowers of most hostas. The large, rounded, dark green to slightly glaucous leaves form a lush, mounded clump 2–3 feet tall and 3–4 feet wide. It is also one of the more heat-tolerant hosta species, performing well in zone 8–9 in consistently moist, deeply shaded conditions where many hybrid cultivars decline. Unlike most hostas, it seeds true from open-pollinated seeds, making it easy to naturalize in appropriate shaded conditions. The cultivar 'Aureomarginata' (golden-edged blue hosta) adds variegated interest with a gold to cream margin on the dark leaves.

plant_note: Sow in place; needs darkness to germinate, cover seed.
directions: Sow blue lace flower directly where it is to grow, covering seed completely since it needs darkness to germinate. The powder-blue umbels resemble a colored Queen Anne's lace and are cherished by florists for airy arrangements. Provide full sun, well-drained soil, and support for the slender stems in exposed sites. Keep evenly moist during establishment and stagger sowings for a longer cutting season.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in moist to wet, slightly acidic, fertile, loamy soil in partial shade to full sun; blue Louisiana iris tolerates slightly drier conditions than other Louisiana irises and blooms later in the season, making it valuable for extending the iris bloom season in Gulf Coast gardens.
directions: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in moist to wet, slightly acidic, fertile soil in partial shade to full sun — the zigzag iris is one of the parent species of the spectacular Louisiana iris hybrid group, producing large, rich blue-purple flowers on distinctively zigzag stems that hold the blooms close to the foliage in late spring. It is native to the wet bottomlands, swampy thickets, and stream margins of the Lower Mississippi Valley and Gulf Coast, where it grows in moister, shadier conditions than most other Louisiana iris species. The flowers have the classic Louisiana iris form — broad, spreading falls with a yellow signal patch and narrow, upright standards — in deep blue-violet that is stunning in mass plantings along pond edges and in rain gardens. It is one of the more cold-hardy Louisiana iris species, tolerating zone 6 winter cold well. An essential component of any wetland or rain garden featuring native iris species.

plant_note: Direct sow on the soil surface 2–4 weeks before last frost; press seed lightly into soil as light aids germination. Division of established clumps is faster than seeding.
directions: Sow seed on the soil surface (light required for germination) in moist, average soil in partial shade to full sun; this native ageratum look-alike fills the critical late-season window with fluffy blue-lavender flower clusters that attract migrating monarchs and late-season bees from August through first frost. Plant in large drifts for the greatest visual impact and pollinator value. The plant spreads aggressively by rhizome in moist, fertile soil — divide every 2–3 years in spring to control spread and maintain vigor. Excellent companion for goldenrods and fall asters in a native pollinator garden.

plant_note: Start indoors; surface sow, needs light to germinate.
directions: 'Blue Planet' is a tall ageratum whose fuzzy, long-lasting blue flowers look like tiny explosions and shine in gardens, containers, and bouquets. Start seed indoors and surface sow, since the seed needs light to germinate. Grow in full sun to part shade in rich, well-drained soil kept evenly moist. Unlike many ageratums, this variety branches freely without pinching and flowers prolifically. It pairs beautifully with white flowers and blooms from summer to frost.

plant_note: Direct sow or transplant after last frost in lean, very well-drained soil in full sun; in zone 9–11 it naturalizes as a self-sowing perennial subshrub.
directions: Plant in lean, very well-drained, sandy to average soil in full sun after temperatures warm; blue porterweed is a star butterfly plant of the tropical Gulf Coast, producing long slender spikes with intense blue-purple flowers that open progressively up the spike from the base throughout summer and fall. The flowers are critically important for gulf fritillary, zebra longwing, and monarch butterflies; hummingbirds also visit the spikes regularly. In zone 9–10 it naturalizes as a weedy self-sowing subshrub in disturbed, sandy areas — appropriate for a wild, naturalistic butterfly garden but not a formal border. The common name 'porterweed' comes from the dark, beer-like color of the steeped leaves historically used as a beverage. Deadhead spent spikes to encourage additional blooming.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; do not fertilize — rich soils produce floppy plants. The milky sap may irritate skin.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, average to well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; blue star is a native perennial that earns its keep in three seasons — in late spring with starry pale blue flowers on arching stems, in summer with clean willow-like foliage that remains attractive and disease-free all season, and most dramatically in autumn with spectacular golden-yellow fall color that rivals the finest maples. It is one of the most structural and elegant native perennials available, naturally found in moist woodland edges and stream banks. It tolerates light to moderate shade well. The deer-resistant foliage contains a milky latex. Do not over-fertilize; lean conditions produce compact, self-supporting plants. Cut back lightly after bloom to maintain tidy habit.

plant_note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring or fall in moist to moderately dry, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil or in a container with orchid-type epiphytic potting mix; blue star fern often grows as an epiphyte on the rough bark of cabbage palms and oak trees in the wild. It tolerates both wet and dry conditions remarkably well for a fern.
directions: Plant in humus-rich, well-drained to moderately dry soil in partial to full shade; blue star fern (golden polypody) is one of the most striking native ferns of the Gulf Coast and Florida, producing large, deeply lobed, silvery blue-green fronds with a distinctive blue-gray cast unusual in ferns — the fronds can reach 3 feet long and create a bold, sculptural effect in the shade garden. It is one of the few ferns that grows naturally as an epiphyte on the rough, furrowed bark of cabbage palms (Sabal palmetto) and live oaks in coastal Florida and the Gulf states, where it wedges into bark crevices and grows without soil, sustained by rainfall and humidity. In garden cultivation it is more drought tolerant than most ferns, making it valuable for drier shaded spots. The orange-yellow round sori on the frond undersides are an attractive feature. An outstanding large, architectural fern for zone 8–12 shade gardens.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil; soak seed first.
directions: Butterfly pea is a vigorous tropical climber with velvety, vivid blue blooms prized as a natural dye and for herbal tea. Soak the seed overnight and direct sow after frost in warm soil, providing a trellis for the twining vines to climb. Grow in full sun to part shade in well-drained soil kept evenly moist through the warm season. It flowers best in heat and attracts pollinators as it winds up its supports. Outside frost-free zones grow it as an annual or in a container to overwinter indoors.

plant_note: Fall sow or cold-stratify seed before spring planting.
directions: Sow blue vervain in fall so winter cold breaks its dormancy, or cold-stratify the seed before a spring sowing. This native perennial sends up tall, candelabra spikes of blue-violet flowers that feed a wide range of pollinators through summer. It naturally favors moist meadows, rain gardens, and pond edges, so keep the soil damp. Long valued in herbal traditions, it combines ecological benefit with striking vertical form.

plant_note: Start indoors 6-8 weeks early.
directions: 'Blue Victory' offers a long season of deep violet-blue flower spikes on compact 20-inch plants that look stunning massed or in containers. Start seed indoors six to eight weeks before the last frost. Grow in full sun in well-drained soil where it performs well even in hot, humid climates and is fairly drought tolerant once established. It makes an excellent cut or dried flower and attracts bees and hummingbirds. Deadhead spent spikes to keep the plants blooming through the season.

plant_note: Plant divisions in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade to dappled light; blue walking iris is tender below 28°F but resprouts from established clumps in zone 9 after light freezes. In zone 8 treat as a large container plant and overwinter indoors.
directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to filtered light — blue walking iris is one of the most dramatically beautiful foliage and flowering plants for the shaded Gulf Coast garden, producing bold, fan-shaped clumps of upright, iris-like leaves 3–4 feet tall. The large, intricately patterned blue-purple flowers — each 3–4 inches across with white and golden markings — open from plantlets produced at the tips of flower stems; when these plantlets become heavy enough, the stem bends to the ground and the offset roots to start a new plant nearby, 'walking' the clump gradually across the garden. The flowers last only a single day but appear in succession over several weeks in late spring and early summer. An outstanding large-scale accent or specimen plant for the shaded side of buildings, under oaks, and in tropical-style garden compositions in zone 9–11. Grow in containers in cooler zones.

plant_note: Scarify seed with sandpaper and soak 24 hours before sowing; direct sow in fall for natural stratification and spring germination. Transplant potted plants in spring. Plants are slow to establish and should not be divided or moved once sited.
directions: Plant in well-drained, average to poor, even dry soil in full sun; blue wild indigo is a long-lived native prairie perennial that forms a substantial, rounded, blue-green shrub-like clump bearing stunning spikes of true-indigo-blue pea flowers in May, followed by interesting inflated, rattling seedpods that persist through winter. It is a larval host plant for several native butterfly and moth species. Choose its permanent location carefully — blue wild indigo has a deep taproot and strongly resists transplanting once established, but plants can persist for 50+ years with no maintenance. Initially slow to establish (3 years to a full-sized clump), but thereafter essentially indestructible and extremely drought-tolerant. A specimen plant that will outlast the gardener.

plant_note: Direct sow seed in fall or start indoors in late winter in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained to average soil in partial to full shade; blue wood aster is one of the most shade-tolerant of all native asters, blooming reliably in light to moderate shade where almost no other asters will flower.
directions: Sow seed in fall or start indoors in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade — blue wood aster is exceptional among native asters for its genuine shade tolerance, producing masses of small, light blue to lavender ray flowers with yellow centers in September and October in light to moderate shade where most asters cannot bloom. The heart-shaped basal leaves (giving the alternate common name 'heart-leaved aster') are distinctive and attractive through the growing season. It grows naturally in open woodland and woodland edge settings throughout the eastern US, forming loose colonies that self-sow in appropriate conditions. An outstanding source of late-season nectar for migrating monarchs, native bees, and other pollinators that are searching for food in fall. One of the most valuable native wildflowers for the shaded fall garden where late-season color and pollinator support are both needed.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring or fall; needs light to germinate.
directions: 'Blue and Breezy' perennial flax opens charming 1-inch sky-blue flowers each dawn on airy, arching stems, with fresh blooms replacing the day-old ones each morning. Surface sow where plants are to grow, pressing the seed in lightly since it needs light to germinate. Grow in full sun to part shade in well-drained soil where it shrugs off heat, drought, cold, and poor ground. Its constant color and fine texture suit meadows, borders, and naturalized areas. It self-sows readily to fill in over time.

plant_note: Direct sow on the soil surface in spring (seed needs light to germinate); can also self-sow readily once established to naturalize in a meadow or edge planting.
directions: Scatter seed on the soil surface — lightly pressed in but not covered — in moist, average soil in full sun to partial shade; despite belonging to the iris family, this diminutive native looks like a grass until the small blue-violet flowers with yellow centers open in late spring. It blooms most heavily in spring but continues sporadically through summer, and the tufted grass-like foliage remains attractive all season. Allow some seed heads to ripen for naturalization; the plant self-sows freely in favorable conditions to form charming colonies in meadow edges and lightly shaded areas. Divide clumps every 3–4 years in spring to maintain vigor.

plant_note: Direct sow seed in fall or start by division in spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich to average, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; blue-stemmed goldenrod is one of the very few goldenrods that blooms reliably in shade, making it irreplaceable for the shaded fall garden.
directions: Grow in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich to average, well-drained soil in partial to full shade — blue-stemmed goldenrod is one of the most valuable native goldenrods for woodland and shaded gardens, distinguished by its distinctive blue-green to purple-blue stems and its genuine shade tolerance in a genus that is almost entirely full-sun. The small golden flower clusters emerge from the leaf axils along the upper portion of the arching stems in September and October, creating a wreath-like effect (explaining the alternate common name 'wreath goldenrod'). It grows naturally in moist, rich woodland understories throughout the eastern US and is well-adapted to the dry shade under large trees where most goldenrods cannot survive. An outstanding plant for bringing late-season native pollinator value into the shaded garden when almost no other native plants are blooming. The seeds provide fall food for native sparrows and other seed-eating birds.

plant_note: Grown from a potted shrub or cutting; plant after frost danger passes.
directions: The Blueberry Pie butterfly bush produces dense clusters of deep blue-violet, sweetly fragrant blooms that draw butterflies, bees, and hummingbirds all summer. This vigorous flowering shrub is easy to grow and provides months of pollinator activity in the sunny border. Plant a potted shrub in full sun with well-drained soil and water moderately until established. Deadhead spent flower spikes to prolong bloom and prune hard in early spring to keep the plant compact and full of new flowering wood.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall; scarify seed to aid germination.
directions: The Texas bluebonnet is a cherished heirloom lupine that carpets roadsides and meadows with spikes of blue spring flowers. Scarify the hard seed coat and direct sow in fall for the strongest spring display, since the seedlings resent transplanting. Grow in full sun in lean, well-drained soil where it tolerates drought. Plant it in mass for the most captivating effect in naturalized wildflower areas. Allow pods to ripen and it will self-sow to return year after year.

plant_note: Direct sow fresh acorns in fall at 1–2 inch depth in infertile, sandy, extremely well-drained, acidic soil in full sun. Bluejack Oak demands xeric, nutrient-poor conditions and will not tolerate clay, excessive moisture, or shading from taller vegetation.
directions: Bluejack Oak is a distinctive native tree of sandhills, xeric upland forest, and fire-maintained scrub across the southeastern coastal plain from Virginia to Texas, immediately recognizable among oaks by its blue-gray, paddle-shaped leaves with pale, hairy undersides that give the small tree a frosted, silvery appearance unlike any other eastern oak. Adapted to the driest, most nutrient-poor sandy soils of the coastal plain, Bluejack Oak is extremely drought-tolerant and fire-resilient, regenerating rapidly from its extensive root system after low-intensity fires that historically maintained the sandhills where it is one of the dominant trees. The small acorns ripen in fall and are a critical food source for white-tailed deer, wild turkeys, small mammals, and Florida Scrub-Jays in dry upland communities where larger mast-producing oaks may be absent. Direct sow fresh acorns in fall at 1–2 inch depth in infertile, sandy, extremely well-drained, acidic soil in full sun — Bluejack Oak will not tolerate clay, excess moisture, or shade.

plant_note: Surface sow (light required for germination) in moist, slightly acidic soil; the plant self-sows to naturalize in moss gardens, lawn alternatives, and moist, shaded rock gardens.
directions: Scatter seed on moist, acidic soil in partial shade in early spring; this diminutive native wildflower carpets lawns, mossy banks, and lightly shaded meadows with pale blue four-petaled flowers in spring, creating an effect like scattered stars across a green sky. It naturally grows in thin, slightly moist, acidic soil of lawns and forest edges and is a wonderful component of a native lawn alternative. Allow it to go to seed after flowering to maintain the colony. The plant tolerates light foot traffic and can naturalize into an existing lawn if mowing is delayed until it sets seed in late spring. Does not perform well in heavy clay or alkaline soil.

plant_note: Direct sow seeds in fall in moist to wet, acidic, peaty, poorly drained soil in full sun to partial shade. Bog Goldenrod is specifically adapted to the saturated, acidic, nutrient-poor conditions of sphagnum bogs, fens, and wet meadows — it will not thrive in ordinary garden soil without significant moisture.
directions: Bog Goldenrod is a native perennial of the cool, acidic sphagnum bogs, fens, wet meadows, and peaty swamp edges of the northeastern United States and Canada, occupying a specialized ecological niche shared with carnivorous plants, bog orchids, and other acid-loving bog species that most other goldenrods cannot tolerate. The slender, wand-like spikes of small yellow flowers are produced in August and September in the open, sunny conditions of floating sphagnum mats and sedge fens, attracting late-season native bees and butterflies that visit these specialized wetland habitats. Bog Goldenrod provides an important native plant option for water gardeners, bog garden enthusiasts, and restorers working with cold northern wetland habitats. Direct sow seeds in fall in moist to wet, acidic, peaty, permanently saturated or nearly saturated soil in full sun to partial shade; established plants spread slowly by rhizomes in appropriate bog or fen conditions.

plant_note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in permanently wet, strongly acidic (pH 3.5–5.0), nutrient-poor, sphagnum peat bog or fen conditions in full sun to partial sun. Bog Rosemary requires exceptionally cool, wet, acidic growing conditions and is only suitable for northern bog gardens or cool, high-altitude peat-moss landscapes — it will not survive in warm, dry, or alkaline conditions.
directions: Bog Rosemary is a native circumpolar evergreen shrub of the coldest, wettest, most acidic sphagnum bogs and arctic tundra of the Northern Hemisphere, prized among bog garden enthusiasts for its attractive, narrow, blue-green, rosemary-like evergreen leaves with rolled margins and beautiful clusters of small, pink, globe-shaped flowers in spring. Like other members of the blueberry family adapted to the specialized bog environment, Bog Rosemary requires extreme growing conditions — permanently saturated, strongly acidic (pH 3.5–5.0), nutrient-poor peat — and is entirely unsuitable for conventional garden soil or climates south of Zone 6. All parts of the plant contain grayanotoxins and are toxic to livestock; the specific epithet polifolia ('gray-leaved') refers to the glaucous appearance of the foliage. Plant rooted cuttings in permanently wet sphagnum peat in full sun; Bog Rosemary is an outstanding native specimen for cold-climate bog gardens, peat gardens, and tundra-habitat restorations where its specialized conditions can be maintained.

plant_note: Root stem cuttings or plant container transplants in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; bog sage spreads aggressively by stolons in ideal moist conditions and requires regular division or containment to prevent it from overwhelming smaller garden neighbors.
directions: Root cuttings and plant in moist to consistently wet, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade — bog sage from South America produces one of the most pure, clear, vivid sky-blue flower colors available in any garden plant: small, two-lipped, brilliant cerulean-blue flowers in dense terminal spikes from July through hard frost, on tall, gracefully arching stems of narrow, bright green leaves with a pleasant herbal fragrance. It grows naturally in boggy, wet meadows and stream margins and requires consistently moist soil — ideal for rain gardens, wet low areas, and the moist margins of ponds. The plant spreads vigorously by underground stolons in suitable conditions, eventually forming large patches: excellent for covering wet, difficult areas but needs management in smaller beds. In zone 7–8 it may die back in winter but resprouts reliably from established stolons; in zone 9–10 it grows as a large, nearly evergreen perennial. One of the finest late-season blue flowers for the moist garden.

plant_note: Start indoors 4-6 weeks early or direct sow after frost.
directions: 'Bonita Top Blue' China aster bears richly saturated purple-blue blooms on sturdy, dark stems all summer long. Start seed indoors four to six weeks before the last frost, or direct sow once frost has passed. Grow in full sun in rich, well-drained soil, and rotate its planting spot yearly to reduce disease. The dark stems add contrast in bouquets and hold the blooms upright in the garden. With a long bloom period and excellent vase life, it is a top choice for cutting.

plant_note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; Boston fern requires consistently high humidity — the fronds turn yellow and shed leaflets rapidly in dry indoor air or low-humidity outdoor conditions. In zone 9–11 it can be grown outdoors year-round in sheltered, shaded spots.
directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; Boston fern is the world's most widely grown ornamental fern and perhaps the most popular hanging basket plant in temperate and tropical horticulture, with long, graceful, arching fronds of soft, bright green pinnae that cascade elegantly over pot edges, porch railings, and tree branches. In zone 9–12 it thrives outdoors year-round in sheltered, shaded positions on porches, under pergolas, and beneath the canopy of large trees, where the naturally high humidity and warm temperatures suit it perfectly. The key cultural requirement is consistent moisture and humidity — fronds desiccate and shed leaflets rapidly in dry, hot, or low-humidity conditions. In outdoor zone 9 culture, light frost nips the fronds but established plants typically regrow from the crown. Divide congested clumps and remove dead fronds from the crown in spring. The related 'Kimberly Queen' fern (N. obliterata) is generally more heat and drought tolerant and better suited to humid Southern gardens.

plant_note: Direct sow fresh seed in fall in moist to moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade; bottlebrush grass self-sows reliably in appropriate conditions and naturalizes to form a loose colony in moist woodland edge settings.
directions: Direct sow seed in fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial shade — bottlebrush grass is one of the few native ornamental grasses that genuinely thrives in partial to light shade, making it exceptional for the shaded garden or woodland edge where most grasses cannot grow. The distinctive, upright, cylindrical seed heads — radiating individual spikelets outward from the stem in a perfect bottle-brush pattern — are among the most ornamental of any native grass, remaining attractive from summer through winter as they dry to tan. It grows naturally in moist, rich woodland edges, ravine banks, and moist forest floors throughout the eastern US. Self-sows reliably in appropriate conditions to naturalize. Outstanding for adding native grass texture to shaded areas of the garden or in combination with native wildflowers and ferns along a woodland edge.

plant_note: Plant container-grown specimens in spring in very well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; bougainvillea strongly dislikes wet feet and disturbed roots — plant with care, minimizing root disturbance, and do not overwater. Deliberately stressing the plant by slightly withholding water after establishment triggers the most prolific bloom.
directions: Plant in very well-drained soil in full sun; bougainvillea is the quintessential tropical vine of warm-climate gardens, producing massive displays of brilliantly colored papery bracts (the true flowers are small and white at the bract center) in shades of magenta, red, orange, pink, white, and bicolor from spring through fall — especially spectacular in late winter and spring in zone 9. The plant is a vigorous, thorny scrambler that must be trained on a strong trellis, arbor, or fence; it is not self-clinging and must be tied. Light stress (slight drought, slightly root-bound) dramatically increases bloom production. In zone 9, it may suffer frost damage in cold winters but typically regrows from the base; in zone 10–11 it blooms nearly year-round. Prune after each major bloom cycle to encourage re-blooming. Extremely heat, drought, and salt tolerant once established — one of the most spectacular flowering vines available for Gulf Coast landscapes.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring in cool soil; soak seed first.
directions: Bouquet Blend is a colorful mix of Spencer-type sweet peas with extra-large flowers, long sturdy stems, and many blooms per stem for cutting. Soak the seed overnight and direct sow in early spring in cool soil, since sweet peas relish chilly weather. Provide a trellis or netting for the vines to climb and grow in full sun to part shade in rich, well-drained soil. Keep evenly watered and cut flowers frequently to prolong the lightly scented bloom. Frequent cutting and cool conditions extend the season into early summer.

plant_note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; Bowman's root is slow to establish but extremely long-lived once settled — give it 2–3 years to reach full flowering potential.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade — Bowman's root is a graceful, airy native perennial of eastern woodland understories, producing a cloud of delicate five-petaled white to pale pink flowers on reddish-tinged branching stems in late spring to early summer — one of the most elegant of all native shade-garden plants. The leaves are trifoliate (three leaflets), giving it the specific epithet, and color attractively to orange-red in fall. It grows naturally in moist, rich, rocky woodland slopes and ravine banks from Ontario south to Georgia. Unlike most shade perennials it has a somewhat shrubby, upright structure rather than a mounding habit, adding height and structure to the shaded border. Bowman's root serves as a host plant for several native moth species and provides nectar for hummingbirds. Once established it is long-lived and low-maintenance. An outstanding native for the woodland garden in zones 4–9.

plant_note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in any well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Boxelder self-seeds prolifically and should be planted thoughtfully as a intentional native tree rather than allowed to spread unmanaged in formal landscapes.
directions: Boxelder is a native maple unlike any other — with pinnately compound leaves (3–7 leaflets) that bear no resemblance to the classic maple leaf — native throughout almost the entirety of North America east of the Rockies and naturalized well beyond, growing in river floodplains, bottomlands, stream corridors, and disturbed urban habitats with the same casual colonizing energy as cottonwood. Often dismissed as a weed tree in horticultural circles, Boxelder provides important ecological services that more ornamental trees cannot match: it is the only host plant for the Boxelder bug (Boisea trivittata), a harmless true bug that is a significant food source for many native birds; female trees produce abundant samaras (paired winged seeds) that are an important winter food for evening grosbeaks, finches, and other seed-eating birds; and the fast-growing tree provides rapid canopy cover for riparian restoration. Tolerant of flooding, drought, poor soils, and urban pollution, Boxelder is effectively impossible to kill once established and self-seeds prolifically — use it intentionally in naturalistic, riparian, or wildlife plantings where its resilience is an asset.

plant_note: Start indoors 6-8 weeks early; darkness aids germination.
directions: Brazilian vervain, also called tall or purpletop verbena, sends up airy wands of rose-violet to lavender blooms atop tall, slender stems. Start seed indoors six to eight weeks early, covering it since darkness aids germination. Grow in full sun in well-drained soil where it tolerates drought once established. Its see-through height creates a light, floating layer of color in the border and is a magnet for butterflies, bees, and hummingbirds. It reseeds readily as a welcome volunteer and is grown as an annual where winters are cold.

plant_note: Direct sow in very early spring or fall; seeds germinate in cool soil and seedlings tolerate frost.
directions: Surface sow by scattering seed on bare ground and pressing lightly without covering, as light aids germination. Breadseed poppies resent root disturbance and must be sown in place. Thin to 6–12 in when seedlings are 2 in tall. The ornamental seedpods are striking after petals drop and are valuable dried; harvest when the cap at the top turns from green to tan.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil.
directions: 'Bright Lights' is a vibrant sulphur cosmos blend with bold 2.5-inch flowers in warm oranges, golds, and reds from midsummer to first frost. Direct sow after the last frost once soil is warm, barely covering the seed. Grow in full sun in average to lean soil, since rich ground favors foliage over flowers, and it is drought tolerant once established. Butterflies and hummingbirds flock to the nectar-rich blooms, making it superb for wildflower and naturalized areas. Deadhead or cut regularly to keep flowers coming.

plant_note: Sow after the last frost; thrives in heat and poor soil.
directions: Direct sow Bright Lights cosmos after the last frost, barely covering the seed in a sunny, well-drained spot. This heat- and drought-tolerant heirloom produces semi-double blooms in glowing shades of gold, orange, and scarlet that keep coming until frost. Lean soil actually encourages more flowers and less floppy growth, so hold back on fertilizer. Deadhead or cut for bouquets to fuel a nonstop pollinator show.

plant_note: Surface sow in early spring or fall; needs light and cool soil.
directions: 'Brilliant' oriental poppy is a majestic perennial with intense red 4-inch blooms that make a spectacular focal point in spring and early summer. Surface sow the fine seed in early spring or fall, leaving it uncovered since it needs light and cool soil to germinate. Grow in full sun in well-drained soil, and direct sow where it is to grow because it resents having its taproot disturbed. The foliage dies back after flowering, so pair it with later perennials to fill the gap. Pair its red blooms with yellow, gold, purple, or blue for striking contrast.

plant_note: Plant balled-and-burlapped or container stock in spring or early fall while soil is workable.
directions: Set this narrow, columnar juniper in full sun and well-drained soil; it tolerates poor, dry, and rocky ground once established. Space plants at least 5 feet apart when used as a screen so the slender pyramidal form fills in without crowding. Water regularly the first year to establish deep roots, then reduce watering as it becomes quite drought-tolerant. Its dense evergreen foliage holds green year-round, making it a reliable windbreak or accent needing almost no pruning.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost; soak seed to speed germination.
directions: 'Broken Colors' four o'clock delights with mottled and bicolored tubular flowers in shifting shades, no two blooms alike, carrying a lilac-and-jasmine fragrance. Soak the large seed to speed germination and direct sow after the last frost in full sun to part shade. Grow in average, well-drained soil with moderate water. The flowers open in the late afternoon and evening, perfuming the garden, and attract hawk moths and hummingbirds. In cold zones the plants form tubers that can be dug and stored, or grown fresh from seed each year.

plant_note: Start indoors; surface sow, needs light to germinate.
directions: 'Bronze Queen' brings warm, smoky bronze-toned trumpet flowers and a captivating evening fragrance to borders and cottage gardens. Start seed indoors and surface sow, pressing it in lightly since the fine seed needs light to germinate. Grow in full sun to part shade in rich, well-drained soil kept evenly moist. The elegant blooms perfume the garden in the evening and attract pollinators and hummingbirds. Deadhead spent flowers to keep the plants blooming through the warm season.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall in poor, dry, well-drained, acidic soil in full sun; broomsedge thrives in neglected, nutrient-poor conditions that most plants cannot tolerate — it is a pioneer species that colonizes disturbed soils and old fields and should not be planted in fertile garden beds where it will be outcompeted.
directions: Broomsedge bluestem is one of the most common and ecologically important native grasses of the American South — the first grass to colonize old fields, roadsides, and disturbed land, where its upright copper-bronze clumps create the characteristic tawny winter landscape of fallow farmland across the Gulf Coastal Plain and Piedmont. While often dismissed as a 'weed grass' in agricultural contexts because it indicates overgrazed or impoverished soil, broomsedge provides critical winter habitat: the dry, papery clumps shelter overwintering quail coveys, song sparrows, and small mammals, and its seeds are consumed by field sparrows, grasshopper sparrows, and many other birds through fall and winter. In ecological restoration and native meadow projects, broomsedge functions as a pioneer species that stabilizes disturbed soils and builds organic matter as a precursor to diverse plant communities; it also provides excellent visual interest in naturalized areas where its orange-copper fall and winter color creates a warm, burnished landscape texture. Combine with native wildflowers like goldenrods, asters, and ironweeds in dry, sunny meadow restorations.

plant_note: Direct sow around last frost date or start indoors 6–8 weeks earlier for bloom the first season.
directions: Press seeds onto moist soil surface or cover very lightly; they need light to germinate and germinate readily in 7–14 days. Unlike Rudbeckia hirta, R. triloba is a true biennial-to-short-lived-perennial that produces abundant small golden-yellow flowers with a dark center from midsummer through hard frost. Plant in full sun to partial shade in average garden soil; plants self-sow prolifically and naturalize easily in meadow and prairie plantings. Deadhead some stems to extend the season and let others set seed to perpetuate the colony.

plant_note: Plant young, nursery-grown trees in fall in well-drained, limestone-based, alkaline to neutral, rocky or sandy soil in full sun; Buckley's oak is a calcicole (limestone lover) native to the Edwards Plateau and Cross Timbers — avoid clay, poorly drained, or highly acidic soils.
directions: Buckley's oak (also called Texas red oak) is a native tree of the limestone hills and rocky canyons of the Texas Hill Country, Edwards Plateau, and Cross Timbers region — the most important red-oak-type tree for autumn color in central Texas. In October and November, its deeply lobed leaves turn brilliant shades of orange-red, red, and scarlet — spectacular fall color unusual for the semi-arid Texas Hill Country where most oaks turn only dull brown or rust. Because it grows naturally on the shallow, rocky limestone soils and caliche of the Texas Hill Country and Edwards Plateau, it is better adapted to alkaline soils than most other red oaks in its group; it is one of the few native oaks that will tolerate the alkaline, calcium-rich soils common across central Texas that cause iron chlorosis in acid-adapted species. The medium-sized acorns are consumed by white-tailed deer, wild turkey, and scrub-jays, and the tree provides important larval host resources for hundreds of native Lepidoptera. Excellent choice for limestone-soil native landscapes in central Texas and the Oklahoma Cross Timbers.

plant_note: Plant in wet to moist, acidic, sandy or peaty soil in full sun to partial shade; buckwheat tree is a wetland obligate native to pond margins, bogs, and bottomland swamps of the Gulf Coast Coastal Plain — keep roots consistently wet.
directions: Buckwheat tree is an evergreen native shrub or small tree of the Gulf Coastal Plain, forming dense thickets in bogs, bay swamps, and along black-water stream margins from South Carolina to eastern Texas — a dominant species in some Gulf Coast wetland plant communities known as 'titi bays.' In early spring, dense racemes of fragrant white to pinkish flowers smother the branches before the leaves fully emerge, providing critical early-season nectar for native bees, including honeybees, which produce a distinctively flavored 'titi honey' prized by beekeepers. The distinctive buckwheat-shaped winged fruits ripen in summer and persist into fall, giving the tree its common name. Grow at the margins of rain gardens, constructed wetlands, or low-lying acidic areas where the soil stays moist to wet — it forms a naturalistic thicket that provides excellent wildlife cover and spring pollinator support.

plant_note: Sow after last frost once soil consistently warms to 65°F; use cold-treated (stratified) seed for better germination. Plugs and sod are faster than seed for establishing.
directions: Sow cold-treated seed in full sun after soil warms to 65°F in lean, clay to loam, alkaline to neutral soil; buffalograss is the most drought-tolerant of the true lawn grasses, the historical turf of the Great Plains that once covered millions of acres, and it requires one-tenth the water of Kentucky bluegrass. The soft, curly, blue-green blades stay short (3–5 inches) without mowing and go dormant tan in winter. It establishes slowly from seed (use plugs or sod for faster coverage) and will not tolerate acidic soil, heavy shade, or heavy foot traffic. It is dioecious — plant a mix of male and female seed or use non-seeding cultivars for a tidier appearance.

plant_note: Plant divisions or rooted runners in spring or fall; plants spread by stolons and will fill in quickly — space 6–12 inches apart for coverage within one growing season.
directions: Plant in moist, well-drained soil in partial to full shade, though ajuga tolerates more sun in cooler climates; it spreads by surface stolons to form a dense, weed-suppressing carpet topped by spikes of blue-violet flowers in spring. The low, rosette foliage comes in a range of colors from deep burgundy-bronze ('Caitlin's Giant', 'Burgundy Glow') to chocolate-purple, making it one of the most ornamentally versatile shade groundcovers. In hot, humid climates, crown rot can strike dense stands — improve air circulation by thinning plants if rot appears. Mow lightly after flowering to remove old spikes and encourage a tidier rosette. Spreads aggressively; install edging to keep it out of lawns.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost or start indoors 6 weeks early.
directions: Bunny tails forms an upright fountain of charming, fluffy flower heads as soft as a real bunny's tail. Direct sow after frost or start indoors about six weeks early, in full sun to part shade. Grow in average, well-drained soil where it becomes very drought tolerant once established. It is easy to grow and excellent for the cutting garden, adding whimsy and texture wherever it is planted. The plumes dry beautifully for lasting arrangements and can be harvested before they fully mature.

plant_note: Plant young trees in fall in deep, moist to dry, well-drained to clay soil in full sun; bur oak is extraordinarily adaptable to a wide range of soils including alkaline clay but develops best in deep, moist, well-drained loam — plant small as the deep taproot resists transplanting.
