directions: Bur oak is the most majestic and long-lived of all North American oaks — individual trees commonly live 400–600 years and may exceed 1,000 years on favorable sites, developing massive, deeply furrowed, corky trunks and broad, spreading crowns of great character and presence. The acorns are the largest produced by any North American oak, each nearly half-enclosed in a distinctive fringed, mossy cup that gives the species its alternate name 'mossycup oak' — these large, low-tannin acorns were the most important food item for many plains tribes, who harvested and processed them into flour. Bur oak's extraordinary drought resistance — the result of a massive taproot that may penetrate 14 feet in the first year — made it the tree that historically crossed the prairie/forest boundary into the Great Plains, creating the legendary prairie grove ecosystems that supported bison herds. Like all oaks, it supports hundreds of native Lepidoptera species as host plants and its acorn crops feed deer, turkeys, bears, woodpeckers, and dozens of other wildlife species. A tree to plant for future generations.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil; surface sow, needs light.
directions: Burgundy amaranth makes a dramatic backdrop in the flower garden with towering deep-red plumes and a bold presence in arrangements. Surface sow after the last frost in warm soil, pressing the fine seed in lightly since it needs light to germinate. Grow in full sun in average, well-drained soil where it tolerates heat and drought. About 105 days after sowing, beige, protein-rich grain forms in the plumes and can be cooked like rice. The tender young leaves are also edible cooked like spinach, and the plumes dry well for crafts.

plant_note: Plant container-grown divisions or transplants in fall in dry to average, well-drained, acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; bush honeysuckle is extremely adaptable to dry, rocky, or poor soils and is one of the most shade and drought tolerant of all native flowering shrubs.
directions: Bush honeysuckle (Diervilla) is an easy-to-grow native flowering shrub of eastern North American rocky woods and dry slopes, producing small but cheerful, tubular yellow flowers in summer that turn orange-red as they age, providing an attractive bicolored display on the same plant; the compact habit and beautiful foliage that turns brilliant orange-red in fall add to its landscape value through three seasons. Despite sharing the 'honeysuckle' common name with the invasive Japanese honeysuckle (Lonicera japonica), Diervilla is a completely different and well-behaved native genus that poses no invasive risk and is in fact declining in parts of its range due to habitat loss. It spreads slowly by underground stolons to form colonies — useful for erosion control on shaded, dry slopes where few other shrubs perform reliably — and tolerates dry shade better than almost any other flowering native shrub, including spots under shallow-rooted maples and in the dry, poor shade near building foundations. Hummingbirds and native bumblebees visit the flowers, and the autumn foliage provides late-season color in woodland garden situations.

plant_note: Plant in spring or early fall in a site with room for a slow-growing specimen conifer.
directions: This slow-growing spruce features weeping, lace-like branches clothed in soft blue-green needles for an elegant textured specimen. Plant in full sun and cool, moist, well-drained soil; it appreciates protection from harsh, drying winds. Keep the root zone mulched and evenly moist, especially while young, as Engelmann spruce dislikes prolonged drought. Little pruning is needed beyond removing damaged branches, letting the graceful cascading habit develop naturally.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall or spring in moist to wet, well-drained to poorly drained soil in full sun; bushy bluestem thrives in the wet, sunny conditions along pond margins, drainage ditches, and seasonal wet depressions of the Gulf Coastal Plain.
directions: Bushy bluestem is one of the most attractive native grasses for moist to wet, sunny sites in the Gulf Coastal Plain — in fall, the tops of the upright stems explode into distinctive fluffy, silvery white seed heads that give the grass a spectacular cotton-like appearance unlike any other native grass, making it highly ornamental for fall and winter interest in the garden and landscape. Found naturally in wet flatwoods, swales, pond margins, and roadside ditches throughout the Gulf South and southeastern coastal plain, it grows in exactly the sunny, wet conditions that are often difficult to plant — making it a superb choice for rain gardens, detention ponds, seasonal depressions, and moist bioswales in zone 6–10 landscapes. Like other bluestems, its seed heads provide food for grassland birds through winter, and the dense clumps offer nesting and escape cover for small mammals and ground-nesting birds. Its tolerance of periodic flooding and standing water makes it one of the most reliable native grasses for wet, sunny sites where little else will establish.

plant_note: Plant rhizomes or divisions in spring or early fall with the crown just below the soil surface.
directions: This Siberian iris opens bicolor blooms of soft butter-yellow falls and crisp white standards atop slender, grassy foliage. Plant in full sun to light shade in moist, humus-rich soil; it tolerates wetter ground better than bearded iris. Keep evenly watered during the spring bloom period and divide clumps every few years to maintain vigor. It is low-maintenance and reliably hardy, spreading into graceful clumps that make excellent border and waterside plantings.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring or early fall in consistently moist, acidic soil.
directions: Japanese iris produces large, flat, ruffled blooms whose delicate petals resemble butterflies taking flight in late spring and summer. Plant in full sun to part shade in rich, acidic soil that stays consistently moist; it thrives at pond edges and in rain gardens. Never let the soil dry out during the growing season, and avoid lime. Divide congested clumps in early fall and remove spent flower stalks to keep plantings tidy.

plant_note: Chilling the seed briefly before sowing improves this dwarf delphinium's germination.
directions: Start these dwarf delphiniums indoors several weeks before the last frost, keeping the soil cool since they germinate best below 65F. This heat-tolerant blend blooms the first year in shades of marine blue, light blue, soft rose, and white, making it well suited to small spaces and the front of cottage borders. Provide rich, well-drained soil and consistent moisture, and give plants afternoon shade in hot climates. Deadhead spent spikes to encourage a second flush of bloom.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions 2–4 inches deep in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; butterfly ginger is invasive in Florida, Hawaii, and parts of the Gulf Coast — consider this before planting and do not plant near natural areas. Container planting prevents spread.
directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich soil in partial shade; butterfly ginger is among the most intensely fragrant of all flowering plants — the pure white, butterfly-shaped flowers in summer and fall fill the garden with a heady sweet fragrance that carries 20–30 feet in still evening air. It is the national flower of Cuba and is widely grown throughout the tropics for its extraordinary perfume, used to scent garlands and leis. The tall, handsome reed-like stems 4–6 feet tall with broad, lance-shaped leaves make it an imposing accent or screening plant in shaded areas. In zone 8–9 the foliage dies back in winter but the roots resprout reliably from established rhizomes. Note that this species is listed as invasive in Florida, Hawaii, and the Pacific islands — plant responsibly, do not plant near waterways or natural areas, and contain the rhizomes if needed.

plant_note: Fall sow or cold-stratify 30 days before spring planting.
directions: Sow butterfly milkweed in fall or cold-stratify the seed for about a month before a spring sowing to break dormancy. This native perennial forms compact, bushy clumps crowned with vivid orange flower clusters that are a critical nectar and host resource for Monarchs. Its deep taproot makes it drought-tolerant but slow to establish and best left undisturbed once planted. Give it full sun and sharp drainage, and avoid rich, wet soils.

plant_note: Cold-stratify seeds for 4–6 weeks in the refrigerator, then start indoors; or direct sow in fall for natural stratification and spring emergence.
directions: Cold-stratify seeds by mixing with moist sand in a sealed bag and refrigerating for 30 days before sowing at soil surface — seeds need light and should not be covered. Butterfly weed has a deep taproot that makes it drought tolerant once established but very sensitive to transplanting; direct sowing or using deep individual cells causes the least disturbance. First-year plants rarely bloom, investing energy into root development; blooms appear reliably from the second year onward in vivid orange clusters that are among the most attractive nectar sources for monarchs and other butterflies. Leave seedheads through winter to self-sow and feed birds.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost danger passes; nasturtiums resent transplanting.
directions: Sow the large seeds directly where plants are to grow once the soil has warmed, planting about half an inch deep. These compact plants show warm butterscotch flowers that are edible and add a peppery bite to salads and garnishes. Avoid overly rich soil, which pushes foliage at the expense of blooms, and let plants dry slightly between waterings. Their tidy habit makes them ideal for containers and the front of a border.

plant_note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, humus-rich, moderately moist soil in partial to deep shade; button fern is unusually drought tolerant for a fern and prefers somewhat drier, rockier conditions than most shade ferns — it will not tolerate waterlogged soil.
directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, humus-rich, moderately moist soil in partial to deep shade — button fern from New Zealand is one of the most charming and distinctive small ferns in cultivation, producing graceful, arching, dark-stemmed fronds lined with small, round, button-like leaflets (pinnae) that give it a uniquely textured, decorative appearance quite unlike typical ferns. It is substantially more drought tolerant than most ferns, preferring rocky or well-drained, humus-rich soils rather than the constantly wet conditions needed by many shade ferns. This makes it an excellent choice for shaded container gardens, terrariums, hanging baskets, and rocky shade garden pockets where soil dries between waterings. It grows well as a houseplant in moderate to bright indirect light. In the Gulf Coast and South, it is hardy to around 25°F and grows outdoors year-round in zone 8–11 in partial to deep shade under trees and on north-facing banks.

plant_note: Plant container-grown transplants in spring in well-drained to brackish, sandy or rocky coastal soil in full sun. Buttonwood is highly salt-tolerant and drought-tolerant once established, making it an excellent choice for coastal landscaping in Zone 9b–11. It is not technically a mangrove but grows in the upland fringe of mangrove forests.
directions: Buttonwood is a native coastal tree or large shrub of the upper tidal fringe and coastal upland that transitions from saltwater to terrestrial habitats, growing at the landward edge of mangrove forests along both coasts of Florida, the Florida Keys, and throughout the Caribbean. Though often grouped with mangroves in coastal conservation contexts, Buttonwood is not a true mangrove but a member of the Combretaceae family that grows in the mangrove associates zone and shares the extreme salt tolerance that characterizes its habitat. The small, button-like seed heads that give it its common name are distinctive; the dense, slow-growing wood is exceptionally hard and was historically used for fuel, charcoal production, and as a building material. Highly salt-tolerant, drought-tolerant, and wind-resistant once established, Buttonwood is an excellent native landscaping choice for coastal South Florida homeowners seeking an attractive, low-maintenance native tree or large hedge plant for Zone 9b–11.

plant_note: Plant container-grown or field-dug trees in spring in any well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to alkaline, sandy to loamy soil in full sun to partial shade; cabbage palm transplants remarkably well even as a large tree and quickly re-establishes its root system. Remove all fronds at transplanting to reduce transpiration stress.
directions: Plant in any well-drained to moist soil in full sun to partial shade — cabbage palm, the state tree of Florida and South Carolina, is the most cold-hardy of all the large, single-trunk native palms and the defining tree of the southeastern coastal plain from the Carolinas through Florida and around the Gulf Coast. The arching, blue-green fronds make it instantly recognizable as the symbol of the South. It is remarkably adaptable — tolerating salt spray, flooding, drought, alkaline soil, and hurricane-force winds (the flexible trunk bends dramatically but rarely breaks). White flower clusters in summer attract pollinators; the small black fruits feed dozens of bird species and are an important food source for cedar waxwings, fish crows, and raccoons. The growing bud ('heart of palm' or 'swamp cabbage') is edible but harvesting kills the tree. It transplants unusually well even when large, as shown by the millions of palms transplanted annually in Florida landscaping.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost; zinnias grow quickly in warm soil.
directions: Direct sow after the last frost in a warm, sunny bed, or start indoors a few weeks earlier. This blend produces some of the largest zinnias available, with rolled, quill-like petals four to six inches across in pink, white, red, orange, and yellow. Space plants for good airflow to reduce powdery mildew and water at the base rather than overhead. Cut blooms frequently for bouquets to keep the plants flowering until frost.

plant_note: Start indoors for earlier bloom; tubers can be lifted and stored over winter.
directions: Start seed indoors about a month before the last frost, then transplant into rich, well-drained soil after the weather settles. This blend produces tall, sturdy plants with narrow, elongated petals in peach, yellow, and deep maroon, and blooms up to six inches across make excellent cut flowers. Stake taller plants and deadhead often to keep them blooming into fall. In colder zones, dig and store the tubers over winter to replant the following spring.

plant_note: Plant tubers after all danger of frost has passed and the soil has warmed.
directions: This prolific dahlia shifts through the season from vivid pink and purple to a smoky mauve, giving weeks of color changes on strong stems. Plant tubers 4 to 6 inches deep in full sun and rich, well-drained soil after frost, and pinch the growing tip early to encourage bushy, branching growth. Stake or corral tall plants and cut blooms often to keep flowers coming. In zones colder than 8, lift and store the tubers in a cool, dry place over winter.

plant_note: Plant tubers after all frost danger passes and soil is reliably above 70°F; cold soil causes rot.
directions: Set tubers knobby-side up, 2 in deep in rich, moist, well-drained soil; for a head start, pot tubers indoors 6–8 weeks before the last frost date in a warm (75–80°F) location. Caladiums are prized for spectacular heart-shaped foliage in combinations of red, pink, white, and green rather than for their flowers. They excel in shady spots where little else provides vibrant color and thrive in humid conditions. In fall, before temperatures drop below 50°F, dig tubers, allow to cure for 2 weeks, and store in dry vermiculite at 60–65°F.

plant_note: Plant divisions in moist, well-drained, peat or coir-based, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.5) soil in deep shade to bright indirect light; calathea is sensitive to fluoride, chlorine, and salt in water — use rainwater or filtered water for best results. Never expose to direct sun, which bleaches and burns the foliage.
directions: Plant in moist, peat-based, slightly acidic soil in bright indirect shade; calatheas are among the most spectacularly decorative foliage plants in the world, producing large, rounded or lance-shaped leaves with intricate patterns of dark green, silvery green, and cream in bold graphic patterns that look almost painted. C. orbifolia in particular produces enormous round leaves with elegant silver stripes. The foliage of most calatheas moves with the light — leaves rise in the evening and lower in the morning in a circadian rhythm that gives the related marantas their name 'prayer plants.' In zone 10–12 calatheas thrive as outdoor shade garden plants under tree canopy; in zone 9a they are outstanding patio container plants in bright, indirect shade. They require consistently moist (but never waterlogged) soil, high humidity, and protection from drafts and direct sun.

plant_note: Direct sow 4–6 weeks before last frost in cool soil; young plants tolerate frost and prefer cool weather.
directions: Sow seed 1/4 in deep in cool, well-drained soil in early spring. Calendula prefers cool weather and will slow or stop blooming when summer heat arrives, often rebounding in fall. Deadhead consistently to prevent seed set and extend the blooming period. The ray petals are edible and can be used as a garnish or in salads.

plant_note: Direct sow on bare soil in fall for spring germination; seeds benefit from cold stratification; calico aster also spreads readily by self-seeding and can be transplanted as container divisions in spring or fall.
directions: Calico aster is one of the most wildlife-valuable of all native asters, producing enormous quantities of tiny white flowers with magenta-to-purple centers arranged in sprays along horizontal branches — the 'calico' name aptly describes the two-tone disk flowers that change color as they age from yellow to red-purple, creating a patchwork effect. This species is distinguished from similar asters by its tolerance of dry to moist shade under tree canopy, making it one of the few native asters that genuinely thrives in partial woodland shade rather than requiring full sun. Over 70 species of specialist native bees use it as a critical late-season pollen source, and the seeds feed dozens of sparrow, junco, and finch species through fall and winter. Plant in woodland edges, naturalized areas, and native hedgerows where the naturalistic branching form and prolific flowering provide a long season of wildlife habitat.

plant_note: Direct sow in cool soil; seeds germinate best in darkness, so cover lightly.
directions: Direct sow this heirloom in early spring or fall in mild climates, covering the seed since it needs darkness to sprout. Also called desert bluebells, it shrugs off cold and drought and rewards a large sowing with upward-facing, luminous blue chalices. Give it lean, well-drained soil and full sun, and avoid overwatering once established. It reseeds readily and is a favorite of bees.

plant_note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to alkaline soil in full sun. California Fan Palm is more cold-tolerant than Mexican Fan Palm (Washingtonia robusta) and can survive brief dips to 15–18°F when established; avoid wet, poorly drained soils.
directions: California Fan Palm is the only palm native to the contiguous western United States, native to natural oases in the desert canyons and spring-fed washes of the Sonoran and Mojave Deserts in southern California and far western Arizona, where it forms dramatic palm oases — some of the most distinctive ecosystems in North America — around underground water sources. Stouter and shorter than its close relative Mexican Fan Palm (Washingtonia robusta) and more cold-hardy (to about 15°F), California Fan Palm features large, bold, gray-green fan-shaped fronds with characteristic hair-like threads (fila) hanging from the leaf segments and retains a thick skirt of hanging dead fronds that provides nesting habitat for orioles, house sparrows, and exotic monk parakeets. The small, dark fruits are an important food source for desert wildlife and were historically an important food for indigenous peoples of the Southwest. Grow in full sun in any well-drained soil — it is extremely drought-tolerant once established and requires minimal care beyond occasional irrigation to maintain appearance.

plant_note: Sow after the last frost in warm soil.
directions: Direct sow California Giants zinnias a quarter inch deep after the last frost once soil is reliably warm. This classic heirloom produces massive four-to-six-inch double blooms in a rainbow of colors on tall, sturdy stems ideal for cutting. Pinch young plants to encourage branching, and cut or deadhead often to keep flowers coming until frost. Give good airflow to limit powdery mildew, and enjoy the constant parade of butterflies.

plant_note: Direct sow into warm soil after all danger of frost.
directions: Sow directly in a warm, sunny spot after frost, or start a few weeks early indoors. This heat-loving heirloom, grown since 1926, forms tall plants topped with large blossoms in a full range of colors that are outstanding for cutting. Space plants for airflow and water at the soil line to limit mildew. Cutting or deadhead regularly keeps the flowers coming right up to frost.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring or fall; seeds need cool soil to germinate and tolerate light frost.
directions: Scatter seed on the soil surface and rake lightly to cover 1/16–1/8 in deep. California poppies hate transplanting due to a delicate taproot, so sow where plants are to grow. They thrive in poor, dry soils and need little or no supplemental water once established. Deadhead to extend bloom, or let plants self-sow freely for a naturalized planting.

plant_note: Sow directly in early spring or fall; resents transplanting.
directions: Direct sow California poppy in early spring or fall, pressing the fine seed onto the surface since it needs light and dislikes root disturbance. This native wildflower opens brilliant orange, cup-shaped blooms above ferny blue-green foliage on compact plants. It thrives in poor, well-drained soil and full sun, needing almost no water once established. Let it self-sow freely and it will return year after year as a cheerful drift.

plant_note: Surface sow; compact form good for edging.
directions: Surface sow dwarf calliopsis where it is to grow, pressing seed into contact with the soil since light aids germination. This compact selection forms neat mounds smothered in bicolor yellow-and-red daisies loved by pollinators. It tolerates poor, dry soil and full sun and is ideal for edging, containers, and the front of borders. Deadhead or shear lightly to encourage continued bloom into fall.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall, 4–6 in deep in moist, fertile soil; camassia tolerates seasonally wet conditions better than most bulbs.
directions: Plant bulbs 4–6 in deep in organically rich, moisture-retentive soil; unlike many spring bulbs, camassia tolerates and even prefers seasonally moist or wet ground. Star-shaped flowers in blue, violet, or creamy white appear on tall spikes in late spring, bridging the gap between early bulbs and summer perennials. Allow foliage to die back naturally after blooming; bulbs naturalize readily in meadow or prairie-style plantings. Deer and rodents avoid camassia bulbs.

plant_note: Start indoors early; the tiny seeds need light, so press onto the surface.
directions: Start the very fine seed indoors well ahead of the last frost, pressing it onto the surface since it needs light to germinate. Cambridge Blue offers soft light-blue flowers that smother the pale foliage from spring to fall, ideal for baskets, window boxes, and edging. Keep the soil evenly moist and give plants afternoon shade in hot regions to prolong bloom. Shearing lightly midseason encourages fresh flushes of flowers.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring in well-drained, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic to slightly alkaline, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun; camphor daisy grows naturally on Gulf Coast beaches, dunes, and coastal prairies and is highly tolerant of salt spray, sandy soil, and reflected heat.
directions: Direct sow in spring in well-drained, dry, sandy soil in full sun — camphor daisy is a native coastal plain annual of the Gulf Coast, naturalized on barrier island beaches, coastal prairies, and sunny, sandy disturbed areas from Texas to Florida, producing masses of small, bright yellow daisy flowers from summer through fall on aromatic, distinctively camphor-scented foliage. The strong, medicinal camphor fragrance is distinctive and immediately recognizable; the plant is named for it. A fast-growing, tough annual that handles the extreme conditions of the Gulf Coast shore: salt spray, drought, nutrient-poor sandy soil, and reflected heat from sand. It self-sows readily to maintain colonies in coastal gardens and is excellent for naturalistic sandy, sunny gardens and coastal erosion-prone areas. Very low maintenance and requires no supplemental irrigation in coastal conditions. The flowers attract small bees and butterflies including skippers and sulfurs.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Canada anemone spreads aggressively by rhizome and seed and is best used where mass coverage is desired or where natural spreading is welcome — it will crowd out less competitive plants.
directions: Plant in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade — Canada anemone is one of the most vigorous native groundcovers available, producing a long-lasting display of pure white, 1.5-inch five-petaled flowers above attractive, deeply lobed leaves from late spring through early summer, then spreading steadily by rhizome to form a dense, weed-suppressing colony. It grows naturally in moist prairies, stream banks, and moist, open woodland edges from Canada south to the mid-Atlantic states. Its aggressive spreading habit makes it ideal for naturalizing large, moist areas along pond edges, woodland margins, and rain garden overflows — it quickly colonizes disturbed, moist ground and crowd out invasive weeds. Use it with appropriate caution in smaller gardens where it may overwhelm neighbors. An outstanding erosion-control plant for moist, sunny to partly shaded slopes. Important early-season nectar source.

plant_note: Plant bulbs 4–6 inches deep in fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial shade; Canada lily requires consistently moist soil and will not tolerate drought or dry, alkaline conditions — it grows naturally in moist meadows, stream banks, and open woodland edges.
directions: Plant bulbs 4–6 inches deep in fall in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial shade — Canada lily is one of the most graceful and spectacular of all eastern wildflowers, producing tall, whorled-leafed stems 3–5 feet high topped with pendant clusters of 3–16 large (3-inch), nodding, bell-shaped flowers in yellow to orange to deep red with dark spots inside, held on gracefully recurved pedicels in June–July. The flowers are visited by hummingbirds, swallowtail butterflies, and bumblebees. Canada lily grows naturally in moist meadows, wet prairies, stream banks, and open woodland edges throughout the northeastern and Appalachian US, in conditions that remain consistently moist through the growing season. It is more adaptable to partial shade than most lilies and blooms reliably in dappled light. Naturalize in moist, shaded meadow gardens and along stream banks for a stunning effect.

common_name: Canada Mayflower (Wild Lily of the Valley)
plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in acidic, moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; this plant is best suited to cool-summer climates and will struggle in zone 7–8 summer heat.
directions: Plant in acidic, moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; Canada mayflower is a delicate, miniature native groundcover of boreal and northern hardwood forests, spreading by slender rhizomes to form mats of glossy, 1-2 inch heart-shaped leaves just 4–6 inches above the forest floor. The tiny white flowers in May and June in fluffy clusters resemble lily of the valley; the pea-sized fruits turn speckled pale red in fall and are eaten by forest birds. It grows naturally across vast areas of North American boreal forests and performs best in cool, acidic, shaded conditions similar to those under conifers or at altitude. In northern woodland gardens it is an excellent carpet beneath ferns and native wildflowers. Not suited to hot-summer climates; best in zones 2–6.

plant_note: Direct sow seed in fall or divide clumps in early spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; Canada violet is one of the tallest and most shade-tolerant native violets, producing flowers over a longer season than most violets.
directions: Sow seed in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade — Canada violet is one of the most garden-worthy and longest-blooming native violets, producing white, five-petaled flowers with a distinctive purple-veined lower petal and faint purple reverse on the upper petals from spring through early summer on stems 8–16 inches tall — significantly taller than most native violets. It grows naturally in moist, rich, shaded forests and mountain woodland throughout northern North America and is genuinely shade-adapted, performing well in deep woodland conditions where most violets cannot thrive. The heart-shaped leaves are attractive throughout the season. Canada violet spreads moderately by rhizome and self-sows to naturalize, forming groundcover colonies in appropriate conditions. All violet species are larval host plants for native fritillary butterflies, making them essential components of the native butterfly garden. An outstanding shade-garden violet for zones 3–8.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost; climbs by twining petioles.
directions: Direct sow canary creeper after the last frost or start indoors a few weeks early in short-season areas. This relative of nasturtium climbs by twining leaf stalks and bears fringed, bright yellow flowers resembling tiny canaries. Provide a trellis or netting in full sun to partial shade with moist, moderately fertile soil. Keep well watered in summer heat and it will bloom until frost.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost; cosmos prefer lean soil and full sun.
directions: Direct sow after the last frost in average-to-lean soil, since rich ground reduces flowering. Candystripe bears golden-centered blooms of white edged and streaked in rose, making a sweet cut flower on tall airy stems. Once established these plants are drought tolerant and prefer to be left largely alone. Deadhead for continued bloom or let some flowers set seed, as they reseed readily for the following year.

plant_note: Plant rhizomes after the last frost when soil is warm; or start indoors in pots 4–6 weeks early.
directions: Plant rhizomes 2–4 in deep with the growing eyes pointing up after all frost danger has passed and soil has warmed; starting in pots indoors 4–6 weeks early extends the season in short-summer regions. Cannas are heavy feeders — apply a balanced slow-release fertilizer at planting and a monthly liquid feed during the growing season. Deadhead by removing the entire spent flower spike back to a side shoot to keep the display going. In zones below 8, dig rhizomes after frost, allow to dry for a day, then pack in barely moist peat or newspaper at 50–55°F for winter storage.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions 3–5 inches deep in moist, rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in full sun to partial shade after frost danger passes; in zone 8–11 rhizomes can remain in the ground year-round with light mulch.
directions: Plant rhizomes in moist, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; canna lilies are among the most spectacular and versatile tropical perennials for southern gardens, producing bold, paddle-like leaves in green, bronze, or striped and large, iris-like flowers in red, orange, yellow, pink, or bicolor from summer through frost. Dozens of named cultivars are available in heights from 18 inches ('Dwarf' types) to 8 feet, suiting them for containers, borders, water's edge, and bold accent planting. In zone 7–11 the rhizomes overwinter in the ground with mulch and re-emerge vigorously each spring. The rhizomes multiply rapidly; divide every 2–3 years. The large tropical leaves make canna an outstanding foliage plant even out of bloom.

common_name: Cape Blue Water Lily (Tropical Blue Water Lily)
plant_note: Plant tubers in aquatic pots filled with heavy garden loam, topped with gravel, and submerged 12–18 inches below the water surface after water temperatures consistently exceed 70°F; Cape blue water lily requires warm water to grow and bloom and will not tolerate water temperatures below 60°F.
directions: Plant tubers in heavy loam aquatic pots submerged 12–18 inches in warm water after temperatures reach 70°F — Cape blue water lily is one of the most spectacularly beautiful aquatic plants for warm-climate water gardens, producing large (6–10 inch), star-shaped flowers in brilliant sky-blue to lavender-blue on tall stems that rise 6–12 inches above the water surface, blooming by day from late spring through fall. Unlike most tropical water lilies, the flowers have an intensely sweet, vanilla-like fragrance and remain open until early afternoon. The large, round, notched floating leaves (12–24 inches across) cover the water surface effectively and provide shade that discourages algae growth and shelters fish and aquatic life. In zone 10–12 the tubers overwinter in the pond; in zone 9 lift and store tubers indoors at 60–65°F over winter. One of the parent species of many popular tropical water lily hybrids and among the finest blue-flowered aquatics available for warm water garden cultivation.

plant_note: Start indoors before the last frost and transplant out after.
directions: Start cape daisy indoors six weeks before the last frost, covering the seed lightly, then transplant to a sunny, well-drained bed once frost danger has passed. It bears elegant white petals around rich purple centers over a long bloom season and is grown as a tender perennial in mild climates. Deadhead spent flowers to keep the display fresh through the warm months. It tolerates some drought but flowers best with steady, moderate moisture.

plant_note: Plant rooted cuttings or container plants in spring in any well-drained soil in full sun; cape honeysuckle is extremely adaptable to a wide range of soil conditions and is virtually foolproof in warm climates. Train the scrambling stems onto a support structure early — the plant does not twine or cling on its own and must be tied.
directions: Plant in full sun in any well-drained soil — poor, sandy, alkaline, and rocky soils are all acceptable; cape honeysuckle is not a true honeysuckle but a South African scrambling shrub that produces clusters of brilliant orange-red tubular flowers almost continuously from late fall through spring in zone 9 — filling the garden with color exactly when other flowering plants are dormant. It is extraordinary as a hummingbird and butterfly plant. The plant does not twine or cling like a vine but scrambles vigorously through other shrubs or must be tied to a trellis or fence; alternatively it makes an excellent sprawling groundcover on slopes or a large informal mound-shaped specimen shrub. A yellow-orange cultivar ('Aurea') is also available. Cape honeysuckle withstands heat, drought, salt spray, and poor soil with ease once established and grows rapidly in warm weather. In zone 8 it may suffer significant frost damage but recovers vigorously from roots in spring.

plant_note: Direct sow in cool spring soil; blooms open in response to sunshine.
directions: Direct sow in early spring in well-drained soil and full sun, where the flowers can enjoy plenty of light. The daisy-like blooms in orange, yellow, white, and salmon open when hit by the sun to reveal a dark, purple-ringed center that bees and butterflies love. Provide lean soil and let plants dry between waterings, as they tolerate drought well. Deadhead to prolong the display through the cooler part of the season.

plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring after frost danger passes in moist, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; plumbago establishes quickly and begins blooming within one growing season. In zone 8 it may die back to the ground in hard freezes but regrows vigorously from the roots in spring.
directions: Plant in well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; cape plumbago is one of the most beloved and widely used flowering shrubs of Gulf Coast landscapes, producing continuous clusters of sky-blue to powder-blue flowers from spring through the first frost — a color rarely achieved by other summer-blooming shrubs in the heat. The arching, semi-scandent stems naturally mound and sprawl, making it ideal for slopes, large containers, espalier on fences, or informal hedges. It is remarkably drought tolerant once established and thrives in the heat and humidity of zone 9. A white cultivar ('Alba') is also widely available. Prune back by one-third in early spring to encourage fresh, dense regrowth and a compact habit; in zone 9 it remains evergreen year-round. An outstanding long-season butterfly and bee plant, particularly attractive to swallowtail butterflies.

plant_note: Nick and soak seed before sowing to speed germination; provide a trellis.
directions: Nick and soak the hard seeds overnight, then direct sow after frost at the base of a trellis, pole, or fence. This vigorous vine climbs six to twelve feet and carries deep-red, white-throated flowers that hummingbirds and pollinators adore, along with delicate fern-like foliage. Give it full sun and steady moisture through the growing season. Provide sturdy support early, as the twining stems grow quickly once warm weather arrives.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; surface-sow without covering as seeds need light to germinate.
directions: Surface-sow tiny seeds on moist mix and provide light at 65–70°F; seedlings emerge in 2–3 weeks and grow slowly their first season. Plant in consistently moist to wet soil — rain gardens, pond edges, and low-lying areas suit this plant perfectly. Brilliant red spikes appear in midsummer to early fall and are hummingbird magnets. Individual plants are short-lived (2–3 years), so allow self-sowing or divide offsets each spring to maintain the colony.

plant_note: Surface-sow seed (do not cover — seed needs light to germinate) indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; seed requires light and warmth to germinate. Transplant to the garden after frost danger passes, placing in consistently moist to wet soil in partial shade.
directions: Plant in moist to wet, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun near water features, rain gardens, or boggy areas; cardinal flower produces brilliant scarlet flower spikes from midsummer into fall that are the premier nectar source for ruby-throated hummingbirds in eastern North America. The intensity of the red is almost unmatched in the native plant palette. Plants are short-lived perennials that self-seed freely — allow some seedlings to remain to perpetuate the colony, as individual plants typically live only 2–3 years. Division of offsets (basal rosettes) in early fall also maintains the planting. Plants tolerate standing water and thrive along stream banks and pond edges. The great blue lobelia (Lobelia siphilitica) is a closely related blue-flowered species equally valuable for pollinators.

plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring or fall; note that seedling-grown plants have variable fragrance — purchase named cultivars or divisions from plants whose fragrance you have evaluated in person.
directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; Carolina allspice is a native understory shrub of the Southeast with extraordinary, deeply fragrant maroon flowers in late spring that smell like strawberry, banana, and clove combined. The flowers are pollinated by small beetles that become temporarily trapped while feeding, ensuring cross-pollination. Fragrance intensity varies tremendously among seed-grown plants — evaluate or buy named cultivars like 'Athens' (fragrant, cream-colored) for reliable results. The bark and leaves are also aromatic. The shrub spreads by root suckers to form a colony over time; remove suckers to maintain a single-stemmed specimen or allow to form a thicket.

plant_note: Plant container-grown transplants in spring in well-drained, loamy to sandy-loam soil in full sun to partial shade; established plants tolerate drought and moderate clay. All parts of Carolina Cherry Laurel contain hydrogen cyanide compounds — never eat leaves, bark, or seeds.
directions: Carolina Cherry Laurel is an evergreen native tree or large shrub of the coastal plain and piedmont from North Carolina to central Florida and west to Texas, where it grows at forest edges, in disturbed sites, and along stream banks in full sun to partial shade. The glossy, lance-shaped evergreen leaves, fragrant white flower clusters in early spring, and dense branching habit make it one of the most popular native plants for hedges, privacy screens, and naturalized windbreaks in the Southeast, especially in Zones 8–10 where it grows vigorously year-round. Clusters of glossy black drupes ripen in fall and winter and are eagerly consumed by mockingbirds, cedar waxwings, robins, and other fruit-eating birds, though all parts of the plant contain hydrogen cyanide compounds and must never be eaten by humans or livestock. Plant in well-drained, loamy to sandy-loam soil in full sun for best flowering and fruiting; established plants tolerate extended drought, moderate clay, and salt spray in coastal settings.

plant_note: Plant container-grown transplants or rooted cuttings in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun. Carolina Holly is dioecious — plant one male for every 2–3 females within 50 feet for reliable berry production. Seeds require double stratification and are slow to germinate.
directions: Carolina Holly is a native deciduous holly of the coastal plain and lower piedmont from the Carolinas south to Florida and west to Texas, growing as a large shrub or small tree at forest edges, hammock borders, and along stream banks in partial shade to full sun. The small but vivid red to orange-red berries, held in clusters along the bare winter branches after leaf drop, are an important food source for robins, cedar waxwings, bluebirds, hermit thrushes, and other fruit-eating birds during fall migration and winter. Like all native hollies, Carolina Holly is dioecious — both male and female plants are required within pollinator range for berry production — and its modest size compared to American Holly makes it suitable for smaller gardens where the larger species may overwhelm the space. Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; established plants tolerate moderate drought and require little maintenance.

plant_note: Plant in spring or fall near a trellis, fence, or arbor for the vine to climb.
directions: This evergreen vine covers fences, trellises, and arbors with fragrant, bright yellow trumpet blooms in late winter to early spring. Plant in full sun to part shade in moist, well-drained soil and provide sturdy support for the twining stems. Water regularly while establishing, then it tolerates moderate drought once mature. Note that all parts of the plant are toxic if eaten, so site it away from areas frequented by pets and children; prune lightly after flowering to control size.

plant_note: Plant bulbs 4–5 inches deep in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 5.0–6.0), sandy to loamy, humus-rich soil in partial shade; Carolina lily is a dry-land lily adapted to the sandy, well-drained, acidic soils of southeastern pine forests — unlike most lilies, it does not tolerate wet or poorly-drained conditions.
directions: Plant bulbs 4–5 inches deep in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic, sandy, humus-rich soil in partial shade — Carolina lily is the southeastern counterpart to Canada lily and the most drought-tolerant of our native lilies, adapted to the dry, sandy, acidic soils of longleaf pine and oak-hickory forests from the mid-Atlantic through the Deep South. It produces nodding, orange to red, strongly reflexed (turkscap-type) flowers with dark spots and a prominent yellow center in July–August on stems with attractive whorled leaves. The flowers are intensely sought by ruby-throated hummingbirds and swallowtail butterflies. Unlike most lilies, it thrives in the sandy, well-drained, dry-summer conditions of southeastern pine flatwoods and is one of the few native lilies suited to zone 8–9 heat and drought. Outstanding for naturalizing under pines in the coastal South. Endangered by habitat loss; purchase only nursery-propagated bulbs.

plant_note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; Carolina phlox is more mildew-resistant than tall garden phlox (P. paniculata) and more tolerant of heat and partial shade.
directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade — Carolina phlox is a native Phlox of the southeastern US that closely resembles the familiar garden phlox (P. paniculata) but is superior in two important respects: it is significantly more resistant to the powdery mildew that disfigures garden phlox by late summer, and it tolerates partial shade better, blooming acceptably in morning sun with afternoon shade. The showy clusters of pink to magenta (rarely white) flowers appear in early to midsummer, slightly earlier than P. paniculata, and are fragrant and highly attractive to hummingbirds and swallowtail butterflies. The thick, glossy leaves (giving the common name 'thick-leaved phlox') remain in good condition through the season. An excellent native substitute for garden phlox in the shaded or partly shaded border in zones 4–9.

plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs in fall in moist, acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), well-drained, humus-rich soil in partial shade; Carolina rhododendron is more drought tolerant than most rhododendrons but still requires consistently moist, acidic soil — mulch heavily to maintain moisture and protect shallow roots.
directions: Carolina rhododendron is one of the most compact and garden-worthy of all native North American rhododendrons — a small-leaved, dense, evergreen shrub from the high-elevation heath balds and rocky slopes of the southern Appalachians that produces masses of rose-pink to white flower clusters in April and May that can cover the entire plant in bloom. It is notably more heat-tolerant, drought-adaptable, and pest-resistant than the large-leaved rhododendrons (R. maximum and R. catawbiense) and adapts to typical garden conditions more readily; its compact size (2–6 feet) makes it appropriate for small-space native gardens and foundation plantings where large rhododendrons would overwhelm the space. The tiny, dark-spotted dots (puncta) on the leaf undersides give it the alternate name 'punctatum' and help identify it in the field; the small, leathery evergreen leaves roll upward in cold temperatures as a water-conservation mechanism, creating a distinctive winter appearance. One of the best native rhododendrons for shaded rockeries, acidic slopes, and native woodland gardens in the mid-Atlantic and upper South.

plant_note: Plant hardwood cuttings or container plants in spring in full sun to partial shade; this species is more drought tolerant than most roses and accepts poor, rocky soils.
directions: Plant in average to poor, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; this tough native rose requires far less care than hybrid tea roses, resists most diseases, and thrives in neglected areas, roadsides, and dry meadows where other roses struggle. The single, five-petaled pink flowers in late spring attract native bees, and the persistent red rose hips provide late fall and winter food for bluebirds, mockingbirds, and foxes. Rose hips are edible and nutritious, very high in vitamin C; make into jelly, syrup, or tea after removing seeds. Remove the occasional dead cane in winter but otherwise leave unpruned to preserve the natural, wiggly growth habit.

plant_note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped trees in late winter or spring in moist, acidic (pH 4.5–6.0), humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade; Carolina silverbell is an understory tree that requires acidic soil and performs best with afternoon shade in zone 7–8.
directions: Plant in moist, acidic (pH 4.5–6.0), humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade; Carolina silverbell is one of the most beautiful and underused native understory trees of the southern Appalachians, producing pure white, bell-shaped flowers in clusters all along the branches in mid-spring when the tree is still essentially leafless — making the display exceptionally striking. The flowers are an important nectar source for early bumblebees and long-tongued native bees. The four-winged seed pods are ornamental through summer and fall. Fall color is clear yellow. It grows naturally as a subcanopy tree beneath taller hardwoods in moist, acidic ravines and slopes and is genuinely shade-tolerant, making it one of the best small-to-medium flowering trees for the shaded garden or woodland garden. An outstanding substitute for Bradford pear in the southern landscape, providing similar white spring bloom but with genuine wildlife value and a graceful natural form.

plant_note: Surface sow around the last frost; light aids germination.
directions: Sprinkle Carpet of Snow alyssum onto firmed soil around the last frost and press in without covering, as light aids germination. This low, spreading heirloom carpets beds and container edges with masses of tiny, honey-scented white flowers. It draws in bees and beneficial insects and blooms from spring into fall, pausing only in extreme heat. Shear lightly if it flags mid-season to spark a fresh flush of bloom.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring or early fall in rich, moist, acidic soil.
directions: Cascade Spice bears large, ruffled Japanese iris blooms in rich purple and white for a bold late-spring to summer display. Site in full sun to part shade in acidic, humus-rich soil kept consistently moist, ideal at the edge of a pond or in a rain garden. Do not allow the soil to dry out during active growth, and avoid alkaline amendments. Deadhead spent stalks and divide crowded clumps in fall to sustain strong flowering.

plant_note: Start indoors early; surface sow the tiny seed, which needs light.
directions: Sow the fine seed on the surface indoors several weeks before the last frost, as it requires light to germinate. This trailing blend spills over hanging baskets and container edges with blooms of blue, blue with a white eye, lilac, red, ruby, and white. Keep it consistently moist and provide afternoon shade where summers are hot. A light midseason shearing renews the display and keeps plants full.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring or fall in any moist to dry, well-drained to moderately wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full shade to deep shade; cast iron plant is essentially indestructible in zones 7–11 and establishes in conditions where almost nothing else will grow — deep shade, root competition, poor soil, drought, and even flooding are all tolerated.
directions: Plant in any soil in full shade to deep shade — cast iron plant lives up to its common name as the most tolerant, indestructible landscape plant available for zones 7–11. No other evergreen perennial survives deep shade, drought, root competition, salt, flooding, neglect, and poor soil with the same equanimity. The broad, lance-shaped, glossy dark-green leaves (12–24 inches long) emerge from ground-level rhizomes and form a dense, weed-suppressing clump that deepens and spreads slowly over years. It is the premier plant for impossible spots: the north side of the house, under overhanging eaves, beneath dense magnolias or live oaks where nothing else grows. Variegated forms ('Milky Way' with white speckles, 'Asahi' with white-tipped leaves) add more visual interest. The plant grows very slowly — increase your initial spacing to 18 inches and know it will take 3–5 years to fill in. Once established, it is permanent: decades-old plantings of cast iron plant are commonly found thriving with zero maintenance.

plant_note: Plant in spring after frost in a warm, sunny site with good drainage.
directions: This fast-growing crape myrtle covers itself in vivid purple flower panicles from summer into fall, followed by attractive fall foliage and exfoliating bark. Plant in full sun for the heaviest bloom in average, well-drained soil; too much shade reduces flowering. Water regularly while establishing, then it tolerates heat and moderate drought. Prune lightly in late winter to shape, removing crossing branches rather than topping the tree to preserve its natural form.

plant_note: Direct sow scarified seeds in spring in dry, rocky, alkaline, or sandy soil in full sun; catclaw acacia develops a deep taproot quickly and is extremely drought tolerant once established — not suitable for moist, clay, or frequently irrigated soils.
directions: Catclaw acacia — nicknamed 'wait-a-minute bush' because its small, curved, cat-claw-like thorns snag clothing and skin, halting passage — is a native shrub to medium tree of dry washes, rocky slopes, and desert grasslands from south Texas to California and south into Mexico. Despite its formidable armament, catclaw acacia is one of the most important native bee plants in the Chihuahuan Desert and south Texas, producing abundant cream-colored, cylindrical flower spikes in spring that support hundreds of native bee species and are the source of a prized honey; it is considered the single most important native honey plant in the Sonoran and Chihuahuan Deserts by commercial beekeepers. The long, flat seed pods ripen in summer and fall and are consumed by deer, javelinas, and large-seeded birds; the dense, thorny structure of established shrubs creates impenetrable wildlife brush habitat essential for quail, roadrunners, and nesting songbirds throughout south Texas. Best suited to xeric native plantings, dry washes, and wildlife habitat gardens where its thorny nature is an asset rather than a liability.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall in dry to average, well-drained soil in full sun; catclaw mimosa has long, fleshy taproots that make transplanting difficult — direct sow in permanent locations and scarify seeds with hot water or sandpaper before planting for best germination.
directions: Catclaw mimosa is a native scrambling perennial of dry prairies, rocky glades, and open woodlands in the south-central United States, armed with small, recurved prickles that catch on clothing and slow passage through its loose, sprawling stems — the 'catclaw' refers to these hook-shaped thorns, while 'sensitive briar' describes the leaves that fold instantly when touched, a characteristic shared with many Mimosa species. Pink, globe-shaped, pompom-like flower heads appear from May through August and are vigorously worked by native bumblebees and smaller bees for pollen; as a legume, it fixes nitrogen through root nodules and helps improve soil in dry, impoverished areas. Its spreading, semi-prostrate habit and prickles make it most appropriate for naturalized areas, dry meadow restorations, and native prairie gardens where its sprawling character can provide ground-level interest and soil improvement without maintenance; it is drought-tolerant to an extreme degree once established on deep, sandy or rocky soil.

plant_note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil; plants establish quickly and bloom the first season.
directions: Plant in lean, well-drained soil — catmint thrives in conditions that would challenge many perennials and blooms heavily from spring into early summer. Shear the entire plant back by one-half after the first flowering flush to refresh the mounding habit and trigger a second wave of lavender-blue flowers in late summer. The silvery-green aromatic foliage is deer-resistant and provides a reliable edging throughout the season. Divide in spring every 3–4 years or propagate from cuttings taken in late spring.

plant_note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to alkaline, deep soil in full sun. Cedar Elm tolerates alkaline limestone soils, compaction, drought, and urban conditions better than most other elms and is the preferred native elm for landscapes in central and south Texas.
directions: Cedar Elm is the most drought-tolerant and heat-tolerant of the native elms, native to the river bottoms, limestone floodplains, and upland limestone woods of Texas, Oklahoma, Arkansas, and Louisiana, where it frequently grows adjacent to cedar (Juniperus species) — hence its common name. Its most distinctive habit is bloom timing: unlike all other North American elms that bloom in late winter before leaf-out, Cedar Elm blooms in late summer and fall (August–October), producing tiny, inconspicuous flowers followed by small, papery, winged seeds; this unusual timing means it can be confused with a disease-stricken elm (sparse fall foliage is normal, not worrying). A tough, adaptable native shade tree that tolerates the alkaline limestone soils, compaction, and summer heat of Texas landscapes with exceptional resilience, Cedar Elm is one of the most widely recommended native shade trees for central and south Texas gardens. Grow in full sun in any well-drained to moist soil; it adapts readily to both alkaline limestone and acidic sandy soils.

plant_note: Plant divisions or container transplants in fall in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, lean, dry to moderately moist soil in partial shade; cedar sage is one of the few native salvias that genuinely prefers shade and is found growing naturally under cedar (Juniperus) and oak woodlands in central Texas.
directions: Plant in well-drained, lean, dry to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade; cedar sage is a compact, low-growing native perennial of the limestone woodlands of the Texas Hill Country and Edwards Plateau — one of the very few native salvias that grows naturally in shade under Ashe juniper (cedar) and live oak canopies, making it exceptionally valuable for shaded Texas gardens. The small, rounded, hairy leaves are fragrant, and the brilliant red tubular flowers are produced on short spikes from spring through fall in shade conditions where few other flowering plants thrive. It is a critical hummingbird food plant. The plant stays compact and low (12–18 inches), spreads slowly by underground stolons, and requires almost no maintenance in well-drained shaded sites. Combine with turk's cap and inland sea oats for a beautiful dry shade planting.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification; the plant self-sows reliably and will naturalize once established, filling a shaded bed with bright spring color.
directions: Direct sow in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade in fall; celandine poppy produces stunning bright yellow four-petaled flowers in early spring, making it one of the most brilliant color plants for the shade garden before the tree canopy closes. The plants bloom for several weeks in spring, then go partially dormant in summer heat but re-emerge in fall for additional sporadic flowering in cool climates. The bright orange sap from cut stems has traditional medicinal uses but is toxic and a skin irritant. Once established, it self-sows abundantly to naturalize throughout a shaded bed, so site carefully if you don't want it to spread. Combine with Virginia bluebells and trilliums for a classic spring shade display.

plant_note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; transplant only after soil is warm, as cold temperatures cause stunted plants.
directions: Sow seed on the surface of moist starting mix and lightly press in — seed needs light and warmth to germinate. Do not rush transplanting into cold soil, as chilling causes plants to stall and go prematurely to seed. Deadhead spent plumes on crested and feathered types to extend the display. Celosia makes an excellent dried flower; harvest spikes just as they reach full color.

plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs in fall or spring in extremely well-drained, alkaline to neutral, lean, rocky or sandy soil in full sun; cenizo is adapted to the caliche and limestone soils of the Chihuahuan Desert and Edwards Plateau and will decline rapidly in rich, moist, or poorly drained soils. Never amend the planting hole with organic matter.
directions: Plant in full sun in extremely well-drained, lean, alkaline soil; cenizo is the iconic native shrub of the Texas Hill Country and Edwards Plateau and one of the most drought tolerant and xeric shrubs in the South — its silver, feltlike leaves reflect heat and reduce water loss, allowing it to thrive in the hottest, driest conditions where other shrubs fail. The lavender-purple tubular flowers burst open in response to humidity — most dramatically in the 24–48 hours before a summer rain — giving it the folk name 'barometer bush.' Bloom cycles repeat with each moisture event from summer through fall, covering the entire silver shrub in purple. Cultivars include 'Green Cloud' (dark green leaves, rich purple flowers), 'White Cloud' (white flowers), and 'Compact' (dense, 3–4-foot habit). Never overwater or fertilize — excess moisture is the primary killer. Outstanding for xeric, native, and wildlife gardens in zones 8–11.

plant_note: Plant tubers after frost in warm, well-drained soil.
directions: Center Court is an elegant creamy-white dahlia that blooms nonstop all season and produces tubers reliably, making it a great choice for beginners. Plant tubers 4 to 6 inches deep in full sun and fertile, well-drained soil once the ground has warmed. Pinch young plants to promote branching and cut flowers regularly to prolong blooming. In cold-winter regions, dig and store the tubers indoors after the first frost blackens the foliage.

plant_note: Plant pups (offsets from the mother rosette) or container-grown plants in spring in extremely well-drained, sandy to rocky, alkaline to neutral, lean soil in full sun; agave requires perfect drainage above all else — it will rot quickly in clay soil or where water pools. Plant slightly above grade to improve drainage.
directions: Plant in full sun in extremely well-drained, lean, rocky or sandy soil — perfect drainage is the only critical requirement; century plant is one of the most dramatic and architectural plants available for hot, dry landscapes and can survive on rainfall alone in appropriate climates once established. The large blue-gray rosette of thick, succulent leaves with sharp terminal spines and hooked marginal teeth grows slowly over 10–30 years before producing a single towering flower stalk (15–30 feet tall) laden with thousands of yellow flowers — then the main rosette dies, leaving behind offsets (pups) that perpetuate the clump. The juice of the leaf base was fermented by indigenous peoples of Mexico to make pulque; the young flower stalk was roasted and eaten as a sweet vegetable. Despite the name 'century plant,' blooming typically occurs after 10–30 years, not 100. Outstanding as a bold specimen and accent plant for xeric landscapes; handle with caution due to the sharp terminal spine which can cause serious injury.

plant_note: Start indoors early since these carnations are slow to reach bloom.
directions: Start seed indoors well before the last frost, as this blend is slow to reach flowering. Chabaud carnations bear fragrant, clove-scented double blooms in scarlet, purple, pink, white, yellow, and bicolors atop blue-green foliage, one flower per stem for excellent cutting. Grow in full sun with well-drained soil and even moisture. Deadhead and pinch early to build sturdy, well-branched plants.

plant_note: Start indoors well before the last frost; slow to reach bloom.
directions: Start Chabaud carnations indoors eight to ten weeks before the last frost, covering seed lightly, then harden off and transplant after frost danger passes. This heirloom Dianthus bears fragrant, fully double blooms in a rich mix of colors prized for cut arrangements. Give it full sun, well-drained soil, and support the tall flower stems. Deadhead to prolong the display, and in mild zones it will return as a short-lived perennial.

common_name: Chalice Vine (Cup of Gold, Hawaiian Wedding Flower)
plant_note: Root woody cuttings in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic, rich soil in full sun; chalice vine is slow to establish but eventually forms a massive, woody-stemmed vine requiring a very large, sturdy support. Bloom is best on established plants and often increases when the plant experiences mild drought stress in late summer-fall.
directions: Plant in fertile, rich, well-drained soil in full sun; chalice vine is one of the most spectacular large-flowering plants in the world, producing enormous chalice-shaped flowers 8–10 inches across in golden yellow with purple veins and a powerful, tropical, coconut-like fragrance, strongest at night. The massive, woody vines with large, glossy evergreen leaves can eventually cover a large wall, pergola, or building. It is one of the showiest of all tropical vines for zone 10–11 landscapes and a traditional feature of California and Florida coastal gardens. Established plants are surprisingly drought tolerant. Bloom is typically heaviest in late winter through spring and again in fall. Caution: all parts of this plant, including the nectar, are highly toxic — the active alkaloids can cause severe poisoning. Do not plant where children, pets, or livestock can access the plant.

plant_note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped trees in late winter or spring in moist to dry, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial shade; chalk maple performs better in zone 7–9 summer heat than sugar maple, Japanese maple, or most other ornamental maples.
directions: Plant in moist to dry, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; chalk maple is a small-to-medium native understory maple of the Southeast Appalachians and Piedmont, closely related to sugar maple but significantly more heat-and-drought-tolerant and better suited to the zone 7–9 garden than its more famous relative. The common name comes from the distinctive light-colored, chalky-white to pale gray bark. The 3–5 lobed leaves turn magnificent shades of yellow, orange, and scarlet in fall — one of the best native maples for fall color in the South. It grows naturally as an understory tree on rocky slopes and bluffs with well-drained, calcareous soils. An outstanding and underused native small tree for shaded gardens in the Southeast where sugar maple and Japanese maple both struggle with the heat and humidity.

plant_note: Direct sow where plants are to grow, as flax resents transplanting.
directions: Direct sow in early spring where plants will grow, since flax does not transplant well. This easy, low-maintenance blend produces showy flowers on delicate stems that dance in the breeze and draw pollinators. It tolerates hot, dry conditions once established, so water sparingly. Allow some plants to go to seed for reseeding and successive bloom.

plant_note: Often grown as a biennial; sow in spring or fall for bloom the next year.
directions: Sow in spring or fall in a sunny, sheltered spot, as this classic biennial typically flowers the second year. Chater's Double, developed in the 1880s, produces tall spires of ruffly double blooms in golden-yellow, rose, maroon, salmon-pink, scarlet, and white over a long bloom period. Stake the tall stalks in windy sites and water at the base to reduce rust. Let some flowers set seed, as hollyhocks reseed reliably.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall promptly; they dry out quickly.
directions: Plant checkered fritillary bulbs in fall as soon as possible, since the delicate bulbs dry out quickly, setting them about four inches deep. The nodding bell flowers display a distinctive checkerboard pattern in purple or white in mid-spring. It prefers moist, humus-rich soil in partial shade and naturalizes well in damp meadows and woodland edges. Handle the fragile bulbs gently at planting and let foliage die back to ensure return.

plant_note: Plant divisions or container plants in fall in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry, rocky to sandy soil in full sun; cheddar pinks are among the most drought and heat tolerant of all Dianthus species — they are exceptional in alkaline, rocky, or sandy soils where other perennials fail.
directions: Plant in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry, rocky to sandy soil in full sun — cheddar pinks are native to the limestone gorges of Cheddar, England and are among the most drought-tolerant and heat-resistant perennial dianthus available, forming compact, mat-like mounds of very narrow, blue-gray, evergreen foliage tipped with intensely fragrant, deep rose-pink to cerise flowers in late spring. The rich clove fragrance is as strong as cottage pinks but the plant is smaller, tidier, and notably more heat and alkaline-soil tolerant. The Perennial Plant Association named it Perennial Plant of the Year in 2015. Superb for rock gardens, dry walls, gravel gardens, and edging in alkaline or sandy soils where drainage is excellent. Shear lightly after bloom to maintain compact mounds and often promotes a second flush of bloom in fall. Very long-lived in appropriate conditions; clumps may persist for decades.

plant_note: Plant rooted stem cuttings in spring after temperatures are consistently above 60°F in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; chenille plant roots easily and establishes quickly in warm weather. Pinch growing tips regularly to encourage dense, bushy growth and maximum flower production.
directions: Plant in moist, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; chenille plant is one of the most unusually beautiful tropical flowering plants, producing long, pendulous, fuzzy catkin-like flower spikes in brilliant crimson-red that dangle like fluffy chenille tassels 12–18 inches long — an unmistakable and astonishing floral display unlike anything else in the garden. The flowers are produced nearly year-round in frost-free zones. It thrives in the warm, humid conditions of zone 10–12 and is used as a summer annual or large container plant in cooler zones; in zone 9 it may be grown as a seasonal annual or overwintered in a greenhouse. Bright filtered light or morning sun produces the most flowers while preventing leaf scorch in the afternoon heat. Regular watering and monthly fertilization during the growing season keeps the plant vigorous and blooming well. Propagate from 6-inch stem cuttings in late spring.

plant_note: Plant in spring or fall in a site with morning sun and afternoon shade in hot climates.
directions: Cherokee Brave opens deep pink spring blooms with reddish new foliage, followed by red fall color and berries that attract birds. Plant in moist, acidic, well-drained soil rich in organic matter, siting it in part shade or where it gets afternoon protection in hot regions. Keep the root zone mulched and evenly moist, as dogwoods are shallow-rooted and dislike drought. This selection shows improved resistance to powdery mildew; prune only to remove dead or crossing branches after flowering.

plant_note: Plant in spring or fall in a sheltered spot with dappled light in warm climates.
directions: Cherokee Chief bears rich rosy-red bracts in spring on graceful horizontal branches, with glossy foliage that turns red in autumn. Plant in acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil and give it part shade or afternoon protection where summers are hot. Maintain even soil moisture with mulch, since the shallow roots suffer in dry conditions. Prune sparingly right after bloom to shape the canopy and remove any damaged wood.

plant_note: Plant in spring or fall in dappled light with cool, moist, acidic soil.
directions: Cherokee Princess is prized for its heavy set of large, pure-white spring bracts and a well-branched, rounded form. Plant in moist, acidic, organically rich soil in part shade, protecting it from harsh afternoon sun and drying wind. Mulch to keep the shallow root system cool and consistently moist. Enjoy red fall foliage and berries that feed birds; prune only lightly after flowering to maintain the tidy structure.

plant_note: Surface sow, as the seed needs light; blooms the first year from an early start.
directions: Start indoors and surface sow, since the seed needs light to germinate and benefits from an early start to bloom the first year. Cherry Brandy bears three-to-four-inch maroon-red velvet petals around a dark chocolate center for a rich, luxurious look. Grow in full sun with average, well-drained soil and moderate water. Deadhead to extend the bloom and cut stems freely for long-lasting arrangements.

plant_note: Start indoors 8 weeks early; surface sow for light.
directions: Surface sow this unique deep-red rudbeckia indoors eight weeks before the last frost, as light aids germination. Cherry Brandy produces rich burgundy daisies with dark centers, prized as long-lasting cut flowers. Transplant to full sun and average well-drained soil after frost danger passes. Deadhead regularly to keep new blooms coming, and treat it as an annual though it may overwinter in mild zones.

plant_note: Sow in darkness, as phlox seed germinates best when covered.
directions: Start seed indoors and cover it, since annual phlox germinates best in darkness. Cherry Caramel offers unusual creamy-beige flowers with dark cherry centers that make dreamy, fragrant cut flowers and draw pollinators and hummingbirds. Grow in full sun to part shade with well-drained soil and even moisture. Bloom may pause in intense summer heat and renew as cooler weather returns, so keep plants deadheaded.

plant_note: Direct sow the large seeds after frost; they resent root disturbance.
directions: Direct sow the large, pebble-like seeds after frost about half an inch deep, an easy task for young gardeners. Cherry Rose Jewel bears bright, peppery, edible blossoms and rounded leaves that make elegant additions to salads and appetizer plates. Keep soil lean rather than rich to encourage flowers over foliage, and water moderately. Nasturtiums are known to deter some garden pests while attracting pollinators.

plant_note: Plant in spring or fall in morning sun with afternoon shade in hot climates.
directions: This bigleaf hydrangea produces color-shifting blooms that mature to rich cherry-red tones from spring into fall on a compact, rounded shrub. Plant in part shade with morning sun and afternoon protection in moist, well-drained, organically rich soil. Water deeply and regularly, especially in heat, and mulch to conserve moisture. It blooms on both old and new wood, so prune only to remove dead stems in early spring for the most reliable flowering.

common_name: Cherrybark Oak (Swamp Red Oak, Elliott's Oak)
plant_note: Plant container-grown trees in fall in rich, moist to moderately moist, slightly acidic, deep, well-drained bottomland or bluff soil in full sun; cherrybark oak is native to the rich, well-drained ridges and terraces above flood lines in bottomland hardwood forests — it does NOT tolerate prolonged flooding.
directions: Plant in rich, moist, well-drained, slightly acidic bottomland or bluff soil in full sun; cherrybark oak is generally regarded as one of the finest and most beautiful of all North American oaks — a majestic timber tree reaching 80–120 feet with a straight, clear trunk and distinctive shaggy, plated bark reminiscent of black cherry (hence the name). The deeply lobed, very large leaves have outstanding scarlet to orange-red fall color. The abundant small acorns are a critical food source for deer, turkeys, wood ducks, and squirrels. Unlike many red-group oaks, cherrybark oak is intolerant of flooding and requires the rich, well-drained ridges and terraces of bottomland hardwood forests — the 'cherrybark ridges' of bottomland forest. It is the fastest-growing of the large southern oaks (4–5 feet per year when young) and develops into an impressively large, long-lived specimen tree.

plant_note: Start indoors in warmth; celosia needs steady heat to grow well.
directions: Start seed indoors in warm conditions a few weeks before the last frost and transplant only after the soil is warm. Red Flame forms compact, uniform cockscomb heads made of countless tiny flowers in waves of deep red that last a long time in the garden or vase. Give it full sun, warmth, and well-drained soil for the best color. Harvest stems for fresh or dried arrangements as the heads reach full size.

plant_note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost risk has passed.
directions: Sow seed 1/8 in deep in moist starting mix at 65–70°F. Rotate planting locations each year, as China asters are prone to Fusarium wilt when grown in the same soil repeatedly. Avoid overhead watering and ensure good air circulation to reduce disease pressure. Harvest cut flowers when petals are just beginning to open fully for maximum vase life.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost or start indoors; succession sow for prolonged bloom.
directions: An old-fashioned single-flowered China aster blend in cool reds, pinks, purples, and white for midsummer color. Sow seed directly after frost or start indoors, barely covering the seed. Plant toward the back of the bed where the two-to-three-foot stems provide height and long-lasting cut flowers. Deadhead spent blooms and rotate planting sites each year to reduce aster wilt.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant into the garden 2–4 weeks before last frost, as plants tolerate light frost.
directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep in well-drained germination mix; seedlings grow quickly and can be transplanted while still small. Plant in full sun in well-drained, neutral-to-slightly-alkaline soil; heavy clay and overwet conditions lead to crown rot. Deadhead spent flowers continuously to prevent early seed set and extend the blooming season well into summer. Plants often decline in intense summer heat but may rebloom in fall if sheared back and kept watered.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring 2–4 weeks before last frost; can also sow in fall in mild winter climates.
