directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep in early spring directly where they are to grow; cover seeds lightly as they germinate better in darkness. The clusters of tiny, intensely blue flowers resemble true forget-me-nots but bloom for a much longer period — from late spring well into summer. Shear plants back by one-third after the first main flush to encourage a second wave of bloom. Plants self-sow freely; allow some seeds to drop for naturalised succession planting in subsequent years.

plant_note: Plant in spring after the last frost, or in fall in zones 8–9; loropetalum establishes quickly in warm soil and rewards with rapid growth during the first growing season with adequate moisture.
directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; loropetalum is one of the most popular landscape shrubs for the Gulf Coast and Southeast, prized for its burgundy to purple-bronze foliage that holds color year-round and its showy fringe-like flowers in spring and sporadically through the year. The purple-leaved forms ('Plum Delight', 'Burgundy', 'Purple Diamond') are especially striking contrasted against green neighbors. It tolerates summer heat and humidity better than most ornamental shrubs. Prune immediately after the main spring flowering to shape; do not shear into tight balls — this destroys the natural graceful arching habit and removes flowering wood. Apply a slow-release, acid-forming fertilizer in early spring.

plant_note: Root softwood cuttings or plant container specimens after all frost risk has passed; in zone 9–10 Chinese hibiscus can be grown as a large, long-lived landscape shrub with winter protection. Mulch heavily around the base in fall in zone 9; established trunks resprout even if top growth is killed.
directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade — Chinese hibiscus is the quintessential tropical flowering shrub, producing the largest and most spectacular flowers of any landscape shrub: blooms 4–8 inches or more across in virtually every color of the rainbow — red, pink, orange, yellow, white, lavender, and complex multi-toned combinations — blooming from spring through frost in zone 9 and nearly year-round in zone 10–12. Flowers last only a single day but the plant produces so many buds that the display is constant. Prune hard in early spring to encourage vigorous new growth and maximum bloom; apply slow-release fertilizer every 4–6 weeks during the growing season. In zone 9, mulch heavily in fall and expect some stem dieback in hard winters, but established plants resprout reliably from the base. Essential for the tropical-theme summer garden in any zone as a magnificent container plant moved outdoors in summer.

plant_note: Chilling seed before sowing improves germination; spreads by runners.
directions: Start seed indoors well before the last frost, ideally after a brief cold period to improve germination. Also called winter cherry, this hardy perennial forms papery orange husks prized for dried arrangements and fall decor. Grow in full sun to part shade with average soil, giving it room since it spreads by underground runners and can be aggressive. Harvest the lanterns once they turn bright orange and hang them to dry.

plant_note: Sow after the last frost in warm soil.
directions: Direct sow Chocolate Cherry sunflowers an inch deep after the last frost, spacing them to allow for their branching habit. Each plant carries multiple five-to-eight-inch blooms in deep burgundy-to-black petals around dark chocolate centers on six-to-seven-foot stems. They make dramatic, pollen-rich cut flowers that keep coming through summer and fall. Cut regularly to encourage more side blooms and leave a few for the pollinators.

plant_note: Direct sow in lean, well-drained soil; drought tolerant once established.
directions: Direct sow in spring in lean, well-drained soil and full sun, where the plants thrive with minimal fuss. Native to the dry southwestern U.S., this hardy heirloom, also called chocolate daisy, gives off a genuine chocolate fragrance strongest in the morning and late afternoon. Plant it along walkways or patios where visitors can catch the scent, and water sparingly once established. Deadhead to encourage repeat bloom through the season.

plant_note: Nick and soak seed before sowing; provide a trellis for the vines.
directions: Nick and soak the seeds, then direct sow after frost at the base of a trellis, arbor, or fence. This Japanese morning glory carries large, four-to-five-inch, rosy-chocolate blooms above attractive green-and-white variegated foliage. Give it full sun and moderate water, and avoid overly rich soil that favors leaves over flowers. Provide sturdy support early since the vines climb quickly in warm weather.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring or fall in a sheltered, shaded spot; tolerates transplanting well.
directions: Set crowns just below soil level in well-drained but moisture-retentive soil amended with leaf mold or compost. This native fern stays evergreen through winter in zones 6 and warmer, making it valuable for year-round ground cover under deciduous trees. Once established it tolerates moderate drought better than most ferns, though it prefers steady moisture through summer. Old fronds flatten to the ground in winter but remain green; remove them in early spring to allow unobstructed emergence of the new season's fronds.

plant_note: Plant container-grown specimens after last frost in well-drained, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; in zone 9 and south, Christmas senna is perennial and shrubby — in zone 8 it often freezes back but regrows from the roots; avoid heavy clay and wet soils.
directions: Christmas senna is a semi-evergreen, fast-growing tropical shrub from the Caribbean and Central America that earns its common name by producing showy clusters of bright canary-yellow flowers from October through December and into winter — blooming abundantly when most other landscape plants have shut down for the season. It is one of the most important larval host plants for cloudless sulphur butterflies and orange-barred sulphur butterflies, with adults laying eggs on the foliage throughout the warm months and caterpillars consuming the leaves until the plant is bare, then quickly regrowing — a cycle that makes Christmas senna an outstanding butterfly garden plant. In zone 9 Gulf Coast gardens, it grows rapidly to form a large, billowing shrub that can be cut back hard in late winter to maintain a manageable size and stimulate vigorous new growth; it tolerates poor, sandy soils and drought once established, making it low-maintenance in suitable climates. Note: it can naturalize and spread by seed in zone 9b–11 — monitor and remove seedlings in ecologically sensitive areas.

plant_note: Plant crown divisions in early spring, keeping the fibrous root ball intact and the crown at soil level; constant moisture is critical during establishment.
directions: Plant in consistently moist to wet, acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; this stately native fern is one of the few that thrives in saturated conditions around pond margins, rain gardens, and boggy areas while also adapting to ordinary woodland garden soil. The erect, fertile fronds with their distinctive cinnamon-colored spore cases emerge in the center of the vase-shaped plant in spring and are the source of the common name. Divide the large, fibrous rootball in early spring every 5–7 years; each division should include multiple crowns and a generous portion of roots. The plant is deer resistant and largely pest-free.

plant_note: Set out nursery plants in spring; roses are grown from grafted or rooted stock, not seed.
directions: Plant this compact shrub rose in spring in a sunny, well-drained bed, spacing plants for good airflow. Cinnamon Hearts forms tidy mounds topped with clusters of fragrant cherry-red blooms from spring into fall. Water deeply and regularly during the first season to establish roots, then keep soil evenly moist. Prune lightly in late winter and remove spent blooms to encourage continued flowering.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring 2–4 weeks before last frost while soil is still cool; succession sow every 3 weeks for extended bloom.
directions: Direct sow on the soil surface or barely cover; seeds germinate best at cool soil temperatures and seedlings dislike transplanting. Thin ruthlessly to 9–12 in because overcrowded plants bloom poorly and are prone to powdery mildew in humid conditions. Clarkia thrives on neglect in poor, well-drained soil — fertilizing too heavily causes lush foliage at the expense of flowers. Plants naturally set seed and die when summer heat arrives; in mild-winter climates, sow in fall for late-winter to spring bloom.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring on moist to wet sandy or loamy soil; clasping coneflower is a prolific self-seeder and will return annually once established in the garden — scatter seed in moist areas after last frost for summer bloom.
directions: Clasping coneflower is a native annual of moist prairies, roadsides, and disturbed fields across the south-central United States, named for its distinctive stem-clasping leaves that wrap around the stalk — it stands apart from other coneflowers by producing a single, upright, daisy-like yellow flower with a long, domed, dark brown cone on each branching stem, giving it a resemblance to a miniature black-eyed Susan on a taller, more upright plant. It is one of the most reliable warm-season annuals for the Gulf Coast and lower South, germinating quickly in warm weather and blooming in as little as 8–10 weeks from seed — its tolerance for summer heat and humidity makes it far more dependable than many other coneflowers in zone 8–9 gardens. In meadow plantings and naturalized areas, it self-seeds prolifically and gradually forms dense colonies that provide continuous summer-to-fall bloom with zero maintenance. Excellent companion for Gulf Coast natives like prairie verbena, standing cypress, and annual gaillardia in dry, sunny native meadow designs.

plant_note: Direct sow at or 2 weeks before last frost; cold stratification of seed for 7 days in the refrigerator improves germination.
directions: Press seed lightly onto the soil surface with barely 1/8 in of cover — light aids germination. Cleome self-sows prolifically and can become weedy; deadhead to limit reseeding if preferred. The tall, airy flower heads open from the base upward over several weeks, providing an extended season of bloom. Handle plants with care as stems bear small spines and the foliage has a musky scent.

plant_note: Plant container specimens in spring or fall in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to moderately acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; cleyera is very cold hardy once established but prefers protection from harsh winter winds and reflected heat from walls. It is a slow-growing, long-lived shrub that rarely needs pruning.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly to moderately acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade — cleyera is one of the most refined and underused evergreen shrubs for southern shade gardens, producing elegant, glossy, dark green leaves with reddish new growth on a naturally pyramidal to oval form. The small, creamy-white, fragrant flowers in spring are followed by dark red to black berries that attract birds; the plant is sacred in Shinto tradition in Japan, where it is planted at shrines. Cleyera is an outstanding foundation plant, informal hedge, or large specimen for the shaded, acidic, humus-rich conditions under tall pines and oaks throughout zone 7–9. It grows more slowly than many companion plants (camellias, gardenias) but eventually becomes a large, refined, long-lived shrub requiring almost no maintenance beyond an occasional light shaping. Excellent companion plant for camellias and azaleas in woodland garden compositions.

plant_note: Plant near a fence, shrub, or trellis in moist to wet, acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; stems scramble and sprawl through surrounding vegetation rather than twining — provide sturdy support or allow it to clamber through a native shrub border.
directions: Climbing aster is the only vining aster native to North America, an extraordinary scrambling perennial from the Gulf Coastal Plain and Florida that clambers 6–15 feet through shrubs and fences to display enormous masses of small, lavender-to-pink flowers with yellow centers in late fall, often blooming well into December in zone 8 and 9 gardens. Because it flowers so late in the season — October through December in the Deep South — climbing aster serves as a critical last nectar source for late-migrating monarchs and queens, sulphur butterflies, and overwintering native bees that remain active through early winter in the Gulf Coast region. Plant it where it can scramble through a wax myrtle, yaupon holly, or native shrub border; its somewhat weedy scrambling stems are hidden by surrounding plants while the spectacular late-autumn bloom is the garden's last big show before frost. Cut back hard in late winter to control spread and encourage vigorous new stems for next season's bloom.

plant_note: Plant in moist to wet, acidic, organically rich soil in partial to full shade near a trellis, fence, or tree; climbing dogbane is a bottomland native that tolerates brief flooding and performs best in the shade of bottomland hardwood forests along Gulf Coast waterways.
directions: Climbing dogbane is a rare native alternative to the widely planted Confederate jasmine (Trachelospermum jasminoides), naturally found in the moist bottomland forests, swamp margins, and stream banks of the Gulf Coast from Virginia to Texas — it is the only species in its genus native to North America. Unlike its Asian relative, climbing dogbane adapts to shaded conditions under bottomland oaks and cypress, making it one of the few woody ornamental vines that will thrive in genuine shade; it produces clusters of small, fragrant, yellowish-white five-petaled flowers in early summer that are visited by native bees and butterflies. The milky latex sap makes it unpalatable to deer, and the plant is host to several specialist milkweed butterflies and moths in the family Apocynaceae. While less commonly available commercially than Confederate jasmine, climbing dogbane is worth seeking out from native plant nurseries for shaded trellises, arbors, and woodland garden structures where a fragrant, native evergreen vine is needed.

plant_note: Direct sow after last frost in moist, rich soil near a trellis or at the margin of a pond, stream, or rain garden; climbing hemp weed is a fast-growing perennial vine in zones 8 and warmer, dies back after hard frost but returns reliably from the roots in Gulf Coast gardens.
directions: Climbing hemp weed is a vigorous native twining vine of the aster family (Asteraceae), native to wetland margins, pond edges, freshwater tidal marshes, and moist thickets across the Gulf Coastal Plain and eastern United States — despite being little-known in cultivation, it is one of the highest-value native vines for late-season pollinators. Dense clusters of small, fluffy white to pinkish flowers cover the vine from late summer through fall and are magnets for migrating monarchs and a remarkable diversity of native specialist bees; it is among the most important native late-season nectar sources in Gulf Coast wetland habitats. The vine climbs and twines quickly in warm seasons, making it useful for rapidly covering trellises, fences, and chain-link at the edges of rain gardens, constructed wetlands, and moist riparian restoration plantings. In zone 9 and south it remains semi-evergreen; in zone 8 it freezes to the ground but returns vigorously from the roots each spring.

plant_note: Plant in spring or fall against a sturdy wall, fence, or large tree it can cling to.
directions: This vigorous woody vine climbs walls and trees by aerial rootlets, covering surfaces with heart-shaped leaves and fragrant, lacy white blooms in late spring. Plant in part shade to full shade in moist, well-drained, humus-rich soil; it also tolerates morning sun with steady moisture. Be patient, as it is slow to establish but grows rapidly once settled, eventually reaching great heights. Provide strong support and prune after flowering only to control size.

plant_note: Plant container-grown palms in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in partial shade to full sun; fishtail palm handles more shade than most palms and performs well under the canopy of larger trees. The berries and fruit are extremely irritating to skin and mucous membranes — wear gloves when handling fallen fruit.
directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile soil in partial shade to full sun; clustering fishtail palm is one of the most distinctive and architecturally unusual of all tropical palms, producing large, bipinnate fronds composed of distinctive irregularly triangular leaflets that look exactly like the tails of fish — a completely different texture from the familiar pinnate or palmate fronds of most palms. The multiple, slender stems grow in tight clusters, producing a graceful, multi-trunked tropical form that is outstanding as a large specimen or informal privacy screen. Each individual stem blooms once (from the top of the trunk down over several years) and then dies, but the cluster as a whole persists indefinitely as new stems are produced from the base. The fruits and fallen berries contain calcium oxalate crystals that cause intense irritation to skin — collect and dispose of fallen fruit promptly, and wear gloves when working near fruiting clusters. An outstanding specimen palm for sheltered zone 9–12 landscapes.

plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs in fall in moist to dry, acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), sandy, well-drained soil in partial shade; coast azalea is one of the most drought-tolerant of all native azaleas once established and thrives in the sandy, acidic coastal plain soils of the Atlantic Seaboard.
directions: Coast azalea is a compact, stoloniferous native azalea of the Atlantic and Gulf Coastal Plain's sandy pine barrens and oak scrublands — producing one of the most intensely fragrant flowers of any native azalea, with white to pale pink blooms that emit a strong, sweet vanilla-clove fragrance in April and May before the leaves fully expand. Unlike most native azaleas that form upright, single-stemmed shrubs, coast azalea spreads slowly by underground stolons (horizontal stems) to form broad, low colonies — a growth habit that makes it one of the finest native azaleas for use as a naturalized groundcover or mass planting under open pinelands and dry oak canopy. Its compact, low stature (rarely over 4 feet) and tolerance of dry, sandy, nutrient-poor acidic soils make it more versatile in the landscape than taller species; it is also more heat-tolerant than northern azaleas and performs reliably in the upper Gulf Coastal Plain. Several hybrid azaleas have been developed from this species for their fragrance and compact habit.

plant_note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in moist, well-drained, highly acidic (pH 4.5–6.0) soil in partial to full shade. Coastal Doghobble is one of the most shade-tolerant native broadleaf evergreen shrubs available for southeastern gardens and can thrive under dense tree canopy where few other shrubs persist.
directions: Coastal Doghobble is a graceful, arching, broadleaf evergreen native shrub, native to moist, acidic, shaded streambanks, ravine slopes, and moist, sandy forest understories of the southeastern coastal plain from Virginia to Florida and west to Mississippi, where its arching stems weave through the understory to form dense, impenetrable thickets — hence the 'doghobble' name, as hunting dogs became entangled in the arching stems. In April and May it produces racemes of small, white, urn-shaped flowers along the undersides of the arching branches, followed by attractive dark green, leathery, lance-shaped leaves that turn burgundy-red to bronze in winter, providing excellent cold-season color in the shade garden. One of the most shade-tolerant native broadleaf evergreen shrubs available, Coastal Doghobble thrives under dense tree canopy where azaleas and camellias struggle; provide consistently moist, acidic soil and avoid alkaline conditions or prolonged drought.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall in moist, acidic, sandy or loamy soil in full to partial sun along the coastal plain; coastal Joe-Pye weed is more compact than inland species and thrives in the moist, acidic soils of Atlantic and Gulf coastal lowlands.
directions: Coastal Joe-Pye weed is the compact, coastal-adapted Joe-Pye species of the Atlantic and Gulf Coastal Plain, typically shorter and more restrained than its inland relatives (hollow Joe-Pye and spotted Joe-Pye) while producing equally beautiful, rounded, domed flower clusters of dusty pink to light purple that smother the plant in late summer and fall. Its compact size (3–5 feet) makes it more appropriate than the 6–8 foot inland species for typical garden borders, perennial beds, and rain gardens where the giant inland Joe-Pye weeds would overwhelm surrounding plants. Like all Joe-Pye weeds, it is a specialist butterfly plant — tiger swallowtails, monarchs, and large skippers congregate on the flower heads in late summer with impressive regularity — and an important late-season nectar source for native bees preparing for winter. It is naturally adapted to the acidic, sandy, moderately moist soils of coastal flatwoods, wet savannas, and streamside thickets of the Gulf Coast Coastal Plain, making it an ideal choice for naturalized woodland-edge gardens in the Deep South.

plant_note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in dry to moderately moist, strongly acidic, sandy, well-drained soil in full sun. Coastal Plain Staggerbush is adapted to the xeric, fire-maintained scrub and flatwood habitats of the Florida peninsula and requires excellent drainage — it will not tolerate clay, wet soils, or alkaline conditions.
directions: Coastal Plain Staggerbush is a native evergreen shrub endemic to the dry, sandy, fire-maintained scrub and flatwood habitats of the Florida peninsula, where its dense, leathery-leaved growth and small white to pink urn-shaped flowers in spring make it a characteristic component of the open, sunny, infertile sand communities dominated by oaks and palmettos. Adapted to the same xeric conditions as Florida Rosemary, Scrub Hickory, and other Florida scrub specialists, Coastal Plain Staggerbush requires excellent drainage and periodic low-intensity fire to maintain the open, competitive-free conditions it requires. Like other Lyonia species, the foliage contains grayanotoxins and is toxic to livestock if consumed. Plant in dry, strongly acidic, sandy, well-drained, infertile soil in full sun — Coastal Plain Staggerbush thrives in the nutrient-poor, fire-managed conditions of its native habitat and requires no supplemental fertilizer or irrigation once established.

plant_note: Take hardwood cuttings 8–12 inches long in late winter before bud break and insert directly into moist soil at the water's edge in full sun; rooting is rapid and reliable. Coastal Plain Willow establishes quickly from cuttings and can also be established by planting dormant rooted stems directly in saturated soil.
directions: Coastal Plain Willow is the most abundant native willow of the coastal plain wetlands, stream banks, tidal margins, and disturbed wet soils from Virginia south through Florida and west to Texas and the Caribbean, forming dense multi-stemmed thickets that provide critical cover and nesting habitat for waterfowl, shorebirds, and muskrats. Like all willows, it produces copious pollen and nectar in early spring that are among the first resources available to emerging queen bumblebees, native sweat bees, and early butterflies; the foliage hosts the caterpillars of Viceroy, Mourning Cloak, and numerous native moths. Propagate easily from hardwood cuttings taken in late winter before bud break, inserting 8–12 inch stems into moist, wet soil in full sun; rooting is rapid, establishment reliable, and fallen branches that contact moist soil root naturally. Coastal Plain Willow spreads aggressively by root suckers in wet areas and is best used in large, naturalized riparian settings where its vigorous growth habit is an asset.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall in dry to average, alkaline to neutral, rocky or sandy, very well-drained soil in full sun; cobaea penstemon is a calcicole (limestone lover) that absolutely requires sharp drainage and does not tolerate clay or wet soils — excellent for rock gardens and dry limestone slopes.
directions: Cobaea penstemon bears the largest flowers of any native American penstemon — its tubular, inflated blooms in shades of lavender to pale purple (occasionally white) with purple guidelines are two inches or more across, giving a tropical richness that seems almost too spectacular for a native of dry limestone prairies. Native to the rocky, alkaline prairies and cedar glades of Oklahoma, Texas, Kansas, and Missouri, it is one of the premier spring-blooming wildflowers of the southern Great Plains, with flower spikes rising from handsome rosettes of glossy, evergreen leaves in April and May. Bumblebees force their way into the large floral tubes to reach nectar, and the plant is an outstanding spring pollinator plant in dry, sunny native gardens. Sharp drainage on limestone or alkaline sandy soil is non-negotiable for this species in cultivation — it thrives in raised beds, rock gardens, and dry stone walls where water runs away quickly; it dies from root rot in typical garden soils with any tendency toward moisture retention.

plant_note: Plant container-grown trees in fall in average, well-drained to moist, adaptable soil in full sun to partial shade; cockspur hawthorn is extremely adaptable to a wide range of soils and moisture conditions and is one of the most drought-tolerant and pollution-tolerant of all native hawthorns.
directions: Cockspur hawthorn is a native small tree or large shrub of the eastern United States named for its long, curved thorns (3–4 inches) that resemble a rooster's spur and made it one of the most effective barrier hedges historically used to create impenetrable property boundaries before barbed wire. Its extremely wide, horizontal branching creates a distinctive, picturesque silhouette that provides year-round interest: white flower clusters in May, glossy red fruits persisting through winter, and spectacular scarlet-to-orange fall foliage color. As a wildlife tree, it is exceptional: the long-persistent red fruits are consumed by cedar waxwings, mockingbirds, robins, and bluebirds through winter when other food is scarce; the dense, thorny structure provides the safest possible nesting habitat for catbirds, thrashers, and wood thrushes. A thornless cultivar ('Inermis') is available for locations where the formidable thorns would be a hazard, providing all the wildlife and ornamental benefits of the species without the thorns.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost in moist seed-starting mix; do not cover seeds as they require light to germinate. Pinch flower spikes as they form to maintain foliage color and density. In zone 9–11 coleus may be grown as a perennial with winter protection.
directions: Start seeds indoors or root cuttings easily in water; coleus is one of the most versatile and vividly colored foliage plants for shade and part-shade gardens, producing spectacular leaves in virtually every color combination imaginable — red, burgundy, orange, yellow, lime, pink, purple, bronze, and intricate multicolored patterns and textures. Modern sun-tolerant hybrids extend its use to full sun, but traditional coleus is happiest with morning sun and afternoon shade or dappled light under trees, where its colors often intensify beautifully. It is a workhorse of summer container gardening and shade bedding. Pinch the small flower spikes that develop in late summer to maintain bushy growth and color; once allowed to flower and set seed, foliage quality declines. In zone 9–11 old plants often survive mild winters and resprout in spring; take cuttings in fall to overwinter indoors in colder zones.

plant_note: Start indoors and surface sow, as light aids germination; transplant to well-drained soil.
directions: A hardy, drought-tolerant perennial yarrow blend with feathery foliage and flat flower clusters in red, rose, pink, yellow, and white. Surface sow seed indoors and press it in without covering, since light aids germination. Transplant to lean, well-drained soil in full sun, where it needs little water once established. Deadhead to prolong bloom, and cut flowers for fresh or dried arrangements.

plant_note: Plant in spring or early fall with plenty of room for the broad pyramidal form.
directions: This iconic conifer forms a stately pyramid of silvery-blue needles, serving as a striking specimen or windbreak. Plant in full sun in deep, moist, well-drained soil and give it space, as mature trees grow large. Water regularly while young to establish deep roots, then it tolerates cold and moderate drought well. Avoid crowding to reduce needle-cast and canker problems, and prune only to remove dead or damaged branches.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification, or cold-stratify seeds indoors for 3–4 weeks before spring sowing.
directions: Sow seeds shallowly on the soil surface and provide 3–4 weeks of cold stratification (fall sowing outdoors achieves this naturally) to break dormancy. Columbines bloom most freely in their second and third years and are prolific self-seeders that will hybridize freely, creating a constantly shifting mix of colors. Leafminer damage creates white tunnels in the foliage but rarely threatens plant health; remove affected leaves and dispose of them. Plants are short-lived perennials (3–5 years) but the colony self-perpetuates readily through self-seeding.

plant_note: Surface sow, as the seed needs light; stock thrives in cool weather.
directions: Start seed indoors and surface sow, since stock germinates best with light, then transplant while the weather is still cool. This blend forms upright, single-stemmed columns of fully double, clove-scented blooms excellent for bouquets and drying. Grow in full sun with rich, well-drained soil and even moisture, and set plants out early because stock dislikes summer heat. It performs especially well in containers and cool spring gardens.

plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring or fall in wet to consistently moist soil; this is one of the few large shrubs that thrives in standing water and wet clay where most plants fail.
directions: Plant in moist to wet, or even periodically flooded soil in full sun to partial shade; buttonbush is a native shrub of swamps, pond margins, and floodplains throughout eastern North America, producing creamy white spherical flower heads resembling pincushions in July and August that are among the most intensively visited flowers by butterflies, native bees, and hummingbirds. The round seed heads provide food for waterfowl and songbirds through winter. It is one of the premier plants for rain gardens, bioswales, and pond edges where periodic flooding occurs. Prune in late winter to shape and stimulate new flowering wood; it can be cut back hard if it becomes too large.

plant_note: Fall sow or early spring; biennial that blooms in its second year.
directions: Surface sow common evening primrose in fall or early spring, pressing the seed in lightly since it needs light to germinate. This hardy native biennial forms a low rosette its first year, then sends up tall stems of fragrant yellow flowers that open at dusk the second season. It thrives in poor, dry soil and tolerates neglect, making it ideal for tough spots and pollinator plantings. Let it self-sow to keep the two-year cycle going in the garden.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost on the surface of moist seed-starting mix (light-dependent germination); or purchase transplants. Avoid downy mildew (Plasmopara obducens) by purchasing disease-resistant varieties such as 'Beacon' or 'Compact Power' series and avoiding overhead irrigation.
directions: Plant transplants in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; common impatiens is the world's best-selling bedding plant for shaded areas, producing a continuous flush of flowers from planting until frost without deadheading. Available in red, pink, orange, white, salmon, coral, lavender, and bicolor, it provides reliable, long-season color in the shade garden. A downy mildew outbreak in 2012–2013 devastated impatiens plantings across North America; resistant varieties developed since then should always be chosen over traditional types. Keep soil consistently moist — impatiens wilts dramatically in drought and does not recover well from repeated dry-out. Avoid overhead watering to reduce disease. In zone 9–11 it may overwinter and continue blooming through mild winters; elsewhere treat as an annual.

plant_note: Direct sow seeds on bare soil in fall; seeds require cold stratification to germinate well — fall sowing lets winter temperatures do this naturally; thin to 24-inch spacing in spring once seedlings reach 3–4 inches tall.
directions: Common ironweed is a tough, adaptable native perennial found naturally in moist prairies, roadsides, and streambank edges across the central and eastern United States — its compact size (3–5 feet) compared to tall ironweed makes it more useful in typical garden settings while delivering the same brilliant purple pollinator impact. Dense, flat-topped clusters of vivid purple flowers open from midsummer into fall, overlapping with many other prairie perennials and providing critical late-season nectar for migrating monarchs, swallowtails, and native bumblebees. Plants spread slowly by rhizomes and self-seeding, eventually forming clumps that can be divided every 3–4 years to maintain vigor and share plants. Combine with yellow-flowered natives like sneezeweed, black-eyed Susan, and prairie coneflower for maximum visual impact — the complementary warm yellow and cool purple combination is a classic of native meadow plantings.

plant_note: Fall sow or cold-stratify 30 days before spring planting.
directions: Sow common milkweed in fall or cold-stratify the seed for a month before spring planting to break dormancy. This vigorous native perennial grows two to three feet tall with broad leaves and fragrant clusters of pink star-shaped flowers that are vital to Monarch butterflies. It spreads by rhizomes and can colonize, so site it where it has room to naturalize. Give it full sun and average soil, and it will host caterpillars and feed countless pollinators.

plant_note: Fall sow or cold-stratify seed for several weeks before spring sowing.
directions: Sow in fall or cold-stratify the seed for several weeks before spring sowing to break dormancy. This native heirloom is an essential host plant for monarch caterpillars and offers fragrant pink to purplish or white summer flowers followed by decorative seedpods. Grow in full sun in average soil, giving it room since it spreads by rhizomes. Leave some pods to mature for reseeding and to support the monarch lifecycle.

plant_note: Sow directly outdoors once soil has warmed and frost danger has passed.
directions: This native wild sunflower is a vigorous, widely branching annual that can top ten feet, so give it room and a sunny, open spot. Sow seeds an inch deep directly in warm garden soil after the last frost, thinning seedlings to prevent crowding. The branching habit produces many golden-yellow blooms that feed bees and later provide seed for birds. Stake tall plants in windy sites and water steadily until they are well established.

plant_note: Sow fresh seed in fall for natural stratification; seed requires 60-90 days cold-moist stratification before germination. Direct sow 1/4 inch deep where plants are to grow permanently — compass plant has a deep taproot and transplants very poorly.
directions: Sow seed directly in its permanent location — deeply and well-drained, full sun; compass plant is a dramatic prairie giant, eventually producing 6–10 foot flower stalks lined with sunflower-like yellow flowers from July through September in mature plants. The deeply cut, pinnate basal leaves are oriented north-south on the plant (hence the common name), a feature used by prairie travelers for navigation. The deep taproot (reaching 15+ feet in mature plants) makes it essentially indestructible to drought and allows it to colonize harsh, rocky, and dry sites. Plants are extremely slow to bloom from seed — typically 3–5 years — but then persist for decades without any care. A cornerstone statement plant for native meadow gardens and prairie restorations.

plant_note: Cold-stratify seeds for 60–90 days or direct sow in fall; requires 2–3 years from seed to first bloom. Deep taproot makes transplanting of established plants impossible.
directions: Sow cold-stratified seed in deeply prepared, well-drained soil in full sun and be patient — the compass plant spends its first 2–3 years building a massive taproot that may reach 14 feet deep before producing its first flower stalk. Once established it is virtually drought-proof and may live for decades. The deeply lobed basal leaves align their edges north-south to minimize midday sun exposure, giving the plant its common name and serving as a primitive compass for prairie travelers. The tall, yellow composite flower stalks in midsummer provide pollen for native bees; goldfinches harvest the seeds in fall. Never transplant established plants.

plant_note: Plant container-grown plants or rooted cuttings in spring or fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moist to moderately dry soil in full sun to partial shade; confederate jasmine is adaptable to a wide range of soil conditions and thrives in both sun and partial shade. Train onto a trellis or fence early — without support it sprawls as a mounding groundcover.
directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; confederate jasmine (not a true jasmine but a related genus) is one of the most beloved and widely planted fragrant vines in Gulf Coast landscapes, producing masses of small, pinwheel-shaped, pure-white flowers with an intoxicating, sweet-spicy fragrance that perfumes an entire garden in spring bloom. Glossy, dark-green, evergreen leaves on twining stems provide year-round foliage interest. It is remarkably adaptable — trained on trellises and arbors as a vertical element, allowed to sprawl as a groundcover, used as a sprawling shrub, or pruned as a low hedge. In zone 8–11 it is fully evergreen and essentially indestructible once established, tolerating heat, drought, poor soil, and salt spray. Prune back after flowering to keep it tidy and encourage dense regrowth. One of the most reliable and rewarding plants in the Gulf Coast garden.

plant_note: Plant rooted cuttings or container plants in spring after frost danger passes in moist, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; confederate rose grows rapidly in warm weather and can reach 10–15 feet in a single season from a cutting. It dies back to the ground after hard freezes in zone 7–8 but regrows vigorously from the roots.
directions: Plant in moist, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; confederate rose is one of the most spectacular autumn-blooming flowering shrubs of the American South, producing enormous (4–6 inch), double, peony-like flowers that open white in the morning and gradually deepen through pink to rose and finally deep red by evening — the color change of a single flower over one day is one of the most remarkable visual effects in horticulture. Single-flowered forms also exist. Blooming from late summer through frost, confederate rose provides critical fall color just when other shrubs are fading. The plant grows very large — a true shrub-tree — and propagates effortlessly from cuttings, which root in a cup of water on the windowsill. It was passed from garden to garden across the antebellum South this way for generations. Prune to 2–3 feet in late winter; the plant regrows vigorously each season and blooms on new wood.

plant_note: Sow in darkness; violas are frost tolerant and thrive in cool weather.
directions: Start seed indoors and cover it, as violas germinate best in darkness, then set out the frost-tolerant plants early. This blend blooms reliably in cool weather and does best with afternoon shade to prolong flowering during warm spells. Keep soil evenly moist and deadhead to encourage continued bloom. Violas often reseed, popping up in unexpected corners the following season.

plant_note: Plant container-grown plants in spring in sandy, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; purchase only nursery-propagated plants. The starchy trunk contains cycasin toxins and was traditionally processed through extensive washing before use as food starch.
directions: Plant in sandy, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; coontie is the only cycad native to continental North America and the only host plant for the spectacular Atala butterfly (Eumaeus atala), a striking Caribbean species with iridescent blue-green wings that was once thought extinct in Florida before recovering after landscape plantings of coontie increased. This prehistoric-looking plant with stiff, dark green pinnate fronds emerges almost stemless from a large underground trunk and is extraordinarily drought, heat, and salt-spray tolerant once established in zone 8–11. The large red seeds are produced in dramatic cones and are toxic; plant away from areas accessible to children and pets. Plant coontie specifically to support Atala butterfly populations in South Florida.

common_name: Copper Canyon Daisy (Mountain Marigold, Lemmon's Marigold)
plant_note: Root tip cuttings easily in spring or plant container-grown transplants in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to alkaline soil in full sun; copper canyon daisy is native to Copper Canyon (Barranca del Cobre) in Mexico and is drought tolerant once established. Prune by one-third after bloom flushes to maintain compact shape.
directions: Plant in well-drained, lean soil in full sun; copper canyon daisy is one of the finest large perennial flowering shrubs for zone 8–11 gardens — a sprawling, aromatic shrub covered in masses of small, bright golden-yellow daisy flowers in fall and winter (and sometimes spring) when very little else is blooming. The deeply divided, fernlike leaves are pungently aromatic (a strong citrus-mint-marigold scent that is powerfully deer repellent) and retain an attractive gray-green color through much of the year. It is outstanding for fall color, butterfly attraction, and combining with blue salvias or purple ageratum for a stunning gold-and-purple fall combination. Prune back by one-third after each bloom flush to keep the plant compact; an unpruned shrub can spread to 6 feet or more. In zone 8, top growth may be killed by hard freezes but roots resprout reliably.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions in late summer to early fall in moist to wet, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; fall planting allows roots to establish before winter.
directions: Plant rhizome divisions in moist to wet, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; copper iris is the only native American iris with brick-red to burnt-orange-copper flowers — a unique and striking color that no other wild iris can match. It grows naturally in swamps, bayou margins, and bottomland forests of the lower Mississippi Valley and Gulf Coast, where it tolerates periodic inundation. The plant is one of the parent species of the spectacular Louisiana iris hybrids and contributes their warm red-copper-orange color range to hybrid cultivars. Plant at pond margins, in consistently wet rain garden areas, or in soil that stays moist throughout the growing season. It spreads by rhizome into colonies and can be divided in late summer to expand plantings.

plant_note: Plant bulbs 2–3 inches deep in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, loamy to sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; copper lily is native to the dry, grassy prairies and roadsides of the Gulf Coast and performs best with a summer dry period followed by rainfall to trigger flowering.
directions: Plant bulbs 2–3 inches deep in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade — copper lily is a charming, small-bulbed rain lily native to the coastal prairies and grassy roadsides of southeastern Texas and Louisiana, producing star-shaped, copper-pink to rosy-pink flowers on short scapes in late summer and fall following rain events. Like all rain lilies, the flowers appear seemingly overnight after summer storms or irrigation, lasting 2–3 days before fading, with successive flushes following each rain. The narrow, rush-like leaves are nearly evergreen in zone 9–10 and die back in hard freezes. It naturalizes freely in well-drained, open soils, spreading by both offsets and seed to form dense, floriferous colonies in lawn-edge plantings, rock gardens, and sunny to lightly shaded perennial borders. An outstanding low-maintenance bulb for the Gulf Coast that requires no irrigation once established in well-adapted soils.

plant_note: Plant rooted cuttings or container plants in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; copper plant establishes quickly in warm weather and roots readily from stem cuttings. Pinch growing tips regularly during establishment to encourage a bushy, compact habit.
directions: Plant in moist, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; copper plant is one of the most spectacularly colorful tropical foliage plants for zone 9–12 landscapes, with large, heart-shaped leaves in a remarkable range of copper, red, orange, bronze, green, and bicolored combinations — the foliage color is as vivid and jewel-like as any flower. The hundreds of named cultivars include types with solid copper-red leaves, mottled green-and-red, white-edged, and brilliantly painted multicolor forms. It grows rapidly into a large, bold shrub and is used extensively as a colorful screening plant, hedge, or specimen in tropical and subtropical landscapes. The best foliage color develops in bright light or partial shade — deep shade reduces color intensity. Regular fertilization during the growing season is important for maintaining vigorous growth and best color. In zone 9 it may suffer frost damage in cold winters but typically regrows from the base; in zone 10–12 it is evergreen and persistent.

plant_note: Plant in spring or fall in a sheltered site with protection from hot afternoon sun.
directions: Prized for its glowing coral-red winter bark, this Japanese maple also offers soft green spring foliage that shifts to golden-yellow in fall. Plant in part shade with rich, moist, well-drained soil, protecting it from drying wind and intense afternoon sun that can scorch leaves. Keep the root zone mulched and evenly moist. Prune minimally in late winter to reveal the colorful stems and maintain the graceful branching form.

plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring in well-drained, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; in zone 8 the stems freeze to the ground in hard winters but resprout vigorously from the deep, heat-tolerant root system each spring.
directions: Plant in well-drained, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; coral bean is one of the most striking native shrubs of the Gulf Coast and Southeast, producing brilliant scarlet tubular flowers in long spikes in spring before the leaves emerge — they are among the most important hummingbird nectar plants in the Southeast. In fall the pods split open to reveal vivid coral-red seeds that are highly ornamental but extremely toxic if swallowed. The stems bear recurved spines that deter browsing; the plant dies to the ground in hard freezes in zone 7–8 but regrows rapidly from an established root crown. It tolerates partial shade, sandy soil, drought, and salt spray — making it an outstanding native plant for difficult spots in Gulf Coast gardens. The red seeds were traditionally used as ornamentation and have important Native American cultural uses.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; surface sow as seeds need light to germinate — do not cover.
directions: Press seeds onto the surface of a moist germination medium — do not cover, as light is required for germination; transplant to a humus-rich, well-drained site after last frost. Grown primarily for their spectacular mound-forming foliage in shades of burgundy, copper, lime, silver, and purple, heucheras also produce airy wands of tiny flowers beloved by hummingbirds. Divide every 3–4 years in early spring to rejuvenate the woody crowns; reset divisions with the crown at soil level. Most modern hybrids tolerate more sun with adequate moisture but prefer afternoon shade in hot climates.

plant_note: Plant rooted cuttings or container plants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; coral fountain establishes quickly and roots readily from stem cuttings. It is outstanding cascading over walls, planted at the top of raised beds, or in hanging baskets where the arching stems can drape downward.
directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; coral fountain (firecracker plant) is a uniquely textural and continuously blooming tropical perennial with masses of thin, arching, rush-like green stems cascading in a weeping habit, studded nearly year-round with small, tubular, coral-red flowers that hummingbirds visit with enthusiasm. The stems have virtually no leaves — the photosynthesis occurs in the green stems themselves, giving the plant an unusual, airy, almost fern-like texture when not in bloom that is strikingly different from any other flowering plant. It is exceptionally drought, heat, and salt tolerant once established and performs at its best cascading over retaining walls, spilling from large containers, or planted in raised beds where the arching, weeping habit shows to best effect. In zone 9 it may suffer frost damage to stems but typically regrows from established roots. A yellow-flowered form is also available. One of the most reliable and continuously blooming hummingbird plants for Gulf Coast gardens.

plant_note: Surface sow into warm soil, as the fine seed needs light to germinate.
directions: Surface sow into warm soil after frost, since the small seed needs light to germinate. Coral Fountain produces dramatic pendulous tassels in shades of coral that dry beautifully for everlasting arrangements, wreaths, and crafts. Grow in full sun with average soil and moderate water for the fullest tassels. Harvest stems as the flowers reach full color and hang them to dry.

plant_note: Plant container-grown vines in spring near a trellis, fence, or other support; stems twine and will climb 10–15 feet.
directions: Plant in average, well-drained soil at the base of a trellis or fence; unlike invasive Japanese honeysuckle this native species is well-behaved and will not escape into natural areas. The long, tubular scarlet-to-coral flowers bloom heavily in spring and continue sporadically through fall, providing the long-tube nectar that ruby-throated hummingbirds require. Prune in late winter or just after the main spring flush to shape the vine and remove dead wood. In the deep South it is semi-evergreen and may retain foliage through mild winters; in colder zones it loses leaves but regrows vigorously from the base in spring.

plant_note: Plant tubers or container-grown plants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to alkaline, lean to moderately fertile soil in full sun; coral vine is fast-growing and can cover a fence or trellis in a single season. The tuberous roots store carbohydrates and allow rapid regrowth from the base after frost damage in zone 7–8.
directions: Plant tubers in well-drained soil in full sun on a fence, trellis, or arbor; coral vine is one of the most beloved and reliable vining plants of the Gulf South — a fast-growing, drought-tolerant vine from Mexico that covers itself in sprays of small heart-shaped pink to magenta flowers in summer through frost, creating a solid curtain of pink that is irresistible to bees, particularly bumble bees and honeybees. The vine climbs by tendrils and can reach 20–30 feet when established. In zone 7–9 the tops die back in hard frost but established tuberous roots resprout vigorously each spring, often growing 10–15 feet in a single season. The flowers and tubers are edible (tubers roasted like sweet potatoes in Mexico). A white-flowered form ('Album') is less vigorous but charming. A fixture in traditional Gulf South gardens for generations.

plant_note: Direct sow into warm soil after all danger of frost has passed.
directions: Direct sow into warm soil after frost, or start indoors a few weeks earlier for a head start. Coral Zinnia carries four-to-five-inch blooms in sun-kissed coral on sturdy stems that are ideal for cutting and beloved by butterflies. Space plants for good airflow and water at the base to limit powdery mildew. Cut or deadhead often to keep the plants blooming until frost.

plant_note: Plant container shrubs or rooted suckers in spring in any soil in partial to full shade; coralberry tolerates dry shade better than almost any other native shrub, making it useful under mature trees where most plants fail.
directions: Plant in any well-drained soil — including dry, rocky, and infertile — in partial to full shade; coralberry is one of the most shade-tolerant and drought-tolerant of all native shrubs, growing naturally on dry limestone bluffs, rocky woodland slopes, and under dense tree canopy where little else survives. The small pinkish flowers in early summer attract native bees; the small coral-pink to deep red berry-like drupes that follow in fall persist into winter and provide food for bobwhite, wild turkey, mockingbirds, and other birds. The plant spreads by root suckers to form dense, erosion-controlling colonies — excellent for dry, difficult slopes under trees. The cultivar 'Hancock' is particularly dense and ground-hugging. All parts mildly toxic if ingested in quantity.

plant_note: Plant tubers after frost in warm, well-drained soil and stake tall plants.
directions: Coralie blends the most perfect blush and pink shades, pairing beautifully with other soft-toned flowers in the cutting garden. Plant tubers 4 to 6 inches deep in full sun and fertile, well-drained soil after the ground warms. Because the plant grows tall, provide sturdy staking or support unless you are cutting frequently. Pinch early for branching, cut often to keep blooms coming, and lift tubers to store indoors over winter in cold zones.

plant_note: Direct sow around last frost date; seeds tolerate light frost and germinate best in cool soil.
directions: Sow seed on the soil surface and tamp lightly; good drainage is essential since coreopsis rots in wet soil over winter. Deadhead regularly to keep the bright yellow flowers coming all summer. Shear plants back by half after the first flush to encourage compact regrowth and a second bloom. Divide clumps every 2–3 years in spring to prevent the center from dying out.

plant_note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in wet to moist, slightly acidic, sandy or loamy soil in partial shade to full sun. Corkwood is a rare species that should only be sourced from specialty native plant nurseries; it is listed as threatened or of conservation concern in most states where it occurs.
directions: Corkwood is one of the rarest and most botanically unusual native shrubs in North America — a monotypic genus (Leitneriaceae family) with no close relatives among living plants, native to the tidal swamps, brackish marshes, and wet bottomlands of scattered, disjunct localities in Georgia, Florida, Texas, Arkansas, and Missouri, where it forms thickets in periodically flooded, organic soils. Its most remarkable characteristic is its wood, which is lighter than cork — making it the least dense wood of any known North American woody plant — and the plant occupies a phylogenetically isolated position in the evolutionary tree of flowering plants. Catkin-like male and female flowers appear on separate plants in spring before the leaves emerge. Despite its rarity, Corkwood is adaptable to a range of wet to moist soil conditions in partial shade to full sun and is occasionally available from specialty native plant nurseries; plant male and female specimens together for seed production. Listed as threatened in several states and on NatureServe's watch list.

plant_note: Plant tubers after frost in warm, well-drained soil.
directions: Cornel Bronze is a burnt-orange ball dahlia related to Cornel, sharing its prolific bloom, excellent tuber production, and tidy plant habit. Plant tubers 4 to 6 inches deep in full sun and rich, well-drained soil after the last frost. Pinch young plants for bushier growth and harvest the round, tightly petaled blooms frequently for the vase. Lift and store the abundant tubers indoors over winter in regions colder than Zone 8.

plant_note: Plant tubers after frost in warm, well-drained soil.
directions: Cornel is the classic burnt-red ball dahlia, a shorter, sturdy plant that often needs no staking and blooms prolifically. Plant tubers 4 to 6 inches deep in full sun and fertile, well-drained soil once the soil has warmed. Pinch the growing tips to encourage branching and cut the tightly formed blooms often for long-lasting arrangements. In cold-winter regions, dig and store the tubers, which this variety produces reliably, after the first frost.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost; give the long-season vines a sturdy trellis.
directions: Direct sow after frost in rich soil and full sun, giving the vigorous vines a strong trellis to climb. Corsican produces round, hard-shelled gourds about six to ten inches across that cure to last indefinitely and can be carved, painted, or drilled for crafts. Provide a long, warm growing season and steady moisture while the fruit develops. Harvest before hard frost and cure the gourds in a dry, airy spot for several weeks.

plant_note: Direct sow after last frost; cosmos thrives in poor soil and needs no fertilizing.
directions: Press seed lightly onto the soil surface and cover with 1/4 in of fine soil. Cosmos germinates best in poor, well-drained soil — avoid fertilizing, which promotes foliage over flowers. Pinch young plants at 12 in to encourage branching. Deadhead regularly or allow seed to set for self-sowing the following year.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; costus is one of the most shade-tolerant of all ornamental gingers and grows naturally as a forest understory plant in tropical Asia. In zone 8–9 the tops die back in frost but roots resprout reliably from established clumps.
directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; spiral ginger is one of the most structurally unusual and striking plants for the tropical shade garden — the broad, elliptical leaves are arranged in a tight spiral up the stem (giving it the name 'stepladder plant'), and the plant culminates in a dense, pinecone-like terminal inflorescence of overlapping red bracts from which large, white, orchid-like flowers emerge one at a time over several weeks. The architectural form of the spiraling stems creates a dramatic, bold presence in the shaded garden. It is one of the best shade-tolerant tropicals for Gulf Coast gardens and makes an excellent companion for heliconias, anthuriums, and alocasias in the tropical shade garden. Cut old stems to the ground after flowering; new stems arise from the base.

plant_note: Direct sow or transplant in fall in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry to moderately moist, loamy to rocky soil in full sun; cottage pinks require excellent drainage and will not survive in wet or heavy clay soils. Avoid overhead watering to prevent crown rot.
directions: Direct sow or transplant in fall in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun — cottage pinks are the classic old-fashioned garden pinks of European cottage gardens, producing intensely fragrant, fringed (hence 'plumarius' — feathery), five-petaled flowers in shades of white, pink, rose, crimson, and bicolors in May–June on a low, spreading mat of glaucous blue-green, grass-like foliage that remains attractive year-round. The rich, spicy-sweet, clove-like fragrance is extraordinary — unmatched by modern hybrid dianthus — and one of the defining scents of the traditional cottage garden. They require excellent drainage and perform best in slightly alkaline soils, making them excellent choices for rock gardens, dry stone walls, gravel gardens, and raised beds with sharply drained soil. Deadhead spent flowers to extend bloom; shear plants by one-third after flowering to maintain a tidy, compact mound.

plant_note: Start indoors early; tolerant of coastal conditions and needs no cold period.
directions: Start seed indoors several weeks before the last frost and transplant after the soil warms. This blend produces pastel, papery blooms on stems up to eighteen inches long that are prized for fresh and dried arrangements. It grows well in coastal areas and needs no cool period to flower. Harvest stems when the blooms are fully open and hang them upside down to dry for lasting color.

plant_note: Direct sow seed in spring in well-drained, alkaline to neutral, dry to moderately moist, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; cowpen daisy thrives in disturbed, poor soils and hot, dry conditions that defeat many garden plants — excellent for waste areas, roadsides, and dry hillsides.
directions: Direct sow in spring in well-drained, alkaline to neutral, dry, rocky or sandy soil in full sun — cowpen daisy is a bold, fast-growing native annual of prairies, roadsides, and dry disturbed areas throughout the southern US and Central America, producing masses of bright yellow, 2-inch daisies with a yellow central disk from mid-summer through frost on coarse, silvery-white-leaved, branching plants. It grows in some of the most challenging conditions encountered by any garden plant: caliche, dry rocky limestone, alkaline clay, and hot, exposed roadsides, where it seeds into cracks and disturbed patches and blooms prodigiously with zero irrigation. The flowers are reliably visited by monarch butterflies during fall migration and by native bees. An excellent, self-sowing annual for difficult dry, sunny spots in Gulf Coast and Texas gardens where more refined plants would fail. The seeds are an important winter food for mourning doves and other seed-eating birds.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost, or start indoors a few weeks early for earlier bloom.
directions: Direct sow after frost or start indoors a few weeks early for a jump on the season. Crackerjack is a superior tall blend with a mounding form and sturdy stalks bearing four-inch blooms in tangerine orange, rich gold, and lemony yellow. Grow in full sun with average, well-drained soil and moderate water. Deadhead spent blooms to keep the plants tidy and flowering into fall.

plant_note: Start indoors a few weeks before last frost or sow directly once soil warms.
directions: Crackerjack is a tall African marigold prized for large, showy double pompons in gold, orange, and yellow on sturdy stems. Start seeds indoors for a head start or sow directly after frost danger passes, barely covering them. Plant in full sun where the strong scent helps deter garden pests, making it a classic vegetable-garden companion. Deadhead spent blooms to keep flowers coming all summer and pinch young plants to encourage branching.

plant_note: Plant only nursery-propagated corms in fall in moist, well-drained, acidic, humus-rich, undisturbed soil in full to partial shade under mature deciduous hardwoods; crane-fly orchid requires an undisturbed, leaf-littered woodland floor with appropriate mycorrhizal fungi.
directions: Crane-fly orchid is one of the most ecologically fascinating native orchids of eastern North American forests — a shade-dwelling terrestrial orchid that shows a remarkable seasonal reversal: a single, attractively mottled, purple-green leaf emerges in fall, remains through winter as a solitary, easy-to-overlook woodland groundcover, then withers and disappears in late spring as the leafless flowering stalk emerges in midsummer bearing 20–40 tiny, translucent, crane-fly-shaped flowers that give the species its name. This reversed phenology — leafy in winter, leafless when flowering in summer — is unique among North American orchids and perfectly adapted to deciduous forest light patterns: the leaf photosynthesizes during winter when canopy trees are leafless, storing energy in the underground corm for summer flowering. Nocturnal moths are the primary pollinators, attracted by a faint fragrance at night. A subtle, quiet orchid of the undisturbed forest floor that rewards patient observers; best appreciated in its natural woodland setting rather than cultivated, though nursery-propagated corms are occasionally available from specialist orchid growers.

plant_note: Direct sow seed in fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; cream violet is one of the most adaptable native violets for the shaded garden, tolerating a range of moisture conditions and significant shade.
directions: Sow seed in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade — cream violet is a native woodland violet of eastern deciduous forests, distinguished by its cream to white flowers with distinctive purple veining (striping) on the lower three petals that gives it both the species name striata and the common name striped cream violet. Unlike the stemless violets (V. sororia, V. cucullata), cream violet is leafy-stemmed, growing from branched stems rather than a basal rosette, which gives it a somewhat different growth form in the woodland garden. It blooms in spring and often reblooms again in fall. Grows naturally in moist, rich woodland, stream banks, and moist forest clearings throughout the eastern US. Forms attractive groundcover colonies in shaded settings. Like all native violets, it supports native fritillary butterfly caterpillars as a larval host plant. An excellent white-flowered native violet alternative to the more common blue species.

plant_note: Plant rooted divisions or stem sections in spring or fall in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; creeping jenny roots readily from stem segments and establishes quickly — it can spread aggressively in consistently moist conditions. The golden cultivar 'Aurea' is slightly less vigorous.
directions: Plant in moist to wet, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; creeping jenny is a low-growing mat-forming perennial with round, bright green (or golden in the cultivar 'Aurea') coin-shaped leaves that hug the ground on trailing stems — the common name moneywort refers to the round leaf shape. Small, bright buttercup-yellow flowers appear along the stems in early summer. It is exceptionally useful as a living mulch in moist shaded sites: under trees, along shaded stream banks and pond margins, in rain gardens, and in container arrangements where it trails beautifully over the edge. The golden cultivar 'Aurea' is one of the brightest foliage plants for deep shade, glowing even in heavily shaded corners. In consistently moist conditions it spreads vigorously — trim back as needed to control. Outstanding in hanging baskets and window boxes where the trailing stems show their full effect.

plant_note: Plant divisions or plugs in spring in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; do not confuse with moss phlox (Phlox subulata), which requires full sun — this species is the shade-tolerant woodland species.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; creeping phlox is the shade-tolerant native phlox species of the Southern Appalachian woodland floor, quite different from the sun-loving moss phlox (Phlox subulata) that cascades over walls in full sun. It produces fragrant lavender, pink, or blue-violet flowers in early spring just as trilliums and spring ephemerals are blooming, spreading by stolons to form a low, attractive mat of semi-evergreen foliage that brightens shaded areas year-round. It performs best in the cool, moist, shaded conditions of a woodland garden and is not suited to zone 9 and warmer. Named cultivars like 'Blue Ridge' (blue-violet) and 'Sherwood Purple' (vivid purple) are widely available from native plant nurseries.

plant_note: Plant corms in spring around last frost; in zones 6–7 apply a thick mulch in fall to protect overwintering corms.
directions: Plant corms 3–4 in deep in groups of 6 or more for the best visual impact; crocosmia forms clumps through underground stolons. Arching sprays of scarlet, orange, or yellow tubular flowers appear from midsummer into fall and are excellent for cutting. Where hardy, divide congested clumps every 3–4 years in early spring to refresh bloom; in zone 5 and colder, lift and store over winter like gladiolus. 'Lucifer' is among the hardiest cultivars, tolerating zone 5 with reliable mulching.

plant_note: Plant corms in fall, 6–10 weeks before the ground freezes, for late-winter to early-spring bloom.
directions: Plant corms pointed side up at a depth of 3–4 in in well-drained soil; they naturalize quickly and look best planted in large, informal drifts. Squirrels frequently dig freshly planted corms, so covering the bed with wire mesh until the ground freezes can protect plantings. Foliage is narrow and grassy; allow it to die back completely before removing. Species crocus tend to be more reliably perennial than large-flowered Dutch types.

plant_note: Plant container vines near a sturdy support structure in spring; crossvine will attach to brick, wood, or bark by tendrils and does not damage surfaces the way some ivies do. Blooms most heavily in sun but performs well in shade.
directions: Plant in well-drained, moist to moderately dry soil in full sun to full shade; crossvine is a vigorous native semi-evergreen climbing vine of the Southeast with spectacular trumpet-shaped orange-red flowers with yellow throats in early spring that are among the first abundant nectar sources for hummingbirds returning from migration. The common name comes from the cross-shaped pith visible in cross-section of the stem. It climbs by branched tendrils and clings securely to brick, masonry, bark, and fences without causing damage. Unlike many vines, it blooms well even in moderate to deep shade and is one of the best native choices for covering a shaded fence or wall. The foliage is semi-evergreen to evergreen in zone 8–9 and takes on bronze-purple winter color. Prune after bloom to control size.

plant_note: Surface sow the tiny seed indoors early, as it needs light to germinate.
directions: Surface sow the fine seed indoors several weeks before the last frost, since it needs light to germinate. Crystal Palace forms compact mounds of rich, dark-blue flowers with bronze-tipped foliage that deepens in full sun, lovely in containers, borders, and baskets. Keep it evenly moist and provide afternoon shade where summers are hot. A light shearing midseason refreshes the plant and renews bloom.

plant_note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped trees in late winter or spring in moist, slightly acidic, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; cucumber magnolia is the most cold-hardy of the deciduous magnolias and tolerates more cold than virtually any other Magnolia species.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade — cucumber magnolia is the largest and most cold-hardy native magnolia of eastern North America and the only magnolia native to the Great Lakes region and far north, surviving to zone 3. The greenish-yellow flowers (2–4 inches) in spring are not the showiest, but the distinctive cucumber-shaped bright red-pink fruiting cones in summer that split to reveal brilliant orange-red seeds are highly ornamental. The tree develops an impressive pyramidal to broadly rounded form 50–80 feet tall — one of the largest deciduous trees in the native flora. Crucially, it is a parent in the development of numerous hybrid 'yellow magnolias' such as 'Elizabeth' (with Magnolia denudata), which have transformed the magnolia world. The tree produces a yellow pigment in the wood historically used by Native Americans. An excellent choice for large-scale landscape planting and ecological restoration in zone 3–8.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; cold stratification for 4–6 weeks improves germination rates.
directions: Sow seeds indoors after cold stratification or plant divisions in spring in moist to moderately moist, organically rich soil; culver's root is an imposing native prairie plant with whorled, lance-shaped leaves and tall candelabra-like spikes of white to pale lavender flowers in mid- to late summer. The architectural form makes it a striking back-of-border plant, and the flowers attract a wide variety of native bees and butterflies. Rarely needs staking when grown in adequate light; divide every 4–5 years in spring to maintain vigor. Excellent companion for joe-pye weed, prairie blazing star, and native grasses.

plant_note: Cold-stratify seed for 60 days before spring sowing, or direct sow in fall for natural stratification; plants bloom in the second year from seed.
directions: Sow stratified seed or transplant into deep, fertile, moist soil in full sun; the distinctive paired leaves wrap the square stem to form a cup that collects rainwater — providing a drinking and bathing site for birds. Tall yellow composite flowers bloom from midsummer through fall and are important sources of pollen and nectar for native bees, including specialist prairie bees. The large stature (6–10 feet) requires placement at the back of the border or in a wildflower meadow. Divide established clumps in spring every 4–5 years; the plant spreads by self-seeding in congenial conditions and can naturalize aggressively. Goldfinches harvest the seeds in fall.

plant_note: Start indoors 8 weeks early; sow seed on edge.
directions: Start cup and saucer vine indoors eight weeks before the last frost, sowing the flat seeds on edge to prevent rot. This vigorous climber uses tendrils to scale trellises and can reach twenty feet in a single season. The large bell-shaped flowers open greenish and mature to deep purple, resembling a cup on a leafy saucer. Transplant after frost into full sun and rich, moist soil, providing sturdy support.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after last frost into a dry, sunny site.
directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost and transplant to a sunny, very well-drained site — catananche is native to dry Mediterranean hillsides and rots in moist soil. The papery, cornflower-blue blooms on wiry stems are excellent cut or dried flowers that hold their color well. Shear plants by half after the first flush in early summer to encourage repeat flowering into fall. Best treated as a short-lived perennial; replace plants every 2–3 years or allow to self-sow for a continuing colony.

common_name: Curcuma (Siam Tulip, Hidden Lily, Thai Tulip)
plant_note: Plant rhizomes 2 inches deep in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade; curcumas emerge from dormancy late (May–June), so patience is required. In zone 8–9 mulch heavily in fall and the rhizomes typically overwinter successfully in the ground.
directions: Plant rhizomes 2 inches deep in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; Curcuma alismatifolia (Siam tulip) is one of the most striking ornamental gingers, producing elegant upright flower spikes in late summer with a cone of showy bracts in pink, purple, or white, resembling a large tulip — hence the name. The broad, tropical foliage and exotic inflorescences make curcumas outstanding cut flowers and exceptional accent plants in the shaded summer border. They emerge late in spring from dormancy (don't panic and dig them — wait until June), grow and flower through summer, then die back to the ground in fall. The ornamental Curcumas differ from culinary turmeric (C. longa) but the two are closely related; several curcuma species have both edible and ornamental value.

plant_note: Direct sow into warm soil after frost for fast, easy germination.
directions: Direct sow into warm soil after frost, or start indoors for an earlier display. As the name promises, cutting these old-fashioned two-to-two-and-a-half-inch blooms makes the plants flower more, producing continuously until fall frost. Space for airflow and water at the base to limit powdery mildew. Cut stems often to fill bouquets and keep the plants covered in blooms.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall or early spring in moist to wet, rich soil in partial shade to full sun; cutleaf coneflower spreads aggressively by rhizomes in moist conditions and should be sited where it has room to naturalize — divide clumps every 2–3 years.
directions: Cutleaf coneflower is the tallest of all North American coneflowers — commonly reaching 6–8 feet or more in moist, rich soil — producing long-stalked, deeply lobed leaves with a coarse, tropical texture and airy sprays of bright yellow, drooping-petaled flowers with a distinctive greenish-yellow dome from July through September. Unlike most other coneflowers that require full sun and dry to average soils, cutleaf coneflower thrives in partial shade in moist bottomland conditions, making it invaluable for the sunny-to-shaded edges of rain gardens, stream banks, and moist woodland borders where a tall, dramatic native perennial is needed. The double-flowered heirloom cultivar 'Hortensia' (Golden Glow) has been grown in American gardens since the 1890s and still persists in old homesteads across the South, where it spreads freely by rhizomes through shaded gardens. It spreads vigorously and can be invasive in small spaces — site it where it can naturalize freely in large native beds or along stream margins.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained woodland soil in deep shade under deciduous trees. Cutleaf Toothwort goes completely dormant by late spring — mark its location to avoid disturbing the dormant rhizomes during summer garden work.
