directions: Cutleaf Toothwort is a native spring ephemeral of moist, rich, deciduous woodlands across eastern North America, producing clusters of small white to pale pink flowers on 6–15 inch stems in early to mid-spring, shortly before the forest canopy closes and blocks the light that spring ephemerals depend on. The deeply cut, three-parted leaves with their toothlike lobes give the plant its common name and are an identification feature distinguishing it from the similar Slender Toothwort; the leaves appear in early spring, the plant flowers, sets seed, and retreats entirely underground by early summer, remaining dormant until the following winter. A member of the mustard family, the rhizomes have a mild, peppery, horseradish-like flavor and were eaten raw by various Native American peoples. Plant rhizome divisions in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral woodland soil in deep shade; Cutleaf Toothwort self-seeds freely in hospitable woodland gardens and gradually colonizes the forest floor.

plant_note: Direct sow fresh seeds in fall in dry, rocky, sandy, or gravelly, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; seeds cold-stratify naturally over winter and germinate in spring. Cylindrical Blazingstar is among the most drought-tolerant of all blazingstars and specifically adapted to calcareous or gravelly prairie and alvar soils.
directions: Cylindrical Blazingstar is a compact, drought-tolerant native perennial of dry prairies, rocky outcrops, calcareous alvars, and gravelly open woodlands of the Great Lakes region and Midwest, producing spikes of few, widely spaced, cylindrical purple flower heads on stiff, slender stems in July and August that are prized for cut flowers and dried arrangements as well as for their wildlife value. Notably more drought-tolerant than most blazingstars and specifically adapted to thin, rocky, calcareous or gravelly soils of limestone outcrops and dry prairie remnants, Cylindrical Blazingstar is an outstanding choice for rock gardens, xeriscapes, and dry native plant gardens across the northern United States. The compact size and long vase life of the cut flowers make it one of the most popular Liatris species among native plant enthusiasts and cut flower gardeners. Direct sow fresh seeds in fall in rocky, sandy, or gravelly, well-drained, neutral to slightly acidic soil in full sun; established corms are extremely long-lived and slowly form clumps.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall, 4–8 weeks before the ground freezes, for spring bloom.
directions: Plant bulbs in fall at a depth of 3 times the bulb's height (typically 6–8 in) in well-drained soil with the pointed end up. Daffodils naturalize readily and multiply over time; deer and rodents avoid them because the bulbs are mildly toxic. Allow foliage to yellow and die back naturally — at least 6 weeks — so the bulb can store energy for next year's bloom. Divide congested clumps every 5–7 years when flowering diminishes.

plant_note: Plant tubers after the last frost date when soil has warmed to at least 60°F.
directions: Plant tubers horizontally 4–6 in deep with the eye pointing up after the last frost when soil reaches 60°F; set a stake at planting time to avoid spearing the tuber later. Pinch the growing tip when plants are 12 in tall to encourage branching and more flowers. In zones below 8, dig tubers after the first killing frost, cure them for a week, then store in slightly damp peat at 40–50°F through winter. Disbudding side buds around the main bud produces exhibition-quality blooms.

plant_note: Plant container-grown specimens in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic, sandy to loamy soil in partial shade to full sun; dahoon holly grows naturally in swamps, stream banks, and wet flatwoods and tolerates standing water and high water tables better than any other native holly except possumhaw. Plant male and female plants for berry production.
directions: Plant in moist to wet, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun — dahoon holly is one of the most beautiful and ecologically important native trees of the Gulf Coast and Southeast, growing naturally in the understory of bottomland swamps, cypress heads, wet pine flatwoods, and stream banks from Virginia to Texas. Female plants (a male nearby is needed for pollination) produce abundant clusters of small, brilliant red to orange-red berries that persist from fall through winter, providing critical food for robins, cedar waxwings, bluebirds, and dozens of other fruit-eating birds during winter when food is scarce. The narrow, elliptic, dark evergreen leaves are attractive year-round. It tolerates flooding and wet soils better than almost any other tree in cultivation and is outstanding for rain gardens, wet low spots, pond margins, and other challenging wet sites in zone 7–11. An excellent winter-interest tree for the shaded, wet areas of Gulf Coast landscapes.

common_name: Dainty Red and Yellow Lady's Purse
plant_note: Surface sow the fine seed, which needs light; keep cool and evenly moist.
directions: Surface sow the very fine seed indoors, since it needs light and cool conditions to germinate well. This compact, bushy plant produces one-to-two-inch inflated, slipper-shaped blooms in sunny yellow marked with striking red blotches. It performs beautifully cascading from containers, hanging baskets, and garden edges. Keep it consistently moist, give it part shade in warm climates, and protect it from intense heat.

plant_note: Surface sow the fine seed, which needs light; keep cool and evenly moist.
directions: Surface sow the tiny seed indoors, giving it light and cool temperatures for reliable germination. This charming, compact plant bears one-to-two-inch slipper-shaped blooms in sunny yellow dusted with delicate red spots for a hand-painted effect. It shines in containers, hanging baskets, and along garden edges. Keep the soil evenly moist and provide part shade and protection from heat in warm regions.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions 1–2 inches deep in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade; globba emerges from dormancy in late spring and blooms through summer and fall. In zone 8–9, mulch rhizomes heavily before the first frost to protect from freeze.
directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; dancing ladies ginger is one of the most delightful and enchanting of all the ornamental gingers, producing pendant clusters of small mauve-purple flowers with reflexed wings and a long, curved, yellow lip that resembles tiny ballerinas or dancing ladies when the breeze sets them in motion. The nodding flower clusters 2–4 inches long appear on arching stems from summer through fall. It is one of the most shade-tolerant of all the ornamental gingers and is an excellent candidate for bringing color to shaded tropical gardens from summer through fall. In zone 8–9 it often produces small bulbils (little bulb-like structures) in the leaf axils that can be separated and planted to propagate new plants. Combine with ferns, caladiums, and impatiens in the shaded border.

plant_note: Direct sow, as this ornamental carrot resents transplanting.
directions: Direct sow where plants are to grow, since this ornamental carrot dislikes root disturbance. Also called false Queen Anne's lace, Dara adds soft, airy, lacy umbels to the garden and to fresh or dried arrangements without the aggressiveness of the true species. It is fairly drought tolerant once established, so water moderately. Cut stems when the umbels are freshly open for the longest vase life.

plant_note: Plant a young tree in spring after the ground has thawed, in deep moist soil with room to grow large.
directions: Dawn redwood is a fast-growing deciduous conifer often called a living fossil, prized for feathery fern-like foliage that turns coppery orange before dropping in fall. Give it full sun and consistently moist, well-drained soil; it tolerates wet sites and even seasonal flooding better than most conifers. Choose a location with abundant vertical and horizontal space, as mature trees reach 70 to 100 feet with a broad conical form. Water deeply during establishment and through dry spells for the first few seasons. It needs no pruning to develop its naturally elegant shape.

plant_note: Plant tubers after all danger of frost has passed and soil has warmed.
directions: Day Dreamer is a waterlily-form dahlia bearing soft blooms blending apricot, pink, and buttercream on productive plants. Plant tubers horizontally about 4 to 6 inches deep once soil warms, positioning the eye upward, and hold off watering until sprouts emerge to prevent rot. Pinch the growing tip when plants reach about a foot to encourage bushier growth and more blooms. Stake or corral tall stems and cut flowers regularly to keep the plant producing. In zones colder than 8, lift and store the tubers in a cool, dry place over winter.

plant_note: Plant bare-root divisions in spring or fall; set crowns just 1 in below soil level.
directions: Plant fans with the crown about 1 in deep, spreading roots outward over a small mound of soil in the hole. Water thoroughly after planting and keep consistently moist the first season. Divide overcrowded clumps every 4–5 years in early spring or late summer when bloom quality declines. Removing spent scapes after all buds have opened keeps the planting tidy.

plant_note: Cold-stratify seed for a few weeks to improve germination of this perennial.
directions: Cold-stratify the seed for a few weeks and start indoors, since this perennial germinates better after a chill. Dazzler Blend suits any garden and especially rarely watered spots, offering colorful, pollinator-friendly blooms and excellent drought tolerance once established. Grow it in full sun with lean, sharply drained soil, making it a fine choice for rock gardens. Deadhead to tidy plants and encourage additional bloom.

plant_note: Plant corms in fall in mild climates (zones 7–10) or in early spring after danger of hard frost has passed in colder zones.
directions: Soak the small, irregular corms in water for 4–6 hours before planting; set them about 2 in deep with the pointed side down (or on their side if unsure). Anemones prefer cool, well-drained soil and perform best where winters are mild and springs are long and cool. Poppy-like blooms in vivid red, blue, purple, white, and bicolors appear over a 6–8 week period in late winter to spring. In cold climates treat as an annual, planting corms in spring for early summer color.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring or fall in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; not suitable for zone 9 and warmer where summer heat causes crown rot.
directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; dead nettle is one of the most effective and ornamental shade groundcovers for zones 3–8, valued for its silvery-marked, heart-shaped leaves that brighten dark corners throughout the growing season and its whorls of pink or white flowers in late spring and early summer. It is a rapid spreader that forms a dense, weed-suppressing carpet and is easy to propagate by stem cuttings or division. Unlike the weedy annual red dead nettle (L. purpureum), this perennial species is non-invasive and stays where it is planted. Named cultivars like 'White Nancy' (white flowers, heavily silver leaves), 'Beacon Silver', and 'Purple Dragon' are widely available. In the South, plant in a cool, shaded spot and provide afternoon shade from heat; it goes dormant and may die back in high summer in zone 8.

plant_note: Start indoors for earlier bloom; tubers can be lifted and stored over winter.
directions: Start seed indoors about a month before the last frost and transplant into rich, well-drained soil once weather settles. This very tall blend delivers eye-popping colors on long, sturdy stems, blooming about 90 days from sowing and returning year after year in mild zones. Stake the tall plants and plant them toward the back of the border. Dig and store the tubers over winter in colder climates to replant the following spring.

plant_note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring in moist, acidic (pH 4.5–6.0), humus-rich, well-drained soil in full shade; deer fern requires strongly acidic soil and consistent moisture — it declines in neutral or alkaline conditions. It is best suited to the Pacific Coast, Pacific Northwest, and mild Atlantic coastal gardens with cool summers.
directions: Plant in moist, strongly acidic, humus-rich soil in full shade; deer fern is one of the most distinctive of all woodland ferns, producing two very different types of fronds simultaneously from the central crown: outer, spreading, dark-green sterile fronds that lie nearly flat to the ground, and inner, erect, narrowly pinnate fertile fronds that stand upright with narrow, blade-like pinnae bearing the spore-producing sori on their undersides. This dramatic dimorphism makes it immediately recognizable in the forest. It is a characteristic plant of the cool, moist, acidic coniferous and mixed forests of the Pacific Northwest (where it is extremely common) and also occurs in Atlantic coastal forests and in Europe, where it was traditionally fed to deer (hence the common name). It requires cool, humid conditions and consistently moist, strongly acidic soil to thrive — it is less suited to hot, dry interior gardens. An outstanding structural fern for the cool, shaded woodland garden.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost; fresh seed germinates best and benefits from 1–2 weeks of cold stratification.
directions: Use fresh seed — viability drops quickly in storage — and cold-stratify for 1–2 weeks before sowing at 60–70°F for best germination. Transplant after the last frost into rich, well-drained, slightly alkaline soil and stake early, as the tall spikes are highly susceptible to wind damage. Cut flower spikes back to basal foliage after the first bloom to encourage a second, smaller flush in late summer. All parts of the plant are toxic; all plant contact should be followed by hand washing.

plant_note: Plant divisions in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in deep shade; delta maidenhair requires consistently moist (never waterlogged) soil, high humidity, and shelter from wind and afternoon sun — dry air, cold drafts, and bright direct light will cause fronds to brown and die back rapidly.
directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial to deep shade with high humidity — delta maidenhair is the most widely grown maidenhair fern, celebrated for its extraordinary, lacey delicacy: tiny, fan-shaped, bright green leaflets arranged in sprays on impossibly fine, wiry black stems that seem to float the foliage in mid-air. The fronds tremble with the slightest air movement, giving the plant an ethereal, shimmering quality. It demands consistent moisture, high humidity, and shelter from drying winds — the most common failure is under-watering or low humidity, which causes instant browning. Outdoors it is suitable only in zone 10–12 in sheltered, shaded, humid locations; in zone 8–9 it is an outstanding houseplant and covered-patio container plant. The classic indoor maidenhair; cultivars like 'Fragrantissimum', 'Fritz Lüthii', and 'Brilliant Else' are among the most refined forms. Mist frequently, water consistently, and keep away from heating vents and air conditioning.

plant_note: Fall sowing provides the cold stratification the seed needs; spring sowing also works after chilling.
directions: This native prairie perennial sends up tall, upright spikes of vivid purple bottlebrush flowers that bloom from the top down. Seeds germinate best after a cold period, so sow outdoors in fall or cold-stratify before spring sowing. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil, where the plants are drought tolerant once established and popular with butterflies. The long-lasting spikes make excellent fresh or dried cut flowers.

plant_note: Plant container-grown plants or rooted cuttings in spring in any very well-drained, alkaline to neutral, lean soil in full sun; desert honeysuckle requires excellent drainage and lean soil — rich, moist soil causes rank growth and reduced flowering. Cut back hard to 12 inches in late winter to encourage compact, dense growth and maximum summer bloom.
directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; desert honeysuckle (hummingbird bush) is a native Southwestern shrub with an extraordinarily long season of tubular, orange-red flowers that are among the most powerfully attractive hummingbird plants in the xeric and arid Southwest. Blooming from summer through fall with each successive flowering flush triggered by summer monsoon rains, the plant produces dense masses of 1.5-inch, orange-red tubular flowers that provide rich nectar for migrating and resident hummingbirds. It is extremely drought tolerant and actually declines in fertility and blooming if given rich soil or excess water — thriving in the hard, dry, alkaline conditions of the Southwest and Edwards Plateau. Cut back hard each late winter before new growth begins. The species A. wrightii (flame anisacanthus, Wright's desert honeysuckle) has similar requirements and is equally outstanding as a hummingbird plant; both are outstanding native choices for xeric wildflower and butterfly gardens.

common_name: Desert Willow (Flor de Mimbre, Willowleaf Catalpa)
plant_note: Plant container-grown trees in spring in very well-drained, sandy or rocky, alkaline to neutral, lean soil in full sun; desert willow is native to dry arroyos, stream banks, and desert washes of the Chihuahuan and Sonoran Deserts and is extremely drought tolerant — it actually performs better with no supplemental irrigation after the first year.
directions: Plant in well-drained, lean, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; desert willow is one of the most beautiful flowering native trees of the Southwest and South Texas, producing masses of showy, orchid-like flowers in pink, lavender, rose, white, or bicolor from late spring through fall in multiple flushes — a spectacular hummingbird and butterfly plant. Despite its name it is not a willow at all but rather a member of the Bignonia family, closely related to catalpa. The long, willow-like leaves emerge late in spring and drop early in fall. It is remarkably drought tolerant once established, thriving on natural rainfall alone in zone 7–11. The decorative seed pods hang on through winter. Many named cultivars are available with different flower colors and forms; 'Dark Storm,' 'Bubba,' and 'Art's Seedless' are popular selections for southern gardens.

plant_note: Plant divisions or potted plants in spring in rich, moist soil.
directions: Deutschland astilbe is a shade-loving perennial that sends up feathery white plumes above glossy green foliage in early summer. Plant it in partial to full shade with consistently moist, humus-rich soil, as it dislikes drying out. Work compost into the planting hole and mulch to conserve moisture around its shallow roots. Divide clumps every three to four years in spring to maintain vigor. Deadheading is optional; the dried plumes add winter interest, and the flowers attract pollinators.

plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring in any moist to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; devil's walking stick spreads by suckering roots and can colonize large areas over time — site it where spreading is welcome or in contained plantings.
directions: Plant in moist to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; devil's walking stick is one of the most architecturally dramatic native plants in eastern North America — a large, coarse shrub or small tree with massive bipinnate-to-tripinnate compound leaves (sometimes 3–4 feet long) arranged in a palm-like whorl at the top of unbranched, viciously prickle-armed stems. The effect in the landscape is aggressively tropical and primitive — unlike anything else in the native plant palette. In late summer, enormous flat-topped clusters of tiny white flowers (12–18 inches across) provide a spectacular nectar feast for dozens of native bee and butterfly species. The dark purple berries in fall are consumed by more than 100 bird species during fall migration. It suckers to form dense colonies in moist woodland edges — give it space or contain it.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost into lean soil and full sun.
directions: Direct sow after the last frost in lean soil and full sun, avoiding rich ground that reduces flowering. Diablo carries vibrant, bold blossoms on tall stems from midsummer until first fall frost, feeding bees, hummingbirds, and butterflies. Once established it is drought tolerant and low maintenance, so water sparingly. Deadhead or cut for bouquets to keep the flowers coming, and let a few set seed to reseed.

plant_note: Plant tubers after frost; lift and store where not hardy.
directions: Plant dinnerplate dahlia tubers after the last frost, laying them horizontally about six inches deep with the eye facing up. These giant blooms can reach ten to twelve inches across and require staking at planting to support the tall stems. Grow in full sun and rich, well-drained soil, watering deeply once shoots emerge and pinching to promote branching. In cold climates lift and store the tubers in a frost-free place over winter.

plant_note: Plant tubers after frost danger passes and soil warms.
directions: Diva is a tall, prolific dahlia producing rich plum blooms with exceptional vase life for a large flower. Set tubers about 4 to 6 inches deep in full sun once soil is warm, and wait to water heavily until growth appears. Because plants grow very tall, install sturdy stakes or a support cage early in the season. Cut or deadhead spent flowers frequently to prolong blooming into fall. Lift and store tubers over winter where the ground freezes.

plant_note: Plant by direct seed or division in spring in any sunny, well-drained soil; dog fennel is a robust, fast-growing perennial throughout the Gulf Coast and effectively self-seeds to return annually even where it is treated as an annual in colder zones — cut back hard in winter.
directions: Dog fennel is a ubiquitous native perennial of roadsides, old fields, and disturbed open areas throughout the Southeast, instantly recognizable by its thread-fine, feathery, intensely aromatic foliage — the camphor-like scent has historically made it a natural insect repellent, and bundles were placed in farm buildings and bedding to repel fleas and lice. Despite its weedy reputation in some circles, dog fennel is an ecologically important native: the clouds of tiny white flowers produced on 3–6 foot wiry stems in late fall are among the final nectar sources of the season for migrating monarchs and late-flying native bees in the Gulf Coast region, often blooming well into November in zone 9. The fine-textured, billowing foliage provides striking textural contrast in the naturalistic garden and is used by some designers as a feathery filler among coarser-leaved perennials. Cut back to a few inches in late winter; it regrows rapidly from the base and will reach full height by midsummer even in the Gulf South's hot climate.

plant_note: Surface sow indoors, as the seed needs light to germinate.
directions: Surface sow the seed indoors several weeks before the last frost, since it needs light to sprout, and transplant after frost. Dondo White forms billowy clusters of fluffy, pure-white blooms on upright plants reaching twenty-four to thirty inches, adding vertical interest and texture to borders and containers. It flowers continuously from summer through frost. Grow in full sun with even moisture and deadhead to prolong the display.

plant_note: Surface sow into warm soil, as the fine seed needs light to germinate.
directions: Surface sow into warm soil after frost, since the fine seed needs light to germinate. These low-growing plants form a carpet of brilliantly colored, rose-like double flowers that open on sunny days through the heat of summer. Very drought tolerant, moss rose needs only occasional watering and thrives in dry banks and hot, reflected-heat spots. Give it full sun and sharply drained soil for the best flowering.

plant_note: Often grown as a biennial; sow in spring or summer for bloom the next year.
directions: Sow in spring or summer, since this short-lived perennial is usually grown as a biennial that blooms its second year. Double Blend forms large clusters of edible, double flowers in many colors that rank among the longest-lasting cut flowers. In the garden the sweet, spicy-scented blooms draw butterflies and bees, and the mild clove-flavored petals garnish salads. Grow in full sun to part shade with well-drained soil and even moisture.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost into lean soil and full sun.
directions: Direct sow after frost in average-to-lean soil and full sun, since rich ground limits flowering. Double Click produces a profusion of mostly double, some semi-double, chrysanthemum-like blooms in pink, white, and carmine red. Once established it is easy to care for and drought tolerant, so water sparingly. Deadhead or cut for bouquets to prolong bloom, and let a few flowers set seed to reseed.

plant_note: Set out nursery plants in spring; grown from grafted or rooted stock, not seed.
directions: Plant this low-maintenance shrub rose in spring in full sun and well-drained soil, spacing plants for airflow. Double Knock Out blooms from spring to fall with two layers of fiery red petals and strong disease resistance. Water deeply during the first season to establish, then keep the soil evenly moist. It needs little pruning, though a hard cut in late winter keeps the shrub vigorous and shapely.

plant_note: Set out the shrub after danger of hard frost; it is a woody deciduous shrub, not typically grown from seed for this cultivar.
directions: Plant this double-flowered althea in full sun with well-drained soil, spacing shrubs 5-8 feet apart to allow their broad upright habit room to mature. It blooms late summer into fall on the current season's growth, so prune in late winter or early spring to shape and encourage flowering wood. Water regularly the first two seasons until the roots establish, after which it tolerates heat and short dry spells. The full, layered pink blooms are sterile, so this cultivar reseeds far less than single-flowered forms.

plant_note: Sow around the last frost, barely covering seed since light aids germination.
directions: Direct sow or start indoors, pressing the seed onto the surface since light improves germination. This carefree perennial produces semi-double and double golden-yellow blooms from summer until frost and thrives in full sun even in heat, humidity, and poor or dry soil. Deadhead spent flowers to prolong the display and prevent excessive self-seeding. Once established it is quite drought tolerant and needs only occasional watering.

plant_note: Start indoors early; surface-sow since light is needed to germinate, and set out hardened plants around last frost.
directions: Start seed indoors 8-10 weeks before the last frost, pressing seed onto the mix surface as light is required for germination. This variety carries delicate 1.5-inch bicolor blooms in soft pink and white that resemble tiny apple blossoms on heavily branched, wind-resistant stems. Snapdragons prefer cool weather, so transplant early once seedlings are hardened, and pinch young plants to encourage more flowering stems. Regular deadheading and cutting keeps the plants producing long, upright spikes for bouquets.

plant_note: Surface-sow indoors early and set out hardened transplants near last frost for cool-season bloom.
directions: Sow indoors 8-10 weeks ahead of the last frost, leaving seed uncovered since light triggers germination. This F1 hybrid bears luminous 1.5-inch open-faced double blooms that shift from peach in cool light to buttery yellow in full sun, on strong branched stems reaching 20-24 inches. Pinch seedlings when a few inches tall to build bushy, floriferous plants and transplant while weather is still cool. Cut and deadhead frequently to keep the wind-resistant stems flowering through the season.

plant_note: Root softwood cuttings in moist potting mix or plant container specimens after last frost; downy jasmine grows rapidly in zone 9–11 and can be trained as a sprawling groundcover, a low hedge, or a soft mounding shrub depending on how it is pruned.
directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade — downy jasmine is an Indian native jasmine widely grown along the Gulf Coast for its masses of small, starry, pure white flowers with a light, sweet fragrance produced from fall through spring during the cooler months. The soft, downy-hairy leaves and stems give it an appealing, fine-textured, gray-green appearance when out of bloom. It is a sprawling, loose shrub that can be used as a large-scale groundcover, a slope stabilizer, or a mounding informal hedge; with light pruning after bloom it stays tidy and compact. Unlike many jasmines, it tolerates partial shade and performs excellently along the shaded sides of buildings and fences. In zone 9 the foliage may drop or be damaged in hard freezes but the plant resprouts quickly from roots; in zone 10–11 it is fully evergreen. Extremely drought tolerant once established, requiring virtually no supplemental irrigation in adapted soils.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before the last frost date; surface-sow on moist medium as seeds need light to germinate. Transplant to moist, slightly acidic soil in partial shade after frost danger has passed. Downy Lobelia re-seeds freely in favorable conditions.
directions: Downy Lobelia is a native perennial wildflower of the southeastern United States, native to moist woodlands, woodland edges, streambanks, and open meadows from New Jersey south to Florida and west to Texas, blooming from late summer to fall with dense spikes of bright blue-violet flowers that are excellent for attracting hummingbirds and bumblebees. Similar in appearance to the more widely cultivated Cardinal Flower (Lobelia cardinalis) and Great Blue Lobelia (Lobelia siphilitica), Downy Lobelia is distinguished by its downy-hairy stems and leaves and its preference for somewhat drier, better-drained soils than its relatives. A robust, short-lived perennial that perpetuates itself by self-seeding in the garden, it is a valuable late-season pollinator plant for naturalistic borders and woodland gardens in zones 5–9. Provide partial shade, moderately moist and slightly acidic soil, and allow seed heads to mature and disperse for natural re-seeding.

plant_note: Plant only nursery-propagated specimens in fall in moist, acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich, well-drained woodland soil in full to partial shade under conifer or hardwood canopy; rattlesnake plantain requires an undisturbed, leaf-littered woodland floor with appropriate mycorrhizal support.
directions: Downy rattlesnake plantain is among the most attractive native orchids for year-round woodland interest — its distinctive rosettes of dark green leaves with a striking network of silver-white veins create a bold, graphic pattern reminiscent of a rattlesnake's scale markings that makes each plant an eye-catching feature of the forest floor throughout the year, regardless of the brief summer bloom. An evergreen, low-growing orchid of the leaf litter under eastern hemlock, pine, and mixed hardwood forests across a broad range from Canada to the Gulf Coastal Plain, it is one of the most shade-tolerant of all native orchids and thrives in the darkest parts of mature forest understory. In summer, a fuzzy, upright flower spike produces small, white, globular flowers pollinated by bumblebees; the spike can reach 16 inches tall and is ornamental in its own right. The striking foliage pattern was historically associated with snakebite medicine by some indigenous peoples — hence the 'rattlesnake plantain' name — though there is no medicinal efficacy. Purchase only nursery-propagated plants; wild plants depend on mycorrhizal fungi and rarely survive transplanting.

plant_note: Direct sow seed in fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; downy yellow violet is the primary yellow-flowered violet of eastern woodland understories and naturalizes readily by self-seeding in appropriate conditions.
directions: Sow seed in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade — downy yellow violet is the most common and widespread yellow-flowered native violet of eastern deciduous woodlands, producing bright yellow, purple-veined five-petaled flowers on hairy stems 6–14 inches tall in early to mid-spring, simultaneously with the emergence of the trout lilies and trilliums in the same woodland community. The broad, heart-shaped, softly hairy leaves (giving the common names 'downy' and 'hairy') are attractive throughout the growing season. It grows naturally in rich, moist, deciduous hardwood forest from Canada to the Deep South. Like all violets, downy yellow violet is a critical larval host plant for native fritillary butterfly caterpillars (Speyeria and Boloria species) — the presence of violets in a garden directly supports breeding populations of these spectacular native butterflies. Self-sows modestly to naturalize. Outstanding native for spring woodland wildflower gardens.

plant_note: Plant in spring with the crown a couple inches below the soil surface.
directions: Dr. Ruppel is a vigorous large-flowered clematis with pink, star-shaped blooms accented by a bold magenta bar down each petal. Give it a sunny position for the vines but keep the roots cool and shaded with mulch or low companion plantings. Provide a trellis, arbor, or fence for the climbing stems to grip, and tie in new growth as needed. This variety blooms on both old and new wood, so prune only lightly in late winter to shape and remove dead stems. Water regularly and feed in spring for the best flower show.

plant_note: Plant in fall or early spring in acidic, well-drained soil.
directions: Dream Weaver is a sasanqua camellia bearing abundant double blooms of deep pink to lavender edged in white, flowering from fall into winter. Plant it in partial shade in moist, acidic, well-drained soil enriched with organic matter, and mulch to keep roots cool. Protect from harsh afternoon sun and drying winds, especially in warmer zones. Prune lightly right after flowering to shape the evergreen form. Feed with an acid-loving-plant fertilizer in spring for lush foliage and reliable bloom.

plant_note: Plant container shrubs in spring or fall in moist, acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; the plant naturalizes by suckering to form attractive colonies along stream banks and shaded slopes.
directions: Plant in consistently moist, deeply acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; drooping leucothoe is a graceful native evergreen shrub of mountain stream banks and ravines in the Southern Appalachians, with long arching stems clothed in lustrous lance-shaped leaves that turn bronzy-burgundy-purple in winter — one of the finest winter foliage colors in the shade garden. Tiny white bell-shaped flowers in May are fragrant and nectar-rich for early bees. The plant spreads by stolons to form a layered, naturalistic colony that suppresses weeds effectively. The cultivar 'Rainbow' has cream-pink-green variegated foliage; 'Girard's Rainbow' and 'Zeblid' (Scarletta) are also widely available. All parts are toxic. Excellent massed under rhododendrons, azaleas, or as an understory layer beneath oaks.

plant_note: Direct sow after the last frost once soil has warmed.
directions: Direct sow after frost about an inch deep in full sun, thinning to 12-18 inches apart. This branching mix delivers 4-5 inch florist-quality blooms in deep burgundy, valentine red, and pumpkin-orange-red shades on strong stems ideal for cutting. Sow successively every couple weeks for continuous bouquets through the season. Water evenly while young and stake in windy sites, since heavy branching heads can lean.

plant_note: Seeds need darkness to germinate; cover completely.
directions: Cover the seed completely because Drummond phlox requires darkness to germinate. Direct sow in early spring in mild climates or start indoors six weeks before the last frost. The clustered flowers come in a wide range of colors and make excellent low bedding and cut arrangements. Deadhead regularly and shear back leggy plants in midsummer to encourage a fresh flush of bloom.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall; naturalizes and self-sows.
directions: Plant drumstick allium bulbs in fall about four inches deep in well-drained soil and full sun. The egg-shaped flower heads emerge green and mature to deep burgundy on wiry stems in early summer, weaving beautifully through borders. Bees and butterflies love the blooms, and the seed heads dry well for arrangements. Keep the site on the dry side in summer and the bulbs will naturalize and self-sow over time.

plant_note: Start indoors and set out after frost; grown as an annual in cold climates.
directions: Start seed indoors 6-8 weeks before the last frost and transplant to full sun once weather settles. This florist favorite produces cheerful golden globe blooms on tall wiry stems that are prized in fresh and dried arrangements. The tough plants tolerate drought, heat, and heavy or poor soil, so avoid overwatering. Harvest stems when the orbs are fully colored and hang upside down to dry for long-lasting decor.

plant_note: Plant in spring in moist soil with room for a large shade tree.
directions: Dura Heat is a river birch selection with creamy exfoliating bark and strong tolerance of summer heat and humidity. Plant it in full sun to part shade in moist, slightly acidic soil; it thrives in wet spots where other trees struggle. Water deeply during establishment and dry spells to prevent leaf drop. Its resistance to bronze birch borer makes it a durable landscape and shade tree. Prune only in summer or fall, since birches bleed heavily if cut in late winter or early spring.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost risk passes.
directions: Surface-sow tiny seeds on moist germination mix and provide light at 65–75°F; germination is slow and uneven over 10–14 days. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil; excellent salt and drought tolerance makes this ideal for coastal and hot dry sites. The silvery white, felted foliage is the ornamental feature — pinch off yellow flower buds when they appear to keep plants neat and maintain vigorous leaf growth. In zones 8–11 dusty miller overwinters as a short-lived perennial.

plant_note: Plant corms in fall in drifts for best effect.
directions: Plant Dutch crocus corms in fall three to four inches deep in well-drained soil, setting them in generous drifts. The large goblet-shaped flowers in purple, white, and yellow open in early spring for bees emerging from winter. Grow in full sun to light shade and avoid summer irrigation while corms are dormant. Let the grassy foliage ripen fully, and the corms will multiply into showy colonies over time.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall, 4–6 in deep; in zones 5–6 apply a winter mulch for protection.
directions: Plant bulbs 4–6 in deep in well-drained, moderately fertile soil; Dutch iris bloom in late spring to early summer and are superb cut flowers with a vase life of 7–10 days. The large falls and standards come in shades of blue, purple, yellow, white, and bicolor. After bloom, allow foliage to die back naturally; in warm, humid climates the bulbs may rot in summer — lift and store dry in a cool place if this occurs. Replant in early fall and incorporate grit into heavy clay soils to improve drainage.

plant_note: Plant small, grain-like tubers 1–2 inches deep in fall in groups of at least 12 for effect; they naturally self-sow after flowering to build colonies over several years.
directions: Plant tiny tubers shallowly in humus-rich, moist, well-drained soil in full to dappled shade under deciduous trees; this charming spring ephemeral blooms for only 2–3 weeks in early April with distinctive white pantaloon-shaped flowers dangling from arching stems above ferny blue-green foliage. The plant then goes completely dormant by late spring, disappearing entirely until the following year. Plant in large drifts of at least 25–50 tubers for a meaningful display, and interplant with hostas, ferns, or native gingers to fill the void after dormancy. The entire plant is toxic; do not confuse with edible plants. Plants self-sow slowly to build colonies over many years.

plant_note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil.
directions: Dwarf Alberta spruce is a slow-growing conifer forming a naturally dense, cone-shaped pyramid of soft green needles, ideal for small gardens and containers. Plant it in full sun and well-drained soil, avoiding hot, reflective spots that can scorch the fine foliage. Provide good air circulation to reduce spider mite pressure, and rinse the foliage periodically in dry weather. Water consistently, as its shallow roots dry out quickly in pots. It rarely needs pruning thanks to its tidy, formal habit.

plant_note: Direct sow fresh acorns in fall at 1–2 inch depth in dry, sandy or gravelly, well-drained, acidic to slightly alkaline soil in full sun. Dwarf Chinkapin Oak produces sweet acorns with very low tannin content — one of the most palatable native acorns for wildlife and humans. The rhizomatous root system spreads to form colonies in appropriate conditions.
directions: Dwarf Chinkapin Oak is a colony-forming native shrub oak of dry, rocky, sandy, or calcareous upland soils across the eastern United States from New England to Texas, spreading by underground rhizomes to form dense thickets in acid barrens, shale outcrops, dry prairies, and other open, infertile habitats. Though modest in stature at 3–8 feet, it produces large, sweet, low-tannin acorns with coarsely toothed chestnut-like leaves, and the acorns were historically harvested and eaten raw — without the leaching required for most other acorns — by Native Americans across its range; they remain among the best-tasting acorns of any North American oak. Spreading steadily by rhizomes to form wildlife thickets, Dwarf Chinkapin Oak provides year-round habitat value: dense nesting cover, summer leaf-litter decomposition, and a reliable annual fall acorn crop for turkeys, deer, squirrels, and many bird species. Direct sow fresh acorns in fall in dry, infertile, well-drained soil in full sun; no supplemental irrigation needed once established in xeric conditions.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions just at or slightly above the soil surface in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; do not plant too deeply or the rhizome will rot.
directions: Plant rhizomes just at the soil surface in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; dwarf crested iris is one of the most charming native shade wildflowers, carpeting the woodland floor with colonies of pale lavender-blue iris flowers marked with white and yellow crests in early spring before the tree canopy fully opens. It grows naturally on wooded slopes, creek banks, and woodland paths across the eastern US. The low-growing, grass-like foliage spreads quickly by shallow rhizome to form dense, weed-suppressing mats — an excellent living mulch under trees. Plant where you can enjoy the flowers up close. The plant goes semi-dormant in summer heat but greens up in fall. Divide crowded colonies in late summer or early fall. The white form 'Alba' is occasionally available.

plant_note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 4.5–6.0) soil in partial shade to full sun. Dwarf Fothergilla performs best in consistently moist, acidic soil — avoid alkaline or poorly drained locations.
directions: Dwarf Fothergilla is one of the finest native shrubs for four-season interest in the southeastern garden, native to wet pine savannas, pocosins, and moist, acidic coastal plain habitats from North Carolina to Alabama, producing fragrant, white, bottlebrush-like flower spikes of pure white stamens in March and April before the leaves emerge — among the most beautiful of all native spring-blooming shrubs. The broadly ovate, blue-green summer leaves are attractive in their own right; they turn to exceptional shades of crimson, orange, and yellow in fall, often with all three colors on a single leaf. Easy to grow in moist, well-drained, acidic soil in full sun to partial shade, Dwarf Fothergilla is compact enough for small gardens and is valuable as a foundation planting, border accent, or naturalized understory shrub. Provide acidic, consistently moist soil and avoid alkaline conditions, which cause chlorosis.

plant_note: Plant nursery-grown container trees in fall in rocky, dry, well-drained, alkaline or acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; dwarf hackberry is among the most stress-tolerant of all native trees, thriving on exposed limestone outcrops and dry rocky ridges where few trees survive.
directions: Dwarf hackberry is a small native tree of rocky limestone glades, cedar barrens, dry cliffs, and open woodland edges across the eastern and central United States — the most compact and stress-tolerant of the native hackberries, growing slowly into a rounded shrub or small tree on exposed, droughty sites where little else establishes. Like larger hackberries, it is one of the most important wildlife trees for native butterflies: the larvae of hackberry emperor, tawny emperor, and American snout butterflies feed exclusively on hackberry foliage, making all Celtis species invaluable host plants for these specialist butterflies. Its small, orange-to-dark-purple berries ripen in fall and are consumed by at least 48 species of birds — it is one of the top bird-attracting fruiting native trees for fall migrants. Its tolerance of rocky, alkaline, shallow limestone soils and exposed cliff-face conditions makes it ideal for dry stone walls, rocky native plantings, and limestone-soil landscapes where conventional trees fail to thrive.

plant_note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil.
directions: Dwarf Hinoki cypress is a slow-growing evergreen with rich green, fan-shaped sprays of foliage that form a graceful, sculptural pyramid. Plant it in full sun to light shade in moist but well-drained soil, avoiding soggy sites that cause root rot. It appreciates shelter from harsh drying winter winds, which can brown the foliage. Water regularly during establishment and in droughts, then it becomes fairly self-sufficient. Little pruning is needed, and its naturally elegant layered form makes it a favorite for rock gardens and containers.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in very well-drained, strongly acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), sandy, humus-rich, dry to moist soil in partial shade; dwarf iris requires excellent drainage and strongly acidic soil — it will not tolerate clay, wet, or neutral to alkaline conditions.
directions: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in very well-drained, strongly acidic, sandy, humus-rich soil in partial shade; dwarf iris is one of the smallest and most charming native irises of the eastern US, producing miniature (2–3 inch) violet-blue flowers with yellow signals on stems only 3–5 inches tall in early spring — often the first iris to bloom, along with hepatica and spring beauty. It grows naturally in dry, acidic, sandy soils of open pine woodlands and dry, rocky woodland edges from Virginia to Georgia, in conditions very different from the moist requirements of most native irises. The leaves are narrow and grasslike. It spreads slowly by slender rhizomes to form a low colony. Excellent for naturalizing in acidic, dry-shaded rock gardens or under pines in the Southeast — one of the few dwarf irises suited to zone 8–9 conditions.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant out after all frost danger passes.
directions: This compact dahlia mix grows to about eighteen inches, making it ideal for containers, borders, and small-space gardens. Start seeds indoors under bright light six weeks before the last frost, then move plants outdoors once nights stay warm. The cheerful single blooms come in reds, pinks, yellows, and whites and draw bees and butterflies through summer. Deadhead regularly for continuous color, and in cold zones lift and store the tubers over winter.

plant_note: Start indoors well ahead of last frost; seeds need light to germinate.
directions: This dwarf petunia blend stays compact and covers itself in trumpet-shaped flowers in red, purple, blue, and white. Surface-sow the tiny seeds on moist mix and press them in without covering, since they need light to sprout, and start them indoors a couple months before planting out. Grow in full sun in containers, borders, or hanging baskets and feed regularly for nonstop bloom. Pinch back leggy stems and deadhead to keep the plants tidy and flowering.

plant_note: Sow in cool soil in early spring; soaking seeds overnight aids germination.
directions: This naturally compact sweet pea mix keeps the classic fragrance without needing a tall trellis, so it suits containers and small beds. Soak seeds overnight and sow in cool early-spring soil, since sweet peas love chilly starts and dislike summer heat. Provide steady moisture and a sunny spot, and pick blooms often to prolong the flowering season. A short bit of twiggy support or a low fence helps the bushy vines stay upright.

plant_note: Plant container-grown palms in spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; virtually all growth is underground for the first few years as the palm establishes an extensive root system before producing visible trunk.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; dwarf palmetto is the most shade-tolerant of the native southeastern palms, growing naturally in the understory of wet coastal plain forests, swamp margins, and bottomland forests from North Carolina to Florida and Texas. It is usually stemless or produces a very short trunk close to the ground, with bold fan-shaped leaves creating a dramatic tropical effect in the shaded garden. More cold-hardy than cabbage palmetto (surviving to -5°F), it is an excellent choice for providing a Gulf Coast tropical appearance in zone 7 and warmer. The palm grows extremely slowly but is very long-lived. The small black berries produced in tall panicles are eaten by birds. It tolerates periodic flooding and is one of the few palms that performs in significant shade.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall; needs a dry summer baking.
directions: Plant dwarf reticulated iris bulbs in fall about four inches deep in gritty, well-drained soil and full sun. The jewel-toned blue and purple flowers with contrasting markings bloom in late winter before most other bulbs. It needs a warm, dry summer dormancy to bloom well in subsequent years, so avoid irrigated beds. Plant it at the front of borders or in rock gardens where its early flowers can be admired up close.

plant_note: Plant divisions in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; dwarf sweet flag tolerates a wide range of moisture conditions from constantly wet to moderately moist and is more shade-tolerant and smaller than the species calamus. The golden cultivar 'Ogon' is especially valued for brightening shaded areas.
directions: Plant divisions in moist to wet, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade — dwarf sweet flag is a compact, grass-like Japanese native widely used as a shade-tolerant groundcover, edging plant, and water garden marginal throughout zone 5–9 gardens. The narrow, arching leaves are evergreen in warmer zones and have a pleasant, sweet-spicy fragrance when brushed or crushed. The golden cultivar 'Ogon' (Ogon Japanese Sweet Flag) is one of the finest shade-brightening plants available: its year-round golden-chartreuse foliage creates a luminous effect in dark corners, along shaded walkways, or at the edge of reflecting pools. 'Pusillus' is an extremely miniature form only 2–3 inches tall used in terrariums and troughs. Tolerates wet feet and very moist conditions but adapts to moderately moist soil in shade; divide every 3–4 years to maintain vigor and spread.

plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs in fall or spring in any well-drained to moderately moist soil in full sun to full shade; dwarf yaupon is extraordinarily adaptable — it tolerates poor soil, wet soil, dry soil, heat, drought, salt spray, and air pollution, making it virtually indestructible in Gulf Coast landscapes. No fertilizer needed once established.
directions: Plant in any well-drained to moderately moist soil in full sun to full shade — dwarf yaupon holly is one of the most versatile, low-maintenance native shrubs for Gulf Coast landscapes, remaining permanently at 2–4 feet without pruning in contrast to the standard yaupon (Ilex vomitoria) which reaches 15–20 feet. The dense, mounding form with tiny, dark-green, evergreen leaves creates a refined, naturally compact habit that requires no shearing to maintain its shape. It is extraordinary in its tolerance of challenging conditions: extreme heat, extended drought, coastal salt spray, wet soils, deep shade, and urban pollution are all handled with equanimity. A large-scale mass planting of dwarf yaupon under mature live oaks creates a dramatic, maintenance-free understory planting that will look perfect for decades. Female plants ('Schillings Dwarf' is the most common selection) produce tiny red berries in winter that attract dozens of bird species. One of the few native shrubs that genuinely thrives in deep, dry shade.

plant_note: Sow in cool soil a few weeks before the last frost; soak seed overnight to speed germination.
directions: Soak seed overnight, then direct sow in cool soil a few weeks before the last frost at the base of a trellis or support. This blend blooms up to two weeks earlier than typical early types, producing mildly fragrant flowers in nearly every color but yellow on long sturdy stems. Keep the roots cool and evenly moist, and cut flowers often to prolong blooming before summer heat. Note that parts of the plant are toxic if eaten, so keep away from children and pets.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost once soil warms.
directions: Direct sow after the last frost about an inch deep in full sun, thinning to 12-18 inches apart. Earthwalker is a side-branching sunflower in warm earthy tones of orange, mahogany, coppery brown, and rusty red, excellent for cutting and for feeding bumblebees and seed-eating birds. Because it branches, a single plant supplies many stems over a long period. Water regularly while establishing and stake in exposed sites to keep tall stalks upright.

plant_note: Plant nursery transplants (or hardwood cuttings rooted in moist soil) in spring in moist, well-drained, sandy or loamy alluvial soil in full sun. Eastern Cottonwood grows extraordinarily fast — up to 6–8 feet per year — but is short-lived (60–100 years) and prone to branch breakage in ice storms; plant male cultivars to avoid the cottony seed mess of female trees.
directions: Eastern Cottonwood is the fastest-growing native tree in North America, capable of adding 6–8 feet of height per year in moist, fertile, alluvial soils — a growth rate no other native tree approaches — and is native to the river bars, floodplains, and moist bottomlands of virtually every drainage system in eastern and central North America from Quebec to Florida and west to the Rockies. The triangular (deltoid) leaves tremble and shimmer in the slightest breeze due to their flattened petioles, creating a distinctive rustling sound audible at considerable distances; in late spring, female trees produce enormous quantities of cottony-tufted seeds that fill the air like midsummer snow, settling on every surface and creating what many observers find an enchanting spectacle and others find a nuisance. An important pioneer tree for streambank stabilization and riparian restoration, Eastern Cottonwood provides critical early-successional habitat for many birds and insects. Plant male cultivars to avoid seed dispersal; provide full sun and deep, moist soil near water features or for rapid windbreak and privacy screening.

plant_note: Direct sow or plant divisions in spring in moist, fertile, well-drained to moderately moist soil in full sun to partial shade; eastern gamagrass performs best in deep, rich bottomland soils and tolerates periodic flooding — it establishes from seed slowly but grows vigorously once rooted.
directions: Eastern gamagrass is among the most ancient and important native grasses of North America — as the wild ancestor and closest relative of corn (Zea mays), it was once a dominant grass of the tallgrass prairie and Gulf Coast bottomlands and is now valued both ecologically and for its productive, high-quality forage that can exceed corn in protein content. Dense, arching clumps of broad, corn-like leaves produce jointed seed spikes in summer bearing large, hard, nutritious seeds that are edible and were historically ground into flour by indigenous peoples of the southeastern United States; wildlife from wild turkey to deer consume the seeds and foliage extensively. Eastern gamagrass tolerates partial shade, seasonal flooding, and mowing and can be managed as a productive pasture grass in areas too wet for most warm-season grasses; as a landscape plant its bold, corn-like texture and tolerance of moist bottomland conditions make it ideal for rain garden borders, bioswales, and moist native meadows. Clumps expand slowly over years and can be divided in spring to propagate additional plants.

plant_note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped trees in late winter or early spring in moist, acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich, cool, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; eastern hemlock is one of the few conifers that actually grows better in shade than in full sun, especially in zone 6–7 where afternoon shade is essential. Treat proactively for hemlock woolly adelgid in eastern US with imidacloprid soil drench.
directions: Plant in moist, acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich, cool soil in partial to full shade — eastern hemlock is the only large native conifer that genuinely thrives in shade and is often the dominant tree in cool, shaded, north-facing ravines and steep, moist slopes where other conifers cannot compete. The delicate, feathery, dark green foliage and graceful, pendulous branch tips create an exceptionally beautiful, textured specimen in the shade garden. It is considered a foundational 'keystone' species supporting 96 species of Lepidoptera caterpillars, and its dense, multilayered canopy provides critical winter shelter for birds. However, the hemlock woolly adelgid, an introduced pest from Asia, has devastated populations throughout the eastern range — all planted trees in the eastern US should receive preventive treatment with systemic insecticide. In zone 3–7, an irreplaceable shade conifer for the woodland garden.

plant_note: Plant nursery transplants in fall or early spring in any well-drained soil in full sun; Eastern Red Cedar is extremely adaptable and tolerates poor, thin, rocky, dry, alkaline, and compacted soils where few other trees will grow. Female plants produce berries only when male plants are nearby.
directions: Eastern Red Cedar is the most widely distributed conifer in eastern North America, native from Nova Scotia to Florida and west to the Great Plains, colonizing abandoned fields, roadsides, fence lines, and rocky glades with a tenacity matched by few other native trees — it is the quintessential first colonizer of open, disturbed ground in eastern North America and has expanded its range dramatically following suppression of natural fire and the abandonment of agricultural fields. The aromatic, reddish-brown wood was historically prized for cedar chests, pencils, and fence posts due to its rot-resistance and insect-repelling properties; the fleshy, glaucous-blue, seed-bearing 'cones' (botanically berries) are an essential winter food for cedar waxwings, bluebirds, mockingbirds, and dozens of other birds, and the dense, scale-like foliage provides critical winter nesting cover. Eastern Red Cedar is a host for the alternate life stage of cedar-apple rust (Gymnosporangium juniperi-virginianae), which can infect nearby apple trees — avoid planting within 1,000 feet of apple orchards. An extremely tough, drought-tolerant, adaptable native tree for landscapes from zone 2–9.

plant_note: Direct sow on the soil surface in fall for natural cold stratification and spring germination; or cold-stratify seed 4 weeks before spring sowing. Surface sow only — seed needs light to germinate. Self-seeds freely once established.
directions: Sow on the soil surface in partial shade to full sun in moist to well-drained, average soil; wild columbine is a delicate native perennial of rocky woodlands, ledges, and open forest edges whose unique red-and-yellow nodding spurred flowers in April–June are the primary nectar source for ruby-throated hummingbirds returning from spring migration. The blue-green, lobed foliage is attractive all season. Plants self-seed prolifically and naturalize freely in appropriate conditions — allow seedlings to establish a colony rather than removing all volunteers. Short-lived (3–5 years) but self-perpetuating through seeding. Columbine leaf miners leave unsightly tunnels in foliage; tolerate minor damage or simply remove affected leaves.

plant_note: Surface-sow in fall or early spring; cold stratification improves germination for this native perennial.
directions: Surface-sow this North American native in fall or early spring, as a period of cold moist chilling greatly improves germination. Its nodding red and yellow lantern-like blooms attract hummingbirds and thrive in the dappled light of woodland edges, borders, and streamsides. Give it moist, well-drained soil and part shade, though it tolerates more sun where summers are cool. It self-sows freely and is short-lived, so let some flowers set seed to maintain the planting.

plant_note: Plant in spring in well-drained soil; provide room for a small ornamental tree.
directions: Eastern redbud is a small deciduous tree famed for the rosy-pink blossoms that clothe its bare branches in early spring before the heart-shaped leaves emerge. Plant it in full sun to partial shade in moist, well-drained soil; it naturally grows as an understory tree and tolerates some shade. Water regularly during the first couple of years to establish its somewhat sensitive root system. Prune only to remove dead or crossing branches after flowering, since it blooms on old wood. Its early nectar is valuable to bees and other pollinators.

plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; this is a native alternative to the invasive Asian burning bush (E. alatus).
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; eastern wahoo is a native alternative to the invasive Asian burning bush (Euonymus alatus) and performs the same landscape function — stunning fall color — while supporting native wildlife instead of escaping into woodlands. In fall, the foliage turns brilliant scarlet-red while the seed pods split open to reveal dangling bright coral-pink and orange arillate seeds that attract migratory birds including thrushes, bluebirds, and waxwings. The purple-tinged flowers in late spring are modest but charming. The plant tolerates deep shade and is one of few shrubs that thrives under the dense canopy of mature oaks and maples. All parts are toxic to humans and livestock. This is the true 'burning bush' of the native landscape.

plant_note: Plant divisions or container plants in fall or early spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately moist to dry, humus-rich soil in partial to deep shade; ebony spleenwort is one of the most adaptable and drought-tolerant of all native ferns, succeeding in both moist woodland and dry, rocky conditions in shade.
directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately moist to dry, humus-rich soil in partial to deep shade — ebony spleenwort is one of the most widely distributed and adaptable native ferns in North America, distinguished by its narrow, once-pinnate fronds on shiny, dark reddish-brown to black (ebony) stems that give it its common name. It grows in an extraordinary range of conditions: dry, rocky woodland slopes; bases of limestone outcrops; pine forest floors; moist ravine walls; and the dry shade under large trees where most ferns would fail. Unlike many ferns, it tolerates moderately dry, rocky conditions that defeat less adaptable species. The fronds are semi-evergreen in zone 7–9, providing year-round greenery in shaded gardens. It self-sows by spores to naturalize quietly in appropriate shaded, well-drained conditions, never becoming weedy. An outstanding low-maintenance fern for dry shade under trees in southern gardens.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; lobelia has extremely tiny seeds and a slow early growth rate.
directions: Surface-sow the dust-like seeds on moist fine-textured mix; do not cover, as they need light to germinate, and use a spray mist to avoid washing them away. Grow seedlings on at cool temperatures (60–65°F) once germinated; pot up in small clusters of 3–5 seedlings rather than separating individuals. Lobelia blooms best in cool weather and partial shade in hot climates, often slowing or stopping in midsummer heat — shear back by half and water regularly for rebloom as fall approaches. The vivid blue, trailing types are classic basket and container plants.

plant_note: Sow in cool soil before the last frost; in mild climates sow in fall for winter and spring bloom.
directions: Soak seed overnight and sow in cool soil several weeks before the last frost, or in fall where winters are mild. This blend offers mildly scented, long-stemmed cut-flower cultivars that flower in the short days of winter and cool spring. Provide a trellis, keep roots cool and evenly moist, and harvest often to extend the bloom period. Parts of the plant are toxic if eaten, so keep away from children and pets.

plant_note: Direct sow in cool soil before the last frost for spring and early summer bloom.
directions: Soak the seed overnight and sow in cool soil a few weeks before the last frost beside a sturdy trellis. This striking variety bursts with large ruffled blooms in electric magenta-red and a powerful sweet perfume, carrying three to four flowers per long, sturdy stem. Keep roots cool and consistently moist, and cut frequently for vase-worthy bouquets that hold well. Parts of the plant are toxic if ingested, so site it away from children and pets.

plant_note: Direct sow seed in fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich to average, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; elm-leaved goldenrod is one of the most shade-tolerant goldenrods in the eastern US, blooming reliably in the conditions of an open woodland understory.
directions: Sow seed in fall in moist, humus-rich to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade — elm-leaved goldenrod is an eastern North American native recognized by its leaves, which closely resemble elm leaves with strongly toothed, slightly rough-textured blades on short petioles. It grows naturally in moist, open woodland and woodland edges, making it one of a small number of goldenrods genuinely adapted to shaded conditions. The arching, one-sided (secund) racemes of small golden flowers appear in late summer through fall in shaded locations where most goldenrods cannot bloom. Like blue-stemmed goldenrod, it fills an important ecological niche by providing late-season nectar and pollen in shaded settings where pollinators searching for fall resources would otherwise find nothing. Spreads by rhizome to form a colony. An excellent native goldenrod for adding late-season ecological value to the woodland garden.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost; compact enough for containers.
directions: Direct sow after the last frost about an inch deep in full sun, spacing 8-12 inches apart. This blend of shorter, branching sunflowers includes classic single forms and multi-petaled pom-pom types that draw pollinators and make cheery bouquets. Their compact, drought-tolerant habit suits containers, borders, and small gardens. Water regularly while young and deadhead to keep new blooms coming.

plant_note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil, spacing for a hedge if desired.
directions: Emerald Green arborvitae is a narrow, upright evergreen with dense sprays of bright green foliage, widely planted in rows for privacy screens and hedges. Plant in full sun to light shade in moist, well-drained soil, spacing plants roughly 2 to 4 feet apart for a solid living wall. Water regularly during the first two seasons to establish deep roots and prevent winter browning. It holds its color and slender form well without shearing, though it can be trimmed lightly. Protect young plants from heavy snow load and browsing deer.

plant_note: Plant in spring or fall in rich, moist soil with afternoon shade.
directions: Endless Summer is a reblooming bigleaf hydrangea that flowers on both old and new wood, producing large mophead blooms from spring through fall. Plant it in morning sun with afternoon shade in rich, consistently moist soil, and mulch to keep roots cool. Flower color shifts with soil chemistry: acidic soil yields blue blooms while alkaline soil produces pink, so amend accordingly. Because it blooms on new growth too, light pruning is forgiving, though it rebounds best with minimal cutting. Keep it well watered, as wilting in heat is common.

plant_note: Root stem cuttings or plant container transplants in fall in moist to dry, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; English ivy is widely adaptable but can be invasive outside cultivated areas — do not plant near forests, natural areas, or unmanaged land. In zone 9a it may struggle in summer heat and drought.
directions: Plant cuttings in moist, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; English ivy is the classic, most widely used shade groundcover in temperate gardens — the glossy, lobed, evergreen leaves in many different sizes and variegations (solid green, silver-variegated, gold-variegated, small-leaved, large-leaved) create a dense, weed-suppressing carpet under trees. It climbs structures by aerial rootlets and has been used for centuries to cover buildings, walls, and slopes. However, English ivy is invasive in many parts of the United States east of the Rockies, smothering native understory plants and climbing trees — where naturalized, it can eventually kill large trees by girdling. Plant only in maintained, contained situations and remove regularly from any areas where it might spread to natural woodland. In zone 9a summer heat and humidity can reduce vigor; the heat-tolerant 'Needlepoint' and small-leaved varieties perform better in the Gulf South.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil.
directions: Direct sow after frost in warm soil, or start indoors a few weeks early, spacing plants about a foot apart. This unusual heirloom bears 3-4 inch chartreuse-green edible blooms that are striking in the garden and on the plate. Heat-loving and productive, it flowers endlessly until frost when regularly cut and deadheaded. Water at the base and space for airflow to reduce powdery mildew.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring or fall in any well-drained to dry, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; once established, epimedium is remarkably drought tolerant and can thrive in the dry root zone under large trees.
directions: Plant in well-drained to dry, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to deep shade; epimedium is among the most valuable groundcovers for difficult dry shade — one of the few plants that genuinely thrives in the dry, root-filled zone under mature maples, beeches, and other large shade trees where almost nothing else survives. The heart-shaped, often evergreen or semi-evergreen leaves are attractive year-round, turn bronze in winter, and the small but intricate crimson and pink bicolored flowers in early spring are delightful at close range before most shade perennials emerge. It spreads slowly but persistently by rhizome to form a dense, tidy, weed-suppressing mat. Shear back old foliage in late winter before new growth and flowers emerge. The genus contains dozens of outstanding species and cultivars; E. × rubrum is one of the most widely available and adaptable.

plant_note: Start indoors and set out after frost; surface-sow as light aids germination.
directions: Start seed indoors 6-8 weeks before the last frost, pressing it onto the surface since light aids germination, and transplant once frost danger passes. This blend produces upright architectural spikes of red, purple, white, and Fleuroselect gold-medal coral that bloom nonstop and draw butterflies and other pollinators. Give it full sun and evenly moist, well-drained soil. Deadhead faded spikes to keep the color coming through the season.

plant_note: Start indoors early; surface-sow and provide light, then set out after frost as a foliage annual.
directions: Start seed indoors 8-10 weeks before the last frost, leaving it uncovered on the surface where light and cool nights encourage germination. Grown for its fragrant, frosty blue-green rounded foliage, Silver Drop makes long-lasting filler for fresh and dried arrangements. Transplant to full sun after frost and pinch to encourage bushy, well-branched stems. Harvest foliage once stems are firm and cure it upright or hang to dry.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; European wild ginger is slower to establish than native Asarum canadense but has glossier, more ornamental leaves and is more tolerant of hot, dry shade.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade — European wild ginger is a low, slowly spreading shade groundcover with extraordinarily glossy, deep green, kidney-shaped leaves that remain evergreen through winter in zones 5–8, creating a lustrous, polished carpet under trees and shrubs. The leaves are significantly glossier and more ornamental than native wild ginger (A. canadense), which is deciduous. Small, brownish-purple, three-lobed flowers appear hidden at ground level in spring. It spreads slowly and politely by rhizome, creating a refined, weed-suppressing mat in appropriate conditions. It tolerates dry shade better than the native species once established. Unlike native Asarum, the European species does not host certain specialist insects, but its ornamental value is superior. An outstanding foliage plant for shaded woodland gardens in zones 4–8.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil.
directions: Direct sow after frost about an inch deep in full sun, thinning to 12-18 inches apart. This branching heirloom bears 4-6 inch sunset-hued blooms with dark centers in many rich tones on one plant, over a long bloom period that keeps vases full and pollinators fed. Because it branches, cut stems regularly to encourage more flowers. Water while establishing and stake tall stalks in windy locations.

plant_note: Plant in spring or fall in acidic, well-drained soil with afternoon shade.
directions: Ever After Ruby is a reblooming azalea that produces vivid ruby-red flowers in spring and again from summer into fall. Plant it in dappled or partial shade in acidic, well-drained soil rich in organic matter, and mulch to protect the shallow roots. Keep the soil evenly moist but never waterlogged, since azaleas resent both drought and soggy feet. Shear lightly right after the spring flush to encourage a fuller shape and stronger repeat bloom. Feed with an acid-loving-plant fertilizer in spring.

plant_note: Plant in spring in well-drained soil; excellent drainage is essential.
directions: Evolution Emerald Ice is a low, drought-tolerant sedum grown as a groundcover, offering fresh green succulent foliage and pink summer blooms that draw pollinators. Plant it in full sun in gritty, sharply drained soil, since wet or heavy ground causes rot. Once established it thrives on neglect and needs little supplemental water. Shear back lightly after flowering to keep the mat dense and tidy. Divide clumps in spring every few years to expand your planting or renew vigor.

plant_note: Plant nursery-propagated rhizome divisions in fall in moist, humus-rich, well-drained acidic soil in partial to full shade; this rare species is restricted to the Savannah River drainage in Georgia, South Carolina, and adjacent North Carolina and should only be sourced from licensed nurseries propagating their own stock.
directions: Faded Trillium is a rare and geographically restricted sessile trillium endemic to the Savannah River drainage in the Piedmont and upper Coastal Plain of Georgia, South Carolina, and North Carolina, growing in moist, rich deciduous hardwood forests over well-drained, loamy soils. Its pale yellow to cream-colored sessile flowers are smaller and less showy than many trilliums — giving the species its common name — but the silvery-mottled leaves are attractive throughout the spring season. Classified as a species of conservation concern, this trillium should never be collected from wild populations; it is occasionally propagated by specialty native plant nurseries. Plant rhizome divisions in fall in deep, humus-rich, moist but well-drained acidic soil in the shade of deciduous trees, allowing the plants to emerge and bloom in spring before the canopy leafs out fully.

plant_note: Direct sow in cool soil before or around the last frost; it dislikes summer heat.
directions: Direct sow in cool soil around the last frost, barely covering the fine seed. Fairy Bouquet is a charming blend of dainty three-quarter-inch snapdragon-like blooms in bright colors above airy foliage, also known as toadflax. It makes a colorful mass planting, container filler, or border edging and attracts pollinators. It prefers cool weather, so sow early and provide even moisture before summer heat arrives.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification, or scarify and cold-stratify seeds before spring planting.
directions: Scarify seeds and cold-stratify for 4–6 weeks before spring sowing, or direct sow in fall to let natural winter conditions break dormancy. Baptisia is a slow-starting plant — expect minimal bloom for the first 2–3 years, but once established, the clump requires virtually no care and lives for decades. The indigo-blue lupine-like flower spikes appear in late spring and are followed by attractive, inflated seedpods that rattle in the wind. Do not attempt to transplant established plants, as the deep taproot does not tolerate disturbance.
