plant_note: Plant container shrubs in spring in any moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; false indigo is extremely adaptable to difficult wet, flooded, or droughty conditions once established and can be used for streambank stabilization.
directions: Plant in any moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; false indigo is a large, airy native shrub with pinnately compound leaves and 3–6 inch spikes of unusual dark purple-violet flowers with bright orange anthers in early summer — a striking color combination. It blooms at a time when few other shrubs are flowering, providing important early nectar for native bees and the southern dogface butterfly, whose caterpillars feed exclusively on the plant. It is one of the most adaptable native shrubs for wet, poorly drained soils, riverbanks, and flood-prone areas, tolerating temporary inundation and spreading to stabilize streambanks. After the flowers, clusters of resin-dotted seed pods remain through winter. Prune back by one-third after flowering to maintain a compact form. Despite its common name it has no connection to true indigo (Indigofera).

plant_note: Plant offsets or direct sow seeds in spring in extremely well-drained, sandy to rocky, infertile, neutral to alkaline, coastal soil in full sun. False Sisal produces offsets (pups) freely around the base of the rosette — these are the most reliable propagation method. It is monocarpic, blooming once after 10–20 years then dying, but the offsets ensure colony persistence.
directions: False Sisal is a native agave of the coastal scrub, pine rocklands, and coastal dune communities of southern Florida and the Caribbean, producing a dramatic, architectural rosette of long, sword-like, blue-green leaves armed with sharp terminal spines and marginal teeth. Like all agaves, False Sisal is monocarpic — the main rosette blooms once after 10–20 years, sending up a spectacular branched flower stalk reaching 15–25 feet tall bearing thousands of pale yellow flowers that attract a spectacular diversity of moths, bats, and native bees, before the main rosette dies. However, the plant compensates by freely producing offsets (pups) around its base for years before flowering, ensuring colony persistence. Plant in extremely well-drained, sandy to rocky, infertile, neutral to slightly alkaline coastal soil in full sun; False Sisal is highly drought-tolerant and salt-tolerant once established but cannot tolerate frost, clay, or persistently wet conditions.

common_name: False Solomon's Seal (Feathery False Lily of the Valley)
plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions in early spring, setting them horizontally 2–3 inches deep; each piece should have at least one visible growth bud.
directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in full to partial shade; distinguished from true Solomon's seal (Polygonatum) by its feathery terminal cluster of tiny white flowers in spring rather than hanging bell-shaped flowers. The arching stems hold alternating oval leaves and terminate in airy white plumes in May, followed by green berries that turn red and speckled in fall. The berries are edible in small quantities but mildly laxative and should be consumed cautiously. The plant spreads slowly by rhizome and self-seeds minimally, making it a well-behaved companion for trilliums, ferns, and wild ginger in a woodland garden.

plant_note: Plant rhizomes 1–2 inches deep in spring; the rhizome is longer than Solomon's seal and should be laid horizontally in a trench rather than planted vertically.
directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; false Solomon's seal closely resembles true Solomon's seal in arching habit and foliage, but differs by bearing a plume of creamy white flowers at the stem tip in late spring rather than pendant bells along the stem. Ruby-red berries follow in late summer, providing important food for migratory thrushes and other birds before turning speckled red-purple at maturity. The architectural arching stems are ideal for naturalized woodland plantings combined with ferns, hostas, and trilliums. Plants spread slowly by thick horizontal rhizomes to form colonies; division every 4–5 years maintains vigor.

plant_note: Direct sow around last frost date or start indoors 4–6 weeks earlier for bloom the first season.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep directly in the garden or start indoors; unlike true perennial rudbeckia, heliopsis often blooms robustly in its first season from an early start. Plant in full sun in average, well-drained soil; plants tolerate heat and short dry periods better than helenium. Deadhead regularly throughout summer to keep the golden-yellow, semi-double or double flowers coming continuously from July through September. Divide clumps every 3 years in spring or fall to maintain vigor and prevent the center from dying out.

plant_note: Direct sow in place; tolerates poor dry soil well.
directions: Direct sow this western native in early spring, barely covering the small seed. Farewell to spring produces spikes of ruffled pink to lavender flowers on slender upright stems that make good cut flowers. It prefers lean, dry soil and full sun, blooming as spring transitions into summer. Avoid overwatering and fertilizing, which reduce flowering, and let plants self-sow for future displays.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after frost, or direct sow in warm soil.
directions: Start seed indoors 4-6 weeks before the last frost or direct sow in warm soil, spacing plants about a foot apart. Fata Morgana offers soft, ruffled pincushion blooms in shades of peach and apricot on slender stems, lovely in borders, cottage gardens, and bouquets. Deadhead and cut regularly to keep the long-lasting flowers coming and to draw pollinators. Give it full sun and well-drained soil.

common_name: Fatsia (Japanese Fatsia, Japanese Aralia, Glossy-Leaf Paper Plant)
plant_note: Plant container-grown specimens in fall or spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; fatsia is exceptionally shade tolerant and actually performs better in deep shade than in full sun, where the large leaves can bleach and burn in heat.
directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; fatsia is one of the most dramatic and tropical-looking shade shrubs for zone 7–11 gardens, producing enormous, glossy, deeply lobed leaves 12–18 inches across on bold, multi-stemmed plants that create an instant tropical feel in the darkest garden corners. The leaves hold their rich, dark green color year-round and are remarkably resistant to wind damage. Clusters of small white ball-shaped flowers appear in fall, followed by black berries. It is one of the most shade-tolerant large shrubs available and thrives under the dense canopy of live oaks and other large trees where most plants fail. The variegated form ('Variegata') has attractive white-edged leaves. Equally at home as an outdoor landscape shrub in zone 7–11 and as a bold container plant for covered patios and atriums.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost or start indoors a few weeks early.
directions: Direct sow after frost or start indoors 4-6 weeks early, spacing compact plants 8-10 inches apart. These single-row-petaled French marigolds make pollen easy for bees to reach, and their tidy, full habit shines at the front of a bed or tucked among vegetables. They grow and flower quickly with continuous blooms from spring to frost. Deadhead for tidiness and prolonged flowering, and water at the base.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring; 'Karl Foerster' is sterile and must be propagated by division.
directions: Plant in full sun to light shade in average, moisture-retentive soil; 'Karl Foerster' feather reed grass is one of the most reliable and widely adapted ornamental grasses, tolerating clay soil, humidity, and cold winters that defeat many others. Strongly upright, narrow clumps emerge early in spring with feathery bronze-pink plumes appearing in early summer that fade to wheat-gold and persist through winter. Cut back to 4–6 in in late winter or early spring before new growth begins. Divide every 4–5 years in spring when the center begins to die out.

plant_note: Plant container shrubs in spring in strongly acidic (pH 4.0–5.0), moist to wet, humus-rich soil in partial shade; fetterbush requires very acidic, waterlogged conditions and will not thrive in neutral or alkaline soil.
directions: Plant in strongly acidic (pH 4.0–5.0), moist to wet, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; fetterbush is a native evergreen shrub of pine flatwoods, pocosins, and bog margins across the coastal South, producing glossy, leathery, sharply ribbed leaves (the midrib runs parallel to the leaf margin — a key identification feature) and small pink to white urn-shaped flowers in early spring that are critically important for native mining bees and early bumblebee queens. The plant naturally grows in waterlogged, very acidic soil and is adapted to conditions that are difficult for most other plants. An outstanding native plant for bog gardens, wet acid borders, and naturally moist sandy soils in the Southeast. All parts are toxic to livestock and humans if ingested.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost; nasturtiums resent transplanting.
directions: Direct sow the large seed about half an inch deep after frost, since nasturtiums dislike root disturbance from transplanting. Fiesta Blend brings spicy edible flowers in orange, yellow, and orange-peach bicolors that brighten hanging baskets, containers, and garden walls. Both the peppery blossoms and leaves are edible and prized in salads. Grow in lean soil and go easy on fertilizer, as rich soil favors foliage over flowers.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring in a sheltered, partly shaded spot; cold stratification for 30 days improves germination. Division of crowns in fall provides faster establishment.
directions: Plant in well-drained, rocky to loamy, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; fire pink naturally grows on rocky woodland slopes and bluffs, where its brilliant scarlet flowers from spring through early summer are among the most vivid colors in the eastern woodland garden. The tubular, five-petaled flowers are perfectly shaped for ruby-throated hummingbirds and are a critical food source for hummingbirds moving through the mid-Atlantic states in spring. This plant is short-lived (3–4 years) but self-sows reliably to maintain a colony. Unlike most Silene, it performs best with afternoon shade rather than full sun. Combine with wild columbine and native ferns for a stunning spring vignette.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil.
directions: Direct sow after frost in warm soil, spacing tall plants about a foot apart for airflow. Fireball Blend delivers intense 4-5 inch blooms that are stunning at the back of a bed or planted in mass. The long-lasting flowers hold well both on the plant and in the vase, so cut and deadhead often to keep them coming until frost. Water at the base and space generously to limit powdery mildew.

plant_note: Plant container stock after last frost; firebush is a Florida and Gulf Coast native that quickly reaches shrub size and blooms nonstop in summer heat.
directions: Plant in any well-drained soil in full sun; firebush tolerates heat, drought, and poor soils once established and reblooms continuously from late spring through frost. The tubular orange-red flowers are magnets for hummingbirds and butterflies, while the small black fruits that follow attract songbirds — making it one of the most wildlife-productive native shrubs for Gulf Coast gardens. In zone 8–9 the top freezes back in winter, but the plant regrows vigorously from the base each spring; cut dead stems to the ground once new growth begins. Native firebush (var. patens) is superior to the commonly sold 'Compacta' cultivar for wildlife value and cold hardiness.

plant_note: Nick seed and direct sow after frost.
directions: Nick the seed coat and direct sow firecracker vine after the last frost in warm soil. This twining morning glory relative bears small scarlet trumpet flowers with yellow throats that draw hummingbirds. Provide a fence or trellis in full sun and average soil, and it will climb quickly through summer. Deadheading is unnecessary, but remove faded flowers if you wish to limit self-seeding.

plant_note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil.
directions: Firefly is a compact bush honeysuckle prized for its glowing amber-to-gold foliage and small yellow flowers borne over a long season. Adaptable and tough, it grows in full sun to part shade and tolerates poor, dry, or rocky soils once established. Its dense habit and spreading roots make it useful for erosion control on slopes and banks. Prune in late winter or early spring to renew and control size, since it blooms on new wood. The nectar-rich flowers attract bees and other pollinators.

plant_note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring after temperatures are reliably above 50°F; firespike establishes rapidly in warm, humid weather in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade to full shade. It can also be planted in fall in zone 9–10 and will establish over winter with reduced watering.
directions: Plant in partial to full shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil; firespike is one of the most valuable shade plants for Gulf Coast gardens, producing tall, dramatic spikes of brilliant scarlet-red tubular flowers from late summer through frost — at exactly the time when ruby-throated hummingbirds are fueling up for migration, making it one of the premier hummingbird plants of the Southeast. Unlike most flowering plants, firespike performs best in bright shade to partial shade and actually blooms more reliably away from direct afternoon sun in the heat of zone 9. The large, glossy, deep-green leaves provide bold tropical texture throughout the season. Cut back by half in early spring to encourage dense branching and more flower spikes; in zone 8 it may die back to the ground in hard freezes but regrows from the roots reliably. Propagates easily from stem cuttings taken in summer.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil.
directions: Direct sow after frost, barely covering the seed, and thin plants to 12-18 inches apart. Fizzy White is a show-stopper with 2.5-4 inch semi-double pure white blooms of pleated, zigzag-edged petals around fluffy tufted centers. This heat-loving annual attracts butterflies and other pollinators and makes an airy cut flower. Grow in lean soil with modest water, since rich, wet conditions favor foliage over flowers; deadhead to prolong bloom.

plant_note: Plant rooted cuttings or container transplants in spring in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, lean to moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; flame acanthus is native to the limestone hills and brushlands of south-central Texas and northeastern Mexico and is extremely drought tolerant and heat tolerant once established.
directions: Plant in well-drained, lean soil in full sun to partial shade; flame acanthus (hummingbird bush) earns its name with brilliant orange-red tubular flowers that literally glow like flames and attract hummingbirds from the moment the plants begin blooming in late spring through fall. It is a tough, deciduous or semi-evergreen native shrub of the Texas brush country that thrives in rocky alkaline soil, extreme heat, and drought conditions that would wilt most plants. The orange-scarlet flowers are produced in profusion from spring through frost, providing hummingbirds with a nearly continuous nectar source. It is deer resistant and rabbit resistant. The plant dies to the ground in hard freezes in zone 7–8 but resprouts vigorously from established root systems. An essential and easy plant for the wildlife-friendly Gulf Coast garden.

plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring in moist, acidic, humus-rich soil in dappled shade; does not tolerate transplanting well once established — site carefully.
directions: Plant in moist, deeply acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade from taller deciduous trees; flame azalea is the most spectacular of the native deciduous azaleas, producing brilliant orange, red, and yellow flowers (often in multiple hues on the same plant) in late spring that blaze against the emerging green foliage at the peak of the Appalachian wildflower season. The flower display can range from pale yellow to deep orange-red depending on the individual plant's genetics — purchase in bloom to get the color you want. Plant with the crown above soil level and mulch lightly with pine bark or oak leaves to maintain acidity. Prune only lightly after bloom to shape.

plant_note: Plant in spring in well-drained soil with room for a small tree.
directions: Flame Thrower is a redbud selection celebrated for multicolored foliage that emerges burgundy and matures through orange and yellow to green, plus rosy-red spring flowers on bare branches. Plant it in full sun to partial shade in moist, well-drained soil, giving the best color in a spot with some sun. Water regularly during the first couple of seasons to establish its roots. Prune lightly after flowering only to shape or remove dead wood. The early blooms are a valuable nectar source for pollinators.

plant_note: Root stolon cuttings easily in moist potting mix; flame violet requires warm temperatures (above 60°F night minimum), high humidity, and consistent moisture — it will not tolerate cold, dry air, or drought. In zone 10–12 it can be grown outdoors in deep shade as a groundcover.
directions: Root stolon cuttings in moist potting mix and grow in bright indirect light to deep shade with high humidity — flame violet is a spectacular tropical groundcover from South America, closely related to the African violet, producing velvety, elliptic leaves with bronzy-copper, silver, and green iridescent patterns (the 'peacock' coloration) and brilliant scarlet-red tubular flowers with yellow throats throughout the warm season. The plant spreads by long, slender stolons that root wherever they contact moist soil, quickly filling a hanging basket or ground space in tropical garden conditions. It is a superb houseplant, terrarium plant, and outdoor shade groundcover in zone 10–12, thriving in the warm, humid, deeply-shaded conditions under tropical trees and structures where few other flowering plants succeed. The foliage alone is outstanding; the brilliant flowers are a bonus. Dozens of cultivars offer variations in leaf pattern (silver-veined, pale green, bronze) and flower color (pink, orange, white, lavender).

plant_note: Start indoors and set out after frost, or direct sow in warm soil.
directions: Start indoors 4-6 weeks before the last frost or direct sow in warm soil, spacing plants about a foot apart. This wheat celosia adds a vertical element with rosy-pink, soft, textured flower spikes that invite a touch and shine in beds and large containers. It loves heat and full sun and makes an excellent fresh or dried cut flower. Water evenly and harvest spikes for drying when fully colored.

plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring or fall in moist, humus-rich, acidic soil in partial to full shade; excellent as a specimen, screen, or hedge under tree canopy.
directions: Plant in moist, acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; Florida anise tree is one of the finest native evergreen shrubs for deep shade in the Deep South, producing lustrous dark green aromatic foliage (strongly anise-scented when crushed) that forms a dense, refined screen under mature trees. The unusual maroon-red star-shaped flowers in spring are striking at close range though small. It grows naturally in moist, shaded ravine heads and stream banks in the Florida Panhandle and adjacent states. An excellent substitute for the invasive Japanese anise tree. All parts of the plant are toxic; it is not related to culinary anise despite the scent. Performs best with consistent moisture and mulch to keep roots cool.

plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring in moist, acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade; keep the crown at or above soil level and mulch to maintain moisture and acidity.
directions: Plant in moist, acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade; Florida azalea is one of the earliest and most fragrant of the native deciduous azaleas, producing clusters of brilliant golden-yellow to orange-yellow tubular flowers with exserted stamens in early spring before or with the emerging leaves. The fragrance is intensely sweet and carries far in the still spring air. It is native to the wooded ravines and stream banks of the Florida Panhandle, south Georgia, and Alabama and is one of the best native azaleas for Gulf Coast zone 8–9 gardens, tolerating the heat and humidity better than northern species. Plant as a foundation shrub, in a native garden, or massed along a woodland edge. Prune only immediately after bloom if needed; never shear. The color is exceptionally vivid when purchased in bloom.

plant_note: Direct sow fresh seeds in fall in pure sandy, well-drained, infertile, acidic soil in full sun; Florida Blazingstar is a Florida endemic best suited to xeric scrub and flatwoods conditions. The small corms are slow to establish but persistent once settled in appropriate soil.
directions: Florida Blazingstar is a small native perennial endemic to the sandy scrub, sandhill, and dry flatwood communities of the Florida peninsula, producing slender spikes of small, pinkish-purple flower heads in fall that provide a vital late-season nectar source for native bees, skippers, and migrating monarchs in one of the most imperiled native plant communities in the United States. Adapted to the infertile, extremely well-drained, white-sand soils of Florida's ancient scrub ridges, this compact blazingstar forms tight clumps from persistent corms in habitats that most other plants cannot tolerate. Like other Florida scrub plants, it depends on periodic, low-intensity fire to maintain the open, sunny conditions it requires. Direct sow fresh seeds in fall in pure, infertile, sandy, acidic soil in full sun; established plants require no supplemental irrigation or fertilization in appropriate xeric conditions.

plant_note: Plant container-grown transplants in spring in well-drained, sandy to sandy-loam soil in full sun; Florida Cherry Palm is highly drought-tolerant and salt-tolerant once established but is cold-sensitive and restricted to frost-free Zone 10–11 landscapes. Fresh seeds germinate in 2–6 months in warm, moist, well-drained medium.
directions: Florida Cherry Palm — also called Buccaneer Palm — is one of the rarest native palms in the continental United States, known from only a handful of natural populations in the Florida Keys, while more widespread through the Caribbean and the Yucatan Peninsula of Mexico. The trunk is distinctively swollen in the middle and tapered at both ends, giving the palm a unique hourglass or belly-shaped profile not found in any other palm native to Florida; the small, round, cherry-sized fruits ripen from green through yellow to bright red and are edible, eaten fresh or used in Caribbean countries to make a cherry liqueur. Extremely drought-tolerant and highly salt-tolerant once established, Florida Cherry Palm is among the toughest ornamental palms for Zone 10–11 coastal and xeric landscapes. Plant fresh seeds in warm, well-drained, sandy soil in full sun; germination takes 2–6 months and young plants grow slowly before accelerating in their preferred warm, sunny, frost-free conditions.

plant_note: Plant container-grown transplants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, rocky or sandy, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun. Florida Clover Ash is a rare native restricted to Miami-Dade pine rocklands and is best suited to frost-free Zone 10–11 South Florida landscapes. It is available from specialty native plant nurseries.
directions: Florida Clover Ash — also called West Indian Clover Ash — is a native member of the Melastomataceae (melastome) family, found in the United States only in the pine rocklands and tropical hardwood hammocks of Miami-Dade County, Florida, where it is listed as critically imperiled. The lance-shaped, three-veined leaves are glossy and attractive; clusters of small white to pale pink flowers appear in spring and summer and are followed by dark purple-black berries eaten by birds including the White-crowned Pigeon. An ornamental and ecologically significant native shrub of the highly threatened South Florida pine rockland community — one of the most endangered ecosystems in the United States — Florida Clover Ash deserves wider cultivation in Zone 10–11 South Florida native plant gardens. Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, rocky or sandy, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; established plants are drought-tolerant in the rocky, thin soils of its native pine rockland habitat.

plant_note: Direct sow fresh seeds in fall in sandy, well-drained, infertile, acidic soil in full sun. Florida Goldenrod is a Florida endemic best suited to xeric scrub, sandhill, and flatwoods habitats; it does not thrive in clay, amended, or waterlogged soils. Established plants spread slowly by short rhizomes.
directions: Florida Goldenrod is a native perennial endemic to the sandy, xeric scrub, sandhill, and flatwoods habitats of the Florida peninsula, producing bright yellow flower spikes in fall that provide critical late-season nectar for native bees, monarch butterflies, and numerous other pollinators in the imperiled Florida scrub ecosystem. Distinctly compact compared to most goldenrods, with a more open, arching inflorescence suited to the sparse, sunny, sandy conditions of Florida's ancient scrub communities where many competing plants cannot grow. Like the other specialized plants of Florida's scrub, Florida Goldenrod depends on periodic, low-intensity fire to maintain the open, well-lit conditions it requires. Direct sow fresh seeds in fall in sandy, infertile, acidic, extremely well-drained soil in full sun; established plants are virtually maintenance-free and very drought-tolerant once the root system is established.

plant_note: Plant container-grown specimens in fall in moist, acidic, organically rich soil in partial to full shade; mulch well to retain moisture and protect the fibrous root system — avoid dry or alkaline soils.
directions: Florida leucothoe is an upright, arching, semi-evergreen to evergreen native shrub that thrives beneath pines and oaks in moist, acidic, sandy or loamy soils — one of the Southeast's most graceful shade-garden shrubs, seldom seen in commerce despite its outstanding ornamental qualities. In spring, fragrant clusters of small white, urn-shaped flowers hang along the arching branches, feeding native bees; glossy, poplar-shaped leaves persist through winter in the Deep South. Plants form loose colonies via underground runners, providing excellent erosion control on shaded slopes and stream banks in the Gulf Coastal Plain from South Carolina to Florida and Mississippi. Grow in consistently moist soil away from drought and reflected heat; pair with native azaleas, swamp roses, and Syngonium for a woodland understory that supports diverse wildlife.

plant_note: Plant divisions in moist, slightly alkaline to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; Florida maidenhair fern grows naturally on shaded limestone outcrops and the bases of limestone walls and tree roots in Florida hammocks and the Caribbean, requiring good drainage and slightly alkaline to neutral (not acidic) conditions.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly alkaline to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to deep shade — Florida maidenhair fern is native to Florida hammock forests and limestone outcrops throughout Florida, the Caribbean, and tropical America, closely resembling the popular houseplant maidenhair (A. raddianum) but more cold and heat tolerant and uniquely adapted to the limestone-influenced, neutral to slightly alkaline soils of Florida garden settings. The fronds are more robust and somewhat less fragile than A. raddianum, making it more suitable as an outdoor landscape plant in zone 9–11. New fronds emerge with a lovely pinkish-bronze color that matures to bright green, creating a multicolored effect on established clumps. It is excellent for shaded patio containers, covered porches, and shaded garden beds with neutral to slightly alkaline limestone soils, where it provides an elegant, lacey texture. Unlike A. raddianum, it tolerates the outdoor humidity and heat of zone 9–10 without the constant intensive care needed by its more tropical relative.

plant_note: Plant container specimens in fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained to moderately moist, loamy soil in full sun to partial shade; Florida maple is significantly more heat, drought, and alkaline-soil tolerant than the northern sugar maple (Acer saccharum) and is the maple of choice for Gulf Coast gardens.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained to moderately moist, loamy soil in full sun to partial shade — Florida maple is the southern adaptation of the sugar maple group, a medium-sized native tree of limestone bluffs, rich upland woods, and creek bottoms throughout the Southeast, providing outstanding fall color (orange, scarlet, and gold) in zone 7–9 where the northern sugar maple fails in summer heat. The distinctive 3-lobed leaves are smaller than those of northern sugar maple; trees are compact, multi-season ornamental trees with smooth gray bark, golden spring flower clusters, and the classic maple winged seeds (samaras) in spring. Florida maple tolerates the heat and soil alkalinity of zone 8–9 gardens significantly better than northern sugar maple cultivars and is the appropriate choice for fall color, shade, and wildlife value in Gulf Coast landscapes. Trees yield a small quantity of sap that can be made into a light maple syrup in the brief late-winter cold spells of zone 7–8.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall in well-drained to moist, average to dry soil in full sun to partial shade; Florida mistflower is more drought-tolerant than the common blue mistflower (C. coelestinum) and performs well in dry, sandy Gulf Coast soils.
directions: Florida mistflower is a Gulf Coast native closely related to but distinct from the common blue mistflower (Conoclinium coelestinum), with deeply dissected, pinnately cut leaves that give it a finer, lacier texture — the species name 'dissectum' refers to these divided leaves. It grows naturally in dry to mesic pine flatwoods, scrub oak habitat, and open sandy uplands of the Gulf Coastal Plain from Texas to Florida, making it more drought-tolerant and better adapted to the dry, sandy soils common across the region than its close relative. The vivid electric-blue flower clusters appear in fall and provide a spectacular nectar source for monarchs making their southward migration through the Gulf Coast; it is one of the most important native fall-blooming perennials for the Texas-to-Florida monarch flight corridor. Its compact, finer-textured form and better drought tolerance compared to C. coelestinum make it ideal for dry-soil native gardens, rock gardens, and xeric restoration plantings in the coastal Southeast.

plant_note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to alkaline, sandy or rocky soil in full sun to partial shade. Florida Privet tolerates salt spray, drought, and alkaline soil, making it ideal for coastal landscapes in zones 8b–11 where other native shrubs struggle.
directions: Florida Privet is a tough, adaptable native shrub, native to coastal hammocks, scrub, and beach margins of south Florida, the Keys, and the Caribbean, where it grows in thin, well-drained, sandy or rocky soils under the influence of salt spray and periodic drought. Related to the ornamental privets used in hedges but native to the Southeast, it produces tiny yellow-green flowers in very early spring (sometimes as early as January) that are among the first nectar sources of the year for native bees, followed by small, dark blue to black oval berries that are important food for migrants and resident birds. An excellent native hedge, screen, or windbreak plant for coastal zones 9–11 where it tolerates salt, alkaline soil, drought, and occasional flooding; male and female plants are needed for berry production, so plant a group for wildlife benefit. Prune as needed for shaping — it is fast-growing and responds well to trimming.

plant_note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in very well-drained, highly acidic (pH 3.5–5.5), dry, sandy soil in full sun. Florida Rosemary requires the harsh, fire-maintained, nutrient-poor scrub conditions to which it is adapted; it will decline in rich, moist, or shaded conditions.
directions: Florida Rosemary is one of the most ecologically fascinating native shrubs in North America — a dominant species of the imperiled Florida scrub habitat, native to the ancient, white sand ridges of the Florida peninsula where it creates one of the strangest visual phenomena in American botany: "Florida rosemary rings," circular patches of bare white sand 3–20 feet in diameter surrounding each plant, kept barren by allelopathic chemicals (terpenes and phenolics) that the plant releases through its roots to prevent competing plants from establishing. Not a true rosemary (Rosmarinus), it bears superficial resemblance to the culinary herb with its needle-like, aromatic, evergreen leaves densely arranged on erect stems, but is a monotypic genus in the heath family (Ericaceae). Small red-orange flowers in fall are followed by tiny berries consumed by Florida scrub-jays, gopher tortoises, and other scrub species. Grow in the driest, most infertile, most acidic sand you can provide in full sun; it is intolerant of irrigation, rich soil, shade, or the weed-suppressive conditions that most ornamental plants require.

plant_note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or loamy soil in full sun. Florida Royal Palm performs best in moist, humid conditions and benefits from regular irrigation and fertilization during the growing season; it is intolerant of frost below 30°F.
directions: Florida Royal Palm is the most stately and formal of the native Florida palms, endemic to the wet, subtropical forests and swamp edges of the Everglades and extreme southern Florida, where it rises with a dramatically smooth, grayish-green, ringed trunk topped by a brilliant emerald-green crownshaft and an arching crown of long, gracefully curving feather-leaf fronds — a silhouette that defines the classical tropical landscape aesthetic. Once widely planted along formal avenues and boulevards in south Florida and Cuba (where the related Roystonea regia has the same role), Florida Royal Palm is a protected native species in Florida and an important component of Everglades ecosystem restoration. The white to purple flowers attract native bees, and the small olive-like fruits feed a wide variety of native birds and are an important food for the threatened Florida manatee in flooded situations. Plant in moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun in zone 10–11; fertilize regularly with a palm-specific fertilizer to prevent nutrient deficiencies.

plant_note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in well-drained, sandy or rocky, slightly acidic to alkaline soil in full sun to partial shade. Florida Thatch Palm is slow-growing and does not tolerate frost — it is appropriate only for zones 10–11. Source only from nurseries to protect wild populations in the Florida Keys.
directions: Florida Thatch Palm is an elegant, slender native palm endemic to the coastal hammocks and rocky shorelines of the Florida Keys and extreme southern Florida, with a natural range extending through the Caribbean and Central America, where it grows in limestone soils near the coast and in coastal hammock understories. Its graceful crown of silvery-green fan-shaped fronds provides an authentically tropical appearance in zone 10–11 landscapes, and small white flowers in spring produce clusters of round white berries that are important food for Florida Keys birds, particularly white-crowned pigeons. Slow-growing, drought- and salt-tolerant once established, and tolerant of brief cold snaps to about 30°F, Florida Thatch Palm is an excellent specimen palm for small gardens or use as an understory palm beneath taller coconut or royal palms in south Florida landscapes. Wild populations are protected in Florida; always purchase nursery-grown plants.

plant_note: Plant conservation-sourced nursery transplants in spring in cool, moist, well-drained, rich, acidic, humus-rich soil on a north-facing or east-facing slope in deep to partial shade. Florida Torreya requires the cool, humid microclimate of a shaded ravine and will not survive in exposed, hot, or dry conditions. Obtain plants only from reputable conservation nurseries growing ex situ material.
directions: Florida Torreya — also called Stinking Cedar for its pungent, resinous foliage — is one of the most critically endangered trees on Earth, native to a handful of steep, moist ravines along the Apalachicola River in the Florida Panhandle and adjacent Georgia, where the cool, humid microclimate provides conditions unlike anywhere else in Florida. Once abundant in these ravine forests, the species was devastated by an unknown fungal disease beginning in the 1950s and is now functionally extinct in the wild, surviving only as root sprouts and in ex situ conservation plantings maintained by botanical gardens and the nonprofit Torreya Guardians. The plum-like seeds were roasted and eaten by the Creek Nation and early settlers, and the dense, aromatic wood was historically used for fence posts and fuel. Plant in cool, moist, well-drained, rich, acidic soil in deep shade on a north-facing slope; obtain plants only from conservation nurseries producing legally sourced stock, as wild collection is not permitted.

plant_note: Divide clumps in spring and plant in moist to wet, acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade. Florida Wood Fern is adapted to the subtropical conditions of the Florida peninsula and Gulf Coast and is one of the most ornamental evergreen ferns for Zone 8b–10 shade gardens.
directions: Florida Wood Fern is a handsome evergreen native fern of the southeastern coastal plain, native to the moist, shaded understories of cypress swamps, bayheads, and bottomland hardwood forests from South Carolina to peninsular Florida, where it grows in the humid, warm-temperate and subtropical conditions of the lower coastal plain. Its dark, glossy, leathery fronds are fully evergreen in zones 9–11, making it one of the most attractive year-round ferns for warm-climate shade gardens, and its tolerance of briefly flooded conditions suits it to rain garden depressions and pond margins in partial shade. Closely related to Southern Wood Fern (Dryopteris ludoviciana) but adapted to the warmer, more subtropical conditions of the Florida peninsula, it is a better choice than its northern relative for zone 9b–10 landscapes. Divide clumps in spring and plant in deeply amended, consistently moist, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; mulch generously to maintain moisture.

plant_note: Plant conservation-sourced nursery transplants in spring in cool, moist, well-drained, rich, acidic soil in deep shade on a north- or east-facing slope. All parts except the red aril are extremely toxic — do not plant where children or livestock have access. Seeds require an extended warm-then-cold stratification of 12–18 months before germination.
directions: Florida Yew is an extremely rare and critically endangered native conifer restricted to fewer than two dozen sites in the moist, shaded ravines along the Apalachicola River in the Florida Panhandle, where it shares habitat with Florida Torreya in one of the most species-rich temperate forest communities in the southeastern United States. The small evergreen tree or large shrub produces fleshy, bright red arils surrounding a single hard seed in fall; the aril is the only non-toxic portion of the plant, as all other parts including the seeds, bark, and needles contain highly toxic taxine alkaloids that can be rapidly fatal to humans and livestock. Related to the yews exploited in the development of Taxol, a significant cancer chemotherapy drug, Florida Yew has declined catastrophically from unknown causes and numbers fewer than 250 remaining individuals in the wild. Plant conservation-sourced transplants in cool, moist, well-drained, acidic, humus-rich soil in deep shade — this species is intolerant of direct sun, drought, heat, or compacted soils and demands the microclimate of a deeply shaded, cool ravine.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil; succession sow for continuous cutting.
directions: Direct sow after frost in full sun, spacing closer at 6 inches for single stems or wider for branching plants. This florist mix blends apricot, lemon, peach, and gold shades from named cultivars, offering both single-stemmed and branching types for varied bouquets. Sow successively every couple weeks for a continuous cutting supply. Cut when the first petals lift and water evenly while establishing.

plant_note: Plant balled-and-burlapped or container-grown specimens in fall in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade; in the South, flowering dogwood requires afternoon shade protection and is significantly more stressed and disease-prone in full sun — understory conditions mimic its natural habitat.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to dappled shade — flowering dogwood is one of the most beloved native understory trees of the eastern US, producing its iconic four-bract 'flowers' (actually modified leaves surrounding tiny true flowers) in brilliant white or pink in early spring before the leaves emerge, covering the tiered, horizontal branches in a sheet of bloom visible through the forest from a distance. The leaves are followed by clusters of brilliant red berries in fall that feed migrating birds, and the foliage itself turns brilliant scarlet and purple in autumn. In the South, flowering dogwood is more susceptible to dogwood anthracnose fungal disease in hot, wet, poorly-aired conditions than in the North; plant in well-drained soil with good air circulation and avoid wetting foliage to reduce disease pressure. Zone 9 specimens benefit from afternoon shade and consistent moisture during summer heat.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed.
directions: Surface sow tiny seed on moist starting mix under bright light — do not cover, as seed is extremely small and needs light to germinate. Nicotiana tolerates partial shade better than most annuals and performs well in dappled light. Fragrant varieties release scent primarily in the evening, making them ideal planted near a patio or window. Deadhead spent flower clusters to encourage continuous blooming.

plant_note: Nick and soak seed, then direct sow after frost at the base of a support.
directions: Nick the hard seed coat and soak overnight, then direct sow after frost at the base of a trellis or fence. Flying Saucer bears 5-inch white blooms streaked with blue pinwheel stripes and occasional flecks, each flower slightly different, on vigorous vines to 10 feet. On cloudy days the flowers stay open all day, and the heart-shaped leaves lend a tropical look. Note that parts of the plant are toxic if eaten, so keep away from children and pets.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost with a cold stratification period; or plant divisions in spring or fall.
directions: Plant in humus-rich, consistently moist, well-drained shade; foamflower spreads by stolons to form a weed-suppressing ground cover with attractive, deeply lobed, often patterned foliage. Frothy spikes of tiny white to pink flowers appear in mid-spring above the foliage and last for 4–6 weeks. Divide clumps every 3–4 years in early spring to prevent overcrowding and maintain vigor. Many named cultivars with exceptional leaf markings are available and are best propagated by division to maintain cultivar characteristics.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring or early fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil; avoid planting too deeply — the crown should rest at soil level.
directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade, mimicking its native habitat on the forest floor; foamflower produces mounded clumps of deeply lobed, often burgundy-marked foliage and sends up airy white flower spikes in mid-spring that give the plant its common name. Running forms spread by stolons to colonize an area; clump-forming varieties stay tighter. Modern cultivars (many under the Heucherella group when crossed with Heuchera) offer extraordinary foliage variation. Pair with ferns, hostas, and wild ginger for a classic woodland floor combination. Plants dislike summer drought — mulch to retain moisture and site in a spot with consistent moisture.

plant_note: Sow in late summer to early fall so plants overwinter as rosettes and bloom in spring alongside tulips and daffodils.
directions: Scatter seeds on the soil surface or barely cover them; they germinate best in cool conditions and seedlings overwinter as low rosettes in mild climates. Forget-me-nots self-sow prolifically once established — let plants go to seed, then pull them after flowering to control spread. The sky-blue flowers are traditionally combined with spring bulbs in beds and create a charming filler under shrubs in partial shade. Remove spent plants when temperatures rise, as they become straggly and die back in summer heat.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring; self-sows readily in the garden.
directions: Chinese forget-me-not produces soft clouds of tiny sky-blue flowers that suit borders and cottage plantings. Scatter seeds directly on prepared soil in early spring and press them in lightly, keeping the bed evenly moist until seedlings appear. This easy annual tolerates partial shade and often self-sows to return in following seasons. Cut a few stems for small bouquets, and shear lightly after the first flush to encourage more bloom.

plant_note: Plant divisions in fall or early spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to deep shade; Formosa toad lily is more heat tolerant than the Japanese toad lily (T. hirta) and is the toad lily species to choose for zone 7–9 gardens.
directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to deep shade — Formosa toad lily is a shade garden gem from Taiwan, producing extraordinarily intricate, orchid-like flowers in September–November with white petals heavily spotted with purple-maroon and a bright yellow central structure, on arching stems with glossy, clasping leaves. Unlike most flowering plants, it blooms in the cooling days of fall precisely when the shade garden most needs color. It is more heat tolerant than the Japanese toad lily (T. hirta), making it the better species for zone 7–9 gardens where summer heat can stress T. hirta. The stolon-spreading habit allows it to naturalize over several years into expanding colonies in appropriate moist, shaded woodland conditions. The botanical intricacy of the individual flowers rewards close inspection — few shade perennials produce a more beautifully detailed flower. Outstanding in shaded borders alongside hostas, ferns, and autumn fern.

plant_note: Sow after frost; soaking hard seeds overnight speeds sprouting.
directions: Four o'clocks earn their name by opening fragrant, trumpet-shaped blooms in the late afternoon and evening in a lively mix of colors. Soak the hard seeds overnight and sow after frost in a warm, sunny spot where the fast-growing bushy plants can fill out. The evening flowers attract moths and hummingbirds, and the plants form tuberous roots that can be lifted and stored in cold regions. In mild zones they return each year and often self-sow.

plant_note: Plant rooted cuttings or divisions in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; forsythia sage is a large plant that needs room — it can grow 6–8 feet tall in a single season. In zone 8a, cut to the ground in winter and mulch; roots are reliably hardy to at least 15°F.
directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; forsythia sage is a bold, large-growing Mexican salvia that produces masses of bright clear-yellow flowers in dense whorls up tall, square stems from October through frost — its bright yellow blooms at the same time forsythia blooms in spring create a striking parallel, giving rise to its common name. The plant is remarkable for its tremendous size — growing 5–8 feet tall in a single season — and for its late-season bloom when most other plants are finishing. The large, heart-shaped leaves are powerfully aromatic when bruised. It is an outstanding hummingbird plant in fall when migrating birds need energy. The stems die back after frost but resprout vigorously from established root crowns in spring. One of the finest large fall-blooming perennials for the Gulf South.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost; a brief chill before sowing can improve germination.
directions: Direct sow after frost, barely covering seed; chilling the seed in the fridge for a week beforehand can boost germination. Fountain Blend forms tall plants topped with large clusters of fragrant spider flowers in white, pink, rose, and violet, with interesting seed pods and palmate leaves. It attracts butterflies and hummingbirds and needs little care once established. Give it full sun and lean, well-drained soil, and expect some self-sowing.

plant_note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is warm; can also start indoors 4–6 weeks early.
directions: Sow seed 1/4–1/2 in deep in a warm, sunny location after the last frost. Four o'clocks form a tuberous root and tolerate drought well once established. Flowers open in late afternoon and remain open through the following morning, releasing a sweet fragrance that attracts sphinx moths. Plants self-sow prolifically and the fleshy roots can be dug and stored in frost-prone areas.

plant_note: Direct sow seeds 1 inch deep or start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; in zone 8–10 the large carrot-like tuberous roots overwinter in the ground and resprout vigorously from established tubers each spring — often behaving as a spreading perennial. The seeds and roots are poisonous; do not plant where children or pets may ingest them.
directions: Sow seeds or plant tubers in well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; four o'clock is a vigorous, heat-loving, tuberous perennial native to tropical America that has been grown in gardens worldwide since the 16th century. The trumpet-shaped flowers in red, yellow, pink, white, or bicolor (sometimes more than one color on the same plant) open in the late afternoon (hence the name) and stay open through the night, closing in morning — making them essential for moths and evening pollinators including sphinx moths, which are their primary pollinators. The fragrance intensifies in the evening. Established tubers in zone 7–11 can grow to grapefruit size and resprout from deeper in the soil each year, often becoming permanent garden residents. In zone 9a they naturalize and can self-sow vigorously — seedlings are easy to remove if unwanted.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry, rocky to sandy soil in full sun; four-nerve daisy requires excellent drainage and is highly intolerant of wet or clay soils — it thrives in the rocky, caliche, and sandy alkaline soils of central and south Texas.
directions: Direct sow in fall in very well-drained, rocky to sandy, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry soil in full sun — four-nerve daisy is a compact, ever-blooming Texas Hill Country native that produces cheerful yellow daisy flowers with four-nerved ray petals on wiry scapes above low, grayish-green, linear leaf rosettes nearly continuously from early spring through late fall, often returning to bloom again in winter during mild spells. Each individual plant blooms for months without pause, making it exceptionally useful for small-scale rock gardens, dry borders, and native landscapes where its dependable yellow color brightens the garden through long hot seasons. It thrives in the rocky, alkaline, caliche, and limestone-rubble soils of the Edwards Plateau and South Texas, requiring no irrigation once established. A xeriscape standout: one of the longest-blooming and most drought-tolerant native daisies in North America, equally at home in crevices between limestone slabs and in open, sunny garden beds.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; seeds need light and should not be covered.
directions: Press seeds onto the surface of moist potting mix under bright light — do not cover, as light aids germination. Foxgloves are biennial or short-lived perennials; most produce a rosette in year 1 and the spectacular tall spikes in year 2 before setting seed and dying. Cutting the main spike after bloom often stimulates smaller side spikes for additional flowers, and self-sowing keeps the planting going indefinitely. All parts of the plant are highly toxic; use caution and wash hands thoroughly after handling.

plant_note: Plant the spider-like fleshy roots in fall, spreading them horizontally 4 in deep in a well-drained, sunny site.
directions: Spread the octopus-like fleshy roots horizontally over a mound of gritty soil, crown just below the surface, with perfect drainage — wet winter soil rots the crowns quickly. The strap-like foliage emerges in early spring and can be killed by a late frost; a light cover of straw protects emerging growth in zones 5–6. Spectacular bottle-brush spikes up to 8 ft tall erupt in late spring to early summer in shades of white, yellow, orange, and pink. Leaves die back after bloom, so interplant with later-emerging perennials to fill the gap.

plant_note: Plant divisions in fall or early spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial shade; fragrant hosta is more heat tolerant than most hostas and is one of the best hosta species for zone 8–9, where it performs more reliably than the northern-bred cultivars that need cold winters to thrive.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to dappled shade — fragrant hosta stands apart from all other hostas by being the only species with intensely, beautifully fragrant flowers — large, pure white, trumpet-shaped blooms on tall scapes in August that fill the evening garden with a sweet, lily-like perfume. The light-green, glossy, somewhat corrugated leaves are unlike the typical blue-green, waxy hostas — they are brighter and more luminous in the shade garden. Fragrant hosta is also significantly more heat-tolerant than most hosta cultivars, performing reliably in zone 8–9 where most hybrid hostas struggle to thrive without cold winters. The evening fragrance is outstanding when planted near patios, pathways, and windows. Derived cultivars like 'Royal Standard' and 'Aphrodite' (double flowers) extend the bloom period and flower character. Exceptional as a long-lived, low-maintenance shade perennial for warm-climate gardens.

plant_note: Plant container-grown specimens in fall in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in partial shade to full sun; sweet olive is adaptable but performs best with morning sun and afternoon shade in the hottest climates. It is slow-growing but very long-lived once established.
directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; fragrant olive (sweet olive, tea olive) is one of the most beloved fragrant shrubs of the Gulf South — a classic fixture in old Southern gardens, cherished for its tiny, creamy white flowers that perfume the air with an intense, sweet apricot-like fragrance that can carry 100 yards on a still autumn morning. The plant blooms repeatedly from fall through spring in zone 8–9, often triggered by cool snaps, and the fragrance is one of the most powerful and instantly recognizable in the Southern garden tradition. The large, dense, glossy evergreen shrub makes an outstanding screen, hedge, or specimen. It grows slowly but lives for decades and eventually becomes a small tree. The variety O. fragrans f. aurantiacus has orange flowers; 'Fudingzhu' is a particularly free-flowering white selection.

plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring in very well-drained, dry to moderately moist, slightly acidic to slightly alkaline soil in partial shade to full sun; fragrant sumac is one of the best native shrubs for dry, shaded slopes and is excellent for erosion control.
directions: Plant in very well-drained, dry to moderately moist, slightly acidic to slightly alkaline soil in partial shade to full sun; fragrant sumac is one of the most versatile, adaptable, and underused native shrubs in North America — tolerating dry shade, rocky slopes, alkaline soil, and drought conditions that defeat most plants. The crushed leaves have a distinctly pleasant lemon-citrus fragrance. Small yellow flowers in early spring before the leaves emerge provide important early nectar for native bees emerging from overwintering. The fuzzy red fruit clusters feed dozens of bird species through fall and winter. The three-parted leaves turn brilliant orange-red and burgundy in fall — among the best native plants for fall color — and the low, spreading form makes it excellent for groundcover use on slopes. The cultivar 'Gro-Low' is a compact, 2-foot-tall groundcover form widely used in landscape planting.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, shady soil.
directions: Francee is a classic shade hosta with dark green leaves crisply edged in white, forming a tidy mounded clump and sending up lavender flower spikes in summer. Plant it in partial to full shade in rich, moist, well-drained soil, as too much sun scorches the variegated foliage. Keep the soil evenly moist and mulch to retain moisture and suppress weeds. Divide clumps in spring every few years to propagate or reduce crowding. Watch for slugs, which can chew the leaves, especially in damp conditions.

plant_note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade. Fraser Magnolia is an understory tree that performs best in sheltered positions with protection from late spring frosts that can damage the large, showy flower buds.
directions: Fraser Magnolia is a deciduous native magnolia of the southern Appalachians, native to the rich, moist cove forests and ravine slopes from Virginia and West Virginia south to Georgia and Alabama, immediately recognizable by its enormous leaves (up to 18 inches long) with distinctive ear-like lobes (auricles) at the base — the largest leaves of any magnolia native to the southern mountains. In May and June it produces large, cup-shaped, creamy-white, fragrant flowers 8–10 inches across that emit a sweet, lemony fragrance, followed in fall by striking pink to red cone-like fruit clusters that split to reveal bright red seeds, providing ornamental interest from spring through fall. Fraser Magnolia is a shade-tolerant understory tree that grows naturally in the deepest, richest, most sheltered cove forest conditions alongside cucumber magnolia, yellow buckeye, and basswood, and it translates beautifully to shaded woodland gardens in zones 5–8. Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade, protecting from prevailing wind to prevent the large leaves from tearing.

plant_note: Plant corms in fall in frost-free areas (zones 9–10) or start indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost in cold climates.
directions: Plant corms 2 in deep and 2–3 in apart in gritty, well-drained soil; freesias resent wet conditions especially after bloom. The fragrant, funnel-shaped flowers on arching stems need staking or a grow-through support as stems are slender. In frost-free gardens the corms are perennial and naturalize freely; in colder climates lift after foliage dies, dry thoroughly, and store in a cool dry place. Corms multiply quickly — divide every 2–3 years to maintain bloom quality.

plant_note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed.
directions: Sow seed 1/4 in deep in warm starting mix. Pinch out the first flower bud to encourage bushier branching and heavier bloom set. Deadhead spent flowers regularly to keep plants producing through summer. Blooms appear approximately 6–8 weeks after transplant and continue until frost.

plant_note: Start indoors for early bloom or direct sow after frost.
directions: This French marigold mix stays compact at about a foot tall with bright, two- to three-inch double blooms in gold, orange, yellow, and red. Sow seeds shallowly after the last frost or start indoors for earlier color, and place in full sun. The tidy mounded plants edge beds neatly and pair well with vegetables to help repel pests. Deadhead often for continuous flowering right up to fall frost.

plant_note: Plant in spring or fall in rich, moist soil with afternoon shade.
directions: Frill Ride is a bigleaf hydrangea bearing bold mophead blooms with distinctive frilled petal edges in shades that shift with soil pH. Plant it in morning sun and afternoon shade in rich, consistently moist, well-drained soil, mulching to keep roots cool. Acidic soil pushes blooms toward blue while alkaline soil yields pink, so amend to steer the color you want. Prune just after flowering, since it blooms mainly on old wood. Water generously in heat to prevent the large leaves and flowers from wilting.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; also sow seed in fall for natural cold stratification. Plants may also be divided in fall.
directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; fringed bleeding heart is the native eastern species with deeply cut, ferny blue-green foliage and pendant rosy-pink heart-shaped flowers that bloom from late spring through fall — an exceptionally long season for a shade perennial, unlike the common garden bleeding heart (Dicentra spectabilis) which dies back completely after spring bloom. It is native to rocky woodland slopes in the Appalachians from New York to Georgia. The foliage may die back partially in summer heat but rebounds with fall cooling. It spreads slowly by self-seeding to naturalize a shaded bed and is an outstanding companion for hostas, ferns, and native wildflowers. All parts are toxic if ingested.

plant_note: Start indoors in darkness; transplant while cool, or sow in late summer for fall and spring bloom.
directions: Start seed indoors 8-10 weeks before setting out, covering it since pansies germinate best in darkness, and transplant while weather is cool. Frizzle Sizzle Lemonberry features ruffled 2 to 2.5-inch tricolor blooms of lemon, deep burgundy, and berry-purple edges that intensify in cool spring temperatures. These low-growing edible flowers suit beds, borders, and containers. Deadhead to prolong blooming and provide even moisture, as they fade in summer heat.

plant_note: Plant rooted cuttings or plugs in spring in any well-drained, slightly acidic to alkaline, moist to dry soil in full sun to partial shade; frogfruit is extremely adaptable and roots readily wherever stems contact moist soil, spreading quickly to fill in bare ground.
directions: Plant plugs or rooted cuttings in any well-drained, moist to dry soil in full sun to partial shade — frogfruit is a tough, trailing native groundcover of Gulf Coast roadsides, disturbed prairies, and open woodland margins, hugging the ground so tightly (1–3 inches tall) that it can be used as a low-maintenance lawn substitute or mowed path edge in full sun. Despite its modest appearance, it is one of the most ecologically important low-growing native plants in the South: the tiny white to pink flower heads bloom nearly year-round in warm climates and serve as a critical nectar source for many small native bees and flies, while the leaves are larval host plants for the Phaon crescent and common buckeye butterflies. It roots at stem nodes wherever it contacts moist soil, spreading rapidly to colonize bare areas but never invasively outcompeting other established plants. Heat, drought, and salt tolerant; extremely low-maintenance once established. Blooms are most prolific in spring and fall.

plant_note: Plant in spring in acidic, well-drained soil in a sheltered spot.
directions: Frost Proof is a cold-tolerant gardenia bearing intensely fragrant white flowers against glossy evergreen leaves, and it withstands cooler temperatures better than typical gardenias. Plant it in morning sun with afternoon shade in acidic, well-drained soil rich in organic matter, and mulch to keep roots cool and moist. Keep watering consistent, as fluctuating moisture causes bud drop. Feed with an acid-loving-plant fertilizer during the growing season and prune lightly after the main bloom. Protect from harsh winter wind at the colder edge of its range.

plant_note: Direct sow seeds in fall in moist to moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in partial shade; frostweed grows naturally in moist woodland edges, creek bottoms, and open woods from the eastern US to Texas and tolerates considerable shade while still producing flowers.
directions: Sow seeds in fall or plant transplants in moist, partially shaded woodland-edge soil; frostweed is a tall, commanding native perennial wildflower that earns its evocative name from a remarkable winter phenomenon: when the first hard freeze strikes the green stems, the sap freezes and expands, splitting the bark and extruding curling ribbons and sheets of ice in fantastical flower-like formations called 'ice flowers' or 'frost flowers' that persist for hours after sunrise. This natural ice art is one of the most spectacular surprises in the winter garden. The plant itself produces masses of small white flowers beloved by butterflies — particularly migrating monarchs — from late summer through fall. It is an important nectar source at a critical migration season. Tolerates part shade and moist conditions, making it one of the few native tall perennials suited to moist woodland edges and rain gardens.

plant_note: Nick and soak seed, then direct sow after frost at the base of a trellis.
directions: Nick and soak the seed overnight, then direct sow after frost at the base of a trellis or screen. This easy vine climbs with delicate ferny foliage and produces small tubular blooms that stay open all day and attract hummingbirds. Give it full sun and a support to twine up, watering while young until established. Most parts of the plant are toxic if eaten, so keep it away from children and pets.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in moist, very acidic (pH 4.0–5.0), humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; purchase only from nursery-propagated sources — galax has been over-collected from the wild for the floral trade and is declining in many areas.
directions: Plant in moist, very acidic (pH 4.0–5.0), humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; galax is one of the premier evergreen groundcovers for deep shade in acidic woodland gardens. The round, glossy, leathery leaves form a dense, weed-suppressing carpet that turns brilliant bronze-wine-red through fall and winter before returning to rich green in spring — providing bold, year-round color in the shade garden. The small white flowers on tall wands in late spring are attractive to bees. It grows naturally on the rocky, acidic woodland slopes of the Southern Appalachians, where it is sometimes called 'coltsfoot.' Galax leaves are highly prized in the floral industry and are heavily wild-harvested — purchase only nursery-propagated plants. Spreads slowly but persistently by rhizome to form colonies.

plant_note: Sow in cool soil before the last frost, or in fall in mild climates; seed benefits from chilling.
directions: Direct sow in cool soil a few weeks before the last frost, or in fall where winters are mild; chilling the seed improves germination. This memorable cut flower blooms from late spring into early summer in carmine, lilac, salmon, blue, pink, and white, adding height at the back of the bed. It reseeds readily and prefers cool conditions. Note that all parts of the plant are toxic if eaten, so site it away from children and pets.

plant_note: Plant container-grown plants in spring after temperatures are consistently above 60°F in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in full sun to partial shade; crotons require consistent warmth and thrive in hot, humid, tropical weather. Cold below 50°F causes leaf drop. The milky sap is irritating — wear gloves when pruning.
directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade — the most vibrant leaf coloration develops in brighter light; deep shade produces greener, less intensely colored foliage. Garden croton is one of the most spectacularly colorful foliage plants in tropical horticulture, with leathery leaves that combine vivid red, orange, yellow, green, and purple in patterns ranging from fine-speckled splashes to broad blotches, depending on the hundreds of named cultivars available. Hundreds of leaf shapes exist: broad and lobed, narrow and twisted, curled, or oak-leaf shaped. It thrives in the heat and humidity of the Gulf Coast and Florida in full outdoor culture in zone 9–12. Mite infestations are the most common pest problem, particularly in dry conditions — maintain adequate soil moisture and mist foliage during droughts. Crotons drop leaves readily when moved, so establish carefully. An outstanding specimen and landscape plant for bold tropical color.

plant_note: Plant divisions or rooted cuttings in spring, 2–6 weeks after the last frost, for fall bloom.
directions: Plant in spring into fertile, well-drained soil; pinch growing tips every 2–3 weeks from planting until midsummer (around July 4) to develop compact, well-branched plants with dozens of buds. Mums are short-day plants that begin flower bud formation as nights lengthen; stop pinching by midsummer to avoid disrupting this process. Mulch heavily after the first hard frost to improve winter hardiness in colder zones. Divide annually in spring to rejuvenate the planting and maintain plant vigor.

plant_note: Start indoors early; surface-sow as light is needed, and set out after frost.
directions: Start seed indoors 8-10 weeks before the last frost, pressing the tiny seed onto the surface since light is required for germination. Garden Party Blend produces large 3-inch ruffled floribunda blooms in abundance all season on mounding, upright plants that draw butterflies. Set out after frost in full sun in beds or containers around the patio. Deadhead and feed lightly to keep the colorful display going into fall.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring as new growth emerges or in early fall for establishment before frost.
directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, humus-rich soil with good air circulation to reduce powdery mildew, which is the plant's main problem. Thin stems to 5–7 per clump in spring to improve airflow and produce larger flower trusses on the remaining stems. Deadhead the central flower cluster as it fades to encourage lateral buds to open and extend the bloom season. Divide every 3 years in spring, replanting only the outermost, most vigorous sections.

plant_note: Start seed indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost, or purchase transplants; garden verbena requires warm soil (above 60°F) to grow and bloom — transplant only after frost risk has fully passed. Deadhead regularly to extend bloom into summer; in zone 9–10 it blooms spring through winter with minimal care.
directions: Start indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost, or plant purchased transplants in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately dry to moist soil in full sun — garden verbena is one of the most popular and versatile bedding plants in the world, available in a continuous spectrum from pure white through pink, salmon, red, scarlet, purple, lavender, and bicolor combinations, blooming prolifically from spring through fall. The trailing to mounding habit (depending on cultivar) makes it outstanding in containers, hanging baskets, window boxes, and as a low bedding plant. Modern disease-resistant series ('Superbena', 'Lascar', 'Obsession') provide significantly better powdery mildew resistance and longer bloom seasons than older varieties. In zone 9–11 garden verbena often overwinters and behaves as a short-lived perennial, providing bloom through winter; in zone 7–8 it is an annual. Remove spent flower clusters regularly to maintain maximum bloom production through summer.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost and transplant after all frost danger has passed.
directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep in loose, well-drained germination mix; plants develop a deep taproot early so pot on before roots become crowded. Plant in full sun and lean, well-drained soil — gaura thrives on neglect and declines in fertile, wet conditions. The wand-like stems bearing butterfly-shaped white or pink flowers sway gracefully all season without deadheading. Cut plants back to 6 in after the first heavy bloom flush to encourage a tidy second flush through autumn.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost and transplant after all frost risk has passed.
directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep; they germinate readily at 65–75°F and grow quickly into compact transplants. Plant in sharply drained, even poor soil in the hottest, sunniest location available — gazania excels in sandy or gravelly beds where other plants fail. Flowers close at night and on overcast days, but modern hybrid varieties stay open longer than older types. Drought tolerance is excellent once established; overwatering and heavy clay soils are the most common causes of failure.

plant_note: Plant rooted cuttings or container-grown transplants in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun. Georgia Holly is dioecious and requires both male and female plants nearby for berry production. It is rare in cultivation but available from specialist native plant nurseries.
directions: Georgia Holly is a rare and regionally endemic native deciduous holly of the upper coastal plain and piedmont, found in a narrow range across Georgia, Alabama, and adjacent states, where it grows as a spreading shrub in the understory of moist, well-drained, sandy-loam upland forest. Distinctive among native hollies for its unusually long flower and fruit pedicels — the scientific epithet longipes means 'long-footed' — the bright red berries appear to float in space on their elongated stalks, making them more visible and accessible to birds than the closely bunched berries of many other holly species. A candidate for greater cultivation in the native plant garden, Georgia Holly is a multi-season ornamental shrub with small white spring flowers, deep green summer foliage, vivid fall berries, and clean branch structure in winter. Plant rooted cuttings in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade; as with all native hollies, both male and female plants must be present for berry production.

plant_note: Plant nursery-propagated transplants in spring in dry to moist, well-drained, acidic, sandy soil in partial shade to full sun. Georgia Plume is critically rare in the wild and should only be sourced from specialty native plant nurseries; the species rarely produces viable seed and is most effectively propagated by root cuttings.
directions: Georgia Plume is one of the rarest and most endangered native shrubs in the United States, a monotypic genus (the only species in its family in North America) endemic to the Sandhills and longleaf pine region of Georgia, where fewer than 50 wild populations survive in dry, sandy, acidic longleaf pine savannas and turkey oak ridges. In July it produces spectacular terminal plumes of small white, four-petaled flowers in drooping clusters 6–12 inches long — an arresting display on a plant that looks unlike anything else in the native flora. The species rarely sets viable seed in the wild (likely due to the collapse of its pollinator community), and natural populations reproduce almost entirely by root sprouting; specialty native plant nurseries propagate it by root cuttings. Grow in dry to moist, well-drained, sandy, acidic soil in partial to full sun — it is intolerant of heavy, wet, or alkaline soils. Supporting cultivation of Georgia Plume in private gardens is an important conservation act.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost and transplant after all frost danger has passed.
directions: Sow seeds vertically, pointed end down, barely covered, as they are sensitive to orientation; maintain 70–75°F for germination. Plant so the crown sits slightly above soil level to prevent crown rot, which is the most common cause of plant failure. Water at the base rather than overhead; gerberas prefer a dry-wet-dry cycle rather than constantly moist soil. Deadhead spent flowers by cutting the stem to the base to encourage continuous bloom from spring through fall in mild climates.

plant_note: Plant large bulbs in fall at a depth of 3–4 times the bulb diameter (typically 6–8 in) in well-drained soil.
directions: Plant bulbs in fall, pointed side up, at 3–4 times their diameter in depth; good drainage is essential as bulbs rot in waterlogged soil. The bold, softball-sized purple globes atop tall stems create dramatic vertical accents in late spring to early summer borders. Foliage, which smells of onion when crushed, often begins to yellow before flowers are fully open — conceal the base with later-rising perennials. Allow seedheads to remain for architectural interest; deadhead if you wish to prevent excessive self-seeding.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in wet to moist, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile, loamy to mucky soil in full sun to partial shade; giant blue iris naturally grows in salt-influenced freshwater and brackish marshes of coastal Louisiana and is uniquely tolerant of poor drainage, flooding, and coastal salt influence.
directions: Plant rhizome divisions in wet to moist, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade — giant blue iris is one of the showiest of all Louisiana iris species and one of the primary parents of Louisiana iris hybrids, endemic to the coastal freshwater and brackish marshes, swampy forests, and wet prairies of coastal Louisiana.
