Its flowers are spectacularly large (5–7 inches across) in clear blue to blue-purple on tall stems (3–5 feet) that tower above the robust, broad leaf fans, blooming in April–May in spectacular drifts. It is significantly more salt and flood tolerant than other Louisiana irises, thriving in the challenging conditions of coastal garden areas with periodic saltwater influence. The species is an important wetland stabilizer in its native coastal marsh habitat. In gardens, it is outstanding for pond margins, rain garden edges, and any wet, low spot that holds water seasonally. Louisiana iris hybrids derived from this species have expanded greatly on its already exceptional ornamental traits.

plant_note: Plant divisions or offsets in spring after temperatures are reliably above 65°F in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained to moist soil in partial shade to full sun; Alocasia macrorrhiza grows vigorously in warm, humid weather and produces new leaves rapidly in good conditions. In zone 9 it may die back to the ground after hard freezes but regrows from established rhizomes in spring.
directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; giant elephant ear is one of the most dramatically bold-textured tropical plants available for zone 9–12 gardens, producing truly enormous, upward-pointing, arrow-shaped leaves — 2–4 feet long — on stout petioles that can reach 6–15 feet tall, creating an unmistakably tropical visual impact that transforms any landscape. Unlike the Colocasia elephant ears (which droop their leaves), Alocasia macrorrhiza holds its leaves upward and outward, creating a distinctive architectural silhouette. It thrives in the bright, filtered shade beneath large trees and palms in the hot, humid Gulf Coast climate, where high moisture, rich soil, and warmth produce the largest, most spectacular plants. The massive rhizomes are edible (raw parts are toxic due to calcium oxalate crystals — must be cooked) and are used as a starchy vegetable in Pacific Island cuisine. Remove damaged leaves promptly and fertilize monthly during the growing season for best growth.

plant_note: Surface sow seed in fall in moist to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; seeds require cold stratification so fall sowing or winter sowing is preferred. Giant ironweed naturalizes readily in moist soils and can be aggressive — allow 4–6 feet of space.
directions: Sow seed in fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; giant ironweed is one of the most dramatic native wildflowers for the back of a large border or naturalized area, growing 5–10 feet tall with flat-topped clusters of intense royal purple flowers in late summer and fall that are irresistible to monarchs, painted ladies, giant swallowtails, and dozens of native bee species. The vivid purple color is one of the most saturated of any native wildflower. It grows naturally along stream banks, moist woodland edges, and roadside ditches throughout the eastern US and is one of the most reliable and showy native plants for late-season pollinator value. The tall stems and architectural seed heads provide winter interest and bird food. It spreads by rhizome in moist soil and should be given space or divided annually to control spread in the border.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil.
directions: Direct sow after frost in warm soil, spacing tall plants about a foot apart for good airflow. Giant Purple bears bright 4-6 inch blooms on long, sturdy stems that make excellent long-lasting cut flowers. It is heat tolerant and disease resistant, blooming endlessly until frost when cut and deadheaded. Water at the base and space generously to reduce powdery mildew.

plant_note: Attach offset pups to the rough bark of a host tree or mount on a piece of untreated wood using monofilament or cotton string; do not use wire or metal clips. Giant Wild Pine requires no soil, absorbing water and nutrients through its leaf scales — mist 2–3 times per week in warm months and once per week in winter.
directions: Giant Wild Pine is the largest native bromeliad in the continental United States, native to the cypress swamps, mangrove edges, and tropical hardwood hammocks of south Florida and the Florida Keys, where it grows as a true epiphyte on the branches of cypress, live oak, and other trees, absorbing all its water and nutrients from the air, rain, and debris through specialized scales (trichomes) on its leaves. Its dramatic tank-forming rosette of stiff, gray-green leaves holds a cup of water that serves as a vital micro-habitat for tree frogs, insects, and even small lizards; a tall, multi-colored flower spike of red and purple bracts emerges in spring, followed by the death of the main rosette and production of one or more offset pups that continue the plant's life. Listed as Threatened in Florida due to the invasion of the Mexican Bromeliad Weevil (Metamasius callizona), which has devastated wild populations, Giant Wild Pine is most effectively grown secured to the rough bark of a host tree in bright, indirect light with regular misting to replace the moisture it would receive from tropical rains and morning dew.

plant_note: Divide clumps in early spring or plant nursery transplants in moist, neutral to slightly alkaline, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade. Glade Fern is often found naturally on moist limestone outcrops and in rich, calcareous woodland glades.
directions: Glade Fern is an unusual and distinctive native fern, native to the rich, moist, shaded glades, ravine floors, and calcareous woodland slopes of eastern North America from Quebec to Georgia and Tennessee, where it grows in the mineral-rich soils derived from limestone or dolomite that many ferns cannot tolerate. Unlike most ferns with finely divided pinnate fronds, Glade Fern produces long, strap-shaped, single-pinnate fronds with widely spaced, pointed pinnae that give it a loose, airy, elegant appearance quite different from the denser-textured wood ferns and lady ferns. A deciduous fern that dies back to the ground in fall and re-emerges in spring, it spreads slowly by short-creeping rhizomes to form loose colonies and is best used as a textural contrast plant among finer-textured shade ground covers. Grow in moist, humus-rich, neutral to slightly alkaline soil (pH 6.5–7.5) in partial to full shade; the preference for slightly alkaline conditions makes it a good companion to plants that enjoy limestone-derived soils.

plant_note: Plant corms after the last frost; make successive plantings every 2 weeks through early summer for extended bloom.
directions: Plant corms 4–6 in deep in well-drained soil with the pointed end up after the last frost; succession plant every 2 weeks through midsummer for continuous cut flowers. Tall varieties need staking; a single bamboo cane tied loosely to the stem works well. In zones below 7, dig corms after frost blackens the foliage, dry for 2–3 weeks, remove offsets (cormels), and store at 35–45°F in mesh bags through winter. Harvest cut spikes when the lowest 2–3 buds show color for longest vase life.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; plants typically bloom in their second year from seed.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep; cold stratification is not required but may speed germination. Established globe thistle is extremely drought-tolerant and thrives in poor, sandy, or chalky well-drained soil — fertile, moist soil causes lax growth and flopping. The spiky steel-blue globe flowers are excellent for fresh cutting and dry perfectly for everlasting arrangements if cut just before fully open. Divide clumps in spring every 4–5 years using a sharp spade; they are slow to re-establish so divisions should be generous.

plant_note: Plant large bulbs in fall for dramatic late-spring globes.
directions: Plant Globemaster allium bulbs in fall six to eight inches deep in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun. Each stout stem produces a massive violet globe up to ten inches across that lasts for weeks and draws countless bees. Position the plants among perennials that hide the fading foliage, which yellows before the flowers finish. The dried seed heads make striking architectural accents in the garden or in arrangements.

plant_note: Plant tubers horizontally 4 in deep after last frost; in zones 8+ can be planted in early spring once soil is 65°F.
directions: Plant the fragile, finger-shaped tubers horizontally 4 in deep, handling carefully to avoid snapping them; provide a trellis or support as the vine climbs by leaf-tip tendrils. The striking reflexed flowers in flame-red and yellow with wavy-edged petals are produced freely from midsummer into fall. All parts of the plant are highly toxic if ingested — site away from children and pets. In zones below 8, dig tubers after foliage dies, dry them, and store in dry peat moss at 50–60°F over winter.

plant_note: Plant small bulbs 3 in deep in fall; naturalize in lawns, rock gardens, or under deciduous trees.
directions: Plant bulbs in fall, 3 in deep and 2–3 in apart, in any well-drained soil; glory-of-the-snow blooms very early, often pushing through snow in late winter to early spring. The vivid blue, star-shaped flowers with white centers naturalize freely by both offsets and self-seeding, forming large colonies over time. Plant in large drifts under deciduous trees where they receive full sun while leafless and will not be disturbed during summer dormancy. No special care is needed once established; bulbs rarely need dividing.

plant_note: Surface-sow since light aids germination; as a biennial it flowers the second year.
directions: Surface-sow spring through midsummer since light aids germination, thinning plants to 12-18 inches. This sturdy biennial produces foliage the first year and tall spikes of extra-large gloxinia-like blooms the second year, and it self-sows readily for naturalized areas. It performs best in part shade with rich, moist, well-drained soil. Note that all parts of foxglove are toxic if eaten, so keep away from children and pets.

plant_note: Sow seeds on the surface (needs light to germinate) in early spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade; goatsbeard is dioecious — plant multiple plants for the best display, as male plants produce slightly more dramatic flower plumes.
directions: Sow on the soil surface in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade in early spring; goatsbeard is one of the most magnificent native shade perennials in North America, forming large, bold clumps of compound, ferny leaves topped in early to midsummer by massive, arching plumes of creamy-white flowers resembling a giant astilbe — earning it the common name. Male plants produce the showiest, most upright plumes; female plants produce somewhat more pendulous plumes followed by seed heads that dry attractively. It grows naturally in moist, rich woodland slopes and creek banks throughout the eastern US and Pacific Northwest, performing best in cool, moist, shaded conditions. An outstanding bold-textured plant for the large-scale woodland garden or shaded border, paired with hostas, ferns, and native wildflowers. Not suited to hot, dry climates; performs best in zone 3–7.

plant_note: Surface-sow as light aids germination; blooms the first year from an early start.
directions: Surface-sow, pressing seed onto the mix since light aids germination, and thin compact plants to about a foot apart. Goblin is a first-year-flowering, compact perennial with red and yellow bicolored blooms that keep coming from summer through frost and attract butterflies. Round, reddish-brown seed heads follow the petals and add interest. It thrives in full sun and lean, well-drained soil and tolerates heat and drought once established.

plant_note: Sow in place as soon as soil is workable; dislikes transplanting.
directions: Direct sow godetia into lean, well-drained soil in early spring, barely covering the fine seed. It resents rich soil and heavy feeding, which produce foliage at the expense of the satiny cup-shaped blooms. Thin seedlings to 8-12 inches and keep evenly moist until established, then water sparingly. Cool weather intensifies the pink and lavender flower colors, so it performs best in spring and early summer before extreme heat.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification; seed requires 60–90 days of cold, moist conditions before spring germination. Division of established clumps in spring provides faster establishment.
directions: Sow cold-stratified seed in fall or early spring in moist, average to rich, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; this early-blooming native carrot-family plant produces flat-topped clusters of small yellow flowers in April–May, making it one of the earliest sources of pollen for native bees, beetles, and flies at a time when little else is blooming. It is also an important early host plant for black swallowtail butterflies, whose caterpillars feed on the ferny foliage. The plants are short-lived but self-sow reliably in suitable conditions. Excellent at the front of the native plant border or naturalized in a moist meadow.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in the saturated muddy soil at pond margins or submerged up to 12 inches in still to slow-moving water. Golden Club tolerates both shallow standing water and periodically dry pond margins.
directions: Golden Club is a striking native aquatic perennial, native to the still waters, bayous, and blackwater swamps of the eastern United States from Massachusetts south to Florida and west to Louisiana, producing distinctive golden-tipped white spadices (flower spikes) that emerge from the water in early spring before the leaves fully expand — one of the earliest aquatic plants to bloom and a valuable early pollinator resource. The large, velvety, blue-green elliptic leaves float on the water surface in deep water or stand erect in shallower zones, and the waxy leaf cuticle causes water to bead and roll off in perfect spheres — a phenomenon visible throughout the growing season. Easy to grow in full sun to partial shade in the marginal zone of water gardens, ponds, and rain gardens, Golden Club forms clumps that slowly expand by rhizome and requires minimal care once established. Use in naturalized pond margins alongside pickerelweed, blue flag iris, and lizard's tail for a layered native aquatic planting.

plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring after frost danger passes in moist, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; golden dewdrop grows rapidly in warm weather and can reach 6–8 feet in a single season. Fruit and leaves are toxic to humans and pets — site away from areas frequented by children and animals.
directions: Plant in well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; golden dewdrop is a vigorous tropical shrub that provides two seasons of ornamental interest — lavender to purple tubular flowers from spring through fall, followed by clusters of small golden-yellow berries that drape like strings of dewdrops and persist on the plant for months, creating the common name. The berries are the primary food source for many songbirds, particularly mockingbirds and robins. Be aware that the berries and leaves are toxic to humans, dogs, and cats. The popular weeping cultivar 'Geisha Girl' has larger, richer purple flowers; 'Gold Mound' is a compact, dwarf golden-foliage form. Prune severely in late winter to maintain a compact, manageable shape — unpruned plants can reach tree size. Outstanding as a large informal screen, espalier, or specimen shrub for zone 8–11 gardens where its rapid growth and dual seasonal interest make it highly effective.

plant_note: Plant divisions or plugs in early spring in moist, shaded areas; spreads by stolons and self-sows to form dense colonies in favorable conditions.
directions: Plant in moist to wet, fertile, slightly acidic soil in full shade to partial sun; golden groundsel is one of the few native plants that blooms brilliantly in deep shade, producing bright yellow daisy-like flowers in early spring that provide critical early pollen for native mining bees. The semi-evergreen, kidney-shaped basal leaves form an attractive groundcover year-round. It spreads aggressively in moist shade by stolons and self-seeding — excellent for naturalizing a problem wet-shade area but may need management in a manicured garden. Plants in dry shade will grow more slowly and remain smaller. Combine with wild blue phlox and Virginia bluebells for a spring shade spectacle.

plant_note: Nick or scarify the hard seed coat and soak 24 hours before planting, or plant rooted container specimens in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; golden shower tree is fast-growing and drought tolerant once established. In zone 9a, plant against a south-facing wall for frost protection.
directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; golden shower tree is one of the most spectacular of all tropical flowering trees — the national tree of Thailand — producing cascading chains of brilliant golden-yellow flowers 12–18 inches long that completely cover the tree before the new leaves emerge in spring, creating a breathtaking golden cascade. It grows fast (3–5 feet per year) in frost-free conditions, reaching 25–40 feet, and tolerates drought, alkaline soil, and seaside conditions once established. The flowers are edible in some cultures (flower buds in curries). In zone 9a it may die back in severe freezes but typically regrows from established root systems; site in a protected location sheltered from north winds. A magnificent specimen or street tree for zone 9–11 Gulf Coast landscapes.

plant_note: Root stem cuttings in moist potting mix or plant container plants after nighttime temperatures are consistently above 55°F; golden shrimp plant establishes quickly in warm weather and begins blooming within 6–8 weeks of transplanting.
directions: Root cuttings or plant nursery transplants in moist, fertile, well-drained soil in partial shade to filtered sun — golden shrimp plant is one of the most continuously showy flowering plants for shaded Gulf Coast gardens, producing upright spikes of overlapping golden-yellow bracts with small white tubular flowers emerging from the tips, blooming nearly year-round in frost-free climates from late spring through fall. The bright golden cones create striking color in shaded beds, under trees, and along north-facing walls where few other flowering plants perform well. Pinch stem tips regularly in the first month after planting to encourage bushy growth; established plants can be cut back by half in late winter to rejuvenate. In zone 9, top growth is killed by frost but plants often resprout from roots if the freeze is brief; in zone 10–11 it grows as a permanent landscape shrub. An outstanding container plant for shaded patios and screened porches.

plant_note: Plant bulbs with the neck at or just below the soil surface in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in partial shade to full sun; golden spider lily blooms most reliably when allowed to dry out in summer — reduce irrigation or plant in an area with summer dry conditions.
directions: Plant bulbs with the neck at or near the soil surface in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in partial shade to full sun — golden spider lily is the warm-climate companion to the red spider lily (Lycoris radiata), adapted to zone 7–10 and particularly well-suited to the summer heat and humidity of the Gulf Coast, where it thrives with neglect and summer drought. Like its red relative, the dramatic golden-yellow spider lily flowers appear on bare stems in late summer and early fall — naked, leafless scapes 18–24 inches tall topped with spidery clusters of reflexed petals and long, arching stamens — well before the strap-like leaves emerge in fall and persist through winter. The brilliant golden-yellow color is uniquely striking among Lycoris species and combines beautifully in the fall garden with red spider lilies, hurricane lilies, and copper rain lilies. Clumps increase slowly over years; divide only when flowering declines.

common_name: Golden St. John's Wort (Shrubby St. John's Wort)
plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs in fall in average to dry, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; golden St. John's wort tolerates rocky, shallow, or poor soils and is drought tolerant once established — avoid poorly drained or consistently wet sites.
directions: Golden St. John's wort is a native deciduous shrub of dry, rocky hillsides and cedar glades in the southeastern United States, producing profuse, large, golden-yellow flowers with prominent, brush-like clusters of yellow stamens for several weeks in summer — among the most floriferous and ornamentally attractive of all native shrubs for dry, rocky, or difficult soil situations. Unlike the weedy, herbaceous common St. John's wort (Hypericum perforatum), this species is a well-behaved woody shrub that stays in bounds without invasive spreading, making it an excellent low-maintenance landscape plant for dry sunny borders, rock gardens, and native shrub masses. The blue-green foliage and exfoliating gray bark are attractive through the growing season, and the persistent, reddish seed capsules provide winter interest; it is deer resistant and tolerates drought, heat, and poor soil with minimal care. An Award of Garden Merit recipient from the Royal Horticultural Society, it is popular in European gardens and deserves much wider use in American native plant landscapes as an alternative to non-native ornamental shrubs.

plant_note: Plant rooted cuttings or container plants in spring after temperatures are consistently above 60°F in moist, very well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile soil in full sun; all parts of allamanda are toxic — wear gloves when pruning and wash hands thoroughly. The milky sap causes skin irritation in sensitive individuals.
directions: Plant in full sun in moist, fertile, very well-drained soil; golden trumpet vine is one of the showiest tropical flowering plants available for Gulf Coast gardens, producing an almost continuous display of large (3–4 inch), brilliant canary-yellow trumpet-shaped flowers from spring through fall on glossy, leathery-leaved vining or shrubby stems. It can be trained as a vine on trellises and fences or pruned as a rounded shrub in formal plantings. The shrubby cultivar 'Cherries Jubilee' has deep rose-purple flowers; 'Hendersonii' bears larger golden flowers. Regular fertilization during the growing season is essential for continuous bloom. All parts of the plant are toxic — wear gloves when handling the milky sap and keep away from children and pets. In zone 9, protect from hard freezes; plants may suffer foliar damage but typically recover from established roots once warm weather returns.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring where plants are to grow; poppies resent transplanting.
directions: This California poppy carries sunny yellow petals with warm orange centers on wispy, blue-green foliage. Sow seeds directly where they are to grow, since the taproot dislikes being moved, and simply press them into the soil surface in early spring. Give it full sun and lean, well-drained ground, where it thrives with little water once established. It self-sows freely and naturalizes beautifully in meadows and dry banks.

plant_note: Sow seed in early spring or fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; the golden cultivar 'Aureum' (Bowles' Golden Grass) seeds true to color and naturalizes readily in appropriate shaded, moist conditions.
directions: Sow seed in early spring or fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; golden wood millet — especially the cultivar 'Aureum' (Bowles' Golden Grass) — is one of the most luminous and valuable shade plants available, producing tufts of graceful, arching bright gold-yellow leaves that seem to glow with internal light in the darkest garden corners. The delicate, open-branched flower panicles appear in spring with golden seeds carried on wispy threads, adding airy texture. A cool-season grass that peaks in spring, goes semi-dormant in summer heat, then revives in fall. It self-sows generously (the golden form seeds true) to naturalize in moist, shaded woodland gardens. Cut back in late summer if it looks tired. Combines beautifully with blue hostas, spring wildflowers, and ferns where its golden light provides striking contrast in deep shade.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring or fall; clump-forming cultivars are better-behaved than the straight species.
directions: Plant in full sun in average to poor, well-drained soil; goldenrod thrives in lean conditions and becomes overly lax and floppy in rich, moist soil. The arching plumes of golden-yellow flowers from late summer through fall are essential late-season food for monarch butterflies and dozens of native bee species. Contrary to popular belief, goldenrod does not cause hay fever — its pollen is heavy and insect-carried, not windborne. Choose named compact cultivars like 'Fireworks' or 'Little Lemon' for well-behaved garden varieties; divide every 2–3 years to control spread.

plant_note: Divide clumps in early spring or fall and plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; Goldie's Fern is the largest of the native eastern wood ferns and benefits from deep, rich, consistently moist soil to produce its full-size fronds.
directions: Goldie's Fern is the largest of the native eastern wood ferns, native to rich, moist deciduous forests from Quebec and the Great Lakes south to Georgia and Tennessee, where it grows in deep, humus-rich, neutral to slightly acidic soils — often in ravines, north-facing slopes, and sheltered hollows alongside sugar maple, yellow birch, and basswood. Its massive, arching, golden-green fronds can reach 4 feet in length and create a dramatic bold-textured mass in the shade garden; though deciduous, the fronds remain attractive through much of the season before dying back in late fall. Goldie's Fern is less common in cultivation than many wood ferns but is reliably hardy and long-lived in appropriate conditions — it simply requires the richest, most consistently moist soil and the deepest shade you can provide. Divide clumps in early spring before the fronds unfurl, or plant nursery transplants; mulch heavily with leaf litter to maintain consistent moisture and soil temperature.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil.
directions: Direct sow after frost about an inch deep in full sun, thinning to 12-18 inches apart. Goldy Honey Bear has thick, multi-branching stalks that stay upright without staking and open to reveal green centers that mature into fully double, solid-gold 5-7 inch blooms. Its nearly pollenless flowers are ideal for clean-cut bouquets. Water while young and cut regularly to encourage more of the branching blooms.

plant_note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed.
directions: Sow seed 1/4 in deep in warm starting mix; soak seed overnight to speed germination. Gomphrena thrives in heat and humidity and is highly drought tolerant once established. The clover-like flowers hold their color and shape exceptionally well when dried — harvest stems just as the heads reach full color. Deadheading encourages continuous fresh bloom throughout the summer.

plant_note: Start indoors in darkness and transplant while cool, or sow late summer for fall and spring color.
directions: Start seed indoors 8-10 weeks before setting out, covering it since pansies germinate best in darkness, and transplant while weather is cool. This custom mix brightens the garden with light and dark blue blooms and provides color for spring and fall even in cold climates. The edible flowers are lovely as a garnish on soft cheese or baked goods. Deadhead to prolong bloom and keep the soil evenly moist.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil; needs a long season to mature and cure.
directions: Direct sow after frost in warm soil, giving the vines plenty of room or a sturdy trellis to sprawl. This playful blend yields petite festive gourds in warty, smooth, striped, and bumpy shapes in vibrant orange and green, perfect for fall decorating and crafts. Allow the gourds to fully mature on the vine, then cure the hard shells so they last for years. Water evenly during fruit set and keep foliage dry to limit mildew.

plant_note: Plant large bulbs with the neck at or just above the soil surface in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moist to moderately moist, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; grand crinum requires ample room to develop — the mature bulb can reach 12 inches across and the leaf spread 5–6 feet. Avoid disturbing established clumps as transplanting sets the plant back significantly.
directions: Plant with the bulb neck at or just above the soil surface in well-drained, moist, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; grand crinum lily is one of the most enduring and spectacular large-scale ornamental bulbs for coastal Gulf Coast landscapes, producing massive strap-like leaves and large, fragrant, spider-like clusters of white flowers with long, recurved petals and prominent red anthers on stout stems 2–3 feet tall — blooming repeatedly from spring through fall. A single well-established bulb produces a clump 4–5 feet wide with multiple bloom stalks simultaneously, creating a dramatic tropical effect. Crinum asiaticum is exceptionally salt tolerant — it is one of the few large ornamental plants that thrives in coastal beachfront conditions right to the edge of salt spray. It also tolerates periodic flooding and waterlogged soils remarkably well, making it valuable for low spots and wet areas where other plants struggle. Essentially indestructible once established; plants decades old are commonly found thriving without any maintenance in old Gulf Coast gardens.

plant_note: Nick and soak seeds, then direct sow once soil is warm.
directions: This beloved heirloom climbs quickly to produce large, velvety purple trumpets marked with a striking star-shaped center. Nick the hard seed coats and soak overnight, then sow directly against a trellis or fence once the soil is warm. Blooms open in the morning and feed pollinators, with heart-shaped foliage covering the support all season. Provide sturdy vertical structure, as vines can reach ten feet or more.

plant_note: Nick and soak seed, then direct sow after frost at the base of a support.
directions: Nick the seed coat and soak overnight, then direct sow after frost at the base of a fence or trellis. This dependable heirloom is a vigorous climber with deep purple blooms and heart-shaped leaves that cover supports, trail on the ground, or spill from baskets. It may self-sow, so remove spent flowers if you want to limit volunteers. Note that parts of the plant are toxic if eaten, so keep away from children and pets.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall, 3–4 in deep; foliage emerges in fall and overwinters, so plant early enough for roots to establish.
directions: Plant small bulbs 3–4 in deep in any well-drained soil; foliage emerges in fall and overwinters as a low ground cover, with tight clusters of cobalt-blue, urn-shaped flowers appearing in early to mid-spring. Muscari naturalizes aggressively by offsets and self-seeding and can spread widely — site where this is desirable or divide clumps every few years in fall to control spread. Excellent planted beneath tulips and daffodils to create complementary color combinations. Deer and rodents generally leave them alone.

plant_note: Plant corms from nursery-propagated sources in spring in wet, acidic, peaty or sandy, nutrient-poor soil in full sun to partial shade; grass pink orchid requires an open, wet, acidic bog or seep environment with full sun to partial shade and nutrient-poor conditions.
directions: Grass pink orchid is one of the most common and widely distributed of all eastern North American bog orchids — a single grasslike leaf and an upright flower stem bearing up to 10 vivid magenta-pink flowers with a distinctive bearded lip appear in late spring and early summer in acidic, wet bogs, seepage slopes, pitcher plant savannas, and longleaf pine flatwoods across the Gulf Coastal Plain. Unlike most orchids, grass pink orchid grows from small, underground corms that can be purchased from specialist native plant nurseries and planted in appropriate bog garden conditions, making it more accessible than most native terrestrial orchids. The flowers practice deceptive pollination — the bearded yellow lip (which resembles a pollen-covered stamen to naive bees) flips downward when a bee lands, dropping the bee onto the actual pollen masses on the column below; bees learn quickly and return infrequently, so the plant depends on a steady supply of inexperienced native bees. An important component of Gulf Coast bog gardens when combined with pitcher plants (Sarracenia), sundews, and bog sedges in full-sun, wet, acidic soil.

plant_note: Direct sow seed in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, dry to average, poor to average soil in full sun; gray goldenrod is the most drought-tolerant of the common eastern goldenrods, thriving in dry, sandy, and rocky soils where other goldenrods cannot establish.
directions: Sow seed in fall in well-drained, dry to average, poor to average soil in full sun — gray goldenrod is the most drought-tolerant of the common eastern North American goldenrods, taking its common name from the grayish, downy-hairy stems and leaves that help reduce water loss in its characteristic dry, open habitats: rocky outcrops, sandy barrens, dry roadsides, and dry open woodland edges throughout the eastern US and into the Great Plains. The one-sided arching racemes of golden yellow flowers curve gracefully toward the ground in late summer and fall, providing important late-season nectar for migrating monarch butterflies and a wide range of native bees. While not as showy as some taller goldenrods, gray goldenrod is invaluable for difficult dry, sandy, or rocky sites where creating a pollinator-supporting native planting is otherwise challenging. Historically used by Native American nations as a yellow dye plant.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring or fall; cold stratification for 30 days improves germination rate. Plants may bloom the first year from an early sowing.
directions: Sow in well-drained, average to poor, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun; the gray-headed coneflower is a tall, drought-tolerant prairie native with drooping yellow petals reflexed away from a distinctive elongated gray-brown cone, giving it an unmistakable silhouette in the summer meadow. It blooms from midsummer through fall and spreads gently by self-seeding without becoming aggressive. The crushed foliage smells pleasantly of anise. An important pollen source for prairie bees and the seed is taken by goldfinches in fall. Mixes beautifully with other tall prairie plants like goldenrod and ironweed in naturalistic plantings.

plant_note: Surface-sow seed (do not cover — light required for germination) 4–6 weeks before last frost; alternatively divide rosettes in early spring from established plants.
directions: Scatter seed on the soil surface — do not cover, as light is essential for germination — in consistently moist, fertile soil in partial shade to full sun. This native provides brilliant blue flower spikes in late summer when most other plants are winding down, bridging the gap between midsummer and fall bloomers. It naturally grows in wet meadows, stream banks, and woodland edges and is one of the best plants for rain gardens, retention areas, and moist shade gardens. The plant is monocarpic (dies after flowering) but reliably self-sows in favorable conditions, and new basal rosettes can be separated and replanted in fall or early spring. Excellent companion for cardinal flower (L. cardinalis) in moist areas.

plant_note: Plant rooted divisions or stem sections in spring or fall in any moist, well-drained soil in full shade to full sun; greater periwinkle is extraordinarily adaptable and establishes quickly from stem sections placed in moist soil. It spreads aggressively by arching stems that root at the nodes — contain with edging where spread must be controlled.
directions: Plant in any well-drained to moderately moist soil in full shade to partial sun; greater periwinkle is a vigorous, trailing, evergreen groundcover with larger, glossier, darker green leaves than the more refined Vinca minor — it covers ground more aggressively and thrives in the heat and humidity of zones 7–11 where V. minor can struggle. Sky-blue, star-shaped, 1.5-inch flowers appear from early spring through summer. Particularly valuable in the hot Southeast and Gulf Coast where its heat tolerance far exceeds V. minor. The variegated form ('Variegata', with leaves edged in creamy white or yellow) provides striking year-round foliage interest in shaded spots. Be aware that V. major can be invasive in riparian zones and warm-climate areas where it spreads into native plant communities — do not plant near natural areas or waterways in warm climates. Excellent for covering slopes, under trees, and in difficult shaded areas in contained residential landscapes.

plant_note: Soak tubers overnight before planting in fall.
directions: Soak the small knobby tubers overnight, then plant Grecian windflower in fall about two to three inches deep in well-drained, humus-rich soil. The daisy-like flowers in blue, pink, or white carpet the ground in early spring beneath deciduous trees and shrubs. It naturalizes freely in sun to partial shade and goes dormant by early summer. Plant in generous drifts and leave the foliage to ripen so the colony can expand year after year.

plant_note: Plant corms in fall in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade; green dragon is closely related to jack-in-the-pulpit but differs in having a single large compound leaf and a dramatically elongated spadix protruding 3–6 inches from the spathe.
directions: Plant corms in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade — green dragon is the less-commonly-grown native relative of jack-in-the-pulpit, equally bizarre and fascinating in flower but with a different structure: a single large compound leaf (7–15 leaflets radiating from the tip of the stem) and a distinctive flower featuring a narrow, elongated spadix that projects 3–6 inches beyond the green spathe like the tongue of a dragon — giving both the common name and the specific epithet dracontium. The bright red berries clustered on the spadix in late summer are strikingly ornamental. It grows naturally in moist, rich, shaded bottomland forests and stream banks throughout the eastern US. The corms and all parts of the plant contain calcium oxalate crystals and are toxic if consumed raw; like jack-in-the-pulpit, historically used by Native American nations after careful preparation to neutralize the crystals. Outstanding bold-textured native for shaded, moist woodland gardens.

plant_note: Direct sow in cool soil around the last frost; it dislikes root disturbance.
directions: Direct sow in cool soil around the last frost since ammi resents transplanting, barely covering the seed. Green Mist produces large rounded umbels that start bold green and turn white as the tiny flowers open, a favorite filler for mixed bouquets. Also called bishop's flower or false Queen Anne's lace, it is nearly carefree and attracts pollinators. Provide full sun and steady moisture, and cut stems for the vase as blooms open.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring once the soil is workable; foliage emerges after the last frost.
directions: Green Patriot is a shade-loving perennial grown for its bold foliage of dark-green centers edged in lighter margins, forming a tidy mound. Plant in partial to full shade in rich, moisture-retentive soil and keep it evenly watered. Lavender summer flowers rise above the leaves and attract pollinators. Cut back spent foliage in fall and divide clumps every few years in early spring to maintain vigor; watch for slugs.

plant_note: Plant divisions or plugs in spring in partial shade; space at 12 inches for coverage within one full growing season as plants spread by both stolons and seed.
directions: Plant in moist to moderately dry, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; green and gold is one of the finest native groundcovers for zone 8–9 southern gardens, producing bright yellow daisy flowers from early spring through late fall — an extraordinarily long bloom season for a shade plant. The semi-evergreen foliage forms a dense, weed-suppressing mat that spreads by stolons and self-sows without becoming invasive. It tolerates the root competition and dry summer shade under mature trees better than most groundcovers. The northern ecotype (var. virginianum) has a more upright habit, while the southern ecotype (var. australe) is more prostrate and heat tolerant — choose the southern ecotype for zone 8–9 gardens.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring; tolerates light frost and cool weather.
directions: This rare heirloom calendula opens warm gold, peach, and orange blooms each accented by an unusual lime-green center. Sow seeds directly in early spring, covering lightly since they germinate best in darkness, and thin to give plants room. This cool-season annual tolerates light frost and blooms best before summer heat, so successive sowings extend the show. The edible petals brighten salads and the plant has a long history of medicinal use; deadhead often to prolong flowering.

plant_note: Direct sow scarified seeds in spring in dry, well-drained, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; guajillo tolerates rocky caliche soils and thrives in the Hill Country and south Texas brush country — avoid clay soils and poor drainage.
directions: Guajillo is a large native shrub or small tree of south and central Texas brush country, producing very fragrant, fluffy white-cream flower heads in late winter and early spring when few other plants are in bloom — its abundant, sweetly scented blossoms make guajillo the most important honey plant in south Texas, where beekeepers prize 'guajillo honey' as a premium single-varietal product with a distinctive, mild floral character. The plant is unarmed (thornless), making it more landscape-friendly than many other native acacias; its feathery, twice-pinnate leaves cast dappled shade and give the plant a graceful, airy appearance throughout the growing season. Dense thickets of guajillo provide critical thermal cover and escape cover for white-tailed deer, wild turkey, and quail in south Texas brush country, and the plant is commonly used in habitat restoration on the Edwards Plateau and south Texas plains. It is highly drought tolerant once established and thrives in the caliche and limestone soils that typify the Texas Hill Country and Winter Garden regions.

plant_note: Direct sow fresh seeds in fall in sandy, well-drained, acidic to neutral soil in full sun; seeds naturally cold-stratify over winter and germinate in spring. Established corms can be divided in fall and replanted immediately. Gulf Blazingstar is highly specific to dry, sandy soils and will not thrive in clay or poorly drained locations.
directions: Gulf Blazingstar is one of the most distinctive and beautiful of the southeastern blazingstars, native to the dry, sandy soils of longleaf pine savannas, coastal plain sandhills, and open flatwoods from North Carolina to Texas along the Gulf and South Atlantic coastal plain. The feathery, pinkish-purple flower heads are fringed by conspicuously elongated, spreading scales (phyllaries) that give each cluster a soft, paintbrush-like appearance that immediately sets it apart from the tighter, more cylindrical heads of most other Liatris species. Blooming from September through November, Gulf Blazingstar is a critical late-season nectar source for migrating monarch butterflies, native bumblebees, and sphinx moths preparing for winter. Direct sow fresh seeds in fall in sandy, infertile, well-drained, acidic soil in full sun; the corms that form are persistent and long-lived, eventually forming impressive clumps that can be divided every 3–4 years.

common_name: Gulf Coast Muhly (Gulf Muhly, Gulf Hairgrass)
plant_note: Plant divisions or container transplants in spring in very well-drained, sandy, slightly acidic to neutral, lean soil in full sun to partial shade; Gulf Coast muhly grows naturally in coastal flatwoods, pine savannas, and well-drained sandy areas from the Carolinas to Texas and tolerates both drought and brief flooding.
directions: Plant in well-drained, sandy, lean soil in full sun to partial shade; Gulf Coast muhly is one of the most spectacular native grasses of the southeastern coastal plain, producing dense tufts of fine, arching leaves topped with extraordinary clouds of pink-purple to reddish plumes in October–November — the fall bloom is breathtaking in mass plantings, creating a haze of color resembling smoke drifting over the landscape at sunset. It is the native counterpart to the widely planted inland pink muhly (M. capillaris) but is even better adapted to the sandy, humid Gulf Coast environment and more salt-tolerant. The fine-textured foliage is attractive throughout the growing season. Shear to 4–6 inches in late winter before new growth begins. An outstanding fall-blooming native for the Gulf Coast landscape.

plant_note: Plant offsets or container specimens in spring in very well-drained, sandy to loamy, dry, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Gulf Coast yucca grows naturally in the sandy coastal prairies and open pine flatwoods of eastern Texas and Louisiana and is extremely well adapted to the heat, humidity, and poor soils of the Gulf Coast.
directions: Plant offsets or container specimens in very well-drained, dry, sandy to loamy soil in full sun — Gulf Coast yucca is a stemless or short-stemmed native yucca endemic to the sandy coastal prairies and pine flatwoods of Louisiana and East Texas, forming low rosettes of long, flexible, dark blue-green leaves with softer, less-rigid tips than Spanish bayonet (Yucca aloifolia). Tall, upright flower stalks 3–6 feet emerge in early summer bearing large clusters of creamy-white, bell-shaped flowers that are the exclusive food source for the yucca moth (Tegeticula sp.), which simultaneously pollinates the flowers and lays its eggs in the ovary — one of the most specialized and obligate plant-pollinator relationships in nature. The relationship is so specific that Gulf Coast yucca will not set seed without its yucca moth pollinator. An excellent, low-maintenance native structural plant for sandy, poor soils, drought garden designs, and coastal prairie restoration.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun in saline, alkaline, or brackish wet to seasonally flooded soils along Gulf Coast bay margins, salt flats, and transition zones; gulf cordgrass tolerates both salt and alkaline conditions that would kill most grasses.
directions: Gulf cordgrass (formerly Spartina spartinae) is a dominant warm-season perennial grass of Gulf Coast salt flats, tidal margins, and alkaline coastal prairies from Texas to Florida, forming dense monocultures that bridge the ecological transition zone between tidal salt marsh and upland prairie. Its stiff, round, sharply pointed leaf blades make it distinctive and somewhat prickly to handle, but the plant is invaluable for stabilizing saline, alkaline, or brackish coastal soils where few other plants establish — it has exceptional salt tolerance that exceeds most native coastal grasses. Historically, gulf cordgrass prairies were important feeding and nesting habitat for Attwater's prairie chickens and migratory shorebirds; coastal prairie bird species including horned larks, savannah sparrows, and sedge wrens use the dense stands for winter foraging. In Gulf Coast coastal restoration projects and living shoreline designs, gulf cordgrass is planted in the transition zone between smooth cordgrass tidal marsh and upland black needle rush or salt flat communities, where it reliably establishes and spreads via rhizomes.

plant_note: Plant plugs or divisions in spring in wet, moist to seasonally flooded, brackish or fresh, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; gulf paspalum is a wetland-obligate coastal grass that thrives in tidal marsh margins, freshwater-saltwater transition zones, and seasonally flooded Gulf Coast flats.
directions: Gulf paspalum is a native grass of Gulf Coast tidal marshes, freshwater-brackish ecotones, and moist coastal flats from Texas to Florida, commonly forming dense colonies in the transition zone between fresh and salt water that are among the most important habitat areas for mottled ducks, blue-winged teal, and other dabbling waterfowl in the western Gulf Coast. The grass produces single-spoked seed heads distinctive among Gulf Coast grasses that ripen in summer and fall and are heavily consumed by waterfowl; its dense, matting rhizomes stabilize the soft, saturated soils of tidal margins and help resist shoreline erosion from boat wake and tidal action. In coastal garden ponds, rain garden overflow areas, and constructed freshwater wetlands in zone 8–11, gulf paspalum provides low, dense, wildlife-friendly groundcover in the shallowest water zones; it tolerates both freshwater flooding and moderate salinity. A component of Gulf Coast wetland restoration seed mixes, it establishes quickly and provides immediate stabilization and wildlife value.

plant_note: Direct sow seed in fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained to moderately moist, loamy to sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; Gulf penstemon is one of the few penstemons adapted to the heat and humidity of the Gulf Coast, where most other species struggle.
directions: Direct sow in fall or transplant plugs in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade — Gulf penstemon is a rare native treasure: one of only a handful of Penstemon species adapted to the humid, wet winters and brutally hot summers of the Gulf Coast region from eastern Texas to Louisiana and western Mississippi. In spring it produces tall, airy spikes of small, tubular lavender to violet-blue flowers with white-striped throats that are intensely attractive to native bees (particularly bumblebees) and ruby-throated hummingbirds. It blooms prolifically in March through May, then sets seed and dies back to a basal rosette through summer heat. Self-sows reliably to persist in the garden; it thrives in moist, slightly acidic soils of pine flatwoods and stream margins — conditions very different from the dry, alkaline soils preferred by most western penstemons. The species to choose for humid South garden penstemon trials.

plant_note: Set out the potted or bare-root shrub after hard frost; this named cultivar is grown from plants, not seed.
directions: Plant this shrub rose in full sun with rich, well-drained soil, spacing plants 2-3 feet apart for their rounded habit. Gumball Goody blooms from spring to fall in a candy mix of light pink, deep pink, and yellow on a compact, tidy plant well suited to borders and containers. Water deeply and regularly during establishment and the growing season, keeping foliage dry to limit disease. Prune in late winter to shape and remove dead wood, and deadhead to encourage repeat flushes.

common_name: Habranthus (Copper Lily, Rain Lily, Upward-Facing Zephyr Lily)
plant_note: Plant bulbs 2–3 inches deep in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; habranthus is distinguished from Zephyranthes by its flowers, which are borne at an upward-angled rather than strictly upright orientation. Plant and then largely ignore — they naturalize and bloom after summer and fall rain events.
directions: Plant bulbs 2–3 inches deep in well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; habranthus (copper lily) is a summer-dormant rain lily that puts on a brief, intense show of large, bright pink trumpet flowers shortly after summer and fall rains, earning the name 'rain lily' for this reliable weather-triggered response. Unlike the straight-up flowers of Zephyranthes, habranthus flowers are borne at a jaunty upward angle, giving them a distinctive, graceful appearance. The flowers are 2–3 inches across, pale to deep rose-pink with a yellow base, and often striped. This species is more vigorous and larger-flowered than most rain lilies. In zone 7–10 the small bulbs naturalize freely to form expanding colonies in lawns and borders. A classic, low-maintenance companion to other Gulf South summer rain lilies (Zephyranthes) with which it can be mixed for a longer combined bloom season.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; hairy alumroot is the most heat-and-humidity-tolerant native Heuchera species and outperforms H. americana and H. sanguinea cultivars in zone 7–9 summer heat.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; hairy alumroot is the native Heuchera of the Southern Appalachians and Ozarks and is significantly more tolerant of heat and humidity than other native Heuchera species — making it the top choice for shade gardens in zone 7–9 where summer temperatures are brutal. The large, maple-shaped leaves with dense soft hairs (giving a velvety texture) are handsome throughout the season, and small white flowers on 2–3 foot stems appear in late summer when little else in the shade garden is blooming. This species is the parent of many popular hybrid 'coral bells' cultivars, including 'Autumn Bride' (exceptionally heat-tolerant with large white flowers in fall). The foliage is semi-evergreen in zone 7–8 and provides attractive winter interest. Divide every 3–4 years as the crown heaves upward from the soil.

plant_note: Direct sow seeds in fall in dry, rocky or sandy, well-drained, acidic to neutral, infertile soil in full sun. Hairy Goldenrod is well-adapted to dry rock outcrops, ledges, and sandy open areas and is particularly drought-tolerant among goldenrods once established.
directions: Hairy Goldenrod is a compact native perennial of dry, rocky or sandy open areas, shale barrens, rocky outcrops, and infertile upland soils across the northeastern United States, recognizable among goldenrods by its densely hairy (hispid) stems and leaves that give the entire plant a soft, grayish appearance. The small clusters of bright yellow flowers are produced on short, compact stems from August through September and attract native bees, small butterflies, and other pollinators visiting rocky, dry habitats where fewer plant species grow. Distinguished from other goldenrods by its smaller stature, hairy texture, and preference for lean, rocky, dry soils, Hairy Goldenrod is an excellent native choice for rock gardens, dry slopes, sandy paths, and xeriscapes where most goldenrods would languish. Direct sow seeds in fall in dry, rocky or sandy, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in full sun; established plants are very drought-tolerant and require no supplemental irrigation or soil amendment.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall in dry to average, well-drained, acidic to neutral, rocky or sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; hairy penstemon is exceptionally drought tolerant and performs well in rocky, poor, infertile soils where other plants struggle.
directions: Hairy penstemon is a widely adaptable native penstemon of rocky outcrops, dry meadows, open woodlands, and roadsides in the northeastern and north-central United States, producing spikes of pale violet to whitish, hairy-coated tubular flowers in late spring that are a critical food source for bumblebee queens emerging from winter dormancy and seeking early-season pollen and nectar. The plant is covered in fine hairs from the stems to the flower tubes — hence 'hirsutus' — giving it a soft, gray-green, velvety texture that is attractive in its own right; rosettes of glossy, toothed leaves persist year-round as a semi-evergreen groundcover in milder zones. It is one of the most drought tolerant and cold hardy of all eastern penstemons, adapting to virtually any dry, well-drained site in zones 3–8 with no supplemental irrigation once established; excellent for rock gardens, dry slopes, and hell-strip plantings in regions with cold, dry winters. Self-seeds moderately to form naturalized colonies in suitable rocky or sandy soils.

plant_note: Direct sow seed in fall in dry to moist, slightly acidic, well-drained, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; hairy skullcap grows naturally in dry to moist hardwood forest understories and tolerates a range of shade from partial to fairly deep — one of the more adaptable native skullcaps.
directions: Sow seed in fall in dry to moist, slightly acidic, well-drained, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade — hairy skullcap is a native Scutellaria of eastern deciduous woodland, producing terminal clusters of blue-violet, 3/4-inch, two-lipped flowers on hairy stems 6–16 inches tall from late spring through early summer. The genus name comes from the small, helmet-shaped (scutellum) protrusion on the upper side of the calyx visible in fruit. Native bumblebees are the primary pollinators. Hairy skullcap grows naturally in dry to moist hardwood forest understories from New York south to Florida and west to the Midwest — one of the more shade-tolerant and drought-tolerant native skullcaps, able to perform in the challenging dry-shade conditions under mature deciduous trees. The basal and stem leaves are opposite and elliptic, distinctly hairy. An attractive and ecologically valuable small native for the shaded wildflower garden and native plant border.

plant_note: Scarify or soak seeds for 24 hours before direct sowing at the soil surface or just below in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, wet to moist soil near pond margins, streambanks, or rain garden depressions. Halberd-leaf Rose Mallow self-sows readily in favorable wet conditions.
directions: Halberd-leaf Rose Mallow is a tall, dramatic native perennial, native to the streambanks, marshy shores, and wet meadow edges of eastern North America from New York to Florida and west to Nebraska and Oklahoma, producing a succession of large, showy, white to pink flowers with a dark red center from July through September on stems reaching 6 feet in wet soil. The deeply three-lobed, halberd-shaped leaves distinguish it from the more common Swamp Rose Mallow (Hibiscus moscheutos), and the flowers are slightly smaller but equally attractive to hummingbirds and large native bees such as carpenter bees, which are among the few insects strong enough to force their way into the blossom. A perennial that dies to the ground in winter and re-emerges reliably from a stout root crown in spring, Halberd-leaf Rose Mallow is excellent for naturalizing the wet margins of ponds, detention basins, and rain gardens where its moisture requirement is easily met. Sow scarified seeds in place in wet soil in early spring, or divide established clumps.

plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs in fall in moist, acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), well-drained to moderately wet, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; hammock sweet azalea tolerates wet, shaded conditions better than most azaleas — it naturally grows in moist hammock forests and swamp margins.
directions: Hammock sweet azalea is a large, summer-blooming native azalea of the Gulf Coastal Plain — the most heat-tolerant and latest-blooming of all eastern native azaleas, producing fragrant clusters of tubular, white (occasionally pink-tinged) flowers with a clammy, sticky texture in July and August, when summer heat makes gardening difficult and most flowering shrubs have long since finished blooming. Its natural habitat is the moist, shaded understory of hardwood hammocks, bay swamps, and magnolia-dominated wet forests from Florida to Louisiana, making it the most shade- and moisture-tolerant of all Gulf Coast native azaleas. In Gulf Coast landscape gardens, it fills a critical niche as a large, fragrant, summer-flowering shade shrub for moist, acidic woodland conditions — pair with ferns, Louisiana irises, and swamp spider lily in moist, acid-soil shrub borders for a true Gulf Coast hammock garden. The late summer bloom time and fragrant flowers attract hummingbirds and giant native sphinx moths that are still active in summer heat.

plant_note: Plant tubers 2 inches deep after the last frost date once soil warms; in zone 7 and south, tubers often overwinter in the ground with a light mulch — lift and store in colder climates.
directions: Hardy begonia is the most cold-tolerant of all begonias, thriving in full to partial shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil where few other flowering perennials will bloom dependably — it is especially valuable for deep shade gardens in the South. Drooping clusters of soft pink (occasionally white) flowers appear from midsummer through frost on arching, succulent stems with distinctive asymmetrical leaves, providing months of color. Plants produce tiny bulbils in leaf axils that drop to the ground and naturalize freely, gradually forming a lush colony without becoming invasive. In zones 7 and warmer, the tuberous roots survive mild winters underground; top-dress with 2–3 inches of compost each spring to maintain vigor.

plant_note: Plant tubers shallowly in late summer under trees.
directions: Plant hardy cyclamen tubers shallowly in late summer or early fall in well-drained, humus-rich soil under deciduous trees. The dainty pink or white reflexed flowers appear in autumn, followed by beautifully marbled ivy-shaped leaves that persist through winter. It thrives in dry shade where little else grows and slowly self-sows to form colonies. Keep the tubers on the dry side during their summer dormancy and mulch lightly with leaf mold.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring or fall; tolerates a wide range of soil conditions once established.
directions: Plant in well-drained soil in a location with morning sun and afternoon shade; hardy geraniums are adaptable to poor soils and are generally low-maintenance once established. Shear plants back by one-half after the initial bloom to refresh the foliage and encourage a second flush of flowers. The attractive lobed leaves often turn red or orange in fall, extending the ornamental season. Divide every 3–5 years in spring to reinvigorate the planting.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost after nicking the seed coat and soaking 24 hours; transplant after all frost danger has passed.
directions: Nick or file seed coats and soak in warm water for 24 hours before sowing to improve germination rates significantly. Plant in full sun in moist, fertile soil — hardy hibiscus is native to wetlands and performs best with consistent moisture but tolerates average garden beds with regular irrigation. This is a late riser in spring; the shoots emerge much later than other perennials, so mark plants to avoid accidental disturbance. The dinner-plate-sized flowers in white, pink, and crimson open each morning and close by evening, with new blooms produced daily from midsummer through frost.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions horizontally in a wide, shallow container of heavy clay soil without drainage holes, then submerge 12–24 inches below the water surface in full sun; water lily requires at least 6 hours of direct sun daily for good flowering — too much shade dramatically reduces bloom production.
directions: Plant rhizomes in heavy clay soil in a wide, shallow container submerged 12–24 inches below the water surface in full sun; the fragrant water lily is the quintessential aquatic ornamental — its floating, circular pads 6–12 inches across and beautiful white or pink flowers 3–5 inches wide that open each morning and close by noon are iconic in ponds and water gardens across North America. N. odorata is native to eastern North America and is the most fragrant of the hardy water lilies, perfuming the air around the pond with a sweet, sweet scent. The rhizomes are edible (a traditional Native American food) and the seeds and flower buds were also consumed. Divide rhizomes every 2–3 years when they crowd their container; feed during the growing season with aquatic plant tablets pushed into the soil. In zone 3–7 lower the container to the pond bottom below the freeze line for winter protection.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; cold stratify seeds for 30 days before sowing. Transplant to moist to slightly dry, rocky or sandy, slightly acidic woodland soil in partial to full shade after frost has passed.
directions: Heartleaf Skullcap is a shade-tolerant native perennial mint-family herb, native to dry to moist rocky woodlands, forest edges, and limestone bluff bases from Maryland and Ohio south to Florida and west to Texas, blooming from May to July with loose racemes of blue to violet flowers held above large, heart-shaped, opposite leaves. Unlike many skullcaps that prefer full sun, this species thrives in the dappled shade of open woodland and is one of the more shade-tolerant native salvias for the naturalistic shade garden. It provides excellent nectar for bumble bees and is a larval host plant for several native moth species. Grow in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, rocky or sandy soil in partial shade; it is notably drought-tolerant once established and performs better in drier shade than most native woodland wildflowers.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in humus-rich, moist, slightly acidic soil in full to partial shade; slow to establish from division but once settled forms a dense weed-suppressing mat. Purchase only from nursery-propagated sources.
directions: Plant rhizome divisions in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; heartleaf wild ginger is the evergreen southeastern counterpart to the deciduous Canadian wild ginger (Asarum canadense), forming a dense, low mat of glossy, evergreen heart-shaped leaves that is one of the finest shade groundcovers available for southern gardens. The curious brown jug-shaped flowers (earning the folk name 'little brown jugs') bloom at ground level in early spring, hidden beneath the leaves, and are pollinated by small ground-crawling insects. The aromatic rhizomes smell and taste of ginger. It colonizes slowly from rhizomes to fill a shaded bed over several years. Plant under oaks, maples, or at the base of large shrubs in woodland gardens. All parts mildly toxic if consumed in quantity.

plant_note: Nick and soak seed, then direct sow after frost at the base of a support.
directions: Nick the seed coat and soak overnight, then direct sow after frost at the base of a trellis or fence. This showy climber opens sky-blue flowers in the cool of morning that close in afternoon heat but stay open all day when cloudy or in fall. Give it full sun and a support to twine up, watering while young until established. Note that parts of the plant are toxic if eaten, so keep away from children and pets.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil.
directions: Direct sow after frost about an inch deep in full sun, thinning to 12-18 inches apart. This heirloom mix blends the sunny yellows of Lemon Queen, warm tones of Autumn Beauty, and deep reds of Velvet Queen in 4-8 inch multi-branching blooms. Because the plants branch, cut stems regularly to encourage a long succession of flowers for bouquets and pollinators. Water while establishing and stake tall stalks in exposed sites.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost; transplant only after all frost risk has passed.
directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep; germination is slow (14–28 days) and benefits from bottom heat at 70–80°F consistently. Transplant into rich, moist, well-drained soil in full sun; heat brings out the intensely vanilla-scented, deep purple flower clusters. Pinch tips once or twice before transplanting to produce bushier plants with more flowering heads. In zones 10–11 heliotrope is a shrubby perennial; elsewhere take cuttings in late summer to overwinter prized fragrant selections indoors.

plant_note: Plant in spring or early fall in humus-rich, well-drained shade; transplanted divisions establish more reliably than seed.
directions: Plant with the crown at soil level in well-drained, humus-rich soil in dappled to full shade — morning sun is tolerated, but afternoon sun causes leaf scorch. Hellebores bloom in late winter to early spring, often through snow, and the nodding flowers face downward; tipping the pot or planting on a slope allows a better view of the flower interior. Remove old foliage in late winter before new growth emerges to improve air circulation and reduce leaf spot diseases. Hellebores self-sow freely, and seedlings from hybrid plants will vary; divide named selections in early spring to maintain specific colors.

plant_note: Plant divisions or nursery-grown plants in early spring in humus-rich, slightly alkaline soil; purchase only nursery-propagated plants as wild collection is unsustainable and transplants rarely survive.
directions: Plant in humus-rich, well-drained, slightly alkaline soil (unlike most woodland plants, hepatica prefers near-neutral to mildly basic soil from calcareous bedrock) in full to dappled shade. One of the earliest spring wildflowers in eastern North America, the cup-shaped flowers in shades of white, pink, and lavender open in late February–March before any leaves appear, emerging through snow on fuzzy, insulating stems. The leathery, three-lobed leaves that develop after flowering persist through winter and often have beautiful mottled markings. Extremely slow-growing from seed (3–5 years to bloom); purchase only from ethical native plant nurseries. May live for decades undisturbed.

plant_note: Plant container-grown transplants in spring in well-drained, moderately infertile, sandy to loamy soil in full sun to light shade. Hercules' Club spreads by root sprouts and can form thickets — site accordingly. Handle with care due to the large, sharp spines on trunk and branches.
directions: Hercules' Club is a large, dramatic native shrub or small tree of the coastal plain and piedmont of the southeastern United States, notorious for the massive, knobby, claw-like spines that stud the trunk and branches and make it instantly identifiable from a distance. A member of the citrus family (Rutaceae) and closely related to Sichuan pepper, the aromatic bark and leaves contain numbing, anesthetic compounds that were applied by many Native American peoples directly to sore gums and toothaches — earning it the folk name toothache tree — as well as used as a fish poison and in various medicinal preparations. A primary host plant for the giant swallowtail butterfly, one of the largest butterflies in North America, Hercules' Club is an outstanding native choice for wildlife gardens in the Southeast. Plant in well-drained, moderately infertile, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; drought-tolerant once established, it spreads by root sprouts and benefits from open, sunny, naturalized settings.

plant_note: Sow as soon as soil can be worked in early spring; sweet peas thrive in cool weather.
directions: Nick or soak the hard seeds overnight to speed germination, then sow directly 1 inch deep in cool, moist, well-drained soil in early spring. This tall vining variety produces intensely fragrant cream flowers edged in lavender, so provide a trellis or netting 5 to 6 feet tall for support. Keep roots cool and evenly moist, and cut blooms frequently to prolong flowering. Plants fade in summer heat, so start them early where summers are hot.

plant_note: Direct sow seeds in fall in moderately moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich woodland or edge soil in partial shade to full sun. Hoary Skullcap tolerates a range of light conditions from partial shade to full sun and is one of the more adaptable native skullcaps for garden use.
directions: Hoary Skullcap is a native perennial of upland woods, forest edges, and open thickets of the eastern United States from New York south to Georgia and west to Missouri, producing spikes of beautiful blue-violet tubular flowers on softly white-hairy (hoary) stems from June through August — an exceptionally long bloom period for a native woodland wildflower. The flowers are specialized for long-tongued native bumblebees that are the primary pollinators, fitting into the bottom lip of each bloom and triggering pollen release; the plant is also an important nectar source for several native skippers and small butterflies. Tolerating partial shade more readily than many native prairie wildflowers, Hoary Skullcap is a versatile native plant that performs well at woodland edges, in dappled shade gardens, and in moderately sunny perennial borders. Direct sow seeds in fall in moderately moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; established plants slowly spread by short rhizomes to form modest colonies.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant out in cool spring weather for best bloom.
directions: Hoary stock, also called gillyflower, produces tall stems packed with intensely fragrant blooms in soft shades. Start seeds indoors and set young plants out in cool spring weather, as stock performs best in mild temperatures and dislikes heat. Grow in full sun with rich, well-drained soil and even moisture for the fullest flower spikes. Harvest stems for long-lasting, sweetly scented bouquets.

plant_note: Direct sow seed in fall in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile, humus-rich soil in full sun to partial shade; hollow Joe-Pye weed is the tallest of the common Joe-Pye weeds — regularly reaching 6–12 feet in moist, fertile soil — and requires adequate moisture to achieve its impressive stature.
directions: Sow seed in fall in moist to wet, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade — hollow Joe-Pye weed is the largest native Joe-Pye weed, regularly reaching 5–12 feet in moist, fertile conditions, making it one of the most imposing native perennials in the eastern flora. It is identifiable by its hollow, purple-spotted stems (the 'fistulosum' meaning tubular, and the purple nodes). Large, domed clusters of mauve-pink flowers in midsummer to late summer are massively attractive to migrating monarchs, eastern tiger swallowtails, fritillaries, and dozens of native bee species — few plants concentrate as many pollinators in a single location as a blooming Joe-Pye weed. It grows naturally along stream banks, moist meadows, and wet open woodland edges throughout the eastern US. Named varieties including 'Gateway' (more compact, 5–6 feet) are widely available. Outstanding back-of-border or screening native for moist, sunny to partly shaded gardens.

plant_note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist to moderately dry, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; holly fern is one of the most drought-tolerant of all ferns and one of the best for Gulf Coast shade gardens — tolerating clay soil, drought, and considerable heat.
directions: Plant in moist to moderately dry, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial to full shade — holly fern is the most drought-tolerant and heat-tolerant of the widely grown shade ferns and one of the finest ferns for Gulf Coast gardens. The bold, lustrous, dark green fronds with glossy, sickle-shaped (falcate) pinnae have an unmistakably bold, coarse texture reminiscent of holly leaves — giving the fern its common name. It is essentially evergreen through zone 6–11, maintaining handsome fronds year-round even through light freezes. Unlike most ferns it tolerates dry spells between watering and grows in relatively dry soil conditions once established. Excellent for shade planting under trees where soil competition and drought would stress more delicate ferns. An outstanding and low-maintenance fern for southern shade gardens in zones 6–11.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring around the last frost date or in late summer for bloom the following year.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep; hollyhocks are biennial or short-lived perennials that flower the second year from seed and self-sow freely to maintain the planting. Rust fungus is the most common disease — look for orange pustules on leaf undersides and remove affected foliage promptly, avoiding overhead watering. Plant against walls or fences and provide loose staking for the tall, top-heavy flowering spikes. Allow some seed to mature and fall to keep the colony self-renewing.

plant_note: Sow in spring for bloom the following year, or in fall to establish over winter.
directions: This heirloom hollyhock sends up dramatic five- to six-foot spikes lined with large blooms in a mix of colors, perfect for the back of a cottage border. Sow seeds shallowly in spring or fall, keeping in mind that as a biennial it usually flowers the second season. Plant in full sun with support in windy sites, and space generously for good airflow to reduce rust. Allow some seed to drop and it will happily naturalize year after year.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring or in summer for flowers the following spring; behaves as a biennial in most climates.
directions: Sow seeds in spring or early summer; plants develop as a rosette the first year and flower the following spring — a true biennial. Purple or white cross-shaped flowers appear in mid-spring, followed by the flat, papery-silver seed pods that give the plant its common names 'honesty' and 'money plant.' To harvest pods for dried arrangements, cut when the outer husks have dried and carefully peel away both sides to reveal the lustrous translucent membrane. Plants self-sow freely and once established will perpetuate themselves year after year.

plant_note: Plant container-grown trees in fall in any well-drained soil — honey locust is exceptionally adaptable to alkaline, compacted, salty, wet or dry soils and urban conditions; thornless cultivars ('Inermis', 'Sunburst', 'Skyline') are preferred over the wild thorny species for landscapes.
directions: Honey locust is one of the most broadly adaptable native trees in North America, tolerating alkaline soils, periodic flooding, urban air pollution, road salt, and heat that defeat other trees — widely planted as a tough street and lawn tree in the form of thornless, podless cultivars developed from its natural variety inermis. The feathery, twice-pinnately compound leaves create dappled shade that allows turf and understory plants to grow beneath the canopy more easily than under most large shade trees; the fine-textured leaves also break down quickly in fall without raking.
