plant_note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade; leopard plant is one of the finest shade perennials for the Gulf Coast, tolerating both deep shade and the summer heat and humidity that defeat many shade perennials in zone 8–9.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to deep shade — leopard plant is one of the most valuable and underused shade perennials for the Gulf Coast, producing extraordinarily bold, round, glossy, deep-green, leathery leaves up to 18 inches across that retain a tropical freshness through the hottest Gulf Coast summers. The common cultivar 'Aureomaculatum' (spotted leopard plant) has large yellow spots scattered across the dark green leaves, giving a striking, almost artificial-looking pattern. Yellow daisy flowers appear in fall to winter on tall stalks, providing late-season color when most shade perennials are shutting down. Completely evergreen in zone 8–10; the leathery leaves resist frost damage better than most shade perennials and the plant is reliably root-hardy through zone 7 winters. Clumps increase slowly and need division only every 5–7 years. An outstanding long-term, zero-maintenance shade perennial for Gulf Coast gardens.

plant_note: Plant rooted stem cuttings or divisions 12–18 inches apart in spring or fall; stems root wherever they contact soil, filling in gaps quickly once plants establish.
directions: Plant in almost any soil in partial to deep shade; lesser periwinkle is one of the toughest shade groundcovers, tolerating dry shade under mature trees once established. The trailing stems root at nodes to form a weed-suppressing mat studded with periwinkle-blue pinwheel flowers in spring, with scattered repeat bloom into fall. Variegated forms such as 'Illumination' and 'Ralph Shugert' brighten dark spaces. Shear plants back by half in early spring to maintain density and remove dead or straggling stems. Note that Vinca minor can spread into natural areas — consider native alternatives (Pachysandra procumbens, Asarum canadense) in gardens bordering woodlands.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring or fall; benefits from cool soil to germinate.
directions: Lewis blue flax is a graceful native perennial that opens clear sky-blue flowers each morning atop slender, wiry stems. Sow seeds directly in spring or fall on well-drained soil, pressing them in lightly and keeping the surface moist until they sprout. Once established it is remarkably drought tolerant and thrives in full sun across a wide range of conditions. Allow spent flowers to set seed and it will self-sow to form drifts over time.

plant_note: Plant corms 3–4 in deep in spring as soon as the soil can be worked; they establish quickly and bloom the first season.
directions: Plant corms 3–4 in deep with the flat side down in well-drained, average to poor soil in full sun; rich or wet soil leads to floppy growth. Liatris is unique in opening its flowers from the top of the spike downward, prolonging the bloom period for 3–4 weeks in midsummer. The purple to rose-pink spikes are outstanding cut flowers and highly attractive to butterflies and bees; stems last 10–14 days in a vase. Clumps multiply steadily; divide every 3–4 years in spring to prevent overcrowding and maintain strong bloom.

plant_note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil or attach to rough bark in partial to full shade; licorice fern is an epiphytic Pacific Coast fern that grows naturally on mossy boulders, tree trunks, and cliff faces — it requires excellent drainage but consistent moisture during its winter growing season and goes dormant in summer heat.
directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade — most charming when allowed to grow on the surface of mossy boulders, log stumps, or the rough bark of large-trunked trees; licorice fern is a graceful, small, creeping fern of the cool, moist Pacific Coast woodlands, named for the sweet licorice-like flavor of its slender rhizomes, which were chewed fresh by Pacific Northwest indigenous peoples to sweeten foods and treat respiratory ailments. The fronds are bright, glossy green, simply pinnate, and arch gracefully; the plant is unusual in that it grows actively through the cool, wet winter months and goes completely dormant in summer when conditions become warm and dry. In gardens it performs best in mild coastal climates (Pacific Coast, mild Atlantic coast) with cool, moist winters and moderate summers. Established plants drape beautifully over mossy surfaces and create a lush, ancient, fern-grotto effect in shaded rock gardens.

plant_note: Direct sow after the last frost once soil has warmed.
directions: Direct sow after the last frost in warm soil, covering seeds about 1/4 inch, or start indoors a few weeks early. 'Lilliput' produces a never-ending show of vibrant, small double pompon flowers on long stems all summer. Cutting the blooms for bouquets encourages the plants to produce even more, delighting butterflies and bees. Grow in full sun with good air circulation to limit mildew, and deadhead or harvest often to keep it flowering.

plant_note: Plant rhizome clumps or container plants in spring after frost danger passes; set the crown just at soil level in a generous planting hole. In zones 7–8, plant against a south-facing wall and mulch heavily in winter.
directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun; agapanthus produces strappy, strap-like evergreen foliage and tall, sturdy flower stems topped by globose umbels of blue, purple, or white trumpet-shaped flowers in June–July that are extraordinarily striking in mass plantings. It is one of the signature plants of Gulf Coast landscapes, thriving in heat, humidity, and coastal conditions with very little care. Clumps become slightly root-bound over time and actually flower more prolifically when congested — do not divide unless absolutely necessary. Deciduous types (A. praecox) are more cold-hardy to zone 7 with protection. Deadhead spent stems for neatness and to prevent abundant self-seeding of named cultivars.

plant_note: Plant pips in fall or early spring in shade.
directions: Plant lily of the valley pips in fall or early spring, setting the rhizomes just below the soil surface in humus-rich, moist shade. The arching stems of nodding white bells are intensely fragrant and spread by rhizomes to form a dense groundcover. It thrives under trees and in woodland gardens and can become invasive, so site it where spread is welcome. All parts are toxic if eaten, so keep it away from children and pets.

plant_note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring or early fall; plants establish quickly and begin spreading within the first growing season.
directions: Plant in almost any well-drained soil, from deep shade under dense evergreens to full sun, though the darkest green foliage color develops in partial shade. This evergreen, clump-forming groundcover produces spikes of violet flowers in late summer followed by black berries that persist through winter; it is one of the most reliable plants for covering problem areas under trees where lawn grasses fail. Shear or mow plants to 3–4 inches in late winter before new growth begins to remove tattered winter foliage. Divide congested clumps every 4–5 years to maintain vigor; divisions establish quickly and can be replanted immediately.

plant_note: Plant divisions or container-grown transplants in spring in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, lean soil in full sun; Lindheimer's muhly is native to limestone-based soils of the Edwards Plateau and south-central Texas and is exceptionally drought and heat tolerant once established.
directions: Plant in well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline soil in full sun; Lindheimer's muhly is one of the most striking large ornamental grasses native to Texas, forming graceful, arching clumps of narrow blue-green leaves 3–4 feet tall that turn golden-tan in fall, topped with silky silver-gray flower plumes 4–5 feet high from fall through winter. Named for Ferdinand Lindheimer, the pioneering Texas botanist, it is remarkably tough and adaptable — thriving in heat, drought, alkaline soils, and even rocky caliche where most ornamental grasses fail. The large, dramatic clumps provide outstanding winter structure and movement. It is somewhat tolerant of clay soils if drainage is adequate. Cut to 6 inches in late winter before new growth begins; established clumps need division only every 4–5 years when they become hollow in the center.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 14–16 weeks before last frost date; lisianthus requires a very long lead time and is challenging from seed.
directions: Surface-sow tiny, pelleted seeds on sterile, fine-textured mix; do not cover as seeds need light and maintain steady 70–75°F. Seedlings grow extremely slowly for the first 8–10 weeks, so patience is essential — do not overwater at this stage as damping off is common. Transplant carefully after last frost into rich, well-drained, alkaline-leaning soil in full sun. The ruffled, rose-like blooms in white, purple, pink, and bicolor make lisianthus one of the finest cut flowers; harvest stems when 2–3 buds per stem have opened.

plant_note: Direct sow after last frost in spring, or plant divisions in spring in full sun and lean, well-drained soil.
directions: Direct sow in full sun in lean, well-drained to dry soil; little bluestem is a native prairie grass of exceptional ornamental value that thrives in poor, sandy, or rocky soil where it develops the best fall color. Blue-green summer foliage turns brilliant coppery-orange and burgundy-red in fall, and the silver-white fluffy seedheads persist through winter, capturing hoarfrost beautifully. In rich, moist soil plants become floppy and lose their characteristic upright form. Cut back to 4–6 in in late winter before new growth emerges; divide every 3–4 years if needed.

plant_note: Sow in early spring as soon as soil can be worked; prefers cool weather.
directions: Soak seeds overnight and direct sow 1 inch deep in cool, moist soil in early spring. Unlike vining sweet peas, 'Little Sweetheart' is a dwarf bush type with profuse, lightly scented small flowers in a rainbow of colors. Its compact habit suits hanging baskets, patio containers, window boxes, and the front of a border. Keep roots cool and evenly moist, and cut blooms often to prolong flowering before summer heat.

plant_note: Plant small container-grown trees (1–2 gallon) in winter or early spring in well-drained, sandy to loamy soil; live oak establishes best when young. Large container or balled-and-burlapped trees are slow to re-establish. Plant acorns immediately upon collecting in fall.
directions: Plant in well-drained, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; live oak is the defining tree of the Gulf Coast landscape, forming magnificent broad, sweeping canopies of twisted horizontal branches draped with Spanish moss, with a single mature tree providing habitat for hundreds of species of insects, birds, and wildlife. The leaves are semi-evergreen in zone 8 (dropping briefly in late winter as new leaves emerge) and evergreen in zone 9–10. It tolerates salt spray, sandy soils, and periodic drought once established and grows steadily — 1–2 feet per year when young. The small acorns are critically important fall and winter food for blue jays, wild turkeys, squirrels, and deer. Plant far from structures and utility lines — mature trees reach 40–60 feet tall with 60–100 foot spread. A heritage landscape tree meant for centuries.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions in shallow water (1–4 inches) or saturated soil at pond or stream margins in partial shade once soil warms; requires consistent moisture and will not survive drought.
directions: Plant rhizome divisions in shallow water or saturated, slightly acidic soil at pond or stream margins in partial shade; lizard's tail is a distinctive native aquatic perennial of southeastern swamps, stream banks, and freshwater wetlands, producing arching spikes of tiny white fragrant flowers that droop at the tip like a lizard's tail in midsummer, above large glossy heart-shaped leaves. It grows naturally in standing water and at stream margins from Quebec to Florida, and is an excellent choice for pond margins, boggy borders, and rain gardens where its attractive foliage and unusual flowers provide interest through the season. The plant spreads by rhizome to form naturalistic colonies. The fragrant flowers attract many small native bees and beetles. Excellent combined with pickerel rush and blue flag iris for a native wetland plant community.

plant_note: Plant container specimens in spring in moist to wet, strongly acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich, sandy to loamy soil in partial shade to full sun; loblolly bay requires strongly acidic, consistently moist to wet soil — it will not grow in neutral, alkaline, or dry conditions and must have peaty, humus-rich, well-aerated acid soil.
directions: Plant in moist to wet, strongly acidic, humus-rich, peaty, sandy to loamy soil in partial shade to full sun — loblolly bay is a spectacular native evergreen tree of the Gulf Coast and Atlantic coastal plain bay swamps and wet pine flatwoods, producing 2.5–3 inch, pure white, camellia-like flowers with prominent gold stamens that appear from June through October in the hottest part of the summer when few other flowering trees bloom. The dark, glossy, evergreen leaves are handsome year-round; the bark is attractively furrowed and gray. It grows naturally in the acidic, peaty, frequently saturated soils of bay swamps, seeps, and wet flatwood depressions — conditions exactly matching an acidic, moist bog garden or the wet margins of a natural pond. It is closely related to the camellia family and equally demanding in its soil pH requirements; failure is almost always caused by insufficiently acidic soil. An outstanding native flowering tree for wet, acidic, shaded garden sites in zone 7–9.

plant_note: Plant bareroot seedlings in fall or winter in moist to well-drained, slightly acidic soil; loblolly pine is one of the fastest-growing southern pines (2–4 feet per year when young) and establishes readily from small seedlings.
directions: Plant bareroot seedlings or container trees in fall or early spring in moist to well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; loblolly pine is the most common and commercially important tree of the South, but it is also a critical wildlife tree — the seeds are consumed by dozens of bird species including red-cockaded woodpeckers (which nest exclusively in mature pines), white-breasted nuthatches, and pine siskins, while the open structure of mature pine canopy supports a rich understory community of native plants. It grows extremely fast (2–4 feet per year) and provides rapid screening, wind protection, and habitat structure. The three long needles in bundles and heavy cones are distinctive. Susceptible to pine bark beetles when stressed by drought; plant in appropriate moist, sandy soils and avoid planting far outside its natural range.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring after temperatures are reliably above 65°F in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich, fertile soil in partial shade to full sun; heliconia requires consistently warm temperatures to thrive — avoid planting while soil remains cool. Divide clumps in spring every 2–3 years to maintain vigor.
directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; heliconia is one of the most exotic and spectacular tropical plants available for zone 9–12 gardens, producing large, bold banana-like foliage and dramatic flower bracts in brilliant shades of red, orange, and yellow that attract hummingbirds prolifically. H. psittacorum (parrot heliconia) is one of the smaller and most adaptable species, with slender upright stems 3–5 feet tall and colorful upright flower bracts in red-orange with green and yellow tipping — produced nearly year-round in frost-free gardens. The flowers are outstanding in tropical cut flower arrangements. Heliconias require consistent heat, moisture, and humidity to thrive; they are not drought tolerant and decline quickly when chilled below 50°F. In zone 9a they may suffer frost damage to foliage but regrow from rhizomes in spring if the soil does not freeze. Apply a thick layer of mulch to protect roots during cold snaps.

plant_note: Direct sow seed in fall in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade; long-spurred violet is an Appalachian mountain species that performs best in cool, moist, shaded conditions with good drainage and slightly acidic soil.
directions: Sow seed in fall in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade — long-spurred violet is one of the most distinctive and elegant of all native violets, immediately recognizable by its unusually long nectar spur — 0.5 to 0.75 inches long, compared to the very short spurs of most violets — and its pale lavender flowers with darker veining and a darker eye. The long nectar spur specifically coevolved with certain long-tongued bees and butterflies that can access the nectar. It grows naturally in cool, moist, rich Appalachian woodland from Pennsylvania south to the Carolinas, preferring the well-drained, acidic, forest soils of mountain slopes and ravine banks. Less adaptable to hot, humid lowland conditions than most native violets. A beautiful specialty violet for cool-climate woodland gardens and native plant collections in zones 4–8, and a host plant for native fritillary butterflies.

plant_note: Plant bareroot seedlings in fall or winter in sandy, well-drained, infertile, acidic soil; longleaf pine establishes best from small seedlings planted in fall. Do not fertilize or amend soil — longleaf requires poor, sandy conditions. Mow around young plants to control competing vegetation.
directions: Plant bareroot seedlings in fall or winter in sandy, infertile, acidic, extremely well-drained soil in full sun; longleaf pine is one of the most ecologically critical trees in North America, once covering 90 million acres of the southeastern coastal plain but now reduced to less than 3% of its original range — one of the continent's most severe ecological losses. Planting longleaf is an act of ecological restoration. The tree is famous for its 'grass stage' — for 3–7 years, the seedling looks like a grass clump with no visible stem while it develops a deep taproot; then growth accelerates dramatically. Mature longleaf lives 300–500 years, supporting 29 endangered species including the red-cockaded woodpecker, indigo snake, and gopher tortoise. The long needles (12–18 inches), massive cones, and fire-adapted bark make it an outstanding specimen tree in appropriate sandy soils.

plant_note: Soak seeds and sow in cool early-spring soil at the base of a trellis.
directions: Lord Nelson is a richly fragrant heirloom sweet pea with deep, nearly navy-blue blooms on vigorous climbing vines. Soak seeds overnight and sow in cool early-spring soil at the foot of a trellis or fence, since sweet peas thrive in chilly starts and fade in heat. Keep the roots cool and evenly moist and pick flowers frequently to keep new buds forming. Provide sturdy vertical support for the five- to six-foot vines.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring; nigella dislikes transplanting.
directions: Direct sow in early spring where plants are to grow, since nigella resents transplanting. 'Delft Blue' bears distinctive dual-colored blooms of violet-blue over snowy white with burgundy stamens amid feathery foliage. Drought-tolerant and easy to grow, it is perfect for borders and attracts pollinators, while the ornamental seed pods are prized for arrangements. Grow in full sun with well-drained soil, and let some pods mature so plants self-sow for next year.

plant_note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is warm; do not start too early indoors as plants grow quickly and become rootbound.
directions: Sow seed on the soil surface and press lightly — seed needs light to germinate. Direct sowing is preferred as plants grow fast and resent transplanting. Thin seedlings to 18–24 in to allow the dramatic, rope-like crimson tassels to develop fully. The hanging panicles can be cut and hung upside down to dry when they reach full length.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost in early spring; also sow in fall in zones 7–10 for spring bloom.
directions: Scatter seeds directly on prepared soil and rake in 1/8 in deep; nigella resents transplanting and germinates best in cool soil. Thin seedlings to 9–12 in to prevent overcrowding — plants are quick to grow and will self-sow freely if allowed to set the inflated seed pods. The blue or white flowers are surrounded by thread-like bracts that give the plant its misty appearance; after bloom the ornamental balloon-like pods are equally attractive and excellent for dried arrangements. Make succession sowings every 3–4 weeks until early summer for continuous bloom.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring or fall in a shaded, moist site; division is the most reliable propagation method.
directions: Plant in deep shade to partial shade in humus-rich, consistently moist soil; lungwort is one of the best shade perennials, offering both attractive silver-spotted foliage and early spring flowers. Pink buds open to blue or violet tubular flowers in early spring, often when little else is in bloom, providing nectar for early pollinators. In hot, dry summers the leaves may develop powdery mildew — shear back affected foliage after flowering and new, fresh leaves will emerge. Divide clumps every 3–4 years immediately after flowering to maintain vigor.

plant_note: Direct sow outdoors in early spring or fall; nick or soak seeds overnight to improve germination.
directions: Scarify seeds with sandpaper or soak overnight in water to soften the hard seed coat before sowing 1/4 in deep in cool, slightly acidic, well-drained soil. Lupines fix atmospheric nitrogen via root nodules and thrive in lean soils without heavy fertilization — too much nitrogen promotes foliage over flowers. They prefer cool summers; in hot climates, they often perform as cool-season annuals, blooming in late spring and fading by midsummer. Deadhead promptly after the first flowering spike fades to encourage a second flush and prevent excessive self-seeding.

plant_note: Plant tubers after the soil has warmed and frost has passed; lift and store tubers over winter in cold zones.
directions: Maarn is a classic orange ball dahlia that produces an abundance of round, uniform blooms perfect for cutting. The plants are large and well-balanced, often standing without staking, and they generate plenty of tubers for dividing. Plant tubers 4 to 6 inches deep in full sun and rich, well-drained soil after frost has passed. Cut flowers frequently and deadhead spent blooms to keep the plant producing all season. In zones colder than 8, dig and store the tubers in a cool, dry place over winter.

plant_note: Sow in darkness indoors; seeds need darkness to germinate.
directions: Sow seed in darkness indoors 4 to 6 weeks before the last frost, since light inhibits germination. Above slim, silvery foliage, tiny star-shaped blooms form 2 to 3 inch pincushion-like flowerheads with a light scent that draws pollinators. This Australian wildflower is a soft, fragrant beauty that makes a lovely, airy cut flower. Grow in full sun with well-drained soil, provide support in windy sites, and harvest stems for delicate bouquets.

plant_note: Surface-sow indoors since light aids germination; snapdragons like cool weather.
directions: Surface-sow the fine seed indoors 8 to 10 weeks before the last frost, since light aids germination. This dwarf snapdragon blend offers a lovely mix of colors that charms in flower beds, borders, containers, and hanging baskets. Snapdragons prefer cool weather, so transplant hardened seedlings out around the last frost. Grow in full sun to part shade with well-drained soil, pinch young plants to encourage branching, and deadhead to prolong bloom.

plant_note: Plant bulbs with the neck at or just at the soil surface in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; magic lily performs best when allowed to dry out after the spring foliage dies — do not water heavily in June–July as the bulbs are dormant.
directions: Plant bulbs with the neck at or near the soil surface in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun — magic lily earns its name by following a seemingly magical seasonal cycle: broad, strap-like leaves emerge in early spring, grow through April, then disappear entirely by June, leaving no trace for two months. Then, in July or August, apparently bare ground suddenly produces clusters of tall, leafless stalks topped with large (3–4 inch), fragrant, pink to lavender-pink lily-like flowers in a spectacular summer surprise that inspired the common names 'surprise lily' and 'resurrection lily.' The flowers last 1–2 weeks. Interplant with hostas, daylilies, or groundcovers to hide the disappearing spring foliage. Clumps increase slowly and bloom more prolifically when undisturbed — divide only every 7–10 years when overcrowded. One of the most reliably carefree bulbs for the southern garden.

plant_note: Surface-sow indoors since light aids germination; prefers cool, moist conditions.
directions: Surface-sow the tiny seed indoors 8 to 10 weeks before the last frost, since light aids germination. This mix dazzles with blooms in yellow, orange, pink, and red on plants ideal for borders, containers, and wildflower gardens. Monkey flower thrives in cool, moist conditions and blooms continuously from spring into summer. Grow in sun to part shade, keep the soil consistently moist, and deadhead to keep the colorful show going.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring when soil has warmed; miscanthus establishes best in warm soil and can be slow in cold springs.
directions: Plant divisions in full sun in any well-drained to moderately moist soil; maiden grass forms large, dramatic clumps with arching, green or variegated foliage and showy, feathery silver plumes in late summer and fall. Cut back hard in late winter or early spring before new growth emerges — use pruners or a chainsaw on large clumps; tie the clump with twine before cutting to simplify cleanup. Divide every 3–5 years in spring when the center of the clump dies out. Some cultivars are sterile; check before planting, as seeding types can naturalize aggressively in some regions.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions or plugs in spring in shallow water (0–12 inches), wet marsh margins, or permanently saturated soil in full sun; maidencane spreads aggressively by rhizomes in aquatic conditions — site where it has room to spread or contain with barriers in smaller water features.
directions: Maidencane is the dominant emergent aquatic grass of the Gulf Coastal Plain's freshwater marshes, lake margins, and slow-moving waterways — forming vast, grass-like stands in shallow water from Texas to Florida that serve as one of the most important waterfowl habitat grasses in the southeastern United States. Dabbling ducks including mallards, teal, mottled ducks, and wood ducks consume the seeds profusely during fall migration; the emergent stands also provide critical nesting cover for king rails, common gallinules, and purple gallinules. In constructed wetlands, stormwater retention ponds, and rain garden overflow areas, maidencane stabilizes wet banks and shallow margins with its extensive rhizome system while providing year-round wildlife value. It spreads rapidly in aquatic conditions by rhizomes and self-seeding and can form dense monocultures in productive conditions — appropriate for naturalized pond and wetland margins but requires management in small water gardens to prevent it from overwhelming other plantings.

plant_note: Plant nursery transplants or small divisions tucked into crevices of a rock garden or dry stone wall in partial to full shade, in neutral to slightly alkaline, freely draining, rocky or gritty soil. Maidenhair Spleenwort is a rock crevice specialist and thrives in the tight drainage and consistent humidity of dry stone walls and rocky outcrops.
directions: Maidenhair Spleenwort is one of the world's most widely distributed and delightful small ferns, native to rock crevices, cliff faces, and mortared stone walls throughout the temperate Northern Hemisphere from North America to Europe, Asia, and beyond, where its neat rosettes of tiny, dark-stemmed, pinnate fronds tuck into the smallest gaps between stones with remarkable grace. The evergreen, arching fronds — typically 4–8 inches long with rounded, alternating pinnae on wiry, dark, lustrous stems — are persistent through winter and provide delicate year-round texture in rock gardens, dry stone walls, and shaded rocky outcrops. Unlike most ferns that prefer moist, humus-rich soil, Maidenhair Spleenwort is adapted to excellent drainage and even slightly alkaline conditions derived from limestone or mortar — making it ideal for growing in rock garden crevices and the joints of dry stone walls, where its fine texture contrasts beautifully with the rugged stone. Plant in tight crevices with minimal amended soil and virtually no supplemental watering once established.

plant_note: Plant rooted cuttings or container-grown transplants in spring in moist to wet, strongly acidic, humus-rich, poorly drained to seasonally wet soil in partial shade to full sun. Maleberry thrives in the wet, acidic conditions of pocosins, bog margins, and wet flatwoods where most other ornamental shrubs will not succeed.
directions: Maleberry is a native deciduous shrub of wet, acidic soils across the eastern United States, found in pocosins, bog margins, wet flatwoods, and seasonally flooded stream banks from Maine south to Florida and west to Texas. The clusters of small, urn-shaped white flowers in late spring attract native bees and bumblebees, while the attractive fall foliage ranges from yellow to orange-red in appropriate sunlight. A member of the blueberry family (Ericaceae), Maleberry shares the extreme soil requirements of its relatives — strongly acidic, humus-rich, poorly drained to saturated conditions that would stress most landscape shrubs. Well-suited for wet, acidic garden settings including bog gardens, rain garden overflow zones, and moist native plant hedges in acid soil conditions, Maleberry is rarely cultivated but deserves wider use for challenging wet, acidic sites. Plant rooted cuttings in strongly acidic, moist to wet, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun.

plant_note: Sow in early spring as soon as soil can be worked; likes cool weather.
directions: Soak seeds overnight and direct sow 1 inch deep in cool, moist soil in early spring. Mammoth sweet peas boast extra-large, sweetly scented flowers on long stems that are perfect for cutting. Bred from an Early Multiflora Gigantea Spencer type, this blend offers extra drought and heat resistance for a longer season. Provide a tall trellis, keep roots cool and evenly moist, and cut flowers frequently to prolong the bloom.

plant_note: Soak and sow in cool spring soil beside a trellis.
directions: This mammoth sweet pea mix delivers large, fragrant blooms in soft rose, pink, lavender, and white on vigorous vines. Soak seeds overnight and sow in cool early-spring soil against a trellis, since these cottage-garden climbers love a chilly start. Keep roots cool and moisture steady, and harvest often to encourage a long succession of scented flowers. The five- to six-foot vines need sturdy support to climb.

plant_note: Sow in early spring as soon as soil can be worked; likes cool weather.
directions: Soak seeds overnight and direct sow 1 inch deep in cool, moist soil in early spring. This Mammoth selection produces striking, extra-large blooms in deep velvety purple-blue atop long stems ideal for cutting gardens. Bred from Early Multiflora Gigantea, it offers Spencer-type beauty with added drought and heat resistance. Provide a tall trellis, keep roots cool and evenly watered, and cut flowers often to extend flowering.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost; needs warm soil and full sun.
directions: This giant heirloom sunflower grows on a single stem to ten or twelve feet and crowns it with a massive golden head up to a foot across. Sow seeds an inch deep directly in warm soil after frost, spacing generously so the huge plants have room. Give full sun and steady water, and stake tall stems in exposed sites to keep them from toppling. The abundant seeds are excellent for roasting or feeding birds once heads mature.

plant_note: Direct sow after the last frost once soil has warmed.
directions: Direct sow seeds 1 inch deep after the last frost, spacing plants well to support their large size. This giant grows fast on a single stalk that can reach 12 feet tall and produces one enormous 10 to 12 inch flower head. Not fussy about soil and drought-tolerant, it makes an attractive screen when grown in a tight row. Sow monthly for continual bloom, and leave the seed heads for birds and for harvesting edible seeds.

plant_note: Plant in fertile, moist, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.5) soil or potting mix in full sun to partial shade; mandevilla requires warm temperatures above 50°F at all times — cold damage occurs below 45°F. In zone 9–10 it may be grown in the ground in a protected location; elsewhere treat as a container plant brought indoors in winter.
directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; mandevilla is one of the most popular tropical vines for patio containers in the United States, producing large, trumpet-shaped flowers 3–4 inches across in brilliant pink, red, or white from spring through frost. The climbing stems have glossy, dark green leaves that are attractive even out of bloom. Modern compact ('Sundaville' and 'Sun Parasol') series stay under 3 feet and are outstanding in large pots and hanging baskets. The larger vining types need a trellis or support and can reach 8–10 feet. Feed every 2 weeks with a bloom-booster fertilizer for maximum flower production. In zone 9–10 mandevilla may be left outdoors year-round in a sheltered location; in zone 8a bring inside at first frost and keep barely moist through winter. Very sap is milky white and mildly toxic — wear gloves when pruning.

plant_note: Plant bulbs 4–6 inches deep in well-drained, sandy to rocky coastal soil in full sun in Zone 10–11 frost-free landscapes. Mangrove Spider Lily tolerates salt spray and brief flooding once established. Divide overcrowded clumps every 3–4 years in fall to maintain vigor and flowering.
directions: Mangrove Spider Lily is a native bulbous perennial of southern Florida's coastal strand, coastal hammocks, and pine rocklands, as well as much of the Caribbean, producing spectacular, intensely fragrant white flowers with a fringed central cup and reflexed white petals on 18–30 inch stems from late spring through summer. The broad, strap-like evergreen leaves and showstopper flowers make it one of the most spectacular native perennials available for frost-free South Florida landscapes, and its tolerance of salt spray, periodic flooding, and moderate drought makes it practical for a wide range of coastal garden conditions. Plant bulbs 4–6 inches deep in well-drained, sandy to rocky coastal soil in full sun; established clumps slowly expand through bulb offsets and become increasingly floriferous as they mature. Mangrove Spider Lily is cold-sensitive and is best suited to Zone 10–11, though it may survive brief cold snaps in protected Zone 9b microclimates.

plant_note: Plant container shrubs in spring or fall in dry to moist, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to deep shade; this is one of the few viburnums that thrives and fruits in full dry shade under mature trees.
directions: Plant in dry to moderately moist, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to deep shade; mapleleaf viburnum is exceptional among viburnums for its tolerance of both dry conditions and full shade — characteristics that together make it one of the most useful native shrubs for planting under established deciduous trees where root competition and low light challenge most plants. The maple-like leaves turn brilliant pink to rose-pink to crimson-magenta in fall, some of the most vivid fall color of any shade-tolerant shrub. Flat-topped white flower clusters in late spring attract native bees, followed by small dark blue-black berries (technically edible but not palatable) that are consumed by songbirds. Spreads slowly by rhizome to form naturalistic colonies; one of the best native shrubs for difficult dry shade situations.

plant_note: Plant divisions or nursery-grown plants in spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained, humus-rich soil in full shade; marginal wood fern is highly drought tolerant once established for a fern and can grow on dry, rocky, shaded slopes. Avoid waterlogged soil.
directions: Plant in well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in full shade; marginal wood fern is one of the most reliable and widely adaptable native ferns of eastern North America, named for the distinctive placement of its spore clusters (sori) at the very margins of the leaflet undersides — a useful identification feature. Unlike many ferns, it tolerates moderately dry, shaded conditions remarkably well, thriving under the dry shade of large deciduous trees where soil moisture is scarce and most ferns fail. The broad, leathery, dark blue-green fronds are semi-evergreen to evergreen in milder zones, maintaining ornamental value through winter and providing year-round structure in the shade garden. It grows as a neat vase-shaped crown and does not spread by running rhizomes, making it exceptionally well-behaved in formal plantings. One of the easiest native ferns for the beginning shade gardener.

plant_note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial shade. Marlberry is not to be confused with the invasive Ardisia crenata (Coral Ardisia) — Marlberry is a native species with smaller, less toothed leaves and larger black berries. Always verify you are purchasing the native species.
directions: Marlberry is a handsome native evergreen shrub or small tree, native to the coastal hammocks, tropical hardwood forests, and pinelands of south Florida and the Keys, growing as a common and important component of the hammock understory layer in partial to full shade. It produces clusters of small white flowers from summer through fall, followed by clusters of round, glossy black berries that are one of the most reliable and abundant bird food sources in south Florida's hammock ecosystem — the berries are consumed by dozens of species of warblers, thrushes, vireos, and resident birds throughout fall and winter. An adaptable and attractive native shade shrub for zone 9b–11 landscapes, Marlberry fills the role of native understory shrub in conditions too shaded for most flowering shrubs, providing year-round evergreen structure, summer-fall blooms, and exceptional wildlife value. Important note: always confirm you are purchasing native Marlberry (Ardisia escallonioides) and not the look-alike invasive Coral Ardisia (Ardisia crenata), which has escaped cultivation and invaded Florida's native hammocks.

plant_note: Plant in spring or fall in humus-rich, well-drained soil; grown mainly for its foliage.
directions: Marmalade is a coral bells cultivar grown for its ruffled amber, caramel, and rose-tinged foliage that holds color through the seasons. It forms a neat mound and sends up airy flower spikes that attract hummingbirds. Plant in partial shade in rich, well-drained soil, keeping the crown at soil level to prevent rot. Water evenly during dry spells and mulch lightly to keep roots cool. Divide clumps every few years in spring to keep the plant vigorous and refresh its color.

plant_note: Plant container-grown specimens in spring in moist to wet, saline or brackish, sandy soil in full sun along the high tide line; marsh elder is a tidal wetland obligate and should not be planted in fresh water or upland sites.
directions: Marsh elder, also called high-tide bush, is a native deciduous shrub of the Atlantic and Gulf Coast salt marshes, growing precisely at the upper limit of regular tidal flooding — its common name 'high-tide bush' describes its ecological niche as the shrubby fringe that marks the upper boundary of the tidal marsh zone on both Atlantic and Gulf shorelines. Despite its unglamorous appearance for most of the year — a stiff, woody shrub with small, succulent leaves and inconspicuous late-summer flowers — marsh elder is ecologically critical: it provides nesting cover for marsh wrens, seaside sparrows, and clapper rails, and its seeds are consumed by dabbling ducks and shorebirds; it stabilizes the marsh-upland ecotone against erosion. In coastal restoration plantings along the Gulf, it is a primary native shrub for establishing the high marsh zone on restored or disturbed shorelines, often planted in combination with black needle rush (Juncus roemerianus) and saltmarsh cordgrass. It also serves as a windbreak and salt spray filter for adjacent upland plantings.

plant_note: Divide rhizome clumps in spring and plant in wet to moist, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun. Marsh Fern spreads by long-creeping rhizomes to form colonies and is excellent for naturalizing wet meadows, rain gardens, and pond margins.
directions: Marsh Fern is one of the most adaptable and widely distributed native ferns in North America, native to marshes, wet meadows, sedge bogs, streambanks, and swamp margins from Newfoundland to Florida and west to Alaska and Texas, growing in full sun to partial shade in consistently moist to wet, slightly acidic soil. Its delicate, light green, pinnately divided fronds are deciduous (dying back to the ground in fall) and emerge fresh and bright in spring from long, creeping rhizomes that spread to form open, wispy colonies in wet areas. Unlike many ferns that require deep shade, Marsh Fern tolerates and even thrives in open, sunny wet meadow situations, making it one of the more versatile native ferns for rain gardens, wet roadside ditches, and pond margins. Divide and replant in spring; the plant requires consistently moist to saturated soil and declines if allowed to dry out for extended periods.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions in very early spring in wet, mucky, slightly acidic soil at the edge of a pond, stream, or bog garden; the plant requires consistently wet conditions and will not survive dry summer soils.
directions: Plant rhizome divisions in wet to saturated, mucky, slightly acidic soil at the margin of a pond, stream, or bog garden in partial shade to full sun; marsh marigold is one of the earliest spring wildflowers in cold-climate wetlands, producing brilliant golden-yellow waxy flowers like large buttercups in March and April — sometimes pushing through the last snow at the edge of a thawing stream. It is the quintessential cold-climate bog and rain garden plant, growing naturally in springs, bogs, and stream banks from Labrador to North Carolina. All parts are mildly toxic when raw but the flower buds have been used as a caper substitute when pickled. The plant goes completely dormant in summer heat; interplant with other wetland perennials to fill the gap. Not suited to zone 8 and south where summers are too hot.

plant_note: Direct sow seed in fall in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; marsh violet thrives in the consistently moist to wet conditions of stream banks, pond margins, and rain garden areas where most violets would rot.
directions: Sow seed in fall in moist to wet, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade — marsh violet is the native violet of wet, shaded environments, growing naturally along stream banks, pond margins, seepage areas, and wet woodland clearings where it produces blue-violet flowers with distinctive darker veining and a white base on the lower petals, held on stems above the characteristic heart-shaped leaves from spring through early summer. It is distinguished from the common blue violet (V. sororia) by its requirement for consistently moist to wet soil and its preference for shaded sites. An outstanding native for rain garden margins, wet woodland gardens, pond edges, and boggy areas in partial to full shade where few flowering plants thrive. Like all violets, a critical larval host plant for native fritillary butterflies. Forms attractive colonies in appropriate moist, shaded conditions by self-seeding and spreading by runners.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall at least 6 weeks before the ground freezes; disturb as little as possible once established.
directions: Plant bulbs 6 in deep in humus-rich, well-drained soil; martagon lilies are unusual in tolerating partial shade and actually prefer dappled woodland conditions. Whorled leaves and pendant, turks-cap-shaped flowers in pink, purple, or white with recurved petals appear in early summer. Martagon lilies are slow to establish and may not bloom for 2 years after planting — once settled they are very long-lived and resent being disturbed. Self-seeding is common; seedlings take 4–7 years to bloom.

plant_note: Direct sow after the last frost; soak seed to speed germination.
directions: Soak seeds overnight and direct sow after the last frost, covering about 1/2 inch. Named for opening in late afternoon, this fast-growing plant flourishes almost anywhere, including hot, windy spots, and tolerates most soils. Fragrant tubular flowers open after 4pm to attract hummingbirds and other pollinators, making it an excellent annual hedge. It reseeds freely and forms tubers that can be dug and stored over winter in cold climates.

plant_note: Set out after frost; treated as an annual in cold zones and a woody perennial in zones 7 and warmer.
directions: Mary Ann is a heat- and drought-tolerant lantana bearing clusters of yellow, orange, and purple-pink flowers from spring through fall. The blooms are a magnet for butterflies and other pollinators while shrugging off humidity and poor soil. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil and water sparingly once established, as it thrives on neglect. In zones colder than 7 it is grown as an annual or overwintered indoors. Trim back leggy stems midseason to keep the plant compact and flowering freely.

plant_note: Sow in darkness indoors; pansies thrive in cool spring and fall weather.
directions: Sow seed in darkness indoors 10 to 12 weeks before the last frost, since light inhibits germination. Matrix Red Blotch bears extra-large 3.5 inch scarlet-red flowers with dramatic dark blotches on compact, well-branched plants. Pansies love cool weather, so plant them out in early spring or fall for lush, tidy color. Grow in sun to part shade with moist, rich soil, and deadhead the edible blooms to keep flowering strong.

plant_note: Sow in darkness indoors; pansies thrive in cool spring and fall weather.
directions: Sow seed in darkness indoors 10 to 12 weeks before the last frost, since light inhibits germination. Matrix Rose Blotch features soft rose petals with dark centers plus occasional creamy-rose blooms for elegant color. Its compact, spreading habit gives lush coverage with little upkeep, and the edible flowers brighten dishes and drinks. Grow in sun to part shade with moist, rich soil, planting in early spring or fall and deadheading to prolong bloom.

plant_note: Sow in darkness indoors; pansies thrive in cool spring and fall weather.
directions: Sow seed in darkness indoors 10 to 12 weeks before the last frost, since light inhibits germination. Matrix Solar Flare dazzles with red, orange, and yellow blooms and burgundy centers outlined in golden yellow. The color shifts with temperature, showing more red in cool weather and brighter yellow tones as it warms. Grow in sun to part shade with moist, rich soil, planting in early spring or fall and deadheading to keep the display vivid.

plant_note: Fall sow for natural stratification; cold-treat seed before spring sowing.
directions: This native perennial sunflower towers up to ten feet and lines its stems with golden-yellow blooms in late summer and fall. Sow seed outdoors in fall or cold-stratify before spring planting, and give it full sun with room to spread since it forms robust colonies. It is drought tolerant once established and provides valuable late-season nectar and seed for pollinators and birds. Cut back or divide clumps periodically to keep the vigorous spread in check.

plant_note: Plant rhizome sections 2 inches deep in early spring; the rhizome spreads aggressively underground to form colonies — give it ample space in a naturalized area.
directions: Plant in moist, rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; this woodland native spreads by horizontal rhizomes to form dramatic colonies of large umbrella-like leaves that carpet the forest floor. The single white flower hidden beneath the paired leaves in spring develops into a yellowish, lemon-scented fruit by midsummer — the only part of the plant that is edible when fully soft and ripe. The roots, leaves, and unripe fruit contain podophyllin, a potent toxin, and should never be consumed. The plant goes dormant and disappears by late summer, so interplant with ferns or other shade perennials to fill the void.

plant_note: Sow after all danger of frost has passed and soil has warmed.
directions: Sow seeds directly into warm, well-drained soil once frost danger passes, barely covering them, or start indoors a few weeks before the last frost. This compact variety produces three-inch deep pink blooms with white-tipped petals, so pinch young plants to encourage bushy branching. Deadhead spent flowers regularly to prolong bloom into fall. Zinnias tolerate heat well and make excellent cut flowers and pollinator draws.

plant_note: Cold stratification improves germination; may be fall-sown.
directions: Chilling the seed for a few weeks before sowing, or fall sowing, improves germination for this hardy perennial. Press seeds onto the surface as light aids germination, and keep soil evenly moist. This strain produces large three-inch flowers with long, graceful spurs in a wide color range, blooming in the second year. It thrives in dappled shade in rock gardens and perennial borders and self-sows readily.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall for spring germination; seeds require cold stratification which fall planting provides naturally — meadow parsnip self-seeds reliably in moist woodland gardens and can also be divided in early spring.
directions: Meadow parsnip is a native woodland wildflower of the carrot family (Apiaceae) closely resembling golden alexanders (Zizia aurea) but with distinctly three-parted lower leaves and wider habitat tolerance — it grows in moist to mesic woodland openings, stream banks, and shaded meadow edges where filtered light is available. Flat-topped clusters of tiny golden-yellow flowers appear in late spring and are critically important early-season food for short-tongued native bees, parasitic wasps, soldier beetles, and early butterflies; it is also a documented larval host plant for the black swallowtail butterfly, making it doubly valuable in the native pollinator garden. The delicate, fernlike foliage remains attractive through the season in shade gardens, providing fine texture among coarser companions like wild ginger and hostas. Combine with Virginia bluebells, native columbine, and other spring ephemerals in a moist woodland garden for layered bloom from late winter through summer.

plant_note: Plant container-grown or bare-root transplants in fall in average, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full to partial sun; meadow rose is nearly thornless compared to most native roses and adapts to a wide range of moist to moderately dry soil conditions.
directions: Meadow rose is the most cold-hardy and one of the most nearly thornless of all North American native roses — its species name 'blanda' means smooth, mild, or agreeable, referring to the nearly unarmed (spineless) character of mature stems that make it among the more garden-friendly of all native roses for areas near walkways. Fragrant, five-petaled, medium-pink flowers appear in June on arching stems, followed by small, orange-red hips that persist through winter and are consumed by ruffed grouse, mockingbirds, and a remarkable variety of songbirds. Native to the eastern and north-central United States from Maine to Colorado, it forms slowly spreading colonies by suckers and is among the first plants to colonize disturbed sites and woodland edges, providing excellent wildlife cover and erosion control. Like all native roses, it is host to native mining bees (Halictus and Andrena species) that collect rose pollen as a primary food source, and many moth species use Rosa as a larval host plant. Excellent for naturalizing in prairie and meadow borders, hedgerows, and native garden edges.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost with cold stratification; or plant divisions in early spring.
directions: Plant in humus-rich, consistently moist soil in partial shade; tall meadow rue creates an airy, vertical accent with blue-green, columbine-like foliage and billowing clouds of lavender-purple flowers in midsummer. The delicate flowers are held on strong, self-supporting stems and sway gracefully in a breeze. Seldom needs staking if grown in adequate light; divide every 4–5 years in early spring to keep plants vigorous. Excellent in a woodland border or at the back of a shaded perennial bed.

plant_note: Direct sow once soil is warm and frost has passed.
directions: Direct sow into warm soil after frost, or start indoors for an earlier display. This variety produces vibrant scarlet, semi-double to double blooms four to five inches across on long, sturdy stems ideal for cutting. Give plants full sun and good air circulation to reduce mildew, and deadhead to keep blooms coming through frost. A reliable pollinator and butterfly magnet.

plant_note: Plant container stock after last frost; Mexican bush sage blooms in fall and is the showiest late-season salvia for zone 8–11 landscapes.
directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun; Mexican bush sage is drought tolerant once established and demands good drainage over fertility. It is a short-day plant that blooms from September through frost, producing velvety purple flower spikes loved by hummingbirds and bumblebees; the later into fall it blooms, the more spectacular the display. Cut the entire plant back to 6–8 in after the last hard freeze in late winter to stimulate dense new basal growth. Propagate easily by dividing the clump in spring or rooting stem cuttings in moist perlite.

plant_note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to slightly alkaline soil in full sun. Mexican Fan Palm tolerates drought, alkaline soil, and poor sandy soil remarkably well once established; avoid wet, poorly drained sites.
directions: Mexican Fan Palm is one of the most widely planted palms in the world, native to streams and oases in Sonora and Baja California, Mexico, and now the defining skyline palm of Los Angeles, the Gulf Coast, and tropical resort landscapes worldwide — its extremely slender, tall trunk topped by a compact crown of fan-shaped fronds is the prototypical palm silhouette recognized globally. Fast-growing (1–3 feet per year once established), it reaches 50–70 feet in cultivation and is among the most drought- and heat-tolerant of all palms, thriving in poor, sandy, alkaline soil with minimal irrigation once established. Clusters of small white flowers on arching inflorescences are followed by small black fruits that attract birds; the old fronds form a brown 'skirt' of dead leaves along the trunk unless trimmed for neatness. Plant in full sun in any well-drained soil; it is intolerant of wet, clay soils and prolonged cold below 22°F but otherwise requires minimal care.

plant_note: Direct sow around last frost date in a sunny, dry site; self-seeds profusely — site away from natural areas.
directions: Sow seeds directly in full sun in lean, very well-drained soil — rich or moist conditions produce weak, floppy plants. The hair-fine, bright green leaves form airy, billowing mounds that catch every breeze, creating a constant motion in the landscape; the foliage turns blonde and golden in summer heat. This grass self-seeds very aggressively and is considered invasive in California and some southwestern states — deadhead before seeds ripen if growing in areas with native grasslands. Cut back hard in late winter before new growth begins.

common_name: Mexican Flame Vine (Orange Glow Vine, Fireman's Cap)
plant_note: Root stem cuttings in spring in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil in full sun; Mexican flame vine grows rapidly and blooms from mid-summer through winter in zone 9–11. In zone 8a it may die back in severe frost but resprouts from established roots. Provide a trellis, fence, or large shrub to climb through.
directions: Root cuttings in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun on a fence or trellis; Mexican flame vine is one of the most spectacular winter-blooming vines for zone 9–11, producing dense clusters of bright orange, daisy-like flowers with contrasting yellow or orange centers from October through March — exactly the cool-season period when most other tropical vines have stopped blooming. The loose, twining stems 6–15 feet long bear small, dark green, slightly succulent leaves and are covered in vibrant orange blooms that attract butterflies. The orange color is exceptionally vivid and warm, excellent for combining with blue salvias or purple queen for autumn and winter color. In zone 8a it is killed to the ground in hard freezes but established root systems resprout reliably each spring to bloom again by mid-summer.

plant_note: Plant container-grown plants in spring in moist, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; Mexican heather establishes quickly and begins blooming almost immediately after transplanting. In zones 9–10 it often self-sows freely and may behave as a short-lived perennial; it is used as an annual in zones 8 and colder.
directions: Plant in moist, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; Mexican heather is a fine-textured, dense-growing tropical perennial that produces a continuous profusion of tiny lavender, pink, or white flowers on compact mounds of tiny, shiny, narrow leaves — resembling true heather (Calluna) in growth habit and foliage texture but adapted to tropical heat rather than cool climates. It blooms almost without pause from planting through frost and is one of the best butterfly and skipper nectar plants available for Gulf Coast gardens. In zone 9 it is typically a short-lived perennial that often reseeds to maintain a permanent presence. It is outstanding as a low edging plant, in mass plantings, along pathways, or at the front of mixed borders where its fine texture contrasts beautifully with coarser tropical foliage. Requires almost no deadheading or maintenance; simply trim lightly in early spring to refresh the habit.

plant_note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in any moist to dry, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; Mexican petunia is extremely adaptable and spreading — contain with deep edging if planting near lawn or naturalistic areas, as it spreads aggressively by underground runners and self-sown seed in warm climates. Use the compact, non-invasive cultivar 'Purple Showers' or 'Katie' in contained plantings.
directions: Plant in any well-drained to moist soil in full sun to partial shade; Mexican petunia is one of the most widely used and lowest-maintenance flowering perennials in Gulf Coast landscapes, producing a continuous display of funnel-shaped flowers in purple, pink, or white from spring through frost on upright stems with narrow, dark-green leaves. Each individual flower lasts only one day, but the plant produces new flowers in such abundance that the display is uninterrupted throughout the season. It is remarkably drought, heat, and salt tolerant once established and thrives in the conditions that challenge most other perennials. However, it spreads aggressively by underground runners and abundant self-seeding — R. simplex is considered invasive in natural areas in Florida and other Gulf states. Compact, sterile cultivars such as 'Purple Showers' and 'Katie' (dwarf, 8–12 inches) are strongly recommended for residential use as they do not spread by seed. Deadhead or shear to refresh and stimulate new bloom flushes.

plant_note: Plant rooted cuttings after last frost in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in partial shade to full sun; in zone 8 it may die back to roots in hard freezes but re-sprouts in spring.
directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in partial shade to full sun; Mexican sage is a large, bold, shrubby species with broad, deeply veined, highly aromatic leaves and long wands of blue-purple flowers from late summer through fall that attract hummingbirds and bumblebees. It is native to mountain forests and canyon edges in central Mexico at moderate elevation and tolerates partial shade better than most sages, making it useful in the lightly shaded areas of a Gulf Coast or zone 8–9 garden. The foliage has a strong, pleasant herbal fragrance when brushed and is deer resistant. It grows rapidly to a large sub-shrub in one season and may be damaged or killed by hard freezes but typically re-sprouts from the root crown in zone 8. Shear back by half in mid-spring to encourage compact, heavily blooming growth.

plant_note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger passes — one of the best heat-loving annuals for attracting butterflies and hummingbirds.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep in warm starting mix; transplant carefully as Tithonia dislikes root disturbance — use biodegradable pots if starting indoors. Mexican sunflower thrives in heat, poor soil, and dry conditions, making it ideal for back-of-border plantings in Gulf Coast summers when other annuals falter. The vivid orange, 3-inch daisy-like flowers are irresistible to monarch butterflies, swallowtails, and hummingbirds from midsummer through frost. Deadhead regularly by cutting stems (not just the flower head) back to a side shoot to maintain tidy plants and prolong heavy bloom.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil and full sun.
directions: Mexican sunflower is a tall, bushy annual covered in vibrant orange-red daisy blooms that are a favorite of monarch butterflies. Sow seeds directly after frost in warm soil and full sun, thinning to give the vigorous plants room to reach up to six feet. It tolerates heat and poor soil well and blooms nonstop from midsummer until frost. Deadhead to keep the flush going, and stake in exposed sites since the branching stems can grow heavy.

plant_note: Plant in spring or fall in consistently moist, humus-rich soil in part shade.
directions: Mighty Chocolate Cherry is a tall astilbe with dramatic cherry-red plumes rising above green-burgundy chocolate-toned foliage. It brings bold vertical color to shaded, moist borders and woodland gardens. Plant in partial shade in rich soil that stays evenly moist, since astilbe suffers if it dries out. Water regularly and mulch to conserve moisture and keep roots cool. Cut back spent foliage in late fall and divide clumps every three to four years to maintain vigor.

plant_note: Surface sow; needs light and dislikes transplanting.
directions: Direct sow mignonette on the soil surface, as it needs light to germinate and resents root disturbance. The unremarkable greenish flower spikes release an intensely sweet raspberry-like fragrance beloved in Victorian gardens. Grow in full sun to light shade in cool weather and fertile, alkaline soil. Position it near paths and seating where its perfume can be enjoyed, and make successive sowings for a long season.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring or fall; resents transplanting.
directions: Sow directly where plants are to grow, as California poppies have a taproot and dislike transplanting. Scatter seed on prepared soil in early spring or fall and press in lightly. This selection bears saucer-shaped, bright orange-red flowers that open in sun and close at night or on cloudy days. Heat- and drought-tolerant once established, it needs little care and often self-sows for years of color.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring where plants will grow; do not transplant.
directions: This California poppy strain glows with deep red-orange, saucer-shaped blooms that open in sun and close at night. Sow seeds directly in early spring where they are to grow, pressing them onto the surface since the taproot resents transplanting. Full sun and lean, well-drained soil suit it best, and it needs little water once established. It self-sows reliably to return and naturalize in gardens and meadows.

plant_note: Cold-moist stratify seed or fall sow for best germination.
directions: Cold-moist stratify the seed for several weeks or sow in fall to break dormancy for this heirloom perennial. Direct sow where plants will grow, as the deep taproot resists transplanting. From summer onward it produces clusters of fiery red-orange flowers, a preferred nectar source for monarchs and other pollinators, followed by ornamental seed pods. Drought tolerant once established, it is best left undisturbed to form a long-lived clump.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring; dislikes root disturbance.
directions: Direct sow in early spring since this annual dislikes transplanting; successive sowings extend the bloom. Cover seed lightly and keep evenly moist until germination. Airy, fern-like foliage surrounds jewel-toned blue and pastel flowers that attract pollinators and shine in bouquets. The inflated seed pods that follow are prized for dried arrangements; leave some to self-sow for next year.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring or fall; resents transplanting.
directions: Sow seed directly onto prepared soil in early spring or fall, since the taproot resents being moved. This blend produces ruffled, saucer-shaped double and semi-double flowers in rose, red, orange, white, and yellow. Heat- and drought-tolerant, it asks little once established and readily self-sows for years of carpeted color. Grow in full sun for the best flowering.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring or fall; needs cool soil to germinate.
directions: Direct sow in cool soil in early spring or in fall, as larkspur germinates best at cool temperatures and dislikes transplanting; chilling the seed helps. This variety bears densely packed nine- to twelve-inch spikes of double, ruffled florets in soft greyed lavender and dusty mauve-purple. Provide full sun and support for the tall stems, which make excellent fresh or dried cut flowers. Frost tolerant when young.

plant_note: Plant divisions 2–4 inches apart in spring or fall; in warm climates fall planting takes advantage of cooler temperatures for root establishment before summer heat.
directions: Plant divisions in virtually any soil type — from dry sandy to moderately moist — in full shade to partial sun; this tough Asian native is one of the most versatile shade groundcovers for southern gardens. Once established it smothers weeds, requires no mowing, and remains attractive year-round with its dark, grass-like evergreen foliage and small blue-black berries in fall. Mow or shear the entire planting to 2–3 inches in late winter every few years to remove older, ratty foliage and encourage fresh, uniform growth. Divide overgrown clumps every 4–5 years, replanting at the original spacing to maintain density.

plant_note: Sow in spring or fall; as a biennial it flowers the second year.
directions: Money plant, or silver dollar plant, is grown for its charming purple spring flowers and the translucent, coin-shaped seed pods that follow. Sow seeds shallowly in spring or fall, remembering that as a biennial it forms leaves the first year and blooms the second. It grows well in partial shade with average moisture and self-sows to keep returning. Leave the pods to dry on the plant, then peel the papery outer layers for silvery dried arrangements.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost when soil is warm.
directions: Direct sow one inch deep after frost in a sunny, sheltered site protected from strong wind, given the towering height. Each single-stem plant produces one enormous yellow flower up to eighteen inches across filled with large, tasty kernels. Provide rich soil and steady moisture to support the massive growth, and stake in exposed locations. Leave heads to ripen for edible seeds or to feed birds.

plant_note: Plant tuber-like roots in fall or early spring in a cool, moist, partially shaded site; wear gloves — all parts are highly toxic.
directions: Plant with the tuber-like roots 3–4 in deep in rich, cool, moist, well-drained soil; monkshood is a stately perennial with bold, deeply lobed foliage and tall spikes of deep blue-violet helmet-shaped flowers in late summer to early fall. All parts of the plant are extremely toxic if ingested or if the sap contacts broken skin — always wear gloves when handling and wash hands thoroughly after. It is one of the best late-season blues in the perennial garden and thrives where summers are cool. Divide every 4–5 years in early spring; keep plants where they will not be disturbed by children or pets.

plant_note: Plant corms in spring; naturalizes and can spread widely.
directions: Plant montbretia corms in spring three to four inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun. The arching sprays of fiery orange to red tubular flowers in mid to late summer are magnets for hummingbirds. It multiplies rapidly by cormlets and can spread widely, so site it where vigorous growth is welcome. Divide congested clumps every few years and mulch for winter in the colder end of its range.

plant_note: Start indoors 2–4 weeks before last frost after nicking or soaking seed; transplant after all frost risk has passed.
directions: Nick or soak seeds in warm water for 12 hours before sowing 1/2 in deep. Moonflower is a vigorous, fast-growing vine that needs a strong trellis or fence. Unlike morning glories, its large, fragrant white blossoms open in the evening and remain open through the night and into the following morning. Avoid high-nitrogen fertilizers to encourage blooming.

plant_note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is warm; or nick and soak seed overnight then start indoors 2–4 weeks early.
directions: Nick the hard seed coat with a file or soak in warm water for 8–12 hours before sowing 1/2 in deep to speed germination. Morning glories need a fence, trellis, or other support and are best sown directly at the base of their support. Avoid over-fertilizing, which promotes leaves at the expense of flowers. Blooms open in the morning and close by afternoon on each individual day.

plant_note: Surface sow in early spring or fall; dislikes transplanting.
directions: Surface sow the fine seed directly in early spring or fall, as poppies need light to germinate and resent root disturbance. This blend glows in soft shades of pink, white, and purple with crepe-paper petals and contrasting dark centers. Low-maintenance and drought-tolerant, it is stunning massed along borders and self-sows to return each year. Thin seedlings to give plants room.

plant_note: Plant clump divisions in early spring in moist to moderately dry, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in deep to partial shade; Mottled Wild Ginger is slow-growing and long-lived. Maintain consistent moisture until established, then reduce watering as the plant is drought-tolerant in deep shade once its root system is developed.
directions: Mottled Wild Ginger is a beautiful native evergreen ground cover of the southern Appalachians and upper coastal plain, producing low mounds of heart-shaped, leathery leaves decorated with silver and pale green mottling against dark green — one of the most ornamentally spectacular native shade plants of the southeastern United States. Unlike the more northern Asarum canadense, which is deciduous, Mottled Wild Ginger retains its attractively patterned foliage through winter and forms a weed-suppressing carpet in dry to moist shade where few other plants grow reliably year-round. The curious, urn-shaped brownish-purple flowers are produced near the soil surface in late spring, hidden beneath the foliage where they are pollinated primarily by ground beetles and ants attracted to their earthy fragrance. Plant divisions in spring in moist to moderately dry, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in deep to partial shade; plants spread very slowly but are virtually permanent once established, gradually forming dense colonies that suppress weeds under deciduous trees.

plant_note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 4.5–6.5), humus-rich soil in partial shade. Mountain Camellia is somewhat easier to establish than Silky Camellia (Stewartia malacodendron) and more cold-hardy; avoid waterlogged or alkaline soils.
directions: Mountain Camellia is a striking native understory shrub or small tree, native to rich, moist, wooded slopes, ravine walls, and stream terraces in the southern Appalachians from Virginia and Kentucky south to Georgia and Alabama, blooming in July and August with large, white, five-petaled flowers bearing a prominent cluster of orange-tipped stamens. Larger than Silky Camellia (Stewartia malacodendron) and more cold-hardy (to zone 5), Mountain Camellia blooms later in summer when few other native shrubs are flowering, extending ornamental interest deep into the season. The smooth, exfoliating gray bark provides attractive winter interest, and the foliage turns shades of orange and red in fall. Grow in moist, well-drained, acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade — it is somewhat more tolerant of garden conditions than Silky Camellia and establishes more readily from nursery transplants, though it is still slow-growing and long-lived.

plant_note: Plant container specimens in fall in moist, slightly acidic (pH 5.0–6.5), humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade; mountain hydrangea is significantly more shade and heat tolerant than the common bigleaf hydrangea (H. macrophylla) and is the better choice for zone 7–9 shaded garden conditions.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade — mountain hydrangea from the mountains of Korea and Japan is a smaller, more elegant, and significantly more shade and heat tolerant cousin of the common bigleaf hydrangea (H. macrophylla), producing delicate, lace-cap or mophead flower clusters in blue, pink, or white (depending on soil pH and cultivar) in summer on compact, upright shrubs. It is more reliably heat tolerant than bigleaf hydrangea and flowers more consistently in zone 7–9 warm-climate gardens. Like bigleaf hydrangea, flower color shifts between blue and pink based on soil pH (blue in acidic soil, pink in alkaline) and is adjusted with aluminum sulfate applications. Outstanding cultivars include 'Blue Billow' (lacecap, blue), 'Preziosa' (mophead, pink aging to deep rose), and 'Tuff Stuff' (remontant). Its modest size makes it more practical than bigleaf hydrangea in smaller gardens.

plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring in well-drained, deeply acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich soil in partial shade; mountain laurel blooms on old wood — prune only immediately after flowering and never remove more than one-third of the plant.
directions: Plant in well-drained, deeply acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich soil in partial to light shade; mountain laurel is the state flower of Connecticut and Pennsylvania and one of the finest native flowering shrubs of the eastern woodlands, producing intricate, geometrically precise cup-and-saucer flowers in white, pink, bicolored, and banded forms in late spring. The flowers have a remarkable mechanism — the stamens are bent under tension and spring upright when touched by a bee, catapulting pollen onto the pollinator. All parts of the plant are toxic. It performs best in cool, moist, acidic woodland gardens and benefits from afternoon shade in zone 7–8. Named cultivars like 'Olympic Fire' (deep red buds, pink flowers), 'Elf' (compact dwarf), and 'Galaxy' (burgundy-red buds) offer exceptional garden forms. Mulch heavily with pine bark or oak leaves to maintain soil acidity and moisture.

plant_note: Tuck nursery transplants into crevices of acidic rock (sandstone or granite preferred, not limestone) in a shaded to partially shaded rock garden, with excellent drainage and consistent humidity. Mountain Spleenwort is challenging to grow outside of natural rock environments and performs best when the root zone is sheltered within a tight rock crevice.
directions: Mountain Spleenwort is a small, neat, evergreen native fern, native to the shaded, acidic rock outcrops, cliff faces, and boulder crevices of the Appalachian Mountains from New England south to Georgia and Alabama, where it colonizes the smallest cracks in sandstone and granite in the deep shade of north-facing cliffs. Its triangular, twice-pinnate fronds — reaching just 4–10 inches — are dark green, leathery, and persistent through winter, and the plant forms compact tufted rosettes anchored tightly in rock crevices with minimal root access to soil. Unlike the Maidenhair Spleenwort (Asplenium trichomanes), which prefers limestone or neutral-alkaline rock, Mountain Spleenwort is strictly an acidic-rock specialist, growing naturally on sandstone, granite, and quartzite but not on calcium-rich rocks. Most challenging to establish in cultivation outside of natural rock garden conditions — it is best planted directly into a tight crevice in an acidic rock garden or dry stone wall built with sandstone, where the roots can remain sheltered and consistently humid even as the fronds dry rapidly in the open air.

plant_note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in moist, acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade. Mountain Sweet Pepperbush is more shade-tolerant and grows taller than its coastal relative (Clethra alnifolia) and is better suited to mountain and Piedmont gardens in zones 5–8.
directions: Mountain Sweet Pepperbush is a tall native understory shrub, native to the rich, moist, hardwood forests and mountain cove edges of the southern Appalachians from Virginia and North Carolina south to Georgia and Alabama, growing to 12 feet or more in deep, humus-rich mountain soils and producing fragrant white flower spikes in July and August that attract bumblebees and native butterflies.
