Amend soil heavily with compost and pine bark before planting.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant 2 weeks before last frost as plants tolerate light frost.
directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep in a well-drained mix; germination is reliable at 60–70°F and seedlings grow quickly. Plant in full sun in well-drained, slightly alkaline soil — scabiosa performs best in cool to moderate temperatures and fades in intense summer heat. Cut flowers frequently to encourage continuous bloom from early summer until fall; flowers are excellent for cutting with long, wiry stems and last 7–10 days in a vase. In zones 7–10 try sowing in fall for earlier spring bloom before heat sets in.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger has passed into a sunny, alkaline-friendly site.
directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost, sowing 1/4 in deep in a light germination mix; transplant outdoors after frost into well-drained, slightly alkaline or neutral soil. The domed, pincushion flowers in rich maroon, lavender, white, pink, and burgundy are magnets for butterflies and superb for cutting. Deadhead consistently to prevent self-seeding and extend the long bloom period from early summer through frost. Avoid rich, wet soils — scabiosa thrives in moderately lean conditions and is drought-tolerant once established.

plant_note: Direct sow fresh seeds in fall in moist to moderately wet, sandy, acidic, poorly drained or seasonally wet soil in full sun to partial shade; Pine Barren Goldenrod is one of the few goldenrods that tolerates and prefers seasonally wet flatwoods and pine barren conditions. Established plants spread steadily by rhizomes.
directions: Pine Barren Goldenrod is a native perennial of the moist to seasonally wet sandy pine barrens, flatwoods, and boggy savannas of the Atlantic and Gulf coastal plain from New Jersey south to Florida and west to Louisiana, distinguished among goldenrods by its hollow, pithy stem (fistulosa means 'hollow' or 'pipe-like') and its tolerance for the wet, acidic, nutrient-poor soils of flatwood depressions and moist pine barrens. The bright yellow flower spikes in fall are an outstanding nectar source for monarch butterflies, native bees, and numerous other pollinators migrating or overwintering along the southeastern coastal plain. Spreading reliably by rhizomes to form colonies in appropriate conditions, Pine Barren Goldenrod is an excellent choice for wet meadow, rain garden, and moist flatwood restoration plantings. Direct sow fresh seeds in fall in moist to wet, sandy, acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; established plants naturalize readily in wet, open, seasonally flooded settings.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in spring after soil warms; lift where not hardy.
directions: Plant pineapple lily bulbs in spring about five inches deep in rich, well-drained soil once the ground has warmed. Each stout stem produces a dense spike of starry flowers topped by a tuft of leafy bracts resembling a pineapple. Grow in full sun with regular summer moisture, and it also performs beautifully in large containers. In cold climates lift the bulbs before frost or grow them in pots moved indoors for winter.

plant_note: Direct sow seeds in spring after temperatures warm consistently in sandy, well-drained, infertile, acidic to neutral soil in full sun. Pineland Heliotrope thrives in the dry pinelands and open sandy habitats of Florida and the Caribbean and self-seeds prolifically in hospitable conditions.
directions: Pineland Heliotrope is a native low-growing wildflower of the dry pinelands, pine flatwoods, and open sandy habitats of Florida and the Caribbean, producing a profusion of tiny yellow to creamy-white flowers on small, spreading, leafy mats throughout the warm season. A member of the borage family, the small flowers display the characteristic scorpioid cyme (coiled flower arrangement) of the heliotrope group and provide nectar for small native bees, Ceraunus Blue butterflies, and other small pollinators of the Florida pineland community. The narrow, resin-dotted, gland-bearing leaves give the plant a pleasant, somewhat spicy fragrance when brushed. Direct sow seeds in spring in sandy, well-drained, infertile, acidic soil in full sun; Pineland Heliotrope self-seeds freely in appropriate sandy conditions and is a useful groundcover for open, xeric Florida gardens where its continuous bloom and compact form fill bare sandy spaces with pollinators.

plant_note: Divide and plant pups in spring after all frost danger has passed, in moist, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun to light shade. Pink Banana is one of the smaller ornamental bananas, reaching only 4–6 feet, and is well-suited to containers or small garden spaces where larger bananas would be overwhelming.
directions: Pink Banana is one of the most ornamental of the small bananas, native to the forests of Assam, India, and grown worldwide for the striking combination of its vivid pink, velvety flower bracts and small, bright pink, hairy fruits that split open when ripe to reveal sweet white flesh studded with seeds — an unusual display that makes the plant a conversation piece in any garden. Growing only 4–6 feet tall, it is well-suited to container culture, small garden spaces, and use as an accent among larger tropical foliage plants, and it blooms and fruits within its first year from a good-sized pup. In zones 8–9 the pseudostem may die to the ground in freezing weather, but the rhizome re-sprouts reliably in spring; in zones 9b–11 it is effectively evergreen. Divide pups from the parent clump in spring, plant in rich, well-drained soil in full sun, and water and fertilize generously throughout the growing season.

plant_note: Plant only nursery-propagated, tissue-cultured specimens — never transplant from the wild (it is illegal in most states and plants almost never survive wild transplanting); plant in fall in acidic (pH 4.0–5.0), moist, humus-rich, sandy or rocky soil with full mycorrhizal fungal support in partial to full shade.
directions: Pink lady's slipper is perhaps the most iconic wildflower of eastern North American forests — its single inflated, pouched, rose-pink to magenta flower with a central cleft is instantly recognizable and has captured the imagination of generations of woodland hikers. It grows in acidic, humus-rich, sandy or rocky soil under pines, oaks, and hemlocks throughout eastern North America, requiring a very specific soil-dwelling fungus (Rhizoctonia species) to germinate from seed and to survive through its multi-year seedling stage. This fungal dependence, combined with slow growth (plants take 10–15 years to reach blooming size from seed) and sensitivity to transplanting, makes it one of the most challenging native wildflowers to establish; wild plants are strictly protected in most states. Purchase only nursery-propagated, tissue-cultured specimens from specialist native orchid nurseries and establish them in an area that has never been disturbed — once established in a suitable location, individual plants may persist and bloom for 50 or more years. Never collect from the wild.

plant_note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring; avoid fall planting in zones 6–7 as plants need a full season to establish before winter.
directions: Plant in full sun and well-drained, lean to average soil; muhly grass is tolerant of sandy soil, heat, humidity, and drought once established and is an outstanding choice for hot, dry southern landscapes. The main attraction is the spectacular cloud of airy, rose-pink flower plumes in September–October that transform the planting into something otherworldly when backlit by low autumn sun. Cut back to 4–6 in in late winter before new growth begins; avoid heavy organic mulching which can cause crown rot in humid climates. Divide every 4–5 years in spring to rejuvenate large clumps.

plant_note: Direct sow seeds on the surface of sandy, well-drained, infertile soil in full sun after all danger of frost has passed and soil has warmed to 70°F; do not cover seeds, as they require light to germinate. Pink Purslane self-seeds prolifically in sandy soil and may establish itself as a recurring volunteer in the garden.
directions: Pink Purslane is a native annual wildflower of the southeastern United States and tropical Americas, sprawling across sandy roadsides, coastal dunes, dry flatwoods openings, and disturbed sunny areas with tiny, vivid magenta-pink flowers that open in the morning sunshine and close by midday. A close relative of the Moss Rose (Portulaca grandiflora) sold in garden centers, Pink Purslane is smaller and more delicate but shares the same remarkable heat and drought tolerance that makes portulacas ideal for hot, dry, infertile spots where other flowering annuals fail. The fleshy, succulent leaves are edible — with a slightly sour, mucilaginous texture similar to common purslane — and were eaten by various indigenous peoples of the Americas. Direct sow seeds on the soil surface in full sun after soil warms to 70°F; Pink Purslane self-seeds prolifically in sandy, infertile, well-drained soil and may establish as a recurring garden volunteer.

plant_note: Plant bulbs 2–3 inches deep in spring after last frost, or in fall in zone 8–10 where they are hardy; the bulbs naturalize quickly and bloom most reliably after summer rain events.
directions: Plant bulbs 2–3 inches deep in well-drained, moderately moist soil in full sun to partial shade; pink rain lily is the most commonly grown zephyr lily in the South, producing soft rose-pink funnel-shaped flowers that emerge just days after summer and fall rainfall — an event that earns them the name 'rain lily.' The bulbs go dormant through winter and dry summers, then spring to life reliably after soaking rains from June through October. In zone 9–10 they may also bloom sporadically through winter with sufficient moisture. Plant in drifts of 20 or more for the most impactful display along path edges, in rock gardens, or at the front of borders. The bulbs naturalize readily and multiply over years into showy colonies. In zones colder than 7, lift and store bulbs dry through winter.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring; dislikes transplanting.
directions: Direct sow in spring where plants will grow, as rose mallow dislikes transplanting, and expect blooms in about sixty-five days. The lush plants stand two to four feet tall and cover themselves in pink, hibiscus-like flowers that magnetize pollinators. Give full sun and average soil, thinning seedlings for good air movement. It reseeds readily, so leave a few flowers to set seed for next year.

plant_note: Sprinkle seeds on the surface of moist, acidic, nutrient-poor medium (live sphagnum moss or 50/50 peat and perlite) without covering; keep standing in a tray of pure rain water or distilled water in full sun — pink sundew self-seeds prolifically in appropriate bog conditions.
directions: Pink sundew is the most common and widespread native sundew of the Gulf Coastal Plain, found abundantly in open, wet, acidic pine flatwoods, bogs, and roadside ditches from Virginia to Texas and Florida — its tiny rosettes of reddish, glistening, gland-tipped leaves studded with sticky droplets of mucilage trap and digest tiny insects (especially gnats, midges, and small flies) to supplement nutrients in its nutrient-poor habitat. Despite its miniature size (rosettes rarely exceed 2 inches across), pink sundew produces an outsized display of delicate, pink flowers on long stems in late spring, and in appropriate bog conditions it self-seeds prolifically to form dense mats of glittering, glandular rosettes that capture insects throughout the warm season. One of the easier carnivorous plants to grow from seed or to establish in a native bog garden alongside pitcher plants and grass pink orchids; seeds require no stratification and germinate readily on the surface of moist sphagnum or peat. Grow in standing water (the tray method) using rain water or distilled water only — tap water hardness kills all carnivorous plants over time.

plant_note: Plant bulb divisions or container transplants in fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial shade; pink woodsorrel is a reliable, low-care groundcover that spreads slowly by bulb offsets to fill shaded areas — it can also be grown as a container or hanging basket plant.
directions: Plant bulb divisions in fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial shade — pink woodsorrel is a compact, ever-blooming bulbous perennial producing bright pink, five-petaled flowers nearly year-round in mild climates above neat, dark green, three-leaflet clover-like foliage that is ever-present and attractive. Unlike the common weedy Oxalis species that invade lawns, this species is a tidy, manageable ornamental that spreads slowly by bulb offsets without becoming a nuisance. It is especially effective as a low groundcover or container plant for partially shaded areas, and blooms reliably in conditions of moderate to bright indirect light. The 'Alba' form offers pure white flowers; 'Rosea' produces deeper rose-pink. An underused and highly rewarding perennial for consistently blooming color in the partial-shade border or container garden in zone 7–10.

plant_note: Surface-sow and press in; seed needs light to germinate.
directions: Press the tiny seed onto the soil surface without covering it, since light aids germination. This reddish-pink heirloom yarrow forms feathery, aromatic foliage topped with flat clusters of bloom above soft leaves. Once established it is exceptionally drought-tolerant and thrives in lean, well-drained soil in full sun. Deadhead spent flowers to prolong bloom, and divide clumps every few years to keep them vigorous.

plant_note: Plant container-grown transplants in spring in moist, consistently acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich soil in partial shade to light shade. Pinkshell Azalea requires reliable moisture and good air circulation and does not tolerate dry, alkaline, or compacted soils; it is most reliable in cool, high-humidity environments in Zones 4–7.
directions: Pinkshell Azalea is a beautiful and rare native deciduous azalea endemic to a small area of the Blue Ridge Mountains of North Carolina, where it grows on boggy hillsides, stream banks, and acidic seeps at elevations above 3,000 feet — one of the most restricted native azaleas in the eastern United States, known from fewer than 20 natural populations. The delicate, five-petaled, light to deep pink flowers borne in early spring before leaf emergence have a distinctive butterfly shape lacking the long floral tube typical of most azaleas, making the blooms uniquely recognizable among native azalea enthusiasts and adding exceptional elegance to the spring garden. Among the hardiest of all native azaleas, surviving USDA Zone 4 winters with ease, Pinkshell Azalea is a superb choice for northern gardens seeking a spectacular, native spring-flowering shrub for moist, partially shaded spots. Plant in moist, acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade with good air circulation; it requires reliable moisture and high humidity to perform well, especially in warmer zones.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost; nick and soak seed to aid germination.
directions: Nick and soak the hard seed, then direct sow after frost at the base of a trellis or fence for these quick-growing heirloom vines. Heart-shaped leaves and pinwheel-patterned blossoms open to greet the morning, with a fresh set of flowers each day. Give full sun and support to climb, and avoid overly rich soil, which favors leaves over blooms. Note that morning glory seeds are toxic if ingested.

plant_note: Plant container specimens in fall in moist, slightly acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich, very well-drained soil in partial shade; pinxter azalea requires excellent drainage and strongly acidic conditions — it will not survive in alkaline or clay soils. Mulch heavily with pine bark to maintain acidic soil pH and moisture.
directions: Plant in moist, strongly acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich, very well-drained soil in partial to dappled shade — pinxter azalea is a graceful, open-branching deciduous native azalea of the eastern piedmont and coastal plain, producing masses of pale to deep pink tubular flowers with long, exserted stamens in April–May before or just as the leaves unfurl, filling shaded woodland gardens with a lovely, sweet-spicy fragrance. Unlike evergreen azaleas, pinxter's open, airy form is beautiful even out of bloom, with yellow-green to orange fall foliage. It grows naturally in acidic, sandy, well-drained soils at the edges of pine and oak woodlands and along stream banks in the Southeast. Slower-growing and longer-lived than most garden azaleas, with exceptional cold and heat tolerance for zone 5–9. An outstanding native alternative to introduced evergreen azaleas that thrives in the challenging, sandy, acidic soils of Gulf Coast pine woodlands without the pest and disease issues of Asian azalea species.

plant_note: Plant container-grown vines near a sturdy trellis, pergola, or tree trunk in partial to full shade; the vine grows quickly once established but may be slow to emerge in spring.
directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade; pipevine is a vigorous native climbing vine with enormous heart-shaped leaves that creates a dense, lush shade canopy and unusual curved pipe-shaped brown flowers in late spring that lure and temporarily trap small flies as pollinators. It is the essential host plant for the stunning pipevine swallowtail butterfly, which lays eggs exclusively on Aristolochia species — the caterpillars absorb aristolochic acids from the plant, making both larva and adult butterfly toxic to predators. Plant several vines together to provide enough leaf mass for the caterpillars. Train on a sturdy arbor, pergola, or fence; the vine can grow 20–30 feet and covers itself with tropical-looking foliage by midsummer. Cut back hard in late winter to control size.

plant_note: Plant with crown at soil level in well-drained, limestone-based or neutral to slightly alkaline soil in partial shade to full sun; Pitcher's clematis is a prairie-edge species naturally found on limestone bluffs, rocky slopes, and thicket edges in the south-central US.
directions: Pitcher's clematis is a native vine of the south-central United States — Texas, Oklahoma, Arkansas, and adjacent states — producing nodding, pitcher- or urn-shaped flowers of purplish-blue to violet-blue with thick, recurved petal tips from May through August on a twining vine that scrambles through shrubs and fences with a restrained, manageable habit. Its distinctive flowers are adapted to specialist bee pollinators and are particularly visited by native metallic green sweat bees that navigate the closed floral tube; the feathery, silvery seedheads that follow persist into winter as the seed plumes expand and catch the light. As a species adapted to drier, alkaline limestone soils, Pitcher's clematis is among the most heat- and drought-tolerant of all native clematis, performing well in zones 7–9 where summer heat and occasional drought challenge other species in the genus. Cut stems back to 12–18 inches in late winter to encourage vigorous new growth and prolific flowering the following season.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring; nick and soak seed to aid germination.
directions: Nick and soak the seed, then direct sow in spring where plants will grow, as lupines resent transplanting. This compact variety stays just twelve to eighteen inches tall with loose eight-inch spikes in pink, blue, purple, and white, some bicolored. Plant in groups for a splash of color in smaller gardens or containers. Provide full sun and well-drained soil, and deadhead spent spikes to prolong bloom.

plant_note: Sow directly; a top pollinator and self-sowing native.
directions: Direct sow plains coreopsis on the soil surface, as light aids germination. This North American native bears masses of yellow daisies banded in mahogany-red that attract bees and butterflies. It tolerates poor, dry soils and full sun, making it excellent for meadows and low-maintenance borders. Allow some seed to ripen for dependable self-sowing, or deadhead to extend the bloom period.

plant_note: Surface-sow; needs light to germinate. Self-seeds readily.
directions: Scatter seed on prepared soil and barely press it in, as light triggers germination. This easy native annual, also called calliopsis or golden tickseed, opens bright yellow petals marked with burgundy centers from early summer to fall. It thrives in poor, dry soil and full sun, quickly filling meadows and borders with color. Allow some flowers to set seed and it will reliably return the following season.

plant_note: Direct sow scarified seed in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to poor, deep soil in full sun; plains wild indigo has a very deep taproot and resents disturbance once established — sow where it will remain permanently. Scratch seed lightly and cold-stratify before spring sowing.
directions: Sow scarified seed in fall in well-drained, average to poor, deep soil in full sun — plains wild indigo is a low, spreading prairie legume of the Great Plains and midwestern prairies producing dense racemes of cream to pale yellow pea flowers in early spring, among the earliest native prairie plants to bloom. Unlike the tall blue false indigo (B. australis), plants remain compact and spread broadly rather than upright — the procumbent, mounded form covered with 4-inch cream flower spikes is striking in the spring landscape. A nitrogen-fixing prairie legume with an extremely deep taproot that survives drought and fire. Extremely long-lived once established (decades) but slow to establish from seed. Excellent for prairie garden restoration and native plant meadow plantings in zones 4–9. The inflated black seed pods are interesting ornamentally and rattle when dry. A host plant for specialist bees and several moth species.

plant_note: Plant container-grown transplants in early spring in moist to wet, acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; Planer Tree tolerates and prefers seasonally flooded conditions. Seeds should be sown fresh in moist, saturated soil as soon as they ripen in spring, as viability diminishes quickly.
directions: Planer Tree — also called Water Elm — is a small and uncommon native tree of permanently wet swamps, river floodplains, and cypress-tupelo bottomlands of the coastal plain from South Carolina to Florida and west to Texas, where it often grows in the understory beneath bald cypress, water tupelo, and swamp cottonwood. The small, rough-textured leaves and warty, inconspicuous drupes superficially resemble those of the closely related elms, but the single nutlet with a distinctly rough, tuberculate surface is the diagnostic feature that separates Planera from Ulmus. Seeds should be collected and planted fresh in moist to wet, acidic soil under light shade, as viability drops quickly; container-grown nursery transplants are the most reliable method for establishing this species in cultivation. Planer Tree is rarely grown in ornamental settings but makes a distinctive small specimen for rain gardens, pond margins, and naturalized floodplain plantings where saturated soils and periodic flooding prevent most woody plants from thriving.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; plantain-leaf sedge grows naturally in moist, rocky, north-facing woodland slopes in rich, deep soil and requires similar conditions in the garden.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; plantain-leaf sedge is the most architecturally distinctive of all the native shade sedges, immediately recognizable by its unusually broad, pleated, dark green leaves (1–2 inches wide) with prominent parallel veins resembling plantain (Plantago) leaves — a completely different aesthetic from typical narrow-leaved sedges. The leaves remain evergreen or semi-evergreen through winter, providing year-round ground-level interest in the woodland garden. Burgundy-red stems bear small, elegant fruiting spikes in early spring before most other plants emerge. It grows naturally on moist, north-facing, rocky woodland slopes in the Appalachians and eastern US. Slower to spread than some native sedges but forms a beautiful, refined groundcover in appropriate conditions. An outstanding accent plant for the front of a shaded border or as a specimen among ferns and hostas.

plant_note: Plant container specimens in fall in well-drained, alkaline to slightly acidic, rocky to loamy, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun; plateau live oak is significantly more cold and drought tolerant than the coastal live oak (Q. virginiana) and is the live oak of choice for inland Texas gardens away from the mild Gulf Coast climate.
directions: Plant in well-drained, alkaline to slightly acidic, rocky to loamy, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun — plateau live oak is the iconic evergreen oak of the Texas Hill Country, Edwards Plateau, and Balcones Escarpment, where it forms the defining, spreading, ancient trees of the landscape. Nearly identical to the coastal live oak (Q. virginiana) in appearance, it is distinguished by its superior cold tolerance (surviving zone 6 winter cold without damage) and extreme drought tolerance, adapted to the thin, rocky, alkaline limestone soils of the Hill Country where moisture is scarce. Trees develop the same magnificent, broadly spreading, rounded crown with leathery, dark evergreen leaves and massive, gnarled trunks as coastal live oak but in harsher conditions. Small acorns in the red oak group ripen in two years. This is the live oak for central and west Texas gardens — offering the same magnificent landscape presence as its coastal relative but with dramatically greater cold hardiness and drought performance.

plant_note: Allow cuttings to dry and callus for 1–2 weeks before planting in extremely well-drained, sandy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; do not water until new growth appears — plumeria cuttings rot easily if kept wet before rooting. Established plants are dormant and leafless in winter and require complete dryness during dormancy.
directions: Plant cuttings in extremely well-drained, sandy soil in full sun — plumeria is a tropical American tree sacred in Buddhist and Hindu cultures, widely planted around temples throughout Asia and used in Hawaiian leis, and one of the most intensely fragrant of all flowering plants. The waxy, 4-inch flowers in white, yellow, pink, red, or multicolor combinations have a powerful sweet tropical scent that is strongest at night. Plumeria is deciduous (leafless in winter), drought tolerant, and thrives in heat — full sun and excellent drainage are its only requirements. In zone 9a it is widely grown in containers: bring indoors dry and leafless when temperatures drop below 40°F, store in a frost-free location, and return outdoors in spring. Hundreds of named cultivars are prized by collectors; the Singapore White (P. obtusa) is an evergreen species suited to zone 10+.

plant_note: Plant container shrubs in spring in moist, acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; keep crown at or above soil level. Do not allow to dry out during establishment.
directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), well-drained soil in partial to full shade; plumleaf azalea holds a unique distinction among native deciduous azaleas — it blooms in July and August, later than every other native azalea species, and is therefore in bloom when every other azalea in the garden has long finished. The large, brilliant orange-red (rarely yellow) flowers appear on the shrub in midsummer, providing color and hummingbird nectar in a season when the woodland garden often lacks bloom. This is one of the rarest native azaleas in the wild, found only in southwestern Georgia and eastern Alabama, but it is a superb and underused garden plant. It tolerates the heat and partial shade of zone 8–9 summers better than most native azaleas. Mulch heavily with pine bark to maintain soil moisture and acidity.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring; cover seed as darkness aids germination.
directions: Direct sow in early spring, covering the seed since darkness aids germination and plants tolerate light frost. This dwarf mix averages sixteen to twenty-four inches and blooms in blue, purple, pink, red, and white, making it great for containers. Drought tolerant once established, it flowers from spring into summer and attracts pollinators. Deadhead to extend bloom and let some flowers set seed to self-sow.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 14–16 weeks before last frost after surface-sowing and cold-stratifying at 35–40°F for 3–4 weeks.
directions: Surface-sow tiny seeds on moist, sterile mix and cold-stratify at 35–40°F for 3–4 weeks to break dormancy, then move to 65°F for germination. Grow on at cool temperatures (55–60°F) since heat causes etiolation; transplant into partial shade in humus-rich, moist, well-drained soil in early spring. Primroses bloom best in cool, moist conditions — they are outstanding spring plants but typically go dormant in summer heat. Divide crowded clumps after bloom in spring and replant divisions in refreshed, compost-amended soil.

plant_note: Plant tubers after frost; excellent long-lasting cut flower.
directions: Plant pompon dahlia tubers after the last frost about four to six inches deep in rich, well-drained soil. The small, perfectly spherical, tightly quilled flowers are outstanding long-lasting cut flowers. Grow in full sun, stake the stems, and pinch young plants to encourage more blooms. Deadhead regularly and, in cold zones, dig and store the tubers over winter after the first frost blackens the foliage.

plant_note: Plant container specimens in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; pond cypress is more tolerant of still, stagnant water and poor, sandy flatwoods soils than bald cypress and is the better choice for small water gardens, rain gardens with standing water, and sandy coastal plain sites.
directions: Plant in moist to wet, slightly acidic, sandy to loamy soil in full sun — pond cypress is the smaller, more columnar companion to bald cypress (Taxodium distichum), native to the shallow, still, slightly acidic waters of coastal plain ponds, sloughs, bayheads, and flatwoods pools of the eastern Gulf Coast from Virginia to Louisiana. It is more columnar and narrower in crown than bald cypress — an important distinction for smaller water gardens and landscape plantings where bald cypress would eventually become too wide. Like bald cypress, it is deciduous, producing soft, feathery, gray-green needles that turn brilliant orange-brown in fall before dropping, and develops picturesque 'knees' (pneumatophores) in wet soils. It tolerates standing water throughout the growing season and acidic, nutrient-poor, sandy soils that defeat other trees. The ideal tree for small wet garden features, bioswales, and moist low areas requiring a deciduous, fine-textured, low-maintenance large tree.

plant_note: Direct sow fresh seeds in fall in moist to wet, acidic, sandy or peaty soil in full sun; the serotinous cones require heat (fire or prolonged heating) to release seeds naturally, so for cultivation purposes collect cones and gently heat them in an oven (250°F for 30 minutes) to open. Pond Pine establishes most reliably in its natural wet, acidic habitat.
directions: Pond Pine is an ecologically fascinating native pine, the dominant tree of the pocosins (shrubby Carolina bogs) and wet pine flatwoods of the southeastern coastal plain from New Jersey to northern Florida, where it grows in the most permanently saturated, acidic, peaty soils of the coastal plain in full sun — a habitat as harsh as any in the temperate world. Its cones are strongly serotinous — sealed shut with resin — and open most reliably after fire, giving Pond Pine a remarkable fire-dependent life history: the dense, wet habitat rarely burns, but when lightning does ignite a pocosin, the heat opens the cones and the charred, open landscape gives the fire-adapted seedlings their best chance for establishment. Pond Pine sprouts vigorously from the root crown after fire, regenerating rapidly even when top-killed. In natural landscape and restoration plantings, Pond Pine is appropriate for the wet, boggy, acidic conditions of pocosins and seepage bogs in zones 6–9, where its tolerance for waterlogged soils matches or exceeds that of any other eastern pine species.

plant_note: Direct sow on the soil surface after last frost when soil is warm; seed needs warmth and light to germinate.
directions: Scatter seed on the surface of sandy or gritty soil and press lightly — do not cover seed, as light is needed for germination. Portulaca is one of the most drought- and heat-tolerant annuals available, thriving in poor, dry soil where other flowers fail. Blooms open in full sun and close at night or in cloudy weather; modern varieties stay open longer. Self-sows readily in warm climates.

plant_note: Root stem cuttings easily in moist potting mix or transplant container specimens in spring; possum grape establishes rapidly in warm weather and is virtually indestructible once established — it tolerates extreme drought, deep shade, and root competition from large trees that kill most other vines.
directions: Root cuttings or plant in virtually any soil type — from dry, rocky, alkaline limestone to moist, sandy loam — in full shade to full sun — possum grape is one of the toughest native vines of the Gulf Coast and South Texas, a member of the grape family with succulent, deeply lobed, somewhat grape-like leaves on twining stems. The succulent stems store water, giving this vine exceptional drought tolerance even under large trees where root competition is intense and soil is bone dry. Small clusters of inconspicuous flowers in summer are followed by small, dark purple to black berries that are eagerly consumed by mockingbirds, cedar waxwings, and other fruit-eating birds. It tolerates the deep shade under live oaks, cedar elms, and other large trees better than almost any other vine and is useful as a tough groundcover or fence cover for difficult, dry, shaded exposures. Deer avoid it. Deciduous in zone 7–8; semi-evergreen in zone 9–10.

plant_note: Plant at least one male and three to five female shrubs for berry production; match male and female cultivar bloom times. The male plant 'Red Escort' is widely used with female cultivars of this species.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil in full sun to partial shade; possum haw is a native deciduous holly of the Southeast naturally found in stream margins, swamp edges, and moist bottomland forests, valued for the brilliant orange-red berries that adorn bare branches all winter long after leaves drop in fall. It is an excellent alternative to winterberry (I. verticillata) for zone 8–9 gardens where winterberry can struggle with heat. Like all hollies, it is dioecious — both male and female plants are required for berry set, in a ratio of at least one male per five females. Cedar waxwings, robins, bluebirds, and mockingbirds rely on the persistent berries throughout winter. Named female cultivars like 'Warren's Red' and 'Fineline' are widely available.

plant_note: Plant young, container-grown trees in fall in dry to average, sandy, acidic, well-drained soil in full sun; post oak has a deep taproot and is extremely difficult to transplant from the wild — only use nursery-grown stock and plant when small for best survival.
directions: Post oak is one of the most drought-resistant and ecologically critical native oaks of the southeastern United States, forming a distinctive cross- or plus-shaped leaf outline (the 'stellata' — starry — refers to the star-shaped hairs on the leaf underside) that makes it immediately identifiable among the dozens of native oaks. Found on dry, sandy, or rocky ridges and uplands across its enormous range from New England to Texas, it is the characteristic oak of the 'post oak savanna' ecoregion of east Texas and the Cross Timbers region — a unique fire-maintained landscape of widely spaced oaks over a diverse grass and wildflower understory. The hard, rot-resistant wood was historically used for fence posts and barn sills (giving the tree its common name), and post oak acorns are among the most important mast crops for white-tailed deer, wild turkey, and dozens of bird species across the Southeast. Post oak supports over 500 species of Lepidoptera (moths and butterflies) as a larval host — making it one of the top native trees for butterfly and moth diversity in the region.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions in late spring after all frost danger has passed, in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil at the margins of ponds, streams, or water gardens; Thalia dealbata tolerates water up to 12 inches deep over the crown and thrives in the wet, low-oxygen soils of bayous and marsh edges.
directions: Powdery Thalia is a bold, tropical-looking native marginal aquatic plant, native to the shallow waters, bayous, and marshy pond margins of the southeastern United States from South Carolina to Florida and west to Texas, producing large, blue-green, powdery (glaucous) leaves reminiscent of a giant canna on stems reaching 4–6 feet and small violet-purple flowers on tall, branched spikes in summer. It is one of the most architecturally impressive native aquatic plants for water gardens, rain gardens, and naturalized pond edges, providing year-round coarse texture in the shallow water zone and offering excellent cover and nesting habitat for ducks and wading birds. In zones 6–7 the crown may die to the waterline in severe winters but re-sprouts reliably from the roots in spring; in zones 8–11 it is evergreen or semi-evergreen. Plant rhizome divisions in still or slow-moving water at pond margins, in containers submerged 2–4 inches below the water surface, or in saturated soil at the water's edge.

plant_note: Direct sow scarified seeds in spring in dry to average, well-drained soil in full sun; prairie acacia regrows vigorously from deep roots after frost and fire — site where seasonal die-back is acceptable, as it goes completely dormant in cold winters.
directions: Prairie acacia is a native thornless (or nearly thornless) shrub of open prairies, rocky hillsides, and dry woodlands from Texas and Oklahoma to Arizona and south into Central America — one of the most ornamentally appealing of all native acacias due to its feathery, twice-pinnate leaves and profuse, creamy-white fluffy flower balls that emerge throughout summer and fall. As a legume, it fixes atmospheric nitrogen and builds soil organic matter through prolific leaf fall — traditionally it was valued as a fodder and soil improvement plant in tropical dry regions as well as for wildlife cover. The delicate, fernlike foliage is grazed by white-tailed deer and provides larval host resources for several butterfly species; the abundant seed pods attract quail, bobwhite, and songbirds in fall. In Gulf Coast gardens and south Texas habitat restorations, it is one of the more reliable, low-maintenance native shrubs for full sun in poor, dry soil — it dies back to the ground in cold winters in zones 7–8 but regrows vigorously from deep roots each spring.

plant_note: Surface sow seed in fall (needs light to germinate; do not cover seed) in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to poor soil in full sun to partial shade; prairie alumroot is more drought-tolerant and sun-tolerant than the woodland alumroot species and thrives in the open prairie garden.
directions: Surface sow seed in fall (light required for germination) in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to dry soil in full sun to partial shade — prairie alumroot is the native Heuchera of midwestern prairies and prairie openings, producing airy wands of tiny greenish-white flowers on slender stems above mounds of rounded, shallowly lobed, evergreen to semi-evergreen leaves. While the garden coral bells (H. sanguinea) and its many modern hybrids are more familiar in cultivation, prairie alumroot is a tough, drought-tolerant native species offering excellent ecological value — it is an important nectar source for hummingbirds and long-tongued native bees and is the host plant for specialist Heuchera-feeding insects. More drought-tolerant and sun-hardy than the woodland-origin Heuchera species, making it better suited to drier prairie-garden settings. Evergreen rosettes add winter interest.

plant_note: Direct sow seed in fall in moist to well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to moist, deep prairie soil in full sun; prairie blazing star is the tallest of the common blazing stars and requires a site with good moisture in summer to achieve its full 4–5 foot flowering height.
directions: Sow seed in fall in moist to well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, deep prairie soil in full sun — prairie blazing star is the most dramatic of the native liatris species, producing the tallest and most densely packed flower spikes — 3–5 feet tall — of dense magenta-rose florets that open from the top of the spike downward (a reversal of the usual bottom-to-top progression) in midsummer. Like all liatris species, the flowers are intensely attractive to monarch butterflies (which use them heavily during fall migration), native bumblebees, and a wide array of specialist prairie bees. It grows naturally in the wet to mesic tallgrass prairies of the Midwest, requiring better soil moisture in summer than the drier-site liatris species. The corms (thickened stem bases) survive prairie fires, drought, and cold with equal ease. An outstanding focal-point perennial for prairie gardens and naturalistic plantings where maximum height and drama are needed.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification; alternatively cold-stratify seed at 35–40°F for 60 days before spring sowing. Plant corms 2–3 inches deep in well-drained soil in full sun.
directions: Plant corms 2–3 inches deep or direct sow in well-drained, average to dry soil in full sun; prairie blazingstar is one of the tallest blazingstars, producing dense, 2–3 foot spikes of bright rose-purple flowers from July through August that open from the top of the spike downward — the reverse of most flowering spikes. It is one of the premier late-summer nectar plants for monarch butterflies preparing for fall migration, as well as a magnet for native bees and swallowtails. The grass-like, basal foliage is unobtrusive early in the season; plants emerge from small corms that multiply slowly over the years. Excellent cut flower. Tolerates clay soil better than many prairie plants and adapts well to average garden conditions.

plant_note: Direct sow on dry to average, well-drained soil in fall or early spring; prairie coneflower prefers poor, lean soils — excessive fertility leads to floppy stems and reduced flowering; self-seeds freely to form naturalizing colonies in dry prairies and meadows.
directions: Prairie coneflower (Mexican hat) is one of the most distinctive and drought-tolerant of all native prairie wildflowers — the flowers have a tall, thimble-shaped, elongated central cone surrounded by reflexed, drooping golden to red-brown ray petals that create the unmistakable 'Mexican sombrero' silhouette that inspired its popular name. Native to prairies, roadsides, and dry grasslands from Canada to Mexico, it is among the toughest of all native wildflowers in the heat and drought of the southern plains, remaining reliably perennial in zones 3–10 with essentially no supplemental water once established in lean, well-drained soil. The aromatic foliage has a distinctive camphor-like smell when crushed and is rarely browsed by deer; the long, wiry stems sway gracefully in wind, earning it a place in naturalistic meadow plantings throughout the Great Plains and Gulf Coast. Grows well mixed with black-eyed Susans, blue wild indigo, and native grasses in drought-tolerant native meadows and highway wildflower plantings.

plant_note: Cold-stratify seeds for 60–90 days then sow in spring, or direct sow in fall for natural stratification; first bloom occurs in the second or third year.
directions: Sow cold-stratified seed or plant transplants in deep, well-drained, preferably rocky or clay loam soil in full sun; this long-lived prairie plant grows a massive, 6-foot-deep taproot that anchors it against drought and enables it to survive prairie fire. The basal rosette of enormous, rough-textured heart-shaped leaves — up to 18 inches across — is distinctive all season, while the wiry branched stems carry yellow composite flowers to 8 feet in mid- to late summer. It is extremely slow to establish, requiring 3–4 years to first bloom, but once rooted it is virtually indestructible and may live for decades. Do not transplant established plants as the taproot cannot be severed without killing the plant.

plant_note: Direct sow after last frost; very slow to establish from seed — divisions or plugs reach ornamental size much faster.
directions: Sow seeds in full sun in lean, well-drained, sandy or rocky soil after last frost; prairie dropseed is a slow-growing native that takes 2–3 years from seed to develop into an ornamental clump. The extremely fine-textured, emerald-green foliage forms a perfect arching fountain shape, and open, airy flower panicles in late summer emit a distinctive fragrance of warm popcorn or cilantro. Fall color is a beautiful tawny orange-gold and the foliage persists attractively through winter. Once established it is extremely drought- and heat-tolerant and virtually maintenance-free.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, moist to moderately dry, loamy to sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; prairie gentian grows naturally in wet prairies, roadsides, and disturbed areas in the Gulf Coast region and self-sows readily in open, sunny, slightly alkaline conditions.
directions: Direct sow in spring in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, moderately moist to dry soil in full sun — prairie gentian is the wild ancestor of the popular florist lisianthus, a Gulf Coast native annual of wet prairies and roadsides from Texas to Florida that produces elegant, upward-facing, funnel-shaped flowers in clear blue-purple with a velvety texture and a prominent yellow and white center — one of the most beautiful wildflower blooms on the Gulf Coast prairies. The florist lisianthus (E. grandiflorum) is a cultivated form of this species bred for larger, showier, often double flowers in expanded color ranges. The native species is shorter, tougher, and self-sows freely in Gulf Coast gardens, returning annually in open, sunny, slightly alkaline areas. The silvery-blue to blue-gray, glaucous, clasping leaves are attractive even out of bloom. A lovely wildflower for naturalistic prairie plantings, roadsides, and open, sunny garden borders in the Gulf Coast region.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring after cold stratification for 30 days, or sow in fall for natural stratification; plants may rebloom in fall in cool climates.
directions: Direct sow cold-stratified seed in early spring in lean, well-drained, sandy to gravelly soil in full sun; prairie phlox is more drought tolerant and heat tolerant than the commonly planted P. divaricata and grows in dry prairie soils and open woodland edges across the eastern US. The fragrant pink to lavender flower clusters bloom in late spring and attract swallowtail butterflies and native bee species. The downy (pilosa) stems give the plant a soft texture throughout the season. Shear back by one-half after bloom to prevent disease and encourage fresh growth; plants in rich soil or shade are more susceptible to powdery mildew. Excellent combined with native grasses in a dry meadow or prairie planting.

plant_note: Plant bare-root or container shrubs in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to poor soil in full sun to partial shade; prairie rose is the only native climbing rose in the Midwest and East and is remarkably adaptable to difficult sites.
directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to poor soil in full sun to partial shade; prairie rose is the only native climbing rose of the Midwest and Great Plains, producing clusters of beautiful deep pink, single, 2-inch flowers in early to midsummer on long arching canes that trail or climb through shrubs and fences. The flowers attract native bees including leafcutter bees and mason bees; the red hips persist into winter providing food for mockingbirds, cedar waxwings, and other fruit-eating birds. Unlike most garden roses, prairie rose requires no spraying, no deadheading, and no special care — once established it is remarkably drought-tolerant and adaptable. It blooms only once in summer (it is not repeat-blooming) but the display is spectacular. The 3-leaflet leaves give it a distinctive character among roses. An excellent native rose for naturalistic gardens, fence lines, and erosion control on slopes.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification; or start indoors in late winter with 8–10 weeks of cold moist stratification.
directions: Sow seeds in fall and allow winter cold to naturally stratify them, or cold-stratify indoors for 8–10 weeks before spring sowing; plant in lean, well-drained to dry soil in full sun. The nodding, burgundy-pink flowers in mid-spring are followed by the spectacular feathery, smoke-like seed plumes that give the plant its name and provide ornamental interest for weeks. Prairie smoke is a rugged, drought-tolerant native wildflower that thrives in rocky or sandy soils where more demanding plants struggle. It spreads slowly into a weed-suppressing groundcover in open, sunny areas.

plant_note: Direct sow seed in fall in moist to well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to dry soil in full sun to partial shade; prairie spiderwort self-sows readily and naturalizes in prairies and open areas. The three violet-blue petals have a distinctive triangle formation typical of all Tradescantia species.
directions: Sow seed in fall in moist to well-drained, average to dry soil in full sun to partial shade — prairie spiderwort is a Great Plains native Tradescantia closely related to the more widely grown Ohio spiderwort (T. ohiensis), producing the same distinctive three-petaled violet-blue to pink-purple flowers whose delicate petals last only a single morning before shriveling, but with new buds opening daily over many weeks in spring and early summer. Like all spiderworts, the clear blue-violet flowers are striking when massed, creating a wave of cool color in the spring garden. Prairie spiderwort is generally more compact and drought-tolerant than Ohio spiderwort, reflecting its origin on drier Great Plains prairies. Foliage goes dormant and yellows in midsummer heat — plant with later-emerging perennials to fill in. An excellent native for dry, sunny to partly shaded prairie gardens and informal settings in zones 3–9.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost danger passes.
directions: Sow seed directly after the last frost, one-half inch deep in warm, well-drained soil. This branching native annual produces many small yellow flowers with reddish-brown centers on graceful stems. It is highly drought-tolerant and thrives on the lean soils of open meadows and restoration plantings. Bees feed on the blooms while finches and other birds harvest the ripe seed in fall.

plant_note: Direct sow seed in fall in well-drained, sandy to loamy, neutral to alkaline, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun to partial shade; prairie verbena requires excellent drainage and tolerates no standing water. Self-sows readily to naturalize across open, dry areas.
directions: Direct sow or transplant into very well-drained, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun — prairie verbena is a sprawling, finely cut-leaved native groundcover of the southern Great Plains and Gulf Coast prairies, producing clusters of bright rose-pink to lavender-pink flowers nearly year-round in warm climates from fall through spring and sporadically through summer. It is one of the most important native nectar plants for low-growing prairie butterflies including the Phaon crescent, bordered patch, and sulfur species. The silvery-green, finely dissected foliage forms an attractive, trailing mat that roots where stems contact soil, spreading to cover rocky slopes, dry banks, and open gravel. It thrives in the alkaline, caliche-heavy, or sandy soils of the southern Gulf Coast that defeat many garden plants. Essentially no supplemental irrigation is needed in adapted soils; good drainage is critical — the plant will not survive in wet or clay soils.

plant_note: Plant divisions in moist, peat-based, slightly acidic (pH 5.5–6.5) soil in bright indirect shade; maranta requires consistent moisture, high humidity, and warmth above 60°F. Avoid hard water — use rainwater or allow tap water to sit 24 hours before using to allow chlorine to off-gas.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, peat-based soil in bright indirect shade; the prayer plant is named for its remarkable circadian leaf movement — the broad, oval leaves with their intricate dark green and silver herringbone pattern are flat during the day but fold upward in the evening in a prayer-like posture, then return to horizontal at dawn. This nyctinasty (night-time leaf folding) is especially dramatic in the variety 'Erythroneura,' which has brilliant red veins against velvety dark green with light green feathering — one of the most beautiful leaf patterns of any plant. As an outdoor shade plant in zone 10–12, maranta spreads as a low groundcover under trees and in shaded planting beds. Indoors it thrives in any room with bright indirect light. Keep soil consistently moist and mist frequently or place on a pebble tray with water to maintain humidity.

common_name: Pride of Barbados (Red Bird of Paradise)
plant_note: Direct sow scarified or nicked seed 1 inch deep after the last frost, or start indoors 6–8 weeks earlier; pride of barbados germinates rapidly after scarification in warm soil. The plant dies back to the ground after hard freezes in zone 8 but regrows vigorously from established roots and can bloom within the first season from seed.
directions: Plant in full sun in any well-drained soil — sandy, rocky, and even alkaline soils are acceptable; pride of barbados is one of the most spectacular flowering tropical shrubs for hot, dry, difficult sites, producing large showy clusters of exotic-looking flowers in brilliant shades of red-orange with long protruding red stamens (resembling a bird of paradise arrangement) over finely pinnate, lacy green foliage from summer through frost. It is exceptionally heat, drought, and poor-soil tolerant and blooms most prolifically in the hottest part of the summer when many other plants flag. The entire shrub is typically killed to the ground by hard freezes in zone 8 but regrows robustly each spring, often re-blooming within 60 days. In zone 9–12 it is woody and persistent, reaching shrub-tree size. Seeds germinate quickly after the hard seed coat is nicked or soaked. All parts are toxic if ingested. An outstanding butterfly and hummingbird plant for xeric, full-sun gardens.

plant_note: Plant rooted cuttings or container plants in spring after frost danger passes in moist, well-drained, acidic to slightly acidic (pH 5.5–6.5), humus-rich soil in full sun to partial afternoon shade; tibouchina requires consistently moist soil and an acidic reaction to thrive. Chlorosis (yellow leaves with green veins) indicates iron deficiency from overly alkaline soil.
directions: Plant in moist, fertile, acidic, well-drained soil in full sun to partial afternoon shade; princess flower is one of the most eye-catching flowering shrubs available for zone 9–11 gardens, producing large (3–4 inch), intensely saturated violet-purple flowers with prominent red stamens almost continuously from spring through frost. The soft, velvety leaves with prominent veining provide beautiful texture between bloom cycles, and new leaves often have a reddish tinge. Prune back by one-third in early spring to keep the plant compact and encourage maximum branching and flower production — unpruned plants become leggy. Maintain acidic soil pH (5.5–6.5) for best performance; apply chelated iron if leaves yellow with green veins. In zone 9a it may suffer frost damage in hard winters but often regrows from the base. Tibouchina is outstanding as a specimen plant, in large containers, or as an informal hedge where its brilliant purple flowers create a stunning focal point.

plant_note: Start indoors 10 weeks early; slow to establish.
directions: Start purple bell vine indoors ten weeks before the last frost, surface sowing the fine seed and providing warmth. This delicate climber produces dangling flowers with rosy-pink parasol-shaped calyces and dark tubular petals. Grow on a light trellis or in hanging baskets in full sun to partial shade with rich, moist soil. It is grown as a tender annual in most climates and blooms until frost.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost or direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification.
directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep; cold stratification for 4–8 weeks improves germination rates significantly. Transplant after last frost into well-drained soil — established plants tolerate drought well. Flowers typically appear in year 2 from seed; deadhead spent blooms to extend the season or leave seedheads for birds. Divide clumps every 3–4 years in spring to maintain vigor.

plant_note: Cold-moist stratify for 30 days to improve germination; often blooms the second year from seed.
directions: Give seed a month of cold-moist stratification, then sow in spring for the strongest stands. This hardy native perennial bears purple petals around a raised, coppery cone from summer into fall. Plant in full sun and well-drained soil where it will tolerate heat and drought once established. Leave the seed heads standing over winter to feed songbirds and encourage self-sowing.

plant_note: Direct sow after soil warms, or cold stratify seed for 4 weeks to improve germination.
directions: This native heirloom is a long-lived perennial that blooms for a month or more from midsummer, with pinkish-purple petals surrounding a large dark cone. Sow seed in spring after the last frost, or cold stratify beforehand to break dormancy. Space plants a foot or more apart in well-drained soil and full sun to part shade. Once established it is drought tolerant and self-sows. Leave spent cones standing to feed goldfinches through fall and winter.

plant_note: Direct sow after danger of frost has passed; nasturtiums resent transplanting.
directions: This trailing variety carries deeply veined petals in shades of purple fading to lavender and dusty rose, making it ideal for hanging baskets or as a flowering ground cover. Sow the large seeds directly where they are to grow once the soil has warmed, planting about half an inch deep. Avoid rich soil and heavy feeding, which produces leaves at the expense of blooms. It is fairly drought tolerant once established. Both the peppery blossoms and leaves are edible and add a colorful kick to salads.

plant_note: Plant container-grown transplants after last frost; this cultivar does not come true from seed and is propagated by division.
directions: Plant after last frost in full sun and very well-drained soil; purple fountain grass is grown as an annual north of zone 9 for its arching burgundy-red foliage and showy, rose-tinged plumes from midsummer through frost. It thrives in heat and drought once established but requires consistent moisture the first few weeks after transplanting. In frost-free climates it is a perennial that can become invasive — avoid planting near natural areas and remove flower heads before seeds ripen. Bring container-grown specimens indoors before frost to overwinter as a houseplant in colder zones.

common_name: Purple Heart (Purple Queen, Purple Wandering Jew)
plant_note: Root 3–4 inch stem cuttings in water or moist mix in spring; purple heart roots easily and quickly. Plant in any well-drained soil from full sun to partial shade. Full sun intensifies the deep purple color; shade causes the stems to elongate and the color to fade to green-purple. In zone 7–8 tops die back in frost; roots survive to zone 7.
directions: Root cuttings in water and plant in any well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; purple heart is one of the most valuable and widely grown groundcovers for Gulf Coast gardens, producing thick, spreading mats of deep, intensely violet-purple succulent stems and strap-like leaves with a metallic sheen. The color is among the most saturated purple of any plant — a striking design element in the landscape. It produces small pink flowers in summer. Purple heart is nearly indestructible — tolerating drought, heat, salt spray, poor soil, and reflected heat from pavement. In zone 7–11 it is perennial (tops die back in freezes but resprout); it can also be overwintered indoors as a container plant or trailing houseplant. An outstanding container plant for providing dramatic color contrast with silver, chartreuse, or orange-flowering companions. Deer resistant.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring on dry, sandy, well-drained, infertile soil in full sun; purple lovegrass self-seeds freely and forms naturalized colonies in open, dry areas — it establishes quickly and is one of the fastest native grasses to produce ornamental value from seed.
directions: Purple lovegrass is one of the most spectacularly ornamental native grasses of eastern North America — in late summer and fall, the compact, low tufts produce enormous, airy, branching panicles of tiny pink-purple to reddish-purple spikelets that stand well above the foliage and glow with iridescent color in slanted afternoon light, catching the eye from considerable distance in the garden and roadside landscape. The loose, tumbling seed heads eventually break off and roll across fields like tumbleweed, scattering seeds widely — in dry, sandy soils the grass self-seeds prolifically and can form beautiful naturalized meadows without any maintenance. It is one of the lowest-maintenance, highest-reward native grasses available for dry, sunny sites in zones 4–9: remarkably drought and heat tolerant, requiring absolutely no supplemental irrigation or fertilization once established, and producing its spectacular fall display with zero input. Use it in masses on dry slopes, sandy roadsides, and xeric garden borders where its cloud-like purple seed heads create a fall and winter spectacle.

plant_note: Surface sow seed 4–6 weeks before last frost in moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; seeds need light to germinate. Can also sow in fall for natural stratification. Plants are dioecious — both male and female plants produce flowers, but only females set seed.
directions: Surface sow seed in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; purple meadow rue is one of the tallest and most architecturally striking native shade perennials, producing airy sprays of lavender-purple to white flowers on dark purple stems in late spring to early summer that rise above bold, blue-green, aquilegia-like foliage. It grows naturally in moist prairies, stream banks, and open woodland edges across the Midwest and Great Plains, tolerating light shade well. The flowers are pollinated by wind and native bees. The plant provides excellent vertical structure in the middle-to-back of a woodland edge border and combines beautifully with native ferns and woodland wildflowers. It spreads slowly by rhizome and self-seeding to naturalize in favorable conditions.

plant_note: Scarify and cold-stratify seed; inoculating with legume rhizobia aids nitrogen fixation.
directions: Lightly scarify and cold-stratify the seed, then sow into a firm, weed-free seedbed in full sun. This native legume fixes nitrogen and produces cone-shaped clusters of vivid purple flowers in early to mid-summer. Its deep taproot makes it very drought-tolerant and slow to establish, so be patient in the first year. It is a superb choice for prairie restoration, pollinator plantings, and improving lean soils.

plant_note: Plant small rhizomes (bulblets) 1 inch deep in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade; purple shamrock grows from small, scaly rhizomes that can be left in the ground year-round in zone 7–10. The plant goes summer-dormant in intense heat and re-emerges in fall.
directions: Plant small rhizomes 1 inch deep in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade; purple shamrock is one of the most charming and versatile small shade plants, producing triangular, folded leaves in deep burgundy-purple with three leaflets that look exactly like large, richly colored shamrocks. The leaves fold downward at night and in strong sun (nyctinasty), 'sleeping' closed before reopening each morning. Small pink flowers are produced on delicate stems above the foliage from spring through fall. It is outstanding in containers, window boxes, and as a low border edging in partial shade. The leaves and flowers are edible with a pleasant sorrel-like tartness (high in oxalic acid — consume only in small amounts). In zone 7–10 the rhizomes overwinter in the ground; the plant may go partly dormant in hot summers and resprout in fall with cooler temperatures.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring; soak seed overnight to speed germination.
directions: This unusual heirloom prized since 16th-century England bears nodding, tear-shaped purple bracts on blue-green foliage that pairs beautifully with bronze and deep-purple plants. Soak seed overnight, then sow directly into the garden in early spring for the strongest plants. It thrives in heat and makes a long-lasting cut flower. Give it well-drained soil and full sun. Plants often self-sow to return the following year.

plant_note: Plant rooted cuttings in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; purple wreath prefers bright, hot sun and blooms most prolifically when slightly drought stressed between rain events. In zone 9b–10 it may survive mild winters; established plants freeze-killed in zone 9a often resprout from roots.
directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun; purple wreath is one of the most spectacular tropical flowering vines, producing cascading clusters 12–18 inches long of small, star-shaped violet-purple flowers with contrasting deeper purple calyces that remain decorative for weeks after the petals fall — the persistent purple calyces give the vine a second ornamental period. The rough-textured, sandpaper-like leaves give rise to the alternate name 'sandpaper vine.' Established plants bloom in a spectacular spring flush with intermittent repeat bloom through summer and fall. The woody vine can reach 20–40 feet and requires a large, sturdy support. It is drought tolerant once established and blooms most prolifically with minimal irrigation. A stunning specimen for zone 10–12 landscapes; in zone 9b it can survive in sheltered south-facing locations.

plant_note: Plant nursery transplants or root hardwood cuttings in spring in wet to moist, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade. Pussy Willow catkins can also be cut and displayed indoors in water, where they will root and can be potted or planted out; cut branches in late winter while catkins are still tightly in bud.
directions: Pussy Willow is the beloved harbinger of spring throughout eastern North America, a large native shrub or small tree native to wet meadows, swamp margins, streambanks, and moist thickets from Newfoundland to British Columbia and south to the Appalachians and Pacific Northwest, producing in February and March the large, plump, silvery-gray male catkins that are one of the most universally recognized signals that winter is ending. Male plants produce the showiest catkins (female catkins are smaller and less attractive); catkin branches cut in late winter and placed in water indoors immediately become a beloved floral display that reliably roots — the resulting rooted cuttings can be potted or planted directly in the garden. An important early pollinator plant, providing pollen to native bees, honey bees, and other emerging insects before virtually any other tree flowers; willows in general are one of the most important native pollinator plants in North America. Plant in wet to moist, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade near pond margins, rain gardens, or wet depressions.

plant_note: Plant container-grown palms in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moist, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; pygmy date palm establishes slowly and resents root disturbance — handle the root ball carefully during planting. Apply palm-formulated slow-release fertilizer in spring and summer to prevent micronutrient deficiencies.
directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; pygmy date palm is one of the most elegant and widely used small palms in subtropical and tropical landscaping worldwide, prized for its graceful, arching, feathery pinnate fronds on a slender, slightly rough-textured trunk that gives it the appearance of a miniature tropical date palm — exactly the feeling of the tropics in a compact size. Typically reaching 6–12 feet in the landscape over many years, it fits where larger palms do not: in courtyards, as potted specimens on patios, as accent plants beside entrances and pools, and in shaded positions under taller trees where its tolerance for reduced light is valuable. Multi-stem specimens (3 trunks planted together) create a particularly lush, tropical effect. Spines on the lower fronds and petioles can cause injury — wear long sleeves when working near established palms. Apply specialized palm fertilizer formulated for Florida and Gulf Coast soils to prevent manganese and potassium deficiencies.

plant_note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade. Pyramid Magnolia is one of the rarest and least-known native magnolias and should only be sourced from specialty native plant nurseries.
directions: Pyramid Magnolia is a rare native deciduous magnolia of the southeastern coastal plain, found in scattered populations in Georgia, Florida, Alabama, Mississippi, and Texas, growing in moist, rich, well-drained soil in the understory of mixed hardwood forest — a much more restricted habitat than the closely related Fraser Magnolia (Magnolia fraseri), which it resembles closely but with a more pyramidal form and consistently smaller stature. White, cup-shaped flowers 3–4 inches across appear in April and May before or with the emerging leaves; the large, auriculate (ear-lobed) leaves and attractive pink-red fruit clusters provide multi-season interest similar to its Appalachian relatives. Among the least-known of the native magnolias in horticulture despite being well-suited to zone 7–9 gardens, Pyramid Magnolia is increasingly available from specialty native nurseries as interest in deciduous native magnolias grows. Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, deeply amended soil in partial shade; protect from strong winds that damage the large leaves.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after all danger of frost has passed.
directions: This blend produces bright, papery globe-shaped blooms in warm sunrise tones that add bold color and unique texture to the summer garden. Start seed indoors about six to eight weeks before the last frost, as germination is slow and heat-loving. Transplant into full sun after the soil warms, where the plants tolerate heat and drought with ease. The more you cut, the more it blooms, making it excellent for fresh and dried arrangements. Pollinators are drawn to the long-lasting flowers all season.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring or fall; seed germinates best in cool soil and darkness.
directions: Pure white lacy umbels top plants reaching two feet or more, creating an airy, romantic filler for cut-flower bouquets. Sow seed directly in cool early-spring or fall soil and cover lightly, as it germinates best in darkness. Plants produce one strong flush of blooms beloved by pollinators and beneficial insects. Give them full sun to part shade and well-drained soil. Succession sow for a longer supply of the delicate white flowers.

plant_note: Direct sow after the last frost; safflower has a taproot and dislikes transplanting.
directions: This ornamental safflower opens creamy yellow and matures to deep saffron orange, giving evolving color and thistle-like texture through midsummer. Sow seed directly where plants are to grow, since the taproot resents disturbance. Sturdy upright stems make excellent fresh and dried cut flowers. Full sun and lean, well-drained soil suit it best, and it is quite drought tolerant. The edible petals can be used as a saffron substitute.

plant_note: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun; queen palm needs a slightly acidic soil (pH 5.5–6.5) to avoid manganese and potassium deficiencies that cause yellowing fronds. Apply palm-specific slow-release fertilizer 3 times per year.
directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic, moderately fertile soil in full sun; queen palm is one of the most popular and widely planted tropical palms in Florida, southern California, and the Gulf Coast, and for good reason — it grows rapidly (3–6 feet per year), has beautiful arching bright-green feather-compound fronds, and produces pendant clusters of small orange-yellow fruit beloved by wildlife. The smooth, light gray trunk has distinctive leaf scar rings that give it a clean, elegant appearance. It is not as cold-hardy as sabal or windmill palms — significant damage occurs below 25°F — making it marginal in zone 9a. Plant in a protected south-facing microclimate for best results. Queen palm is highly susceptible to Ganoderma root rot (avoid wounding roots and overcrowding) and may develop nutrient deficiencies in alkaline soils — regular palm fertilizer applications with micronutrients are essential.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions in early spring in consistently moist soil; this plant grows best with reliable moisture and will struggle without it.
directions: Plant in consistently moist to wet, rich, slightly acidic soil in full sun to light shade; queen of the prairie is a magnificent native for rain gardens, pond edges, and any low area with reliable summer moisture. The enormous clouds of fluffy, peach-pink flowers on tall stems in midsummer are among the most dramatic native perennial displays available and attract native bees in large numbers. The plant spreads by rhizome to form large colonies and is not suited to small gardens or dry sites. Divide every 4–5 years in spring; each division should include a generous section of rhizome with multiple crowns. Cut back in late fall after frost.

plant_note: Direct sow after all danger of frost; zinnias grow quickly in warm soil.
directions: This tall standout carries an extraordinary lime-and-orange color blend never before seen in a zinnia, with long-lasting blooms on sturdy stems. Sow directly into warm soil after frost, or start indoors a few weeks early in short-season areas. Pinch young plants to encourage branching and cut often to prolong bloom. Full sun and good air circulation help prevent powdery mildew. The flowers attract pollinators and make exceptional cut flowers all summer.

plant_note: Direct sow after all danger of frost; zinnias grow quickly in warm soil.
directions: Sturdy plants produce long-stemmed blossoms in a rich lime-and-red blend all summer, drawing in pollinators. Sow seed directly into warm soil after the last frost for fastest growth. Deadhead and cut regularly to keep the plants blooming into fall. Provide full sun and space plants for airflow to reduce mildew. The colorful, edible petals make a pretty garnish for salads and desserts.

plant_note: Start indoors and surface sow; seed needs light to germinate. Transplant after frost.
directions: Grown for its dazzling foliage in white, bronze, gold, copper, yellow, pink, red, and purple, this blend brightens shady spots where flowers struggle. Start seed indoors eight to ten weeks before the last frost, pressing it onto the soil surface since it needs light to sprout. Transplant after all danger of frost into partial shade and rich, moist soil. Pinch out flower spikes to keep the leaves vibrant and the plants bushy. It also makes an excellent houseplant to add color through winter.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring or start indoors; often blooms the first year from an early start.
directions: In late spring a blue-green mound of foliage is covered with heavily fringed, fragrant, edible blooms in white, pink, lilac, and rose that butterflies adore. Sow seed directly in the garden in spring, or start indoors early for first-year flowers. Give plants full sun to part shade and well-drained soil. Shear after the first flush to encourage a repeat bloom. The lacy flowers make charming, sweetly scented cut arrangements.

plant_note: Surface-sow indoors; seed needs light and warmth to germinate. Transplant after frost.
directions: Start seed indoors on the soil surface under bright light and warmth, as it needs light to sprout. Grown for its painted foliage rather than flowers, this mix shows a wide range of leaf colors and patterns. Move plants outdoors only after all frost danger passes, choosing a spot with partial shade and consistent moisture. Pinch growing tips regularly and remove flower spikes to keep foliage bushy and vivid; it can be overwintered indoors as a houseplant.

plant_note: Plant rooted cuttings in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Rangoon creeper is a vigorous vine requiring a sturdy trellis or arbor — established plants can cover a fence or shed in a season. In zone 8–9 tops die back in frost but established roots resprout in spring.
directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun on a sturdy trellis; Rangoon creeper is one of the most spectacular and fragrant of all tropical vines, producing pendant clusters of fragrant tubular flowers that open pure white, then change through pink to brilliant red over 3–4 days — the different-colored flowers at different stages create a stunning three-color display on the vine at all times during the long blooming season from late spring through fall. The sweet fragrance is strongest in the evening and carries widely on still air. In zone 8–9 established plants die to the ground in freezes but resprout vigorously in spring from the established root system. The vine can become very large in warm climates and may need annual pruning to keep manageable. Once established it is drought tolerant.
