The seeds are edible and have been used medicinally in Asia for centuries.

plant_note: In mild-winter climates (zones 8–10) plant corms in fall for late-winter/spring bloom; in cold climates start indoors and transplant after last frost.
directions: Soak corms in cool water for 4 hours before planting, then set them claw-side down about 2 in deep in well-drained, fertile soil. Ranunculus thrives in cool temperatures (45–65°F) and will decline when heat arrives, so timing is critical. In zones 9–10 plant October through January for blooms from February through May; provide afternoon shade in the warmest areas. After foliage yellows, allow corms to dry in situ or lift, cure, and store in a cool dry location for replanting.

plant_note: Direct sow fresh seed in fall for natural cold stratification; transplants poorly due to deep taproot.
directions: Sow fresh seed in fall directly in a sunny prairie or meadow garden in lean, well-drained soil; rattlesnake master develops a deep taproot and resents being moved after establishment. The dramatic, yucca-like basal rosette of spiny blue-green leaves is topped by tall branched stems bearing small, spiny, globe-shaped white flower heads in midsummer that are highly attractive to native bees. It is extremely drought-tolerant and actually performs better in poor, sandy or rocky soil than in rich garden beds. The sculptural seedheads provide winter interest and are prized for dried arrangements.

plant_note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade. All parts of Red Baneberry are poisonous — plant away from areas frequented by children and pets.
directions: Red Baneberry is a native woodland perennial closely related to White Baneberry (Actaea pachypoda) and similarly arresting in fruit — clusters of brilliant, glossy red berries on slender stems ripen in late summer and persist into fall, providing vivid color in the shade garden at a time when most woodland plants are fading. Native to moist, rich, deciduous and mixed forests throughout much of North America, from sea level to mountain slopes, Red Baneberry blooms in spring with fluffy white flower spikes similar to those of its cousin before producing its distinctive red (occasionally white) berries that are highly poisonous to humans but consumed by some birds without apparent harm. Grow in consistently moist, deeply amended, humus-rich soil in full to partial shade; the large, compound, deeply lobed leaves provide bold texture throughout the growing season. Keep away from children and pets — all parts of this plant are toxic if ingested.

plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs in early spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; fresh seeds planted immediately after collecting in fall also establish reliably. All parts of the plant are toxic — the seeds especially so.
directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; red buckeye is one of the finest native shrubs for the shaded garden, producing upright panicles of brilliant scarlet-red tubular flowers in April and May precisely timed to the return of ruby-throated hummingbirds from migration. The flowers are visited almost exclusively by hummingbirds and are among the most important early-season hummingbird plants available. It grows naturally as a forest understory shrub throughout the Southeast and performs exceptionally well in zone 8–9 Gulf Coast gardens in partial shade. The glossy, palmate compound leaves are attractive all season but the plant goes fully dormant in summer heat — mark its location and interplant with later-season companions. The large, mahogany-colored seeds are toxic to humans and livestock.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost, or start indoors 4-6 weeks early for earlier bloom.
directions: Each medium bloom showcases deep, sultry red petals around a vivid yellow center on compact, deer-resistant plants only six to ten inches tall. Sow directly after the last frost or start indoors a few weeks early for a quicker show. These marigolds thrive in full sun and average, well-drained soil. Deadhead regularly to keep them flowering until frost. Their compact size makes them ideal for edging beds, borders, and containers while drawing in pollinators.

plant_note: Surface-sow on prepared soil in early spring or fall; needs light and does not transplant well.
directions: Scatter the fine seed on the soil surface in early spring or fall, as it needs light and resents transplanting. This iconic Flanders poppy opens tissue-paper scarlet petals with dark centers in late spring and early summer. Grow it in full sun and well-drained soil where its deep taproot makes it drought-tolerant. Let flowers form seed pods and it will self-sow generously for years to come.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed in spring.
directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep and maintain even moisture at 65–75°F until germination. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil — good drainage is critical, especially in winter, as plants rot in waterlogged conditions. The torch-like flower spikes transition from red or orange in bud to yellow as the florets open, creating a two-toned effect that attracts hummingbirds throughout summer and fall. In zones 5–6 tie the leaves together and mulch deeply around the crown before the first hard freeze to protect the growing point.

plant_note: Plant nursery-grown propagules or container stock in spring in coastal, estuarine, or tidal wetland substrate in full sun; Red Mangrove is viviparous — the seed germinates on the parent tree, producing a cigar-shaped propagule that drops and anchors in the mud. Planting in protected tidal areas with shallow water and fine sediment gives the best establishment results. Never plant Red Mangrove outside of its natural range.
directions: Red Mangrove is one of the most ecologically important coastal trees in the subtropical and tropical Americas, forming the seaward fringe of mangrove forests along sheltered tidal coasts, estuaries, and coastal lagoons of Florida, the Gulf of Mexico, and the Caribbean, where its arching prop roots create a complex three-dimensional habitat supporting hundreds of species of fish, invertebrates, birds, and reptiles. The species is viviparous — the seeds germinate on the parent tree itself, producing cigar-shaped propagules up to 12 inches long that drop into the water and drift with currents before anchoring in tidal mudflats and developing new trees. Red Mangrove forests provide irreplaceable ecological services including coastal erosion control, storm surge buffering, carbon sequestration, and water quality filtration; removing them is illegal in Florida and most coastal states. Plant nursery-grown material only in appropriate tidal coastal zones in full sun in Zones 9–11; Red Mangrove is cold-sensitive and should not be planted north of its natural range.

plant_note: Plant fresh samaras in fall as soon as they ripen (they lose viability quickly) or plant container trees in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; red maple is one of the most adaptable native trees but performs best in moist, slightly acidic soil.
directions: Plant in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade — red maple is one of the most abundant and ecologically important trees in eastern North America, occurring naturally from Canada to Florida in an extraordinary range of conditions including swamps, dry ridges, and ordinary upland sites. It is also one of the first trees to bloom each spring, producing dense clusters of tiny red flowers in February–March before leaves emerge that provide critically important early-season pollen for mining bees emerging from overwintering. The brilliant red fall color — rivaling sugar maple in many individual trees — and the fast growth (2–3 feet per year) make it the most commonly planted shade tree in the eastern US. The samaras ripen in spring (unlike most maples which ripen in fall) and are quickly eaten by squirrels, grosbeaks, and finches. Tolerates flooding, clay, and a wide pH range.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost, or start indoors 4-6 weeks early for earlier bloom.
directions: This color-shifting marigold is deep burgundy in cool weather, then develops splashes of yellow as temperatures warm and reverts to solid burgundy when it cools again. Sow directly after the last frost or start indoors for earlier blooms. Full sun and well-drained soil produce the sturdiest, most colorful plants. Deadhead often to prolong flowering through the season. It makes a striking garden hedge and attracts pollinators.

plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring after frost danger passes in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; red powderpuff grows rapidly in warm, humid weather and can be blooming within the first season. In zone 8b, protect from hard freezes with frost cloth.
directions: Plant in well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; red powderpuff is one of the most dramatically beautiful flowering shrubs of the tropical and subtropical world, producing spectacular round clusters of deep crimson stamens that form feathery, silky balls 3–4 inches across — the individual flowers have no petals, only hundreds of brilliant red stamens that create the distinctive powderpuff effect. Blooms appear nearly year-round in frost-free areas and intermittently spring through fall in zone 9. The new foliage emerges in shades of copper and bronze before maturing to glossy dark green, providing an additional season of ornamental interest. An outstanding hummingbird and butterfly plant. Prune lightly after bloom cycles to maintain shape but avoid heavy pruning in fall; the plant blooms on old wood. A white-flowered species (Calliandra surinamensis, pink powderpuff) is also widely grown in Gulf Coast gardens.

plant_note: Plant tubers after all danger of frost has passed and soil has warmed.
directions: Plant Red Runner tubers 4-6 inches deep once soil warms, laying each tuber horizontally with the eye pointing up. This decorative variety produces dark romantic-red blooms on strong stems well suited for cutting. Pinch the growing tip at 12 inches to encourage bushier plants and more flowers. In zones colder than 8, lift and store the tubers in a cool, dry place over winter. Water deeply once sprouts emerge and avoid overwatering newly planted tubers.

plant_note: Plant bulbs 3–4 inches deep in late summer or fall; do not bury too deeply as shallow planting promotes better bloom. The bulbs have an unusual growth cycle — never remove foliage as it builds the bulb's energy reserve for next year's bloom.
directions: Plant bulbs 3–4 inches deep in well-drained, moist to average soil in partial shade to full sun; red spider lily (originally from China and Japan, long naturalized in the American South) is famous for its 'surprise' appearance — naked stalks push up abruptly from bare ground in September with no foliage, opening into clusters of brilliant crimson flowers with long arching stamens, then disappearing as quickly, followed weeks later by winter-to-spring foliage that dies back in summer. Plant along path edges, under deciduous trees, or in massing where the unusual bloom timing creates a dramatic seasonal event. Do not disturb established colonies — they bloom best when crowded. The bulbs are toxic; plant away from areas where children might dig. A beloved heirloom bulb of Southern gardens.

plant_note: Plant container-grown transplants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to alkaline, sandy to rocky, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun. Red Stopper is best suited to frost-free Zone 10–11 coastal south Florida and the Florida Keys. Berries should be sown fresh as seeds lose viability quickly.
directions: Red Stopper is a native evergreen shrub or small tree of the subtropical coastal hammocks of the Florida Keys and Miami-Dade County, as well as much of the Caribbean, producing small clusters of edible red to black berries that are sweet and aromatic, relished by white-crowned pigeons, mockingbirds, and other subtropical fruit-eating birds. The glossy, elliptical leaves emit a spicy fragrance when crushed, typical of the Eugenia family, and the small, white, brush-like flowers attract native bees and butterflies. A component of the critically imperiled South Florida coastal hardwood hammock community, Red Stopper is an attractive, slow-growing landscape shrub for Zone 10–11 coastal gardens, providing wildlife value, year-round evergreen foliage, seasonal flowers, and colorful edible berries. Plant in well-drained, rocky to sandy, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; established plants are drought-tolerant and salt-tolerant in coastal settings.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in moist, slightly acidic, deeply humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade; never dig trilliums from wild populations — purchase only nursery-propagated plants. The rhizome is easily damaged; plant with care at the same depth as grown.
directions: Plant nursery-grown rhizome divisions in fall in deeply humus-rich, moist, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade — red trillium (T. erectum) is one of the most widespread and variable of all trilliums, producing maroon to dark red (rarely white or pink) three-petaled flowers in mid-spring on erect stalks above a whorl of three broad, mottled leaves. The flower has a distinctive unpleasant odor (resembling carrion) that attracts blow flies and carrion beetles as pollinators. Despite the malodorous flowers, it is a beautiful and ecologically important native wildflower of eastern woodland, associated with rich, mesic forests from Canada to the Appalachians. The seeds are dispersed by ants (a process called myrmecochory) that carry the elaiosome-bearing seeds underground and leave them near established ant colonies. Like all trilliums, red trillium takes 7 years to flower from seed; purchase only nursery-grown, propagated plants — never collect from wild.

plant_note: Plant divisions or container-grown transplants in very well-drained, sandy, alkaline to neutral, lean soil in full sun; hesperaloe is extremely drought tolerant once established and grows naturally in the dry limestone hills of south Texas and northeastern Mexico. Never plant in wet or clay soil.
directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean soil in full sun; despite its common name 'red yucca,' hesperaloe is not a true yucca but a close relative in the Asparagaceae family native to south Texas and northeastern Mexico. It forms striking rosettes of long, narrow, grass-like leaves with thread-like fibers along the margins and produces tall, arching flower stalks 4–6 feet high covered in coral-red tubular flowers from spring through fall — an outstanding hummingbird plant that blooms continuously for months. It is extraordinarily drought tolerant, heat tolerant, deer resistant, and adaptable, thriving in pure gravel or caliche where most plants fail. The yellow form ('Yellow') is an attractive alternative. One of the best xeriscape plants for the Gulf South, equally at home in formal landscapes and naturalistic plantings. Almost no maintenance required after establishment.

plant_note: Attach small clumps to the rough bark of live oaks, southern magnolias, or other large trees by tying gently with jute twine; keep moist until established. Can also grow on old wooden fences or moss-covered rocks.
directions: Attach small clumps to the rough bark of live oaks, magnolias, cypress knees, or mossy rocks in partial to full shade; resurrection fern is a fascinating native epiphytic fern that grows without soil, anchoring itself to bark and extracting water and nutrients from rain and air. Its extraordinary survival ability allows it to lose up to 97% of its water content and appear completely dead and shriveled in drought, then revive to lush green within hours of rain — earning its dramatic common name. In its natural habitat the bark surface of mature live oaks is often entirely carpeted with this fern, and in rain events the transformation from brown crisp desiccation to vivid green is remarkable and occurs overnight. Establish by tying moist clumps to rough bark in spring; once attached it will spread slowly across the bark surface over time and requires no further care.

plant_note: Direct sow scarified seeds in warm soil in spring in any sunny, well-drained site; retama grows rapidly from seed and tolerates alkaline, poor, dry, or sandy soils — avoid heavy clay and standing water; freezes back in zone 8 but regrows from roots.
directions: Retama is one of the most spectacular native flowering trees of the Texas coastal region and Gulf South — its weeping, bright green photosynthetic stems and spine-tipped branches give it a distinctive architectural silhouette year-round, and in spring it erupts into cascading sprays of fragrant, bright yellow flowers with an orange-spotted petal that is so showy it has been called 'Jerusalem thorn' in the Anglo tradition and 'retama' in the Spanish South Texas culture. The photosynthetic green bark allows the tree to produce energy even during droughts when leaves (tiny, feathery leaflets on long, drooping rachises) may drop, enabling survival in extreme heat and drought that would kill most trees. Retama grows exceptionally fast — often 6–8 feet per year in good conditions — making it useful for rapid screening and shade creation in south Texas landscapes; it also serves as an important nectar plant for native bees, butterflies, and hummingbirds and provides nesting habitat for orioles and doves. The tree is armed with paired thorns and is not recommended near high-traffic areas.

plant_note: Plant in shallow, very well-drained, peat-based, slightly acidic (pH 5.5–6.5) soil in bright indirect shade; rex begonias have shallow root systems and perform best in wide, shallow containers rather than deep pots. They are sensitive to overwatering — the rhizome rots quickly in wet or poorly drained conditions.
directions: Plant in shallow, very well-drained, peat-based soil in bright indirect shade; rex begonias are grown almost exclusively for their spectacular foliage — the large, asymmetric leaves in patterns of silver, green, burgundy, red, pink, purple, copper, and bronze in bold swirling patterns are arguably the most ornamental of any leaf in the plant kingdom. Each cultivar has a unique, artistic pattern that looks almost hand-painted. They are outstanding container plants for shaded patios and covered porches where their foliage can be admired up close. As an outdoor shade garden plant in zone 10–12, they thrive under trees in similar conditions to calatheas and marantas. They prefer high humidity, bright indirect light, and shallow, moist-but-never-soggy soil. The rhizomes can be divided and propagated; individual leaves can also be rooted in moist peat to produce new plants.

plant_note: Set tubers out after the last spring frost when the soil is warm.
directions: Rip City bears dramatic dark red-black blooms on long, easy-to-cut stems from early in the season. Plant tubers 4-6 inches deep in well-drained soil after frost danger passes, and stake the plants early since the tall stems benefit from support. This variety is a strong tuber producer, so plants can be divided at season's end. Deadhead spent flowers to keep new blooms coming through fall. Lift tubers before hard frost in cold-winter regions.

plant_note: Plant after frost when soil reaches about 60 F.
directions: Robin Hood produces large salmon-orange blooms whose saturation shifts through the growing season, making each flower slightly different. Because the big blooms are heavy, stake or corral this variety early so stems stay upright. Plant tubers 4-6 inches deep in loose, fertile, well-drained soil once frost danger has passed. Feed with a low-nitrogen fertilizer to favor flowers over foliage. Cut blooms freely to encourage continued production.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring; plants usually bloom the second year.
directions: This perennial painted daisy, also called Persian daisy, produces bright long-stemmed blooms that make excellent cut flowers and pair beautifully with columbine and delphinium. Sow seed in spring in full sun to part shade and well-drained soil. Plants typically flower in their second season and attract pollinators. Divide clumps every few years to keep them vigorous. Some people may experience contact dermatitis, so handle the foliage with care.

common_name: Rock Rose (Texas Pavonia, Rose Mallow Pavonia)
plant_note: Plant container-grown transplants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to alkaline, lean to moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; pavonia is native to the Edwards Plateau and south Texas and is adapted to alkaline limestone soils — it actually grows better in lean, rocky conditions than in rich garden soil.
directions: Plant in well-drained, lean soil in full sun to partial shade; Texas pavonia is one of the most outstanding native shrubs for the Gulf Coast garden — a compact, woody perennial shrub that produces a profusion of small (1.5 inch) single rose-pink flowers with yellow stamens continuously from spring through frost, with peak bloom in summer. Each individual flower lasts only a day, but the plant produces new flowers constantly for 6+ months, creating a long-lasting display. It tolerates alkaline soil, drought, and heat and actually blooms better under mild stress than in rich, moist conditions. Deer resistant and attractive to hummingbirds and butterflies. In zone 7–8, top growth may be killed by hard freezes but roots resprout vigorously in spring. Light pruning in late winter promotes compact, bushy growth.

plant_note: Plant tubers after the last frost when soil has warmed.
directions: Rock Run Ashley is a smaller smoky-salmon dahlia that blends beautifully in mixed bouquets and is a favorite with florists. Plant tubers horizontally 4-6 inches deep after the soil warms, spacing plants about 12-18 inches apart. Pinch young plants to promote branching and more cut stems. Keep soil evenly moist during bloom but let it dry slightly between waterings to prevent tuber rot. Harvest flowers in the cool of the morning for the longest vase life.

plant_note: Plant tubers outdoors once the danger of frost has passed.
directions: Rockstar is a dark anemone-type dahlia with distinctive foliage that blooms continuously through the season and holds well in the vase. Plant the tuber 4-6 inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil after frost, positioning the eye upward. Provide full sun and support tall stems as needed. Deadhead regularly and cut blooms often to keep the plant productive. In cold climates dig and store tubers before the first hard freeze.

plant_note: Direct sow seed after last frost in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry to moderately moist, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; Rocky Mountain bee plant self-sows prolifically and naturalizes in open, sunny, dry areas — no supplemental water is needed once plants are established.
directions: Direct sow after last frost in well-drained, dry to moderately moist, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun — Rocky Mountain bee plant is a native annual of the Great Plains, Rocky Mountain foothills, and desert Southwest, producing clusters of showy, spidery, pink to white flowers on tall, branching, slightly pungent-scented stems from summer through fall. It is one of the most important native bee plants of the central US: bumblebees, mason bees, and honey bees are intensely attracted to the nectar-rich flowers, working the plants all day in good weather. The plant was an important food source for indigenous peoples of the Plains and Pueblos, who boiled the leaves as a green vegetable and rendered the plant to make a black dye used in pottery painting. It self-sows prolifically to naturalize in dry, open, sandy or rocky soils across a wide climate range. The musky scent (a defense against some insect herbivores) is more noticeable up close than at a distance.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring or fall; cold stratification improves germination.
directions: The state flower of Colorado, this heirloom columbine bears long-lasting pale violet and white blossoms above delicate blue-green, fern-like foliage. Sow seed in early spring or fall and expose it to a cold period, as chilling greatly improves germination. It is at home in part shade and moist, well-drained soil. Plants bloom in their second year and self-sow readily. Leave a few seed heads to naturalize the patch over time.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall or early spring; seed benefits from cold stratification.
directions: This long-lived heirloom penstemon sends up exquisite blue-violet flower spikes that bloom for about a month in early summer and attract pollinators. Sow seed in fall or early spring and give it a cold, moist period to break dormancy. Provide full sun and lean, sharply drained soil, as it is drought tolerant once established. Evergreen basal rosettes carry the plant through winter. Leave the satiny brown seedpods for interest and self-sowing.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring around last frost date; plants may bloom the first fall from an early sowing or establish and bloom their second year.
directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep in well-drained soil; germination is fast and reliable. Plant in full sun in poor to average, very well-drained soil — rose campion thrives under dry, sunny conditions that would stress many perennials. The intensely magenta-crimson flowers glow against the woolly, silver-white felted stems and leaves, which are ornamental throughout the growing season. Individual plants are short-lived (2–3 years) but self-sow abundantly so colonies persist indefinitely; thin or transplant self-sown seedlings to prevent overcrowding.

plant_note: Scarify hard seed coat before sowing to speed germination.
directions: Nick or soak the hard seed coat before sowing to encourage even germination in warm soil. This native hibiscus produces large, pink, tropical-looking blooms and thrives in moist to wet ground. Give it full sun and steady water, making it ideal for pond edges, rain gardens, and low, damp spots. The plant dies back in winter and returns from the root, drawing bees and hummingbirds each summer.

plant_note: Cold-moist stratify seed for 30 days; often blooms the second year.
directions: Cold-stratify the seed for about a month, then sow into moist soil in spring. Also called swamp milkweed, this native perennial bears clusters of fragrant dusty-rose flowers atop narrow-leaved stems. Unlike common milkweed it tolerates wet ground, making it perfect for rain gardens and pond margins in full sun. It is an essential Monarch host and nectar source, so avoid pesticides and let caterpillars feed on the foliage.

plant_note: Plant nursery-propagated rhizome divisions in spring in saturated to wet, acidic (pH 3.5–5.5), nutrient-poor, peaty or sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; rose pogonia is a true bog obligate that will not survive in typical garden soil — it requires constantly wet, open, acidic bog conditions with no fertilizer.
directions: Rose pogonia is a delicate and charming bog orchid native to open, acidic wetlands — bogs, bog meadows, seeps, and sphagnum-filled depressions — across eastern North America from Newfoundland to Florida and west to Texas, where it blooms in June and July with a single rose-pink flower bearing a conspicuous fringed and bearded lip that smells faintly of raspberries. Like grass pink orchid, it practices deceptive pollination, offering no reward to pollinators — native bumblebees visit the flower expecting nectar but find none, and only return occasionally due to the lack of reward, which is sufficient for cross-pollination between widely spaced plants in bog meadows. Rose pogonia is one of the more tractable native bog orchids for cultivation because it spreads vegetatively by horizontal rhizomes in appropriate bog conditions, gradually forming small colonies in sphagnum moss or wet, peaty soil without the extreme mycorrhizal fungal dependence of many other native orchids. An essential component of a native bog garden in zones 3–9 alongside pitcher plants, sundews, and butterworts in nutrient-poor, constantly wet, acidic conditions.

plant_note: Plant divisions in consistently moist to wet soil in partial shade; this plant requires reliable moisture and will stress in drought. Blooms 2–3 weeks earlier than the white species (Chelone glabra).
directions: Plant in consistently moist to wet, fertile, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; rose turtlehead produces deep rose-pink inflated flowers from midsummer into fall, blooming 2–3 weeks earlier than the similar white turtlehead and offering richer color. It grows naturally along stream banks, wet meadow margins, and boggy woodland edges from Maryland south into the Southeast. Bumblebees that are heavy enough to force open the closed flower mouth are its primary pollinators. Plant in groups of three or more for best visual impact. Divide clumps every 4 years in spring to maintain vigor; cut to the ground after hard frost. Excellent in rain gardens and wet borders where the reliable summer moisture it needs is naturally available.

plant_note: Direct sow after all danger of frost; zinnias grow quickly in warm soil.
directions: Abundant four- to five-inch rosy pink blooms range from dusty rose to warm pink, evoking an old-fashioned cottage garden when planted in drifts. Sow seed directly into warm soil after the last frost. Cut and deadhead frequently to keep the plants producing into fall. Full sun and good spacing for airflow help avoid powdery mildew. Pollinators, including butterflies, love the long-lasting flowers.

plant_note: Plant young container-grown shrubs in fall in moist, acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade; rosebay rhododendron tolerates deeper shade than almost any other flowering broadleaf evergreen — it naturally grows in the dark understory beneath hemlock and hardwood canopy.
directions: Rosebay rhododendron (great laurel) is the largest native rhododendron in North America — capable of reaching 20–35 feet in the moist, cool, deep shade of Appalachian stream gorges and hemlock forests — and produces massive trusses of white to pale pink, spotted flowers in June and July, a month later than most other rhododendrons, making it the last and largest-flowered of all native flowering shrubs in the eastern forest. Its enormous, glossy, dark green leaves (up to 10 inches long) curl tightly during cold weather as a frost protection mechanism, a distinctive winter character; the dense, multi-stemmed growth creates impenetrable evergreen thickets in shaded stream valleys that are critical winter shelter for ruffed grouse, white-tailed deer, and black bears. It is the most shade-tolerant of all native rhododendrons — thriving in the darkest understory beneath eastern hemlock, where little else with ornamental value grows — and represents one of the few truly shade-tolerant, large-scale evergreen flowering shrubs for the eastern native garden. Grows slowly but long-lived; once established it requires minimal care in appropriate conditions.

plant_note: Plant container-grown transplants in spring in well-drained, strongly acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich, rocky or sandy soil in partial shade to high open shade under deciduous trees. Roseshell Azalea tolerates somewhat drier and rockier conditions than most native azaleas and is the hardiest species, performing well in Zones 3–7.
directions: Roseshell Azalea is a fragrant native deciduous azalea of the northeastern United States and Appalachian Mountains, producing profuse clusters of deep rose-pink to light pink flowers with a strong, sweet clove-like fragrance that perfumes entire gardens in late spring — a quality unique among native azaleas that makes it an outstanding choice wherever fragrance is a garden priority. The most cold-hardy of all native azaleas, surviving USDA Zone 3 reliably, Roseshell Azalea grows in rocky, dry-to-moist, open woods, cliff edges, and forest margins from Quebec south to the Virginia mountains, tolerating somewhat drier and stonier conditions than most other native azalea species. The compact, naturally rounded growth habit, spectacular fragrant flowers, and brilliant orange-red to crimson fall foliage make this a superb multi-season native shrub for northern and mountain landscapes. Plant in well-drained, acidic, humus-rich, rocky or sandy soil in partial shade to high, shifting shade under deciduous trees.

plant_note: Set out nursery plants in spring or fall in acidic, well-drained soil.
directions: This hardy evergreen shrub produces clusters of soft pink, trumpet-shaped blooms in spring above lush foliage. Plant it in spring or fall in acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil and site it in dappled shade or morning sun. Keep the shallow roots cool and evenly moist with a mulch of pine bark or leaves. Avoid deep planting, as the crown must stay near the surface. Prune lightly right after flowering to shape the plant.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring; surface sow as seed needs light to germinate.
directions: This sweetly scented alyssum forms low mats covered in tiny rose-pink flowers in mini-rosettes, perfect for rock gardens, walls, paths, and container edges. Sow seed directly in early spring, pressing it onto the surface since it needs light to germinate. Full sun to light shade and well-drained soil keep it blooming. Shear lightly if it fades in summer heat to spur a fresh flush. The mildly grassy, mustardy flowers are edible and draw in pollinators.

plant_note: Direct sow seed in fall in well-drained to average, slightly acidic to neutral, clay to loam soil in full sun; rosinweed is a deep-rooted prairie plant that takes 2–3 years to bloom but becomes virtually indestructible once established — site it where it can remain permanently.
directions: Sow seed in fall in well-drained to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun — rosinweed is the most compact and garden-adaptable of the native Silphium genus, producing bright yellow, 2–3 inch daisy-like flowers from late summer through early fall on branching stems 3–5 feet tall. Like all silphiums, rosinweed is a deep-rooted prairie species with extraordinary drought and heat tolerance once established — the deep taproot (sometimes 12 feet deep in mature specimens) accesses moisture unavailable to shallow-rooted plants. The rough, sandpaper-textured opposite leaves are distinctive. The flat, disc-shaped seeds are highly attractive to goldfinches, which perch on the sturdy stems to extract them. It is being researched as a potential perennial grain crop because the seeds have a high oil content. More compact and less rank than compass plant or prairie dock, making it more suitable for smaller prairie garden settings. Extremely long-lived — prairie specimens are known to be over 100 years old.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring or fall in mild climates; clarkia dislikes transplanting.
directions: Graceful branching stems carry a profusion of delicate pink, white, and salmon blooms from spring into summer, swaying in the breeze. Sow seed directly where it is to grow, as clarkia resents root disturbance. It prefers cool weather, so sow in early spring or in fall where winters are mild. Give it full sun to part shade and lean, well-drained soil. Pollinators love the flowers, which also last well when cut.

plant_note: Plant divisions or plugs in early spring, spacing at 8–12 inches for coverage within 2 years; tolerates very dry shade once established.
directions: Plant divisions in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; rosy sedge is one of the finest native groundcovers for dry shade under mature deciduous trees, where it forms dense tufts of arching, fine-textured, glossy green foliage. It is not a spreader in the invasive sense — each clump slowly expands but does not colonize aggressively — making it well-suited to a refined shade garden. The plant tolerates drought better than Pennsylvania sedge (C. pensylvanica) and handles the dense root competition of established trees. Self-sows lightly once established to fill gaps over time.

plant_note: Direct sow after last frost; succession sow for continuous cut flowers.
directions: Sow seed one inch deep directly in the garden after the last frost once soil has warmed. This heirloom variety produces deep burgundy-red, velvety petals around dark chocolate centers on sturdy five- to six-foot stems. Give it full sun and moderate water, staking in windy sites to protect the tall stalks. Cut blooms just as they open for long-lasting arrangements, or leave heads to feed pollinators and birds.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost; sunflowers grow best sown where they will bloom.
directions: This stunning true-red sunflower blooms on multi-branching plants, offering a dramatic accent in beds, borders, and bouquets. Sow seed directly into warm soil after frost, spacing plants closer together for longer stems and smaller flowers ideal for cutting. Full sun and steady moisture produce the best display. Cut stems just before the flowers fully open for the longest vase life. Successive sowings extend the bloom season into fall.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification, or cold-stratify seed for 60 days before spring sowing; corms can also be planted in spring at 2 inches deep.
directions: Sow cold-stratified seed or plant corms in sharply drained, lean soil in full sun; this native Liatris blooms later than L. spicata, providing purple-pink flower spikes in late August through September when other prairie plants are winding down. It is more drought tolerant and heat tolerant than the common spike gayfeather, making it particularly well-suited to hot, dry gardens and the southern edges of its range. The open, loose flower arrangement (compared to the denser L. spicata) is preferred by some native bee species. Monarch butterflies and native bumblebees are especially attracted to this plant during their late-summer movements and foraging.

plant_note: Plant rooted suckers or container plants in fall or early spring in well-drained to moderately moist, slightly acidic to alkaline soil in full sun to partial shade; roughleaf dogwood is remarkably adaptable to both dry, rocky, alkaline soils of the southern plains and the moist, acidic soils of eastern woodlands.
directions: Plant in well-drained to moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade — roughleaf dogwood is a tough, suckering native shrub or small multi-stem tree of southern woodlands, thickets, and stream edges, producing flat-topped clusters of small white flowers in late spring followed by pea-sized, creamy-white berries on red pedicels in late summer that are among the most intensely sought fruits by migrating songbirds. The rough-textured, opposite leaves turn attractive shades of maroon and wine-red in fall. It spreads by root suckers to form dense, multi-stem thickets that provide outstanding cover and food for wildlife year-round. Adaptable to dry, rocky, alkaline conditions unusual among dogwoods — an outstanding native for difficult southern sites where flowering dogwood (C. florida) struggles. Prune out oldest stems periodically to maintain vigor and an open, attractive form.

plant_note: Plant fibrous root divisions in fall in moist to moderately dry, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich, stony woodland soil in deep shade. Round-Lobed Hepatica tolerates somewhat drier, sandier, and more acidic soils than Sharp-Lobed Hepatica and is often found in drier oak-hickory woods.
directions: Round-Lobed Hepatica is a beloved native wildflower of eastern North American forests, producing delicate flowers ranging from white through pink to lavender and blue in late winter to very early spring, often blooming while snow still lingers around its base and becoming one of the first wildflowers to greet emerging bees and butterflies each year. The three-lobed, mottled green and reddish leaves remain evergreen through winter as a low basal rosette, distinguished from the closely related Sharp-Lobed Hepatica by the rounded (obtuse) tips of its three leaf lobes and its preference for drier, more acidic, sandy or stony soils in drier oak-hickory forest rather than the rich, base-rich soils of beech-maple forest. A long-lived perennial of extraordinary persistence — individual plants may live for decades — Round-Lobed Hepatica is best established from fall-planted divisions and takes 2–3 years to settle into its permanent location before flowering reliably each spring. Plant in moist to dry, well-drained, acidic, humus-rich woodland soil in deep shade under deciduous trees.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring as soon as soil can be worked; soak seed first.
directions: The Royal series offers a majestic mix of colors with larger flowers and better heat tolerance for a longer bloom period than most sweet peas. Soak the seed, then sow directly in very early spring as soon as the soil can be worked, since they love cool weather. Provide a trellis, fence, or netting for the climbing vines. Keep them well watered and cut frequently to prolong flowering. The fragrant blooms lure pollinators and perfume the house when brought indoors.

plant_note: Surface-sow; seed needs light to germinate.
directions: Press the tiny seed onto the soil surface without covering, since it needs light to sprout. This heirloom sweet alyssum forms a low, spreading carpet of honey-scented violet-purple blooms. Grow it in full sun to light shade as an edging, container filler, or ground cover among vegetables to draw beneficial insects. Shear lightly after the first flush to encourage a fresh round of bloom into cooler weather.

plant_note: Plant crowns at soil level in early spring in consistently moist soil; ideal near ponds or streams where roots can access reliable moisture.
directions: Plant in wet, acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; the royal fern has a naturally wide distribution across bogs, stream margins, and wet woodlands and is among the largest and most impressive of hardy ferns. The broadly compound fronds have a distinctive open, airy appearance unlike the typical fern, and the fertile frond tips turn rusty brown with spores in summer. Provide consistently wet to saturated soil and avoid any drought stress, particularly in the first two years; mulch heavily with leaf mold to retain moisture. Divide the massive, fibrous root mass in early spring every 7–10 years.

plant_note: Scarify seeds by nicking the seed coat or soaking in hot water (not boiling) for 24 hours before planting; or plant nursery transplants in spring in any well-drained soil in full sun. Royal Poinciana is intolerant of frost, heavy clay soil, and poor drainage.
directions: Royal Poinciana is widely considered the most spectacular flowering tree in the world, native to the dry deciduous forests of Madagascar and now naturalized throughout the tropics and subtropics, producing in late spring and summer an overwhelming canopy of enormous, brilliant scarlet-orange flowers with yellow-tipped petals that can completely conceal the wide-spreading, umbrella-shaped crown — a display so vivid that the tree is visible from miles away. The fine, bipinnate, ferny foliage provides delicate shade during the non-flowering months, and the dark brown seed pods (up to 2 feet long) persist through winter, rattling in the wind. In zones 10–11 it is reliably perennial, and in favorable coastal conditions may be semi-evergreen; it is killed to the ground by even light frost and therefore confined to South Florida, the Florida Keys, and other subtropical areas in the continental US. Grows extremely fast — up to 5 feet per year in good conditions — but needs 5–10 years to reach first flowering. Provide well-drained soil, full sun, and space for its wide-spreading roots and canopy.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in average soil; overly rich soil reduces blooms.
directions: This Fleuroselect award winner bears deeply colored three-inch blooms that open red-wine and age to dusty rose, striking against white flowers and silver foliage. Sow directly after frost into average, well-drained soil, as rich soil produces foliage at the expense of flowers. Full sun and modest water suit it best; it is drought tolerant once established. Deadhead to prolong bloom or leave a few seed heads to self-sow. Butterflies flock to the flowers, which also cut well.

plant_note: Direct sow seed or plant container-grown plants after the last frost in any well-drained soil in full sun; ruby grass grows rapidly in heat and begins blooming within 60–90 days of germination. In zone 9–10 it self-sows to overwinter; in colder zones it is grown as a summer annual.
directions: Plant in any well-drained soil in full sun; ruby grass (Melinis nerviglumis 'Savannah') is one of the most spectacular ornamental grasses available for summer color, producing fluffy, shimmering panicles of tiny spikelets in brilliant shades of ruby-red, rose-pink, and burgundy that catch the light and glow with exceptional luminosity — particularly beautiful in the late afternoon sun when the entire plant appears to be lit from within. The fine-textured, narrow, reddish-tinged leaves complement the flowers perfectly. It begins blooming in early summer and continues through frost, and the dried seed heads add winter interest. Unlike many ornamental grasses, it blooms all season rather than just in fall. As a tender tropical grass, it is grown as a perennial in zone 9–11 and as a summer annual in colder zones, where it self-sows freely in mild winters. Excellent in mixed borders, containers, and as a seasonal accent plant wherever summer color is needed.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost when soil is warm; can start indoors 3-4 weeks early.
directions: Grown by Thomas Jefferson at Monticello, this ornamental vine dazzles with pinkish-purple flowers and shiny dark-purple pods against purple-tinged foliage. Sow directly into warm soil after frost, providing a sturdy fence or trellis for the fast, twining stems to climb. Full sun brings out the richest pod color. Keep it evenly watered through the heat of summer. Note that the raw pods and seeds are toxic and should be treated as ornamental only.

plant_note: Plant small tuber clusters in fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; rue anemone is a spring ephemeral that dies back completely by early summer — mark its location to avoid disturbing it during summer dormancy.
directions: Plant small tuber clusters in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial to full shade — rue anemone is one of the smallest and most delicate of all eastern woodland spring ephemerals, producing clusters of white to pale pink (rarely deep pink) five-petaled flowers on slender stems only 4–9 inches tall in early spring, just as the forest canopy begins to leaf out. The leaves closely resemble those of meadow rue (Thalictrum), explaining both the common name and the former genus placement. Like all true spring ephemerals it dies back completely by early summer, leaving no trace above ground. It is a slow-growing but extremely long-lived native that gradually forms a charming colony when well-sited. Named varieties like 'Shoaf's Double Pink' and 'Betty Blake' (double white flowers) are available from specialty native plant nurseries. An outstanding native for the shaded wildflower garden, best combined with later-emerging foliage plants to fill in after it goes dormant.

plant_note: Plant nursery-propagated rhizome divisions in fall in moist, humus-rich, acidic to neutral woodland soil in partial to full shade. Rugel's Trillium is a pedicellate species with strongly reflexed white petals that create a nodding appearance distinct from most other trilliums.
directions: Rugel's Trillium is a rare and distinctive pedicellate trillium native to a limited area in the southern Appalachians of Tennessee, North Carolina, and northern Georgia, where it grows in moist, rich hardwood forest coves and sheltered slopes and blooms in April with three white petals that reflex so strongly backward that they resemble a swept-back cap — an appearance unlike any other eastern trillium. The flower hangs on a short pedicel below the leaf whorl, facing downward and sometimes hidden beneath the foliage, and carries a faint fragrance that attracts small fly pollinators. Listed as a species of conservation concern in several states, Rugel's Trillium should never be collected from wild populations; source only from specialty native plant nurseries propagating their own rhizome divisions. Grow in deep, consistently moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in full shade, mulch generously with leaf litter, and allow the foliage to yellow and die back naturally by summer.

plant_note: Direct sow fresh acorns in fall at 1–2 inch depth in pure, infertile, sandy, extremely well-drained, acidic soil in full sun. Runner Oak requires xeric scrub conditions and will not compete in heavier or more productive soils; the rhizomatous root system means it spreads vigorously once established.
directions: Runner Oak is a low, colony-forming native shrub oak of the coastal plain sandhills and fire-maintained xeric scrub habitats of Georgia and Florida, spreading underground by rhizomes to form dense colonies of knee-high stems across expanses of pure white sand where larger trees cannot survive — making it superficially resemble a grass colony more than a tree. Despite its small stature, Runner Oak produces full-sized acorns that are a critical food source for Florida Scrub-Jays, gopher tortoises, and black bears in the imperiled scrub ecosystem where larger mast-producing oaks may be sparse. Highly adapted to fire, Runner Oak resprouts vigorously from its extensive underground rhizome system within weeks of a scrub burn and is a dominant component of the ground-layer plant community in many Florida scrub sites. Direct sow fresh acorns in fall in infertile, pure, sandy, extremely well-drained, acidic soil in full sun — Runner Oak requires the xeric, open, fire-maintained conditions of native scrub and will not thrive in more productive or shaded environments.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring or fall; nick or soak the hard seed coat to aid germination.
directions: This 1937 English selection offers an array of colors on tall dramatic spikes that shine when planted in masses or mixed borders. Nick or soak the hard seed coat, then sow directly in spring or fall, as lupines dislike transplanting their long taproot. They prefer full sun, cool summers, and well-drained, slightly acidic soil. In zones 4 to 7 they are short-lived perennials; in warmer areas grow them as annuals. Remove spent spikes to encourage more blooms and support pollinators.

plant_note: Scarify and soak seed before sowing; resents root disturbance so direct sow when possible.
directions: Nick and soak the hard-coated seed overnight, then sow where plants are to grow since lupines dislike transplanting. This perennial hybrid sends up tall spikes densely packed with pea-like flowers in blue, purple, pink, yellow, and white. Plant in full sun and cool, well-drained soil; as a legume it fixes nitrogen and improves the ground. Cut spent spikes to encourage rebloom and let a few set seed for next season's plants.

plant_note: Plant in spring after last frost in lean, well-drained soil; established plants are extremely drought-tolerant.
directions: Plant in poor to average, sharply drained soil in full sun — Russian sage declines quickly in fertile or moist soil and is highly susceptible to root rot in clay. Cut plants back hard in early spring (to about 6 in) just as new basal growth emerges; do not cut back in fall as the woody framework helps protect the crown. The airy silver stems and lavender-blue flowers create a haze of color from midsummer through fall and are beloved by pollinators. Deer avoid this plant due to its strongly aromatic foliage.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after frost; surface sow as seed needs light.
directions: This Asian wildflower has an unusual spiky habit and rosy color that adds welcome contrast to the landscape and long-lasting cut and dried flowers. Start seed indoors six to eight weeks before the last frost, pressing it onto the surface for light. Transplant into full sun and well-drained soil after frost. It tolerates heat and dry conditions once established. Harvest stems when most of the tiny flowers have opened for the best dried bouquets.

plant_note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in dry to moist, well-drained, sandy, highly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade. Rusty Staggerbush is adapted to the dry, scrubby flatwoods and ridge habitats of the Florida peninsula and coastal plain, and requires excellent drainage to thrive.
directions: Rusty Staggerbush is a coarse-textured evergreen shrub or small tree, native to the dry, sandy, scrubby flatwoods, pine ridges, and xeric hammocks of the Florida peninsula and coastal plain from South Carolina to Alabama, distinguished by its large, leathery leaves with rust-colored (ferruginous) scales on the undersides that give the species its common name. In spring it produces clusters of white to pale pink, urn-shaped flowers typical of the heath family (Ericaceae), which are important nectar sources for native bees; the plant may grow 8–20 feet tall in ideal conditions, forming small trees in open scrub habitats. All parts are toxic if ingested by livestock — the 'staggerbush' name refers to the neurological symptoms caused in cattle that graze the foliage. Grow in dry to moist, well-drained, sandy, acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; it is drought-tolerant once established and an important component of Florida scrub restoration plantings.

plant_note: Plant container-grown specimens in spring in very well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy to loamy soil in full sun to partial shade; perfect drainage is the primary cultural requirement — sago palms decline rapidly in heavy clay or waterlogged soils. All parts of the plant are extremely toxic to humans and pets — manganese deficiency (yellow fronds) is common in alkaline soils and is treated with chelated manganese.
directions: Plant in very well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; despite the common name, the sago palm is not a true palm but a cycad — one of the oldest surviving plant lineages on Earth, essentially unchanged since the Mesozoic Era. The dramatic, crown of stiff, glossy, dark-green fronds radiating from a thick, rough-textured trunk (which grows extremely slowly — gaining only 1–2 inches per year) makes it one of the most architecturally striking landscape plants available for zones 8–12. An established specimen with multiple trunks is an invaluable focal point in any tropical or subtropical garden. The plant produces bright orange-red seeds in large cone-like structures; every part of the plant — seeds, leaves, and trunk — is extremely toxic to humans and pets. In coastal Gulf Coast gardens it is one of the most reliable landscape plants available, tolerating salt, drought, heat, and wind with ease. Apply palm-formulated fertilizer with added manganese in spring to prevent the common yellowing from micronutrient deficiency.

plant_note: Plant tubers after frost when the soil has warmed.
directions: Salmon Runner is a well-balanced salmon-colored dahlia that produces abundant cut flowers without needing staking. Plant the tuber 4-6 inches deep in loose, fertile soil after the last frost, eye facing up. This sturdy variety stands on its own but still benefits from pinching to increase stem count. Water deeply and consistently once growth is established, and cut blooms often to prolong flowering. Lift and store tubers over winter in regions colder than zone 8.

plant_note: Plant plugs or divisions in spring in irregularly flooded high marsh zones, salt flats, or moist brackish soils; salt meadow cordgrass occupies the high marsh zone flooded only by storm tides, above the daily inundation zone of smooth cordgrass.
directions: Salt meadow cordgrass (formerly Spartina patens) is the characteristic grass of the high salt marsh zone — the broad expanses of short, dense, cowlick-patterned grass visible from coastal roads and causeways along both the Atlantic and Gulf coasts. Its lower height, finer texture, and characteristic 'cowlick' growth pattern (where wind and tidal currents lay the grass in whirling mats) easily distinguish it from the taller smooth cordgrass of the lower marsh. Historically harvested as 'salt hay' for bedding and fodder from colonial times through the early 20th century, salt meadow cordgrass was the backbone of colonial coastal agriculture; today it is a primary plant for high marsh restoration, living shoreline construction, and tidal wetland mitigation projects throughout the eastern United States. Dense stands provide critical nesting habitat for salt marsh sparrows, seaside sparrows, and willets, and the seeds are consumed by migrating birds; the grass also functions as important muskrat, vole, and meadow mouse habitat in coastal marshes.

plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring in any well-drained, alkaline, dry to average soil in full sun; sand hackberry is a thorny, drought-resistant shrub of south Texas and the Rio Grande Valley — it grows rapidly and tolerates poor, alkaline, caliche soils with no irrigation.
directions: Sand hackberry (granjeno in south Texas) is a thorny native evergreen shrub of the lower Rio Grande Valley and south Texas brush country, producing small, yellowish-orange to red fruits abundantly year-round that are among the most important food sources for resident and migratory birds in the region — it is one of the top-ranked native plants for bird feeding in the Rio Grande Valley by wildlife managers and birders. The dense, thorny thickets provide impenetrable nesting and escape cover for green jays, Altamira orioles, and dozens of other brushland birds; the plant is a cornerstone of the native south Texas chaparral community. Hackberry emperor butterflies use this species as a larval host, and white-tipped doves, plain chachalacas, and chestnut-fronted macaws consume the fruits. In south Texas wildlife habitat gardens and Rio Grande Valley native plantings, granjeno is one of the highest-priority native shrubs for creating dense, self-sustaining wildlife habitat that provides food, cover, and nesting resources year-round without supplemental irrigation.

plant_note: Plant nursery-grown container trees in fall in dry, sandy, well-drained, acidic to neutral soil in full sun; sand live oak is adapted to deep, excessively drained coastal sands and does not tolerate poorly drained, heavy clay, or alkaline soils.
directions: Sand live oak is a native evergreen tree of coastal sand dunes, scrub habitats, and sandy pinelands of the Gulf and Atlantic Coastal Plain, closely related to live oak (Quercus virginiana) but distinctly adapted to excessively drained, nutrient-poor, acidic sands of ancient coastal terraces. It is distinguished from live oak by its smaller, more narrow leaves with rolled-under (revolute) margins, lower growth habit, and adaptation to dry sand rather than the moist, fertile soils favored by live oak. In coastal dune restoration and sandy barrier island plantings, sand live oak serves as one of the primary stabilizing species for ancient coastal plain sands, where it eventually develops into sprawling, wind-sculpted specimens of great character; it is more salt spray tolerant than live oak. Like all oaks, it supports an enormous diversity of native moths, butterflies, and beetles as larval hosts; its small acorns feed migrating wood ducks, and the dense evergreen canopy provides nesting habitat for many songbirds. A valuable evergreen tree for coastal sandy sites in zone 7–10 where typical live oak would not thrive.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring on sandy, infertile, well-drained soil in full sun; sand lovegrass requires sharp drainage and lean soils — it declines rapidly in heavy clay or moist, fertile garden conditions and is best suited to sandy prairies and xeric landscape situations.
directions: Sand lovegrass is a taller, more robust native lovegrass of deep sandy soils and sandy prairies across the south-central United States, producing large, open, feathery panicles of light pinkish-green to tan spikelets in late summer that wave gracefully in any breeze — its airy, fountain-like form makes it one of the most elegant of all native prairie grasses for dry, sandy garden situations. Found naturally in sand prairies, sandy bottomlands, and open sandy oak woodlands of the Central Plains and Gulf Coast region, it is adapted to deep, well-drained, infertile sandy soils that challenge most ornamental grasses; it is significantly more tolerant of sand and drought than blue grama or buffalo grass while producing a more visually dramatic plant. The seed panicles provide food for native sparrows and finches through fall and winter, and the dried tufts remain attractive through the cold season. Excellent companion for prairie grasses like indiangrass, big bluestem, and prairie dropseed in sandy-soil native meadow plantings from Texas to the Midwest.

plant_note: Direct sow or transplant in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun; sandpaper verbena spreads aggressively by rhizomes in Gulf Coast conditions — it is most appropriate in large open areas or as a slope cover where spreading is desired.
directions: Plant or direct sow in well-drained, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun — sandpaper verbena is a tough, spreading perennial from South America that has naturalized widely across the Gulf Coast and Deep South, producing brilliant purple to magenta flat-topped flower clusters almost continuously from spring through hard frost. The rough, sandpapery-textured, deeply veined leaves and stems give it a distinctive texture; the plant spreads rapidly by underground rhizomes to cover large areas in hot, dry conditions where lawn and other groundcovers fail. It is an outstanding butterfly plant with exceptional drought tolerance and makes a bold, colorful slope cover, median planting, or informal groundcover in sunny, dry areas. In zone 7–8 it may die back in winter but resprouts reliably from established roots. The intense purple flower color is unusually vivid in the landscape and combines beautifully with yellow or orange companions. Too aggressive for small formal beds but invaluable for large-scale planting.

plant_note: Plant container-grown transplants or rooted cuttings in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade. Sarvis Holly is dioecious — both male and female plants are required within 50 feet for berry production. It thrives in low, wet, shaded settings where many other holly species struggle.
directions: Sarvis Holly is a rarely cultivated native deciduous holly of moist, low-lying forests, wet woods, and stream margins of the coastal plain from South Carolina to Louisiana, producing clusters of small pink to red berries that ripen in late summer and fall and are avidly consumed by mockingbirds, bluebirds, robins, and other fruit-eating birds during the critical pre-migration feeding period. The name 'sarvis' is a folk rendering of 'serviceberry' or 'service tree,' reflecting the ripening time of its berries relative to other fall food sources. Less commonly cultivated than Possumhaw or Winterberry hollies, Sarvis Holly thrives in the wet, shaded conditions of bottomland forest and stream banks where larger hollies may struggle, and deserves wider use in native plant landscapes. Plant rooted cuttings in moist to wet, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade; dioecious, so both sexes are required within pollinator range for berry set.

plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs in fall in well-drained, acidic soil slightly above grade; fall planting allows root establishment before the autumn and winter bloom season begins.
directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 5.5–6.5) soil in partial shade to full sun; sasanqua camellias bloom in fall and early winter, earlier than Camellia japonica, and are more sun-tolerant and heat-hardy, making them the better choice for Gulf Coast gardens in zones 8–9. Single, semi-double, or double flowers in white, pink, red, and lavender provide vivid color from October through January when little else is in bloom. Unlike japonicas, sasanquas can be trained as espaliers against walls, used as hedges, or even grown as small specimen trees. Apply pine-bark mulch and acid-forming camellia fertilizer in spring. Petal blight and scale are the main concerns; improve air circulation and treat scale with horticultural oil in late winter.

plant_note: Plant container specimens in fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in full sun to partial shade; saucer magnolia blooms on bare wood in early spring and is susceptible to late frost damage to the buds — avoid planting in frost pockets or north-facing spots where late freezes are common.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade — saucer magnolia is one of the most spectacular flowering trees in the world for spring impact, producing enormous, tulip-shaped flowers (4–10 inches across) in shades ranging from deep rose-purple to pale pink to white, borne on bare branches before the leaves emerge in late winter to early spring. A single tree in bloom is visible from hundreds of feet away. The hybrid origin (M. denudata x M. liliiflora) combines cold hardiness and floriferous habit; named cultivars include 'Lennei' (deep rose-purple), 'Alexandrina' (pink and white), 'Brozzonii' (nearly white), and 'Rustica Rubra' (rich rose). The main risk in the South is late frost damage to early-opening buds in zone 5–7; in zone 8–9, this is rarely a problem and plants bloom reliably and abundantly. An outstanding focal point or specimen tree for the front landscape.

plant_note: Plant container-grown plants in spring in sandy, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; saw palmetto transplants from containers fairly well but is very slow to establish from small plants. The underground root system is ancient and extensive in wild specimens.
directions: Plant in sandy, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade — saw palmetto is one of the most ecologically important shrubs in the southeastern US, forming the dominant understory layer across millions of acres of Florida and Gulf Coast pine flatwoods and scrub. Individual plants are estimated to live 500–700 years, maintaining an enormous root system that stores carbohydrates and re-sprouts after fire within days. The cream-white flower clusters produce small black berries that are a primary food source for black bears, white-tailed deer, gopher tortoises, and more than 100 bird species. The berries are also harvested commercially as an herbal supplement for prostate health. Saw palmetto tolerates partial shade, salt, drought, and periodic flooding — it is one of the most resilient and valuable native plants for Gulf Coast habitat gardens. The saw-toothed petioles give it its name.

plant_note: Direct sow fresh seeds in fall in dry, rocky or sandy, well-drained, acidic to neutral soil in full sun; seeds cold-stratify naturally over winter and germinate in spring. Scaly Blazingstar tolerates rocky, calcareous, and sandy soils and is more drought-tolerant than most blazingstar species.
directions: Scaly Blazingstar is a distinctive native perennial of dry, rocky or sandy, open woodlands, prairies, glades, and barrens across the eastern United States from New England south to Florida and west to Nebraska, immediately recognizable among blazingstars by the squarrose (stiffly spreading) tips of its phyllary bracts — the scales surrounding each flower head — that curve sharply outward, giving the flower head a rough, star-like appearance. The large, intensely purple flower heads are produced from July through September on stiff, upright stems, providing outstanding nectar for migrating monarch butterflies, native bumblebees, sphinx moths, and many species of native bees and butterflies. Adapted to lean, infertile soils in full sun, Scaly Blazingstar is longer-lived and more reliably perennial than some blazingstars when grown in appropriate dry, xeric conditions. Direct sow fresh seeds in fall in dry, rocky or sandy, well-drained, acidic to neutral soil; established corms multiply slowly to form impressive, long-lived clumps.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring or fall; light cold stratification aids germination.
directions: This southwestern native perennial reaches up to thirty inches and bears deep red flowers that stand out against rich green foliage in pollinator gardens. Sow seed in early spring or fall and give it a cool period to improve germination. It thrives in moderately dry, well-drained soil and full sun to part shade. Once established it is quite drought tolerant. Deadhead to prolong bloom and leave some seed to naturalize.

plant_note: Direct sow in place; short-lived, so succession sow.
directions: Broadcast scarlet flax seed thinly on prepared soil in early spring and rake in lightly. The brilliant red saucer-shaped flowers dance atop wiry stems and are a favorite in wildflower mixes. Because individual plants are short-lived, make successive sowings for continuous color through the season. It thrives in full sun and lean, well-drained soil with minimal supplemental water.

plant_note: Direct sow seeds in spring or fall in dry, sandy, gravelly, or clay, well-drained, neutral to alkaline soil in full sun. Scarify seed coats with sandpaper or soak in warm water for 24 hours before planting to speed germination. Scarlet Mallow is extremely drought-tolerant once established and will not thrive in wet or overly amended soils.
directions: Scarlet Mallow is a native perennial of the western Great Plains, dry prairies, and desert grasslands from Canada south to New Mexico and Texas, producing clusters of vivid orange-red to coral flowers with a satiny sheen on low, spreading mounds of silvery-gray, lobed foliage from spring through summer. One of the most drought-tolerant of all native wildflowers, Scarlet Mallow thrives in the hottest, driest, most exposed sites — dry roadsides, eroded slopes, rock gardens, and xeric borders — where its cheerful flowers provide an extended season of orange color from May through August in full sun. The gray, star-shaped hairs that cover the entire plant give it a silvery appearance that reflects heat and reduces water loss, making it exceptionally adapted to harsh, sunny, dry conditions. Scarify the hard seed coat before planting by rubbing with sandpaper or soaking overnight; direct sow in spring or fall in dry, well-drained soil in full sun — established plants require no irrigation and resent wet or heavy soils.

plant_note: Direct sow seed after the last frost or start divisions in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; scarlet rose mallow thrives in consistently wet to boggy conditions and grows naturally in swamps, marshes, and pond edges. In zone 9 it often reblooms from hard pruning in midsummer.
directions: Plant in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; scarlet rose mallow is one of the most spectacular native perennials of the eastern and Gulf Coast wetlands, producing enormous (6–8 inch) brilliant crimson-red flowers with a deep red eye on tall, robust, deeply palmate-lobed, dramatically architectural plants that grow vigorously in standing water or perpetually wet soil. The flowers have a uniquely delicate, crepe-paper texture and the color is among the most vivid reds in the plant kingdom. Unlike the hybrid hardy hibiscus (H. moscheutos x) which needs drier soil, H. coccineus must have consistently wet or saturated conditions to thrive — it grows naturally in swamps and tidal marshes. It dies back completely to the ground after frost but regrows vigorously from the roots each spring, emerging late (June in zone 9) and quickly reaching its mature height. An outstanding native for rain gardens, pond margins, water features, and wet spots that are otherwise difficult to plant attractively.

plant_note: Plant container-grown transplants in spring in pure, infertile, sandy, extremely well-drained soil in full sun; Scrub Palmetto requires the xeric, nutrient-poor conditions of Florida scrub and will decline in clay, irrigated, or heavily amended soils. No supplemental fertilizer or irrigation is needed once established.
directions: Scrub Palmetto is a small, ground-hugging native palm endemic to the ancient, fire-maintained Florida scrub habitats of the central Florida peninsula, growing in pure white sand soils of extreme infertility and drainage alongside the rarest plants and animals in North America. This palmetto typically remains stemless or develops a very short trunk at or below ground level, producing a cluster of bluish-green, fan-shaped leaves 4–6 feet across that emerge directly from the root mass in a way that makes it fire-resistant and able to regenerate rapidly after scrub burns. The small, black, marble-sized fruits are eaten by Florida Scrub-Jays, gopher tortoises, black bears, and other scrub wildlife, making Scrub Palmetto a keystone species in this imperiled ecosystem. Plant seeds in well-drained, infertile, sandy soil in full sun in warm weather; once the root system is established, the slow-growing plants require no supplemental fertilizer, no irrigation, and no soil amendments.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring or fall; tap-rooted and resents transplanting — sow where plants will remain.
directions: Sow seeds directly in well-drained, even poor, sandy or gravelly soil; sea holly develops a deep taproot and strongly dislikes being transplanted after establishment. The metallic steel-blue stems, bracts, and thimble-shaped flower heads appear in midsummer and hold their color and structure well into fall. Drought-tolerant once established, sea holly declines in rich, moist soil — lean, infertile conditions produce the best blue coloration. Cut stems for fresh or dried arrangements before flowers fully open to preserve the vivid blue color.

plant_note: Plant in moist to wet, salty or brackish, well-drained sandy or silty soil in full sun; sea lavender is a salt marsh specialist that is difficult to establish from seed but transplants well from container-grown nursery stock — do not attempt in alkaline clay or fresh water sites.
directions: Sea lavender is a native perennial wildflower of Atlantic and Gulf Coast salt marshes, producing dense basal rosettes of leathery, paddle-shaped leaves and tall, branching flower stalks covered in masses of tiny, papery lavender-blue flowers in late summer and fall — the dried flowers retain their color indefinitely and are highly prized in floral arrangements, giving sea lavender enduring commercial value as a cut flower crop. The plant grows naturally in the high salt marsh zone alongside glasswort, saltmarsh cordgrass, and seaside goldenrod, thriving in conditions that kill most garden plants: high salinity, periodic flooding, and exposed coastal wind. In coastal gardens and tidal restoration plantings, it serves as a critical late-season nectar source for monarchs and native bees making their fall southward migration along the Gulf Coast, and the dried seedheads persist through winter as bird habitat. Because it tolerates salt spray and sandy, saline soil, sea lavender is also an excellent choice for stabilizing coastal buffer zones, rain gardens near the coast, and any site with brackish soil conditions.

plant_note: Plant plugs or container divisions in spring in very well-drained, sandy, dry, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; sea oats is the primary dune-stabilizing grass of the Gulf Coast and Atlantic shore and plays a critical ecological role in dune formation and erosion control — it is protected from collection in Florida, Georgia, and several other states. Purchase only nursery-grown plants.
directions: Plant nursery-grown plugs in spring in very well-drained, dry, sandy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun — sea oats is the signature grass of the Gulf Coast and Atlantic barrier islands and beaches, its dramatic, gracefully drooping, flat seed heads swaying in the ocean breeze on tall, upright stems from summer through winter in one of the most iconic images of the southern coast. The long, creeping rhizomes spread underground to stabilize shifting dune sand, building and maintaining the dune systems that protect coastal communities from storm surge and erosion. Sea oats is legally protected in Florida, Georgia, and other states — collect no plants from the wild and purchase only nursery-propagated plants and seed. In coastal garden settings it is outstanding for low-maintenance, drought-tolerant, ornamental dune gardens, beach property landscaping, and sandy, salt-spray-exposed areas where few other plants succeed. The seeds are an important food source for birds in the coastal community.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in average soil; overly rich soil reduces blooms.
directions: These charming cosmos have fluted, seashell-shaped petals in white, pink, rose, and crimson that make an excellent cut flower. Sow seed directly after the last frost into average, well-drained soil, since rich soil reduces flowering. They prefer neglect over pampering and become drought tolerant once established. Deadhead to extend the show or leave seed heads to self-sow. Full sun keeps the tall plants sturdy and blooming into fall.

plant_note: Plant divisions or plugs in spring in sandy, well-drained to moist, saline or brackish soils in full sun; seashore dropseed is ideal for sandy beach margins, salt flats, coastal erosion buffers, and stabilizing sandy soils in full sun with high salt spray.
directions: Seashore dropseed is a low, mat-forming native perennial grass of coastal sand dunes, beach margins, and salt flats throughout the Gulf and Atlantic coasts — one of the most salt-tolerant of all native grasses, it thrives directly behind the beach berm where salt spray and occasional saltwater flooding eliminate nearly all competition. Dense, creeping rhizomes and stolons knit sandy coastal soils together tightly, making seashore dropseed one of the most effective native plants for stabilizing sandy beaches, eroding shorelines, and coastal buffer strips without the invasiveness problems of non-native beach grasses. The fine-textured, wiry green leaves form attractive low mounds that turn tawny in winter; small, upright seed spikes provide food for ground-feeding birds.
