In coastal gardens and seaside landscapes, use seashore dropseed as a low-maintenance edging or groundcover grass in areas exposed to salt spray, sandy alkaline soil, and reflected heat from pavement or sea walls — conditions where ornamental grasses and lawn species fail.

plant_note: Direct sow seeds in spring after frost in wet, salt-tolerant, sandy or loamy coastal soil in full sun; scarify the hard seed coat with sandpaper or soak in warm water for 24 hours before planting to speed germination. Seashore Mallow tolerates fresh to brackish coastal marsh conditions and periodic inundation.
directions: Seashore Mallow is a native perennial of coastal marshes, tidal margins, and salt-tolerant wetlands along the Atlantic and Gulf coasts from Long Island south through Florida and west to Texas, where it produces an extended succession of pink hibiscus-like flowers from midsummer through fall on upright, 3–5 foot stems. The seeds are high in protein and edible fatty acids, the roots are starchy and edible, and the mucilaginous leaves and flowers are used in folk medicine similarly to other Malvaceae family members — making Seashore Mallow one of the few native coastal plants with genuine multi-use potential. An important nectar source for native bumblebees, honeybees, and the Cloudless Sulphur butterfly, it has been studied as a potential grain crop for salt-impaired soils where conventional crops cannot grow. Plant in full sun in wet, coastal, sandy to loamy soil; Seashore Mallow tolerates moderate salinity, fresh to brackish conditions, and periodic flooding, self-seeding readily once established in appropriate settings.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall in sandy, well-drained, saline or regular soil in full sun; seaside goldenrod is one of the most salt-tolerant of all native wildflowers and adapts equally well to coastal dunes, inland meadows, and garden borders.
directions: Seaside goldenrod is the salt-tolerant goldenrod of Atlantic and Gulf Coast dunes, salt marshes, and coastal strand habitats — its exceptional salt spray and saline soil tolerance makes it the goldenrod of choice for coastal gardens and living shoreline plantings where other goldenrods would fail. The large, arching sprays of golden-yellow flowers appear from late summer through fall and are among the most important late-season nectar sources on the coastal Atlantic and Gulf Coast, supporting migrating monarchs, native bees, and dozens of butterfly species during their southward migration. Unlike many goldenrod species that spread aggressively by rhizomes to the point of being weedy, seaside goldenrod spreads more moderately and is well-behaved in garden borders; its large, fleshy, salt-tolerant leaves also distinguish it from most other goldenrods. Combine with sea lavender, salt marsh asters, and ornamental grasses in coastal pollinator gardens for a stunning fall display that also provides critical wildlife habitat.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring or fall; division is easily done by digging and separating rooted stems.
directions: Plant in any well-drained soil — even poor, gravelly soil suits sedum perfectly; overly rich or moist soil causes floppy, open-centered plants. Tall sedums may flop in midseason; pinch once in late spring or grow through a support ring to keep them upright. Leave the russet seedheads standing through winter as they are attractive and provide structure in the dormant garden. Divide every 3–4 years in spring by separating the fleshy crown sections.

plant_note: Direct sow after all danger of frost; zinnias grow quickly in warm soil.
directions: This distinguished zinnia produces elegant double three- to five-inch blooms in a dreamy, rare salmon-pink on strong, long stems. Sow directly into warm soil after the last frost for fast growth. Cut and deadhead often to keep the plants flowering all summer. Full sun and airflow between plants help prevent powdery mildew. The edible petals are loved by pollinators and brighten the cutting garden.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in average soil; overly rich soil reduces blooms.
directions: This tall, showy blend produces large three- to four-inch white, pink, rose, and crimson flowers ideal for wildflower plantings and cutting. Sow directly after frost into average, well-drained soil, as lean conditions bring more bloom. Cosmos are drought tolerant and need little attention once up. Full sun keeps the plants upright and flowering; stake in windy sites. An excellent butterfly lure that self-sows for the following year.

plant_note: Direct sow scarified seed in spring or transplant container plants in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry to moderately moist, sandy to loamy soil in full sun to partial shade; sensitive briar has recurved prickles along the stems — handle with care.
directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun — sensitive briar is a fascinating native trailing groundcover of the Gulf Coast and southern prairies, with two remarkable properties: the delicate, fern-like, bipinnate leaves fold closed immediately when touched (thigmonastic movement — the same sensitivity as the tropical 'sensitive plant'), providing an entertaining and educational garden element, and the stems are lined with small, recurved prickles similar to rose thorns. The spherical, bright pink pompom flowers in late spring and summer are delightful and attract native bees. It spreads flat along the ground to cover dry, rocky, or sandy open areas. As a legume, it fixes nitrogen from the air, improving soil fertility for neighboring plants. An outstanding native for dry, sunny slopes, caliche hillsides, and disturbed areas where its drought tolerance and sprawling habit control erosion while providing summer bloom and ecological interest.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; sensitive fern spreads vigorously by rhizome in moist conditions and can colonize large areas quickly — site it where spreading is welcome.
directions: Plant in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun in consistently moist conditions; sensitive fern is one of the most distinctive and bold native ferns of eastern North America, producing large, broadly triangular, undivided-looking fronds with wide, rounded lobes that give it an unusually coarse texture compared to the typically finely cut fern frond. It gets its name from its extreme sensitivity to frost — the fronds collapse at the first light freeze in fall. A separate fertile frond with clusters of bead-like sporangia remains standing through winter and is an attractive structural element in dried arrangements. It spreads readily by rhizome in moist conditions and is excellent for naturalizing wet, shaded areas along stream banks, pond margins, and bog gardens. The coarse, bold foliage provides excellent contrast with fine-textured shade plants.

plant_note: Start indoors; nick or soak the hard seed coat and keep warm. Transplant after frost.
directions: This novelty rapidly folds its fern-like leaves when touched, exposing spines as a defense, delighting children and adults alike. Nick or soak the hard seed and start it indoors in warmth, then grow on as a houseplant or transplant outdoors after frost. Give it bright light, full sun to part shade, and consistently moist, well-drained soil. It is frost tender and typically grown as an annual or potted plant outside the tropics. Handle sparingly to enjoy repeated leaf-folding without stressing the plant.

plant_note: Divide clumps in fall and plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade. Sessile-leaf Bellwort tolerates somewhat drier conditions than Uvularia grandiflora and adapts well to a wider range of shade garden soils.
directions: Sessile-leaf Bellwort is a delicate native woodland wildflower, native to moist to mesic deciduous forests and forest edges from New Brunswick and Manitoba south to Georgia and Oklahoma, spreading slowly by rhizome to form loose, airy colonies in the shade garden. Smaller and more delicate than Large-flowered Bellwort (Uvularia grandiflora), it produces pale cream-yellow, narrowly bell-shaped pendant flowers in April and May on slender, branching stems; the leaves are sessile (non-clasping) rather than perfoliate, distinguishing this species from its cousin. An easy-going, adaptable native wildflower that tolerates slightly drier soil than U. grandiflora, Sessile-leaf Bellwort is a good choice for gardeners working with thinner, drier woodland soils where other spring wildflowers struggle. Divide clumps in fall, plant in humus-rich soil, mulch with leaf litter, and allow colonies to naturalize.

plant_note: Direct sow in very early spring or fall; seed germinates best in cool soil and darkness.
directions: Tall spikes of light and dark blue blossoms make a compelling backdrop for beds and bouquets and reseed readily. Sow seed directly in very early spring or in fall, as it needs cool soil and darkness to germinate and dislikes transplanting. Larkspur blooms in the cool weather of late spring and early summer. Provide full sun and well-drained soil, and stake in exposed sites. All parts are toxic if eaten, so site it away from grazing animals.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring or fall; surface sow as seed needs light to germinate.
directions: This spectacular perennial sends up multiple graceful spikes of white, pink, deep rose, violet, and purple flowers that attract butterflies. Sow seed in spring or fall, pressing it onto the soil surface since it needs light to germinate. It is fast growing and fairly drought tolerant, excelling in dry landscape gardens or mass plantings. Give it full sun and sharply drained soil. Cut spent spikes to encourage rebloom and use the flowers in arrangements.

plant_note: Direct sow fresh seeds in fall in moist to moderately dry, sandy to loamy, acidic, well-drained soil in full sun; Sharp Blazingstar tolerates somewhat moister soils than many blazingstars and thrives in the coastal prairie and savanna conditions of Texas and Louisiana where periodic seasonal flooding alternates with drought.
directions: Sharp Blazingstar is a native perennial of the Gulf Coast prairies, savannas, and open meadows of Texas and Louisiana, producing dense spikes of pinkish-purple flower heads from late summer through fall that provide important nectar for migrating monarchs and native bees during the critical fall migration period along the Gulf Coast. Unlike many blazingstars that prefer dry, xeric conditions, Sharp Blazingstar tolerates moist prairie soils and the seasonal flooding that characterizes the Gulf Coast prairie ecosystem, making it suitable for a broader range of garden conditions than its dry-country relatives. The tight, upright spike of densely packed flower heads is similar in appearance to Prairie Blazingstar but more compact in stature. Direct sow fresh seeds in fall in moist to moderately dry, sandy to loamy, acidic soil in full sun; corms develop slowly but become very drought-tolerant once established in appropriate open conditions.

plant_note: Plant fibrous root divisions in fall in moist, well-drained, slightly to moderately calcareous, humus-rich, stony woodland soil in deep shade. Sharp-Lobed Hepatica prefers richer, more base-rich soils than Round-Lobed Hepatica. Do not plant deeply — the crown should be at or just below the soil surface.
directions: Sharp-Lobed Hepatica is one of the first native wildflowers to bloom in the eastern woodlands, producing delicate white, pink, blue, or lavender flowers on slender, hairy stems in late winter to early spring — often while frost still covers the ground — before the deciduous forest canopy has begun to leaf out. The three-lobed, leathery leaves remain green through the winter as an evergreen basal rosette, often acquiring beautiful reddish-purple tones in the cold months; distinguished from Round-Lobed Hepatica by the pointed (acute) tips of its three leaf lobes, it prefers slightly richer, more alkaline woodland soils with good calcium content. A long-lived, slow-growing wildflower of exceptional garden character, Sharp-Lobed Hepatica is best established from fall-planted divisions of existing clumps rather than from seed, which requires 2–3 years of stratification cycles before germination. Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly calcareous, humus-rich soil with some grit or stone in deep shade under deciduous trees.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger passes.
directions: Sow seeds on the surface of moist potting mix and keep at 60–70°F for germination; transplant after the last frost into well-drained soil. Shasta daisies often bloom lightly the first year from seed and fully the second year — named cultivars from division or plugs bloom more reliably in year 1. Deadhead spent flowers to encourage repeat blooming from midsummer into fall. Divide clumps every 2–3 years in spring, discarding the woody center and replanting vigorous outer sections.

plant_note: Surface-sow and press in; needs light to germinate. Usually blooms the second year.
directions: Press seed onto the soil surface without burying it, since light aids germination. This classic perennial produces crisp white petals around bright yellow centers on strong stems, and the flowers are edible. Grow in full sun and well-drained soil, deadheading regularly to extend the summer bloom. Divide crowded clumps every two to three years in spring to keep plants healthy and floriferous.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring after soil has warmed to at least 65°F; space clumps 36–48 inches apart. In zone 8, mulch heavily in winter to protect the rhizome from killing frosts.
directions: Plant in rich, moist, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; shell ginger is a stunning tropical-looking perennial for Gulf Coast gardens, growing rapidly to 6–10 feet with broad, fragrant, cane-like stems and large lance-shaped leaves. In late spring and summer, mature canes produce cascading clusters of porcelain-white flowers with yellow and pink markings that resemble shells before opening — extraordinarily fragrant and beautiful. Only second-year canes flower; cut spent canes to the ground after bloom to make room for new growth. The variegated form ('Variegata') has gold-striped foliage and is slightly smaller. Plants freeze to the ground in zone 8 frosts but re-emerge from hardy rhizomes in spring with deep mulch protection.

plant_note: Direct sow fresh acorns in fall at 1–2 inch depth in well-drained, moderately fertile, acidic to neutral, loamy to clay-loam soil in full sun to light shade. Shingle Oak is more adaptable to clay and moderately compacted urban soils than many oaks and makes an excellent large shade tree for challenging sites.
directions: Shingle Oak is a distinctive and underappreciated native oak of the eastern United States from New England south to Georgia and west to Kansas, immediately recognizable among oaks by its entire, unlobed leaves resembling those of a water beech — a surprising feature for an oak — making it an excellent conversation piece in any landscape. The dried leaves persist on the branches well into winter in a quality called marcescence, providing landscape texture and winter interest, while the abundant small acorns are a reliable annual mast crop for wood ducks, turkeys, white-tailed deer, and numerous songbirds. An adaptable and durable large shade tree for urban and suburban landscapes, Shingle Oak tolerates heavier clay soils and greater pH variability than many oaks, and develops a graceful, broadly pyramidal to rounded crown at maturity with modest fall color ranging from yellow to russet brown. Direct sow fresh acorns in fall in well-drained, moderately fertile, acidic to neutral soil in full sun to light shade.

plant_note: Surface sow on cool soil; do not cover the fine seed.
directions: Scatter the tiny Shirley poppy seed on the surface of cool, moist soil in very early spring, or sow in fall in mild regions. Do not cover the seed, which needs light and cold to germinate well. The delicate crepe-paper flowers in pink, red, and white are classic cottage and meadow plants. Poppies resent transplanting, so always direct sow, and let some pods ripen for self-seeding.

plant_note: Surface sow in early spring or fall; seed needs light to germinate.
directions: Scatter the tiny seed on the soil surface in early spring or fall, pressing lightly but not covering since it needs light to sprout. This 1880s heirloom bears delicate two-to-three-inch blossoms on slender, nodding stems that dance in the breeze. Poppies resent transplanting, so always direct sow where they are to grow and thin gently. As a carefree annual it readily self-sows, making it ideal for naturalized areas and meadow plantings. Water sparingly once established, as these plants tolerate lean, dry conditions.

common_name: Shock Wave Purple Tie Dye Petunia
plant_note: Start indoors early and surface sow; seed needs light. Transplant after frost.
directions: This petite petunia bears one-and-a-half to two-inch variegated purple flowers with tie-dye patterns that shift with temperature and light, so no two blooms match. Start the tiny seed indoors eight to ten weeks before the last frost, pressing it onto the surface for light. Transplant after frost into full sun and well-drained soil. Its compact, mounded, spreading habit makes it ideal for hanging baskets and containers. Feed regularly and pinch to keep it full and blooming all season.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost; sunflowers grow best sown where they will bloom.
directions: Yellow-tipped, bronze-red petals and contrasting purple-tinged stems make this well-branched sunflower a striking cut flower. Sow seed directly into warm soil after frost, spacing closer for longer stems suited to bouquets. The blooms are pollenless, so they will not shed on tabletops. Give the plants full sun and steady moisture. The edible petals add a bittersweet pop of color to salads.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall in the garden for natural cold stratification; seeds require at least 60 days of cold moist conditions before they will germinate in spring. Division of established crowns is the faster propagation method.
directions: Sow seed in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; the plant is a spring ephemeral that blooms in early spring with distinctive nodding clusters of swept-back petals in white to pink with a yellow ring at the center. After flowering and setting seed in late spring, the foliage yellows and the plant goes fully dormant by midsummer — mark the location well and interplant with later-emerging shade perennials. Plants grown from seed typically require 2–3 years to produce their first bloom. Naturalize in groups of a dozen or more for the most impactful display in a woodland garden.

plant_note: Plant divisions or container plants in fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade; shooting star is a spring ephemeral that dies back completely by early summer — mark planting location to avoid disturbing the dormant roots during summer dormancy.
directions: Plant in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade — shooting star is one of the most spectacular native spring wildflowers, producing umbels of 3–20 nodding flowers whose swept-back petals (white to lavender to deep magenta) and prominent yellow anther cone pointing downward create an unmistakable shooting-star silhouette. The flowers are buzz-pollinated exclusively by bumblebees, which grip the anther cone and vibrate at a specific frequency to release the pollen. A spring ephemeral of open woodland, prairies, and moist slopes, shooting star goes completely dormant by early summer and must be marked to avoid disturbing the underground corms. It grows slowly and persists for decades in appropriate conditions. Requires good drainage despite needing moisture in spring — summer-wet conditions cause rot. Outstanding for naturalizing in moist, open woodland gardens with hostas, ferns, and other spring wildflowers.

plant_note: Plant nursery transplants in fall in well-drained, acidic, sandy to loamy soil in full sun. Shortleaf Pine adapts to a wide range of well-drained, acidic soils from dry sand to moist clay loam, and is one of the most widely distributed southern pines — tolerating both droughty uplands and moderately moist conditions.
directions: Shortleaf Pine is one of the most widely distributed southern pines, native to 22 states from New York and New Jersey south to Florida and west to Texas and Oklahoma, growing in dry to moderately moist, acidic, well-drained to somewhat poorly drained soils across a remarkable variety of upland and foothill conditions — a broader soil and climate tolerance than almost any other southern pine. Its most distinctive adaptive trait is the ability to regenerate from dormant basal buds (called 'root sprouts') after top-killing fire or mechanical damage — a trait shared with longleaf pine but uncommon among other pines — that allows it to recover from disturbance without relying solely on seeding. Shortleaf Pine has been a major timber species in the South for over 200 years and is commercially important for lumber and pulpwood; in restoration contexts it is a key species for re-establishing mixed pine-hardwood forests on former agricultural and cutover lands in the Ozarks and upper Gulf Coastal Plain. Grow in full sun in any well-drained, acidic soil; it is more drought-tolerant than most other southern pines and one of the hardiest (to zone 5) of the typically warm-climate southern pines.

plant_note: Surface-sow; needs light to germinate. Spreads vigorously by runners.
directions: Surface-sow the seed and press it in lightly, as light encourages germination. Commonly called pink ladies, this spreading native perennial opens delicate pink, cup-shaped flowers over a long season. It thrives on neglect in full sun and dry, poor soil, spreading readily by runners to cover slopes and difficult sites. Give it room to roam or contain it, and expect a carefree groundcover loved by bees and butterflies.

plant_note: Plant only nursery-propagated, tissue-cultured specimens in fall in moist to wet, neutral to slightly alkaline, calcium-rich, humus-rich soil in partial shade; showy lady's slipper prefers cooler, wetter conditions and neutral to basic pH (pH 5.5–7.5) unlike most native orchids that need acidic soil.
directions: Showy lady's slipper is the largest and most spectacularly beautiful of all North American native orchids — the official state wildflower of Minnesota — with large, pure white upper petals surrounding a dramatically inflated, rosy-pink to magenta pouch that is rivaled in size only by tropical orchids. Unlike most eastern orchids that need acidic soil, showy lady's slipper prefers neutral to slightly alkaline, calcium-rich soils in cool, moist, shaded swamp forests, tamarack bogs, and calcareous seeps — a habitat preference that makes it more restrictive than yellow lady's slipper but potentially suitable for gardens with moist, organic, neutral soil in zones 2–7. Individual plants are remarkably long-lived and slow-growing, capable of persisting in the same location for 50–100 years when undisturbed, slowly expanding into multi-stemmed clumps of increasing grandeur. As with all native terrestrial orchids, purchase only tissue-cultured nursery propagated specimens — wild collection of any Cypripedium is illegal in most states and ecologically devastating. Note: handling the foliage can cause skin irritation in sensitive individuals.

plant_note: Cold-moist stratify seed for 30 days; often blooms the second year.
directions: Cold-stratify the seed for about a month before sowing in spring for reliable germination. This native perennial produces large, fragrant clusters of soft pink star-shaped flowers on upright stems with broad, fuzzy leaves. Plant in full sun and well-drained soil where it tolerates drought and spreads gradually by rhizomes. It is a vital host for Monarch and Queen butterfly caterpillars, so keep it pesticide-free.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall, or cold stratify seed 4-6 weeks before spring sowing.
directions: This robust heirloom perennial hosts monarch butterflies and draws many pollinators with clusters of star-shaped blooms above green to silvery foliage. Sow seed in fall or cold stratify it for several weeks before spring sowing to break dormancy. It grows best in full sun and well-drained soil and is drought tolerant once established. Its deep roots dislike transplanting, so sow it in place. Leave seed pods to spread the patch and support monarch populations.

plant_note: Sow shallowly and cover; seed germinates best in darkness.
directions: Sow seed lightly covered, since this annual phlox germinates best in darkness. It produces a colorful blend of five-petaled, lightly fragrant blooms on low, spreading plants. Grow in full sun and well-drained soil, sowing in early spring or fall in mild climates for the best bloom. Deadhead to prolong flowering and enjoy the bright carpets it forms in beds, borders, and containers.

plant_note: Direct sow seeds in fall in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich woodland soil in partial to full shade under deciduous trees. Showy Skullcap performs best in the cool, dappled shade of eastern deciduous forest understories — it is one of the few native wildflowers that produces exceptional bloom in moderate to deep shade.
directions: Showy Skullcap is an outstanding native woodland wildflower of the central Appalachians and adjacent regions, producing some of the most vivid blue-violet flowers of any shade-garden native from May through early June — a relative rarity among plants that genuinely thrive in moderate to deep deciduous forest shade. The clear blue, two-lipped tubular flowers are produced in terminal and axillary clusters on 12–24 inch stems and are a significant nectar source for bumblebees and other long-tongued native bees that visit the forest understory in spring. Native to moist, fertile, slightly acidic, humus-rich soils of deciduous woodlands in the central Appalachian region, Showy Skullcap is an ideal native perennial for the shaded garden border, woodland path edge, or native plant shade garden where deep blue flowers in late spring fill a critical color gap. Direct sow seeds in fall in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich woodland soil in partial to full shade; established plants are long-lived and slowly colonize appropriate shaded spots.

plant_note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; shrimp plant grows rapidly and roots easily from stem cuttings — pinch tips frequently during the first few months to encourage branching and a fuller habit before allowing it to bloom.
directions: Plant in partial shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil; shrimp plant is one of the most charming and long-blooming perennials for shaded Gulf Coast gardens, named for the distinctive overlapping reddish-brown bracts (modified leaves) that form arching, drooping spikes 3–4 inches long, closely resembling a cooked shrimp — from the tip of each bract emerges a delicate white two-lipped flower. Bloom is essentially continuous from spring through frost and often persists in warm winters. It performs equally well in light shade or partial sun, making it versatile for difficult spots under the canopy of large trees. Hummingbirds visit the flowers readily. A yellow-bracted form ('Fruit Cocktail' or 'Yellow Queen') provides the same long bloom season in a lighter color. Pinch the plant back after bloom flushes to keep it compact and stimulate fresh growth. Propagate from stem cuttings, which root readily in moist soil.

plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs in fall in average, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; shrubby St. John's wort is adaptable to moist or dry soils and tolerates periodic light flooding better than golden St. John's wort.
directions: Shrubby St. John's wort is one of the most adaptable and cold-hardy native flowering shrubs of eastern North America — a rounded, multi-stemmed deciduous shrub that produces bright, butter-yellow five-petaled flowers with showy stamens prolifically in July and August, when the garden's early-summer bloom has faded and few other native shrubs are at peak flower. It grows naturally in a wider range of moisture conditions than most native shrubby Hypericums, tolerating both dry rocky slopes and moist, seasonally wet bottomland edges, making it one of the more versatile native shrubs for mixed landscape situations. Native bees — particularly specialist pollen collectors in the family Melittidae and Apidae — are the primary pollinators, attracted by the abundant pollen (this genus provides no nectar); the persistent, star-shaped seed capsules are ornamental through winter and consumed by some birds. An excellent choice for naturalistic shrub borders, rain garden edges, and native landscape plantings in the cold northern half of its range where golden St. John's wort is less reliably hardy.

plant_note: Plant fresh acorns in fall or container trees in early spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral, deep soil in full sun; Shumard oak establishes faster than most oaks and tolerates brief flooding, making it an excellent street and yard tree in the South.
directions: Plant fresh acorns in fall or container trees in early spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral, deep soil in full sun; Shumard oak is the premier large native oak of the Gulf Coast and Deep South, growing faster than most oaks (2–3 feet per year when young) while developing into a large, long-lived specimen tree with spectacular deep red-to-scarlet fall color — one of the best oaks for fall color in zone 7–9 where many trees fail to color reliably. It tolerates brief flooding and clay soils better than many oaks, making it an excellent choice for urban and suburban landscapes in the South. The deeply lobed leaves with pointed tips are similar to pin oak but without the drooping lower branches. The acorns provide important fall food for turkeys, deer, and numerous bird species. An outstanding Gulf Coast street tree, shade tree, and wildlife tree.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring as soon as plants can be purchased; also self-seeds moderately in moist, shaded sites.
directions: Plant in partial to full shade in cool, humus-rich, moist but well-drained soil; brunnera is one of the best foliage plants for shade, particularly the silver-variegated cultivars like 'Jack Frost.' Delicate sprays of forget-me-not blue flowers appear in early to mid-spring above the large, heart-shaped leaves. In hot or dry conditions the leaves may scorch — consistent moisture and afternoon shade are essential in warmer climates. Divide clumps every 4–5 years in early spring; variegated cultivars must be divided rather than grown from seed to maintain their markings.

plant_note: Plant divisions in early spring as growth begins, or in late summer after flowering; set rhizomes just below soil surface.
directions: Plant rhizomes just 1–2 in below the soil surface in moisture-retentive, slightly acidic soil; unlike bearded iris, Siberian iris prefers consistently moist conditions and tolerates wet spots. Graceful, narrow foliage forms attractive clumps all season, topped by elegant blue, purple, or white flowers in early summer. Siberian iris are very long-lived and rarely need dividing — only do so when the center of the clump dies out or blooming diminishes, typically every 5–8 years. Division is best done immediately after flowering or in early fall; replant promptly and water well.

plant_note: Plant small bulbs in fall; naturalizes in lawns.
directions: Plant Siberian squill bulbs in fall about three inches deep in well-drained soil in sun to partial shade. The nodding, intensely blue star-shaped flowers appear very early in spring and provide crucial forage for early bees. It naturalizes freely in lawns and under deciduous trees, forming sheets of blue over the years. Plant in generous drifts and leave the foliage to ripen so the colony can expand.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring after last frost in full sun and lean, well-drained soil; do not fertilize as this is a plant of lean, poor soils.
directions: Direct sow in early spring in lean, well-drained, rocky to sandy soil in full sun; side oats grama is the official Texas state grass and one of the finest warm-season native grasses for ornamental use, with slender upright stems bearing distinctive oat-like seed heads all arrayed on one side of the stem in a graceful line. It establishes quickly from seed, tolerates drought and heat extremely well once established, and provides excellent winter structure with its bronze-tan seed heads. Mow or cut back once in late winter before new growth begins. Excellent for low-maintenance meadow plantings, slopes, and bird-supporting landscape borders. The seed is harvested by sparrows, finches, and native buntings.

plant_note: Direct sow on the surface of prepared soil in spring; sideoats grama germinates readily without stratification in warm soil. Also excellent as a fall-dormant sown grass — broadcast seed from October through December for natural spring germination.
directions: Plant in well-drained to dry, sandy or rocky soil in full sun; sideoats grama is the official state grass of Texas and one of the most widely adapted and drought-resistant native grasses in North America. It is called 'sideoats' for its distinctive seed heads, which hang in a row of small oat-like florets all on one side of the arching seed stalks from July through September, creating a charming, delicate appearance in late summer. The deep blue-green foliage turns orange-red in fall. Sideoats is one of the few native grasses that performs reliably in the extreme heat and drought of the Southern Plains, making it invaluable for Texas and Gulf Coast gardens with dry, alkaline soil. An excellent low-maintenance lawn substitute or turf replacement on slopes.

plant_note: Plant divisions in fall or early spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade; Siebold's hosta is one of the larger-leafed hostas — allow generous spacing as clumps can eventually reach 4 feet across.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade — Siebold's hosta is one of the most majestic and widely grown of all hosta species, producing enormous, boldly corrugated, glaucous blue-green to blue-gray leaves up to 18 inches long that give the shade garden a dramatic, tropical-foliage effect. The sluggish pale lavender flowers on low, barely-visible scapes in early summer are secondary to the spectacular foliage. Famous cultivars derived from this species include 'Elegans' (intensely blue, deeply corrugated), 'Frances Williams' (blue with gold-green margins), and 'Big Daddy' (deeply puckered, intensely blue). The waxy, powder-coated leaf surface repels water and resists slug damage somewhat better than thin-leaved hostas. While this species is at its finest in cool-climate zone 4–7 gardens, it grows adequately in zone 8 in deep shade with consistent moisture and limited afternoon sun exposure.

plant_note: Plant container-grown transplants in spring in extremely well-drained, infertile, acidic, sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; Silk Bay will quickly decline in heavy, wet, or overly fertile soils. It is highly susceptible to laurel wilt disease spread by the redbay ambrosia beetle — plant in isolated or protected settings.
directions: Silk Bay is a Florida endemic member of the laurel family, native to the ancient, fire-maintained scrub habitats of the central and southern Florida peninsula, where it grows in pure white sand alongside Scrub Hickory, Florida Rosemary, and scrub oaks in conditions of extreme infertility and drainage. The leaves are smaller and silkier than those of the closely related Red Bay and Swamp Bay, with a distinctive silky pubescence on the undersides; small, dark blue-black drupes produced in summer and fall provide important food for Florida Scrub-Jays, fish crows, and migratory songbirds. Like all native bays in the genus Persea, Silk Bay is highly susceptible to laurel wilt disease, an exotic fungal pathogen spread by the redbay ambrosia beetle that has devastated native bay populations across the Southeast. Plant in extremely well-drained, infertile, acidic, sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; this species demands perfect drainage and will quickly decline in heavy, wet, irrigated, or highly amended soils.

plant_note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in moist, acidic (pH 4.5–6.0), well-drained, humus-rich soil in partial shade with protection from hot afternoon sun. Silky Camellia is slow to establish and resents transplanting; choose the permanent location carefully and amend the soil deeply before planting.
directions: Silky Camellia is widely regarded as one of the most beautiful native shrubs in North America, native to moist, acidic forest understories from Virginia and Tennessee south to Florida and west to Arkansas and Texas, where it blooms in May through July with large, pure white, five-petaled flowers featuring a central boss of deep bluish-purple filaments with yellow anthers — a combination found nowhere else in the native flora and reminiscent of a Japanese camellia. The flowers open sequentially over several weeks and are followed by attractive woody capsules; the fall foliage turns shades of orange and scarlet. Despite its beauty, Silky Camellia is notoriously slow-growing and slow to establish, taking many years to reach blooming size and resenting transplanting once established — but gardeners who invest the time and proper siting conditions are rewarded with a genuinely spectacular flowering shrub. Provide moist, deeply amended, acidic soil in partial shade with protection from hot afternoon sun; mulch heavily and water regularly during the first several years.

plant_note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic, deep soil in full sun to partial shade; Silver Maple is most appropriately planted near permanent water features, in rain gardens, or in low-lying, wet areas where its moisture requirement is naturally met. Avoid planting near sewer lines or foundations — the roots are aggressive in seeking moisture.
directions: Silver Maple is the fastest-growing of the native maples, native to the streambanks, bottomlands, and moist lowlands of eastern North America from New Brunswick to Florida and west to the Dakotas, producing a new flush of growth each season that can exceed 3 feet per year in rich, moist alluvial soil. It is among the first native trees to bloom in late winter (February–March), producing clusters of tiny red flowers on bare branches that are one of the most important early pollen and nectar sources of the year for native bees emerging from winter dormancy. The leaves are deeply lobed with silvery-white undersides that shimmer and flash in the breeze, creating a beautiful display in summer wind storms; fall color is modest — pale yellow to orange. The wood is softer and weaker than other maples and Silver Maple is prone to branch breakage in ice storms, making maintenance planning important; it is most appropriate for wet, low-lying sites near water where its moisture tolerance, fast growth, and wildlife value are assets. Keep away from foundations and sewer lines.

common_name: Silver Ponyfoot (Silver Falls Dichondra, Silver Nickel Vine)
plant_note: Root stem cuttings in moist mix or plant container transplants in spring in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; silver ponyfoot requires excellent drainage and actually thrives in poor, rocky, dry soils where other groundcovers fail. It is sensitive to overwatering and cold below 25°F.
directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, dry soil in full sun; silver ponyfoot (Dichondra argentea) is a stunning silvery native groundcover and container trailing plant, native to dry rocky slopes of south Texas, New Mexico, and Mexico. The small, round, kidney-shaped leaves are densely covered in fine silver hairs that give the plant its brilliant silver color — one of the most intensely silver of any plant, creating a shimmering pool of metallic light in the garden. It is spectacular trailing from containers and hanging baskets, where the silver cascading stems create a waterfall effect. As a groundcover it stays low (2–4 inches) and spreads to cover rocky, dry areas. It thrives in heat and drought, requiring little to no irrigation once established. Excellent for edging, rock gardens, and container combos where its silver tone contrasts beautifully with deep purple or lime-green companions.

plant_note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in well-drained, sandy or rocky, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade. Silver Thatch Palm is highly drought- and salt-tolerant and is excellent for the thin, rocky, alkaline soils of the Florida Keys where other palms struggle.
directions: Silver Thatch Palm is a graceful, slender native palm, native to the rocky, alkaline limestone soils of the Florida Keys, extreme southern Miami-Dade County, and the Caribbean, where it grows in coastal hammocks and pine rocklands as one of the most salt- and drought-tolerant palms in the Florida native flora. Its most distinctive feature is the brilliant silver-white coloration of the undersides of the fan-shaped fronds, which flash dramatically in the breeze and give the tree a shimmering, two-toned effect unlike any other native Florida palm. Slow-growing but long-lived, Silver Thatch Palm produces small white flowers in summer followed by round, dark purple berries consumed by birds; historically the leaves were used for thatching (hence the common name). An outstanding specimen palm for the thin, rocky, alkaline marl and limestone soils of South Florida gardens where faster-growing palms would quickly exhaust the substrate — plant in full sun to partial shade, provide excellent drainage, and supply minimal supplemental water once established.

plant_note: Start indoors and surface sow; seed needs light. Transplant after frost.
directions: Grown for its striking silvery-white, felted foliage, this plant adds contrast and texture as edging, in moon gardens, and as bouquet filler. Start seed indoors eight to ten weeks before the last frost, pressing it onto the surface for light. Transplant after frost into full sun and well-drained soil. It is drought tolerant and may overwinter in mild climates when mulched. If allowed to flower, the yellow blooms attract butterflies.

plant_note: Direct sow seeds in fall in dry to moderately moist, rocky or sandy, acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun. Silverrod is uniquely adapted to the dry, shaded conditions of rocky woodland understories where most goldenrods will not thrive — do not plant in wet, heavy, or overly fertile soil.
directions: Silverrod is the only native goldenrod in eastern North America with white flowers — a distinctive botanical curiosity in a genus that otherwise produces exclusively yellow blooms — and also the most shade-tolerant member of the group, growing in dry to moderately moist, rocky or sandy woodland understories, cliff edges, and open barrens where other goldenrods cannot establish. The wiry stems produce small spikes of creamy-white flowers in late summer and fall that provide nectar for late-season native bees and small butterflies at a time when shade garden options for pollinators are limited. Native to rocky woodlands, sandy barrens, and open forest margins from Quebec south to Georgia, Silverrod is a valuable niche plant for the dry shade garden where its tolerance of drought, poor soil, and low light makes it surprisingly adaptable. Direct sow seeds in fall in dry, rocky or sandy, acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; established plants self-seed modestly and form long-lived clumps without aggressive spreading.

plant_note: Direct sow after danger of frost has passed; nasturtiums resent transplanting.
directions: This blend blooms in single flowers of scarlet, gold, orange, and yellow on long, trailing stems that can be trained up a trellis or fence. Sow the large seeds directly where they are to grow once the soil warms, planting about half an inch deep. Keep the soil lean, as rich conditions favor leaves over flowers. It is drought tolerant once established and thrives in full sun to part shade. Both the peppery blossoms and leaves are edible and add a watercress-like kick to salads.

plant_note: Direct sow fresh seeds in fall in well-drained, acidic, sandy soil in full sun, pressing seeds into the soil surface; germination occurs in spring. Transplant container-grown nursery trees in fall or early spring for best establishment. Slash Pine grows extremely fast and should be given ample space.
directions: Slash Pine is the dominant pine of the Gulf Coast and Florida peninsula, native to the wet flatwoods, mesic pinelands, and pond edges of the southeastern coastal plain from South Carolina to Florida and west to Louisiana, where it forms the foundational canopy of the longleaf pine savanna ecosystem alongside longleaf and pond pines. One of the fastest-growing native pines — typically adding 3–5 feet per year in good conditions — Slash Pine is widely planted for timber, pulpwood, and naval stores (resin/turpentine), and in Florida it is a primary host tree for the red-cockaded woodpecker and the state champion in terms of longleaf pine savanna ecosystem support. Two varieties occur in Florida: var. elliottii (North Florida and coastal plain, tall and fast-growing) and var. densa (South Florida Slash Pine, shorter, denser, more fire-resistant). Plant in full sun in acidic, well-drained to moderately moist sandy soil; it is intolerant of alkaline conditions, compaction, or prolonged drought once young.

plant_note: Direct sow fresh seeds in fall in sandy, well-drained, infertile, acidic soil in full sun. Slender Blazingstar is adapted to the xeric, sandy coastal plain habitats of the Florida peninsula and adjacent states and will not thrive in clay, irrigated, or overly fertile garden conditions.
directions: Slender Blazingstar is a native perennial of the sandy coastal plain, pine flatwoods, and xeric scrub of the Florida peninsula and adjacent southeastern states, producing feathery, slender spikes of pink-purple flower heads on wiry stems in fall that are a critical nectar source for migrating monarch butterflies and native bees traversing the Florida peninsula during their southward fall migration. The narrow, thread-like leaves that cover the slender stems give the plant its common name; the flowers open from top to bottom on the spike, like all Liatris, and are notably fragrant compared to many other blazingstars. Adapted to the poor, sandy, xeric soils of the coastal plain where it often grows in open pine-palmetto flatwoods alongside other fire-adapted wildflowers, Slender Blazingstar requires excellent drainage and periodic burning or mechanical disturbance to maintain its competitive advantage. Direct sow seeds in fall in sandy, infertile, extremely well-drained, acidic soil in full sun; established corms are long-lived and drought-tolerant once settled in appropriate conditions.

plant_note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, deep soil in full sun to partial shade. Slippery Elm is moderately susceptible to Dutch Elm Disease but less so than American Elm; it is adaptable to a wide range of soil conditions including clay, limestone soils, and occasionally flooded areas.
directions: Slippery Elm is a large, somewhat coarser native elm than American Elm, native to rich, moist forest slopes, bottomlands, and upland woods throughout eastern North America from Quebec to Florida and west to the Dakotas, named for the distinctive slippery, mucilaginous quality of its inner bark, which has been used medicinally for centuries as a demulcent (soothing agent) for sore throats, coughs, and gastrointestinal inflammation — the FDA has recognized the inner bark as a safe and effective over-the-counter demulcent. The rough, sandpaper-textured leaves are distinctive and diagnostic for the species; the reddish, hairy leaf buds in late winter are among the first signs of life in the spring forest. While moderately susceptible to Dutch Elm Disease, Slippery Elm is adaptable to a wider range of soil conditions than American Elm, tolerating clay, limestone, and occasionally wet soils. Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; the coarse texture and broad shade make it a valuable native alternative to cultivated elms.

plant_note: Cold-moist stratify seed for 30 days; often blooms the second year.
directions: Give seed a month of cold-moist stratification, then sow in spring. This dependable native perennial bears clouds of small lavender-blue, daisy-like flowers in late summer and fall. Plant in full sun and average, well-drained soil where its smooth blue-green foliage resists disease. It provides critical late-season nectar for bees and migrating butterflies as other flowers fade.

plant_note: Direct sow cold-stratified seed in fall in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, rocky to gravelly, thin soil in full sun to partial shade; smooth coneflower is a Federally Threatened species adapted to disturbed, thin-soil openings in cedar-hardwood woodland — it requires good drainage and open mineral soil.
directions: Sow cold-stratified seed in fall in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, rocky to gravelly soil in full sun to partial shade — smooth coneflower is a Federally Threatened native coneflower of the Appalachian piedmont and Blue Ridge foothills, declining primarily due to habitat loss and canopy closure over its characteristic thin-soil, semi-open woodland and roadside habitats. It is distinguished from the more common purple coneflower (E. purpurea) by its smooth (not rough) stems and leaves and its adaptation to disturbed, thin, rocky soils — often in partially shaded situations unlike most other Echinacea. The pale to deep rose-purple ray flowers and prominent central cone are characteristic of the genus and equally attractive to pollinators and beneficial for gardens. Growing this species supports its conservation by maintaining cultivated populations and seed banks. Purchase only nursery-propagated plants from reputable native plant nurseries.

plant_note: Plant plugs or bare-root divisions in spring in regularly flooded tidal flats, muddy marsh edges, or constructed tidal wetlands; smooth cordgrass must be planted in the intertidal zone where it is flooded by saltwater twice daily — it will not persist in fresh water or fully upland sites.
directions: Smooth cordgrass (formerly Spartina alterniflora) is the dominant grass of tidal salt marshes along the Atlantic and Gulf coasts of North America — arguably the single most ecologically important native grass on the eastern seaboard, as it forms the structural foundation of the salt marsh ecosystem. Dense monocultures of smooth cordgrass trap sediment, reduce shoreline erosion, export enormous amounts of organic material (detritus) that feeds estuarine food webs, provide nesting habitat for clapper rails, marsh wrens, and seaside sparrows, and serve as the primary nursery habitat for shrimp, blue crab, flounder, and dozens of commercially important fish species. In coastal restoration projects, smooth cordgrass is the primary plant used to revegetate denuded tidal mudflats, eroding shorelines, and disturbed salt marsh: it establishes from plugs or bare-root divisions within a single growing season and spreads by rhizomes to form self-sustaining colonies. Use for tidal marsh restoration, living shoreline projects, and coastal erosion control where intertidal conditions are present.

plant_note: Plant container shrubs in spring in moist, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; this hydrangea blooms on new wood — cut it back to 12 inches in late winter for the largest, most vigorous flowers.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; smooth hydrangea is the native parent of the enormously popular cultivars 'Annabelle' and 'Incrediball', which have transformed shade gardens with their enormous round white flower heads up to 12 inches across. Unlike oakleaf hydrangea, smooth hydrangea blooms on new wood — cut all stems to 12–18 inches in late winter for the strongest, most floriferous performance. It grows naturally along stream banks, woodland edges, and cliff faces across the eastern US and tolerates more shade and competition than most shrubs. If flower heads become too heavy for stems, cut back less severely the following year. Pollinators of many species use the tiny fertile flowers at the center of the flower heads.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic, sandy to loamy soil in full sun to partial shade; smooth penstemon adapts to moist woodland edges as well as dry upland sites in the Southeast, making it more versatile than most penstemons for Gulf Coast garden conditions.
directions: Smooth penstemon is the most widely distributed native penstemon in the southeastern United States, found in open woodland edges, roadside banks, and moist to dry uplands from Virginia to Florida and west to Texas — it is one of the more adaptable penstemons for Gulf Coast gardens, tolerating the heat and humidity of the Deep South better than many western species that struggle in the muggy Southeast. The whorled, tubular lavender-to-white flowers appear in April through June and are eagerly worked by large native bumblebees, hummingbirds, and long-tongued native bees; the plant's smooth, glossy leaves (the 'laevigatus' means smooth) and upright flower spikes provide attractive structure in native garden borders. It adapts to both moist woodland edges with partial shade and dry, sunny upland slopes — a versatility unusual among penstemons that makes it a reliable choice for varied garden conditions in the Southeast. Self-seeds modestly and forms small colonies over time; divide crowded clumps in fall every 3–4 years.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in early fall, 4 in deep, in moist but well-drained soil; ideal for naturalizing in damp meadows.
directions: Plant bulbs in early fall, 4 in deep and 3–4 in apart, in organically rich, moisture-retentive but not waterlogged soil; perfect for damp meadow or pond-edge plantings. The nodding, checkerboard-patterned bells in deep purple, white, or pink appear in mid-spring and are among the most intricately patterned flowers in the spring garden. Naturalizes freely in damp grassland — avoid mowing until foliage has died back completely in early summer. Once established the bulbs multiply steadily; divide congested clumps in early fall.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant 2–4 weeks before last frost as plants tolerate light frost.
directions: Press seed onto the surface of moist starting mix and do not cover — seed needs light to germinate. Pinch seedlings once to promote multiple spikes. Transplant to the garden a few weeks before the last frost, as snapdragons thrive in cool conditions and can tolerate a light freeze. After the primary spike blooms, cut it off to encourage a second flush from side branches.

plant_note: Plant tubers after frost has passed and soil is warm.
directions: Snoho Doris is a tall, productive dahlia bearing salmon-coral blooms with uniquely patterned petals on very long, easy-to-harvest stems. Plant tubers 4-6 inches deep in well-drained soil after the last frost, and provide light support for the tall plants. Pinch the central shoot early to encourage more branching and stems. Cut flowers frequently to encourage continued blooming into fall. A great choice for beginning dahlia growers thanks to its reliable production.

plant_note: Plant tubers after the last spring frost when soil warms.
directions: Snoho Jojo is a productive, short-statured dahlia with smoky mauve blooms carried on long stems that are excellent for cutting. Plant tubers 4-6 inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil once frost danger passes. The compact plant produces cut-worthy stems from the start of the season. Deadhead and cut regularly to keep blooms coming through fall. In cold climates, lift the tubers before the first hard freeze and store them in a cool, dry place.

plant_note: Plant corms in fall; naturalizes and resists squirrels.
directions: Plant snow crocus corms in fall about three inches deep in well-drained soil and full to partial sun. This early species crocus naturalizes rapidly in lawns and borders and is more squirrel-resistant than large Dutch crocus. The slender lavender flowers are among the first blooms of late winter, providing vital early forage for bees. Leave the foliage undisturbed until it withers to allow the corms to multiply.

plant_note: Direct sow around the last frost; euphorbia dislikes root disturbance.
directions: Crisp white-edged bracts look hand-painted and give a luminous, frosted glow even in summer heat, brightening beds and contrasting with deeper greens. Sow seed directly around the last frost, since this euphorbia resents transplanting. It thrives in full sun to part shade and average, well-drained soil and tolerates heat and drought. Wear gloves, as the milky sap can irritate skin and eyes. The variegated foliage makes a fine cut accent when the stem ends are seared.

plant_note: Plant container-grown divisions or transplants in fall in average to dry, well-drained to moist, alkaline or acidic soil in partial shade; snowberry is one of the most shade-tolerant of all native fruiting shrubs and thrives under forest canopy where other fruiting shrubs fail.
directions: Snowberry is a low-growing, thicket-forming native shrub of North American forests and woodland edges, producing the remarkable clusters of pure white, waxy berries (technically drupes) in fall that gave the plant its name — the berries are striking against the bare winter stems, remaining on the plant through November and December as one of the last fruits visible in the winter woodland. The berries are technically mildly toxic to humans (containing saponins) but are consumed by birds including ruffed grouse, pheasants, and robins, as well as by black bears and other mammals; they were used medicinally by several indigenous tribes. Snowberry spreads freely by stolons to form dense, erosion-controlling thickets — excellent for stabilizing slopes and difficult, shaded problem areas where other shrubs struggle to establish. The small, pink tubular flowers in summer are a critical nectar source for hummingbirds and are also visited by native bees; the twinberry hummingbird (Calypte anna) in the west is particularly associated with Symphoricarpos species as a nectar plant.

plant_note: Plant small bulbs in fall, 3 in deep; alternatively plant 'in the green' (just after bloom) for better establishment.
directions: Plant bulbs 3 in deep in moisture-retentive, humus-rich soil under deciduous trees or in a shaded border; snowdrops are one of the first bulbs to bloom, often in January or February. They establish most successfully when transplanted 'in the green' — lifted and divided just after blooming while foliage is still active. Clumps multiply steadily and benefit from division every 3–4 years right after flowering to prevent overcrowding. Allow foliage to die back naturally; avoid mowing naturalised areas until leaves have fully yellowed.

plant_note: Direct sow around the last frost, or start indoors 4-6 weeks early.
directions: Delicate ivory pincushion blooms with frilled petals appear continuously from early summer to fall on sturdy two-foot stems ideal for cutting. Sow seed directly around the last frost or start indoors a few weeks early. Provide full sun and well-drained soil and deadhead often to keep the honey-scented flowers coming. Bees and butterflies are drawn to the blooms. The strong stems make it a reliable cut-flower workhorse.

plant_note: Plant offsets in well-drained, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic to slightly alkaline, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun to partial shade; soft agave is the most shade-tolerant of the commonly grown agaves — it actually performs better in partial shade in hot climates, where full sun can bleach the leaves.
directions: Plant offsets in well-drained, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun to partial shade — soft agave is unique among agaves in having spineless, flexible, blue-gray to pale green leaves without a terminal spine, making it the only agave safe to plant near foot traffic, children's play areas, and along walkways. The large, architectural rosettes of softly recurving, powdery blue-gray leaves are among the most beautiful foliage effects in the succulent garden. Unlike most agaves, it tolerates partial shade and actually performs better in dappled light in hot climates where full sun bleaches the gray leaf color to yellow-green. The spectacular arching flower spike (10–15 feet, resembling a giant foxtail) is produced once at maturity, then the parent rosette dies leaving offsets. Cold hardiness is limited to about 26–28°F; excellent for zone 9–11 landscapes and large containers in cooler zones.

plant_note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; soft shield fern requires good drainage and will not tolerate waterlogged soil, but needs consistent moisture during its growing season.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade — soft shield fern is considered by many gardeners the most beautiful of all the commonly grown shade ferns, producing extraordinarily finely divided fronds with a uniquely soft, feathery texture that distinguishes it from coarser ferns. The fronds are twice-pinnate with neatly rounded, finely crenate pinnules that give the whole frond an almost lacy appearance. Many named cultivars exist with even more divided fronds ('Divisilobum Group'), cristate (crested) tips, or plumose forms. Unlike many Polystichum species, soft shield fern is semi-evergreen in zone 7–9, holding its fronds through winter to provide year-round interest. The soft, mounding clumps combine beautifully with hostas, astilbe, and woodland wildflowers. Excellent for the shaded border in zones 5–9.

plant_note: Plant divisions or plug transplants in spring in wet to consistently moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; softrush is adapted to shallow water, pond margins, rain gardens, and wet ditches and tolerates standing water for extended periods.
directions: Plant divisions in wet to consistently moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil at pond margins, in rain gardens, and in wet ditches in full sun to partial shade — softrush is a graceful, clump-forming native rush of wet areas throughout the Americas, producing upright, round, deep-green, leafless stems that form arching, fountain-like clumps at the water's edge. The inconspicuous brownish flower clusters appear to emerge from the side of the stems (actually from a bract that appears continuous with the stem), adding subtle texture in summer. Softrush is a critical habitat plant for many wetland birds that weave it into nest construction, including marsh wrens and red-winged blackbirds; the seeds are consumed by waterfowl. An outstanding structural plant for rain gardens, bioswales, and natural pond margins that also serves as excellent water quality filtration for stormwater. The curly-leaved cultivar 'Spiralis' (Corkscrew Rush) is popular as an ornamental in containers and wet garden beds.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions 1–2 inches deep in fall in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist, deeply humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; plants take 2–3 years from division to reach full flowering size — space generously as they spread slowly by creeping rhizomes.
directions: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; Solomon's plume is one of the most graceful native plants of the eastern woodland understory, producing arching stems with pairs of broad, smooth, strongly veined leaves, topped in mid-spring with fluffy, creamy-white plumes of tiny fragrant flowers — superficially similar to Solomon's seal but blooming above the leaves rather than below. The flowers are followed in fall by clusters of berries ripening from white with reddish speckles to full red, providing excellent songbird food. Young shoots are edible in spring as a cooked potherb with a mild asparagus-like flavor, and the rhizomes were used medicinally by many eastern woodland tribes. Spreads slowly by creeping rhizome to form attractive, low-maintenance colonies under deciduous trees — an indispensable native for the shaded naturalistic garden.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions horizontally 1–2 inches deep in spring before or just as growth emerges; space 18 inches apart. Fall planting is also effective where winters are mild.
directions: Plant rhizomes in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; Solomon's seal is one of the most graceful native woodland perennials, producing arching stems lined with paired oval leaves and pendant white bell-shaped flowers in late spring that hang below the foliage like small lanterns. Bluish-black berries follow in late summer and are highly attractive to birds. The foliage turns clear yellow in autumn before the stems die back completely. Plants spread slowly by rhizome to form dignified colonies; transplant divisions in spring or fall. Giant Solomon's seal (P. commutatum) grows to 5–6 feet and is equally effective in larger shade gardens.

common_name: Something Old Something New Morning Glory
plant_note: Direct sow after frost when soil is warm; nick or soak the hard seed coat first.
directions: This blend pairs nostalgic favorites like sky-blue Heavenly Blue with newer starred varieties for a mix of climbing morning glory flowers. Nick or soak the hard seed, then sow directly into warm soil after frost near a fence or trellis for the twining vines to climb. Give the plants full sun and average soil; overly rich soil favors foliage over flowers. Water regularly to keep the vines vigorous through summer. Note the seeds are toxic if eaten, so keep them away from children and pets.

plant_note: Direct sow fresh seeds in fall in sandy, well-drained, acidic, infertile soil in full sun; seeds cold-stratify naturally over winter and germinate in spring. Southern Blazingstar thrives in the longleaf pine savanna and coastal flatwood conditions of the southeastern coastal plain.
directions: Southern Blazingstar is a small, slender native perennial of the sandy, open longleaf pine savannas, flatwoods, and coastal plain prairies of the Gulf and South Atlantic states from Georgia to Louisiana, producing dense wand-like spikes of small pink-purple flower heads in early fall that attract monarch butterflies, skippers, and native bees during the critical fall migration period. Less showy than the larger Liatris species but ecologically significant in its native longleaf pine ecosystem, Southern Blazingstar is a component of the diverse herbaceous layer that characterizes intact coastal plain savannas. Direct sow fresh seeds in fall in sandy, infertile, acidic, well-drained soil in full sun; established corms are drought-tolerant and long-lived, forming tight clumps that slowly expand over time in appropriate open, sunny conditions.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in moist to wet, slightly acidic soil at the water's edge or in shallow water up to 4 inches deep; fall planting allows roots to establish before spring bloom.
directions: Plant rhizome divisions in moist to wet, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil at the margin of a pond, stream, or rain garden in partial shade to full sun; southern blue flag is the native wetland iris of the coastal South and Southeast, growing naturally in swamps, freshwater marshes, wet meadows, and pond margins from Virginia to Florida. The violet-blue flowers veined with white and yellow in late spring are extraordinarily beautiful and important for early bumblebees and native bee species large enough to pollinate the structured flowers. The bold, sword-like leaves provide vertical structure in water gardens. Plant where the rhizome can stay moist — unlike bearded irises, this species must not dry out. All parts of the plant are toxic if ingested. A superb companion to pickerel rush, arrowhead, and swamp lily in water garden designs.

plant_note: Plant in fall in zones 8–10 for best establishment before bloom; in zones 7–8 spring planting after bloom is also effective. Set root ball 1–2 inches above the surrounding soil grade and backfill with native soil amended with pine bark.
directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 5.0–5.5) soil amended with pine bark in partial shade to morning sun with afternoon shade; Southern Indica azaleas are the classic massive-flowering shrubs of Deep South gardens, producing spectacular displays of white, pink, red, lavender, or salmon flowers in spring that completely cover the plant. These are large, fast-growing plants — give them full mature-size spacing rather than planting them too close. They are much more heat- and root-rot tolerant than Northern azaleas and are specifically bred for Gulf Coast conditions. Mulch deeply with pine straw to maintain moisture and soil acidity. Prune immediately after flowering ends in spring, as next year's buds set on summer growth. Lace bugs are the primary pest — treat with systemic insecticide in late spring.

plant_note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped trees in late winter or spring in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil in full sun to partial shade; southern magnolia tolerates a range of soils but performs best in fertile, moist, slightly acidic conditions.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil in full sun to partial shade; southern magnolia is the defining icon of the Deep South, a massive evergreen tree with leathery, glossy dark green leaves (with rusty-brown undersides) and enormous, intensely fragrant white flowers up to 12 inches across that open from May through September — among the largest flowers of any native North American tree. The tree is native to the coastal plain from North Carolina to Texas and is the state flower and tree of Mississippi and Louisiana. Compact cultivars like 'Little Gem' (20–25 feet, repeat-blooming) and 'Brackens Brown Beauty' make it accessible for smaller gardens. The cone-like fruit with brilliant red seeds is highly ornamental and provides important food for gray squirrels, turkeys, and songbirds. Plant away from foundations, driveways, and structures to allow for its eventual large size.

plant_note: Divide clumps in spring and plant in moist, well-drained, neutral to slightly alkaline, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade. Southern Maidenhair Fern is unusual among native ferns in preferring neutral to alkaline soil and often grows naturally on moist limestone outcrops, seep ledges, and waterfall spray zones.
directions: Southern Maidenhair Fern is the subtropical maidenhair native to the Gulf Coast and southern United States, native to moist limestone ledges, cave mouths, waterfall spray zones, and moist shaded cliffs from Virginia to Florida and west to California and south through the tropics, where it grows in the constant moisture of seeping or spray-wetted rock faces in deep shade. Its delicate, fan-shaped, bright green leaflets on arching, wiry black stems make it one of the most graceful and ornamental of all ferns; in zones 8–11 it is semi-evergreen to evergreen in the garden, providing year-round texture in the deep shade. Unlike most native ferns, Southern Maidenhair Fern prefers neutral to slightly alkaline soil and is naturally found on limestone substrates — amend planting areas with crushed limestone or dolomite and keep the soil constantly moist. An exceptional fern for shaded water features, seep gardens, or moist rockeries.

plant_note: Plant divisions in fall or early spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; southern meadow rue requires consistently moist, humus-rich soil and partial to deep shade — it does not tolerate drought or full sun in hot climates.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade — southern meadow rue is a rare Appalachian native of moist, rich, shaded mountain woodlands and stream-bank forests in Virginia, North Carolina, and Tennessee, producing the classic meadow-rue effect at an impressive scale: tall (3–6 foot) stems clothed in beautiful, blue-green, columbine-like compound leaves, topped in summer with loose, airy panicles of small white flowers with prominent stamens that create a soft, misty cloud-like effect above the foliage. The delicate, divided foliage is exceptionally ornamental throughout the season. It is one of the taller native shade perennials available, providing dramatic vertical interest in shaded borders, woodland gardens, and moist stream-bank plantings. At the northern edge of zone 8 in moist, deeply shaded, well-drained positions it can succeed, but it is most reliable in zone 4–7 woodland conditions.

plant_note: Plant nursery transplants in fall or early spring in well-drained, sandy or loamy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun. Southern Red Cedar is highly salt-tolerant and is an excellent windbreak and wildlife tree for coastal landscapes in the Gulf states.
directions: Southern Red Cedar is the coastal plain and Gulf Coast counterpart to Eastern Red Cedar (Juniperus virginiana), native to the sandy soils, coastal hammocks, maritime forests, and barrier islands from North Carolina through Florida and around the Gulf Coast to Texas, where it is distinguished from J. virginiana by its more pendulous (weeping) branch tips and overall softer appearance. Salt spray-tolerant, drought-tolerant, and adapted to the sandy, low-nutrient soils of the coastal plain, Southern Red Cedar is an excellent native windbreak, privacy screen, and wildlife tree for Gulf Coast landscapes in zones 7–11. Like Eastern Red Cedar, it produces fleshy blue-green berries (technically seed cones) that are consumed by dozens of bird species including cedar waxwings and bluebirds, and provides dense, year-round nesting cover; plant male and female trees together for consistent berry production. Grow in full sun in any well-drained soil; it is notably more heat-tolerant than Eastern Red Cedar and performs better in the lower South and in coastal situations.

plant_note: Plant divisions or container plants in moist to slightly dry, well-drained to moderately moist, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; southern shield fern is exceptionally heat and drought tolerant for a fern once established and is one of the most reliable shade ferns for Gulf Coast and Deep South gardens.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade — southern shield fern (Thelypteris kunthii) is the workhorse fern of the Gulf Coast and Southeast, naturalized along stream banks, drainage ditches, woodland edges, and the shaded understory of pine and live oak forests from Virginia to Texas. It spreads by creeping rhizomes to form large colonies, deciduous in the upper zones but semi-evergreen in zone 9–10, and tolerates more heat, drought, and root competition from large trees than virtually any other native fern in the South. The fronds are medium green, 2–3 feet tall, with pinnate-pinnatifid division and a soft texture; they emerge in spring and provide lush, layered textural coverage all season. Establish with regular water, then let it fend for itself — a uniquely low-maintenance shade groundcover for difficult dry-shade situations under mature trees. Remove old fronds in late winter before new growth emerges.

plant_note: Plant divisions or transplants in fall or early spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade; southern spiderwort naturalizes freely in shaded gardens by self-seeding and division of the fibrous root clumps.
directions: Southern spiderwort is a shade-tolerant native Tradescantia of the moist, rich, rocky slopes and ravines of the Appalachian foothills and Ozarks to the Gulf Coastal Plain — the species name 'subaspera' means 'somewhat rough,' referring to the slightly hairy stems and leaves that distinguish it from the smooth Ohio spiderwort. Like other spiderworts, it produces vivid three-petaled flowers of brilliant blue-violet with bright yellow stamens that open in the morning and close by midday, continuing to produce new flowers throughout a long season from late spring through summer; each flower lasts only a few hours but the plant produces dozens in succession. It grows well in the partial to moderate shade of mature deciduous trees and is one of the few genuinely shade-tolerant native wildflowers with a long bloom season — most shade perennials bloom only briefly in spring. The clumping habit and bold, strap-like leaves provide attractive foliage interest even when not in flower; divide clumps every 2–3 years in fall to maintain vigor.

plant_note: Plant container shrubs in spring or fall in any well-drained to moist soil in any light condition from full sun to deep shade; this is one of the most adaptable native plants for the South.
directions: Plant in any soil — wet or dry, acidic or alkaline, rich or poor, sun or deep shade — wax myrtle tolerates virtually every condition in zone 7–11 once established and is arguably the Gulf Coast's most versatile and valuable native shrub. The aromatic, evergreen foliage provides year-round screening and privacy; both male and female plants produce tiny waxy blue-gray berries that encrust the branches in fall and winter, providing essential food for yellow-rumped warblers, tree swallows, and many other bird species that can digest the high-fat wax. The root nodules fix atmospheric nitrogen, improving soil over time. Prune heavily at any time to maintain a desired size, or allow it to develop naturally into a multi-trunked small tree 15–20 feet tall. The aromatic leaves have traditional use as an insect repellent.

plant_note: Plant divisions in fall or early spring in moist to moderately dry, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; southern wild ginger is slow to spread but forms attractive evergreen colonies and is more heat and drought tolerant than the northern wild ginger (A. canadense), making it the better choice for zones 7–9.
