directions: Plant divisions in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade — southern wild ginger is an elegant native groundcover of the southeastern US Piedmont and coastal plain, producing attractive, arrowhead-shaped evergreen leaves that are beautifully mottled with silver-gray markings along the veins. Unlike its deciduous northern relative (Asarum canadense), this species holds its foliage through winter in zones 7–9, maintaining year-round groundcover. The curious jug-shaped, brown-purple flowers emerge at soil level in early spring and are pollinated by ground beetles — they are almost entirely hidden by the foliage and go unnoticed, but the effect in leaf is outstanding. The foliage has a mild gingery fragrance when crushed. It spreads slowly by rhizome to form dense mats that suppress weeds under trees and in shaded beds, making it an excellent choice for dry to moderately moist shade in southern gardens.

plant_note: Divide clumps in spring or plant nursery transplants in moist to wet, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade. Southern Wood Fern tolerates seasonal flooding and thrives at the margins of ponds, streams, and swamps throughout the Gulf Coast region.
directions: Southern Wood Fern is the premier native wood fern for Gulf Coast gardens, native to the wet bottomland forests, swamp edges, and rich floodplains of the southeastern coastal plain from South Carolina and Georgia west to Florida, Alabama, Mississippi, and Louisiana, where it grows alongside cypress, tupelo, and swamp chestnut oak in semi-permanently wet soils. Its large, dark green, leathery fronds are semi-evergreen to evergreen in zones 8–10, providing year-round structure in the shade garden, and the plant tolerates seasonal flooding better than almost any other wood fern. An outstanding choice for wet, shaded rain garden depressions, pond margins, and the moist shade under magnolias and live oaks in Gulf Coast landscapes. Divide clumps in spring; plant in heavily amended, consistently moist to wet, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade, and mulch deeply to retain moisture during dry spells.

plant_note: Plant offsets or container specimens in spring in very well-drained, sandy to loamy, dry, slightly acidic to alkaline soil in full sun; handle with extreme caution — the leaf tips are rigid, needle-sharp, and can cause serious eye and skin injury. Spanish bayonet is one of the most salt-tolerant yuccas, thriving in coastal conditions.
directions: Plant offsets or container specimens in very well-drained, dry, sandy to loamy soil in full sun — Spanish bayonet is the most commonly encountered native yucca of the Gulf Coast and Southeast Atlantic coast, forming dramatic, architectural rosettes of rigid, dark green leaves tipped with needle-sharp points (hence 'bayonet') on stems that can grow 6–10 feet tall and develop a trunk-like stem over years. Mature plants are truly striking landscape specimens. Enormous panicles of white, pendulous, edible flowers appear in spring-summer; the large, fleshy fruit capsules and flowers are both edible and were important food sources for Native Americans. Plant well away from foot traffic areas — the razor-sharp leaf tips are a serious safety hazard. Extraordinary drought, salt spray, and wind tolerance makes it one of the best structural plants for coastal, dune, and xeriscape landscapes. Deer-proof, essentially pest-free, and immortal in adapted sandy soils.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall; naturalizes readily in shade.
directions: Plant Spanish bluebell bulbs in fall about four inches deep in moist, well-drained woodland soil in partial shade. The upright stems carry many bell-shaped blue, pink, or white flowers in mid to late spring. It naturalizes vigorously beneath trees and shrubs and can spread aggressively, so site it thoughtfully. Allow the foliage to yellow and die back naturally to feed the bulbs for next season.

plant_note: Soak and nick seed; start indoors 6 weeks early.
directions: Nick and soak the hard seeds overnight, then start Spanish flag indoors six weeks before the last frost. The vigorous vine produces one-sided sprays of tubular flowers that shade from scarlet through orange to creamy yellow. Provide a strong trellis in full sun and fertile, well-drained soil, transplanting after all frost danger passes. Hummingbirds and butterflies flock to the blooms, which continue until first frost.

plant_note: Hang clumps of fresh material on tree branches or structures in any location with bright indirect light and high humidity; Spanish moss requires no soil — it absorbs all moisture and nutrients from the air through tiny scales on its leaves. It grows naturally throughout the Gulf Coast from the Carolinas to Texas.
directions: Hang fresh clumps on tree branches or wire supports in partial shade with good air circulation and humidity; Spanish moss is one of the most distinctive and iconic plants of the American Deep South, the gray-green curtains draping from live oaks and cypresses that define the visual landscape of the Gulf Coast. Despite its name, it is neither a moss nor Spanish — it is an epiphytic bromeliad native from Virginia to Argentina that attaches to tree branches without parasitizing them, absorbing rain, dew, and nutrients from airborne dust through thousands of tiny absorptive scales covering each silvery-gray thread. It provides critical nesting habitat for bats, birds, and jumping spiders, and was historically used as mattress and upholstery stuffing, building insulation, packing material, and for making mud-and-moss wattle walls. It produces tiny, fragrant yellow-green flowers in spring. A piece of living southern heritage for zone 8–11 landscapes.

plant_note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or loamy soil in partial shade to full sun. Spanish Stopper tolerates salt spray, drought, and partial shade and is an excellent evergreen screening shrub for coastal landscapes in zones 9–11.
directions: Spanish Stopper is an attractive evergreen native shrub or small tree, native to the coastal hammocks, scrub, and pinelands of south Florida and the Florida Keys, with a natural range extending through the Caribbean and tropical Americas. Small, oval, aromatic, dark green leaves densely clothe the branches and produce a thick, fine-textured screen when planted as a hedge; clusters of small white flowers in summer are followed by round black berries that are avidly consumed by mockingbirds, robins, and waxwings. Highly adaptable to a range of coastal conditions including salt spray, drought, and partial shade, Spanish Stopper is an excellent foundation plant, hedge, or specimen for south Florida and Gulf Coast landscapes in zones 9b–11. Plant in well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; established plants require little care and tolerate periodic drought with minimal stress.

plant_note: Sow after frost danger passes in warm, well-drained soil.
directions: This South African annual thrives in hot, dry sites and forms a low, spreading carpet of succulent foliage topped with neon 1.5-inch daisy-like blooms. Direct sow into fast-draining soil after the last frost, barely covering the fine seed and keeping it lightly moist until germination. Give plants full sun; flowers open in bright light and close at night or under clouds. Space plants so they can knit together along borders, between pavers, or in rock gardens. Avoid overwatering, as soggy roots quickly rot in this drought-loving species.

plant_note: Surface sow in early spring; seed benefits from cool soil and light to germinate.
directions: Sparky produces charming double and semi-double blooms in warm apricot tones with burgundy undersides on compact 8-to-10-inch plants. Scatter the tiny seed on the soil surface in early spring, pressing it in without covering since it needs light to sprout. Poppies resent transplanting, so direct sow where they are to grow and thin seedlings to prevent crowding. Drought-tolerant and deer-resistant once established, this variety draws bees and butterflies to the garden. Let a few blooms go to seed and it will often self-sow for the following year.

plant_note: Plant rhizome sections in 6–18 inches of water in pots of aquatic soil set on the pond bottom, or directly into pond mud in spring; spatterdock thrives in more shade than true water lilies and tolerates slow-moving or slightly turbid water that would defeat Nymphaea species.
directions: Plant rhizome divisions in 6–18 inches of still to slow-moving water in full sun to partial shade — spatterdock is the toughest and most shade-adaptable of all North American native water plants, growing in slow streams, ponds, lakes, ditches, and bayous throughout the continent where it produces large, heart-shaped, floating and emergent leaves up to 16 inches across that provide outstanding surface coverage and shade for fish and frogs. The distinctive globe-shaped, 2-inch yellow flowers with conspicuous red stamens appear above the water surface from spring through fall and are pollinated by beetles attracted to the sweet scent. Unlike true water lilies (Nymphaea), spatterdock tolerates shade, slow-moving water, turbidity, and some salt influence, making it the water plant of choice for shaded natural ponds, sluggish streams, and bayou margins. The rhizomes were an important food source for Native Americans. Spreads vigorously in ideal conditions; contain in pots for small water gardens.

plant_note: Sow in early spring as soon as soil can be worked; tolerates cool weather.
directions: This vigorous, late-flowering Spencer type bears fragrant ivory ruffled blooms 1 to 2 inches wide that resemble delicate confections. Sow seed directly in early spring in rich, well-drained soil, providing a trellis or netting for the climbing vines to grab. Nicking or soaking the hard seed coat overnight speeds germination. Keep roots cool and evenly moist, and harvest flowers often to keep the plants producing through the season. Its slightly shorter stems and late bloom time extend the sweet pea season for intimate bouquets.

plant_note: Sow in early spring as soon as soil is workable; likes cool roots.
directions: Princess Elizabeth blends peachy salmon and soft cream tones in romantic, lightly fragrant blooms ideal for cottage-style beds and vertical gardens. Direct sow in early spring in cool, fertile soil, soaking or scarifying the seed first to improve sprouting. Set up a sturdy trellis for the vigorous vines and keep the soil consistently moist. Frequent cutting encourages the plant to produce armfuls of blooms over a longer window. Plant in full sun with cool roots, mulching to buffer summer heat.

plant_note: Root plantlets (spiderettes) by pinning them to moist soil or placing in water; in zone 9b–11, spider plant can be grown as an outdoor groundcover in deep shade where its stolons will root and spread to cover ground. In zone 8–9a, treat as a container plant or protect from frost.
directions: Root spiderette plantlets in water or moist soil and plant in any moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to deep shade — spider plant is one of the world's most popular houseplants and a valuable outdoor shade groundcover in tropical and subtropical gardens, producing fountains of long, arching, strap-like leaves (solid green or with bold cream-white centers or margins) and long, arching stolons tipped with miniature plantlets that root wherever they contact moist soil. In zone 9b–11 it grows as a naturalized groundcover in deep shade under trees, producing a lush carpet that requires almost no care. It tolerates deep shade, inconsistent watering, and low soil fertility better than almost any other shade groundcover. The variegated form (cream-and-green striped) is especially bright in dark shade. NASA studies identified spider plant as one of the most effective air-purifying houseplants, removing formaldehyde and other volatile organic compounds from indoor air.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring around last frost date; cold stratification for 4 weeks improves germination of seed.
directions: Cold-stratify seeds for 4 weeks before sowing; established divisions establish more reliably than seed-grown plants and can be done in spring or fall. Plant in partial shade to full sun in moist, average to rich soil; plants tolerate wet conditions and make excellent rain garden plants. The three-petaled flowers in purple, blue, pink, or white open only for a single morning but are produced in rapid succession over many weeks. Shear the entire plant to 8 in after the main bloom flush to stimulate fresh foliage and a second round of flowers in fall.

plant_note: Direct sow seed in fall or divide clumps in spring; spiked wood oats is more shade and drought tolerant than the related inland sea oats (Chasmanthium latifolium) and performs well in dry to moderately moist conditions in full to partial shade.
directions: Direct sow in fall or plant divisions in spring in dry to moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade — spiked wood oats is a fine-textured native grass of the southeastern and south-central US, closely related to inland sea oats (C. latifolium) but notably more drought and shade tolerant and somewhat smaller and narrower in leaf. It grows naturally in dry, rocky, or sandy woodland soils under hardwood forest canopies and along dry bluffs and ridges, where its minimal water requirements make it a standout among shade grasses. The attractive seed heads emerge in summer, erect rather than pendulous, and turn a warm tan by fall, providing winter interest and wildlife food. An excellent grass for filling dry shade under oaks and other trees where irrigation is unavailable, or for naturalizing in shaded woodland garden settings. Self-sows moderately; cut back to the ground in late winter to encourage fresh growth.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall on sandy, well-drained, acidic to neutral, dry soil in full sun; splitbeard bluestem is a low-nutrient-requiring grass that performs best in poor, sandy, acidic pine flatwoods soils — excessive fertilization leads to rank growth and reduced ornamental quality.
directions: Splitbeard bluestem is one of the most ornamentally appealing native grasses of the Gulf Coastal Plain and Atlantic Coastal Plain pine country — in fall, the paired flower spikes develop silky, glistening white or silvery seed heads with a distinctive forked, Y-shaped arrangement that gives the species both its common names (splitbeard for the paired stalks, chalky for the whitened seed heads). The plants have a somewhat tufted, wiry character and typically stay shorter than big bluestem or bushy bluestem, making them appropriate for small to medium native garden settings; their chalky-white winter seedheads contrast beautifully with the coppery-bronze stems and leaves. Naturally found in open, dry, longleaf pine savannas, sandhills, and coastal plain flatwoods, splitbeard bluestem is adapted to the fire-maintained, nutrient-poor sandy soils of the Gulf South — combine with native wildflowers like blazing stars, black-eyed Susans, and goldenrods for an authentic pine flatwoods meadow garden.

plant_note: Surface-sow; light aids germination. Short-lived but self-sows.
directions: Surface-sow the seed and press it in, as light helps it germinate. Also called dotted horsemint, this native produces tiered whorls of yellow flowers spotted with purple, set off by showy pink bracts. It thrives in full sun and sandy, dry soil, and its aromatic foliage deters deer. A biennial to short-lived perennial, it self-sows freely and is a magnet for bees, wasps, and other beneficial pollinators.

plant_note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring or early fall in moist, well-drained, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; space 12–18 inches apart for quick coverage.
directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade, avoiding heavy clay that stays waterlogged in winter; spotted deadnettle forms a quick-spreading mat of silver-marked foliage that suppresses weeds effectively under trees and shrubs. The small pink, white, or purple flowers appear in late spring and continue sporadically through summer. Cut plants back hard in midsummer if they become leggy or stressed by heat to encourage a flush of fresh foliage. The silver-splashed leaves of popular cultivars like 'White Nancy' and 'Beacon Silver' brighten dark corners more effectively than most groundcovers. Divide every 3–4 years to maintain vigor.

plant_note: Cold-moist stratify seed for 30 days; surface-sow as light aids germination.
directions: Cold-stratify the seed for a month and surface-sow, since light improves germination. This tall native perennial produces large, domed clusters of fragrant rose-pink flowers from August into September. Grow it in full sun to part shade and consistently moist to wet soil, ideal for rain gardens and pond edges. Its towering stems are a butterfly favorite and add bold structure to the back of the border.

plant_note: Plant nursery-propagated divisions in fall in moist, humus-rich, acidic to slightly acidic woodland soil in partial to full shade. A coastal plain specialist, Spotted Trillium thrives in the warm, moist forests of Georgia, Alabama, and the Florida panhandle.
directions: Spotted Trillium is a sessile trillium of the southeastern coastal plain, native to moist, rich hardwood forests from South Carolina south to Florida and west to Alabama, where it often grows with sweetbay magnolia, titi, and evergreen shrubs in lowland forest understories. Its heavily marbled, silver-spotted leaves and nearly black maroon sessile flowers make it one of the more dramatic members of the genus, and the flowers carry a faint spicy scent that attracts fly pollinators. Rhizome divisions establish best when planted in fall in deeply amended, consistently moist, acidic soil (pH 5.0–6.0) in full shade; the plants go completely dormant by early summer and should never be disturbed while dormant. Source only from reputable nurseries growing their own stock — this species is never legitimately available from wild collection.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; spreading sedge colonizes slowly by long stolons to form a low groundcover that is easy to control if needed.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; spreading sedge is a low-growing native sedge of eastern woodland floors, distinguished by its unusually wide leaves (up to 3/4 inch wide, broad for a sedge) that are a beautiful glaucous blue-green color with a matte, almost frosted surface. The prostrate to arching leaves spread loosely to form a lax groundcover very different in character from the more upright native sedges — relaxed, natural-looking, and graceful. It spreads slowly by long stolons to form a loose, open mat. The cultivar 'Hobb' (sold as 'Blue Bunny') offers especially pronounced blue-green foliage and is widely available in the native plant trade. An outstanding groundcover for the shaded woodland garden in zone 4–8, especially effective paired with native spring wildflowers, ferns, and bleeding heart.

plant_note: Plant small corms 2–3 inches deep in fall in woodland soil; they self-sow freely once established and naturalize to form large colonies.
directions: Plant small, deep-set corms in fall in moist, humus-rich woodland soil under deciduous trees where the plant receives full spring sun before the canopy leafs out; this is one of the earliest spring ephemerals, producing delicate pink-striped white flowers that carpet the forest floor in March and April. The plant goes completely dormant by late spring, so interplant densely with ferns or hostas to cover the void. The tiny underground corms are edible — tasting like a mild, chestnut-flavored radish — but harvesting kills the plant; admire rather than harvest from naturalized colonies. Plant in masses of at least 50 corms for a natural effect, as single plants look sparse.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after all frost danger has passed.
directions: Spring Green is an eye-catching celosia standing about 40 inches tall with fresh lime-green plumes that thrive through late summer heat. Start seed indoors 6 to 4 weeks before the last frost, barely covering it and keeping soil warm around 75 degrees F for best germination. Transplant into full sun after frost danger passes, spacing plants for good airflow. The long, sturdy stems make excellent fresh or dried cut flowers and attract pollinators. Deadhead or harvest regularly to keep the display coming through the growing season.

plant_note: Start indoors in cool conditions; transplant in early spring or fall.
directions: Midnight Glow features rich velvet-purple petals with glowing yellow centers that fade into deep purple edges, producing large 3-inch blooms on uniform, well-branched plants. Start seed indoors in cool conditions and dark until germination, then grow on in bright, cool light before transplanting. Pansies thrive in cool, short-day weather, making them ideal for early spring and fall displays. Set plants in full sun to part shade in rich, moist soil and deadhead to prolong bloom. The edible flowers make a colorful garnish for salads and desserts.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall; foliage smells of onion when crushed.
directions: Plant spring starflower bulbs in fall about three inches deep in well-drained soil in full sun to light shade. The fragrant, star-shaped, pale blue flowers appear over grassy foliage that smells faintly of onion when bruised. It naturalizes readily in mild climates, forming spreading clumps in rock gardens and borders. Keep the site dry during summer dormancy and divide crowded clumps every few years.

plant_note: Plant nursery transplants in fall or early spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, loamy soil in partial shade to full sun. Spruce Pine is ecologically unique among eastern pines in its ability to establish and grow in the partial shade of mixed hardwood forests — the only pine native to the Southeast that tolerates significant shade.
directions: Spruce Pine is the most ecologically unusual of the southeastern native pines — the only pine in eastern North America that regularly grows as an understory component of bottomland hardwood forests and shaded mixed woods, native to the coastal plain and lower Piedmont from South Carolina to northern Florida and west to Louisiana, where it occurs in moist, fertile, loamy soils in the shade of sweetgum, water oak, and magnolia rather than in the open pinelands where other southeastern pines grow. Its smooth, gray bark (glabra means smooth or glabrous) is distinctive among pines and gives the tree an appearance reminiscent of a smooth-barked hardwood; the short, clustered needles and small cones distinguish it from other southeastern species. A valuable native pine for moist, shaded woodland gardens and naturalistic landscapes in zones 7–9 where its unusual shade tolerance sets it apart from all other native conifers. Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic loamy soil in partial shade to full sun; it is not drought-tolerant and requires consistently moist conditions.

plant_note: Plant pea-sized yellow tubers (which resemble corn kernels, giving the plant its name) 1–2 inches deep in fall in groups for a natural display; interplant with hostas or ferns.
directions: Plant the small, yellow, corn-kernel-like tubers 1–2 inches deep in humus-rich, moist, well-drained soil in full to partial shade; squirrel corn is a spring ephemeral closely related to Dutchman's breeches (D. cucullaria) but with rounded, heart-shaped flowers instead of pantaloon-shaped ones, and it has a hyacinth-like fragrance that D. cucullaria lacks. The finely dissected gray-green foliage and delicate white flowers appear in March–April then disappear completely by early June. The plant produces its namesake yellow tubers rather than the white grain-like tubers of its close relative. Entirely toxic — do not confuse with any edible plant. Forms small colonies over time as tubers multiply.

plant_note: Plant rooted cuttings or container-grown transplants in spring in moist, well-drained to moderately wet, strongly acidic, sandy to loamy, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun. All parts of Staggerbush contain grayanotoxins (andromedotoxins) and are poisonous to livestock — do not plant where grazing animals have access.
directions: Staggerbush is a graceful native deciduous shrub of the coastal plain's moist to moderately dry, acidic soils from New England south to Florida and west to Texas, producing elegant, pendulous clusters of white to pink, urn-shaped flowers in May that hang gracefully from the branches before the leaves fully expand. The common name 'staggerbush' refers to the plant's toxicity to sheep and other livestock that consumed the foliage; like all Lyonia species, the leaves contain grayanotoxins (andromedotoxins) that cause staggering, drooling, and potentially fatal poisoning in grazing animals. Despite this toxicity, Staggerbush is an attractive ornamental native shrub with graceful spring flowers, attractive summer foliage, and modest red to orange fall color that supports native bees when in bloom. Plant in moist, well-drained to moderately wet, strongly acidic, sandy to loamy soil in partial shade to full sun; keep away from livestock.

plant_note: Direct sow seed in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy to loamy, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun; standing cypress is biennial — it forms a flat rosette of feathery foliage in the first year and produces its dramatic flower spike in the second year before setting seed and dying. Sow every year to maintain a continuous supply of blooming plants.
directions: Direct sow in fall in well-drained, dry to moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun — standing cypress is a native biennial of the southeastern coastal plain and Gulf Coast, producing one of the most dramatic and unique effects in the spring-summer garden: an erect, rigid, 3–5 foot spike densely clothed in feathery, bright green, cypress-like foliage, then crowned the second year with hundreds of tubular, brilliant scarlet-orange flowers from May through July. The flowers are among the most intensely attractive to ruby-throated hummingbirds of any plant in the eastern flora — a single plant in bloom will hold hummingbirds all day. Because it is biennial, sow seed each fall to maintain continuous bloom from a mixture of first- and second-year plants. It self-sows reliably in sandy, open, sunny soil to maintain naturalized colonies. An outstanding plant for formal and naturalistic gardens alike wherever a vivid vertical accent and hummingbird attraction are desired.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall; huge seed heads dry beautifully.
directions: Plant star of Persia bulbs in fall about six inches deep in sharply drained soil and full sun. Each stem bears an enormous globe up to ten inches across of metallic star-shaped violet florets in late spring. The dried seed heads persist for weeks and are prized for arrangements. Site it in a hot, dry spot and let the bulbs bake during summer dormancy to ensure reliable return.

plant_note: Direct sow; drought tolerant and heat loving.
directions: Direct sow star of the veldt in warm, well-drained soil after frost danger passes. This South African annual bears glowing orange and apricot daisies that open in bright sun and close on cloudy days. It thrives in heat and lean soil with minimal water, making it excellent for dry sunny beds and containers. Deadhead spent blooms and avoid overwatering, which can cause rot.

plant_note: Direct sow around the last frost in full sun.
directions: Starflower opens delicate pale blue and white blooms, but its real magic is the spherical seedhead covered in papery, striped cups each cradling a five-pointed dark star. Direct sow in spring around the last frost, lightly covering the seed and keeping it evenly moist until seedlings emerge. Grow in full sun to part shade in average, well-drained soil. Cut blooms for fresh arrangements or leave them to mature into the ornamental seedheads prized for dried bouquets and crafts. The flowers draw bees and butterflies through summer.

common_name: Starry Solomon's Seal (Star-Flowered False Solomon's Seal)
plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in moist, rich, slightly acidic, well-drained woodland soil in partial to full shade; starry Solomon's seal spreads slowly by rhizomes to form colonies — plant 6-inch-deep rhizome divisions horizontally and allow several years to establish before dividing.
directions: Starry Solomon's seal is a native woodland perennial closely related to the more common false Solomon's seal (Maianthemum racemosum) but producing star-shaped white flowers spaced individually along the arching stems rather than in a terminal plume — the small, starry flowers have a delicate, precise symmetry that rewards close inspection. Like its relatives, it grows in moist to dry, rich woodland soil across a broad range of habitats from Pacific coast to Atlantic, forming slowly expanding colonies of arching, parallel-veined stems that emerge fresh green in spring and age to attractive copper-bronze in fall. Small striped berries (green to red-striped when young, darkening to deep red or black at maturity) are consumed by thrushes, towhees, and small mammals in fall; the berries and foliage are mildly toxic to humans and should not be consumed. Its compact stature and tolerance of dry shade make it one of the most versatile native groundcovers for both moist and moderately dry woodland gardens — use as an edging or naturalizing groundcover under deciduous trees.

plant_note: Direct sow after all frost danger passes in warm soil.
directions: State Fair Mix is a pollinator magnet producing immense flowers dominated by reds and purples with orange, pink, white, and yellow scattered throughout. Direct sow after the last frost in warm soil and full sun, thinning seedlings to give these tall plants room to branch. Zinnias grow quickly and bloom heavily until fall frost when kept deadheaded or cut. The long, sturdy stems make outstanding cut flowers for arrangements. Provide good airflow and water at the base to reduce foliar disease.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant 2–3 weeks before last frost as plants tolerate mild frost.
directions: Sow seed 1/8 in deep in moist starting mix; seeds come in a papery casing and germination is slow. Statice prefers cool conditions early in the season. Harvest flower stems for drying when 75% of the florets on each branch have opened. The papery calyces — which hold their color dried — are the ornamental part; the true petals are tiny and brief.

plant_note: Seed benefits from cold stratification; transplant seedlings in spring.
directions: Sea holly brings striking architectural form with steel-blue thistle-like flower heads and spiny bracts that shimmer in moonlight. Seed germinates best after a period of cold, moist stratification, so chill sown flats or sow outdoors in late fall. Transplant carefully, as the deep taproot dislikes disturbance, into lean, well-drained soil in full sun. Drought-tolerant once established, it blooms profusely through summer and provides winter interest with long-standing seedheads. Cut stems dry beautifully for everlasting arrangements.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to alkaline, dry to moderately moist, sandy to loamy soil in full sun to light shade; stiff goldenrod is drought tolerant and thrives in poor, well-drained soils — it will not thrive in wet or highly fertile conditions where it becomes floppy.
directions: Direct sow in fall or transplant in spring in well-drained, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun — stiff goldenrod is one of the most ornamentally attractive native goldenrods, producing broad, flat-topped clusters of bright golden-yellow flower heads in August–October that contrast beautifully with the coarse, grayish-green, almost sandpapery-textured leaves. Unlike many goldenrods with arching, plume-like inflorescences, stiff goldenrod holds its flowers in rigid, organized, flat-topped corymbs with an unusually bold, architectural quality. It is one of the most valuable late-season pollinator plants on the continent: dozens of specialist bee species including eastern bumble bees, mining bees, and sweat bees depend on goldenrod pollen in late summer as a critical pre-winter protein source, and the flowers host hundreds of beneficial insect species. The stiff, upright habit (rarely needing staking despite the name) makes it more manageable than spreading goldenrod species in garden settings. Blooms reliably with virtually no care in adapted soils.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant 2–4 weeks before last frost as young plants tolerate light frost.
directions: Sow seed on the surface of moist starting mix — light aids germination, so cover with only the thinnest layer of vermiculite if at all. Stock thrives in cool weather (55–65°F nights) and the fragrance is intensified by cool temperatures. Transplant to the garden in early spring; plants begin to decline when daytime temperatures consistently exceed 80°F. Cut flowers when one-third of the buds on the spike are open for the longest vase life.

plant_note: In zone 8–10, start seeds indoors in September–October and transplant to the garden in October–November for winter–spring blooms; stock requires cool growing conditions (40–65°F nights) to flower well and will not bloom in summer heat. Pinch seedlings to encourage branching.
directions: Start seeds in fall and transplant to cool-season beds in full sun; stock is one of the most intensely fragrant of all garden flowers — the densely clustered, four-petaled flowers in white, pink, red, lavender, or yellow on upright spikes have a clove-like scent that carries 20 feet on still evening air. In zone 8–10 it is grown as a cool-season annual planted in fall and blooming through the mild winter months, filling the winter garden with color and fragrance when little else is in bloom. The double-flowered forms are more fragrant than singles. Stock performs best at temperatures of 40–65°F and declines as summer heat arrives. Plant with pansies, snapdragons, and dianthus for a classic cool-season fragrant border. An outstanding and long-lasting cut flower.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost after cold-stratifying for 4–6 weeks; transplant after last frost.
directions: Cold-stratify seeds in moist medium at 35–40°F for 4–6 weeks before sowing indoors; germination is slow but improved by stratification. Plant in full sun in well-drained, sandy or loamy soil; Stokes' aster is native to the American Southeast and tolerates heat and humidity well but dislikes wet winter conditions. The large, cornflower-like blooms in blue, lavender, or white appear from early summer to fall and are excellent for cutting. Divide clumps every 3–4 years in spring to maintain vigor; plants spread slowly and are well-behaved in the border.

plant_note: Biennial; sow in spring or summer for bloom the following year.
directions: Canterbury bells, also called bellflower, is an elegant, tall biennial that makes a dramatic backdrop for shorter annuals and perennials. Surface sow the fine seed, which needs light to germinate, and grow seedlings on before transplanting into rich, moist, well-drained soil. As a biennial, plants form a leafy rosette the first year and send up their cup-and-saucer bloom spikes the second season into early summer. Site in full sun to part shade and stake tall stems in windy spots. The nectar-rich flowers attract bees and other pollinators.

plant_note: Direct sow seeds in fall in moist to moderately dry, rocky, slightly acidic, humus-rich woodland soil in partial shade to full sun. Stout Goldenrod is native to rocky Appalachian woodland edges and tolerates partial shade better than most goldenrods.
directions: Stout Goldenrod is a native perennial of rocky Appalachian woodlands, cliff edges, shale barrens, and dry to moderately moist forest margins from New England south to the mountains of Tennessee and North Carolina, valued as one of the more shade-tolerant goldenrods and distinctive for its stout, robust stems and narrow, leafy inflorescence of bright yellow flowers in August and September. Unlike many goldenrods that require full sun and open meadows, Stout Goldenrod grows happily at shaded rock outcrops, rocky woodland edges, and forest margins with only partial sun, making it a useful native plant for challenging semi-shaded, rocky garden sites. The dense clusters of small yellow flowers attract native bees and late-season butterflies as one of the last significant nectar sources before fall in Appalachian woodland habitats. Direct sow seeds in fall in moist to moderately dry, rocky, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; established plants are long-lived and require little maintenance.

plant_note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost risk has passed.
directions: Sow seed on the surface of moist starting mix and press lightly — light aids germination, so barely cover seed. Strawflowers tolerate drought and poor soils remarkably well. For drying, harvest stems when the central button of each head is still closed, before the outer bracts fully open; they will continue to open as they dry. Deadhead garden plants regularly to maintain continuous bloom.

plant_note: Plant container-grown trees in late winter or early spring in moist, acidic, humus-rich, cool, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; striped maple is a cool-climate understory tree that requires protection from afternoon sun and heat in zone 6+ and will not thrive in zone 7 and warmer.
directions: Plant in moist, acidic, humus-rich, cool soil in partial to full shade — striped maple is one of North America's most beautiful native understory trees, instantly recognizable by the striking vertical white-and-green striping on the young bark (giving it the alternate name 'snakebark maple' or 'moosewood'). The large, three-lobed leaves turn butter yellow in fall. It is a true understory specialist that grows naturally in the shade beneath taller hardwoods in cool, moist, acidic mountain forests — it is shade-tolerant enough to regenerate in nearly complete shade. Moose browse the bark in winter (giving the common name). Not suited to zone 7 and warmer, where the heat and humidity are too much for this cool-climate species. An outstanding ornamental tree for the shaded garden in zone 3–6, particularly in New England, the upper Midwest, and the Appalachian highlands. The ornamental bark is visible year-round and is spectacular.

plant_note: Plant container trees in spring or fresh seed in fall in any well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to slightly alkaline, clay to sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; sugar hackberry is exceptionally adaptable to difficult soil conditions including alkaline soils, clay, and brief flooding.
directions: Plant in any well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to alkaline soil in full sun to partial shade — sugar hackberry is the primary Celtis species of the Gulf Coast and Deep South, better adapted to the heat, humidity, and occasional flooding of zone 7–9 than the common hackberry (C. occidentalis). The distinctive warty, corky-gray bark is unmistakable and one of the most interesting bark textures of any native tree. Small orange to purple-black berries in fall are among the most important food sources for migrating songbirds — more than 50 species consume the berries, including yellow-rumped warblers and hermit thrushes. The tree is the exclusive larval host for the hackberry emperor butterfly and American snout butterfly, two distinctive native butterflies wholly dependent on Celtis species. It tolerates urban conditions, compacted soils, drought, and alkaline pH remarkably well. A keystone wildlife tree for the Gulf Coast landscape.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall in moist, rich, well-drained to moist soil in full to partial sun; sugarcane plumegrass tolerates a wider range of moisture conditions than bent-awn plumegrass and can be planted in moist woodland edges, stream banks, and moist meadow margins.
directions: Sugarcane plumegrass is the tallest native grass of the eastern United States, reaching 6–10 feet in height and producing enormous, feathery, white-to-copper-tinted plumes in fall that rival pampas grass in spectacle while being fully native and ecologically appropriate across the Gulf and Atlantic Coastal Plains. As a close relative of sugarcane in the genus Saccharum, it shares similar bold, architectural characteristics — long, broad, pointed leaves with white midribs, reed-like stems with prominent nodes, and a strongly upright, vase-shaped form that provides a dramatic focal point in any large native planting. The silky plumes attract and shelter migrating birds in fall and provide nesting material for marsh and meadow birds; the tall, dense clumps create excellent wildlife screening habitat. Best suited to moist, rich woodland edges, stream banks, and rain garden borders where its imposing size can be fully appreciated — the fall plumes are stunning backlit by afternoon or evening sun in October and November.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost danger in warm soil and full sun.
directions: Summer Lovin bears a mix of creamy lemon-tipped petals with burgundy or strawberry-pink centers, often several color variations on a single plant. Direct sow the large seed after the last frost in warm, well-drained soil and full sun, planting about an inch deep. The tall, branching stems are pollenless and stiff, perfect for clean cut-flower bouquets. Thin seedlings to give plants room and stake if needed in exposed sites. Harvest often to encourage more blooms across the summer.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall; tolerates wet soils.
directions: Plant summer snowflake bulbs in fall about four inches deep in moist, humus-rich soil in sun to partial shade. Despite its name it blooms in mid to late spring, bearing nodding white bells tipped with green on arching stems. Unusually among bulbs it tolerates heavy, wet soils and naturalizes along ponds and in damp borders. Leave the strappy foliage to ripen fully, and the clumps will slowly increase over the years.

plant_note: Fall sow or cold-stratify seed; taproot resents transplanting.
directions: Sundial lupine, also called wild lupine, is a heirloom perennial native to the eastern U.S. and a vital host plant for the Karner blue butterfly. Scarify or cold-stratify the hard seed and direct sow in fall or early spring, since the deep taproot dislikes transplanting. Grow in full sun in lean, sandy, well-drained soil where it naturalizes in wildflower gardens and meadows. Its blue flower spikes appear in late spring to early summer and draw many pollinators. Avoid rich soil and excess water, which this drought-tolerant native does not need.

plant_note: Direct sow seed in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, dry to moderately moist, loamy to sandy soil in full sun; sundrops (unlike evening primrose) blooms by day and is an excellent day-blooming perennial for open sunny borders where evening primroses would go unnoticed at their peak.
directions: Direct sow in fall or transplant in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun — sundrops is a day-blooming native perennial (unlike its nocturnal cousin the evening primrose) that produces a long season of bright, clear, four-petaled lemon-yellow flowers 1.5–2 inches across from May through July on upright reddish stems with attractive, narrow, reddish-tinged leaves. The red pigmentation of the stems and foliage deepens through summer and turns spectacular bronzy-red in fall, providing excellent late-season color after the blooms end. It spreads by rhizomes and self-sowing to naturalize in open, sunny, dry areas, making it excellent for prairie gardens, wildflower meadows, and low-maintenance dry-slope plantings. The similar O. tetragona and its cultivar 'Fireworks' (with showy orange-red buds opening to yellow) are closely related and widely grown garden selections offering longer bloom periods. Drought tolerant; requires excellent drainage; no care once established.

plant_note: Direct sow at or just after last frost when soil is warm; succession sow every 2–3 weeks for extended bloom.
directions: Direct sow seed 1 in deep after the last frost date, thinning to final spacing once seedlings reach 6 in tall. Sunflowers prefer poor-to-average soil and resent transplanting due to a taproot. Single-stemmed types bloom once; branching varieties produce multiple heads if cut regularly. Harvest seed heads when the back of the head turns yellow-brown and seeds feel firm.

plant_note: Direct sow after last frost; good for containers due to compact habit.
directions: Sow seed one inch deep after the last frost once soil warms. This dwarf heirloom grows just two to three feet tall, producing dozens of fluffy, double golden-yellow pom-pom blooms. Its compact, branching habit suits containers, borders, and small gardens, and the sturdy stems make fine cut flowers. Give it full sun and moderate water, and deadhead to keep new blooms coming through the season.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost; soak or nick seed to speed germination.
directions: Sunrise Blend offers a colorful assortment of trumpet blooms that flutter against vigorous heart-shaped foliage from midsummer to the first fall frost. Soak or nick the hard seed coat, then direct sow after the last frost near a trellis, fence, or arbor for the climbing vines to scale. The flowers open in early morning and may stay open on cloudy or cool days. Grow in full sun in average soil, avoiding overly rich ground that favors leaves over flowers. Note that the seeds are poisonous if eaten, so keep them away from children and pets.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after frost; loves heat and dry soil.
directions: Sunshine Blend gazania is a heat- and drought-loving groundcover in warm colors from yellow to mauve, with lush low foliage and blooms held high on their stems. Start seed indoors 6 to 4 weeks before the last frost, covering lightly since darkness aids germination, then transplant after frost danger passes. Grow in full sun in sandy, well-drained soil where the daisy-like petals unfurl in sunshine and close under clouds. Water sparingly once established, as this tough plant tolerates drought and poor soil. Deadhead to keep the color coming through summer.

plant_note: Plant plugs or divisions in spring in any well-drained, sunny soil; sunshine mimosa spreads vigorously by stolons to form a dense mat — excellent for replacing lawn areas in full sun, but control spread in small gardens by periodic edging.
directions: Sunshine mimosa is the premier native groundcover lawn substitute for the Gulf Coast region — a low-growing, stoloniferous perennial that forms dense, fine-textured mats covered with pink, feathery pompom flowers from spring through fall, handling foot traffic better than most native groundcovers. As a member of the legume family, it fixes atmospheric nitrogen through root nodules, improving soil fertility as it spreads, and it tolerates the heat, drought, and poor sandy soils of Gulf Coast gardens that defeat other groundcovers. The sensitive leaves fold slightly when brushed or walked on — a charming touch-me-not response that delights children — and the spectacular pink pompom flowers are a prolific nectar source for native bees and butterflies throughout the growing season. In lawn renovation projects in zone 8–11, plant plugs on 12-inch centers in spring and within one growing season they will knit together into a solid, mowable (3-inch mow height) lawn alternative that needs no irrigation, no fertilizer, and no pesticides once established.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil and full sun.
directions: Sunspot delivers large sunflower heads on surprisingly dwarf 18-to-24-inch stalks, making it a favorite for kids and small gardens. Direct sow the seed about an inch deep after the last frost in warm soil and full sun. Thin seedlings so each plant has room to develop its outsized bloom. The short stature makes dramatic cut flowers and container plantings, and the heads later provide seeds for birds. Provide steady moisture while young and full sun throughout the season.

plant_note: Start indoors in darkness; transplant after frost.
directions: Salpiglossis, or painted tongue, produces velvety, trumpet-shaped flowers with striking metallic coloration and intricate veining in a range of jewel tones. Start seed indoors 8 to 6 weeks before the last frost, covering it fully since the seed needs darkness to germinate, and keep soil warm. Transplant after frost into rich, well-drained soil in full sun with shelter from strong wind. Pinch young plants to encourage branching and stake the somewhat lax stems. Deadhead regularly and enjoy the blooms as long-lasting cut flowers.

plant_note: Plant container-grown specimens in fall in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately wet soil in partial to full shade; supplejack is a woody vine that forms thickets and climbs high into the forest canopy — site it where it has room to naturalize or provide a large, sturdy arbor.
directions: Supplejack is a vigorous native woody vine of bottomland forests, swamp margins, and moist woodland edges across the Gulf Coast and southeastern United States, climbing high into the forest canopy by twining and branching through the upper shrub and tree layers. Its flexible, wiry stems gave rise to the common name — the wood was historically used to make walking sticks, basket frames, and tool handles because of its exceptional suppleness and strength. Small, inconspicuous greenish-white flowers in late spring produce blue-black drupes that ripen in fall and are consumed by wood ducks, wild turkeys, and at least a dozen species of songbirds and small mammals, making supplejack one of the highest-value wildlife vines in the region. In naturalized woodland gardens or large-scale habitat restorations, allow it to climb through native trees and form dense thickets that provide both food and nesting cover; it is too vigorous for small spaces but invaluable for restoring native forest understory structure.

plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring in consistently moist to wet, acidic soil; do not allow to dry out during establishment. Do not plant too deeply — keep the crown at or above soil level.
directions: Plant in moist to wet, deeply acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich soil in partial shade to light sun; swamp azalea is the most fragrant of all native North American deciduous azaleas, producing intensely clove-spice-scented white flowers (often pink-flushed) in late June through July, extending the native azalea bloom season well into summer after all other species have finished. It grows naturally in swamps, bogs, stream banks, and wet woodland margins from Maine to Florida, and tolerates standing water better than any other Rhododendron. The plant spreads slowly by rhizome and root sprouts to form a colony; purchase container-grown propagated plants only. Mulch with pine bark or pine needle litter to maintain soil acidity and moisture. Never allow to dry out in summer.

plant_note: Plant container specimens in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic, sandy to loamy soil in partial shade to full sun; swamp bay grows naturally in wet pine flatwoods, pond margins, swamps, and bottomlands of the Gulf Coast and Southeast — it requires consistently moist to wet soil and does not tolerate drought.
directions: Plant in moist to wet, slightly acidic, sandy to loamy soil in partial shade to full sun — swamp bay is a native evergreen tree of Gulf Coast wetlands, closely related to the edible bay laurel and redbay, with aromatic leaves historically used as a bay leaf substitute in Creole and Southern cooking. The glossy, elliptic evergreen leaves have a whitish-downy underside and are fragrant when crushed with the characteristic bay laurel scent. Small, yellowish-green flowers in spring attract native bees; dark blue-black berries feed songbirds and the palamedes swallowtail butterfly uses the foliage as a larval host. It grows naturally in cypress swamps, wet flatwoods, and bay swamps throughout the Gulf Coast and is one of the best choices for naturalizing in wet, low areas where other trees refuse to grow. The wood is durable and aromatic; the tree is long-lived and forms the canopy of classic southern bay swamp ecosystems.

common_name: Swamp Chestnut Oak (Basket Oak, Cow Oak)
plant_note: Plant container-grown trees in fall in moist to wet, slightly acidic, rich bottomland or floodplain soil in full sun to partial shade; swamp chestnut oak is native to the rich bottomland hardwood forests of the Southeast and produces the largest acorns of any American oak — critical for wildlife at a time when few other mast crops are available.
directions: Plant in moist, rich, slightly acidic bottomland soil in full sun to partial shade; swamp chestnut oak is a magnificent large native oak of the rich bottomland forests of the Southeast, reaching 60–100 feet with a straight trunk and broad, open crown with large, chestnut-like leaves 5–9 inches long. The large, sweet acorns (up to 1.5 inches) ripen in a single season and are the largest, most palatable acorns produced by any American oak — they were eaten by Native Americans and early settlers and are readily eaten by deer, turkeys, squirrels, and wood ducks. The handsome, ridged, grayish bark and excellent fall yellow-orange color make this a valuable landscape tree where space permits. Tolerates seasonal flooding but also grows well in moist, well-drained upland soils. Somewhat more tolerant of partial shade when young than most oaks. A premier wildlife and landscape tree for zone 5–9.

plant_note: Plant nursery transplants or root hardwood cuttings in spring in wet, moist, slightly acidic to neutral, rich alluvial or clay-loam soil in full sun. Swamp Cottonwood thrives in the deepest, most permanently wet bottomland soils where Eastern Cottonwood (Populus deltoides) begins to struggle.
directions: Swamp Cottonwood is the wetland specialist of the cottonwood family, native to the permanently flooded swamps, blackwater river bottoms, and cypress-tupelo swamp margins of the southeastern coastal plain from Connecticut to Florida and west to Indiana and Oklahoma, where it tolerates deeper and more prolonged flooding than any other native cottonwood. Its leaves are distinctly broader and more rounded than those of Eastern Cottonwood (Populus deltoides) — hence the species epithet heterophylla (variable-leaved) — and the tree often develops a tall, straight trunk in the competitive light environment of bottomland forest. Like other cottonwoods, Swamp Cottonwood is a fast-growing pioneer tree important for rapid canopy establishment in wetland restoration projects; it roots readily from hardwood cuttings pushed into wet soil in early spring. The cottony seeds of female trees are dispersed by wind and water in late spring and early summer. An excellent riparian tree for the restoration and naturalization of permanently wet bottomland habitats in zones 5–9.

plant_note: Plant container shrubs in spring in moist to wet, strongly acidic (pH 4.0–5.0), humus-rich soil in partial shade; this plant grows naturally in very acidic, boggy, waterlogged conditions and will not thrive in alkaline or neutral soils.
directions: Plant in moist to wet, strongly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; swamp cyrilla (also called titi or leatherwood) is a distinctive multi-season native shrub of coastal plain bogs, swamp margins, and pocosins in the Southeast, producing long racemes of small white flowers in June and July that are intensely fragrant and among the most important summer nectar sources for native bees and the basis of the highly prized 'titi honey' produced by southeastern beekeepers. The semi-evergreen leaves turn brilliant orange-red in fall before dropping, and in warm winters may persist through spring. The plant is adapted to very acidic, poorly drained soils where it has little competition. An outstanding specimen for bog gardens, rain gardens, and acid pond margins in zone 7–9.

plant_note: Plant rooted cuttings or container-grown transplants in spring in moist to wet, strongly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade along stream banks or in seasonally wet, shaded woodland settings. Swamp Doghobble requires consistently moist, shaded conditions and performs best at the shaded edge of streams and wet woodland understories.
directions: Swamp Doghobble is a graceful native deciduous shrub of moist to wet, shaded, acidic habitats along shaded stream banks, boggy swamp margins, and wet woodland understories of the eastern United States, producing fragrant, white, cylindrical flowers in drooping clusters along the stems in spring before the leaves fully emerge. Unlike the evergreen leucothoes, Swamp Doghobble is deciduous and displays outstanding orange, red, and purple fall foliage color that rivals the showiest cultivated shrubs. The arching, fountain-like growth habit and multi-season ornamental interest — spring flowers, attractive summer foliage, and brilliant fall color — make Swamp Doghobble a highly desirable native shrub for shaded, moist, acid garden settings. Plant in strongly acidic, moist to wet, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; Swamp Doghobble will not tolerate alkaline soil, drought, or prolonged direct sun.

plant_note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in wet to moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade. Swamp Dogwood thrives along stream banks, pond margins, wet meadow edges, and seasonally flooded areas; it is excellent for rain gardens and bio-retention swales where the soil remains consistently moist.
directions: Swamp Dogwood is a large, multi-stemmed native shrub native to wet thickets, streambanks, pond margins, and swamp edges throughout eastern North America from New Brunswick to Florida and west to Oklahoma, tolerating prolonged seasonal flooding and thriving in moist to wet, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade. It produces flat-topped clusters of small white flowers in June and July that attract a wide variety of native bees and other pollinators, followed by striking porcelain-blue fruits on red-stemmed clusters that ripen in August and September and are eagerly consumed by over 40 species of songbirds, including wood thrush, catbirds, and warblers. The purplish-red winter stems provide attractive four-season interest, and the shrub suckers readily to form dense thickets that stabilize streambanks and provide excellent nesting cover for wildlife. An outstanding plant for wet native gardens, rain gardens, or bio-swales where its moisture tolerance is an asset rather than a compromise.

plant_note: Divide clumps in spring and plant in wet to moist, acidic, sandy or peaty soil in partial shade to full sun. Swamp Fern spreads aggressively by stolons in moist soil and should be given ample room or used where it can naturalize freely.
directions: Swamp Fern is an aggressive, colony-forming native fern of the Gulf Coast and peninsular Florida, native to wet flatwoods, swamp margins, cypress sloughs, seepage slopes, and roadside ditches from Florida west to Texas, and throughout the Caribbean and tropical Americas, where it thrives in warm, wet, acidic conditions that few other ferns tolerate. The upright, glossy fronds — both sterile (wider, with toothed pinnae) and fertile (narrower, with elongated sori) — are produced from creeping stolons that spread widely in saturated or permanently moist soil, forming dense colonies that effectively suppress weeds and stabilize wet soil. An excellent low-maintenance groundcover for the permanently wet spots in Gulf Coast landscapes where most ornamental plants drown; it is fully evergreen in zones 9–11 and semi-evergreen in zone 8. Divide and replant in spring in any moist, acidic, low-lying area.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions in late summer to early fall in moist to wet, acidic soil at the water's edge or in shallow water; fall planting allows roots to establish before winter dormancy and spring bloom.
directions: Plant rhizome divisions in moist to wet, acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; this native species is the parent of the beloved Louisiana iris hybrid group, growing naturally in swamps, bayou margins, ditches, and wet flatwoods of the Gulf Coast from Florida to Texas. The large, elegant blue-violet flowers (occasionally varying to pink or white) in spring are extraordinary among native wildflowers for their sophisticated form and vivid color. Louisiana iris hybrids have expanded the color range to include burgundy, yellow, white, pink, and bicolors — if you want color variety, seek out named Louisiana iris hybrids from specialty nurseries; for native restoration, stick with the true species. All parts of the plant are toxic if ingested. Plant at pond margins, in rain gardens, or in any consistently wet spot.

plant_note: Plant near a trellis or fence in moist to wet, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; swamp leather flower is one of the most flood-tolerant native clematis, naturally occurring along streambanks, swamp margins, and bottomland forest edges.
directions: Swamp leather flower is a native clematis of the Gulf Coastal Plain, blooming from late spring through summer with distinctive nodding, urn-shaped flowers in soft lavender to blue-purple with flared, recurved petal tips that give the flowers a crisp, sculptural quality unlike any other vine — it is one of the finest ornamental native vines of the American South yet remains underutilized in cultivation. The vine is naturally found in swampy thickets, bottomland forests, and moist shrub borders from Virginia to Texas, making it ideally suited to rain garden edges, pond margins, and low-lying moist areas of Gulf Coast gardens that challenge other vines. Unlike many Clematis species, it handles the heat and humidity of the Gulf South reliably without the mildew and root rot problems that affect hybrid large-flowered types in zones 8 and 9. Plant through a native shrub or on a fence in partial shade; cut back hard to the ground each winter to maintain a neat, vigorous plant.

plant_note: Plant large bulbs with the neck just at or above soil level in consistently moist to wet soil in partial shade; does not tolerate dry conditions. Excellent for pond margins and rain gardens.
directions: Plant large bulbs with the neck at or just above soil level in consistently wet to moist, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; swamp lily is a stunning native bulb of the Gulf Coast, Florida, and Caribbean, growing naturally in freshwater swamps, bog margins, stream banks, and wet ditches. It produces large, fragrant, spider-like white flowers with narrow strap petals in summer and into fall — often repeatedly after summer rain events — on upright stalks among bold, strap-like evergreen leaves. Plant at pond margins, in consistently moist rain garden hollows, or in large containers kept in saucers of water on the patio. The bold foliage and exotic flowers provide a tropical look in Gulf Coast zone 8–9 gardens. Divide crowded clumps every 4–5 years by removing and replanting offsets.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall (seeds need 30-day cold stratification) or cold-stratify indoors and start in late winter; transplant to the garden in spring. Unlike butterfly weed, swamp milkweed germinates more reliably after stratification.
directions: Plant in moist to wet, average soil in full sun to partial shade; swamp milkweed is an essential monarch butterfly host plant that tolerates wet conditions where common milkweed struggles, making it ideal for rain gardens, pond margins, and low-lying areas. The bright rose-pink to mauve flower clusters bloom from June through August and are heavily visited by monarchs, swallowtails, and bumble bees. Unlike other milkweeds, it does not spread aggressively by rhizomes and stays in well-behaved clumps, making it one of the best milkweed species for garden planting. Leave seedpods on through winter for wildlife interest and natural reseeding. The white-flowered cultivar 'Ice Ballet' is also excellent.

plant_note: Plant nursery transplants (male and female plants together) in spring in wet to moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade. Swamp Privet is excellent for naturalizing wet bottomlands, streambanks, and seasonally flooded areas throughout zones 5–9.
directions: Swamp Privet is a large, multi-stemmed native deciduous shrub, native to the riverine swamps, oxbow lakes, backwater floodplains, and cypress-tupelo swamps of the southeastern and mid-South United States from South Carolina to Florida and west to Kansas and Texas, tolerating prolonged inundation that few other native shrubs can survive. Inconspicuous yellow flowers appear in February and March before the leaves emerge, providing critical early-season nectar and pollen for native bees emerging from winter dormancy; small, dark blue-purple olive-like drupes ripen in summer and are avidly consumed by wood ducks, mallards, and other water-dependent birds. A valuable plant for the restoration and naturalization of wet bottomland and floodplain habitats, Swamp Privet tolerates prolonged flooding up to 4–6 months and is one of the few native shrubs that can establish in true swamp conditions. Plant male and female individuals together for fruit production; it requires no maintenance once established.

plant_note: Plant rooted cuttings or container plants in spring in moist to wet soil near water features; this is the rose for wet, shaded areas where others cannot survive.
directions: Plant in consistently wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade near ponds, streams, or rain gardens; unlike most roses this eastern native tolerates waterlogged soil and grows naturally in swampy margins and along stream banks. The fragrant, single pink flowers in early summer attract native bees, and the bright red rose hips that follow persist through winter, providing critical food for robins, mockingbirds, and cedar waxwings. Prune only to remove dead wood; the arching, prickly canes can be tied to a support or left to form a dense, impenetrable thicket with high wildlife value. Highly disease resistant in moist conditions where other roses suffer.

plant_note: Nick or soak seed in warm water 24 hours before sowing indoors 8 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger passes when soil has warmed. Alternatively, direct sow in very late spring. Plants emerge late — do not assume they are dead until June.
directions: Plant in moist to wet, rich soil in full sun; swamp rose mallow produces the largest flowers of any perennial hardy to zone 4 — dinner-plate sized blooms up to 10–12 inches across in white, pink, red, or bicolor from July through September. It is native to wetlands, marshes, and stream margins throughout eastern North America and thrives in rain gardens and pond edges where few other plants bloom so dramatically. Plants emerge very late in spring — sometimes not until late May or early June — so mark their location to avoid digging them. Numerous cultivars with red, pink, white, and bicolor flowers are available (Luna series, Disco Belle, Summerific series). Cut stems to the ground in winter after they die back.

plant_note: Plant bulbs 3–4 inches deep in wet to moist, slightly acidic, fertile soil in partial shade to full sun; swamp spider lily thrives in pond margins, wet ditches, and boggy, shaded spots where few other bulbs will grow. It is winter-hardy to zone 7 and resprouts reliably from established bulbs.
directions: Plant bulbs in wet to moist, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun — the swamp spider lily is a spectacular Gulf Coast native wildflower found naturally in wet prairies, swampy ditches, stream banks, and the margins of cypress swamps from Texas to Georgia. In spring it produces clusters of 3–6 intensely fragrant, spider-like white flowers on leafless scapes 18–24 inches tall — the long, narrow, reflexed petals surrounding a prominent cup-like corona are architecturally striking and fill the garden with a rich, sweet perfume. The broad, strap-like leaves emerge in spring and die back by late summer. It naturalizes freely in wet conditions, forming spectacular colonies in roadside ditches and pond margins throughout the Gulf Coast region. Unlike many spider lilies, it thrives in partial shade and wet soils where most other bulbs fail. Excellent at the edge of rain gardens, retention ponds, and boggy low spots.

plant_note: Direct sow after last frost in moist to wet, slightly acidic soil in full to partial sun; plants can also be started from divisions in spring for faster bloom.
directions: Plant in consistently moist to wet, slightly acidic, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; swamp sunflower is arguably the most spectacular of the native sunflowers for fall bloom, producing hundreds of bright yellow flowers on tall multibranching stems from October through frost — often when almost nothing else is blooming. It grows naturally in wet pine flatwoods, swamp margins, and roadside ditches across the coastal South and is perfectly suited to Gulf Coast zone 8–9 gardens where the late season heat and moisture support its growth. The plant spreads aggressively by rhizome and self-seeding; give it space or divide annually in spring. Monarch butterflies and native bees mob the flowers. Pinch tips twice in early summer for a shorter, more compact plant.

plant_note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in wet to moist, acidic, slightly sandy or loamy soil in full sun to partial shade. Swamp Tupelo is adapted to seasonally or permanently flooded conditions and develops distinctive swollen (buttressed) trunk bases in very wet soils; it is most appropriate for rain gardens, wet bottomlands, and natural pond margins.
directions: Swamp Tupelo is a dominant native tree of the permanent swamps, bay forests, and river floodplains of the southeastern coastal plain from Delaware to Florida and west to Texas, often growing alongside bald cypress and water tupelo in pure stands in the deepest, most permanently flooded low-lying land in the South. It is distinguished from the more upland Black Gum (Nyssa sylvatica) by its narrower leaves, preference for wetter conditions, and more southern distribution; in very wet sites it develops the distinctive swollen, buttressed trunk base characteristic of swamp-adapted trees. In fall Swamp Tupelo produces one of the finest foliage displays of any native tree — brilliant crimson, scarlet, and orange — providing spectacular autumn color even in the deep South where fall foliage is often muted. Small blue-black drupes in fall are consumed by a wide variety of birds and mammals. Plant in consistently wet to seasonally flooded, acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; it tolerates standing water better than almost any other native upland tree.

plant_note: Plant fresh acorns in fall or container trees in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral, deep soil in full sun; swamp white oak tolerates extended flooding and clay soils better than most oaks and is an excellent choice for wet, low-lying areas.
directions: Plant in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral, deep soil in full sun; swamp white oak is the premier large native oak for wet, clay-heavy, or periodically flooded sites — conditions where most other oaks decline. The exfoliating, peeling bark on the upper branches is a distinctive and attractive identification feature, revealing lighter bark beneath. The acorns are large, borne in pairs on long stalks (2–4 inches), and are among the most important fall-winter food for wood ducks (which nest in cavities of old trees), turkeys, deer, and dozens of other species. Fall color is yellow to bronze. The tree develops into a large, rounded specimen 50–60 feet tall with a broad, open crown. Its tolerance of wet, clay, compacted, and temporarily flooded conditions makes it an outstanding choice for low areas of the landscape, stream buffers, and rain gardens where it can manage stormwater.

plant_note: Start indoors 6 weeks early; transplant after frost danger passes.
directions: Start Swan River daisy indoors about six weeks before the last frost for earliest bloom. The dainty, fragrant daisies in blue, mauve, and white cover mounding plants ideal for edging, containers, and hanging baskets. Provide full sun and well-drained soil, avoiding overly rich conditions that soften growth. Shear lightly after the first flush and feed lightly to keep the plant blooming through summer.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost; alyssum tolerates light frost and prefers cool weather.
directions: Scatter seed on the soil surface and press gently — light aids germination; just barely cover if needed to prevent the seed from blowing away. Sweet alyssum may slow or stop blooming in peak summer heat; shear plants back by half and they will rebound strongly when temperatures cool in early fall. The honey-scented flowers attract beneficial insects throughout the season.

plant_note: Plant container-grown trees in spring; avoid bare-root transplanting as magnolias resent root disturbance. Site carefully as the roots are shallow and wide-spreading.
directions: Plant in moist to wet, acid soil in full sun to partial shade; this native magnolia naturally inhabits swampy lowlands and stream banks from Massachusetts to Florida and is one of the best large shrubs or small trees for wet areas in the southern garden. The creamy-white, lemon-scented flowers appear over a long season from late spring through summer, and the semi-evergreen to evergreen leaves (depending on winter severity) have a striking silver underside. In the Gulf South it can reach 30 feet as a multi-stemmed, nearly evergreen tree; in colder zones it is smaller and more deciduous. The red fruit clusters provide fall and winter food for songbirds and small mammals.

plant_note: Plant nursery-propagated rhizome divisions in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; never collect from wild populations. Sweet Betsy is a sessile trillium — its maroon flowers sit directly on the three large, attractively mottled leaves without a stalk.
directions: Sweet Betsy trillium is one of the finest sessile trilliums in the Southeast, native to rich, moist deciduous forests from Virginia and Kentucky south to Georgia and Mississippi, where it carpets shaded forest floors with its dramatically silver-mottled leaves topped by stalkless dark maroon to reddish-purple flowers in March and April. The cuneate (wedge-shaped) base of the leaves distinguishes this species from the very similar Trillium sessile, and the flowers emit a faint, sweet fragrance in warm weather that attracts fly pollinators. Like all trilliums, Sweet Betsy requires 5–7 years to bloom from seed — always purchase plants from ethical nurseries propagating their own stock, and never collect from wild populations, which are protected in many states. Plant rhizome divisions in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil (pH 5.5–6.5) in partial to full shade; mulch lightly with leaf litter, keep consistently moist, and allow the foliage to yellow and die back naturally each summer.

plant_note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, cool, humus-rich soil in partial shade. Sweet Birch is an Appalachian mountain tree that performs best in cool, moist, north-facing situations in zones 3–6; in zone 7 it may struggle in hot summer conditions and requires a sheltered, cool, moist site.
directions: Sweet Birch is an elegant native birch of the Appalachian Mountains and northeastern North America, native to cool, moist, well-drained forest slopes and ravines from Maine and Ontario south through the Appalachians to Georgia and Alabama, distinguished from other birches by its dark, cherry-like bark (not peeling white or papery) and the intensely sweet, wintergreen fragrance released when the bark, twigs, or leaves are crushed — the essential oil methyl salicylate (oil of wintergreen) was historically distilled from the bark and twigs for flavoring and as a precursor to salicylic acid. Rich, golden-yellow fall color, pendant pollen catkins in early spring, and an attractive, slightly furrowed dark bark provide four-season ornamental interest in the woodland garden. Sweet Birch is host to dozens of native Lepidoptera species (caterpillars) and provides high-quality nesting habitat for cavity-nesting birds in mature specimens. Grow in cool, moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade; it is most appropriate for the cooler portions of its range and struggles in the heat of zones 7–8.

plant_note: Plant divisions or container plants in fall or early spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial to deep shade; sweet box is excellent for dry shade under trees once established, though it establishes better with consistent moisture in the first season.
directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial to deep shade — sweet box earns its place as one of the finest shade garden shrubs by providing intensely fragrant, tiny white flowers in the dead of winter (January–February in the South), when almost nothing else is blooming, with a honeysuckle-like sweetness that drifts impressively through the still winter air. The small, narrow, glossy, dark evergreen leaves on neat, upright-spreading stems make an attractive groundcover or low shrub year-round. The dwarf variety S. hookeriana var. humilis (dwarf sweet box) is particularly useful as a low-maintenance groundcover substitute for liriope or mondo grass in deep shade, spreading slowly by underground stolons to form a dense colony. Black berries follow the winter flowers. Outstanding for dry to moderately moist shade under trees, requiring essentially no care once established other than removal of dead stems.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall in average to slightly moist, well-drained soil in full to partial sun; sweet coneflower adapts to a wide range of soil types and moisture levels and self-seeds moderately, forming clumps that should be divided every 3–4 years.
directions: Sweet coneflower is one of the most fragrant of all native coneflowers — the flowers emit a noticeable sweet, anise-like scent, particularly on warm afternoons — and is native to moist to dry prairies, savannas, and open woodlands of the central United States from Texas to Illinois. Its 2-foot-wide clumps of deeply textured, softly hairy leaves (the 'subtomentosa' refers to the downy leaf texture) support multiple branched stems bearing dozens of cheerful gold-petaled flowers with dark brown central cones from July through September.
