A popular parent of many prairie garden cultivars, sweet coneflower is valued by horticulturists for its outstanding heat tolerance, mildew resistance (unlike many Rudbeckia species in humid southern climates), and adaptability from clay to sand soils. The seed heads remain ornamental through winter and feed goldfinches, chickadees, and sparrows; pair with prairie grasses and ironweeds for a dramatic native meadow planting.

plant_note: Direct sow outdoors 4–8 weeks before last frost; seeds prefer cool soil (55–65°F) and young plants tolerate frost.
directions: Nick or soak seeds overnight before sowing 1 in deep in cool, enriched soil at the base of a trellis. Sweet peas perform best during cool weather and decline as summer heat arrives — sow as early as possible. Cut flowers frequently, as even a few fading blooms left on the plant will trigger seed set and halt blooming. Provide sturdy support from the start, as vines climb to 4–6 ft.

plant_note: Plant nursery-grown transplants in spring in wet to saturated, acidic, nutrient-poor peat or sphagnum in full sun; sweet pitcher plant has several geographically distinct subspecies adapted to different habitats from the Carolinas to Alabama — select subspecies appropriate to your region.
directions: Sweet pitcher plant earns its name from the intensely sweet, rose-like fragrance of its spring flowers — deep crimson-red blooms that open in April and May on tall, leafless stalks well above the pitchers, producing a fragrance that can be detected several feet away and is considered the most sweetly scented of any Sarracenia flower. The compact, narrow pitchers of this species grow 8–24 inches tall depending on the subspecies and have a distinctive red-veined, green-and-purple coloration that varies widely among the multiple recognized subspecies (Sarracenia rubra ssp. rubra, ssp. jonesii, ssp. gulfensis, ssp. wherryi, and others) distributed across different parts of the coastal plain from North Carolina to Alabama. Subspecies gulfensis (Gulf Coast sweet pitcher plant) is native to the Florida panhandle and Alabama, where it grows in seepage bogs adjacent to other Gulf Coast carnivorous plants; it is one of the most threatened Sarracenia subspecies due to habitat loss. As with all native pitcher plants, purchase only nursery-propagated specimens from ethical native plant dealers and grow in pure water, acidic medium, and full sun.

plant_note: Sow in spring or summer; often blooms the second year as a biennial.
directions: Sweet Rose Magic delights with flowers that open white, shift to soft pink, and mature into rich rose, creating a tricolor display on a single stem. Start seed indoors and barely cover it, or sow outdoors in late spring, transplanting into rich, well-drained soil. As a biennial it typically forms foliage the first year and blooms the second, though it may flower the first season from an early start. Grow in full sun to part shade and deadhead for repeat bloom. The clustered flowers are lightly clove-scented and excellent for cutting.

plant_note: Start indoors 6 weeks early for cut flowers.
directions: Start sweet scabious indoors about six weeks before the last frost, or direct sow once soil warms. The domed pincushion flowers in deep maroon, pink, and white are long-lasting cut flowers and pollinator magnets. Grow in full sun and fertile, well-drained, neutral to alkaline soil, staking tall stems if needed. Harvest or deadhead frequently to keep the plants producing new blooms all summer.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring 2–4 weeks before last frost; does not transplant well due to taproot.
directions: Sow seeds directly in a sunny, well-drained location in early spring; thin to 9–12 in apart as seedlings emerge. The fragrant, fringed, powder-puff flowers in shades of pink, purple, yellow, and white are excellent for cutting, with a pleasant, musky sweet scent. Plants prefer cool weather and will begin to decline as summer heat intensifies — make a second sowing in late summer for fall bloom in warm climates. Successive sowings every 3–4 weeks in spring extend the flowering season through early summer.

plant_note: Plant divisions or stolons in fall or early spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; sweet violet spreads by stolons and self-sown seed to naturalize under deciduous trees. Cold-stratify seed 4–6 weeks if starting from seed.
directions: Plant divisions in partial to full shade in moist, humus-rich soil in fall or early spring; sweet violet is one of the oldest cultivated flowers in the Western world, prized for its intoxicating honey-sweet fragrance and fully edible blooms that appear in late winter to early spring in shades of deep violet, lavender, rose, or white. Both flowers and young leaves are edible — the flowers are crystallized with sugar as classic confections, used fresh in salads, infused into vinegars and syrups, or candied for cake decoration. The plant spreads by stolons and self-sown seed to form a dense, weed-suppressing groundcover under trees and shrubs. Deadhead spent blooms to reduce excessive self-seeding where spread is unwanted. The cultivar 'Königin Charlotte' is considered the most fragrant; 'Rosina' produces soft pink blooms and is slightly less vigorous. An outstanding edible ornamental for shaded paths and beds under deciduous trees in zones 5–9.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification and spring germination; in mild climates can also be sown in early spring. Spreads vigorously by stolons and self-seeds freely once established.
directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich soil in partial shade to light morning sun; in zone 8–9 this European violet stays semi-evergreen in winter and begins flowering as early as December in mild years, providing critical early nectar for emerging queen bumblebees. The fragrant violet or white flowers are fully edible and prized for crystallizing, flavoring syrups, and garnishing desserts and cocktails; the leaves are also edible raw or cooked as greens. The plant spreads energetically by stolons and self-seeds, naturalizing quickly under deciduous trees and shrubs. Remove unwanted seedlings annually if spread needs to be managed in a formal garden.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant can go out 2–4 weeks before last frost as plants are frost-tolerant.
directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep in well-drained germination mix; germination is fast and reliable at 65–70°F. Plant in full sun in well-drained, neutral to slightly alkaline soil; sweet William is technically a biennial that flowers in its second year, but modern annual varieties bloom the first year from an early indoor start. The domed, fragrant flower clusters in vivid shades of red, pink, white, and bicolor appear in late spring and early summer. Deadhead spent clusters promptly to extend the blooming period; plants self-sow readily to maintain a colony from year to year.

plant_note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped trees in late winter or spring in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moist soil; avoid alkaline soils which cause chlorosis. Choose sterile (fruitless) cultivars like 'Rotundiloba' if the spiky seed balls are undesirable.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil in full sun; sweetgum is one of the most common and beautiful deciduous trees of the eastern US, producing perhaps the most spectacular and varied fall color display of any native tree — individual trees vary from pure yellow to orange to red to deep burgundy-purple, and many trees display all these colors simultaneously on the same tree. The star-shaped leaves are distinctive and beautiful. The 'gumballs' (spiky spherical seed capsules) are the tree's only liability — the seeds provide important food for finches and small rodents but the fallen capsules can be a nuisance underfoot. Sterile cultivars like 'Rotundiloba' produce no seeds and have rounder leaf lobes. The tree grows 1–2 feet per year and is exceptionally adaptable to a wide range of conditions. The aromatic resin (storax) has traditional medicinal and fragrance uses.

plant_note: Plant container shrubs in spring in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial shade; sweetleaf is not commonly available in nurseries — look for it from native plant specialists and botanic gardens.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial shade; sweetleaf (also called horse sugar) is a native shrub or small tree of moist woodland slopes and stream banks in the southeastern US, best known for three remarkable characteristics: the leaves have a distinctly sweet taste (hence both common names), deer browse the sweet foliage voraciously; the bright yellow flower clusters in late winter to early spring appear before most other shrubs and provide crucial early-season nectar for queen bumblebees emerging from overwintering; and it is the exclusive larval host plant for Henry's elfin butterfly, a small, understated but increasingly rare butterfly that depends entirely on this plant for its caterpillars. Underused in horticulture, sweetleaf deserves much wider planting for its early nectar value and butterfly support.

plant_note: Start indoors in cool, dark conditions; transplant in early spring or fall.
directions: This Swiss heirloom is the parent of most modern pansies, producing low, bushy plants covered in showy, large, velvety flowers. Start seed indoors in cool conditions and darkness, then grow seedlings on in bright light before transplanting in early spring or fall. Pansies thrive in cool weather and make a fine companion to spring bulbs. Grow in full sun to part shade in rich, moist soil and deadhead to extend bloom. The edible petals garnish soft cheeses and baked goods.

plant_note: Surface sow indoors; transplant after frost danger passes.
directions: Strawflowers live up to their name, with stiff, papery bracts that look like colored straw and never wilt, making them the classic everlasting cut flower. Surface sow the seed indoors since light aids germination, then transplant after frost into full sun and well-drained soil. Swiss Giants produce extra-large double blooms in bright shades on long, sturdy stems. Cut flowers just before they fully open and hang them upside down to dry for lasting arrangements. Deadhead spent blooms to keep plants flowering, and enjoy the pollinators they attract.

plant_note: Sow indoors and cover; seed germinates best in darkness and cool conditions.
directions: Start seed indoors covered and in darkness, keeping the soil cool for best germination. This heirloom mix produces large, velvety, edible blossoms in blue, purple, yellow, orange, and burgundy. Pansies love cool weather, so transplant in early spring or fall and grow in full sun to part shade with steady moisture. Deadhead often and they will bloom prolifically until summer heat or hard freezes arrive.

plant_note: Direct sow after last frost in spring; warm soil (above 60°F) promotes best germination.
directions: Sow seeds in full sun after last frost in average to poor, well-drained soil; switchgrass is extremely adaptable and tolerates clay, sand, wet or dry conditions, and even brief flooding — a true workhorse native grass. The airy, branching panicles of tiny seeds in late summer create a misty, luminous effect, and many cultivars develop spectacular red, burgundy, or orange fall color. Cut back to 4–6 in in late winter; divide every 3–4 years in spring when the clump center dies out. Named cultivars like 'Shenandoah' and 'Prairie Fire' offer predictably strong red fall color.

plant_note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in well-drained, slightly alkaline, rocky limestone or loamy soil in partial shade to filtered sun. Sycamore-leaf Snowbell is a Texas endemic adapted to the alkaline limestone soils of the Edwards Plateau and adjacent Hill Country.
directions: Sycamore-leaf Snowbell is a beautiful native understory shrub or small tree, endemic to the limestone canyons, creek banks, and rocky woodland slopes of the Texas Edwards Plateau and Hill Country, where it grows in the shaded understory of live oak and Texas cedar elm woodlands in thin, alkaline, limestone-derived soils. In April and May it produces clusters of fragrant, white, pendant bell-shaped flowers similar to those of other snowbells (Styrax species), with the blooms arranged more openly than the closely related American Snowbell (Styrax americanus). The large, maple-like (plataniform) leaves are a distinctive characteristic that sets it apart from other native snowbells. An excellent shrub for Texas Hill Country native landscapes and limestone-soil gardens in zones 7–9, it is drought-tolerant once established in well-drained alkaline soil and requires no fertilizer or supplemental irrigation after the first two seasons. Source from specialty native Texas plant nurseries.

plant_note: Direct sow seed in fall (cold stratification improves germination) in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; tall bellflower is a biennial that blooms in its second year — establish a planting from seed in two successive years to ensure continuous bloom thereafter.
directions: Sow seed in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade — tall bellflower is a striking native biennial of eastern forest understories that can reach 3–6 feet tall when in bloom, producing a long terminal raceme and lateral branches covered with flat, five-lobed blue-violet flowers that open wide (unlike the nodding, bell-shaped flowers of most bellflowers) throughout summer. It is one of the most shade-tolerant native wildflowers capable of producing impressive flowering stems in quite deep shade. The long, curved styles and protruding stamens give the flowers a distinctive star-burst appearance. The plant is a true biennial — forming a rosette in year one, blooming and setting seed in year two, then dying. Self-sows reliably in appropriate conditions to maintain a naturalized colony. An outstanding native plant for creating a vertical accent in the shaded native garden in zones 3–8.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring; seed needs darkness to germinate.
directions: Bachelor's button, also known as cornflower, is a drought-tolerant, low-maintenance annual with flowers great for cutting or drying and edible petals for garnishing salads. Direct sow in early spring, covering the seed since it needs darkness to germinate, and thin seedlings once up. Grow in full sun in average, well-drained soil. This tall blend self-sows readily, naturalizing for another crop of flowers the following year. Deadhead or harvest often to prolong the bloom period and support pollinators.

plant_note: Surface sow in spring as seed needs light to germinate; division of established clumps in early spring is more reliable and faster.
directions: Scatter seed on the surface of average, well-drained soil in full sun; tall goldenrod is the single most important plant for migrating monarch butterflies and late-season native bees, providing nectar during the critical fall migration period. Despite its reputation for causing hay fever (the actual culprit is ragweed, which blooms simultaneously), goldenrod pollen is heavy and insect-pollinated, not airborne. The plant spreads aggressively by rhizome in fertile soil; divide every 2–3 years to maintain vigor, using divisions to fill new areas. Excellent companion for ironweed (Vernonia), asters, and Joe-Pye weed in a late-season native garden.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification, or sow indoors 8 weeks before last frost; transplant in spring after frost risk passes — tall ironweed self-seeds freely in moist meadows and stream margins.
directions: Tall ironweed earns its name — plants commonly reach 8 to 10 feet in moist bottomland meadows and woodland edges, bearing brilliant magenta-purple, flat-topped flower clusters in late summer and early fall that stand above neighboring prairie plants and attract a spectacular diversity of butterflies and native bees. As one of the tallest native perennials in the eastern United States, it provides a dramatic vertical accent in native gardens, meadows, and rain garden margins where few other plants achieve its scale while remaining fully native. The intense royal purple flowers are among the most vivid of any North American wildflower — the color is caused by anthocyanin pigments and does not fade even in full southern sun. Cut plants back by half in late June to reduce height and encourage branching if growing in a formal border; allow spent flower heads to stand through winter to feed goldfinches and sparrows.

plant_note: Surface sow indoors; snapdragons tolerate cool weather when hardened off.
directions: This tall blend of snapdragons adds classic charm to gardens and cut-flower arrangements, and its blooms are a bee magnet as bumblebees climb right into the mouths. Surface sow the tiny seed indoors since light aids germination, and grow seedlings on before transplanting into rich, well-drained soil. Snapdragons prefer cool weather, so set out hardened plants a bit before the last frost and provide late-afternoon shade in hot regions. Pinch young plants to encourage branching and stake the tall spikes if needed. The edible flowers make a pretty garnish, and deadheading prolongs the display.

plant_note: Direct sow seed in fall in well-drained to average, slightly acidic to neutral, dry to moist soil in partial shade; tall thimbleweed self-sows readily and naturalizes in appropriate dry to moist woodland conditions without being invasive.
directions: Sow seed in fall in well-drained to average, slightly acidic to neutral, dry to moist soil in partial shade — tall thimbleweed is an upright, woodland-edge native anemone whose white, 1–1.5 inch flowers are followed by the plant's most distinctive feature: elongated, thimble-shaped seed heads that are covered in cottony white fibers (resembling a thimble wrapped in white fluff) and persist well into winter, providing long-lasting ornamental value. The flowers attract solitary native bees and small beetles. It grows naturally in dry to moist open woodland, woodland edges, and clearings throughout the eastern and central US, tolerating the dry shade beneath dense tree canopies better than most native wildflowers. The persistent cottony seed heads are useful in dried arrangements. More drought-tolerant than most anemone species. An excellent and underused native for dry, shaded woodland garden settings.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost after cold-stratifying for 1–2 weeks; often blooms from seed the first year.
directions: Cold-stratify seeds in moist towels for 1–2 weeks at 35–40°F, then sow indoors at 65–75°F; germination remains erratic over 14–28 days. Plant in full sun in poor to average, well-drained soil; this South American native is drought-tolerant and naturalizes readily once established. The wiry, branching stems carry clusters of small purple flowers from midsummer through frost at an unusual height that allows it to weave transparently through other plants. Grown as an annual north of zone 7, it self-sows prolifically even in cold climates and seedlings replace killed parent plants.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost; thrives in heat.
directions: Direct sow tassel flower after the last frost in warm, well-drained soil. The small brush-like scarlet and orange flowers rise on wiry stems above tidy rosettes, adding fine texture to arrangements. It thrives in heat and full sun and blooms freely all summer with little care. Deadhead to prolong flowering and allow occasional self-sowing for future plants.

plant_note: Plant container-grown transplants or rooted cuttings in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun. Tawnyberry Holly is dioecious — both male and female plants are needed for berry production. It is best suited to frost-free Zone 10–11 landscapes in south Florida.
directions: Tawnyberry Holly is a native evergreen holly tree of the Florida Keys, Miami-Dade rockland hammocks, and Caribbean islands, producing small clusters of tawny-orange to reddish-black berries that are eagerly consumed by white-crowned pigeons, mockingbirds, and other fruit-eating birds in the subtropical forests of southernmost Florida. Growing as a slender understory tree to small specimen tree in the hammock community, Tawnyberry Holly has glossy, dark green evergreen leaves and small, inconspicuous white flowers in spring. An important component of the rare Florida Keys and Miami-Dade pine rockland hammock community — one of the most imperiled plant communities in the United States — this species deserves wider cultivation as an ornamental holly in frost-free south Florida landscapes. Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun in Zone 10–11; both male and female plants are needed for berry production.

plant_note: Plant in fall; includes paperwhites for forcing.
directions: Plant tazetta daffodil bulbs in fall six inches deep in well-drained soil, or force paperwhite types indoors in pebbles and water. These clustered, intensely fragrant narcissi are well suited to mild-winter gardens and bloom early. Give them full sun and let the foliage die back naturally outdoors. Forced bulbs are generally discarded after flowering, while garden bulbs naturalize in suitable zones.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil and full sun.
directions: Teddy Bear produces fluffy, bright-yellow puffball blooms 3 to 5 inches across on a compact, multi-branching plant 24 to 36 inches tall. Direct sow the seed about an inch deep after the last frost in warm soil and full sun. Thin seedlings to give each plant room to branch and form its many pompom flowers. It works beautifully as a short hedge and makes a fabulous, long-lasting cut flower. Water steadily while young and enjoy the double blooms all summer.

plant_note: Direct sow cold-stratified seed in fall in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, thin, rocky, cedar glade soil in full sun; Tennessee coneflower is adapted to the extreme conditions of limestone cedar glades and requires excellent drainage — it will rot in clay or wet soils.
directions: Sow cold-stratified seed in fall in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, thin rocky soil in full sun — Tennessee coneflower is a Federally Threatened native species known from only a handful of cedar glade sites in Davidson and Rutherford counties of central Tennessee, where it grows in the extremely shallow, rocky, alkaline soil over limestone bedrock that characterizes these rare ecosystems. It is distinguished from other coneflowers by its upward-reflexed (rather than drooping) ray petals, giving the flower a more open, daisy-like appearance. The species was removed from the federal endangered species list in 2011 after recovery efforts increased its wild populations — a conservation success story. It performs well in gardens as a drought-tolerant, heat-hardy perennial for well-drained, sunny, alkaline to neutral soils in zones 4–9. Growing this species in cultivation helps maintain genetic diversity and reduces collection pressure on wild populations. Purchase only nursery-propagated plants.

plant_note: Plant tubers outdoors after the last frost when the soil is warm.
directions: Terracotta produces romantic terracotta-bronze blooms with wavy petals on long stems, making it an outstanding cut flower and a strong tuber producer. Plant tubers 4-6 inches deep in well-drained, fertile soil once frost has passed, laying them horizontally with the eye up. Provide full sun and stake tall plants to keep the long cutting stems upright. Cut flowers frequently and deadhead to encourage continuous bloom into fall. Dig and store the tubers before hard frost in cold climates.

plant_note: Surface-sow indoors; seed needs light to germinate. Transplant when hardened off.
directions: Start seed indoors on the soil surface under light, since it needs light to germinate. This mix produces tall, upright spikes of ruffled, nectar-rich blooms in vivid colors. Snapdragons prefer cool weather, so transplant in early spring for strong stems and abundant flowers. Pinch young plants to encourage branching and cut spikes regularly for long-lasting bouquets and repeat bloom.

plant_note: Direct sow seed or plant divisions in fall in well-drained, alkaline to neutral, lean, rocky or sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; Texas betony is native to dry limestone slopes and rocky arroyos and requires excellent drainage — it declines quickly in heavy, wet clay soils. The plant self-sows to naturalize in appropriate, well-drained conditions.
directions: Plant in well-drained, lean, alkaline to neutral, rocky or sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; Texas betony is a native wildflower of the limestone hills of Texas and the arid Southwest, producing tubular, brilliant scarlet flowers in whorls along the square stems of a spreading mint-family plant from late winter through frost — one of the longest-blooming native perennials in the Southern garden. The vivid red flowers are irresistible to hummingbirds and provide one of the best native hummingbird plants for xeric, alkaline-soil gardens. It is extremely drought and heat tolerant, thriving in the conditions that defeat most flowering perennials in the hot, dry Southwest. The felted, gray-green leaves provide attractive texture even between bloom cycles. Self-sows freely in appropriate, well-drained conditions to naturalize. Outstanding in native plant gardens, xeric landscapes, and rock gardens in zone 7–11.

plant_note: Sow in fall; scarify seed and inoculate with rhizobia for best results. Blooms the following spring.
directions: Nick or soak the hard seed coat and sow in fall so plants establish for spring bloom. The state flower of Texas, this native annual lupine forms spikes of striking blue flowers tipped with white. Grow in full sun and dry, well-drained soil where, as a legume, it fixes nitrogen and needs little care. Let plants set seed to naturalize, and expect the strongest displays after a cool, moist winter.

plant_note: Plant container-grown specimens in spring in alkaline, well-drained, clay or sandy loam soil in full sun; Texas ebony is slow-growing and long-lived — plant young trees for establishment, as older specimens transplant poorly due to deep taproots.
directions: Texas ebony is one of the finest ornamental trees native to south Texas and northeastern Mexico, producing a dense, rounded crown of small, dark green compound leaves on thorny branches — the tree's extremely hard, black-brown heartwood (comparable in density to commercial ebony) is prized for wood turning and tool handles, and the species' name reflects this extraordinary wood quality. Clusters of fragrant, creamy-white to pale yellow flowers appear in summer and fall and are heavily visited by native bees and honeybees, producing a distinctive honey; the large, woody seed pods split open in fall to reveal shiny black seeds that are edible when roasted and were an important food source for indigenous peoples of the Rio Grande Valley. In south Texas (zone 9–10) it is among the most recommended native trees for year-round evergreen screening, wildlife habitat, and creating dense shade in landscapes — slow-growing but extremely drought-tolerant and long-lived once established. It also provides one of the most important nesting trees for the endangered ferruginous pygmy owl in the lower Rio Grande Valley.

plant_note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in fall in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Texas false dragonhead spreads by rhizomes and self-seeds to form colonies in appropriate conditions — provide ample moisture for best performance.
directions: Texas false dragonhead is a rare native perennial of Texas endemic to rocky, moist, calcareous prairies and streamside meadows in the Edwards Plateau and surrounding regions — producing flower spikes similar to the more widely known obedient plant (Physostegia virginiana) but with deeper, brighter pink-purple flowers and a more compact, heat-adapted growth habit suited to south-central Texas conditions. Like other Physostegia species, the individual tubular flowers are hinged and will stay in position when pushed — the 'obedient' character that gives the related species its common name — making them a quirky, interactive feature of native garden tours. Pollinators, especially bumblebees and hummingbirds, visit the tubular, orchid-like flowers throughout summer; in gardens it provides a reliable mid-season pollinator spike when many spring bloomers have finished. It spreads by rhizomes similarly to its relatives and can form assertive colonies in moist, rich soil — site where spreading is welcome or divide regularly to control.

plant_note: Direct sow seed in fall in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, rocky to loamy, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun to light shade; Texas larkspur is a cool-season annual or biennial that requires cold stratification — fall sowing in Gulf Coast gardens provides natural stratification and leads to bloom in April–May before summer heat arrives.
directions: Direct sow in fall in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, rocky to loamy, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun — Texas larkspur is the native delphinium of southern prairies, limestone cedar glades, and rocky roadsides from Texas to the Carolinas, producing tall, slender spikes of deeply blue, white, or blue-and-white bicolored flowers in April–May before summer heat arrives, with the delicate, finely cut, ferny foliage typical of the delphinium family. The flowers are intensely attractive to ruby-throated hummingbirds and long-tongued native bees. Unlike the Pacific Giant and other hybrid garden delphiniums that struggle in heat and humidity, Texas larkspur is fully adapted to the alkaline limestone soils, hot springs, and dry summers of the southern prairies. It self-sows readily to maintain naturalized colonies in open, well-drained, sunny conditions and is excellent for wildflower meadow plantings in the southern Gulf Coast and Texas Hill Country. All parts are toxic if consumed.

plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring in alkaline, rocky, well-drained soil; extremely slow-growing but extremely long-lived. Scarify seeds before sowing to aid germination.
directions: Plant in alkaline to neutral, rocky or caliche, sharply drained soil in full sun; this aristocratic native evergreen shrub of the Texas Hill Country and desert Southwest is prized for its intensely fragrant violet-purple flower clusters that smell like Concord grape candy and appear in early spring. It is extremely slow-growing (6–12 inches per year), drought tolerant once established, and virtually pest and disease free. The distinctive woody seed pods contain bright red seeds that are highly toxic if eaten. Give it an excellent drainage and poor alkaline soil — rich, moist conditions cause root rot. An excellent foundation or specimen plant for Gulf Coast and zone 8–9 gardens where winter temperatures stay above 10°F.

common_name: Texas Olive (Mexican Olive, Anacahuita, Wild Olive)
plant_note: Plant container-grown trees in spring in very well-drained, alkaline to neutral, lean to moderately fertile soil in full sun; Texas olive is native to extreme south Texas and northeastern Mexico and prefers alkaline, calcareous soils. It is tolerant of clay and caliche but requires excellent drainage and is damaged at 22–25°F.
directions: Plant in well-drained, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; Texas olive is one of the most spectacular native flowering trees of the Rio Grande Plains and an outstanding ornamental for zone 8–11 gardens. The large, tropical-looking leathery leaves and stunning pure white, 2-inch funnel-shaped flowers with a yellow throat are produced year-round in warm weather, attracting bees, butterflies, and birds. The olive-like fruits ripen to white and are eaten by mockingbirds and other wildlife. The tree is semi-evergreen, holding its large velvety leaves in mild winters and dropping them only in cold or drought. It is highly drought tolerant once established and grows naturally on rocky limestone slopes, chaparral, and thorny brush country of the Rio Grande Plains. In zone 8a it may be killed to the ground in severe winters but resprouting from roots is common.

plant_note: Plant container specimens in fall in well-drained, rocky to loamy, alkaline to slightly acidic, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun to partial shade; Texas redbud is significantly more drought, heat, and alkaline-soil tolerant than the standard eastern redbud — it is the variety to choose for Gulf Coast and South Texas gardens.
directions: Plant in well-drained, alkaline to slightly acidic, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun to partial shade — Texas redbud is the heat-and-drought-hardy western variety of our native eastern redbud, offering the same spectacular early spring bloom — dense clusters of magenta-pink pea-flowers covering every branch before the leaves emerge — with significantly improved performance in the rocky limestone soils, alkaline caliche, and intense summer heat of the western Gulf Coast and Edwards Plateau. The foliage is rounded, glossy, and notably thicker and more heat-tolerant than the standard species, holding up through brutal Texas summers without scorching. Selected cultivars like 'Oklahoma' (with very dark, glossy foliage and wine-red flowers) and 'Texas White' (clear white flowers) offer outstanding ornamental refinements over the species. An outstanding small patio or understory tree for dry, alkaline, sunny to lightly shaded garden sites in zone 6–9, requiring minimal water once established.

common_name: Texas Sabal Palm (Rio Grande Palmetto, Mexican Palmetto)
plant_note: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to alkaline, lean to moderately fertile soil in full sun; Texas sabal palm is extremely drought tolerant once established and native to the Rio Grande Delta, where it grows in sandy, saline, and seasonally flooded soils. It is one of the most heat- and drought-tolerant native palms in the US.
directions: Plant in well-drained, sandy, lean soil in full sun; the Texas sabal palm is the only native palm of the Rio Grande Delta — a majestic, fan-leafed palm native to the last remaining wild groves at the southern tip of Texas, now found in only a few protected areas such as the Sabal Palm Sanctuary near Brownsville. It is closely related to the familiar Sabal palmetto of Florida and is one of the most cold-hardy and drought-tolerant of all native palms. The massive, deeply curved fan leaves on 5-6 foot stalks create a dramatic tropical silhouette; established trees develop a broad, imposing crown. Black fruits ripen in fall and are relished by birds. A premier palm for zone 8–11 Gulf Coast gardens — exceptionally wind, salt, and drought tolerant once established and requiring little maintenance. Grow from locally sourced nursery stock; wild populations are endangered.

plant_note: Plant container-grown transplants in well-drained to xeric, alkaline to neutral, lean soil in full sun; sotol requires excellent drainage and grows best in rocky, limestone-based soils. It is extremely slow-growing and patience is required — established plants can live for many decades.
directions: Plant in well-drained, rocky, lean, alkaline soil in full sun; Texas sotol is a dramatic native xeriscape plant of the Chihuahuan Desert and Edwards Plateau, forming a globe of many hundreds of narrow, finely toothed, spoon-based leaves with serrated margins and a distinctive dried-flower-arrangement appearance year-round. Established plants occasionally send up towering flower stalks 10–15 feet tall bearing dense clusters of small white flowers in summer, then produce fruit and seeds over the following months before dying back — the vegetative rosette survives and eventually produces another stalk. The base of the leaves was a critical food staple for the Chihuahuan people who roasted and ate the heart (similar to agave); the leaves are used to make sotol fiber and baskets. Slow-growing but extremely long-lived and virtually maintenance-free once established.

plant_note: Direct sow seed in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, dry to moderately moist, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; Texas verbena overwinters as a basal rosette in zone 7–9 and blooms prolifically in spring before summer heat arrives, then self-sows to maintain a persistent colony.
directions: Direct sow in fall in well-drained, dry to moderately moist, sandy to loamy soil in full sun — Texas verbena is a delicate-looking but tough native annual of the Gulf Coast prairies and open roadsides of Texas, Louisiana, and adjacent states, producing slender, erect stems topped with small but brilliantly colored lavender-pink to pink-purple flower spikes from late winter through spring. Despite its fine, airy texture, it is remarkably heat and drought tolerant and is one of the most important early-season nectar plants for gulf fritillary and phaon crescent butterflies emerging from winter dormancy. It self-sows prolifically in open, disturbed soils to maintain self-sustaining colonies in roadsides, meadows, and sunny, dry garden borders. Excellent for adding fine texture and early spring color to wildflower meadow plantings alongside bluebonnets, Indian paintbrush, and Indian blanket. Essentially no maintenance once established from seed.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring; seed germinates in darkness.
directions: The Bride stands out among bachelor's buttons for its unusual snowy-white, frilly blooms and captivating honey fragrance. Direct sow in early spring, covering the seed since darkness aids germination, and thin the seedlings once established. Grow in full sun in average, well-drained soil where this drought-tolerant annual needs little care. Frequent cutting or deadheading keeps the plants producing their pollinator-friendly flowers, which draw bees and butterflies. Leave a few flowers to set seed and it will often self-sow.

plant_note: Plant the potted or bare-root shrub in spring after frost danger passes.
directions: The Champion Sunblush is an easy-care flowering rose shrub with brilliant, sun-kissed petals suited to both beginner and experienced gardeners. Set the plant in full sun in rich, well-drained soil, digging a hole wide enough for the roots and watering in well. Provide consistent moisture at the base to keep foliage dry, and mulch to conserve water and moderate soil temperature. Prune in late winter to shape the shrub and remove dead or crossing canes, and feed in spring as growth begins. Hardy in zones 5 to 9, it rewards steady care with repeat blooms through the season.

plant_note: Plant nursery stock in spring after the ground thaws; can also plant in early fall.
directions: The Rising Sun redbud is a small deciduous tree prized for new growth that emerges in fiery apricot, orange, and gold before aging to lime and green. Site it in rich, well-drained soil where it receives morning sun and some afternoon shade in hot climates. Rosy-pink pea-like flowers cover the bare branches in early spring before the leaves unfurl. Water deeply during the first two years to establish the root system, then supplement only in dry spells. Prune lightly after flowering to shape the canopy.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring or summer; biennial that blooms the second year.
directions: The Watchman appears nearly black from a distance but reveals a rich purplish-burgundy cast up close on towering flower spikes. Direct sow the seed in spring or summer, barely covering it, since this biennial usually forms a rosette the first year and blooms the second. Grow in full sun in rich, well-drained soil and stake the tall spikes in windy locations. The dramatic towers make an excellent backdrop for pink, red, yellow, or white flowers and attract bees and butterflies. Let some flowers set seed and it will readily self-sow for future years.

plant_note: Sprinkle seeds on moist sphagnum moss or peat/perlite mix in full sun; thread-leaf sundew produces tall, erect, thread-like leaves to 12 inches that are covered in mucilaginous glands — one of the most spectacular of all sundews, capable of trapping large insects including damselflies.
directions: Thread-leaf sundew is one of the most spectacular of all North American carnivorous plants — producing upright, thread-like leaves up to a foot tall that are covered along their entire length with long, glistening, red-tipped tentacles dripping with sticky mucilage, making the plant look like something from a science fiction film. Native to coastal plain bogs of New Jersey and the Carolinas, it is one of the largest and most dramatic of all North American sundews, capable of trapping insects as large as damselflies and small bees on its long, adhesive leaves. Pink flowers appear on tall stalks in summer; the plant goes dormant in winter in zones 5–7, forming a tight, dry bud (hibernaculum) at the soil surface that is cold-hardy to well below freezing, making it one of the more cold-tolerant carnivorous plants for outdoor bog gardens. It produces gemmae (vegetative propagules) in fall that can be planted to create new individuals — an easy propagation method that makes it more accessible than many other carnivorous plants. Grow in the standard wet, acidic, nutrient-poor bog conditions with pure water only.

plant_note: Surface sow seed in early spring or start transplants; threadleaf coreopsis is more reliably propagated by division than seed — divisions establish quickly in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to poor, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade.
directions: Grow in well-drained, average to poor soil in full sun to partial shade — threadleaf coreopsis is the most finely textured and perhaps the most garden-adaptable of all native coreopsis species, producing a season-long display of bright golden-yellow daisy flowers above delicate, needle-like whorled foliage from early summer through fall frost. Unlike most coreopsis, the thread-like leaves create an exceptionally fine-textured mound that contrasts beautifully with bold-leafed perennials. It spreads steadily by rhizome to form wide, weed-suppressing clumps and thrives in poor, dry soils where richer garden perennials struggle. Named cultivars 'Moonbeam' (pale yellow), 'Zagreb' (bright gold, compact), and 'Golden Gain' are widely available and extremely garden-worthy. Deadheading is optional — the plant reblooms regardless, but removing spent flowers extends peak flowering. One of the longest-blooming native perennials available.

plant_note: Plant in spring or fall; space 5-6 ft apart for a solid privacy screen.
directions: Thuja Green Giant is a fast-growing evergreen conifer widely used as a living privacy hedge, adding several feet of dense, rich green foliage each year. Plant in full sun to partial shade in moist, well-drained soil, spacing plants 5 to 6 feet apart for a continuous screen. Water regularly the first season to establish deep roots; once mature it tolerates heat, humidity, and brief drought. Its naturally pyramidal habit rarely needs pruning, though it takes shearing well if a formal shape is desired. It resists deer browsing and most pests.

plant_note: Plant in spring or fall in a sunny, well-drained spot.
directions: Thuja Virginian is a compact arborvitae hybrid that offers the dense evergreen screening of Green Giant in a more manageable size for smaller yards. Plant in full sun in fertile, well-drained soil and water consistently through the first growing season to establish. Its tight, upright pyramidal form makes an excellent privacy hedge or specimen without heavy pruning. Mature plants tolerate heat and humidity and stand up to deer better than many evergreens. Mulch the root zone to conserve moisture and moderate soil temperature.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil and full sun.
directions: Thumbelina is a charming dwarf zinnia and 1963 All-America Selections winner, producing abundant cheerful blooms in a wide range of colors on compact plants. Direct sow after the last frost in warm soil and full sun, thinning seedlings for good airflow. Its small stature suits containers, edging, and the front of borders. Deadhead spent blooms to keep flowers coming through summer and into fall. Water at the base to reduce foliar disease, and enjoy the pollinators the flowers attract.

plant_note: Plant after the last frost in spring in a hot, sunny location.
directions: Thunderstruck Coral Boom is a fast-growing crape myrtle that produces large panicles of vivid coral-pink blooms from early summer into fall. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil; more sun means more flowers and better mildew resistance. Its multi-season interest includes attractive exfoliating bark in winter and colorful fall foliage. Prune in late winter to remove crossing branches and shape the canopy, avoiding heavy topping. Water regularly while establishing, after which it becomes notably heat- and drought-tolerant.

plant_note: Plant after the last frost in a warm, sunny spot.
directions: Thunderstruck Rumblin' Red is a crape myrtle celebrated for its fiery red summer blooms and strong year-round interest. Site it in full sun in well-drained soil, where abundant light maximizes flowering and helps prevent powdery mildew. The dark-tinged foliage and smooth exfoliating bark add appeal beyond the long bloom season. Prune lightly in late winter to maintain a tree-like structure rather than topping it hard. Once established it is quite tolerant of heat and dry conditions, needing supplemental water only in extended droughts.

plant_note: Sow in fall or early spring; a reliable wildflower for meadows.
directions: Scatter tidy tips seed on cool, moist soil in fall or early spring and rake in lightly. This California native produces cheerful yellow daisies tipped in white that attract native bees and butterflies. It thrives on lean soil with minimal water once established, making it ideal for wildflower meadows and drought-tolerant plantings. Allow some flowers to set seed for reliable self-sowing in future seasons.

plant_note: Plant offsets or container plants in very well-drained, sandy to loamy, dry, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; tiger aloe naturally grows in rock crevices and the partial shade of cliff faces in its native South Africa and is one of the few aloes that actively prefers some shade in hot climates.
directions: Plant in very well-drained, dry, sandy to loamy soil in partial shade — unlike most aloes that demand full sun, tiger aloe is unique in preferring partial shade or bright indirect light, making it one of the most valuable and unusual succulents for shaded dry garden conditions. The compact, triangular, dark green leaves arranged in three ranks are beautifully marked with irregular white horizontal bands and spots — a striking, ornamental pattern that gives rise to the common names tiger and partridge breast. The salmon-pink to coral-red tubular flowers on tall stalks appear in winter and spring, providing color when the garden needs it most. Compact enough for windowsills, containers, and small garden pockets; it produces offsets slowly to form small clusters. Native to the scrubby karoo shrubland and rocky hillside outcrops of South Africa. Outstanding as a houseplant in bright indirect light or as an outdoor container plant in shade in Gulf Coast zone 9–11.

plant_note: Plant bulbs after all frost danger passes in spring, once soil has warmed above 60°F.
directions: Plant bulbs 3–4 in deep in well-drained, moderately fertile soil; each exotic three-petaled flower lasts only one day but new buds open in succession over weeks. The vivid blooms come in yellow, orange, red, pink, and white, typically with a heavily spotted or blotched center cup. Plant in groups of a dozen or more for a showy display from midsummer into fall. In zones colder than 8, dig bulbs after first frost, dry thoroughly, and store in paper bags in a cool, frost-free location.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall; propagates from stem bulbils.
directions: Plant tiger lily bulbs in fall six to eight inches deep in rich, moist, well-drained soil in full sun to light shade. The recurved orange flowers heavily spotted with black nod in mid to late summer atop tall sturdy stems. This vigorous heirloom produces small dark bulbils in the leaf axils that can be planted to grow new bulbs. It is a known carrier of viruses, so plant it well away from other prized lilies.

plant_note: Plant in spring or early fall in a spot with morning sun and afternoon shade.
directions: Tilt-A-Swirl is a bigleaf hydrangea whose mophead blooms shift through pink, red, and green as the season progresses, with flower color influenced by soil pH. Plant in rich, moist, well-drained soil where it gets morning sun and shade during the hottest part of the day. Keep the soil consistently moist, especially in summer, as this species wilts quickly when dry. It blooms on old and new wood, so avoid heavy pruning; simply remove spent flowers and dead stems in late winter. Amend soil with aluminum sulfate for bluer tones or lime for pinker ones.

plant_note: Surface sow in early spring; seed needs light to germinate.
directions: Tiny Tim is a compact, honey-scented sweet alyssum that stays covered in tiny flowers all season with no need to shear back. Surface sow the seed in early spring since light aids germination, pressing it into the soil without covering. Grow in full sun to part shade in average, well-drained soil where it forms a fragrant, spreading groundcover. It withstands heat and drought while drawing beneficial insects and pollinators. Use it to edge beds, tuck between pavers, or spill from containers and hanging baskets.

plant_note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil; ideal for containers or small beds.
directions: Tiny Tower is a very slow-growing dwarf Alberta spruce that keeps a naturally dense, narrow pyramidal shape without shearing, making it perfect for small gardens, entryways, and containers. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil and provide good air circulation to discourage spider mites, its main pest. Water regularly the first two seasons and mulch to keep roots cool and moist. In hot climates give it some afternoon shade to prevent needle scorch. Its compact evergreen form also serves as a living holiday tree.

plant_note: Soak seed overnight and sow deep; resents transplanting so direct sow.
directions: Soak the large seed overnight and sow about half an inch deep directly in the garden after frost. This dwarf, non-trailing variety produces a cheerful mix of yellow, red, and orange blooms held above the foliage. Both the peppery leaves and flowers are edible, making it a favorite for salads and companion planting. Grow in full sun and lean soil, since rich ground yields lots of leaves but fewer flowers.

plant_note: Plant divisions in early spring as growth begins; they establish quickly and bloom in their first season from divisions.
directions: Plant in humus-rich, consistently moist, well-drained shade; toad lily is an elegant late-season bloomer that produces exotic-looking, orchid-like flowers in white, purple, or lilac with dark spots from late summer through fall. The arching stems with clasping leaves create a graceful, architectural presence throughout the season. Consistent moisture is essential — plants decline in dry soil; mulch heavily to maintain soil moisture and temperature. Divide every 3–4 years in early spring to manage the slowly spreading clumps.

plant_note: Plant nursery-propagated rhizome divisions in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; never collect toadshade from wild populations — purchase only nursery-propagated plants. The stalkless (sessile) flower sits directly atop the three mottled leaves.
directions: Plant nursery-grown divisions in fall in humus-rich, moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade — toadshade trillium is one of the most distinctive of the eastern native trilliums, recognizable immediately by its sessile (stalkless) dark maroon flower that sits directly upon the three large, dramatically mottled leaves without an intermediate stalk. The heavily mottled, silver-and-green leaves are among the most beautiful of any native spring wildflower, providing ornamental interest even before the flowers open. A wide natural range stretches from New York west to Kansas and south to Georgia, growing in rich, moist hardwood forests and along stream banks. Like all trilliums, toadshade takes 7 years to bloom from seed — always purchase nursery-propagated plants and never collect from wild populations. The flowers attract small flies and beetles as pollinators; the seeds are dispersed by ants. An outstanding native wildflower for the shaded woodland garden.

plant_note: Start indoors; transplant after frost into part shade.
directions: Tom Thumb Blend is a compact, shade-loving balsam standing 12 to 16 inches tall with bushy plants that come alive with violet, white, and rose double flowers. Start seed indoors and barely cover it, keeping soil warm, then transplant after frost into part shade and rich, moist soil. Bumblebees and hummingbirds are drawn to the array of blooms tucked along the stems. Keep the soil consistently moist, as balsam wilts quickly when dry. Its tidy size makes a charming centerpiece for shady beds and containers.

plant_note: Plant rhizome segments 1–2 inches deep in fall in moist, deeply humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained woodland soil in full shade; toothwort is a spring ephemeral that goes completely dormant by early summer — mark its location carefully to avoid disturbing the rhizomes when dormant.
directions: Plant rhizome segments in fall in deeply humus-rich, moist, slightly acidic woodland soil in full shade; toothwort is among the most charming native edibles of the eastern woodland, producing pairs of deeply cut, coarsely toothed leaves on erect stems topped with clusters of small white to pale pink four-petaled flowers in early spring alongside trout lilies and trilliums. The toothlike, segmented underground rhizomes have a sharp, peppery wasabi-horseradish flavor and were gathered by Native Americans and early colonists as a condiment; they can be grated fresh or fermented like horseradish sauce. A true spring ephemeral, the entire plant disappears by late spring after setting seed, forming expanding colonies in rich, shaded conditions. Plant with other spring ephemerals — trout lily, bloodroot, Virginia bluebells — in a rich, shaded woodland garden for a sequence of color from late winter through May before the canopy closes.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil; thrives in heat.
directions: Torch is a genuine heat lover, setting the dog days of summer ablaze with sizzling 3-inch orange flowers on a large, multi-branching plant for the back of the border. Direct sow after the last frost in warm soil and full sun, barely covering the seed. It tolerates infertile soil, drought, and neglect, so avoid overwatering and rich ground. The tall stems benefit from spacing for airflow and may need staking in exposed sites. Its nectar-rich blooms are irresistible to butterflies and other pollinators.

plant_note: Plant container-grown plants or rooted cuttings in spring in any well-drained soil in full sun; trailing lantana is exceptionally drought and heat tolerant and establishes rapidly. Unlike L. camara, it rarely self-seeds invasively in zone 9 and is generally less aggressive in landscapes.
directions: Plant in full sun in any well-drained soil — poor, sandy, and rocky soils are ideal; trailing lantana is a low-growing, wide-spreading groundcover form of lantana that smothers slopes, spills over walls, and covers large areas with a continuous carpet of small lavender-rose to purple flower clusters from spring through frost. Unlike the more common Lantana camara (upright lantana), trailing lantana (L. montevidensis) has a naturally prostrate, cascading growth habit making it superb for slopes, raised beds, and retaining walls where it can drape downward. It is more frost-hardy than upright lantana, less invasive in seed production, and almost indestructible once established. In zone 9 it often persists as a semi-evergreen perennial year-round. An outstanding butterfly and pollinator plant — particularly attractive to skippers and small native bees. Prune back by one-third in late winter to prevent woody buildup and encourage fresh, dense regrowth.

plant_note: Plant in spring in moist soil; tolerates wet sites well.
directions: The Tri-Color Dappled Willow is a fast-growing deciduous shrub whose new spring growth emerges in variegated green, white, and soft pink, with coral-red stems adding winter interest. Plant in full sun to partial shade in moist, fertile soil; it thrives in damp spots where many shrubs struggle. Prune hard in late winter to encourage a fresh flush of the brightest variegated foliage. Water generously, as willows are thirsty plants that dislike drying out. Grow it as a rounded shrub in a mixed border or shear it into a formal shape.

plant_note: Plant in spring in moist, fertile soil; tolerates wet ground.
directions: This is the standard, tree-form version of the tri-color dappled willow, grafted atop a single trunk to create a lollipop-shaped canopy of green, white, and pink variegated leaves. Plant in full sun to light shade in consistently moist, fertile soil where it can show off its cascading foliage. Prune the head in late winter to keep it dense and to renew the most colorful new growth. Keep the soil evenly moist, as willows demand regular water and tolerate wet feet. Remove any suckers that sprout below the graft to preserve the tree form.

plant_note: Plant corms 3–4 inches deep in fall in very well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, dry-to-average sandy or rocky soil in full sun to partial shade; triplet lily is dormant and completely dry in summer — do not water during summer dormancy or the corms will rot.
directions: Plant corms in fall in very well-drained, dry-to-average, sandy or rocky soil in full sun to partial shade — triplet lily is a spectacular western native bulb of California and Oregon grasslands and open woodlands, producing umbels of 15–30 funnel-shaped blue-violet to purple flowers (2 inches long) on 18–24 inch stems in late spring to early summer. The flowers are among the bluest of all native American wildflowers and provide important nectar for native bumblebees and long-tongued bees. The plant is entirely summer-dormant — it emerges in early spring, blooms, and then disappears as summer approaches, requiring dry summer conditions for healthy corm development (do not irrigate in summer). Excellent for naturalizing in dry, warm-summer climates in well-drained rocky or sandy soils — it multiplies steadily by corm offsets in appropriate conditions. An outstanding native bulb for Mediterranean-climate and dry western gardens.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall 6-8 inches deep before ground freezes.
directions: Plant Triumph tulip bulbs in fall about six to eight inches deep and four to six inches apart in well-drained soil. This mid-season class offers sturdy stems and classic egg-shaped blooms in a vast color range, excellent for beds and cutting. Provide full sun and allow the foliage to yellow naturally after bloom to recharge the bulb. In warm climates pre-chill bulbs for ten to fourteen weeks before planting, or treat them as annuals.

plant_note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; in zone 8–11 it acts as a short-lived perennial and blooms from spring through hard frost.
directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep in warm starting mix and transplant after frost danger has passed; tropical milkweed establishes quickly and blooms in its first season unlike most native milkweeds. It is an important monarch host plant and nectar source throughout the Gulf Coast growing season, but in zone 9–11 where it does not freeze back, cut plants to 6 in in fall and winter to disrupt the overwinter buildup of Ophryocystis elektroscirrha (OE), a protozoan parasite that accumulates on non-dormant milkweed. Deadhead spent flower clusters to encourage continuous blooming, or allow seedpods to dry and open for easy self-sowing. The red-and-yellow bicolor flowers distinguish it from native milkweeds at a glance.

plant_note: Direct sow on soil surface (light aids germination) after last frost, or start indoors 6 weeks earlier; in zone 9–11 it self-sows freely and behaves as a naturalized perennial.
directions: Scatter seed lightly on the soil surface after last frost in well-drained, average to poor soil in full sun; this Gulf Coast native thrives in heat and drought where cultivated salvias fail, and it self-sows so freely in zone 9 that it naturalizes into a permanent colony. The brilliant red flower spikes bloom from spring through first frost and are irresistible to ruby-throated hummingbirds; white-flowering varieties attract additional butterfly species. Deadhead lightly to prolong bloom, but allow some seed to set for naturalization. It tolerates poor, sandy soil and partial shade better than many salvias, making it ideal for difficult spots in a Gulf Coast landscape.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall or early spring; never let dry out.
directions: Plant trumpet lily bulbs in fall or early spring about eight inches deep in deep, fertile, well-drained soil. The tall stems carry many large, powerfully fragrant trumpet-shaped flowers in midsummer and often need staking. Give them full sun with cool, shaded roots and never allow the bulbs to dry out completely. Leave stems standing until they yellow so the bulbs can store energy for the following year.

plant_note: Plant container vines near a very sturdy support structure in spring; trumpet vine is vigorous and heavy — it will pull down lightweight trellises. Control root suckers by mowing or digging promptly, as the vine can spread aggressively.
directions: Plant near a very sturdy fence, masonry wall, pergola, or large tree in full sun to partial shade in any well-drained soil — trumpet vine is extraordinarily adaptable and drought tolerant; the large orange-red trumpet flowers appear from June through September and are among the most important native nectar sources for ruby-throated hummingbirds in the eastern US. The vine climbs by aerial roots and is very vigorous — growing 20–30 feet and sending up root suckers widely around the base. Control by mowing suckers, cutting back hard annually, and avoiding planting near the foundation of structures where the roots can damage masonry. The sap causes skin irritation in many people. Despite its aggressive character, trumpet vine is exceptional in rural settings where it can be allowed to cover a fence, outbuilding, or dead tree. The contact dermatitis risk from sap is real; wear gloves when pruning.

plant_note: Plant tubers 2 inches deep in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun after soil warms to 60°F; tuberose requires a long, warm growing season to bloom — plant early and do not allow soil to remain cold and wet, which causes rot. In zone 9–11 tubers can remain in the ground year-round with light mulch.
directions: Plant tubers 2 inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; tuberose is one of the most intensely fragrant flowers in the world — the pure white, waxy flowers on tall spikes in late summer have a heavy, sweet, complex scent that intensifies at night and has been prized for centuries in perfumery and garlands. It is the principal flower in traditional South Asian garlands and is widely used in the Indian perfume industry (the absolute is one of the most expensive floral extracts). The single-flowered variety blooms slightly earlier than the double; 'The Pearl' is the most widely grown double-flowered cultivar. In zone 9–11 the clumping tubers naturalize to form large colonies that bloom reliably each summer; in colder zones dig and store tubers dry through winter. An outstanding cut flower with exceptional vase life of 10–14 days.

plant_note: Start tubers indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost, hollow side up, in shallow trays of barely moist peat or potting mix; transplant to shaded containers or beds when sprouts are 2–3 inches tall. Tuberous begonias are best in containers in the Gulf South, where summer humidity and heat can be challenging.
directions: Start tubers hollow-side up in barely moist peat, then transplant to shaded containers or beds in humus-rich, well-drained soil; tuberous begonias produce enormous, rose-like flowers 3–6 inches across in an unrivaled range of colors and forms — single, double, ruffled, picotee, and pendulous types in red, orange, yellow, pink, white, and bicolor — making them among the most spectacular of all shade-garden plants. They thrive in cool, bright indirect light and perform best in containers on covered porches or under light-filtering trees where afternoon temperatures stay below 85°F. Reduce watering in late summer as plants prepare for dormancy; lift and store tubers dry when foliage yellows. In zone 9–11 tubers may remain in the ground with very light mulch through mild winters. An outstanding container plant for shaded patios.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring or early fall; this cool-season grass is best divided after it finishes its main growth flush in late spring.
directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; unlike most ornamental grasses that demand full sun, tufted hair grass performs beautifully in partial shade and is one of the best cool-season grasses for lightly shaded woodland garden borders. The fine-textured, arching clumps of deep-green foliage are attractive through the growing season, and the airy, golden-bronze flower panicles that appear in midsummer shimmer and move in the slightest breeze. Cut the clump back to 4–6 inches in late winter before new growth begins. This grass thrives in cool, moist conditions and may go partially summer-dormant in hot, humid climates.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall when soil temperatures drop below 55°F, about 4–8 weeks before hard frost.
directions: Plant bulbs pointed end up at a depth of 3 times their height, typically 6–8 in deep, in well-drained soil — standing water will cause rot. In warmer zones (7–8), pre-chill bulbs for 6–8 weeks in the refrigerator before planting to simulate the cold period they need. Most hybrid tulips perform best in year 1; replanting fresh bulbs annually is common practice in mild climates. Allow leaves to die back naturally after bloom to recharge the bulb.

plant_note: Plant in spring in deep, moist, well-drained soil with room to grow large.
directions: The tulip poplar is a fast-growing native shade tree that reaches towering heights, with distinctive four-lobed leaves and cup-shaped, greenish-yellow tulip-like flowers in late spring. Give it a large, open site in full sun with deep, moist, well-drained soil, as its extensive root system needs room. Water regularly for the first few years to establish; mature trees are fairly self-sufficient. Its rapid growth makes it excellent for quick shade, though the soft wood benefits from a sheltered location. Foliage turns clear golden-yellow in autumn.

plant_note: Plant container stock after last frost; an extremely tough Texas native that thrives in heat, part shade, and dry soils where few flowering shrubs will grow.
directions: Plant in well-drained to average soil in partial to full sun; Turk's cap is one of the most shade-tolerant flowering natives for the Gulf South and performs well under the canopy of live oaks. The distinctive red flowers never fully open, forming a twisted turban shape, and are a top hummingbird nectar source from summer through fall. Small red fruits that follow are edible and mildly sweet — birds devour them quickly, and new plants sprout readily from seeds they drop. Prune back to 12–18 in in late winter; in zone 7–8 the top dies back but the roots are reliably hardy to about 10°F.

plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring after last frost in any well-drained soil; this is one of the few true shade-tolerant flowering shrubs that performs in deep shade while also thriving in full sun. Dies to the ground in hard frost zones but re-sprouts vigorously.
directions: Plant in well-drained soil in full sun to deep shade; Turk's cap is one of the South's most versatile and valuable flowering shrubs, producing bright red nodding flowers with tightly spiraled petals that never fully open (resembling a Turkish fez) from summer through frost — and in zone 9–10, flowering nearly year round. It is outstanding in dry shade under large trees where little else will bloom, and is equally at home in full blazing sun. Ruby-throated and rufous hummingbirds are its primary pollinators and mob the flowers from summer through migration. In zone 8 it dies back to the roots in hard freezes but re-sprouts reliably in spring and reaches full size by July. Do not overwater; it tolerates considerable drought once established.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in early spring at 4–6 inch depth, in groups of 3–5 for best effect; handles more shade than most lilies and prefers consistently moist soil.
directions: Plant bulbs 4–6 inches deep in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade to light sun; Turk's cap lily is the tallest native eastern lily, and in optimal conditions can reach 8 feet with dozens of richly spotted orange reflexed (turkscap-shaped) flowers per stem in July–August. Unlike cultivated Asiatic or Oriental lilies, it grows naturally in wet meadows and forest edges and requires consistently moist soil throughout the growing season. It tolerates more shade than most lilies and is valuable in the lightly shaded, moist native garden border. Ruby-throated hummingbirds are primary pollinators. The foliage and bulbs may be toxic to cats; keep away from where cats roam.

plant_note: Plant young, nursery-grown container plants in fall in dry, white, sandy or sandy loam, acidic soil in full sun; turkey oak is adapted to the driest, most nutrient-deficient sandy soils of the coastal plain and requires sharp drainage — it does not tolerate clay or moist soils.
directions: Turkey oak is a native small to medium tree endemic to the scrub and sandhill habitats of the Atlantic and Gulf Coastal Plain from Virginia to Louisiana — one of the most adapted trees in North America to the white, nutrient-deficient, excessively drained sands of the ancient coastal plain terraces where it forms nearly pure stands alongside longleaf pine and sand live oak. Its deeply 3–5 lobed leaves are large, glossy, and architectural — among the most decorative of all oak foliage — and turn vivid shades of red, orange, and burgundy in fall, providing exceptional autumn color even in warm climates where most oaks turn only dull brown. Turkey oak is the critical host tree for the Florida scrub-jay, one of the most endangered birds in North America, which depends on turkey oak acorns as its primary food; the tree is also habitat for dozens of rare invertebrates endemic to scrub habitats. In sandy coastal plain gardens with excessively drained, acidic, nutrient-poor soil — exactly the conditions that challenge most ornamental trees — turkey oak thrives with no supplemental care once established.

plant_note: Plant after the last frost in a sunny, well-drained spot.
directions: Tuscarora is a vigorous crape myrtle that bears long-lasting panicles of hot coral-pink flowers from midsummer into fall. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil to maximize bloom and reduce the chance of powdery mildew. Its smooth, mottled cinnamon bark and orange-red fall color extend the interest well past the flowering season. Prune only lightly in late winter to preserve its graceful vase-shaped structure rather than cutting it back hard. Established plants tolerate heat and drought, needing extra water mainly during long dry stretches.

plant_note: Surface-sow; seed needs light to germinate. Often blooms the second year.
directions: Press seed onto the soil surface without covering, since light is needed for germination. Also called Carpathian bellflower, this compact perennial forms neat mounds covered in violet-blue, upward-facing bells. Grow it in full sun to part shade and cool, well-drained soil, ideal for rock gardens, edgings, and containers. Shear after the first bloom flush to tidy the plant and encourage a second round of flowers.

plant_note: Start indoors well before last frost; slow to germinate.
directions: Tweedia, or blue milkweed, offers rare true-blue, aquamarine star-shaped flowers with lavender buds and older blooms adding extra flair. Start the slow-germinating seed indoors 8 to 6 weeks before the last frost in warm soil, and transplant out after frost danger passes. Grow in full sun in well-drained soil where the sweetly scented flowers draw pollinators. Unlike common milkweeds it stays compact, making it well suited to containers and cut-flower use. Handle the stems with care, as the milky sap can irritate skin.

plant_note: Plant divisions or purchased plants in early spring in humus-rich woodland soil; purchase only from nursery-propagated sources as this plant is slow to propagate and protected in many states.
directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly alkaline woodland soil in full to partial shade; this rare, choice native woodland plant is named for Thomas Jefferson and named for its unique paired leaf halves that look like butterfly wings emerging from a single petiole. The pure white flowers appear briefly in early spring before the leaves expand, lasting only a few days before petals fall, and should be watched closely to catch them at their peak. The plant produces remarkable urn-shaped seed capsules with a hinged lid that opens to release seeds — one of the most distinctive seed vessels in North American flora. Extremely slow from seed, requiring 3–5 years to bloom; purchase propagated divisions from reputable nurseries.

plant_note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to slightly alkaline, humus-rich soil in partial shade. Two-winged Silverbell is more heat-tolerant than Carolina Silverbell (Halesia carolina) and is the better choice for Gulf Coast gardens in zones 7–9.
directions: Two-winged Silverbell is a beautiful native understory tree, native to the rich, moist bottomland forests and floodplain terraces of the southeastern coastal plain from North Carolina to Florida and west to Texas, where it blooms prolifically in March and April with cascading clusters of white, bell-shaped flowers that cover the bare branches before the leaves emerge — one of the finest native spring-flowering trees in the southern garden. Distinguished from Carolina Silverbell (Halesia carolina) by its fruit having only two wings instead of four, and adapted to the warmer, more humid conditions of the lower South, Two-winged Silverbell is the appropriate silverbell for Gulf Coast gardens. The bright green summer foliage, attractive bark, and yellow fall color provide year-round interest; grow in moist, well-drained, acidic to slightly alkaline soil in partial shade under larger canopy trees or at woodland edges. More tolerant of summer heat than H. carolina.

plant_note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped trees in spring in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade; umbrella magnolia grows naturally as an understory tree in moist ravines and shaded slopes and tolerates deep shade better than most flowering trees.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade; umbrella magnolia is a distinctive native understory tree of the Appalachians and Southeast named for the pattern of its enormous leaves — 12–24 inches long — arranged in a dramatic whorl around the branch tips like the spokes of an umbrella, creating a striking bold-textured tropical effect in the shaded garden. The large creamy-white flowers (6–10 inches) in late spring have an unusual, slightly unpleasant fragrance but are still attractive from a distance. Red cone-like fruit clusters in late summer and fall are ornamental and eaten by songbirds. It grows naturally as a subcanopy tree in moist, acidic ravines and tolerates shade better than most Magnolia species, making it one of the few flowering trees that performs well in partial to light shade. The extraordinarily large leaves and exotic texture make it an excellent specimen in shaded woodland gardens.

plant_note: Plant nursery-propagated divisions in fall in moist, humus-rich, acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade. Underwood's Trillium is a warm-climate sessile species suited to the moist hardwood and mixed pine-hardwood forests of the Florida panhandle, Georgia, and Alabama.
directions: Underwood's Trillium is a southeastern sessile trillium native to the moist, mixed hardwood forests of the Florida panhandle, Georgia, and Alabama, where it blooms in February through April with dark maroon to nearly black sessile flowers nestled among three strongly mottled, glossy leaves. It occupies an ecological niche similar to other coastal plain sessile trilliums but tolerates somewhat warmer and more humid conditions than its montane relatives, making it an appropriate choice for gardeners in zones 7–9 seeking native spring woodland plants. Obtain only nursery-propagated plants, as wild collection is destructive and in many areas illegal; plant rhizome divisions in fall in well-amended, moist, acidic soil in the shade of evergreen or deciduous trees. Like all trilliums, it blooms for only a few weeks before going dormant, so interplant with later-emerging shade perennials to fill the gap.

plant_note: Plant tubers after all danger of frost when soil has warmed.
directions: Valley Porcupine is a spiky, pink-and-white cactus-type dahlia that is both an excellent cut flower and a reliable tuber producer, making it a great choice for beginners. Plant tubers horizontally about 4 to 6 inches deep after the soil warms in spring, with the eye facing up. Site in full sun in rich, well-drained soil and stake tall plants to support the heavy blooms. Pinch the growing tip when plants are about a foot tall to encourage bushier growth and more flowers. In cold zones, lift and store the tubers indoors over winter after the first frost blackens the foliage.

plant_note: Plant tubers after the last frost when soil is warm.
directions: Valley Rust Bucket is a smaller red-orange dahlia whose warm autumn tones blend beautifully with bronze and copper companions in the cutting garden. Plant tubers 4 to 6 inches deep in full sun and rich, well-drained soil once frost danger has passed. Stake the plants and pinch the central shoot early to promote branching and a fuller flush of blooms. Deadhead spent flowers regularly to keep the plant producing through the season. Lift the tubers before hard freezes in colder regions and store them in a cool, dry place for replanting.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil and full sun.
