directions: Vanilla Ice brings a creamy, light-yellow color to the garden on multi-branched plants about 5 feet tall bearing sweet 3-to-5-inch flowers. Direct sow the seed about an inch deep after the last frost in warm soil and full sun. Thin seedlings so the branching plants have room to develop their many blooms. Practically care-free, these sunflowers provide months of color and lovely cut flowers, and are striking in a moonlit garden. Water steadily while young and enjoy the pollinators and birds they attract.

plant_note: Plant nursery-propagated rhizome divisions in fall in deep, moist, rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade in a woodland garden setting. Vasey's Trillium is a large pedicellate species that blooms somewhat later than sessile trilliums — April to May in the Southern Appalachians.
directions: Vasey's Trillium is one of the largest and most striking of the native eastern trilliums, native to rich, moist cove forests and sheltered ravines of the southern Appalachians from Virginia and North Carolina south to Georgia and Alabama, where it blooms from April through May with dramatic, deep wine-red petals that recurve strongly back toward the stem, hanging below the leaves and emitting a strong, sweet fragrance to attract fly pollinators. It grows markedly larger than most sessile trilliums, with broad three-parted leaves and stems reaching 24 inches, and its flowers hang on a distinct pedicel below the leaf whorl — a characteristic setting it apart from sessile species. Plant nursery-propagated rhizome divisions in fall in the deepest, most richly amended soil you can provide — cove forest conditions with high organic matter, consistent moisture, and full shade — and mulch heavily with leaf litter. Vasey's Trillium takes 5–7 years to reach blooming age from seed, making nursery divisions the only practical route for most gardeners.

plant_note: Direct sow after last frost; branching habit produces many blooms.
directions: Sow seed one inch deep directly in the garden after the last frost once soil warms. This heirloom bears velvety, multi-toned blooms in mahogany, bronze, and burnt orange on branching five- to seven-foot stems. Its many pollen-rich flowers make excellent cut material and feed bees throughout summer and fall. Give it full sun and moderate water, staking tall plants in exposed sites to prevent lodging.

plant_note: Sow seeds on the surface of moist, nutrient-poor, acidic medium (50/50 peat moss and perlite or long-fiber sphagnum moss) in full sun; water only with rain water or distilled water — tap water minerals and all fertilizers will kill plants. Venus flytrap requires a winter dormancy period (50°F for 3–4 months).
directions: Venus flytrap is the world's most famous carnivorous plant — a native of the Green Swamp region of North and South Carolina, where it grows in wet, nutrient-poor, acidic pine savannas maintained by periodic fire, trapping and digesting insects and spiders with its hinged, toothed, snap-trap leaves to supplement the minimal nutrients available in its impoverished native habitat. Each trap closes in milliseconds when two trigger hairs inside the trap are touched in quick succession — a failsafe mechanism that prevents wasted closure on raindrops or wind-blown debris; the trap then seals tightly and secretes digestive enzymes over 7–10 days to dissolve the prey. Despite its exotic appearance, Venus flytrap is hardy to zone 7 and requires a cold winter dormancy of 3–4 months (50°F) to remain healthy long-term; in zones 7–9 it can be grown outdoors year-round in appropriate bog conditions. Grow in a mix of 50% peat moss and 50% perlite or sand (no potting soil, no fertilizer), keep the medium wet using rain water or distilled water only, and provide maximum sun — the most common causes of failure are tap water, fertilizer, and insufficient sun.

plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs in fall or spring in moist to moderately moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained to moist soil in partial shade to full sun; vernal witch hazel tolerates moist soil and periodic flooding along streams better than most other witch hazels.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade — vernal witch hazel is the earliest-blooming native Hamamelis species, opening its strap-petaled flowers in January through March while the plant is still dormant, even in the coldest conditions of zone 4–5. The flowers come in shades from pale yellow to orange to deep brick-red (dark orange-red in the commonly grown form) and have a sweet, spicy fragrance. Unlike the common witch hazel (H. virginiana), which blooms in fall, H. vernalis blooms in late winter and is the best native species for early-season pollinator value — providing nectar for early queen bumblebees and native mining bees on warm winter days. It is native to the gravel bars and rocky streambanks of the Ozarks and tolerates moist to wet conditions. The fall foliage is brilliant orange-yellow.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring or fall in well-drained soil; excellent drought tolerance once established.
directions: Plant in well-drained soil in full sun; speedwell is notably drought-tolerant and performs poorly in soggy or heavy clay conditions. The upright spikes of blue, pink, or white flowers bloom from late spring into summer and are excellent for cutting. Deadhead by removing spent spikes back to a lateral bud or leaf to extend the flowering period and prevent excessive self-seeding. Divide every 3–4 years in early spring to maintain vigor and an attractive mounding habit.

plant_note: Plant divisions or slips 18–24 inches apart in rows along the contour of a slope in full sun; vetiver is sterile and does not self-sow, making it non-invasive in spite of its vigorous growth. Established plants tolerate drought, flooding, and extreme heat.
directions: Plant slips or divisions along contour lines on slopes in full sun in any well-drained soil; vetiver is the world standard for low-cost erosion control on steep slopes — a tropical grass from South Asia with an extraordinary root system that penetrates 6–10 feet straight down (not laterally) and holds soil with remarkable efficiency against both water and wind erosion. The World Bank promotes vetiver hedgerow systems in developing countries as the most effective and affordable slope stabilization technology available. The aromatic roots are distilled to produce vetiver essential oil, a base note used in hundreds of fine perfumes. Unlike most grasses used for erosion control, vetiver is sterile and non-invasive — it produces no viable seed and spreads only by division. In zone 8–11 it is an outstanding low-maintenance erosion control plant for steep slopes, stream banks, and road cuts.

plant_note: Sow in spring or summer; biennial that blooms the following spring.
directions: Victoria Blue is a darling, compact forget-me-not with petite, star-shaped blue flowers perfect for the front of a shade garden or alongside spring bulbs. Direct sow the seed in spring or summer, covering it lightly since darkness aids germination. As a biennial or short-lived perennial it often blooms the spring after sowing and then self-sows to return. Grow in part shade in moist, rich soil where it heralds spring with its cheerful color. Keep the soil evenly moist and let plants naturalize for a lasting display.

plant_note: Sow in spring or summer; biennial that blooms the following spring.
directions: Victoria Pink is a compact forget-me-not with petite, star-shaped pink flowers that attract pollinators and pair beautifully with spring bulbs. Direct sow the seed in spring or summer, covering it lightly since darkness helps germination. As a biennial or short-lived perennial it typically blooms the spring after sowing and self-sows to return year after year. Grow in part shade in moist, rich soil, keeping it evenly watered. Let plants naturalize at the front of shady beds for a soft, spreading carpet of color.

plant_note: Start indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost; transplant several weeks before last frost as plants tolerate mild freezes.
directions: Sow seed 1/8 in deep in a cool, moist starting mix; darkness during germination is beneficial. Violas are more petite and heat-tolerant than pansies, often self-sowing prolifically in the garden. Deadhead to extend flowering, or allow seed to set for naturalized self-seeding the following year. They shine in early spring containers and border edges.

plant_note: Direct sow around the last frost in full sun.
directions: Violet Queen is a clary-type annual salvia grown for its showy, long-lasting violet bracts rather than its tiny true flowers, with aromatic foliage and blooms. Direct sow around the last frost in full sun and average, well-drained soil, barely covering the seed. It blooms non-stop through summer; simply remove spent spikes to encourage continual color. Drought-tolerant once established, it mixes well with bright annuals in beds and borders. The colorful bracts also dry nicely and draw hummingbirds, butterflies, and other pollinators.

plant_note: Plant small bulbs (corms) 2–3 inches deep in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, dry to moderately moist, sandy to loamy soil in partial shade to full sun; violet woodsorrel naturally grows in open prairies, rocky glades, and open woodland edges and prefers well-drained to slightly dry conditions.
directions: Plant small bulbs 2–3 inches deep in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, dry to moderately moist soil in partial shade to full sun — violet woodsorrel is a charming native bulb of the eastern US prairies and open woodlands, producing clusters of small, five-petaled, rose-purple to violet flowers in spring above neat clover-like leaves with three heart-shaped leaflets that fold at night and in cloudy weather. It is one of the earliest-blooming spring bulbs in the native prairie garden, appearing alongside spring beauty and pasque flower. The corms multiply slowly each year to form small colonies that naturalize in open, well-drained, sunny to partially-shaded conditions. The leaves have a pleasant, mild lemony flavor (from oxalic acid, same as other woodsorrels and sheep sorrel) and can be eaten sparingly in small quantities as a tart, lemony salad accent. It goes dormant in summer heat and reemerges in fall with a brief second bloom before going dormant for winter.

plant_note: Plant in moist to moderately dry, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade near a fence, trellis, or shrubby thicket; virgin's bower scrambles through shrubs and climbs by leaf petioles — site where it can naturalize freely as it self-seeds prolifically.
directions: Virgin's bower is the most widespread and cold-hardy of all native North American clematis, scrambling through shrubs and small trees along streams, roadsides, and woodland edges from Nova Scotia to Florida — in late summer it smothers its support in masses of small, four-petaled white flowers that are intensely fragrant and attract a spectacular diversity of native bees, wasps, beetles, and butterflies seeking late-summer nectar. After bloom, the flowers are replaced by feathery, silvery seed plumes that persist through fall and winter, creating a spectacular 'old man's beard' effect in the landscape — a three-season ornamental vine of understated elegance. While not as showy as large-flowered hybrid clematis, its fine-textured floral masses and subsequent silky seedheads provide months of changing interest with zero maintenance; it is drought-tolerant once established and pest-free. Self-seeds freely to form spreading colonies — allow it to naturalize in native shrub borders or along a fence where it can sprawl without becoming an obstacle.

plant_note: Sow fresh seed in fall directly in a shaded, moist site; or plant divisions/transplants in early spring.
directions: Plant in humus-rich, consistently moist, shaded to partly shaded woodland soil; Virginia bluebells are a spring ephemeral that goes dormant by early summer, disappearing completely underground. The clusters of pink buds open to sky-blue, nodding tubular flowers in early to mid-spring and are among the most beautiful of native wildflowers. Mark the location of clumps so they are not accidentally disturbed; interplant with hostas, ferns, or other perennials that will fill the gap when bluebells go dormant. Divide crowded clumps or transplant in early fall when dormant.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in strongly acidic (pH 4.0–5.5), wet to moist, humus-rich soil in partial shade; Virginia chain fern tolerates deeper standing water than most ferns and can colonize the margins of swamps, bogs, and wet ditches.
directions: Plant in strongly acidic (pH 4.0–5.5), wet to moist, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; Virginia chain fern is a robust, twice-pinnate native fern of Atlantic coastal plain bogs, swamps, and wet pine flatwoods — larger and more dramatic than the similar netted chain fern, with bipinnate fronds reaching 2–5 feet in saturated acidic soil. The sori are arranged in the chain-like pattern that names the genus, running in long lines parallel to the midrib. It tolerates standing water around the root zone and is one of the best native ferns for establishing in swampy, waterlogged, acidic conditions where few other plants thrive. Spreads steadily by rhizome to form bold colonies in appropriate wet, acid conditions. An important structural plant in wetland restoration and naturalistic bog gardens in the Southeast and coastal plain from New England to Florida.

plant_note: Plant container vines in spring near a structure, tree, or fence; the vine clings by adhesive suction-cup pads that do not penetrate surfaces or damage mortar. Do not confuse with poison ivy (three leaflets); virginia creeper has five.
directions: Plant in virtually any soil — wet or dry, rich or poor, sandy or clay — in full sun to deep shade; Virginia creeper is one of the most versatile native vines, blanketing fences, walls, trees, and arbors in deep shade where few other plants grow, and producing a stunning curtain of brilliant crimson-scarlet-burgundy fall color that is among the most vivid of any plant in the North American flora. The small dark blue-black berries that follow in fall are an essential food for migrating thrushes, waxwings, bluebirds, and woodpeckers. It clings by adhesive pads (not invasive roots), making it appropriate on stone and brick. The sap and berries are toxic to humans and can cause contact dermatitis in sensitive individuals; the similar five-leaflet compound leaf distinguishes it from three-leaflet poison ivy. Prune aggressively in late winter to control size.

plant_note: Direct sow seeds in spring after last frost in dry, sandy, gravelly, or rocky, well-drained, infertile soil in full sun to light shade. Virginia False Aloe is extremely drought-tolerant once established; do not amend with compost, fertilize, or irrigate routinely, as overly rich or moist soil causes crown rot and rapid decline.
directions: Virginia False Aloe is a native succulent wildflower of the southeastern United States, producing a compact basal rosette of fleshy, strap-like, often maroon-spotted leaves from sandy or rocky, well-drained soils in prairies, cedar glades, open woodlands, and roadsides from Virginia south to Florida and west to Nebraska. Unlike the dangerous spiny agaves to which it is closely related, False Aloe has soft, non-rigid leaf tips, making it entirely safe to handle; after several years of vegetative growth, it sends up a single spectacular flower spike reaching 4–6 feet tall, bearing fragrant, tubular, greenish-brown to purple flowers that open at night and attract sphinx moths as their primary pollinators. Once the flower stalk sets seed, the rosette dies but is replaced by offsets from the base, allowing clumps to expand gradually over many years without becoming invasive. Plant in extremely well-drained, sandy or gravelly, infertile, rocky soil in full sun; False Aloe is highly drought-tolerant and long-lived in appropriate conditions but quickly rots in wet, poorly drained, or heavily amended soils.

plant_note: Scarify seeds lightly and direct sow in early spring in moist to well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade. Virginia Mallow self-sows from year to year; established plants develop a persistent woody crown and re-sprout each spring.
directions: Virginia Mallow is a tall, bold native perennial, native to the rich, moist soils of bottomlands, riverbanks, woodland edges, and disturbed clearings from Virginia and Pennsylvania south to Georgia and west to Indiana, growing to 8–10 feet tall in fertile, moist soil and producing a continuous succession of small, five-petaled, white flowers from July through September that attract an exceptional diversity of native bees, including many specialist bees that are important pollinators. The large, palmately lobed leaves and towering height give Virginia Mallow a dramatic architectural quality in the native garden, providing bold structure in the back of borders and in naturalized meadow plantings. The abundant seeds are consumed by goldfinches, sparrows, and other seed-eating birds in fall and winter; the plant's stems provide nesting material and stem-nesting sites for solitary native bees. Grow in fertile, moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; it is surprisingly adaptable and self-sows reliably once established.

plant_note: Plant bare-root or container divisions in fall or early spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Virginia rose is extremely cold hardy and tolerates a wide range of soils, including poor, sandy, and dry conditions once established.
directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade — Virginia rose is one of the most ecologically valuable of all native roses, producing simple, five-petaled, clear pink flowers in late spring with a pleasant light fragrance, followed by the best hips of any eastern wild rose: large, bright red, glossy fruits that persist on the canes through winter and provide critical food for catbirds, mockingbirds, robins, and cedar waxwings. The foliage turns brilliant shades of red, orange, and burgundy in autumn — outstanding fall color for a shrub. It spreads by suckers to form dense, wildlife-valuable thickets in naturalistic settings, along fencerows, and at the edges of woodland, and tolerates salt spray, poor sandy soils, and moderate drought. Disease resistance is excellent compared to hybrid roses: no spraying required. Prune out oldest canes after flowering to keep the plant productive; remove suckers to control spread in small spaces.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring or fall; plants establish quickly and begin blooming the following spring. Direct seeding is possible but takes longer — divisions are the preferred propagation method.
directions: Plant in moist to average, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; Virginia spiderwort is a native wildflower of open woods and roadsides producing three-petaled flowers in shades of blue-violet to purple, pink, or white that open in the morning and close by early afternoon. The strap-like, grass-like foliage is bold and attractive in the spring garden. After flowering, plants go semi-dormant in midsummer heat and may look tatty — cut back hard to 4–6 inches to encourage a flush of fresh foliage. Plants self-seed prolifically and spread by clump division; thin as needed. The closed flowers and young shoot tips are edible in salads, and the plant has a long history of use by eastern woodland peoples.

plant_note: Sow fresh seed in fall or transplant tiny corms 2–3 inches deep in autumn; plants require winter cold to break dormancy and will bloom the following spring. Once established, they self-seed to naturalize freely.
directions: Plant small corms 2–3 inches deep in moist, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade beneath deciduous trees; Virginia spring beauty is a spring ephemeral wildflower that carpets the woodland floor with pink-striped white flowers in early spring before tree canopies close. The narrow, fleshy leaves and small corms are edible — the corms taste like a mild potato and were gathered by Indigenous peoples of eastern North America. Plants go fully dormant by late spring, leaving bare soil where they grew; interplant with ferns, hostas, or other summer-active groundcovers to fill the gap. Self-seeds freely once naturalized — scatter seed right after it ripens in late spring to establish new colonies.

plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring or fall; this shrub tolerates transplanting well and establishes quickly. Fall planting is excellent in zones 7–9 where winters are mild.
directions: Plant in moist, average to wet soil in full sun to full shade; Virginia sweetspire is one of the most adaptable native shrubs for difficult garden situations, tolerating wet soil, dry clay, deep shade, and drought once established. Fragrant white flower racemes in June are attractive to butterflies and bees, and the fall foliage turns spectacular shades of orange, red, scarlet, and purple — often the best fall color of any shade-tolerant shrub. The cultivar 'Henry's Garnet' is the most widely grown, with especially long flower racemes and outstanding crimson fall color. Plants spread by suckers to form thickets; remove suckers to maintain a single-stem form or allow them to colonize a slope or wet area as a groundcover.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring for late spring color.
directions: Direct sow viscaria thinly in early spring where plants are to bloom. The dainty rose, blue, and white five-petaled flowers cover slender stems and suit cottage gardens and cutting beds. It appreciates full sun and well-drained soil and blooms quickly from seed. Thin seedlings for good air movement and deadhead to prolong the flowering period.

plant_note: Plant in spring or fall in a shaded, moist location.
directions: Vision In Pink is a shade-loving perennial astilbe that sends up soft pink, feathery flower plumes above mounds of deep green, finely cut foliage in summer. Plant in partial to full shade in humus-rich, consistently moist soil; it will not tolerate dry conditions. Divide clumps every few years in spring to maintain vigor and increase your planting. Cut back spent plumes or leave them for winter interest, and mulch to keep roots cool and moist. It is an excellent choice for woodland gardens, shaded borders, and near water features.

plant_note: Plant divisions or transplants in moist, slightly alkaline to neutral, calcareous (limestone-based), humus-rich, very well-drained soil in deep shade; walking fern is highly specific in its substrate requirements — it grows naturally on shaded, moist, mossy limestone and calcareous rocks and will not thrive in acidic or poorly-drained soils.
directions: Plant in moist, calcareous, humus-rich, very well-drained soil on shaded limestone surfaces, moss-covered rocks, or alkaline woodland beds in deep shade — walking fern is a unique and fascinating native fern of the eastern US, producing long, narrow, tapering fronds with heart-shaped bases that extend to 12–18 inches and root at their tips wherever they contact moist substrate, slowly 'walking' outward to colonize a shaded limestone outcrop or mossy rock wall. The entire plant is distinctive and easily recognized: its uncut, lance-shaped fronds bear no resemblance to typical fernlike foliage. It is strictly associated with shaded, moist, mossy limestone and calcareous rock faces in the eastern deciduous forest, growing in deep shade of overhanging cliffs, ravine walls, and shaded limestone boulders. It is an outstanding plant for the specialized shaded rock garden with limestone substrate, adding a uniquely charming and ecologically interesting element to native wildflower rock gardens.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; walking iris tolerates a range of light conditions but performs best and blooms most freely in bright filtered shade. In zone 9 it may be damaged by hard freezes but regrows vigorously from established rhizomes.
directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; walking iris (Neomarica gracilis) takes its name from its fascinating method of reproduction: after the short-lived fragrant iris-like flowers (white with blue and brown markings) appear at the tip of a fan-shaped leaf stalk, plantlets form at the same tip and the stalk bends under their weight until the plantlets touch the ground and root — 'walking' the plant outward from the mother clump. The tropical-looking, fan-shaped clumps of stiff, flat, strap-like leaves grow in dense stands that are ornamental year-round. The flowers appear several times through the warm season, each lasting only a day but produced in succession over weeks. Also called the 'apostle plant' because the original observation was that each stalk must produce 12 leaves (one for each apostle) before flowering — an observation that is approximately correct for mature stalks. An outstanding low-maintenance shade plant for zone 9–12 that naturalized easily in humid, subtropical gardens.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall in wet to moist, acidic, sandy or peaty soil in full sun; wand goldenrod is a wetland-affiliated species of the Gulf Coastal Plain that prefers seasonally saturated sites — it thrives in moist pine flatwoods, wet savannas, and pond margins.
directions: Wand goldenrod is a native goldenrod of the Gulf Coastal Plain's seasonally wet habitats — wet pine flatwoods, boggy depressions, and moist savannas from New Jersey to Texas — producing upright, unbranched or sparsely branched stems with small basal leaves and terminal wand-like flower spikes of golden yellow that are distinctive among goldenrods for their narrow, column-like form rather than the typical arching plume. Because it grows naturally in wet, acidic, sandy soils — habitats shared with pitcher plants, sundews, and rare orchids in Gulf Coast longleaf pine country — it is an appropriate companion plant for native bog gardens, seepage slopes, and the moist margins of rain gardens in the Deep South. The late-summer to fall bloom coincides with peak monarch migration and provides a critical nectar source for southbound monarchs; native bees are also heavily attracted to the dense, accessible flower clusters. In Gulf Coast flatwoods restorations, wand goldenrod is a keystone species of the low pine savanna ground layer.

plant_note: Root 3–4 inch stem cuttings in water or moist potting mix; cuttings root in 1–2 weeks and can be transplanted to beds or containers. In zone 9–11, establish in spring for summer groundcover coverage. Keep soil moist and provide light shade to partial shade for best foliage color.
directions: Root cuttings in water and transplant to moist, well-drained soil in partial shade; Tradescantia zebrina (wandering jew) is one of the fastest-spreading and most colorful of all tropical groundcovers for shaded areas, producing a mat of trailing stems with striking elliptical leaves in deep green and silver stripes with brilliant purple undersides — the tricolor effect is particularly vivid in good light. Small pink-purple three-petaled flowers are produced nearly year-round. In zone 9–11 it spreads freely as a low groundcover under trees and in shaded planting beds, rooting wherever stems touch moist soil; it can be aggressive and should be kept away from natural areas. For containers, the trailing stems are beautiful in hanging baskets on covered porches. The contact sap may cause contact dermatitis in some people; handle with caution. Easy to propagate from cuttings and nearly impossible to kill.

plant_note: Plant container-grown trees in fall in average, adaptable soil in full sun; Washington hawthorn tolerates a wide range of soils including alkaline, clay, and occasionally wet conditions — one of the most urban-tough of all native hawthorns.
directions: Washington hawthorn is the most widely planted of all native North American hawthorns in cultivation — an exceptionally ornamental small tree with multiple seasons of interest: dense clusters of white flowers in May, glossy, deeply lobed leaves turning orange-red in fall, and abundant clusters of small bright red fruits that persist into winter. It is one of the highest-value wildlife trees available for the eastern garden: at least 20 species of birds consume the fruits, including cedar waxwings, robins, and bluebirds; the dense, thorny structure (1–3 inch curved thorns) provides nesting habitat for catbirds, thrashers, and kingbirds; and over 150 species of Lepidoptera use native Crataegus as larval host plants. Washington hawthorn adapts to urban conditions including soil compaction, air pollution, and alkaline pH better than most native landscape trees, making it suitable for street planting, parking lots, and urban parks where native trees are rarely successful. A top choice for combining year-round ornamental interest with maximum wildlife value in limited space.

plant_note: Scarify seeds and plant nursery transplants in spring in wet to moist, slightly acidic to neutral, deep alluvial or clay-loam soil in full sun. Water Locust tolerates prolonged flooding and is an excellent native tree for the most difficult, permanently wet bottomland sites where other large trees fail.
directions: Water Locust is the swamp-adapted cousin of Honey Locust (Gleditsia triacanthos), native to the permanently flooded bottomlands, river swamps, and slough edges of the southeastern and south-central United States from South Carolina and Florida west to Illinois and Texas, where it tolerates standing water for extended periods that most other large trees cannot survive. Like Honey Locust it bears formidable branched thorns on the trunk and branches, but the seed pods are shorter and usually contain only one or two seeds rather than the many seeds in Honey Locust's long pods. The flat seed pods that fall into floodwater are buoyant and dispersed by currents, making Water Locust an effective natural colonizer of new bottomland habitats after flooding. A valuable riparian restoration tree for wet, bottomland habitats in zones 5–9, providing large tree structure in permanently flooded sites where bald cypress, water tupelo, and swamp cottonwood grow as associates. Plant in full sun in wet, rich, slightly acidic alluvial soil.

plant_note: Plant small container trees in winter or spring in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to wet soil; water oak is fast growing for an oak (2–3 feet per year when young) and establishes readily from planting.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to periodically wet soil in full sun; water oak is one of the most common and fast-growing native oaks of the coastal South, easily identified by its distinctively variable leaf shape — three-lobed duck-foot leaves, paddle-shaped leaves, and narrow elliptic leaves can all appear on the same tree. It grows naturally in bottomland forests, wet stream banks, and moist upland soils from New Jersey to Texas and is among the fastest-growing oaks in the South (2–3 feet annually). The very small, nearly round acorns produced in enormous quantities beginning at 10–15 years are critically important for wood ducks, mallards, wild turkeys, squirrels, and deer. Water oak is smaller than live oak (40–70 feet) and better suited to residential landscapes where space is limited. It typically lives 60–80 years.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring, anchoring in the muddy bottom of a pond or slow stream at a depth of 1–5 feet. Water Shield spreads by rhizome to form floating mats; provide adequate space in larger ponds where it can naturalize without overpowering other plantings.
directions: Water Shield is a distinctive native floating-leaf aquatic plant found in slow ponds, lakes, bogs, and sluggish streams throughout North America, Asia, and Australia, immediately identifiable by the thick, gelatinous mucilage that coats the undersides of the small, peltate (shield-shaped), olive-green floating leaves and the stems — a characteristic not found in any other native aquatic plant. Small, dark maroon to purplish flowers appear just above the water surface in summer, and the leaves and young stems, with their gelatinous coating, have been used as food in Japan (where it is called junsai) and by Native American tribes. Water Shield is a valuable aquatic plant for wildlife ponds, providing food and cover for frogs, turtles, waterfowl, and invertebrates; the floating leaf mats shade the water and suppress algae. Plant rhizomes in soft mud at the pond bottom at 1–5 feet depth; the plant spreads slowly but steadily in favorable conditions.

plant_note: Plant container specimens in spring in wet to flooded, slightly acidic to neutral, clay to loamy soil in full sun to partial shade; water tupelo is one of the very few large trees that tolerates prolonged seasonal flooding of 6–12 feet and thrives in swamp, bottomland, and floodplain conditions inaccessible to most trees.
directions: Plant in wet to seasonally flooded, slightly acidic to neutral, clay to loamy bottomland soil in full sun to partial shade — water tupelo is one of the dominant trees of the Deep South's most iconic wetland ecosystems: the cypress-tupelo swamp. It develops a dramatically buttressed, swollen trunk base that allows gas exchange during prolonged flooding, standing in water for months at a time in the bottomlands of the Gulf Coast and Mississippi River corridor. The large, elliptic leaves turn brilliant orange, scarlet, and crimson in fall, making it one of the finest fall-color trees for wet sites. Clusters of small, oblong, dark blue to black fruit in late summer and fall are among the most sought by migrating birds. Water tupelo honey — gathered by bees from the small spring flowers — is renowned as a premium monofloral honey with an exceptionally delicate, fruity sweetness and crystallization-resistant properties. An outstanding large tree for naturalizing in wet, swampy, or bottomland areas.

plant_note: Plant corms after last frost in spring for summer bloom; in mild-winter climates (zones 9–10) plant in fall.
directions: Plant corms 3–4 in deep in fertile, well-drained soil; watsonia closely resembles gladiolus and thrives in similar conditions. The tall, sword-like foliage produces elegant spikes of tubular flowers in shades of pink, red, coral, white, and bicolor throughout summer. In frost-free climates corms multiply into large colonies and can become invasive — divide every few years to manage spread. In colder zones lift corms after frost kills the foliage, cure, and store in a cool, dry, frost-free location.

plant_note: Start indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost due to slow growth; transplant after frost danger has fully passed.
directions: Sow dust-fine seed on the surface of moist, sterile starting mix under bright light — do not cover, as seed needs light to germinate. Start early as wax begonias are slow growers before transplant size. These self-cleaning plants rarely need deadheading, blooming continuously from spring through frost. Bronze-leaved cultivars tolerate more direct sun than green-leaved types.

plant_note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil; stake to control form.
directions: The Weeping Norway Spruce is a striking evergreen with dramatic cascading branches clothed in rich, dark green needles, ideal as a living sculpture or accent. Its ultimate size and shape depend on how it is staked, so train a central leader upright for a tall specimen or let it sprawl as a ground-hugging form. Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained soil and water regularly during establishment. It is hardy and cold-tolerant, needing little pruning beyond removing damaged wood. Give it space to let the weeping branches drape naturally.

plant_note: Plant in spring in moist ground, away from pipes and foundations.
directions: The Weeping Willow is a graceful, fast-growing shade tree with long, sweeping branches that trail nearly to the ground and sway in the slightest breeze. Plant in full sun in moist to wet soil, ideally near a pond or stream where its thirsty roots have ample water. Keep it well away from septic lines, drains, and foundations, as the aggressive roots seek out moisture. Water deeply and regularly, especially in dry spells and during establishment. Prune in late winter to remove crossing or damaged branches and to raise the canopy as it matures.

plant_note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil in full sun.
directions: The Weeping Yoshino is a flowering cherry with gracefully cascading branches that in spring become a fountain of delicate, blush-white blossoms before the leaves emerge. Plant in full sun in fertile, well-drained soil and provide good air circulation to limit fungal issues. Water regularly during establishment and mulch the root zone, keeping mulch away from the trunk. Prune only lightly right after flowering to maintain the weeping shape, as cherries are prone to disease through large cuts. It makes a spectacular focal point in a lawn or beside a patio.

plant_note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in fall or spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; western sword fern is exceptionally drought tolerant for a fern once established and can grow in drier conditions than most shade ferns. Best suited to mild coastal gardens and the Pacific Coast; tolerates moderate heat in zone 9 with adequate moisture.
directions: Plant in moist to moderately dry, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; western sword fern is the dominant groundcover fern of the Pacific Coast forest — so abundant under the conifer canopy from Alaska to California that it defines the character of the Pacific Northwest woodland aesthetic. It produces large, upright vase-shaped crowns of long, lance-shaped, once-pinnate, deep-green fronds with distinctive little ear-like pinnae at the base of each pinnule. The fronds are evergreen and persistent, providing year-round cover and beauty. Unlike many ferns, western sword fern is remarkably drought tolerant once established, making it suitable for drier shaded conditions under conifers where moisture-loving ferns fail. The leathery fronds are long-lasting as cut foliage in arrangements. A superb structural fern for the cool, moist shade garden; it works at large scale as a groundcover under conifers, at woodland edges, and in large drift plantings where its bold, persistent fronds create a dependable year-round backdrop.

plant_note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial to full shade. All parts of White Baneberry are highly toxic — plant away from areas accessible to children and pets.
directions: White Baneberry, known as Doll's Eyes for its striking porcelain-white berries with a dark purple dot, is a bold native woodland perennial, native to rich, moist, deciduous forests throughout eastern North America from Nova Scotia to Georgia and west to Oklahoma, where it thrives in the deep shade of sugar maple, beech, and basswood forests. Fluffy white flower spikes appear in spring, followed by the distinctive white berries on thick, red-stalked clusters — the berries persist into fall and are visually spectacular, though all parts of the plant are highly toxic and the berries are potentially lethal. An excellent bold-textured foliage plant for the shade garden, with large, deeply divided compound leaves that provide attractive green structure from spring through fall dormancy; plant in deeply amended, moist, humus-rich soil and allow it to slowly spread into colonies. Never ingest any part of this plant.

plant_note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring after temperatures are reliably above 50°F in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; white firespike is even more shade tolerant than red firespike and performs beautifully as an understory plant under large shade trees in humid Gulf Coast gardens.
directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; white firespike is a lesser-known but equally attractive relative of the scarlet-flowered firespike (Odontonema tubaeforme), producing tall, graceful spikes of pure white tubular flowers on dark, glossy-leaved plants in late summer and fall. It is an outstanding shade-garden perennial for zone 9–12, providing important late-season white flower color in deep shade conditions where most other white-flowering plants require more light. The white flowers are particularly effective at brightening dark corners of the garden and glow beautifully in evening light. Like red firespike, it grows most vigorously and productively in the bright shade of large trees or pergolas; it is somewhat more cold-sensitive than the red species and benefits from mulching in zone 9. Propagate from stem cuttings taken in summer. White firespike is less commonly available than red firespike but increasingly offered by specialty nurseries in the Gulf South.

plant_note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped shrubs in late winter or spring in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil in full sun to partial shade; fringetrees are dioecious — female plants produce ornamental black berries if a male is nearby, though both sexes bloom spectacularly.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil in full sun to partial shade; white fringetree is one of the most spectacular spring-flowering native plants in North America, producing masses of drooping, fringe-like, pure white flower panicles (each petal strap-shaped and narrow, like white fringe) that completely cover the plant in late spring, appearing briefly after the leaves emerge. The effect of a fully bloomed fringetree — a large, billowing cloud of pure white flowers — is extraordinary. Female plants produce dark blue-purple olive-like berries in fall that are important food for birds; the leaves turn clear yellow in fall. Remarkably adaptable — tolerating partial shade, moderate drought, clay soils, and a very wide hardiness range (zone 3–9). One of the most valuable and versatile native large shrubs or small trees for the American garden, yet widely underplanted. Slow to establish but very long-lived.

plant_note: Start indoors; transplant in spring into well-drained soil.
directions: White gaura brings airy elegance with long, delicate stems and butterfly-like white blooms that dance in the breeze above the foliage. Start seed indoors 8 to 6 weeks before the last frost and transplant into full sun and lean, well-drained soil. Its deep taproot makes it drought-tolerant once established, so avoid soggy ground and heavy feeding. Cut back spent flowering stems to encourage a fresh flush of bloom through summer and fall. Hardy in zones 5 to 9, it adds graceful height and motion to beds and borders.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring or fall; cool temperatures aid germination.
directions: White Lace produces pure-white, lacy flowers in stunning 3-to-5-inch clusters on strong stems and a compact, bushy habit that suits gardens large and small. Direct sow in early spring or fall, as the seed benefits from cool temperatures and darkness to germinate. Grow in full sun to part shade in average, well-drained soil. This long-lasting cut flower is striking massed or intermingled with colorful blooms. Deadhead to prolong flowering, and let some seed drop to encourage self-sowing.

plant_note: Plant nursery-grown stock in spring in sheltered coastal, estuarine, or tidal sites in full sun in Zone 10–11. White Mangrove typically occupies slightly higher, less frequently flooded ground than Red Mangrove. The seeds germinate rapidly after dropping; propagules should be planted immediately in appropriate coastal substrate. Never plant outside of natural range.
directions: White Mangrove is the most frost-sensitive of the three Florida mangrove species, typically occupying the landward fringe of the mangrove forest on slightly higher ground that is less frequently inundated than the zones occupied by Red and Black Mangrove. Distinguished by its pale, yellowish-gray bark and distinctive pairs of salt-excreting glands (nectaries) at the base of each leaf, White Mangrove lacks the dramatic prop roots of Red Mangrove and the pneumatophore air-root mats of Black Mangrove, though small peg-like root structures occasionally develop in waterlogged conditions. The flowers attract native bees and produce nectar, and the dense canopy provides critical nesting habitat for ibis, egrets, herons, and spoonbills in Florida's coastal rookeries. Restricted to frost-free Zone 10–11 coastal settings in the United States, White Mangrove is protected under Florida law and should only be planted from nursery stock within the appropriate coastal habitat.

plant_note: Plant rooted cuttings or container transplants in spring in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, lean, dry to moderately dry soil in partial shade; white mistflower is native to limestone hills and canyon slopes in south-central Texas and northeastern Mexico and thrives in rocky alkaline conditions where few other plants survive.
directions: Plant in well-drained, lean, alkaline to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; white mistflower is one of the most spectacular fall-blooming native shrubs of the Texas Hill Country and Gulf South, covering itself in dense clusters of pure white, fluffy, ageratum-like flowers in October–November just when most other plants are going dormant. The fragrant flowers are outstanding for butterflies and late-season pollinators, including migrating monarch butterflies that use them as a critical nectar source during the fall migration. The plant is semi-evergreen in mild winters. It grows naturally in the shade of limestone canyon walls and cedars, making it one of the more shade-tolerant native fall-blooming shrubs available. The common name 'mistflower' refers to the soft, misty appearance of the white flower clusters. Cut back by one-third in early spring to encourage compact, dense growth.

plant_note: Plant acorns immediately upon collection (they cannot be stored dry) in fall in deep, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to light shade; white oak develops a very deep taproot and should be planted small and allowed to establish — transplanting large trees is difficult.
directions: Plant fresh acorns in fall or container-grown small trees in spring in deep, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to light shade; white oak is arguably the most ecologically important tree species in eastern North America, supporting more species of caterpillars (534 species of moths and butterflies) than any other native tree genus — a keystone species for the entire eastern food web. Individual trees live 300–600 years and develop into majestic, wide-spreading specimens with deeply furrowed ash-white bark and a broad, irregular crown. The sweet acorns (low tannin, suitable for direct consumption by wildlife and humans) ripen annually and provide critical food for deer, turkeys, wood ducks, blue jays, and dozens of other species. The spectacular crimson, orange, and burgundy fall color rivals the maples. Plant from acorn when possible; the taproot develops early and transplanting large specimens is extremely difficult. A tree for generational thinking.

plant_note: Direct sow seed in fall in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry to average, sandy to rocky soil in full sun; white prairie clover has a deep taproot and does not transplant well once established — direct seeding in fall or early spring produces the best results.
directions: Direct sow seed in fall in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry to average, sandy to rocky soil in full sun; white prairie clover is one of the most valuable native prairie legumes, distinctive for its unique cylindrical flower spikes — the tiny white flowers open progressively upward from the base in a narrow band that travels up the spike over 2–3 weeks, creating an unusual effect quite different from any other native wildflower. The flowers are exceptionally attractive to native bees including specialist Dalea bees (Hesperapis and Protandrena species) that collect pollen exclusively from Dalea plants. It is a nitrogen-fixing legume that enriches the soil and is drought-tolerant and long-lived once established. The deep taproot provides winter hardiness and drought resistance. An outstanding native plant for dry, sunny prairie and meadow gardens, and one of the best plants for specialist native bee support.

plant_note: Plant bulbs 2–3 inches deep and 3–4 inches apart in spring or fall in any well-drained, moist to moderately dry soil in full sun to partial shade; white rain lily is extremely adaptable and easy to grow, naturalizing freely under lawn conditions and in garden borders. Plant in masses for maximum impact — a single bulb produces a modest display, but hundreds of bulbs blooming simultaneously after rain is spectacular.
directions: Plant bulbs in any well-drained, moist to dry soil in full sun to partial shade; white rain lily is one of the most reliable and effortless naturalizing bulbs for zones 7–11, producing masses of pristine white, crocus-like flowers — about 1.5 inches wide with yellow stamens — in late summer and fall, typically triggered to bloom within days of a summer rain after a dry period. The narrow, rush-like, grass-green leaves are present most of the year and are attractive in their own right; the plant multiplies freely by offsets to form an expanding clump over time. In zone 9 it is fully evergreen or semi-evergreen and blooms repeatedly through the warm season with each significant rainfall. It naturalizes beautifully in lawns, under trees, in borders, and in rock gardens, requiring essentially no care once established. Combine with pink Zephyranthes grandiflora, yellow Z. citrina, and salmon-peach Z. 'Grandjax' for a succession of bloom from spring through fall.

plant_note: Surface sow seed in spring in partial to full shade; plants establish readily and self-sow to naturalize. Not appropriate near livestock — the plant's toxins pass through cow's milk and historically caused 'milk sickness'.
directions: Surface sow seed or plant divisions in moist, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; white snakeroot fills a critical niche as a shade-tolerant late-season bloomer, producing clouds of white fluffy flowers from August through October when pollinators most need late-season forage in shaded areas. It naturalizes freely by self-seeding under trees where few other plants bloom in fall. Note that all parts of the plant are toxic to livestock and humans — the toxin tremetol accumulates in milk of cows that graze on it, historically causing 'milk sickness' in settlers. Do not plant near pastures or where children might graze on it; it is safe as an ornamental in a garden setting.

plant_note: Plant bulbs with the neck at or just below the soil surface in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in partial shade; white spider lily follows the same leafless-flower cycle as red spider lily — flowers appear on bare stalks in late summer and fall, before the foliage emerges in winter.
directions: Plant bulbs with the neck at or near the soil surface in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in partial shade to full sun — white spider lily is a rarer and more refined cousin of the familiar red spider lily (Lycoris radiata), producing pure white spider-like flowers with long, arching stamens and reflexed petals on bare 18–24 inch stalks in August–September, just as summer heat begins to break. The white flowers are luminous in evening light and especially effective planted in masse with red spider lilies for striking contrasting colonies. The winter strap-like foliage is attractive through spring, then dies back completely in summer. Like all Lycoris species, the bulbs contain toxic alkaloids and are deer-resistant; they naturalize reliably in the South with no supplemental irrigation once established. The elegant, ghostly white flowers create a dramatic late-summer effect when most other summer flowers have declined in heat.

plant_note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun. White Stopper is an excellent native evergreen hedge or specimen plant for south Florida landscapes; it tolerates salt spray, drought, and some shade.
directions: White Stopper is a native evergreen shrub or small tree, native to the coastal hammocks, tropical hardwood forests, and pineland edges of south Florida, the Florida Keys, and the Caribbean, known for its spicy, clove-like or cinnamon fragrance that can be detected from several feet away — the aromatic foliage distinguishes this species from most other Florida stoppers. Clusters of small, white, four-petaled flowers appear in summer and fall, followed by round berries that ripen from red to black and are an important food source for migrating songbirds in fall and winter. It grows to small tree size in protected hammock situations or can be maintained as a dense, evergreen hedge through pruning. White Stopper tolerates partial shade and is one of the most salt-spray-tolerant of the native Eugenia species, making it a versatile choice for coastal gardens in zones 9b–11 that need a fragrant, wildlife-supporting evergreen screen.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring; biennial that may flower the second year.
directions: White Swan clary sage dazzles with luminous white bracts rising in graceful tiers along each stem for a clean, sculptural summer look. Direct sow in spring in full sun and well-drained soil, barely covering the seed. Though the true flowers are tiny, the papery bracts hold their color beautifully through heat. This drought-tolerant biennial often forms foliage the first year and blooms the second, then may self-sow. Cut the long stems for fresh or dried arrangements and enjoy the pollinators the plant attracts.

plant_note: Cold-stratify seed for best germination; transplant in spring.
directions: White Swan is a graceful white-flowered coneflower selected from the native purple echinacea, with blooms that attract butterflies and seed heads that feed birds. Cold-stratify the seed for a few weeks, then start indoors and transplant into full sun and well-drained soil. This hardy perennial is drought-tolerant once established and needs little care. Leave the cone-like seed heads standing for winter interest and to support birds. Divide clumps every few years to keep them vigorous, and enjoy the pollinator activity all summer.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions in early fall, 2–3 inches deep, with the growing tip pointing up; handle carefully as divisions are slow to recover.
directions: Plant in deep, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil beneath a deciduous canopy where leaf litter accumulates naturally; trilliums are adapted to the nutrient cycling of the eastern forest floor and resent disturbance once established. The plant emerges, blooms, and sets seed all within about six weeks in spring before dying back completely to the ground — mark the location carefully so the dormant rhizome is not accidentally disturbed. Purchase only nursery-propagated plants, as wild-collected trilliums are protected in many states and transplant very poorly. Division for propagation should be attempted only on large, multi-year clumps, as the plant needs 7–10 years from a small piece to reach flowering size.

plant_note: Plant dry corms 2–3 inches deep in fall in moist, rich, humus-rich, well-drained woodland soil in partial to full shade; white trout lily forms colonies slowly by offsets — plant in drifts of 10+ for impact, as single plants are easily lost in the woodland garden.
directions: White trout lily is a native spring ephemeral wildflower of moist deciduous woodlands across the central and eastern United States — its pair of mottled, silvery-green, trout-patterned leaves emerge in early spring and last only until the canopy closes overhead, disappearing completely by June as the plant retreats underground for the rest of the year. The nodding, six-petaled white flowers (with blue or violet anthers) open in April above the characteristic mottled foliage, pollinated primarily by native mining bees and bumblebee queens seeking early-season pollen; the flowers are followed by capsule fruits dispersed by ants (myrmecochory) along with an elaiosome food body the ants consume. White trout lily spreads slowly by underground stolons to form large, showy colonies in undisturbed woodland soil — in ideal conditions, a single planting will gradually expand into drifts of hundreds of plants over decades. Purchase bulbs from native plant nurseries; wild collection is destructive as colonies take 4–7 years to produce the first flower from seed and may not recover from disturbance.

plant_note: Plant divisions in consistently moist to wet soil in partial shade; this plant requires reliable moisture throughout the growing season and will not tolerate drought.
directions: Plant in consistently moist to wet, fertile, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; white turtlehead grows naturally in stream banks, wet meadows, and swamp margins across eastern North America. The inflated, snapdragon-like white (sometimes flushed pink) flowers appear in late summer to fall, providing late-season nectar for bumblebees that are heavy enough to force their way inside the closed flower mouth. It is also the primary larval host plant for the Baltimore checkerspot butterfly, a declining species that depends specifically on this plant. The plant spreads slowly by rhizome. Deadhead spent flowers to encourage additional blooming.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification, or scarify and cold-stratify seed for 60 days before spring sowing; slow to establish from seed, blooming in 3–4 years.
directions: Direct sow in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; white wild indigo grows more slowly than the more common blue Baptisia australis but is better adapted to the drier, hotter conditions of the southern Great Plains and Southeast. The white flower spikes appear in early summer, followed by inflated black seed pods that rattle in the wind and provide winter interest. Like all Baptisia species it develops a large, deep taproot that makes transplanting of established plants impossible; site it carefully and leave it undisturbed once established. It is a larval host plant for wild indigo duskywing and Zarucco duskywing butterflies. Fixes atmospheric nitrogen.

plant_note: Surface-sow and press in; seed needs light to germinate.
directions: Press the fine seed onto the soil surface without burying it, as light aids germination. This native heirloom yarrow bears flat clusters of white flowers above soft, feathery, aromatic foliage. Extremely drought-tolerant once established, it thrives in full sun and lean, well-drained soil. Deadhead to prolong bloom and divide clumps every few years; the flowers are valued in medicinal and dye gardens and dry well for arrangements.

plant_note: Plant nursery-grown transplants in spring in wet to saturated, acidic, nutrient-poor peat or sphagnum bog medium in full sun; keep standing in pure rain water or distilled water — white pitcher plant is a Gulf Coast native that performs reliably in zones 7–9 outdoor bog gardens.
directions: White-top pitcher plant may be the most visually stunning of all native North American pitcher plants — its tall, erect pitchers are dramatically bi-colored with deep green lower tubes transitioning to brilliant, white, red-veined upper sections that function as translucent windows to attract and confuse trapped insects attempting to escape. It is endemic to the Gulf Coastal Plain of Alabama, Florida panhandle, Georgia, Mississippi, and Louisiana, where it grows in open, frequently burned, wet longleaf pine savannas — one of the most threatened habitats in North America, having lost over 97% of its original extent. White pitcher plant grows second pitchers in fall (alongside the primary summer pitchers) that are among the most ornamental of any carnivorous plant in the autumn garden, and the spectacular crimson flowers in spring are pollinated by queen bumblebees. A critical conservation indicator species, white pitcher plant should only be purchased from reputable nurseries propagating from cultivated stock; wild populations are legally protected and ecologically irreplaceable. Grow in an outdoor bog garden in full sun for the most impressive pitcher development.

plant_note: Direct sow seed in early spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial shade; plants spread steadily by rhizome and can naturalize in appropriate moist woodland settings. Do not confuse with purple loosestrife (Lythrum salicaria), an invasive wetland plant — this is a completely different, well-behaved native species.
directions: Direct sow in early spring or plant divisions in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial shade; whorled loosestrife is a charming native woodland wildflower of eastern North America, recognizable by its distinctive architecture — the lance-shaped leaves are arranged in precise whorls of 4 around the stem, and small five-petaled yellow flowers with a red center arise from the axil of each leaf in the whorl, creating a very regular, geometric flowering pattern in early summer. It is in no way related to the invasive purple loosestrife (Lythrum) — this is a well-behaved native Lysimachia that spreads slowly by rhizome in appropriate moist, acidic woodland conditions. It provides early-summer pollen and floral oils for specialist native bees in the family Macropis. A lovely, understated wildflower for the naturalized woodland garden.

plant_note: Direct sow seed in fall or cold-stratify for 4–6 weeks and sow in spring; whorled milkweed thrives in dry, rocky, sandy, or clay soils in full sun and is one of the most drought-tolerant and widely adaptable of all native milkweeds.
directions: Direct sow in fall or transplant in spring in dry, well-drained soil in full sun — whorled milkweed is a delicate-looking but remarkably tough native milkweed of open prairies, dry roadsides, and rocky glades from Massachusetts to New Mexico, distinguished from other milkweeds by its very narrow, needle-like leaves arranged in whorls of 3–6 around slender stems. The small but numerous clusters of tiny white flowers are deliciously fragrant and are visited by dozens of native bee, butterfly, and moth species. Like all milkweeds, it is a monarch butterfly host plant — the larvae consume the foliage — and the foliage is toxic to livestock so should be avoided in pasture situations. It grows in some of the poorest soils in North America and is an ideal native groundcover for dry, sunny slopes, gravel gardens, and along dry roadsides where typical garden plants fail. An important and under-planted native milkweed for garden diversity.

plant_note: Plant rooted stem cuttings or divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich soil; division of established mats in fall is also successful.
directions: Plant in humus-rich, slightly moist, well-drained soil in partial to light shade; this native phlox carpets the eastern woodland floor in pale lavender-blue in mid-spring, flowering before the tree canopy closes overhead and providing early nectar for bumblebee queens and phlox moths. After bloom, the stems sprawl and root at the nodes to spread the colony gradually, forming a semi-evergreen groundcover. Shear lightly after flowering to encourage denser growth and possible rebloom in fall. Do not confuse with creeping phlox (P. subulata), which needs full sun — this species will fail in those conditions.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall and allow natural cold stratification; alternatively cold-stratify seeds for 60 days in damp paper towels in the refrigerator before spring sowing.
directions: Sow seeds in fall in moist, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; this prolific self-seeder quickly naturalizes to form colonies of heart-shaped leaves and violet-blue spring flowers. Both the flowers and young leaves are edible and nutritious — the flowers make beautiful garnishes and violet leaf tea has long been used traditionally. After the showy spring flowers, the plant produces inconspicuous cleistogamous flowers near the soil all summer that set abundant seed; do not confuse these seed capsules with aphid infestations. The plant spreads generously by seed and rhizome — ideal for a naturalized meadow or wild shade garden but potentially aggressive in small formal beds.

plant_note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade. Wild Coffee is one of the finest native shade shrubs for south Florida landscapes and is an important larval host plant for several native butterfly species.
directions: Wild Coffee is one of the most valuable native shade shrubs for south Florida and tropical hammock gardens, native to the dense, shaded understories of tropical hardwood hammocks, coastal hammocks, and pinelands from Brevard and Hillsborough counties south through the Florida Keys and into the Caribbean. Its large, glossy, strongly quilted or pleated dark green leaves with prominent veins are among the most attractive foliage textures in the Florida native palette, providing a lush, tropical appearance in deep shade where few other shrubs thrive. Small white flowers appear year-round, followed by clusters of bright red berries in fall and winter that are a vital food source for migrating warblers, robins, and thrushes. Despite the common name, it is not a true coffee (Coffea), though the plants share family membership; the berries are not edible by humans. An outstanding shade shrub for zone 9b–11 landscapes that fills the difficult deep-shade role with year-round beauty and wildlife value.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring in a shaded bed, barely covering seed; cold stratification for 60 days improves germination. Division of established crowns in early spring is faster.
directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; this native of eastern woodland edges blooms in mid-spring with lavender-pink five-petaled flowers, then develops distinctive long-beaked seed capsules that fling seeds explosively when ripe. The deeply cut, hand-shaped foliage often turns attractive shades of red and orange in fall before dying back. The root has medicinal tannins and has been used historically as an astringent. Divide established clumps every 4–5 years in early spring to maintain vigor; transplants establish readily and begin blooming in their first or second year.

plant_note: Plant bulbs 3–4 inches deep and 4–6 inches apart in groups of 10 or more in fall; the bulbs need a cold period to bloom. Avoid planting in wet soils that stay saturated in summer when bulbs are dormant.
directions: Plant bulbs 3–4 inches deep in fall in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; wild hyacinth is the smaller, more shade-tolerant of the eastern native camas species, growing naturally in moist prairie remnants, open woodland edges, and meadows from Ontario south to Texas. The pale blue-violet flowers on slender stems appear in mid-spring and are among the first native wildflowers to bloom, providing pollen for early native bees before most flowers have opened. Plant in large drifts (25+ bulbs) for the best visual effect. The foliage dies back completely by early summer, so mark plant locations and interplant with later-emerging perennials to fill the gap. The bulbs are edible — they were an important food source for Native American peoples — but require proper preparation.

plant_note: Plant container-grown transplants in spring in well-drained, sandy to rocky, alkaline to neutral, coastal soil in full sun. Wild Lime is highly drought-tolerant and salt-tolerant once established but is cold-sensitive and best suited to frost-free or nearly frost-free landscapes in Zone 9b–11.
directions: Wild Lime is a native evergreen shrub or small tree of coastal hammocks, rocky pinelands, and scrub edges of southern Florida and coastal Texas, recognizable by its small, dark green leaflets attached to a winged rachis — a distinctive feature of the citrus family — that release a sharp lime fragrance when crushed. A critical host plant for the giant swallowtail butterfly, the largest native butterfly in North America, Wild Lime provides the caterpillar's only food source in many south Florida hammock habitats, and its dense, spiny growth makes it an excellent barrier plant or habitat hedge in tropical and subtropical landscapes. The small, gland-dotted aromatic fruits and leaves are used in Caribbean folk medicine and regional cooking, and the plant's salt tolerance and drought-hardiness make it particularly valuable in coastal south Florida settings. Plant in well-drained, sandy to rocky, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; established plants require virtually no irrigation or maintenance in appropriate Zone 9b–11 landscapes.

plant_note: Plant container shrubs in spring in moist, well-drained, acidic soil in partial shade; one of the few native evergreen shrubs that performs in partial to full shade in the Southeast.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; wild olive is the native North American member of the same genus as the prized Asian fragrant sweet olive (O. fragrans), and though its white flowers in late spring are smaller and less flamboyant, they carry a delicate, sweet fragrance and are borne abundantly on large shrubs or small trees in woodland settings. It grows naturally in moist, shaded coastal plain forests and ravine forests of the Southeast, making it well-suited to garden use under tall trees where it provides refined, glossy-leaved evergreen structure year-round. The small, dark blue berry-like drupes ripen in fall and provide wildlife food. Slow growing but extremely long-lived; excellent as a foundation shrub or informal hedge in shade.

plant_note: Direct sow seed in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, dry to average, sandy to rocky soil in full sun to partial shade; wild petunia self-sows prolifically in appropriate conditions and can naturalize in dry, open areas — a welcome attribute in a native meadow but worth noting in a more formal setting.
directions: Sow seed in fall in well-drained, dry to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade — wild petunia (Ruellia humilis) is a native prairie perennial that produces a continuous succession of lavender to pale violet, 1.5-inch funnel-shaped flowers remarkably similar in appearance to garden petunias through summer and into fall, making it a genuinely ornamental native for dry, sunny or partly shaded settings. Individual flowers last only a day, but new ones open continuously over many weeks on the low, hairy, sessile-leaved plants. It grows naturally in dry, open prairies, rocky glades, and open woodland edges. Unlike tropical Ruellia (Mexican petunia, R. simplex), native wild petunia is not invasive — it self-sows modestly and is well-behaved in most gardens. Excellent for rock gardens, dry slopes, and native plant gardens. An important nectar source for bumblebees and long-tongued native bees.

plant_note: Direct sow after last frost in sandy, well-drained, acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; this plant grows naturally in dry, sandy upland soils and pine woods — do not plant in heavy clay or rich, moist soils.
directions: Direct sow in dry, sandy, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; wild petunia is a delicate native wildflower of the dry sandy pine flatwoods and upland forests of the Southeast, producing lavender-blue tubular flowers resembling petunias (though not related) throughout the growing season from spring through fall. It grows naturally in infertile, dry conditions where it avoids competition from more aggressive plants. The trumpet-shaped flowers are visited by hummingbirds, sphinx moths, and small native bees. Plant in rock gardens, in sandy soils under pines, or at dry woodland margins; it will not tolerate wet or clay soils. It self-sows reliably and naturalizes into small colonies in ideal conditions. In zone 9–10 it may behave as an evergreen short-lived perennial.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification; alternatively cold-stratify indoors and spring-sow after last frost.
directions: Sow in lean to average, well-drained soil in full sun; wild quinine is a sturdy native prairie plant that tolerates heat, drought, and clay once established. The flat-topped clusters of small white button flowers appear in midsummer and persist for many weeks, drawing in a wide diversity of native bees, wasps, and beetles. The button-like, persistent seed heads provide architectural interest into fall and winter. A reliable, long-lived plant that requires virtually no maintenance once established in a sunny meadow or native plant garden.

plant_note: Scarify seeds by nicking or soaking in hot water for 24 hours before sowing after last frost; can also be started indoors 4 weeks earlier for transplanting.
directions: Scarify and direct sow in fertile to average, moist to well-drained soil in full sun; wild senna is a bold-textured native perennial with large, compound leaves and bright yellow flowers in midsummer that are structurally perfect for specialist bumblebee species that sonicate (buzz-pollinate) the flowers to release pollen. It is also the larval host plant for cloudless sulphur and sleepy orange butterflies. The persistent seed pods provide food for birds through winter. Cut back to the ground in late fall or early spring; the plant regrows vigorously from the crown each year. Develops a substantial taproot and is difficult to transplant once established; select your site carefully.

plant_note: Root stem cuttings in spring or fall; this native stonecrop takes easily from even small stem sections laid on moist soil.
directions: Plant cuttings or plugs in rocky, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; this is one of the very few native sedums that thrives in shade, growing naturally on shaded stream banks and moist cliff faces where other plants struggle. The flat, succulent rosettes of three-part leaves form a dense groundcover, and the starry white flowers in spring attract native bees. Unlike most sedums, it prefers consistent moisture and will not thrive in hot, dry, sunny locations. Propagate by division or stem cuttings any time during the growing season; pieces root readily when placed in contact with moist soil.

plant_note: Plant small container trees (1–2 gallon) in late winter or spring in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil; willow oak transplants better than most oaks and establishes quickly. Plant acorns immediately upon collecting in fall.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil in full sun; willow oak is one of the most graceful native oaks, producing narrow, willow-like leaves unlike any other oak, giving the tree an unusually fine-textured, feathery appearance in the landscape. It grows naturally in bottomland forests and moist uplands across the coastal South and is one of the most widely planted native oaks as a street tree for its tolerance of urban conditions, moist soils, and relatively fast growth rate (1–2 feet per year). The tiny acorns produced in enormous abundance beginning at 10–15 years provide critical fall and winter food for wood ducks, mallards, wild turkeys, squirrels, and songbirds. Fall color is not as vivid as red oak but pleasant golden-yellow. Very long-lived and ultimately a large landscape tree.

plant_note: Plant in spring in a sunny, well-drained spot or a container that can move indoors in cold zones.
directions: Wilma Goldcrest is a lemon cypress with glowing golden-green foliage that releases a fresh citrus scent when brushed, and a narrow, upright form perfect for tight spaces. Grow it in full sun to keep the foliage brightest, in fast-draining soil that never stays waterlogged. In containers it stays around 3 to 5 feet, while in the ground in mild climates it can reach 10 feet. In zones colder than 7, grow it in a pot and bring it indoors near a bright window over winter. Water when the top inch of soil dries, and avoid soggy roots.

plant_note: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; windmill palm grows slowly but is remarkably cold-hardy for a feather-leafed palm, surviving temperatures as low as 5–10°F with temporary damage in established specimens, making it viable in zone 7b and above.
directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; windmill palm is the cold-hardiest of all feather-leafed palms and one of the most widely planted palms in the temperate-to-subtropical transition zone — a slow-growing, elegant palm with a distinctive burlap-wrapped trunk covered in persistent fibrous leaf bases and a crown of fan-shaped leaves. It brings a genuine tropical accent to zone 7–11 landscapes where other palms cannot survive. It grows naturally on cool mountain slopes in central China and adapts to a wide range of soils, including clay, alkaline, and nutrient-poor sites. Unlike most palms, it tolerates considerable shade, making it useful for planting under taller trees. The slow growth rate (6–12 inches per year) means windmill palms are long-term investments; they become increasingly beautiful with age. Excellent as a container palm in zone 6.

plant_note: Plant container-grown transplants in early spring in well-drained to moderately moist, loamy or clay soil in full sun to light shade. Winged Elm seeds ripen in early spring before leafout and must be sown fresh immediately upon collection, as viability is lost within days; transplanting from nursery stock is the practical choice for most gardeners.
directions: Winged Elm is a medium-sized native tree of dry to mesic upland woods, rocky slopes, fence rows, and disturbed roadsides across the southeastern and south-central United States from Virginia to Florida and west to Kansas, readily identified by the distinctive corky, wing-like ridges that develop along younger branches — a feature unique among eastern elms. The seeds ripen in early spring before the leaves emerge and must be sown fresh in moist, well-drained soil immediately upon collection, as germination viability drops to near zero within days of ripening; nursery transplants are the practical method for establishing this species. Winged Elm is among the toughest and most adaptable of the native elms, tolerating drought, compacted clay, alkaline soils, and periodic flooding, and shows some tolerance to Dutch elm disease, making it a useful native tree for difficult urban sites and naturalized roadsides.
