The vase-shaped canopy, small rough-textured leaves, and light-yellow fall color provide multi-season ornamental interest with minimal maintenance.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring in moist, fertile soil in partial shade; plants establish readily and spread by rhizome and self-seeding, naturalizing aggressively once happy. Do not plant in small gardens without containment.
directions: Plant in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; wingstem is a bold, tall native wildflower of moist woodland edges and stream banks with distinctive narrow wings running down the length of the stem (giving it the common name), topped with loose clusters of raggedy yellow flowers in August through October that are outstanding late-season nectar sources for bees, wasps, and butterflies. The plant is one of the most shade-tolerant of the tall native yellow-flowered composites, blooming reliably in significant shade where few similar plants perform. It spreads aggressively by rhizome and should be given a naturalized area of its own or managed by division. The persistent seed heads are eaten by goldfinches and other seed-eating birds through fall and winter.

plant_note: Plant the potted or bare-root climber in spring after hard frosts.
directions: Winner's Circle is a vigorous climbing rose with bright, cherry-red double blooms and a strong, upright habit that flowers spring through fall. Plant the potted or bare-root rose in spring in full sun and rich, well-drained soil near a sturdy trellis, arbor, or fence to support the canes. Tie in the long canes as they grow and train them horizontally to encourage more blooms. Water at the base to keep foliage dry, mulch to conserve moisture, and prune in late winter to shape and remove old wood. Hardy in zones 4 to 10, it rewards care with repeat flushes of color.

plant_note: Plant tubers in fall as soon as available — they dry out quickly; soak in water for 24 hours before planting.
directions: Soak the small, hard tubers in water for 24 hours before planting to rehydrate them; plant 2 in deep under deciduous trees or shrubs as soon as received in late summer or early fall. Bright yellow cup-shaped flowers surrounded by a green ruff appear in late winter, often alongside snowdrops, before any leaves emerge on overhead trees. Winter aconite self-seeds freely and slowly forms large, cheerful carpets if left undisturbed. Best planted where they can stay permanently; they strongly dislike being moved.

plant_note: Plant at least one male and three female shrubs for berry production; one male can pollinate up to five females. Match the male cultivar's bloom time to the female's — choose male cultivar recommendations from the nursery that match your female selection.
directions: Plant in moist to wet, acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich soil in full sun to partial shade; winterberry is a spectacular native deciduous holly that produces brilliant red (or yellow or orange in some cultivars) berries that encrust every branch after leaf drop in fall, providing dazzling winter interest from October through February when birds finally strip them. It grows naturally in swamps, stream banks, and wet woodland margins and performs best with consistent moisture. This species is dioecious — you must plant both male and female plants, matching cultivars that bloom at the same time, to get berries. Named female cultivars like 'Winter Red', 'Sparkleberry', and 'Red Sprite' (compact) are widely available; match them with the appropriate male. Mulch to maintain soil moisture and acidity.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost risk has passed.
directions: Surface-sow tiny seeds on moist mix without covering; they need light to germinate at 70–75°F. Torenia is one of the best flowering annuals for shaded and part-shaded spots in hot, humid climates where impatiens are lost to downy mildew. The bicolored, snapdragon-like flowers in purple, pink, white, or yellow bloom profusely without deadheading throughout summer. Keep soil consistently moist, as plants wilt quickly when dry but recover readily; fertilize monthly with a balanced liquid feed.

plant_note: Plant container shrubs in early spring or late fall in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade; the plant transplants well from container stock but is slow to establish from seed. The species blooms October–December; vernal witch hazel (H. vernalis) blooms in late winter.
directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade; American witch hazel is the latest-blooming native shrub, producing clusters of fragrant, spidery yellow flowers on bare branches from October through December — often while the leaves are still falling around the flowers. It is a stunning sight in the November woodland garden when nothing else is in bloom. Growing naturally as a multi-stemmed understory tree in moist woodland slopes and ravines across eastern North America, it tolerates full shade but blooms more heavily with some dappled light. The bark and leaves are the botanical source of witch hazel astringent. Allow it to grow naturally as a large multi-stemmed specimen; it resents heavy pruning. The seed pods pop open explosively to disperse seeds.

plant_note: Plant rhizomes in fall in humus-rich shade.
directions: Plant wood anemone rhizomes in fall just below the surface in cool, humus-rich woodland soil in partial to full shade. The starry white spring flowers carpet the ground before deciduous trees leaf out, then the plant goes dormant by summer. It spreads slowly by creeping rhizomes to form a lovely ephemeral groundcover. Keep the soil moist during growth and mark the location, as the foliage disappears entirely in summer.

plant_note: Plant divisions or nursery transplants in fall or early spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade; woodland phlox naturalizes by self-sown seed and stolons. If starting from seed, cold-stratify 60–90 days before sowing; germination is slow and erratic.
directions: Plant divisions in partial shade in moist, humus-rich soil in fall or early spring; woodland phlox is one of the most beautiful native spring wildflowers of eastern North America, producing masses of fragrant flat-faced flowers in shades of pale blue, lavender, violet, and white in mid-spring — blooming at the same time as Virginia bluebells and trilliums to create spectacular native wildflower combinations. The semi-evergreen foliage forms spreading mats that suppress weeds throughout the growing season, and unlike tall garden phlox (P. paniculata), woodland phlox is rarely troubled by powdery mildew when given good air circulation in a shaded site. Selected cultivars include 'Clouds of Perfume' (fragrant blue-violet), 'London Grove Blue' (deep blue), and 'Fuller's White' (pure white). An excellent pollinator plant attracting hummingbirds, swallowtail butterflies, and long-tongued native bees. Spreads slowly by rooting stolons to fill woodland edges and shaded borders naturally.

plant_note: Direct sow seed in fall in dry to average, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial shade; woodland sunflower spreads by rhizome to form large colonies and is best used where aggressive naturalization is welcome — it is exceptionally effective at naturalizing along dry woodland edges and under oak canopies.
directions: Sow seed in fall or divide in spring in dry to average, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade — woodland sunflower is one of the very few perennial sunflowers genuinely adapted to partial shade, producing 2–3 inch yellow daisy flowers in mid-to-late summer on upright stems 3–6 feet tall in conditions where most sunflowers refuse to bloom. It grows naturally in dry, open woodlands, woodland edges, and clearings throughout the eastern US and is especially common along the edges of oak woodland, thriving in the dry shade beneath the oak canopy that challenges most sun-loving perennials. It spreads vigorously by rhizome — use this to advantage in naturalizing dry, shaded areas with low-maintenance native cover and late-season pollinator value. The seeds are eaten by goldfinches and other seed-eating birds. An outstanding native for filling the challenging dry, part-shade niche.

plant_note: Plant container vines or rooted cuttings in spring in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; woolly Dutchman's pipe is the southeastern-native species of Aristolochia, hardier and better adapted to Gulf Coast conditions than A. macrophylla.
directions: Plant in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun with a sturdy support structure; woolly Dutchman's pipe is the southeastern US native species of Aristolochia, distinguished from A. macrophylla by its dense woolly coating of soft hairs on the leaves and stems — a beautiful textural detail. Like all native Aristolochia species, it is the exclusive larval host plant for the pipevine swallowtail butterfly (Battus philenor), one of the most spectacular black-and-iridescent-blue native butterflies in eastern North America. A single established vine supports dozens of caterpillars each season. The unusual curved, yellowish-green to brown pipe-shaped flowers are insect-trapping devices that temporarily trap small flies for pollination. Twining stems reach 10–20 feet and provide rapid screening in partial shade. Highly recommended for butterfly gardens in zones 5–9, particularly in the Southeast where this species is native.

plant_note: Surface sow seed 4–6 weeks before last frost without covering; seeds need light to germinate. Can also divide established clumps in spring. Self-sows reliably and spreads by rhizome.
directions: Surface sow seed in moist, average to rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; wrinkleleaf goldenrod is notable among goldenrods for its significant shade tolerance and preference for moist to wet conditions, growing naturally in wet meadows, stream margins, and lightly shaded bottomlands. The arching stems of yellow flowers in September and October provide critical late-season nectar for migrating monarchs, native bees, and late-emerging moths in shaded settings where most other nectar plants don't grow. The cultivar 'Fireworks' is named for its particularly graceful, arching flower sprays and is the most widely available selection. Spreads by rhizome and self-seed into naturalizing colonies; divide every 3 years in spring to maintain vigor. An excellent choice for moist, partly shaded rain gardens and woodland borders.

plant_note: Plant tubers after the last frost once soil has warmed.
directions: Wyns New Pastel is a tall, vigorous dahlia bearing big pink-and-yellow blended blooms all season on a well-branched, hedge-like plant. Plant tubers 4 to 6 inches deep in full sun and rich, well-drained soil after the danger of frost has passed. Because of its height, provide sturdy staking early and pinch the central shoot to encourage branching. Cut flowers freely and deadhead to keep new buds forming through summer and fall. In cold climates, dig and store the tubers over winter, replanting them the following spring.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in full sun; blooms quickly from seed.
directions: Xanthos is a breakthrough soft-yellow cosmos, an award winner that is early to flower on well-branched, shorter plants ideal for containers and the front of the border. Direct sow after the last frost in full sun and average, well-drained soil, barely covering the seed. It thrives in lean soil, so avoid overfeeding, which produces foliage at the expense of blooms. Deadhead or cut flowers often to keep the plant producing through summer. Drought-tolerant once established, it draws bees, butterflies, and other pollinators.

plant_note: Direct sow after last frost; prefers to be direct sown as transplants disturb its taproot.
directions: Direct sow after last frost in full sun with lean, well-drained soil; xeranthemum is one of the easiest everlastings to grow and tolerates poor, dry conditions well. The papery, daisy-like flowers in shades of lavender, pink, purple, and white are naturally self-preserving and air-dry on the stem. Harvest stems for drying when flowers are about two-thirds open; hang upside down in a warm, airy location for 2–3 weeks. Successive sowings 2–3 weeks apart can extend the harvest season into early fall.

plant_note: Direct sow on the soil surface in early spring (seeds need light to germinate) or plant divisions in spring or fall in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; yarrow thrives in poor, dry soil and actually performs better without fertilizer — rich soil causes floppy, weak growth.
directions: Direct sow or divide in spring in well-drained, lean soil in full sun; yarrow is one of the most drought-tolerant and adaptable native perennials of North America, producing flat-topped clusters of tiny flowers in white (wild type) or warm shades of yellow, gold, salmon, pink, and red in named cultivars from summer through fall. The ferny, deeply divided aromatic foliage is attractive throughout the season. Yarrow is exceptional for dry, sunny, difficult sites — it tolerates poor soil, heat, drought, and deer grazing and is one of the best perennials for planting on slopes and in gravel gardens. The flat flower heads are important landing platforms for beneficial insects including parasitic wasps, lacewings, and hover flies. Cut stems back by half after the first flush to encourage reblooming; divide every 3 years as plants spread outward. Outstanding long-lasting cut and dried flowers.

plant_note: Plant in moist, acidic, organically rich, well-drained to moist soil in full to partial shade; yellow anise tree performs best in the filtered shade of tall pines or oaks and adapts to wet to seasonally dry conditions across the Gulf Coastal Plain.
directions: Yellow anise tree is a rare Florida native evergreen shrub endemic to the Ocala National Forest and surrounding central Florida scrub and flatwoods, closely related to Florida anise (Illicium floridanum) but producing small, inconspicuous yellow flowers rather than red ones — its primary appeal is its dense, lustrous evergreen foliage and exceptional shade tolerance that make it one of the finest screening shrubs for difficult deep-shade sites in Gulf Coast landscapes. All parts of the plant contain toxic compounds similar to true star anise (Illicium verum) and emit a strong, pleasant anise-like fragrance when crushed, making deer browsing rare — an excellent choice where deer pressure limits other evergreen shrubs. Plants grow moderately quickly into dense, mounding 5–10 foot specimens that provide excellent year-round privacy screening in shaded settings where hollies and other broadleaf evergreens might struggle; they tolerate periodic flooding and are well-suited to moist, organic soils. As a critically rare Florida endemic, yellow anise tree deserves wider use in horticulture throughout zones 7–10 wherever a tough, fragrant, shade-tolerant evergreen screening shrub is needed.

plant_note: Direct sow seed or plant container-grown plants in spring after frost danger passes in any well-drained soil in full sun; esperanza grows rapidly from seed and can bloom the first year. It dies back to the ground after hard freezes in zone 8 but regrows strongly from established roots in spring — do not cut back until new growth confirms the roots have survived.
directions: Plant in full sun in any well-drained to dry soil — sandy, rocky, alkaline, and poor soils are all acceptable; esperanza or yellow bells is a native flowering shrub and small tree of Texas, the Southwest, and Mexico that produces brilliant golden-yellow trumpet-shaped flowers in large clusters almost continuously from late spring through frost. It is among the most heat tolerant and drought resistant flowering plants available for zone 8–11 landscapes, thriving in conditions that challenge almost everything else. The showy flowers attract hummingbirds and butterflies prolifically throughout the summer. The cultivar 'Gold Star' is the most widely planted, selected for compact habit and extra-large flower clusters. Esperanza grows vigorously and may need annual cutting back in mild winters; in zone 9 it is woody and persistent year-round while in zone 8 it typically dies to the ground in winter and regrows from roots. It blooms on new wood, so frost damage sets back the plant temporarily, not permanently.

plant_note: Plant container-grown transplants in early spring in moist, well-drained, acidic, humus-rich, cool soil in partial shade to full sun. Yellow Birch does not tolerate heat, drought, alkaline soils, or heavy clay and is best suited to cool northern or mountain climates in Zone 3–7.
directions: Yellow Birch is a graceful, long-lived native tree of moist, cool Appalachian and northeastern forests from eastern Canada south to the mountains of Georgia and Tennessee, distinguished by its golden-yellow to bronze, papery bark that peels in thin, curly horizontal strips and provides remarkable year-round ornamental interest. The young twigs and inner bark carry a distinctive sweet wintergreen fragrance from the natural methyl salicylate they contain, and the sap can be tapped in late winter for a birch syrup similar to maple syrup; the bark was historically distilled to produce oil of wintergreen. An important component of northern hardwood forest ecosystems, Yellow Birch provides critical nesting habitat for warblers, vireos, and thrushes while moose, deer, and snowshoe hares browse the twigs and bark through winter. Plant in moist, well-drained, acidic, humus-rich, cool soil in partial shade to full sun; Yellow Birch demands consistently cool, moist conditions and is not suitable for hot, dry, or alkaline climates.

plant_note: Plant fresh (unsoaked) nuts in fall in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, deep, well-drained soil in partial shade; unlike most trees, yellow buckeye performs best in partial shade, especially afternoon shade in zone 7–8. The fresh nuts lose viability rapidly — plant within weeks of collection.
directions: Plant in moist, fertile, deep, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade — yellow buckeye is unusual among trees in that it actually performs better in partial shade than full sun, making it one of the best large native trees for the shaded garden. Native to rich mountain forests of the southern Appalachians, it produces beautiful upright clusters of yellow flowers in spring that attract hummingbirds and long-tongued native bees, and large smooth seed pods containing 1–3 mahogany-brown nuts with pale patches — striking ornamental fall fruit. The palmate compound leaves of 5 leaflets are among the first to emerge in spring and first to drop in fall (often by August in drought). It tolerates understory conditions and is frequently found growing as a subcanopy tree beneath tulip poplar and basswood. The nuts are toxic to humans but consumed by squirrels. Often considered the most beautiful of the North American buckeyes.

plant_note: Plant nursery-propagated leaf cuttings or divisions in spring in wet, acidic to neutral, nutrient-poor, sandy or peaty soil in full sun to partial shade; yellow butterwort is a Gulf Coast native that tolerates the humid summers of zones 7–9 better than most temperate butterworts.
directions: Yellow butterwort is a native carnivorous plant of the Gulf Coastal Plain's wet pine flatwoods and seepage slopes, producing flat rosettes of bright yellow-green, greasy-textured leaves covered in sticky glands that capture small insects and fungus gnats — the oily, buttery texture of the leaves inspired the 'butterwort' common name. In late winter and spring, beautiful, pale yellow, five-petaled flowers on long, delicate stalks emerge from the center of each rosette, appearing before many other wildflowers in the Gulf Coast bog community. Unlike the complex snap-trap mechanism of Venus flytrap or the drowning pitchers of Sarracenia, butterworts use a simple passive flypaper strategy — insects land on the sticky leaves and are unable to escape; digestive glands then secrete enzymes to break down the prey. Yellow butterwort is relatively easy to grow among carnivorous plants, adapting to a range of wet, acidic to neutral conditions and tolerating the heat and humidity of Gulf Coast winters when other carnivorous plants may struggle. Propagate readily from leaf cuttings laid on moist sphagnum moss in bright light.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification, or cold-stratify seed in the refrigerator for 4–6 weeks before spring sowing; once established the plant self-sows prolifically.
directions: Sow seed in fall in well-drained, gritty soil in partial shade; yellow corydalis is a European native that has naturalized in North American gardens and thrives in wall crevices, shaded rock gardens, and the base of stone steps where drainage is perfect. Once established it self-sows freely and colonizes shaded hard-to-plant areas, blooming with small tubular yellow flowers nearly continuously from spring through fall in cool climates. The finely divided, gray-green foliage is attractive even out of bloom. Remove unwanted seedlings while small; established plants have taproots that resist transplanting.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions at the water's edge or in wet, mucky soil in partial shade to full sun; yellow flag iris spreads rapidly by both rhizome and prolific seed set in wet conditions — it is listed as invasive in many US states and should not be planted near natural waterways, wetlands, or drainage areas. Deadhead promptly to prevent seed spread.
directions: Plant rhizome divisions at pond margins, in wet ditches, or in containers submerged in water gardens in full sun to partial shade — yellow flag iris is one of the most spectacularly showy wetland plants in the world, producing large, bright golden-yellow iris flowers in late spring on tall, sword-leaved clumps 3–5 feet high, thriving in standing water, stream banks, and boggy soils where few other ornamentals succeed. It is a superb water garden marginal and naturalizes rapidly in any wet, disturbed area. However, it is introduced from Europe and is listed as an invasive plant in much of the eastern and western US, crowding out native wetland vegetation — do not plant it near natural wetlands, ponds, streams, or drainage systems, and remove seed heads immediately after blooming. For water gardens, contain the rhizomes in submerged pots to prevent spread. The native blue flag iris (I. virginica) or southern blue flag (I. virginica var. shrevei) are strongly preferred alternatives in most areas.

plant_note: Plant only nursery-propagated specimens in spring in moist to wet, acidic, sandy or peaty, nutrient-poor soil in full sun to partial shade; yellow fringed orchid requires the acidic, wet soils of bogs, seepage slopes, and moist pine flatwoods — plant in partnership with appropriate mycorrhizal fungi.
directions: Yellow fringed orchid is among the most spectacularly beautiful of all North American native wildflowers — a magnificent spike of dozens of bright golden-orange to deep orange flowers, each with a dramatically fringed lower lip that creates a feathery, lace-like appearance unique among wildflowers, blooming in July and August in open bogs, moist savannas, and wet pine flatwoods throughout the Gulf Coastal Plain and eastern United States. The long, slender nectar spur extending from each flower can only be reached by long-tongued swallowtail butterflies (especially tiger and spicebush swallowtails), which are the primary pollinators — the match between floral anatomy and pollinator tongue length is so precise that no other insect can effectively pollinate the flowers. Found throughout the Gulf Coast longleaf pine flatwoods and seepage bogs from Texas to Florida, where it blooms in late summer heat alongside pitcher plants, sundews, and other carnivorous plants of this unique, fire-maintained ecosystem. An unforgettable sight when in full bloom and worth every effort to establish in a properly maintained bog garden.

plant_note: Plant container-grown vines in fall or early spring in average to moist, well-drained soil near a trellis, fence, or shrub to climb; yellow honeysuckle tolerates partial shade and can be trained as a scrambling groundcover or trained vertically for hummingbird gardens.
directions: Yellow honeysuckle is a native semi-evergreen vine of the Ozarks and Appalachian foothills, one of the most ornamentally beautiful of all American honeysuckles — its showy clusters of vivid golden-yellow to orange-yellow trumpet flowers in spring are magnets for ruby-throated hummingbirds during their northward migration and are also visited by long-tongued bumblebees and sphinx moths. Unlike the invasive Japanese honeysuckle (Lonicera japonica) that has escaped across the South, yellow honeysuckle is a well-behaved native that twines moderately and stays in bounds without smothering trees or shrubs; it is semi-evergreen in mild winters in the South. Small red berries ripen in summer and are eaten by mockingbirds and other fruit-eating birds. Train on a picket fence, stone wall, arbor, or through a native shrub border — it blooms most profusely in partial shade to full sun and combines beautifully with coral honeysuckle for extended bloom.

plant_note: Plant only nursery-propagated, tissue-cultured specimens from specialist orchid nurseries in fall; yellow lady's slipper is the most adaptable of the native Cypripedium species and can succeed in more garden conditions than pink lady's slipper — plant in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained, humus-rich soil in partial shade.
directions: Yellow lady's slipper is the most widely distributed and arguably the most garden-adaptable of all native North American orchids — growing in moist, slightly acidic to neutral woodland soils across an enormous range from Canada to the Gulf Coast states, and tolerating a wider range of pH and soil conditions than the highly acid-demanding pink lady's slipper. The golden-yellow, pouch-shaped flowers with maroon-striped lateral petals and a rich, sweet-spicy fragrance emerge in May from stems bearing 1–3 blooms; bumblebee queens are the primary pollinators, entering the pouch to discover there is no nectar and escaping through a back exit that brushes them with pollen — a deceptive but effective pollination mechanism. Like all Cypripedium, it forms a long-lived clump that slowly expands by rhizomes; established plants purchased from nurseries and planted in appropriate woodland conditions can persist for decades, eventually forming spectacular multi-stemmed flowering colonies. The most rewarding and accessible native terrestrial orchid for the shaded woodland garden when purchased as tissue-cultured nursery stock.

plant_note: Divide rhizome clumps in fall or plant nursery transplants in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade. Yellow Mandarin slowly spreads by rhizome to form colonies in the moist woodland garden.
directions: Yellow Mandarin is an elegant native woodland perennial, native to rich, moist, deciduous forests of eastern North America from Ontario south to Georgia and Alabama, with arching stems bearing alternating, broadly oval, clasping leaves and pendant, creamy-yellow, bell-shaped flowers dangling from branch tips in April and May — an effect reminiscent of Solomon's seal but with larger, more distinctly yellow-green flowers. The small, red to orange berries that follow in late summer are attractive and eaten by some birds, adding late-season interest to the shaded garden. Yellow Mandarin spreads slowly by rhizome to form loose, graceful colonies and is one of the most architecturally pleasing of the native spring woodland wildflowers, providing strong vertical structure in layers under taller shade trees. Divide clumps in fall, or plant nursery transplants in spring in well-amended, consistently moist, humus-rich soil.

plant_note: Plant rooted cuttings or container vines in spring in moist to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; yellow passionflower is the most shade-tolerant of the native Passiflora species and naturalizes readily in shaded woodland edges and fence lines in zone 6–9.
directions: Plant in moist to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; yellow passionflower is the most shade-tolerant native Passiflora species and is far less aggressive than maypop (P. incarnata), making it more suitable for smaller gardens and shaded areas. The flowers are smaller than other passionflowers but share the same intricate, layered structure — creamy yellow-green petals and a distinctive corona of filaments — and are visited by long-tongued native bees for nectar. Like all native Passiflora, yellow passionflower is a host plant for the larvae of gulf fritillary, variegated fritillary, and zebra longwing butterflies. The small dark purple fruits are eaten by birds. It grows naturally along shaded woodland edges, thickets, and roadsides throughout the southeastern US and is one of the best native vines for adding butterfly-host value to a shaded fence or trellis.

plant_note: Plant nursery-grown transplants in spring in wet to saturated, acidic (pH 3.5–5.5), nutrient-poor peat or sphagnum bog medium in full sun; keep standing in a tray of pure rain water or distilled water 1–2 inches deep year-round — never fertilize and never use tap water.
directions: Yellow pitcher plant is one of the tallest and most spectacular native carnivorous plants of North America — producing elegant, yellow-green, trumpet-shaped pitchers up to 4 feet tall with distinctive reddish veining, topped by an arching, umbrella-like lid and a flaring collar that guides insects into the prey-filled, rain-protected trap. Insects are attracted by colorful nectar-like secretions around the pitcher mouth, slide down the waxy inner surface, and drown in the accumulated fluid at the base where bacteria and enzymes digest them; a single pitcher can trap dozens of insects per week. Large, drooping yellow flowers appear on separate stalks in April before the pitchers emerge fully, and have an unusual musty, cat-like scent that attracts early spring pollinators including queen bumblebees, solitary bees, and flies. Native to the wetland pine savannas of the Atlantic and Gulf Coastal Plain from Virginia to Florida, this plant is an outstanding garden specimen for acidic bog gardens in zones 6–9 — it requires no special care beyond pure water, wet conditions, and full sun, and will thrive for decades as a dramatic living sculpture in a properly maintained bog.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring, anchoring the thick horizontal rhizome in heavy clay or loamy pond-bottom soil at a depth of 1–4 feet of standing water. Yellow Pond Lily thrives in slow-moving streams, ponds, bayous, and blackwater swamps and should not be planted in fast-moving water.
directions: Yellow Pond Lily is a vigorous native floating-leaf aquatic plant, native to the slow ponds, sluggish streams, bayous, and blackwater swamps of eastern North America from New Brunswick to Florida and across the Gulf states to Texas, bearing large, heart-shaped floating leaves up to a foot across and distinctive globular yellow flowers on stout stems from spring through fall — the flowers are pollinated by beetles and tiny bees attracted to the scent and heat generated by the floral chamber. The thick, horizontal rhizomes anchor in muddy pond bottoms and can spread aggressively, making Yellow Pond Lily best suited to large natural ponds and water gardens where it can spread freely; its roots, seeds, and leaf bases are important food for wood ducks, beaver, and muskrats. Plant rhizomes in heavy clay soil at the pond bottom at a depth of 1–4 feet; once established, plants require no care and will spread naturally to cover the water surface, providing excellent shade that suppresses filamentous algae.

plant_note: Plant small corms (bulbs) 2–3 inches deep in fall; or sow fresh seed immediately after collection in late spring, as seed viability drops quickly. Corms are the practical starting method — bulbs begin blooming in 2–3 years vs. 4–7 from seed.
directions: Plant small corms 2–3 inches deep in moist, humus-rich soil beneath deciduous trees; yellow trout lily is among the most charming spring ephemerals of the eastern forest floor. The single mottled brown-and-green leaf (resembling a brook trout's back) appears in early spring, followed by a solitary nodding yellow lily flower on plants old enough to bloom. Young plants form non-blooming single-leaf colonies for 4–7 years before maturity. Plants go completely dormant by early June, leaving no trace; mark their location to avoid disturbance. The fleshy corms are edible and were eaten by Indigenous peoples, though harvesting from wild populations is unethical. Ideal naturalized en masse under deciduous trees to create a spectacular spring carpet.

plant_note: Direct sow seed in fall or scarify and sow in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy to loamy, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun to light shade; yellow wild indigo is slow to establish — it may take 3 years to bloom from seed — but is extremely long-lived and low-maintenance once established.
directions: Direct sow in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun — yellow wild indigo is the Gulf Coast and south-central species of Baptisia, producing upright spikes of clear, bright yellow pea-flowers in April–May on compact, mounded, blue-green foliage that remains attractive and deer-resistant all season after the flowers fade. It is native to the prairies and open woodlands of Texas, Louisiana, and Oklahoma, where it is a defining plant of the coastal prairie and post oak savanna flora. Like all false indigos, it develops a deep, extensive taproot that makes it highly drought tolerant and difficult to transplant once established — plant it in its permanent location from seed or small transplant and leave it undisturbed. The inflated, spherical seed pods are attractive in dried arrangements. Nitrogen-fixing; improves soil for neighboring plants. Slow to establish but essentially immortal once settled — clumps are known to persist and bloom for decades.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; this plant establishes quickly and spreads by rhizome to form a dense colony. Tolerates periodic flooding.
directions: Plant rhizome divisions in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained to moist soil in partial to full shade; yellowroot is a low, colony-forming native shrub of Appalachian stream banks and moist woodland slopes, grown for its ferny, compound foliage that provides attractive, fine-textured groundcover in shade, its drooping panicles of small maroon-purple flowers in early spring, and its outstanding orange-gold fall color. The roots and inner bark are vividly yellow from the alkaloid berberine (also found in goldenseal), historically used as a natural yellow dye and for medicinal purposes. As a groundcover, it spreads vigorously by rhizome to form weed-suppressing mats under trees, on moist slopes, and along shaded stream banks. One of the best native low-maintenance groundcovers for moist shade.

plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in partial shade to full sun; brunfelsia prefers bright, indirect light or morning sun with afternoon shade in the heat of zone 9. All parts of the plant are toxic to pets, particularly dogs — the alkaloid brunfelsamidine causes severe neurological symptoms.
directions: Plant in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial shade; yesterday-today-and-tomorrow takes its name from one of the most enchanting flower color-change sequences in horticulture: the fragrant, flat-faced flowers open deep violet-purple ('yesterday'), fade to lavender ('today'), and turn white ('tomorrow') before dropping — so at any given moment a single plant displays all three colors simultaneously, creating a remarkable polychrome effect. The sweetly fragrant flowers appear in spring and intermittently through summer and fall. This attractive, moderate-growing evergreen shrub performs best in the bright shade of a pergola or lath house where it is protected from scorching afternoon sun. A critical caution: all parts of the plant are highly toxic to dogs, causing severe neurological symptoms including tremors and seizures — it should not be planted where dogs can access the fruit or flowers. Prune lightly after the main spring bloom flush.

plant_note: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile soil in full sun; ylang-ylang grows very rapidly (6–15 feet per year when young) and produces the characteristic drooping, chandelier-like crown. It is frost sensitive and suited to outdoor culture in zone 10–12 only.
directions: Plant in moist, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; ylang-ylang is the source of one of the most important perfume ingredients in the world — the intensely fragrant yellow-green flowers with long, drooping petals are steam-distilled to produce the essential oil used in Chanel No. 5, Joy, and countless other perfumes. The scent is rich, heavy, and intensely sweet with notes of jasmine, narcissus, and banana. The tree grows remarkably fast — producing a graceful, weeping tropical specimen in just a few years — with pendulous branches and large, oval leaves. Flowers are produced year-round in tropical climates and are used fresh in garlands and as bedroom perfumers throughout Southeast Asia and the Pacific. Ylang-ylang is the national flower of the Philippines, where it is called 'poor man's perfume.' Only suited to outdoor culture in frost-free zone 10–12.

plant_note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun and well-drained soil.
directions: The Yoshino cherry is the classic flowering cherry famed for its spring canopy of fragrant, blush-white blossoms that open before the leaves emerge. Plant in full sun in fertile, well-drained soil, giving it plenty of room for its broad, spreading crown. Water regularly while young and mulch the root zone, keeping the mulch off the trunk. Prune sparingly and only just after bloom, since cherries are susceptible to disease through large wounds. It provides light shade in summer and golden-bronze foliage in fall, making it a beloved landscape specimen.

plant_note: Plant in fall or spring in partial shade with acidic, well-drained soil.
directions: Yuletide is a sasanqua camellia that blooms in late fall and early winter, opening bright single red flowers with showy golden stamens against glossy evergreen foliage. Plant in partial shade in acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil; sheltering it from harsh afternoon sun and drying winds protects the blooms. Keep the soil consistently moist but never soggy, and mulch to conserve moisture and maintain acidity. Prune lightly right after flowering to shape the shrub. Its winter color makes it a standout for foundation plantings, hedges, and holiday-season interest.

plant_note: Sow outdoors around the last spring frost; tolerates light frost and can also be sown in late summer for fall bloom.
directions: Direct sow seeds about a quarter inch deep once the soil is workable in spring, or start indoors a few weeks before the last frost. This cultivar produces bronzy-orange petals that fade to soft pink over a maroon-backed, yellow-centered flower. Deadhead regularly to keep the edible blooms coming and prevent early fade in summer heat. Petals are edible and make a colorful addition to salads or as a garnish. Calendula self-sows readily and thrives in cool weather.

plant_note: Plant divisions or container transplants in spring in well-drained, rocky to sandy, alkaline to slightly acidic, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun to partial shade; zexmenia is native to the rocky limestone hills and caliche prairies of central Texas and is extremely well adapted to alkaline, dry Gulf Coast soils.
directions: Plant in well-drained, rocky to loamy, alkaline to neutral, dry soil in full sun to light shade — zexmenia is a tough, sprawling Texas native perennial that produces cheerful, bright orange-yellow daisy-like flowers nearly continuously from spring through frost in zone 8–9, and virtually year-round in zone 10–11. The rough, hairy leaves and stems give it a slightly weedy texture that suits naturalistic plantings, wildlife gardens, and dry slopes; in borders it benefits from cutting back by one-half in late spring to keep it compact and floriferous. It spreads slowly by both rhizome and self-sowing to form a modest groundcover in dry conditions. Outstanding for wildlife: the flowers are rich in nectar for monarch butterflies, skippers, and native bees. Essentially zero-maintenance once established in well-drained soil — requires no supplemental irrigation in adapted alkaline or caliche soils. A mainstay of the low-water Texas Hill Country garden.

plant_note: Surface sow or plant divisions in spring; this is the only goldenrod species that thrives in shade and can be naturalized under trees where sun-loving goldenrods fail.
directions: Surface sow or plant divisions in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; unique among the goldenrods, this species naturally grows in the understory of deciduous eastern forests and tolerates deep shade that would kill other goldenrods. The common name refers to the distinctly zigzagging (flexicaulis) stem. Yellow flower clusters bloom in late summer to fall, providing late-season nectar in shaded areas where other late bloomers are absent. Spreads moderately by rhizome to form colonies but is far less aggressive than sun-loving goldenrods. Divide clumps in spring every 3–4 years.

plant_note: Sow after all danger of frost has passed and soil has warmed; zinnias resent cold, wet ground.
directions: Direct sow after the last frost about a quarter inch deep, or start indoors a few weeks early for an earlier show. This scabiosa-flowered blend yields 2 to 2.5 inch double, semi-double, and occasional single blooms in soft pastel shades where no two flowers are alike. Pinch young plants once to encourage branching and more stems for cutting. Cut or deadhead frequently to keep the plants productive all season. Provide good air circulation to reduce powdery mildew.

plant_note: Direct sow after last frost when soil temperature is above 65°F; also easy to start indoors 4–6 weeks early.
directions: Sow seed 1/4 in deep directly in the garden once frost risk is past and soil is warm. Pinch seedlings at 6 in tall to promote branching and a fuller plant with more stems for cutting. Cut flowers frequently to the next set of leaves to stimulate continuous bloom through summer. Avoid overhead watering to reduce powdery mildew; water at the base.

plant_note: Sow directly after the last frost; successive sowings every couple of weeks extend the bloom season.
directions: Direct sow about one inch deep after the last frost once soil has warmed. This branching, pollenless-style florist sunflower blooms roughly two months from sowing, bearing golden-yellow 4 to 6 inch flowers on tall, sturdy stems ideal for bouquets. Space plants closer for single-stem cut flowers or wider to encourage branching and more blooms. Its long vase life makes it a favorite for the backyard florist. Stake in windy sites to keep the tall stems upright.

plant_note: In zone 8–10, plant in fall for a winter-spring bloom season; zonal geraniums perform best in cool weather (50–70°F) and decline or stop flowering in summer heat above 85°F. In zone 9a, fall-through-spring is the preferred season. Start cuttings in late summer to have blooming plants ready for fall planting.
directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; despite being called 'geraniums,' these plants are Pelargonium — South African natives in the family Geraniaceae. They are among the most popular and widely grown bedding plants in the world, producing heads of bright red, pink, salmon, white, or bicolor flowers on compact mounds of velvety, horseshoe-zoned foliage. In zone 9a, where summers are too hot for them to thrive, they are grown as cool-season annuals from fall through spring — planted in October and blooming through March or April before summer heat causes decline. Take cuttings in late summer before heat ends the season, root them indoors or in shade, and use them as next fall's transplants. Excellent for containers, window boxes, and cool-season borders.

plant_note: Start indoors about 6 weeks before the last frost, then transplant out once frost danger has passed.
directions: Start seeds indoors about six weeks before the last frost and transplant after the weather warms, or direct sow in warm regions. Also called Monarch of the Veldt, this heat- and drought-tolerant South African annual produces 3 to 4 inch daisy-like flowers above silver-tinged foliage from midsummer to fall. Grow in full sun and well-drained soil, and avoid overwatering as it prefers drier conditions. The blooms attract bees, birds, and butterflies. Flowers close at night and on cloudy days.

plant_note: Plant grafted trees in spring; abiu is cold-sensitive and requires a frost-free year-round climate.
directions: Plant in deep, fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun; abiu is closely related to sapodilla and canistel and shares their preference for warm, humid conditions with regular moisture. Trees are self-fertile and heavy bearers once established; grafted selections begin fruiting in 2–4 years versus 7+ for seedlings. The smooth golden-yellow skin of ripe fruit conceals a translucent, gelatinous, caramel-flavored flesh — eat by cutting in half and scooping; avoid getting the sticky latex near the lips if the fruit is slightly underripe. Harvest when the skin has turned fully golden-yellow and the fruit yields gently to thumb pressure, typically in late summer through fall.

plant_note: Plant container-grown or grafted stock in spring after all frost danger; in zone 9 site against a south-facing wall for extra warmth.
directions: Acerola produces cherry-like fruits with one of the highest natural concentrations of vitamin C of any food plant — up to 65 times that of an orange. The shrubby tree is self-fertile and can bear multiple times per year in truly frost-free climates; it is very sensitive to cold below 30°F and root systems are damaged below 28°F. Plant in well-drained, slightly acid to neutral soil and fertilize lightly with a balanced fertilizer three times per year. Fruit ripens very quickly from green to red and is extremely perishable — harvest when fully red and process (freeze, juice) within 24–48 hours for maximum vitamin C retention.

plant_note: Plant in the warm, wet tropics; strictly frost-free.
directions: The African mango, or bush mango, is a large tropical tree grown for its mango-like fruit and especially its oil-rich seed kernel, called dika nut. It demands a hot, humid, frost-free climate with abundant moisture and deep, fertile soil. Plant in full sun with plenty of room, as mature trees become tall and broad. Trees are slow to bear from seed, so grafted stock fruits sooner; harvest the fallen fruit and dry the prized kernels.

plant_note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter before bud break; almonds bloom very early and need a frost-free spring.
directions: Almonds bloom earlier than almost any other tree fruit and are severely damaged by late spring frosts, which is why commercial production is limited to Mediterranean-type climates. Most varieties require a cross-pollinator — plant at least two compatible cultivars (Non-Pareil with Carmel or Price, for example). Trees thrive in well-drained, alkaline to neutral soils and are drought-tolerant once established. Harvest when the hulls split open and the shells dry and harden, typically in late summer; spread nuts to dry for two weeks before storing.

common_name: Ambarella (June Plum, Golden Apple, Otaheite Apple)
plant_note: Plant air-layered or grafted trees in spring in very well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; ambarella grows quickly and requires protection from frost — only suited for frost-free zone 10–11 gardens or large containers moved indoors in winter.
directions: Plant in very well-drained, fertile soil in full sun; ambarella is a fast-growing tropical fruit tree native to Melanesia and widely cultivated throughout the Pacific islands, Caribbean, and Southeast Asia for its versatile fruit. The oval, golden-yellow to orange fruits are eaten at all stages: the crisp, acidic green fruit is popular in Southeast Asian salads, slaws, and pickles with a tart mango-apple flavor, while fully ripe fruits are sweeter and eaten fresh. The fibrous pit germinates reliably in a few weeks. Ambarella is one of the faster-fruiting tropical trees, often bearing within 2–3 years from air-layers. It drops leaves briefly in dry season. In zone 9a it grows in a large container (25+ gallon) and must be moved indoors or into a greenhouse before the first frost; in zone 10+ it is an outstanding productive landscape tree.

plant_note: Plant bare-root or container-grown trees in early spring in rich, moist, well-drained, acidic soil.
directions: American beech is monoecious and wind-pollinated, but nut production is highly variable and irregular — large mast crops occur every 2–5 years, driven by weather patterns; at least two trees (ideally a grove) dramatically improves mast yield in any given year. The triangular beechnuts are sweet and nutritious, high in fat and protein, and valued by both wildlife and people; they are best eaten raw or lightly roasted and are traditionally used for oil extraction. Beech is extremely sensitive to soil compaction and grade changes around the root zone — construction damage is the primary killer of mature trees in urban settings. Plant in deep, moist, well-drained, acidic soil away from construction zones and plan for very long-term production: significant nut crops typically begin 10+ years after planting.

plant_note: Plant fresh nuts or container-grown seedlings in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic, sandy to loamy, deep soil in full sun; American chestnut requires two or more trees for cross-pollination to set nuts. Consider blight-resistant hybrid trees from the American Chestnut Foundation for greater long-term survival.
directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic, sandy to loamy, deep soil in full sun — the American chestnut was once the dominant tree of the eastern forest, comprising 25% of all trees from New England to the Deep South and providing the primary mast crop sustaining deer, turkeys, bears, and passenger pigeons across millions of acres. The introduction of chestnut blight (Cryphonectria parasitica) from Asia in the early 1900s killed an estimated 4 billion trees within 50 years in the most catastrophic ecological disaster in American history. The root system of most trees survives and sends up shoots that grow to 10–20 feet before dying of blight — these sprouts can produce a small crop of nuts. The American Chestnut Foundation has developed blight-tolerant hybrid trees (about 94% American chestnut genetics) and restoration planting is actively underway. Planting American chestnut seedlings or blight-resistant hybrids is a meaningful conservation act; the trees grow fast, produce sweet nuts, and support 125 species of caterpillars.

plant_note: Plant bare-root or container-grown shrubs in spring; plant at least two different clones (not two of the same named cultivar) for good pollination and maximum fruit set.
directions: Plant in moist, fertile, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; American elderberry is one of the most productive native fruiting shrubs, bearing enormous flat-topped flower clusters in early summer that are irresistible to pollinators, followed by drooping clusters of small dark purple-black berries in late summer used for syrups, jellies, wine, and immune-support preparations. Plant at least two genetically distinct clones for better cross-pollination and heavier fruit set. The shrub spreads by root suckers to form large thickets; remove suckers promptly to control spread. All parts of the plant except fully ripe, cooked berries and flowers should be considered toxic when consumed raw. Cut all canes to 12 inches in late winter every 3–4 years to rejuvenate; older canes are pithy and produce poor crops.

plant_note: Plant bare-root or container shrubs in early spring before leafout; two or more plants are needed for cross-pollination and nut production.
directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; this native thicket-forming shrub tolerates a wider range of conditions than European hazelnut and is resistant to Eastern filbert blight. Plant at least two different seedling-grown plants together, as this species is self-incompatible and requires cross-pollination for nut set. Nuts ripen in September–October, enclosed in leafy husks; harvest when the husks begin to loosen and dry nuts in a single layer before storing. The multi-stemmed habit can be managed by removing the oldest canes at the base every few years.

plant_note: Plant bare-root or container-grown shrubs in spring; cross-pollination is required for nut production — plant at least two genetically distinct individuals within 20 feet. Nuts from stratified seed can be sown in fall for spring germination.
directions: Plant in well-drained to average moist soil in full sun to partial shade; American hazelnut is a native multi-stemmed shrub that produces small, sweet hazelnuts enclosed in fringed husks in late summer — identical in flavor to the European filbert but better adapted to eastern North American conditions. The catkins (male flowers) are showy in late winter before any leaves emerge, providing early nectar for bees. Leaves turn yellow-orange in fall. Squirrels are formidable competitors for the nuts — harvest when the husks begin to turn brown and bring indoors to cure. Eastern filbert blight, a fungal disease, is a concern in the east; choose blight-resistant cultivars ('Winkler', 'Jefferson') or the more resistant hybrid hazelnut crosses if available in your region. Plants sucker freely; control suckers to maintain a clean shrub form.

plant_note: Plant in early spring; persimmons have a deep taproot so handle with care and disturb roots as little as possible.
directions: American persimmon is dioecious — male and female flowers occur on separate trees — so plant at least one of each for reliable fruiting, though some populations produce fruit without pollination. It is exceptionally cold-hardy and tolerant of poor soils, making it one of the easiest native fruit trees. Fruit is intensely astringent until fully ripe and soft, usually after the first hard freeze; pick after the flesh has turned translucent orange and jelly-soft. Little pruning is needed beyond removing dead or crossing wood.

plant_note: Plant bare-root or container trees in spring; American persimmon has a deep taproot and transplants poorly — use young container-grown trees and minimize root disturbance. Most trees are dioecious (separate male and female plants); plant one male per 8–12 female trees for reliable fruiting.
directions: Plant in deep, well-drained to average soil in full sun to partial shade; American persimmon is a tough native tree with striking blocky, alligator-hide bark and excellent fall color that produces small, intensely sweet, apricot-flavored fruits after the first hard frost when they become fully ripe and astringency disappears. The fruits are outstanding for puddings, breads, and fresh eating and are highly attractive to deer, foxes, opossums, and birds. Trees are quite drought-tolerant once established and will grow in poor, rocky, or clay soil where few fruit trees survive. Named female cultivars (Meader, Szukis, Yates, Pieper) with improved fruit size are grafted onto seedling rootstock — grow these for reliable, large-fruited production. Allow fruits to fall naturally after frost and collect from the ground before wildlife does.

plant_note: Plant bare-root trees in early spring before bud break; plant two or more for cross-pollination, as single trees produce little fruit.
directions: Plant in well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; this adaptable native shrub-tree thrives on poor, dry soils and slopes where improved fruit trees fail, and its thicket-forming habit makes it ideal for erosion control and wildlife habitat plantings. The fragrant white flowers bloom in early spring before the leaves emerge, providing important early pollinator forage. The small red to yellow plums ripen in late summer and are excellent for jams, jellies, wine, and fresh eating; tannin content decreases when fully ripe. American plum spreads by root suckers to form thickets — remove suckers regularly if a tree form is desired, or allow colonization in a naturalized area.

plant_note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter to early spring while still dormant, before buds break.
directions: Plant bare-root stock in well-drained soil, spreading roots carefully; stake dwarf trees for the first two seasons. Most varieties require a second cultivar nearby for cross-pollination — check chill-hour requirements to match your climate (600–1200 hours for standard types, under 400 for low-chill selections). Prune annually in late dormancy to an open-center or central-leader form to improve light penetration and air flow. Harvest when fruit separates with a gentle upward twist and seeds have turned brown, 2–5 years after planting.

plant_note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter, as early as the soil can be worked, to allow root establishment before bloom.
directions: Most apricot varieties are self-fertile, which simplifies home-orchard planting, but two trees will increase yields. The key limiting factor in temperate climates is the tree's early bloom time — a hard frost after bud break will eliminate the crop, so choose a sheltered north-facing or slightly elevated site to delay bloom, or select late-blooming cultivars. Prune in early summer rather than winter to reduce risk of cytospora canker infection. Harvest when the fruit is fully orange, fragrant, and gives slightly to the touch.

plant_note: Set out dormant bare-root trees in early spring.
directions: An aprium is an apricot-dominant hybrid with the sweetness of apricot and a hint of plum, often bearing a faint fuzz. Plant in full sun on light, well-drained soil in a frost-protected spot, since apricot-type blooms open very early. Many apriums are self-fruitful but crop more heavily with an apricot pollinator nearby. Prune in dry summer weather to limit disease and thin the fruit for good size.

plant_note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter to early spring; in mild-winter zones plant in fall.
directions: Asian pears ripen on the tree — unlike European pears they do not need post-harvest ripening — so taste-test for sweetness before picking. Most varieties are self-unfruitful and yield best with a second Asian pear or even a compatible European variety nearby. Chill requirements are moderate (450–700 hours), making many selections suitable for zones 5–9. Annual thinning of fruit clusters to one per spur prevents limb breakage and encourages larger, better-flavored fruit.

plant_note: Plant in spring after frost danger has passed; fall planting is fine in zones 8–10.
directions: Asian persimmons come in astringent types (such as Hachiya, best eaten when jelly-soft) and non-astringent types (such as Fuyu, eaten firm like an apple). Most cultivars are self-fertile and produce well as single trees. Trees need very little pruning — maintain a central leader and remove inward-growing branches to open the canopy. In cooler parts of zone 7, choose hardier varieties and site against a south-facing wall; in zones 9–10, low-chill cultivars perform best.

plant_note: Plant grafted container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; atemoya requires hand pollination for reliable fruit set as natural pollinators (nitidulid beetles native to South America) are absent in most US gardens. Hardy to about 28°F when established.
directions: Plant grafted trees in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun in zone 9–11; atemoya is a hybrid between the cherimoya (Annona cherimola) and the sugar apple (Annona squamosa), combining the better qualities of both parents — the large size and richly flavored flesh of cherimoya with the tropical heat tolerance of sugar apple. Atemoya is widely considered one of the finest-flavored fruits in the world, with a rich, creamy white flesh tasting of vanilla custard with notes of pineapple and coconut. It requires hand pollination in most US gardens — transfer pollen from flowers in the male stage to flowers in the female stage in the morning using a small brush. Plant grafted trees for faster fruiting (typically 2–3 years) rather than seedlings. Popular cultivars include 'Gefner' (nearly self-fruitful) and 'African Pride'. An outstanding subtropical fruit for South Florida and Gulf Coast gardens where winter temperatures stay above 28°F.

plant_note: Grown from a grafted or potted tree; move outdoors after frost.
directions: The Australian finger lime bears elongated fruit packed with juicy, caviar-like vesicles that burst with tart citrus flavor, making it a chef's favorite. Native to rugged coastal Australia, this compact, thorny citrus is tough and adapts well to container culture. Grow from a potted tree in full sun with rich, well-drained soil, and protect it from frost by moving it indoors in cold climates. Feed with a citrus fertilizer during the growing season and water steadily without letting roots sit wet.

plant_note: Plant in spring when all frost risk is past and soil is warm; avoid planting in summer heat.
directions: Avocados bear Type A flowers (open as female in the morning) and Type B flowers (open as female in the afternoon); planting one of each type near each other ensures pollen is available throughout the day and greatly increases fruit set. Grafted trees begin fruiting in 3–5 years; seedlings may take 8–10 years. Avocados are sensitive to root rot in heavy or poorly drained soils — plant on a mound or raised bed and irrigate deeply but infrequently. Fruit does not ripen on the tree; pick when full-sized and let ripen at room temperature for 3–8 days.

plant_note: Plant in spring after cold weather passes; bael tolerates light frost once established but does best in warm subtropical to tropical climates.
directions: Bael is a spiny citrus relative (family Rutaceae) highly revered in South Asian horticulture and medicine that tolerates drought, alkaline soils, and brief frosts to about 25°F once the trunk is mature — among the hardiest of the true tropical fruit trees. Trees are self-fertile and pollinated by insects; a single specimen fruits reliably. The hard-shelled, woody fruit takes 9–12 months to ripen on the tree; harvest when the skin turns from green to yellowish-green and the fruit sounds hollow when tapped. The sticky, aromatic flesh is scooped out and eaten fresh with sugar, blended into a popular cooling drink (bael sherbet), or dried; dried slices brew into a medicinal tisane with mild anti-diarrheal properties.

plant_note: Plant offshoots ('pups') with attached roots in rich, moist, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; each stem fruits once then dies, but the clump produces a continuous succession of pups. Plant in a protected spot sheltered from strong winds, which shred the large leaves.
directions: Plant pups in rich, moist, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; banana is the most-consumed fruit on earth and one of the most productive food plants for Gulf Coast zone 8–11 gardens, where established clumps produce a nearly continuous succession of hands. Each pseudostem flowers once and fruits once, then should be cut to the ground after harvest, allowing the pups already pushing up around it to take over. In zone 8–9 the top growth dies back in hard freezes but established root mats resprout reliably in spring; heavy mulching protects the rhizomes. Popular zone-8-hardy varieties include 'Orinoco,' 'Lady Finger,' 'Dwarf Cavendish,' and the ultra-cold-hardy ornamental/edible 'Basjoo' hybrid. The large, exotic leaves make banana a spectacular tropical accent plant even in years when the winter prevents fruiting.

plant_note: Plant rooted cuttings or container-grown plants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; Barbados cherry is one of the easiest tropical fruits for zone 9–11 and fruits multiple times per year in warm climates. It is sensitive to freezing and needs protection below 28°F.
directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; Barbados cherry (acerola) is one of the richest sources of vitamin C of any plant on Earth — the small red fruits contain 65 times more vitamin C per 100g than an orange, making even a few fruits a valuable daily supplement. The plant is a dense, attractive evergreen shrub with small, shiny leaves and clusters of small pink flowers that appear 3–4 times per year in zone 9–11, followed quickly by ripening cherry-like fruits. Berries ripen to bright red in 25 days after flowering and must be harvested immediately — they are extremely perishable at room temperature. Use fresh, blended, or frozen immediately after harvest. The shrub makes an excellent fruiting hedge in frost-free gardens; in zone 9a it is best grown in a container or in a protected south-facing microclimate.

plant_note: Root stem cuttings easily in well-drained, sandy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Barbados gooseberry is a primitive (leafy) cactus that grows as a woody climbing vine and can be trained on a fence or trellis. It is frost tender and suited to zone 9b–12 in the ground, or grown as a container plant.
directions: Root cuttings in sandy, well-drained soil in full sun; Barbados gooseberry is one of the most botanically fascinating of all edible plants — a primitive cactus (family Cactaceae) that retains true persistent leaves unlike all other cacti, making it a living link between leafy plants and the succulent cacti. The woody, thorny scrambling vine can reach 30 feet when climbing, with oval, slightly succulent leaves and masses of fragrant white to pale pink flowers in fall. The yellow to orange fruits ripen to 0.75 inches with multiple seeds embedded in pulp with a pleasant acid-sweet, citrus-like flavor — used fresh, in jams, and as a vegetable cooked with rice in the Caribbean. The young leaves are edible as a potherb. In South America the leaves are consumed as 'ora-pro-nobis,' valued for their exceptionally high protein content (25% dry weight). An unusual and productive edible vine for frost-free gardens.

plant_note: Plant container-grown trees in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; bignay is dioecious — both male and female trees are needed for fruit; female trees produce large, pendulous clusters of berries. Hardy to about 28°F in zone 9b–12.
directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade — bignay is a medium-sized tropical tree of Southeast Asia and the Pacific, producing spectacular long (12–18 inch) pendulous clusters of small (pea-sized) berries that ripen from green through red to deep purple-black in sequence on the same cluster, creating a striking multi-colored effect. The berries have a pleasant tart-sweet flavor with some astringency and are eaten fresh, made into wine, juice, and jam; in the Philippines bignay wine is a traditional fermented beverage. The tree is dioecious and shade-tolerant, growing naturally as an understory tree in mixed tropical forests. The polished, reddish-brown seeds (resembling small nutmegs) are made into decorative necklaces in the Philippines. An outstanding ornamental fruiting tree for zone 9b–12 gardens offering shade tolerance and dramatic fruiting clusters.

plant_note: Plant in spring in a frost-free site; bilimbi is more cold-sensitive than its relative starfruit.
directions: Bilimbi is a cauliflorous tree — clusters of small red flowers and the resulting green, cucumber-shaped fruits emerge directly from the trunk and main branches — creating a striking ornamental effect. It is self-fertile and prolific, producing nearly year-round in warm climates with adequate moisture. The fruit is extremely acidic and rarely eaten raw; it is prized as a souring agent in Southeast Asian and Caribbean cuisines, used as a marinade tenderizer, and made into preserves, chutneys, and cooling drinks. Harvest the pale green to yellow-green fruits while still firm for culinary use; fully ripe fruits become soft and ferment quickly on the tree.

plant_note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter or container trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic, moderately fertile soil; plant two or more trees for better fruit set. Seeds require cold stratification for 90 days.
directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic, moderately fertile soil in partial shade to full sun; black cherry is arguably the most ecologically important fruiting tree in eastern North America, supporting the larvae of over 400 species of moths and butterflies (more than almost any other tree genus), providing early-spring nectar in its white flower racemes, and producing thousands of small black cherries in summer that are consumed by over 70 species of birds — robins, waxwings, thrushes, catbirds, woodpeckers — as well as bears, foxes, and raccoons. The small black cherries are edible and intensely flavorful, traditionally used for cherry wine, jelly, and syrups. The wilted leaves and seeds contain prussic acid and are toxic to livestock. The bark has traditional medicinal use as a cough suppressant. A fast-growing native tree (2–3 feet per year) that can seed prolifically.

plant_note: Plant in spring after frost danger passes in a warm, sheltered position.
directions: The black mulberry is a slow-growing, long-lived tree bearing intensely flavored dark fruit far superior to that of white or red mulberries. Plant in full sun on fertile, well-drained soil in a spot sheltered from cold winds. It is self-fertile, so one tree crops well, though the staining fruit is best sited away from paving. Prune only lightly in winter, as mulberries bleed heavily from large cuts.

plant_note: Plant in spring after all frost risk has passed; young trees are cold-sensitive and need protection until the trunk is at least 2 inches in diameter.
directions: Black sapote is a relative of the Asian persimmon; when ripe its flesh turns chocolate-brown and has a rich, pudding-like flavor often described as chocolate mousse. Trees are dioecious or polygamous — plant at least two seedlings or a grafted female with a male nearby for reliable fruiting. They prefer well-drained, fertile soils with a pH near 6.5 and are sensitive to frost, waterlogging, and salt winds. Harvest when the skin turns from bright olive-green to olive-yellow-green and the fruit gives to very gentle pressure; leave at room temperature 5–7 days until the inside is completely dark and custardy.

plant_note: Plant bare-root or container stock in early spring; site carefully as the tree grows very large and produces juglone.
directions: Black walnut is a native North American species grown primarily for its richly flavored nuts and valuable timber. It produces high concentrations of juglone in its roots, leaves, and hulls, which is toxic to many garden plants including tomatoes, peppers, apples, and blueberries; maintain a 50–80 foot buffer from sensitive plants. Trees are monoecious and partially self-fertile but cross well with a second tree. Hulls stain skin and clothing an indelible dark brown; wear gloves when husking. Nuts are ready when hulls are yellow-green to black and dent when pressed; cure for 2–3 weeks after husking.

plant_note: Plant in spring after frost risk has passed; blood oranges require cool winters for anthocyanin color development — they color best in climates with cold nights during ripening.
directions: Blood orange cultivars (Moro, Tarocco, Sanguinello) develop their signature ruby-red flesh and juice through the accumulation of anthocyanins, a pigment triggered by cool nights during fruit development — temperatures between 40 and 50°F for several weeks from late autumn through midwinter produce the deepest coloring. In frost-free tropical climates the flesh remains orange with little anthocyanin. Trees are self-fertile, medium-sized, and managed identically to other sweet oranges: plant in well-drained soil, fertilize with a citrus blend three times per year, and irrigate deeply but infrequently. Harvest from late winter through early spring when the flesh has developed full color and the fruit is sweet.

plant_note: Plant the dormant or potted dwarf tree in spring; grown from a grafted tree, not seed.
directions: Bonanza is a genetic dwarf peach that fits easily on a patio yet produces full-size, sweet, rosy fruit. Its naturally compact habit and showy pink spring blossoms make it as ornamental as it is productive, ideal for containers and small yards. Plant a grafted tree in full sun with rich, well-drained soil, and provide a large pot or a spot with room for the roots. Thin the young fruit for better size, prune in late winter to shape, and water consistently while fruit is developing.

plant_note: Plant the dwarf ornamental peach in spring; grown from a grafted tree.
directions: Bonfire is a compact ornamental peach grown chiefly for its striking deep-crimson foliage and pink spring flowers, though it can set small edible fruit. Its fiery color and dwarf size make it a dramatic focal point for patios and small gardens. Plant a grafted tree in full sun with well-drained soil in a large container or bed. Prune after flowering to maintain its tidy form, water regularly during the growing season, and expect the best leaf color in full sun.

plant_note: Plant root-shoot suckers or grafted trees in spring; breadfruit requires a fully frost-free year-round climate.
directions: Propagate from root shoots or cuttings rather than seed, as most cultivated varieties are seedless and parthenocarpic — no pollination is required for fruit set. Trees prefer deep, fertile, well-drained soil and consistent moisture; they tolerate brief drought once mature but perform best with regular irrigation. A single mature tree can produce 200–800 fruits per year; thin clusters to 2–3 fruit per stem to improve size. Harvest when the skin transitions from bright green to yellowish-green with slight latex seeping from surface cracks, indicating starch has developed but the flesh has not yet become overripe and sticky.

plant_note: Grown from a grafted or air-layered tree; move outdoors after frost.
directions: The Brewster lychee is a classic favorite bearing sweet, juicy fruit encased in a glossy red shell over fragrant white flesh. This subtropical evergreen prefers hot, humid summers and needs a brief cool period to trigger flowering. Grow a grafted or air-layered tree in full sun with acidic, well-drained soil rich in organic matter, and shelter it fully from frost. Keep the root zone consistently moist, mulch heavily, and protect young trees from wind while they establish.

plant_note: Plant in spring in a frost-free or nearly frost-free location.
directions: The bronze loquat is grown both for its striking coppery new foliage and small, tart edible fruit. Plant in full sun on fertile, well-drained soil in a warm, sheltered position, as the flowers and young growth are frost-sensitive. Water regularly while establishing, then only during dry spells once mature. Prune after fruiting to shape the canopy and remove any frost-damaged tips.

plant_note: Plant in spring after last frost; Brown Turkey establishes quickly and often produces the first small crop in year two. Choose a site with full sun and excellent drainage — the roots do not tolerate standing water.
directions: Plant in well-drained, average soil in full sun; Brown Turkey is among the most widely grown figs in the South and UK, valued for its large, mild, brownish-purple fruits and its exceptional vigor. It is a two-crop fig, producing a small breba crop in June on old wood and a main crop in late summer on new wood. The tree grows vigorously and can reach 15–20 feet unpruned — maintain at 8–10 feet by annual tipping of tall canes in winter. The large, deeply lobed leaves provide striking tropical texture in the landscape. Brown Turkey is more tolerant of cool summers than most figs and produces reliably even when summer heat is limited, making it popular well north of its zone rating when given wall protection.

plant_note: Plant after the last hard frost; in colder zones grow in a container that can be moved to shelter.
directions: This cold-hardy fig tolerates temperatures near 10 degrees F, making it a good choice for cooler regions where most figs struggle. Plant in full sun with rich, well-drained soil and provide consistent moisture while fruit is developing for deep burgundy fruit with a pink center. In borderline zones, mulch heavily around the base or grow in a pot to overwinter in a protected spot. Prune in late winter to keep the tree at a manageable height and encourage a strong branching structure.

plant_note: Plant bare-root or container trees in early spring; pair with another Japanese or American plum for cross-pollination.
directions: This cold-hardy hybrid plum produces juicy reddish-purple fruit with a distinctive sweet, spicy flavor often compared to bubblegum. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil, and include a compatible pollinizer nearby since Toka also serves as an excellent pollinator for other plums. Water consistently during fruit set and thin heavy fruit clusters to improve size and prevent limb breakage. Prune in late winter to an open-center form for good light penetration and air flow.

plant_note: Plant in spring after frost risk passes; Buddha's hand is very cold-sensitive and ideal for container culture.
directions: Buddha's hand is a citron variety bearing distinctive finger-like lobes that contain only aromatic peel with no juice or pulp; it is valued entirely for its intensely fragrant zest used in baking, cocktails, and as a natural air freshener. Trees are self-fertile and small enough to grow in 15–20 gallon containers, making them suitable for patios and sunrooms in colder climates; bring indoors before temperatures fall below 32°F. Fertilize with a citrus-specific fertilizer three times per year and maintain consistent moisture to prevent fruit drop. Harvest when the fingers have turned fully yellow and the fragrance is intense; fruit can be stored at room temperature for 1–2 weeks.

plant_note: Plant bare-root or container stock in early spring; choose disease-resistant hybrid selections due to butternut canker pressure on the native species.
