Harvest the thick, inulin-rich roots in autumn of the second or third year, washing and slicing them before drying for respiratory-supportive preparations. The yellow daisy-like flowers appear in midsummer of the second year and are attractive to pollinators.

plant_note: Sow around the last frost; cold stratification improves germination of this tall perennial.
directions: Elecampane is a bold perennial reaching several feet tall, with sunny yellow daisy-like flowers and aromatic roots used in traditional herbalism and natural dyes. Sow seed after cold stratification into moist, fertile soil in full sun to part shade. Give it plenty of space, as mature clumps grow large and stately. Roots are typically dug in fall of the second or third year for herbal use.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger has fully passed into well-drained, alkaline soil.
directions: Surface-sow seeds and keep barely moist; germination is slow and uneven, so starting from cuttings or divisions is often more reliable. Plant in fast-draining, lean soil — rich or wet conditions promote rot and weak growth. Prune plants back by about one-third after the first flush of blooms to keep growth compact and encourage a second flowering. Harvest flower spikes just as the buds begin to open, bundling and hanging upside down in a warm, airy space to dry for sachets, culinary use, and aromatherapy.

plant_note: Start indoors early after cold stratification; seedlings are slow, so give them a long head start.
directions: English lavender is a fragrant, hardy perennial with silvery foliage and purple flower spikes prized for their sweet aroma and pollinator appeal. Seed germinates slowly and benefits from cold stratification, so start indoors well ahead of transplanting. Grow in lean, sharply drained soil in full sun and avoid overwatering. Prune lightly after bloom to maintain a compact mound and prolong the plant's life.

plant_note: Seed needs light and cold stratification for best germination; set out after frost danger passes.
directions: This tall, old-fashioned English lavender is intensely fragrant and prized for sachets, potpourri, and cut or dried arrangements. Start seed indoors in a light, well-drained mix, as germination is slow and benefits from a few weeks of cold stratification before sowing. Give plants full sun and sharp drainage; lavender resents wet feet and thrives in lean, alkaline soil. Space transplants well for air circulation and shear lightly after bloom to keep plants compact and productive.

plant_note: Surface-sow tiny seed and keep evenly moist until seedlings establish.
directions: English thyme offers a subtle, woodsy flavor that suits a wide range of dishes and is a key component of bouquet garni. Start seed indoors and barely cover, since the seed is fine and needs some light to sprout; transplant to a sunny, well-drained bed after frost. This drought-tolerant perennial forms a low, spreading mat with small leaves and edible flowers that draw pollinators. Harvest sprigs regularly to encourage bushy growth and avoid overwatering, which can rot the crown.

plant_note: Direct sow at or just after last frost once soil has warmed; epazote self-seeds prolifically once established.
directions: Surface-sow the tiny seeds by pressing them gently into soil without covering, as they require light for germination; thin to 12–18 in. Epazote is remarkably drought-tolerant once established and thrives in lean, well-drained soil; rich, moist conditions encourage excessive growth. Deadhead regularly to control self-seeding, which can be invasive in warm climates. Harvest young stem tips throughout the season; the pungent, resinous flavor mellows somewhat with cooking.

plant_note: Warmth-loving; do not set out until nights stay above 50 F.
directions: This columnar basil grows into an upright herbal tower of deep, glossy emerald leaves, making it as ornamental as it is useful. It delays flowering up to twelve weeks longer than standard types, so plants keep producing tender leaves without the flavor decline that comes with bolting. Start seed indoors in warmth and transplant only after the soil has thoroughly warmed. Its compact, non-branching habit suits patio pots and small beds; harvest leaves often and provide steady moisture and full sun.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost; can also sow in late summer for a fall crop.
directions: Soak seeds overnight to speed germination, then sow 1/2 in deep in rows and thin to 6 in apart; fenugreek prefers cool conditions and will bolt quickly in summer heat. Grow in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun. Days to maturity listed is for young leaf harvest; allow 3–5 months for seed pods to develop and dry if growing for seed spice. Harvest leaves at any time for use as a slightly bitter herb, and collect seed pods once they turn tan and feel firm.

plant_note: Direct sow in warm soil; succession sow for a steady supply of greens.
directions: This dual-purpose heirloom herb, also called methi, is grown for both its aromatic leafy greens and its spice seeds. Direct sow seed a quarter inch deep in warm, well-drained soil, since this legume resents transplanting. Harvest young leaves within about a month for cooking, or leave plants to flower and set the small pods. For seed, let pods dry on the plant, then collect and store the golden seeds for culinary and traditional herbal use.

plant_note: Sprout indoors year-round in a jar or tray; no garden bed needed.
directions: Fenugreek sprouts are easy to grow indoors and rich in vitamins A, B6, and C along with iron and potassium. Rinse seeds and soak overnight, then drain and rinse two to three times daily in a sprouting jar or tray. Keep them at room temperature out of direct sun and harvest in about four to seven days when small shoots appear. Enjoy the mildly bitter, nutty sprouts fresh in salads and sandwiches. Refrigerate after harvest to keep them crisp.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; press seeds onto the surface of the mix as they need light to germinate.
directions: Seeds are tiny and require light for germination — press them gently onto moist starting mix without covering. Once established, plants are quite drought-tolerant and spread readily by self-seeding. Shear plants back by half after flowering to maintain a tidy mound and encourage fresh foliage. The leaves are the medicinal part; harvest fresh leaves for tea or tincture — wear gloves as some people experience skin irritation from the foliage.

plant_note: Surface sow around the last frost; seed needs light to germinate.
directions: Feverfew is a cheerful short-lived perennial producing masses of small white daisy-like flowers with yellow centers above aromatic foliage. Surface sow the seed and press it into the soil, since light aids germination. It grows easily in full sun to part shade and self-sows freely once established. Deadhead to prolong bloom and lift some volunteer seedlings each spring to keep a fresh stand.

plant_note: Cold-sensitive; wait for warm soil.
directions: Sow indoors 4-6 weeks before the last frost, barely covering the seed and keeping flats around 75F. This tiny-leaved Italian basil naturally forms a dense round bush without much pinching. Transplant after all danger of frost into warm, fertile soil in full sun. Water evenly and harvest by clipping whole stems just above a leaf pair to keep growth bushy. Remove flower buds promptly to extend the productive leafy season.

plant_note: Also called Florence fennel; direct sow for the bulb, avoid transplant shock that triggers bolting.
directions: Florence fennel forms a swollen, anise-flavored bulb that can be eaten raw, sauteed, or added to soups and pasta, while its feathery leaves garnish fish. Direct sow into rich, moist soil since transplanting can stress plants and cause premature bolting. Keep the bed evenly watered and blanch the bulb by mounding soil around its base as it swells. Grow it as a cool-season crop, timing plantings for spring or fall to avoid the summer heat that pushes it to seed.

plant_note: Vigorous spreader; contain in beds or pots.
directions: Plant rooted divisions in moist to boggy soil in spring, ideally in a sunken container to curb its aggressive rhizomes. This Southeast Asian herb carries a distinctive tangy, fishy-citrus aroma used raw in Vietnamese and Chinese cooking. It thrives in partial shade with constant moisture and even tolerates shallow standing water. Cut back runaway runners to keep it from overtaking a bed. Harvest young leaves and tender shoots through the growing season, and mulch crowns in colder zones.

plant_note: Direct sow seeds or plant tubers in fall in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade. Florida Betony spreads very aggressively by underground tubers and can become difficult to control in lawn and garden settings — intentional cultivation in a contained bed or pot is strongly recommended.
directions: Florida Betony is a native perennial herb of moist, disturbed habitats, roadsides, and open areas across Florida and the southeastern coastal plain, best known for its white, segmented, rattlesnake-rattle-shaped underground tubers — earning it the folk name 'rattlesnake weed' — that are crisp, sweet, and edible raw or cooked with a flavor reminiscent of water chestnuts. The small pink flowers produced in spring attract native bees and are mildly ornamental, though the plant is primarily known as a prolific lawn weed throughout the Southeast due to its ability to spread rapidly from fragments of its segmented tubers. As a cultivated edible herb, the tubers are harvested in late fall through early spring when the plant is dormant or growing slowly, cleaned, and eaten raw in salads, pickled, or stir-fried. Plant in contained beds to prevent uncontrolled spreading — once established in a garden, Florida Betony is virtually impossible to eradicate without repeated effort.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost; sorrel is one of the first herbs to emerge in spring.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep in well-drained, moderately fertile soil; thin to 12 in apart once seedlings are 2 in tall. Remove flower stalks as soon as they appear to prevent the plant from diverting energy away from leaf production. Divide established clumps every 2–3 years to maintain vigor and refresh the planting. Harvest the shield-shaped leaves regularly while they are young and tender; the bright, lemony oxalic flavor becomes more pronounced in larger, older leaves.

plant_note: Plant divisions or rooted cuttings around last frost date; French tarragon does not come true from seed.
directions: Always source French tarragon as a division or cutting—seeds sold as tarragon are typically the flavorless Russian type. Plant in lean, well-drained soil; overly rich soil encourages lush but bland foliage. Divide plants every 2–3 years in spring to maintain flavor and vigor, discarding old woody centers. Begin harvesting once shoots reach 6 in; the anise-like fragrance intensifies just before the plant's inconspicuous flowers open.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions after last frost once soil has thoroughly warmed to at least 70°F; galangal needs extended warm conditions.
directions: Plant rhizome sections with at least one growing bud 2–4 in deep in rich, moisture-retentive, humus-rich soil in partial shade; galangal needs consistent moisture and high humidity to thrive. Unlike ginger, galangal has a distinctly peppery, piney, medicinal flavor with less sweetness, and is a foundational ingredient in Thai, Indonesian, and Malay cuisines. In zones below 9, grow in large containers that can be moved indoors before frost; the rhizomes are frost-sensitive but the plant can be stored dormant above 50°F in winter. Harvest rhizomes after 10–12 months when the foliage begins to yellow in fall, digging with care to take only what is needed while leaving part of the clump to regrow.

plant_note: Fast; succession-sow every two weeks.
directions: Direct-sow thickly in cool weather, barely covering the seed, which sprouts within days. This peppery quick crop is harvested young as a spicy microgreen or baby salad green. Sow in succession every couple of weeks and keep the soil evenly moist to slow bolting. Provide partial shade in warm spells to extend the harvest window. Snip seedlings with scissors just above the soil once they reach a few inches tall.

plant_note: Direct sow in cool weather, or grow indoors year-round as sprouts and microgreens.
directions: Garden cress is one of the fastest herbs to grow, germinating in days and offering a zesty, peppery flavor for salads and sandwiches. Sow thickly on a moist surface for microgreens indoors, or direct sow in cool weather outdoors. Harvest young shoots within a couple of weeks before they turn tough or bolt. Make frequent small sowings for a steady supply of tender, spicy greens.

plant_note: Sow in early spring; clumps return each year and produce edible white flowers.
directions: Garlic chives are a hardy perennial allium with flat, grass-like leaves that carry a mild garlic flavor, plus fragrant edible white flowers late in the season. Sow seed in clumps and harvest leaves by snipping from the outside as needed. The star-shaped blooms attract pollinators but self-sow prolifically, so deadhead if you want to limit spread. Divide crowded clumps every few years to keep them vigorous.

plant_note: Sow directly 4–6 weeks before last frost or transplant established divisions in spring.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep and keep soil moist; germination is similar to common chives but the flat, grass-like leaves have a distinct mild garlic flavor. Grow in well-drained, fertile soil and divide clumps every 2–3 years to prevent overcrowding and maintain productivity. Deadhead flowers promptly, as garlic chives self-seed prolifically and can become weedy. Harvest flat leaves at any height; older leaves become slightly tough but remain useful cooked.

plant_note: Classic pesto basil.
directions: Start seed indoors 4-6 weeks before last frost, covering lightly and keeping soil near 75F. This large-leaved Italian classic is the standard for pesto and Mediterranean cooking. Transplant into warm, rich soil in full sun only after all frost danger passes. Pinch out the growing tips at six leaves to force a bushy, high-yielding plant. Harvest whole stems above a leaf node and remove flower buds promptly to keep leaves tender and sweet.

plant_note: Direct sow or transplant after the last frost once soil and nights are warm.
directions: Genovese basil is the classic Italian pesto basil, prized for its large, tender, deeply fragrant green leaves. Sow once soil is warm and keep plants well watered in full sun. Pinch the growing tips regularly and remove flower buds to encourage bushy growth and prevent the leaves from turning bitter. Harvest often, as frequent picking keeps the plant producing until frost.

plant_note: Surface sow around the last frost; the tiny seed needs light to germinate.
directions: German chamomile is a fast-growing annual with delicate white daisy flowers and a sweet, apple-like fragrance, popular for herbal tea. Surface sow the fine seed and press it into the soil, since light aids germination. Harvest the flower heads when petals are fully open and dry them for tea. It self-sows readily, so let a few flowers mature to reseed the patch.

plant_note: Surface sow; seed needs light to germinate and self-sows readily.
directions: German chamomile bears masses of small, daisy-like flowers whose apple-scented petals make a soothing tea and lend themselves to dried arrangements and crafts. Sow seed on the soil surface and press in lightly, since it needs light to germinate; it establishes easily and often self-sows for future seasons. Grow in full sun with average, well-drained soil and modest water. Harvest the open flower heads regularly to keep the plant blooming and to build up a supply for drying.

plant_note: Sow in early spring; soaking seed overnight speeds slow germination.
directions: Giant of Italy is a vigorous flat-leaf Italian parsley with large, aromatic dark green leaves and a robust, authentic flavor. Germination is slow, so soak seed overnight and keep the soil consistently moist. Harvest outer stems first and the plant keeps producing from the center all season. This hardy biennial often overwinters in milder zones, flowering and setting seed in its second year.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions in early autumn in a shaded woodland bed; seeds require prolonged double dormancy and are best left to experienced growers.
directions: Plant small rhizome pieces 1–2 in deep in autumn in deep, shaded woodland soil rich in leaf mold; rhizome division is far more reliable than growing from seed. Goldenseal requires 70–80% shade and the moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic conditions of mature eastern North American forests — raised beds under deciduous trees work well. This is a slow-growing, at-risk native species; growing your own reduces pressure on wild populations, but expect 4–6 years before roots are substantial enough to harvest. Harvest small portions of the golden rhizome in autumn of the fourth or fifth year, leaving the majority of the root system intact for continued growth.

plant_note: Direct sow as soon as soil can be worked in early spring; seeds may need cold stratification for best germination.
directions: Cold-stratify seeds in the refrigerator for 2–4 weeks before sowing to improve germination rates; sow 1/4 in deep in moisture-retentive, fertile soil. This old European cottage garden vegetable-herb was once widely grown for its young shoots (used like asparagus), leaves (used like spinach), and flower buds (used like broccoli). Grow in moist, humus-rich soil in partial shade; plants are long-lived perennials once established and require little care. Harvest young shoots in spring, leaves throughout the season, and flower buds before they open; do not harvest heavily in the first year to allow the plant to establish.

plant_note: Transplant rooted runners after all frost danger has passed into a warm, moist location; propagation from stolons is far more reliable than seed.
directions: Propagate from stolons (creeping stems with nodes), which root readily in moist potting mix or at the edges of ponds; plant in rich, consistently moist to wet soil in partial shade, as gotu kola thrives in the margins of water bodies in its native tropical Asian habitat. In zones below 9, grow in containers that can be overwintered on a warm, bright windowsill with consistently moist soil. Harvest the small, fan-shaped leaves regularly once the plant is established; the fresh leaves are eaten in salads in Sri Lankan cuisine and used in Ayurvedic and traditional Chinese medicine as a cognitive tonic and wound-healing herb. Harvest leaf material by snipping stems without uprooting the plant to allow continued production.

plant_note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; transplant after nights are reliably above 50°F.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep in warm, moist seed-starting mix and thin seedlings to avoid crowding once they have two true leaves. Greek basil forms a naturally compact, globe-shaped mound with tiny, intensely aromatic leaves — no pinching required to maintain shape, though removing flower buds prolongs leaf harvest. Plant in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; the petite habit makes it an excellent edging plant or container herb. Harvest by snipping small stem tips; the high surface-area-to-volume ratio of the small leaves delivers concentrated flavor.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger passes into very well-drained, rocky or sandy soil.
directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep in well-drained, alkaline starting mix and keep warm at 68–72°F until germination; use gritty, mineral-rich mix to mimic its rocky Mediterranean mountain habitat. Plant out in the poorest, most well-drained spot in the garden — excess moisture and rich soil are the primary causes of failure. The silver-felted stems and leaves are harvested in full bloom in summer when the yellow flowers are visible; cut entire stems and hang in bundles to dry in a warm, airy space. Brew the whole dried stems as an herbal tea by simmering 5–10 minutes; the resulting infusion has a mild, honey-and-herb flavor and is traditionally consumed throughout Greece for immune support and as an everyday tonic.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost and transplant after all frost danger has passed.
directions: Surface-sow seeds by pressing them into barely moist, well-drained seed-starting mix without covering, as they need light to germinate. Greek oregano (also called Italian or true oregano) is the pungent, white-flowered subspecies prized in Mediterranean cooking; it has far stronger flavor than common Origanum vulgare. Plant in lean, gritty, alkaline-leaning soil in full sun — excessive fertility dilutes the essential oils that give it its distinctive bite. Cut plants back by one-third after flowering each year to keep them compact; harvest just as buds begin to open for peak aroma.

plant_note: Seed needs light and benefits from chilling; press onto surface and do not bury.
directions: Also called Japanese basil or beefsteak plant, shiso carries a complex flavor blending cinnamon, mint, and clove with hints of cumin, and this mix offers both green and red-leaved plants. Surface-sow seed indoors as it needs light to sprout, and set out only after the soil has warmed. Heat- and drought-tolerant, it makes a handsome bedding plant that attracts pollinators. Pinch growing tips to encourage branching and harvest young leaves for wraps, pickles, and garnishes.

plant_note: Plant rooted divisions in spring or fall in any moist, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade; ground ivy establishes rapidly and spreads by rooting stolons — site it where spreading is desirable and use edging to contain it in formal plantings. It naturalizes readily in shaded lawns and under trees where grass struggles.
directions: Plant in partial to full shade in any reasonably moist soil; ground ivy is a creeping, mat-forming member of the mint family with distinctively kidney-shaped, scallop-edged leaves that release a pleasantly pungent, minty-medicinal aroma when crushed — small purple to blue flowers appear in whorls along the stems from spring through early summer. The young leaves and shoots are edible: they were used in medieval Europe to clarify and bitter ale before hops (earning the name 'alehoof'), and are used today in small amounts as a bitter herb in salads, soups, and herbal teas. Medicinally, ground ivy has a long tradition in European herbal medicine as an anti-inflammatory and respiratory herb. It is one of very few plants that will produce a dense, attractive groundcover in deep, dry shade under trees — a condition that defeats almost everything else. Can be invasive in favorable, moist conditions; contain with edging where needed.

plant_note: Plant divisions or rooted suckers after last frost; in zones below 8 grow in large containers and overwinter indoors.
directions: Divide established clumps in spring by separating rooted suckers from the base of the mother plant and replanting in rich, moist soil. Hoja santa grows rapidly in warm, humid conditions and prefers partial shade with consistent moisture; the enormous heart-shaped leaves can reach 12 in wide and emit a distinctive anise–root beer aroma from their essential oils. In Mexican cuisine the leaves are used to wrap tamales, season mole verde, and envelop fish for grilling. In zones 8–9, plants die to the ground after frost but reliably regrow from the roots; mulch heavily to protect the root zone through winter.

plant_note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost and transplant after soil has warmed to at least 65°F; or direct sow on the soil surface after last frost when soil is warm. Seed needs light to germinate — do not cover.
directions: Plant in rich, well-drained soil in full sun after all frost danger has passed; holy basil (tulsi) is the sacred herb of Hindu worship and traditional Ayurvedic medicine, with an intensely spicy, clove-and-pepper scented flavor distinctly different from sweet Italian basil. The purple-stemmed Kapoor and Krishna varieties are most fragrant; the green-stemmed Vana type has a stronger, more medicinal flavor. Holy basil makes excellent Thai cooking, fresh teas, and pesto, and is reputed to have adaptogenic properties. Grow exactly as sweet basil — pinch flowers early and often to extend the harvest. In zones 10–12 it grows as a short-lived perennial; everywhere else it is grown as an annual. Seed is produced prolifically if a few flower stalks are allowed to mature.

plant_note: Start indoors 6 to 4 weeks before the last frost and transplant once nights are warm.
directions: Holy basil, or tulsi, is an aromatic tender annual revered in Ayurvedic tradition and grown for fragrant leaves used in tea and wellness blends. Start seed indoors in warmth and transplant only after nights stay warm, as it is frost-sensitive. Pinch flower spikes to prolong leafy growth, or leave some blooms for pollinators and self-seeding. Harvest leaves and flowering tops regularly and dry them for tea.

plant_note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; do not transplant until nights are reliably above 55°F as cold soil stunts growth.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep in warm starting mix at 75–85°F; germination is swift when soil is adequately warm. Transplant after night temperatures stay above 55°F and pinch growing tips immediately to promote bushy growth. Regular pinching and removal of flower spikes prolongs the leaf harvest and delays bitterness; harvest leaves in the morning after dew has dried for the most aromatic result. Fresh or dried leaves are used in adaptogenic teas, Ayurvedic preparations, and as a sacred plant in Hindu tradition.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring in moist, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; honewort self-sows freely and can naturalize in the shade garden. A perennial herb that dies back in winter and re-emerges in early spring from a taproot.
directions: Honewort is a shade-loving native edible herb, native to moist, rich woodlands and forest edges throughout eastern North America from Quebec to Florida and west to Kansas, producing tender, edible leaves and stems in spring and early summer that taste mildly of parsley and celery — used raw in salads or cooked as a pot herb. It is closely related to Japanese mitsuba (Cryptotaenia japonica) and can substitute for it in East Asian cooking; the young leaves are particularly flavorful in early spring. A self-seeding perennial that naturalizes freely in the shade garden, it fills in the difficult deep-shade spaces where most vegetables fail, providing a continuous harvest of tender greens from early spring to summer heat. Harvest outer leaves regularly to maintain plant vigor; allow some plants to set seed for natural self-renewal.

plant_note: Plant rhizome cuttings ('rhizomes') horizontally 2–3 inches deep in spring in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; hops climbs vigorously (up to 20 feet in a single season) — provide a very strong trellis or support. Only female plants produce usable cones.
directions: Plant rhizomes horizontally in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; hops is the perennial vine whose dried female flower cones ('hops') are the essential bittering and preserving agent in virtually all beer styles. Native to temperate North America and Europe, it is a vigorous, tendriled vine that dies to the ground each fall and re-emerges rapidly each spring to climb 15–20 feet on strings, trellises, or arbors. The soft green hop cones (strobiles) ripen in late summer; harvest when papery and fragrant, then dry immediately for use in home brewing. The young spring shoots emerging from the ground are edible as a vegetable, cooked like asparagus. The dried seed cones have traditional medicinal use as a mild sedative. Ornamental cultivars like 'Aureus' (golden-yellow foliage) are widely grown for their dramatic summer screening effect. Performs best in cool-summer climates (zones 3–7); struggles in the persistent heat of zone 8+.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; surface-sow as seeds are small and benefit from light for germination.
directions: Surface-sow onto moist mix 6–8 weeks before transplanting; horehound germinates readily in warm soil but can be slow and erratic. Plant in dry, well-drained, even rocky or sandy soil where most plants would struggle — fertile, moist conditions cause leggy, weak growth. Harvest stems and leaves before flowering for the most potent bitterness, bundling for drying or infusing fresh into candy and cough syrups. Shear plants back in late summer to keep growth compact and prevent excessive self-seeding.

plant_note: Direct sow after last frost once soil is warm; can also be started indoors 4 weeks early for a longer season.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep in well-drained soil after last frost; thin to 18–24 in apart as huacatay grows into a tall, upright plant. This Andean herb — essential in Peruvian cuisine for dishes like ocopa sauce and anticuchos marinades — has a complex flavor combining mint, tarragon, and citrus. Plants are day-length sensitive and flower in late summer to fall; harvest leaves and stem tips throughout the vegetative season before flowering commences. Grow in full sun with moderate fertility; the plant is drought-tolerant once established and spreads by self-seeding in warm climates.

plant_note: Plant rooted cuttings or container plants in spring in very well-drained, lean, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; excellent drainage is essential — this plant rots in heavy, moist clay. Excellent in gravel gardens and rock gardens.
directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; hummingbird mint is a native sage relative of the Texas-New Mexico border region with the most extraordinary foliage fragrance in the genus — a pure, sweet bubblegum-spearmint scent that fills the air when brushed. The deep rose-pink tubular flowers from summer through frost are consumed almost exclusively by hummingbirds, which are so attracted to the flowers that the plant earns its common name. It is genuinely drought tolerant and thrives in hot, dry conditions where it has excellent drainage. The plant self-sows modestly and is short-lived as a perennial (3–5 years); take cuttings in summer to maintain the garden. Deer resistant. An excellent choice for zone 8–9 pollinator gardens in full sun with excellent drainage.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; surface-sow or cover lightly with 1/8 in of fine mix.
directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep indoors in warm starting mix; germination is fast and reliable at 65–70°F. Plant in lean, well-drained, slightly alkaline soil — hyssop is drought-tolerant once established and thrives in conditions similar to lavender. Prune plants back by about one-third after flowering to keep them from becoming woody and to promote a compact, bushy habit. Harvest leafy stems and flowers in the morning just as the blossoms begin to open, drying them for use in expectorant teas, culinary herb blends, and potpourri.

plant_note: Sow around the last frost; this hardy perennial establishes over the first season.
directions: Hyssop is a fragrant, semi-evergreen perennial herb with narrow leaves and spikes of blue flowers that attract bees, butterflies, and hummingbirds. It thrives in full sun and well-drained soil and tolerates drought once established. Sow seed shallowly and thin to give plants room to form a bushy shrub. Trim after flowering to keep the plant compact and encourage fresh aromatic growth.

plant_note: Sow as soon as soil can be worked in spring; tolerates light frost.
directions: This heirloom flat-leaf parsley produces broad, dark green leaves with a clean, robust flavor prized in Mediterranean cooking. Soak seed overnight to speed the naturally slow germination, then sow shallowly and keep the soil consistently moist for the two to four weeks it takes to sprout. Thin seedlings to about eight inches apart in rich, well-drained soil and full sun. Harvest outer stems first so the plant keeps producing tender new growth through the season.

plant_note: Set out only after nights stay reliably above 50 F.
directions: Genovese is the classic Italian basil, offering the traditional fragrance and flavor that make it the top choice for pesto and countless summer dishes. Start seed indoors in warmth and transplant after the soil is warm and settled weather has arrived, as cold stunts young plants. Give it full sun, rich soil, and even moisture for lush growth. Pinch out flower buds and harvest from the top to keep plants leafy, tender, and productive all season.

plant_note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed as young plants are cold-sensitive.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep in warm, moist starting mix and provide a trellis or support on which the vigorous twining vine can climb; jiaogulan grows 10–20 ft if given the right conditions. Plant in moderately fertile, well-drained soil with some afternoon shade in hot climates; consistent moisture promotes the most lush and harvestable leaf growth. In zones 7–8 the plant dies to the ground in winter but regrows from the root; in zone 9 and above it may be evergreen. Harvest the five-lobed leaflets throughout the growing season for fresh or dried tea; known as southern ginseng, the leaves contain saponins similar to those in Panax ginseng and are used in adaptogenic preparations.

plant_note: Plant container-grown trees in spring after frost danger passes in well-drained, slightly acidic soil; in zones 9–10 it can remain outdoors year-round. In zones 7–8 grow in containers and overwinter indoors above 50°F.
directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun; makrut lime is grown primarily for its distinctive double-lobed leaves, which are the indispensable aromatic herb of Thai and Cambodian cooking, used in curries, soups, and stir-fries to impart an intensely floral, citrusy perfume that no other herb can substitute. The bumpy, knobbly green fruit has very little juice but extremely aromatic zest used in marinades and Thai curry pastes. Leaves can be harvested whenever new growth appears; young leaves have the strongest flavor. In containers (the most practical approach north of zone 9), use a quality potting mix, fertilize monthly with citrus fertilizer spring through fall, and overwinter indoors in a bright window above 50°F.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost; kalonji prefers cool spring conditions and dislikes heat.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep directly where they are to grow; kalonji has fine, thread-like foliage similar to its ornamental cousin love-in-a-mist and dislikes transplanting. Grow in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun; the plant bolts quickly in summer heat, so time sowing for a cool-season harvest. Harvest the swollen seed pods when they begin to turn tan and paper-like, cutting whole stems into bags before the pods split and release the seeds. The intensely aromatic black seeds are used in South Asian cooking on naan breads and in spice blends, and have a long history of medicinal use; they are not to be confused with black cumin (Bunium persicum).

plant_note: Surface-sow; light aids germination.
directions: Surface-sow indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost, pressing seed onto the mix without covering it. This East Asian herb has minty-anise leaves used in Korean cooking and herbal teas. Transplant to full sun in well-drained soil, where purple flower spikes draw abundant bees. Deadhead to prolong bloom, and allow a few seed heads to ripen for self-sowing. Harvest young leaves through summer and cut plants back in fall.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost after cold stratification; or sow fresh seed outdoors in autumn for natural stratification and spring germination.
directions: Cold-stratify seed in the refrigerator for 3–4 weeks before surface-sowing; the tiny seeds need cool temperatures and moderate moisture to germinate. Plant in humus-rich, well-drained but moisture-retentive soil; lady's mantle tolerates full sun in cooler climates but benefits from afternoon shade in hot regions. The pleated, velvety leaves catch dewdrops and rainwater, creating sparkling spheres of water — a notable ornamental feature. Harvest leaves and the chartreuse flower sprays in early summer for use in traditional women's tonics; the foliage and flowers also add texture to fresh-cut and dried flower arrangements.

plant_note: Surface sow and press in; light aids germination.
directions: Lamb's ear forms a low, spreading mat of soft, silvery, velvety foliage that adds texture to borders, xeriscapes, and pollinator plantings. Sow seed on the soil surface and barely press in, since light encourages germination, then keep lightly moist until seedlings establish. Once rooted it is remarkably drought tolerant and thrives in lean, well-drained soil with full sun. Space plants a foot or more apart and divide clumps every few years to keep the planting vigorous.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant only after all danger of frost has passed.
directions: This sweet Italian basil is grown for its unusually large, aromatic leaves and slow-bolting habit that stretches the harvest for pesto and fresh cooking. Start seed indoors about six weeks before the last frost and keep it warm, since basil resents cold soil. Transplant into rich, well-drained ground in full sun once nights stay reliably above fifty degrees. Pinch flowering tips regularly to keep the plant bushy and encourage a steady supply of tender leaves.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after the last frost date.
directions: Surface-sow seeds and press lightly into moist mix without covering; they need light and warmth to sprout. Plant in well-drained soil in partial shade, particularly in hot climates, to maintain fresh-looking, non-scorched foliage. Cut plants back by half after the first flush of growth and again after any flowering to encourage compact, leafy regrowth. Harvest leaves before the plant flowers for the strongest lemon fragrance, picking in the morning after dew has dried.

plant_note: Surface sow; seed needs light to germinate.
directions: Lemon balm is a hardy mint-family perennial with a gentle lemony scent that flavors teas, salads, and desserts. Press the tiny seeds onto the soil surface without covering, since they need light to sprout, and keep them evenly moist. It grows readily in average soil with full sun to part shade and spreads by roots, so give it room or contain it. Cut plants back after flowering to keep foliage fresh and prevent heavy self-seeding.

plant_note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; transplant only after nights are reliably above 50°F.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep in warm seed-starting mix at 70°F or above; germination is rapid once soil temperature is adequate. Transplant into rich, well-drained garden soil in full sun and pinch the growing tip early to encourage a bushy, multi-stemmed plant. Lemon basil is compact compared to sweet basil and has a distinct citrus fragrance from the presence of citral; it pairs well with fish, poultry, and Southeast Asian cuisines. Harvest by snipping stems just above a leaf pair once plants are well established, and pinch flower buds promptly to extend the harvest season.

plant_note: Direct sow or start indoors 4 weeks before last frost; lemon catnip self-seeds freely once established.
directions: Sow seeds on the surface of well-drained starting mix and press in lightly; fresh seeds germinate readily in 1–2 weeks. Lemon catnip has all the nepetalactone chemistry of common catnip (making it equally irresistible to cats) but its foliage carries a pleasant lemon-mint scent suitable for tea and aromatherapy. Grow in full sun in any well-drained soil; cut plants back by half after the first flush of flowers to encourage a second bloom and fresh aromatic foliage. Harvest stems before flowers fully open for the best flavor in herbal teas; dry quickly in a warm, airy spot and store in airtight containers.

plant_note: Surface sow; benefits from a period of cool moisture to break dormancy.
directions: Also called lemon bee balm or horsemint, this aromatic Monarda produces tiered whorls of lavender-pink blooms that draw bees, butterflies, and hummingbirds. Surface sow the seed where plants are to grow, pressing it in for good contact, and expect uneven germination over two to three weeks. It thrives in full sun and average, well-drained soil and self-sows to return year after year. Harvest fragrant foliage for tea and leave some flowers standing to feed pollinators.

plant_note: Plant container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moist soil in full sun to partial shade; lemon myrtle grows slowly, reaching 6–8 feet over many years in most garden situations. It is frost-sensitive and performs best in zone 9b–11 where freezes are rare or light.
directions: Plant in well-drained, moist, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; lemon myrtle is an Australian native rainforest tree with the most intensely concentrated lemon fragrance of any plant on Earth — the essential oil content of the leaves (95% citral) vastly exceeds that of lemon balm, lemon verbena, or lemon thyme. Fresh or dried leaves are used in cooking exactly as lemon is used, in teas, baked goods, sauces, marinades, ice cream, and fish dishes, with a clean, pure lemon flavor that holds up better to heat than actual lemon juice. It is increasingly popular as a culinary specialty herb in Zone 9–11 gardens in the US, particularly in coastal California and the Gulf Coast. The shiny, oval, evergreen leaves are attractive year-round, and small clusters of white flowers appear in summer. It makes an excellent large container specimen in colder zones that can be moved to protection before frost. Harvest leaves fresh as needed or dry for long-term use.

plant_note: Root stem cuttings in late spring; named cultivars do not come true from seed, so vegetative propagation is necessary to preserve the lemon character.
directions: Take 3–4 in tip cuttings in late spring or early summer, remove lower leaves, and insert into gritty, free-draining mix; roots develop in 2–3 weeks. Plant in full sun in sharply drained, lean, slightly alkaline soil, as lemon thyme rots in wet or rich conditions just like common thyme. Shear lightly after flowering each season to prevent woodiness and maintain a tidy mound of fragrant foliage. Harvest leafy sprigs at any time; the lemon-thyme combination flavor is prized in fish dishes, salad dressings, and desserts, and is excellent fresh or dried.

plant_note: Transplant rooted cuttings outdoors after all frost danger has passed; bring containers indoors before the first autumn frost.
directions: Propagate from softwood cuttings taken in summer rather than from seed, which has poor viability; root cuttings in moist perlite under plastic until rooted. Plant in full sun and well-drained soil; in zones below 8 grow in containers that can be overwintered indoors in a cool, bright spot. Prune hard in spring when new growth emerges from what may look like dead wood — lemon verbena is often a slow riser after dormancy. Harvest leaves any time for intensely lemon-scented tea, dessert flavoring, and potpourri; fragrance peaks just before the plant begins to flower.

plant_note: Transplant divisions or rooted stalks after last frost when soil is reliably warm.
directions: Propagate by placing fresh grocery-store stalks (with roots intact) in water until roots develop, then transplant into rich, well-drained soil in full sun. Lemongrass needs heat and consistent moisture; water deeply and regularly during the growing season. In zones below 10, grow in large containers and bring indoors before first frost, cutting back to 6 in tall. Harvest outer stalks by cutting or twisting at the base when they reach pencil thickness; use the pale, tender base for cooking.

plant_note: Start indoors in warmth; transplant after frost and soil have warmed.
directions: Lemongrass forms clumps of tall, citrus-scented stalks essential to Thai and Southeast Asian cooking. Start seed indoors in warm soil eight or more weeks before the last frost, as germination is slow and heat-loving. Move plants out to a sunny bed or large container only after nights stay warm, spacing them well to accommodate the spreading clump. In cold regions dig and overwinter a division indoors, since this tender perennial will not survive frost.

plant_note: Warmth-loving; transplant after soil warms.
directions: This exceptionally productive sweet basil produces huge, light green leaves nearly the size of your hand that are more sweet than spicy. The broad leaves make an excellent lettuce substitute in wraps and sandwiches or a bold layer in a caprese salad. Start seed indoors in warmth and move to a sunny bed only after frost danger has passed. Keep soil evenly moist, pinch off flower spikes, and harvest whole leaves to encourage continued production.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost after scarifying or soaking seed overnight; transplant carefully after frost danger passes to avoid disturbing roots.
directions: Scarify seed coat lightly with sandpaper or soak overnight before sowing 1/4 in deep in warm starting mix; germination improves significantly with this treatment. Plant in deep, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun — licorice develops an extensive, deep root system and resents being moved. Roots are ready to harvest in the third or fourth autumn when they are at least pencil-thick, digging carefully to extract as much of the lateral root run as possible. The dried root is used medicinally as a demulcent and anti-inflammatory for digestive and respiratory conditions; note that excessive consumption raises blood pressure.

plant_note: Chartreuse foliage; light-dependent seed.
directions: Surface-sow indoors 8 weeks before last frost, leaving seed uncovered since light aids germination. This golden-leaved anise hyssop offers licorice-scented foliage for teas and blue flower spikes for pollinators. Transplant to full sun in well-drained soil, spacing to allow good airflow. Deadhead spent blooms to extend flowering into fall and prevent excessive self-seeding. Harvest leaves and flowers fresh or dried and cut plants back after frost.

plant_note: Direct-sow into warm soil after frost.
directions: Direct-sow after the soil warms past 65F, scattering seed thinly and thinning to a hand's width apart. The bright citrus scent makes this basil superb in Southeast Asian dishes and cold drinks. Give it full sun and steady moisture in rich, free-draining soil for the best oil content. Pinch growing tips early to build a bushy plant and delay bolting. Harvest leaves in the morning when the fragrant oils are most concentrated.

plant_note: Set out after frost danger; grow in a pot where winters fall below 20 F so it can overwinter indoors.
directions: Little Ragu is a compact, upright form of culinary sweet bay with aromatic evergreen leaves used fresh or dried in soups and stews. It stays tidy at 6 to 8 feet and takes well to pruning or shaping in containers. Plant in full sun to light shade in well-drained soil and let the surface dry slightly between waterings, as it dislikes soggy roots. In cold-winter areas grow it in a pot and bring it into a cool, bright spot before hard frost. Harvest mature leaves any time and dry them for the strongest flavor.

plant_note: Direct sow in succession; cilantro dislikes transplanting and bolts in heat.
directions: Also known as Chinese parsley, this variety is selected for excellent flavor, extra leafiness, and slow bolting, giving you a longer cilantro harvest. Direct sow into cool soil since the taproot resents transplanting, and make successive sowings every few weeks for a steady supply. Provide full sun in cool weather or light afternoon shade as temperatures climb to delay flowering. Let some plants bolt and set seed to harvest coriander for the spice rack.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring, 2–4 weeks before last frost; fresh seed germinates best.
directions: Sow fresh seed 1/4 in deep in moist, fertile soil in early spring; stored seed loses viability quickly. Give lovage a permanent spot as it develops a large taproot that dislikes disturbance, and provide deep, moisture-retentive, fertile soil. Remove flower stalks as they appear to keep the plant producing leafy growth for longer. Harvest young outer leaves and stems throughout the season; the intense celery-like flavor means a little goes a long way.

plant_note: Start indoors or direct sow in spring; a long-lived perennial that returns yearly.
directions: Lovage is a bold, long-lived perennial herb with a strong savory flavor like an intense celery, with hints of anise and cumin. Start seeds indoors or sow directly in spring, giving this large plant plenty of room, as it can reach several feet tall. It prefers full sun to partial shade and rich, evenly moist soil. Harvest leaves and stems through the season for soups and stocks, and cut plants back to encourage fresh growth.

plant_note: Direct sow seed in fall or early spring in moist to well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average soil in full sun to full shade; lyre-leaf sage is one of the most adaptable native sages, thriving in conditions ranging from full sun to fairly deep shade — and self-sowing reliably in lawns and woodland edges.
directions: Sow seed in fall in moist to well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average soil in full sun to full shade — lyre-leaf sage is one of the most adaptable and overlooked native sages, producing basal rosettes of deeply lobed, lyrate (lyre-shaped) leaves — often beautifully marked with dark purple blotches — followed by tall slender spikes of two-lipped, pale blue-violet flowers attractive to bumblebees, mason bees, and hummingbirds in spring and early summer. Unlike most sages, it thrives in shaded conditions including dry shade under trees, making it exceptionally useful for the difficult shaded, dry area where few flowering plants perform well. Self-sows freely in lawns (where it is native and naturalistic), woodland edges, and disturbed areas. The dark-mottled, rosette foliage is attractive in its own right through winter in the South. Traditional Appalachian medicinal plant. An outstanding, adaptable native sage for zones 5–9.

plant_note: Direct sow where it is to grow; the long taproot resents transplanting.
directions: Mammoth dill lives up to its name, reaching at least three feet and sometimes towering to five, with feathery foliage prized for cooking and pickling. Its large flower umbels are magnets for beneficial pollinators, and the plants readily self-sow once seed matures. Direct sow into full sun since the taproot dislikes being moved, and sow in succession for a continuous supply of leaves. Provide staking or a sheltered spot for the tallest plants, and harvest foliage before flowering for the best flavor.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed.
directions: Surface-sow the tiny seeds without covering, pressing them gently into moist, well-drained seed mix and placing in a warm, bright spot. Transplant into well-drained, slightly alkaline garden soil after nights are reliably above 50°F. Pinch out growing tips regularly to keep plants bushy and delay flowering. Harvest stems just as the knotted flower buds form for the sweetest, most aromatic leaves.

plant_note: Surface sow indoors; press seed in and keep warm.
directions: Sweet marjoram is a Mediterranean culinary herb with a warm, gently spicy flavor softer than its relative oregano. Surface sow the fine seed indoors about six weeks before the last frost, pressing it into the mix and keeping it warm and lightly moist. Transplant into well-drained soil in full sun after frost danger passes, spacing plants around ten inches apart. Grown as a tender perennial, it is best treated as an annual in cold climates or potted up to overwinter indoors.

plant_note: Fall sowing supplies cold stratification; spring sowing works after chilling the seed.
directions: Marsh mallow is a tall perennial herb with soft gray-green foliage and pale pink flowers, long valued for its soothing roots and leaves. The seed germinates best after a cold period, so sow outdoors in fall or cold-stratify before a spring sowing. Give it full sun and consistently moist soil, reflecting its natural home in damp meadows and marshy ground. Plants may be slow the first year but form deep roots that can be harvested from established clumps.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; scarifying the seed coat lightly with sandpaper or soaking overnight in warm water speeds germination.
directions: Scarify or soak seed overnight before sowing 1/4 in deep; marshmallow appreciates consistent moisture, particularly during its establishment period. Plant in deep, moderately moist soil as the tap root grows substantial in mature plants. Harvest leaves and flowers in summer for use in soothing teas and poultices; the fleshy root is the most mucilaginous part and is best dug in the plant's second or third autumn. The dried root can be sliced, powdered, or made into a cold-water infusion to extract its slippery mucilage for throat-coat and digestive preparations.

plant_note: Plant bulbs 2–3 inches deep in moist to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun in fall; meadow garlic naturalizes readily and spreads by bulblets and bulbils — allow it to colonize in a meadow or woodland edge setting where spreading is welcome.
directions: Plant bulbs in fall in moist to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; meadow garlic is a widespread native Allium found in moist meadows, woodland edges, and disturbed areas throughout eastern North America — an edible wildflower with a mild garlic-onion flavor. The entire plant is edible: bulbs (used like small garlic cloves), leaves (used like chives), and the bulbils (tiny bulblets produced in the flower head instead of or alongside seeds). It is one of the most cold-hardy and adaptable native edible bulbs and tolerates partial shade in moist conditions. In colonial times it was used as a spring potherb, and it remains a foraging favorite. The plant self-sows and spreads vegetatively by bulblets to naturalize in appropriate conditions. Plants die back in summer heat; mark the location of dormant bulbs to avoid digging them inadvertently.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost after 4–6 weeks of cold stratification; transplant to a moist site after frost danger passes.
directions: Cold-stratify seed in moist medium in the refrigerator for 4–6 weeks before surface-sowing; plant out in consistently moist to wet, humus-rich soil in partial shade or full sun where soil never dries out, mimicking its native pond-margin habitat. Meadowsweet bears frothy, sweetly almond-scented flower plumes in midsummer that were historically used to flavor mead and wines. Harvest flowers and upper leaves just as the buds begin to open, drying quickly at low heat to preserve the salicylate compounds that give the herb its traditional analgesic and anti-inflammatory reputation. The plant is the original source from which salicylic acid — the basis of aspirin — was first isolated.

plant_note: Plant rooted cuttings or nursery transplants after last frost; in zones below 8 overwinter indoors or take cuttings each fall.
directions: Root 4–6 in softwood cuttings in moist perlite; this shrubby herb from the Mexican highlands is unrelated to Mediterranean oregano but delivers a stronger, more citrusy-oregano flavor essential in chili, mole, and refried beans. Plant in sharply drained, lean soil in full sun; Mexican oregano is extremely drought-tolerant once established and actually develops more aromatic foliage under moderate stress. Prune plants lightly after each harvest to encourage fresh, leafy growth and prevent excessive woodiness. Harvest leaves and small branch tips at any time, drying them at low heat for the most authentic dried herb for Tex-Mex and Mexican cooking.

plant_note: Surface sow; seed needs light. Grown as an annual in cold climates.
directions: A member of the marigold family, Mexican tarragon produces anise-flavored leaves that make an easy substitute for French tarragon in warm, humid regions. It handles heat and drought far better than true tarragon and rewards growers with cheerful golden flowers late in the season. Surface-sow seed indoors, as it needs light to germinate, and transplant to full sun after frost. Because the leaves are potent, use them sparingly, and harvest regularly to keep the plant bushy.

plant_note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; in zone 8–11 it overwinters and becomes a dependable perennial where French tarragon languishes in summer heat.
directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep in warm starting mix or propagate by stem cuttings, which root readily in moist perlite. Mexican tarragon thrives in the same hot, humid summers that kill French tarragon and delivers a very similar anise-like flavor; harvest foliage throughout the season by pinching stem tips. It blooms in fall with small golden-yellow flowers that attract butterflies — you can allow blooming or deadhead to maintain leaf production. In zone 9–11 the plant is perennial; cut back hard in early spring to stimulate fresh growth.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring 2–4 weeks before last frost; seeds germinate readily in cool soil and plants can handle light frost.
directions: Direct sow 1/4 in deep in early spring; milk thistle germinates readily in cool, moist conditions and self-seeds vigorously once established. Plants become quite large and spiny — space generously and wear gloves when tending them. Monitor flower heads closely as seeds ripen quickly and can be scattered by wind; harvest heads when the fluffy pappus begins to appear but before seeds fully disperse. Dry seed heads on a tray, then thresh to separate the hard achenes, which are ground or extracted for their silymarin content in liver-supportive preparations.

plant_note: Direct sow in cool spring soil for a cool-season crop.
directions: Grown as milky oats, this Avena sativa is harvested at the soft, immature seed stage prized in herbal wellness, and it also serves as a soil-building cover crop. Broadcast or drill the seed into cool, moist spring soil and rake it in, keeping the bed evenly watered until it establishes. It grows quickly in full sun and average ground, tolerating light frost. Watch the developing seed heads and gather the tops when they exude a milky sap for the traditional harvest.

plant_note: Sow directly in the garden 2–4 weeks before last frost in partial shade; can also be started indoors 4 weeks earlier.
directions: Sow seed shallowly on prepared soil surface and lightly cover with 1/8 inch of fine compost; seed requires light for germination and performs best in cool weather below 75°F. Plant in consistently moist soil in partial shade, as direct afternoon sun causes bolting and bitter leaves. The delicate, parsley-like leaves with a mild celery-anise flavor are used extensively in Japanese cuisine — in soups, salads, tempura, and as a garnish — and the blanched white stems are considered the most flavorful part. In zones 7 and warmer it behaves as a semi-evergreen perennial; treat as a cool-season annual in hotter climates by sowing in fall for winter harvest.

plant_note: Direct sow; grown mainly for its abundant coriander seed.
directions: Moroccan coriander is a fast-growing, four-in-one herb, giving a quick flush of leaves, edible white flowers for beneficial insects, and, above all, large aromatic seeds. It is especially valued for its heavy yield of round coriander seed used in baking and spice blends. Direct sow into cool soil since the taproot resists transplanting, and let plants flower and set seed for harvest. Provide full sun and even moisture, then cut seed heads once they turn tan and dry them fully before storing.

plant_note: Slow to germinate; soaking seed overnight speeds sprouting.
directions: This curled parsley forms dense, ruffled rosettes of deep green that garnish plates beautifully and pack in vitamins A, B, and C. Cold-tolerant, it can be grown as an annual that carries on through fall and into mild winters. Parsley seed is famously slow to sprout, so soak it overnight and be patient, keeping the soil consistently moist. Grow in full sun to part shade with steady water and harvest outer stems first to keep the plant producing.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring as the seed benefits from natural cold stratification or a brief refrigerator stratification period of 2–4 weeks.
directions: Direct sow in early spring or late autumn and allow natural freeze-thaw cycles to stratify the seed over winter for strong spring germination. Motherwort thrives in partial shade and moderately fertile, well-drained soil, often naturalizing along hedgerows and woodland margins. Harvest the upper third of flowering stems just as the lower flowers begin to open, using aerial parts fresh or tinctured in alcohol. Wear gloves when harvesting as the prickly calyces are sharp; avoid use during pregnancy due to its uterine-stimulating properties.

plant_note: Direct sow; cold stratification improves germination.
directions: Motherwort is a hardy mint-family perennial with an upright, bushy form and small purple flowers tucked among spiny bracts. A period of cold, moist conditions improves germination, so fall sowing or a chilling treatment before spring planting helps. It tolerates a wide range of soils in full sun to light shade and self-sows freely once established. Give it room at the back of a bed, as mature plants can reach several feet and attract bees and butterflies.

plant_note: Divide established clumps in early spring or plant nursery divisions around the last frost date; cold-stratified seed can also be sown indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost.
directions: Divide clumps by prying them apart with two forks and replant in moderately moist, average soil in full sun to light shade; mountain mint naturalizes readily and spreads by rhizomes, though less aggressively than true mints. The leaves and silvery-white flower clusters are intensely menthol-scented — far more potent than peppermint — and are excellent as a native tea herb. Harvest leafy stems and the clusters of tiny white flowers in midsummer just as blooming begins for the most aromatic result. The prolific flowers are exceptional for native bees and other pollinators, making this a valuable dual-purpose plant.

plant_note: Divide established clumps in early spring or plant root divisions around the last frost date; mugwort also self-seeds freely if flower stalks are allowed to mature.
directions: Plant root divisions in any well-drained soil; mugwort is extremely vigorous and can become invasive via rhizomes, so consider installing a root barrier or growing in a contained bed. The deeply lobed, silver-backed leaves are strongly aromatic with a bitter, sage-like scent used in traditional European and East Asian medicine. Harvest leafy stems just before flowering in midsummer for use in moxibustion, digestive bitters, dream pillows, and traditional holiday flavoring of fatty meats. Cut plants back hard in late summer to control spread and prevent self-seeding.

plant_note: Surface sow; tiny seed needs light to germinate.
directions: Mugwort is a vigorous aromatic perennial recognized by its silvery-green, deeply cut foliage and tolerance of poor, dry ground. Scatter the tiny seed on the surface and press it in, since it needs light and stays uncovered to sprout. It thrives in full sun with little supplemental water and spreads readily by rhizome, so site it where its enthusiasm is welcome or contain the roots. Cut plants back hard to control size and refresh the foliage.

plant_note: Surface sow; a biennial that flowers in its second year.
directions: Mullein is a striking hardy biennial that forms a rosette of soft, silver-green leaves the first year and sends up a tall yellow-flowered spike the second. Surface sow the dust-fine seed onto disturbed soil and leave it uncovered, as it needs light to germinate. It thrives in poor, well-drained ground and full sun with minimal water, making it ideal for lean sites. Allow a few plants to set seed and it will naturalize, returning on its own from year to year.

plant_note: Cold stratify seed, then surface sow indoors under light.
directions: Munstead is a compact, early-blooming English lavender prized for its sweet fragrance and tidy habit that suits containers and small gardens. Chill the seed for a few weeks, then surface sow indoors under light and be patient, as germination is slow and uneven. Transplant into sharply drained, even gritty soil in full sun, since lavender rots in wet ground. Prune lightly after flowering to keep plants dense and harvest the fragrant flower spikes for culinary or craft use.

plant_note: Cold-stratify seed for a few weeks before sowing to improve germination.
directions: Introduced in 1916 and named for Gertrude Jekyll's garden at Munstead Woods, this compact English lavender blooms earlier than most and stays neatly mounded. Its sweet flowers are among the best for culinary use as well as sachets and dried bouquets. Cold-stratify seed before sowing, then start indoors and transplant to full sun with sharp drainage. Keep water low once established, trim after flowering to maintain a tidy shape, and avoid rich or soggy soil.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions 2–3 inches deep in moist, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist, slightly acidic soil in partial to deep shade; myoga is one of the few edible gingers that actively prefers shade and performs poorly in full sun in hot climates.
directions: Plant rhizome divisions in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to deep shade; myoga is the only ginger species that genuinely prefers shade, making it one of the most valuable edible plants for the shaded food garden. The edible parts are the flower buds and young shoots that emerge directly from the ground in late summer and fall — they are sliced and used as a fresh garnish in Japanese cuisine with a mild ginger-citrus flavor. The tall, arching reed-like stems make attractive foliage throughout summer. In zone 7–11 the roots are winter-hardy and re-emerge each spring, slowly spreading to form a clump; divide every 3–4 years to maintain vigor. An outstanding shade-garden edible for Gulf Coast gardens where deep shade under trees is otherwise difficult to plant productively.

plant_note: Transplant only after soil warms and nights stay above 50 F.
directions: Noga basil balances heavy yields with classic Genovese flavor, producing tall, uniform plants that grow fast and stand up to repeated cutting. Its large, glossy, dark green leaves perform well in the field, greenhouse, or a patio container. Start seed indoors in warmth and transplant only after the soil has warmed thoroughly. Provide full sun, steady moisture, and frequent harvests, pinching out flower buds to keep leaf production high.

plant_note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring; spicebush is dioecious — plant both male and female shrubs for berry production. One male plant is sufficient to pollinate 4–5 female plants within 50 feet.
directions: Plant in moist, average to rich soil in partial to full shade; northern spicebush is one of the few large shrubs truly native to the forest understory, making it invaluable for shaded landscapes. The tiny yellow flowers in early spring appear before the leaves and provide early nectar for native bees; the brilliantly yellow fall foliage is outstanding. Bright red, oil-rich berries on female plants in fall are devoured by migratory thrushes and are the primary larval host plant for the spicebush swallowtail butterfly. The dried berries are edible with a complex allspice-bay-pepper flavor once used as a colonial-era spice — they can replace allspice in recipes. All parts are intensely aromatic when crushed.

plant_note: Start indoors in warmth; transplant after frost has passed.
directions: Opal basil is an ornamental sweet basil with glossy, deep violet leaves and delicate pink blooms that make it as decorative as it is flavorful. Start seed indoors about six weeks before the last frost, keeping the soil warm to trigger quick germination. Transplant into rich, well-drained soil in full sun once nights are reliably warm, spacing plants about a foot apart. Pinch flower buds to prolong leaf production and preserve the vivid purple color for garnishes and infused vinegars.

plant_note: Citrus-scented; propagate vegetatively.
directions: Propagate from cuttings to preserve the distinct orange-balsam fragrance of this thyme. The narrow grey-green leaves lend a citrusy note to poultry, fish, and marinades. Set plants in lean, fast-draining soil in full sun and keep water to a minimum. Shear lightly after bloom to maintain a tidy mound and prevent the base from going woody. Harvest sprigs throughout the season and protect crowns with gravel mulch over winter.

plant_note: Best grown from cuttings; contain roots.
directions: Propagate from stem cuttings or divisions, since named mints do not come true from seed. This citrusy peppermint variety perfumes teas, desserts, and cocktails with orange-bergamot notes. Plant in moist, rich soil in partial shade and confine roots to a buried pot or bottomless container. Water frequently and shear plants back after flowering to renew fresh leafy growth. Divide clumps every two or three years to keep the patch vigorous.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost and transplant after danger of frost has passed.
directions: Sow seeds on the surface of well-drained mix, pressing in lightly without covering since seeds need light to germinate. Plant in lean, gritty soil with excellent drainage; rich soil dilutes the essential oils that give oregano its pungency. Cut stems back by one-third after flowering to keep plants compact and productive. Harvest just as buds begin to open, when aromatic oil concentration is highest.

plant_note: Surface sow indoors; tiny seed needs light to germinate.
directions: Oregano is a hardy Mediterranean perennial with bold, aromatic leaves that are a cornerstone of Italian and Greek cooking. Surface sow the fine seed indoors and leave it uncovered under light, keeping it barely moist until sprouts appear. Transplant into lean, well-drained soil in full sun, where the plant develops its strongest flavor. Harvest sprigs regularly and shear plants back after flowering to keep them compact and productive for years.

plant_note: Plant rooted suckers or stem cuttings after last frost in a warm, sheltered position; requires consistently warm temperatures above 60°F.
directions: Propagate by removing rooted basal suckers from an established plant and potting into rich, moist, well-drained potting mix; cuttings without roots should be allowed to callous before planting. Pandan is a tropical Southeast Asian herb that rarely sets seed in cultivation and must be grown vegetatively; the long, strap-like leaves impart a distinctive vanilla-like, grassy fragrance used throughout Southeast Asian sweets, rice dishes, and drinks. Grow in bright indirect light or dappled shade with consistent moisture and high humidity; in temperate climates it makes an excellent houseplant or conservatory specimen. Harvest mature outer leaves by cutting at the base; knot or tie leaves before use in cooking to release their fragrance, or blend with water to make an extract.

plant_note: Direct sow at or just after last frost once soil has warmed to 65°F; papalo thrives in hot summers when cilantro fails.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep directly in the garden once soil is warm and all frost danger has passed; papalo does not transplant well due to its taproot. This Mexican culinary herb is used raw, added at the table rather than during cooking — heat destroys its unique flavor, which blends notes of cilantro, rue, and citrus. Grow in full sun in well-drained soil; it tolerates heat and moderate drought admirably, making it valuable as a summer substitute for cilantro. Harvest young leaves and stem tips regularly while the plant is in vegetative growth; use very sparingly as the flavor is more intense than cilantro.

plant_note: Direct sow or start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; parsley tolerates light spring frosts once established.
directions: Soak seeds overnight to soften the hull, then sow 1/4 in deep; germination is notoriously slow—up to four weeks. Thin to 6–9 in and keep soil consistently moist, as parsley bolts quickly under drought stress. Apply a light nitrogen feed at mid-season to sustain lush leaf production. Harvest outer stems at the base, leaving the inner crown intact to keep the plant productive.

plant_note: Sow early; soaking seed overnight speeds notoriously slow germination.
directions: Italian Giant is a robust flat-leaved parsley prized by cooks for its strong, fresh flavor and easy-to-chop leaves. Grown as an annual, it produces large, dark green leaves ideal for soups, stews, and garnishes. Sow seed directly or start indoors, keeping the soil consistently moist since germination is slow and can take up to three weeks. Give it full sun to part shade and harvest outer stems first so the plant keeps producing. It tolerates light frost and can be grown in containers on a sunny windowsill.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; surface-sow as seeds need light and keep soil consistently moist.
directions: Surface-sow seeds onto moist starting mix as they need light to germinate, keeping the medium evenly moist throughout germination. Pennyroyal spreads by creeping stems and works well as a fragrant ground cover in moist, partially shaded spots; it spreads less aggressively than other mints. The intensely peppermint-scented leaves are used fresh or dried as a traditional flea-repelling herb for pet bedding and as a strewing herb. Caution: pennyroyal oil is highly toxic and internal use carries serious risk — use the dried herb only in small quantities for tea and keep away from pregnant women and pets.

plant_note: Divide or transplant rooted runners in spring around the last frost date.
directions: Plant divisions or purchased starts in moist, rich soil and install a root barrier or grow in sunken containers, as peppermint spreads aggressively via underground stolons. Keep soil consistently moist and mulch to retain moisture during dry spells. Cut plants back to a few inches after the first flush of growth to encourage denser, more flavorful regrowth. Harvest stems before flower buds open, when menthol content is at its peak.

plant_note: Surface sow; seed needs light and warmth to germinate.
directions: Peppermint is a vigorous, cool-flavored perennial beloved for teas, desserts, and savory dishes. Press the fine seed onto the surface without covering, as it needs light to germinate, and keep it warm and evenly moist. It thrives in rich, consistently moist soil in full sun to part shade and spreads aggressively by runners, so grow it in a container or a contained bed. Harvest sprigs anytime and cut plants back before flowering to keep the foliage flavorful.

plant_note: Direct sow on the soil surface after last frost when soil is warm; shiso seed requires light to germinate — do not cover. Cold-stratification improves germination rate: chill seed at 40°F for 2 weeks before sowing.
directions: Surface sow on moist soil in full sun to partial shade after last frost; perilla (shiso) is the essential Japanese herb with large, ruffled leaves in green or deep purple-red that have a distinctive flavor combining cinnamon, anise, cloves, and mint — unique in the herbal palette. Green shiso (ao-jiso) wraps sushi, flavors salads, and garnishes sashimi; red/purple shiso (aka-jiso) colors umeboshi pickled plums and makes the vivid magenta shiso juice (yukari). Both forms are ornamentally striking, especially the 'Britton' frilly-leafed type. Plants self-seed extremely prolifically — thin seedlings carefully and pull unwanted volunteers before they go to seed, as it can naturalize aggressively in favorable climates.

plant_note: Transplant rooted cuttings outdoors after all frost danger has passed; in zones 8–11 it overwinters as a perennial shrub.
directions: Propagate from 4–6 in softwood cuttings in late spring or early summer, which root readily in moist perlite within 2–3 weeks; plants started from cuttings establish more quickly and reliably than seed. Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun and water consistently during summer; pineapple sage is less drought-tolerant than culinary sage. In zones below 8, take cuttings before the first autumn frost and overwinter plants on a bright, frost-free windowsill. Harvest the pineapple-scented leaves throughout summer for fruit salads, drinks, and tea; the brilliant red flowers bloom in autumn and attract hummingbirds.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions 2–3 inches deep in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial shade; pinecone ginger thrives in the warm, humid conditions of the Gulf Coast and produces its most spectacular inflorescences in the heat of summer and fall. In zone 9 it goes dormant in winter and regrows reliably in spring.
directions: Plant rhizomes in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; pinecone ginger (shampoo ginger, wild ginger) is a spectacular tropical ginger grown for its unusual inflorescences — tight, compact, cone-shaped flower heads that look exactly like green pinecones on thick stems, which mature from green to brilliant red as the season progresses. The small, yellow flowers emerge briefly from between the bracts. The ripe, red cones produce a fragrant, slightly slimy liquid when squeezed that has been used as a hair conditioner and shampoo in Pacific Island and Asian cultures for centuries — the liquid has a pleasant, gingery fragrance and actually conditions hair effectively. The rhizomes are edible with a slightly bitter, medicinal ginger flavor and are used in Southeast Asian cooking. In zone 9 the tall, bamboo-like stems die back completely in winter and regrow from rhizomes in spring, providing both edible, fragrant, and ornamental value throughout the warm season.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring in dry, sandy, well-drained soil in full sun; plains bee balm is an annual or short-lived perennial that self-seeds reliably in dry, open conditions — sow in masses on poor, sandy soil where it can naturalize without competition from taller plants.
