plant_note: Start indoors early; extremely cold-hardy for fall and winter harvest.
directions: American Flag is a cold-hardy heirloom leek with long white shanks, blue-green leaves, and a mild, sweet onion flavor that holds in the garden through fall and into winter. Start seed indoors 10-12 weeks before the last frost, then transplant into holes 6 inches deep to blanch the stems white. Hill soil up around the shanks as they grow to lengthen the tender white portion. Harvest as needed once shanks reach an inch across; the plants tolerate hard frost and can be dug even after snow.

plant_note: Plant tubers horizontally in a container of heavy clay soil and submerge in a pond or water garden after the water reaches 65°F; the curved tip of the tuber should point upward.
directions: Plant tubers in heavy clay-loam soil (not potting mix, which will float) in containers placed in 12–24 inches of water in full sun; the massive circular leaves and huge pale-yellow flowers up to 10 inches across are among the most spectacular sights in water gardening. All parts of this native aquatic plant are edible — the starchy rhizomes are eaten like potatoes, the seeds like nuts, and the young leaves and flower petals as vegetables. The large, sculptural seed heads remain on the plant through winter and are popular in dried floral arrangements. In a natural pond, the rhizomes can spread aggressively; use containers to control spread in small water features.

plant_note: Direct sow in mid to late summer for a fall harvest after frost sweetens roots.
directions: Sow seed 1/2 inch deep in loose, fertile soil about three months before your first fall frost, thinning to 6 inches apart. Roots grow 4 to 6 inches long with sweet, mellow yellow flesh that mashes like potatoes or adds body to soups and stews. The tender tops are also edible as sauteed greens. Rutabagas develop the best flavor in cool weather, so leave them in the ground until after a light frost before harvesting.

plant_note: Start indoors 4 to 6 weeks before last frost for spring, or midsummer for fall.
directions: Start seed indoors 4 to 6 weeks before transplanting, then set out sturdy seedlings in rich, evenly moist soil. This variety forms striking purple, domed heads that mature early and reliably in spring or fall and hold color best with steady moisture. Vigorous plants tolerate a range of climates and perform especially well in the Eastern US. Harvest heads while the curds are still tight, and enjoy the tender, flavorful florets steamed or roasted.

plant_note: Start indoors 4 to 6 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger passes.
directions: Start seed indoors 4 to 6 weeks before the last frost, keeping the mix warm for even germination. This indeterminate heirloom produces meaty, acorn-shaped red fruits of 8 to 12 ounces with few seeds, excellent for rich sauce and paste but also good for slicing. Provide a tall stake or trellis and prune suckers to manage the vigorous vines. Water consistently at the base to prevent cracking and blossom-end rot, and pick fruit as it colors fully.

plant_note: Start indoors, transplant after all frost danger.
directions: Amish Paste is a vigorous indeterminate heirloom yielding large, plum-to-oxheart-shaped fruits of 8-12 ounces with dense, richly flavored flesh ideal for sauce and canning. Start seeds indoors 6-8 weeks before the last frost and transplant deeply once nights are reliably warm. Provide tall stakes or a strong trellis and prune suckers to improve air flow, since the sprawling vines produce all season. Keep watering even and mulch well to reduce cracking and blossom-end rot on the heavy fruit.

plant_note: Start indoors with warmth; transplant well after frost.
directions: Anaheim, also called New Mexico chile, is a mild heirloom pepper producing long, tapered fruits with a smoky flavor prized for roasting, stuffing, and grilling. Start seed indoors 8-10 weeks before the last frost using bottom heat, since the seeds need warm soil to sprout. Transplant only after nights stay above 55F, spacing plants about 18-24 inches apart in full sun. Harvest green for chiles rellenos or leave fruit to ripen red for a sweeter, deeper flavor.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant 1–2 weeks after frost danger passes when soil is warm.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in warm mix at 80°F and provide bright light once germinated to prevent leggy growth; transplant after frost danger has completely passed. Anaheim peppers are mildly hot (500–2,500 Scoville units), long and tapered, and the classic pepper for green chile and chiles rellenos. Harvest green at 75 days for mild flavor, or allow pods to ripen to red for a sweeter, slightly hotter pepper that can be dried and ground into pure chile powder. Consistent watering and a light fertilizer application when flowers begin to set helps prevent blossom drop in heat.

plant_note: Start indoors 6 to 8 weeks before last frost; transplant once nights stay warm.
directions: Start seed indoors 6 to 8 weeks before the last frost, keeping the mix at 75 to 90 degrees since peppers germinate slowly in cool soil. Transplant into warm, well-drained soil after nights are reliably above 55 degrees. The 3 to 6 inch green poblano is excellent stuffed for chiles rellenos, while the dried, reddish-brown ancho anchors sauces like mole poblano, with mild heat around 1,000 to 2,000 Scoville units. Harvest green for fresh use or leave fruit to ripen red for drying.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost danger passes and soil has warmed.
directions: Sow seed 1 inch deep in warm, fertile soil once frost danger has passed, spacing hills to give vines room to run. Prolific plants yield up to fifteen uniform fruits weighing 1.5 to 2 pounds each, sized perfectly for serving on the half-shell. When cooked, the flesh forks away in tender spaghetti-like strands. Cure harvested fruit in a warm, dry spot for a week or two to improve storage life, and keep on the vine until the rind hardens.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost danger passes and soil has warmed to at least 65 degrees.
directions: Sow seed 1 inch deep directly in warm soil, since beans resent transplanting and rot in cold ground. This vigorous variety produces 5 to 5.5 inch pods that stay tender and crisp even as the season heats up. Sturdy, upright plants hold pods off the soil for clean, easy picking and do well in containers. Harvest frequently while pods are young and snap crisply to keep plants producing over a long window.

plant_note: Direct sow around the last frost, or grow indoors year-round for baby greens.
directions: Sow seed 1/2 inch deep and thin lightly, or broadcast densely for a cut-and-come-again baby-greens crop. This chard blend produces colorful young leaves ideal in salads or steamed, stir-fried, and added to soups, omelets, and lasagna. It can be grown indoors any time of year for a fresh winter harvest. Snip outer leaves when a few inches tall and the plants will regrow for repeated cuttings.

plant_note: Start indoors early; transplant crowns to a permanent bed.
directions: Argenteuil is an award-winning French heirloom asparagus with tender, sweet purple-and-green spears tipped in pink, valued for early, heavy yields. Start seed indoors 10-12 weeks before the last frost, soaking seed first to speed the slow germination, then transplant seedlings to a deeply prepared permanent bed. Wait until the third spring to begin cutting spears so the crowns can build strong roots, and let ferns grow all summer to feed the plants. A well-tended bed remains productive for 15 to 20 years.

plant_note: Botanically a melon; needs heat and a long warm season.
directions: Though eaten like a cucumber, this is actually a ribbed, pale-green melon with tender skin that never needs peeling. Direct sow in warm soil after frost or start in pots, and trellis the long vines to keep fruit straight. Provide steady moisture and full sun for the sweetest, most tender fruit. Harvest at 12 to 18 inches while still slender, since oversized fruit turns seedy. Regular picking keeps the vine setting new fruit through the heat of summer.

plant_note: Direct sow after soil warms; heat-loving vine.
directions: Armenian Pale Green is actually a heat-tolerant melon grown like a cucumber, producing long, ribbed, pale-skinned fruits that stay crisp and mild without bitterness. Direct-sow after the soil warms to at least 70F, planting seeds about an inch deep, and thin or transplant to 12-18 inches apart. Grow on a trellis to keep the long fruits straight and clean, harvesting at 12-18 inches while the skin is still tender. Because the thin skin needs no peeling, these are excellent for fresh salads and quick pickles.

plant_note: Plant stem cuttings (side shoots from the crown) in spring in well-drained, deeply cultivated, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; arracacha requires 9–12 months from planting to harvest and performs best where temperatures range 57–75°F — it struggles in heat above 85°F and is best suited to mild coastal or highland climates in zones 8–10.
directions: Plant stem cuttings in spring in deeply cultivated, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; arracacha is one of the most important root crops of the South American Andes and is gaining attention among adventurous food gardeners in mild climates for its exceptional flavor — the white to cream-colored roots have a starchy texture and a flavor that combines the best qualities of celery, carrot, and parsnip with a slightly sweet, buttery richness. The roots are boiled, roasted, fried, or used in soups; they do not store well after harvest and are best consumed within a few days. The plant resembles a flat-leafed parsley or celery relative with compound leaves and naturally produces multiple offsets from the crown that can be replanted as cuttings to propagate. It thrives in the cool, humid conditions of zones 8–10 coastal areas and mild highlands where temperatures remain moderate — it is not well suited to the extreme summer heat of the Gulf Coast interior but performs in coastal areas and in partial shade.

plant_note: Plant tubers or potted divisions in shallow water (1–6 inches deep) or at the margin of a pond or rain garden in full sun to partial shade once water temperature reaches 60°F.
directions: Plant tubers or divisions in shallow water (1–6 inches deep) or in saturated soil at a pond or stream margin in full to partial sun; arrowhead is a native aquatic perennial with distinctive arrow-shaped leaves, three-petaled white flowers in midsummer, and underwater starchy tubers (called wapato or katniss) that were a critically important food source for many Native American peoples across North America, harvested by wading and loosening the tubers with the feet in fall when starches are concentrated. The tubers are nutritious and delicious when boiled or roasted like small potatoes. The plant also provides outstanding wetland habitat — frogs shelter in the leaves, diving ducks (especially mallards and teals) eat the tubers, and shorebirds and herons hunt at its margins. In favorable conditions it spreads aggressively; contain in submerged containers if space is limited.

plant_note: Plant rhizome sections 2–3 inches deep in moist, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil after last frost; in zone 8 and south, rhizomes can overwinter in the ground under mulch — harvest mature rhizomes in fall when foliage begins to die back.
directions: True arrowroot is a tropical perennial native to the Caribbean and northern South America, cultivated for its slender, white rhizomes that produce the purest, most digestible starch of any root crop — genuine arrowroot starch is almost flavorless, dissolves crystal-clear in cooking (unlike cornstarch which turns cloudy), and is prized in gluten-free baking and as a thickener for delicate sauces, jellies, and gravies. In Gulf Coast gardens, it grows vigorously as a shade-tolerant understory crop under fruit trees and along the edges of covered patios — one of the few starchy root vegetables that thrives in partial shade, making it ideal for interplanting beneath taller tropical crops. Harvest slender, finger-like rhizomes 10–12 months after planting when the leafy tops begin to yellow; the rhizomes can be eaten boiled or baked, or processed into starch by grinding, washing, and drying. Start rhizome sections each spring; in frost-free areas it grows as a true perennial, expanding into spreading clumps.

common_name: Artisan Bumble Bee Blend Pole Cherry Tomato
plant_note: Start indoors 4 to 6 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger passes.
directions: Start seed indoors 4 to 6 weeks before the last frost and transplant deeply once nights stay warm. This blend combines Pink, Purple, and Sunrise Bumble Bee cherry tomatoes, each striped and no two alike, sweet and tangy right off the vine. The indeterminate plants grow large and yield heavily, so provide sturdy support and prune suckers for airflow. Water evenly to reduce cracking, and harvest fruit as it colors fully throughout the season.

plant_note: Succession sow spring and fall; bolts quickly in heat.
directions: Arugula is a fast-growing, cool-season green with a bold, peppery bite that is delicious in salads, on pizza, and lightly wilted. Direct-sow shallowly every two to three weeks from early spring through fall for a steady supply of tender leaves. Harvest young outer leaves cut-and-come-again, and cut the whole plant before hot weather triggers bolting and a sharper flavor. In warm regions grow it in partial shade or indoors to extend the harvest window.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost; succession sow every 2–3 weeks through spring and again in fall.
directions: Broadcast or sow in rows at 1/4 in depth in cool soil; arugula is one of the fastest salad greens to harvest. Thin to 4–6 in for full-sized leaves, or leave thicker for baby leaf harvests. Cut-and-come-again harvests are possible but quality drops quickly in warm weather as leaves become very pungent and plants bolt. Baby leaves at 2–3 in are mild; let them grow to 4–6 in for classic peppery flavor.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring or late summer, or grow indoors year-round.
directions: Broadcast seed thickly about 1/4 inch deep for a dense baby-greens crop, sowing every couple of weeks for a steady supply. Peppery leaves are rich in vitamin C and potassium and taste great with balsamic and Parmesan. This crop grows quickly in cool weather and can be raised indoors year-round for fresh winter greens. Cut leaves when 2 to 3 inches tall and the bed will often regrow for a second harvest.

plant_note: Grown indoors year-round; sow densely on a shallow tray of moist medium.
directions: Broadcast the tiny seeds thickly and evenly across a moist tray of soil or a microgreen mat, then keep covered for the first two days to speed germination. Sprouts appear within a few days and grow best in bright indirect light at room temperature. Mist gently to keep the surface moist without waterlogging, and harvest with scissors once the first true leaves show, usually around a week to ten days. The peppery, mustard-like bite is at its best when cut young and eaten fresh.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring and again in late summer for fall harvest.
directions: Sow seed 1/4 inch deep in cool weather and thin for full-size plants, or leave dense for baby greens ready in about 20 days. The peppery, mustardy leaves brighten salads and are also good steamed or on pizza. Grow in full sun to part shade and keep soil moist to slow bolting, which turns leaves bitter and hot in heat. Succession sow every two to three weeks in spring and fall for a continuous harvest.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring or fall; this perennial-type arugula grows slowly.
directions: Sow seed 1/4 inch deep in cool soil; wild rocket grows more slowly than common arugula but offers a stronger, more complex peppery flavor. Harvest as baby greens or let plants reach full size for salads, pizza, or pesto. It grows well in containers and tolerates part shade, and the deeply lobed leaves regrow after cutting. Keep soil evenly moist and harvest regularly to keep leaves tender and slow flowering.

plant_note: Start indoors 4 to 6 weeks before last frost for spring, or midsummer for fall.
directions: Start seed indoors 4 to 6 weeks before setting out sturdy transplants into rich, evenly moist soil. This hybrid combines the mild, sweet flavor of broccoli with tender, asparagus-like stems and matures quickly. After the first central shoots, plants keep producing slender side shoots for multiple harvests. Cut stems when buds are still tight and harvest often to encourage continued production; steam or stir-fry the whole tender stem.

plant_note: Plant one-year-old crowns as soon as soil is workable in spring; dig 8-in-deep trenches and set crowns 18 in apart.
directions: Set one-year crowns in 8-in-deep trenches with roots spread over a ridge of compost, then gradually backfill as growth emerges. Do not harvest any spears in the first year and harvest lightly in year two to let the root system build strength. Beginning in year three, cut or snap spears at soil level when they are 6–8 in tall and before the tips begin to feather open. Stop harvesting after 4–6 weeks to allow ferns to grow and replenish the crown for the next season.

plant_note: Direct sow after soil is thoroughly warm; loves heat.
directions: This heat-loving Asian cowpea is a vigorous pole bean that produces slender pods up to a meter long, best picked young at 12-18 inches for tender eating. Direct-sow after the soil has thoroughly warmed to 70F or more, planting an inch deep at the base of a tall, sturdy trellis. The climbing vines thrive in hot, humid weather where common beans struggle and keep producing with regular harvest. Pick pods frequently while still smooth and firm to encourage continued set.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring or late summer for a long, cool-season harvest.
directions: Sow seed 1/4 inch deep in cool soil and thin for full plants or leave dense for baby greens ready in about a month. This variety has wider leaves and is slower to bolt than other arugulas, giving more leaf per harvest and a milder, peppery flavor. Keep soil moist and grow in part shade during warm spells to delay bolting. Cut outer leaves regularly, and succession sow for a steady supply through spring and fall.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost; very long season, start indoors in short-season areas.
directions: Atlantic Giant is the classic record-breaking pumpkin, capable of producing fruits weighing hundreds of pounds under ideal care. Sow seed after all frost danger in deeply amended, compost-rich soil, giving each plant sprawling room of 10 feet or more. For the largest fruit, allow only one or two pumpkins per vine and remove the rest, then feed and water generously all season. Support the growing fruit on sand or a board to prevent rot, and protect the tender vines from any late frost.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost danger passes; this giant needs a very long, warm season.
directions: Sow seed 1 inch deep in warm, deeply enriched soil and give each plant a very large area to sprawl. Under ideal conditions Atlantic Giant can reach record-breaking size, and it is the classic choice of competitive growers. For maximum size, allow just one pumpkin per vine, feed heavily, and water generously and consistently. Even without a record, you will have plenty of flesh for pies and soups or a dramatic carving pumpkin.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring once soil is workable, with successions every three weeks.
directions: Sow seed 1/4 inch deep in loose, stone-free soil and keep the bed evenly moist until the slow seedlings emerge. This imperator-type carrot grows 8 to 10 inches long with a brilliant deep-red exterior and orange core, and roasting or steaming intensifies the color contrast. Thin seedlings to 2 to 3 inches so roots size up well. Sow successive crops every three weeks for a continuous supply of these colorful, sweet, crisp carrots.

plant_note: Start indoors, transplant after frost danger passes.
directions: Aunt Ruby's German Green is an indeterminate heirloom beefsteak that ripens to a green-and-amber blush and offers a sweet, slightly spicy, well-balanced flavor. Start seeds indoors 6-8 weeks before the last frost and transplant deeply once the weather is settled and warm. Cage or stake the tall vines and prune suckers for airflow, since the large fruits can reach a pound each. Judge ripeness by a slight softening and yellow-green color rather than by red, harvesting before the fruit becomes overly soft.

plant_note: Direct sow or start indoors in early spring, and again in late summer for fall.
directions: Sow seed just 1/8 inch deep and barely cover, since lettuce needs light to germinate and prefers cool soil. This easy variety forms bright green leaves dusted with iridescent bronze tips and combines the buttery texture of a butterhead with the upright habit of a mini romaine. Keep soil consistently moist and provide afternoon shade in warm weather to delay bolting. Harvest outer leaves as needed or cut the whole head once it fills out.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring and again in late summer; heat can trigger bolting.
directions: Sow seed 1/4 inch deep in cool, fertile soil and thin to about 5 inches apart, or grow in containers. This Asian green, also called pak choi, is ready in about 35 days when harvested at 5 to 6 inches tall for tender baby choi. It resists bolting and makes an attractive addition to mixed containers. Keep soil evenly moist to prevent bolting, and harvest whole heads or outer stalks for stir-fries and marinated salads.

plant_note: Sow in early spring or late summer; cold improves flavor.
directions: Sow this blend of curly, flat-leaf, and oak-leaf kales thickly in a band for baby-leaf harvests, or thin to 6 inches for full-size plants. Direct-seed 1/4 inch deep as soon as soil can be worked in spring, and again in late summer for a fall crop. Cut outer leaves at 3-4 inches for tender salad greens, harvesting cut-and-come-again over several weeks. Cold weather and light frost sweeten the leaves, so this mix carries well into winter in mild zones.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in warm starting mix and keep at 75–80°F until sprouts appear. Transplant to the garden after last frost and water evenly to maintain steady growth and prevent fruit splitting. Banana peppers are mild and productive; pick fruits at 4–6 inches long when pale yellow for the mildest flavor. Leaving fruits to mature to orange or red increases both sweetness and slight heat.

plant_note: Plant sets pointed tip up with the neck just above soil.
directions: A large elongated shallot combining the size of an onion with a shallot's refined flavor. Plant individual sets in early spring with the tip barely showing, spacing them 6 inches apart. Unlike clustering shallots, banana shallots often size up as a single large bulb, so give each room. Keep weeded and evenly watered until tops begin to yellow in midsummer. Lift, cure thoroughly, and store the papery bulbs in a cool dry place for months.

plant_note: Start indoors 4 to 6 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger passes.
directions: Start seed indoors 4 to 6 weeks before the last frost and keep the mix warm for even sprouting. This century-old indeterminate heirloom produces thick, meaty slices prized for sandwiches and any dish wanting rich tomato flavor. Vines are extremely vigorous, reaching 6 feet or more, so provide a sturdy cage or wire support and prune suckers for airflow. Water evenly at the base to prevent cracking and blossom-end rot, and harvest fruit fully colored.

plant_note: Start indoors, transplant after frost danger passes.
directions: Beefsteak is the classic large-fruited slicing tomato, an indeterminate heirloom producing meaty red fruits around four inches across that are perfect for sandwiches and burgers. Start seed indoors 6-8 weeks before the last frost and set out sturdy transplants after nights warm past 50F, burying the stem deeply to root along its length. Support the tall vines with strong cages or stakes and prune lower suckers to improve air circulation. Keep soil evenly moist and mulched to avoid cracking and blossom-end rot on the heavy fruit.

plant_note: Start indoors 4 to 6 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger passes.
directions: Start seed indoors 4 to 6 weeks before the last frost and transplant deeply once nights stay warm. This determinate type delivers a rich, umami-packed flavor that finishes with smoky, fire-roasted notes, ripening most of its crop over a concentrated window. Stake or cage the compact bushes to keep fruit clean and support the yield. Water consistently at the base to avoid cracking, and harvest tomatoes when fully colored and slightly soft.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before the last frost; make a second sowing in late summer for a fall crop.
directions: Sow beet clusters (each is a multi-seeded fruit) 1/2 in deep in rows 12 in apart. Because each corky seed can produce 2–3 seedlings, thin early to 3–4 in to avoid crowding, and use the thinnings as salad greens. Maintain even soil moisture to prevent woody, cracked roots. Harvest globe roots at 1.5–3 in diameter; larger roots become pithy and lose sweetness.

plant_note: Grown indoors on trays; soak the corky seed clusters before sowing to improve germination.
directions: Presoak the multigerm seed balls for several hours so they sprout more evenly, then sow them densely across a moist tray. Beet microgreens germinate a little slower than brassicas, so keep them covered and warm until shoots emerge, then move to bright light to develop their deep red stems. Water carefully from below or with a fine mist to avoid crushing the delicate seedlings. Harvest the striking crimson-stemmed greens at the cotyledon or first-true-leaf stage for their mild, earthy sweetness.

plant_note: Direct sow after soil warms; can start indoors 3 weeks early.
directions: Beit Alpha is a burpless, thin-skinned heirloom cucumber that is parthenocarpic, setting smooth, mild fruit without pollination and free of bitterness. Direct-sow after the soil warms to 70F, or start indoors three weeks early, and grow up a trellis to keep fruit straight and clean. Harvest at 4-6 inches while the skin is deep green and tender, picking often to keep the vines producing. Because no peeling is needed, these cucumbers are ideal for fresh salads and snacking.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant 2 weeks after frost danger has passed.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in warm starting mix; peppers germinate best when soil stays above 75°F. Harden off transplants over 7–10 days before planting, and wait until soil temperature reaches at least 65°F. Keep moisture consistent to prevent fruit drop, and apply a balanced fertilizer once flowering begins. Pick green for mild flavor or leave on the plant to ripen to red, yellow, or orange for maximum sweetness.

plant_note: Start indoors 4 to 6 weeks before last frost for spring, or midsummer for fall.
directions: Start seed indoors 4 to 6 weeks before setting out transplants into rich, evenly moist soil. Selected for heat tolerance, this variety suits both spring and late-summer sowings and forms large, 6 to 8 inch, tightly packed blue-green heads. After the main head is cut, many smaller side shoots follow for continued harvests, and it grows well through winter in mild climates. Cut the central head while buds are tight, then keep harvesting side shoots.

plant_note: Start indoors 4 to 6 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger passes.
directions: Start seed indoors 4 to 6 weeks before the last frost and keep the mix warm for even germination. This hybrid crosses Big Dwarf with Brandywine to yield dark pink, ribbed fruits of 12 to 15 ounces with rich flavor and productive, nearly blemish-free yields. Provide a tall stake or trellis for the indeterminate vines and prune suckers for airflow. Water evenly at the base to prevent cracking, and harvest tomatoes when fully colored.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost danger passes; needs a long, warm growing season.
directions: Sow seed 1 inch deep in warm, richly amended soil and give each vine ample space to run. This heirloom is a favorite of giant-pumpkin growers, producing round fruits with smooth, light-orange skin well suited to Halloween carving and decorating. For maximum size, grow one pumpkin per vine and water and feed generously through the season. Leave fruit on the vine until the rind hardens, then cut with a length of stem attached.

plant_note: Start indoors with warmth; transplant well after frost.
directions: Big Red is a sweet heirloom bell pepper with thick walls and large, blocky three-to-four-lobed fruits that ripen from green to deep red and grow sweeter as they color. Start seed indoors 8-10 weeks before the last frost with bottom heat, and transplant only after nights stay above 55F. Space plants 18-24 inches apart in full sun and stake taller plants to keep the heavy fruit off the ground. Pick green for a crisp, mild flavor or leave on the plant to ripen fully red for maximum sweetness.

plant_note: Direct sow in late summer or fall; extremely cold-hardy for winter harvest.
directions: Sow seed 1/4 inch deep in cool soil in late summer or fall, since mache germinates best in cool conditions. This big-seeded variety tolerates heat better than others without turning bitter and is also very cold-hardy, often surviving to about 10 degrees. Enjoy the tender rosettes alone as a salad, mixed with other greens, or lightly cooked like spinach. Harvest whole rosettes at the base as needed through cool and cold weather.

plant_note: Start indoors; needs a long, hot growing season.
directions: Bitter melon, also called bitter gourd or karela, is a tropical climbing vine grown for its warty green fruit widely used in Asian cooking. Soak the hard seeds overnight and start indoors 4-6 weeks before the last frost, since the plant demands a long, hot season. Transplant to full sun after the soil is warm and train the vigorous vines up a strong trellis. Harvest fruit young and green for the mildest flavor, picking often since the gourds turn orange and split when overripe.

plant_note: Start indoors 2–4 weeks before last frost; transplant after soil warms to 75°F — the hotter the summer, the better it performs.
directions: Nick the seed coat or soak seeds 24 hours before sowing to improve germination; sow 1/2 in deep in warm starting mix at 80–85°F. Provide a trellis at least 6 feet tall — the vines climb by tendrils and produce more fruit when grown vertically. Hand-pollinate by transferring pollen from male to female flowers (female has a tiny immature fruit at the base) using a soft brush to improve fruit set in low-insect conditions. Harvest when fruits are still firm, bright green, and about 4–6 in long; fully ripe fruits turn orange-yellow and split open, revealing red-coated seeds that birds relish but the bitter flesh is no longer palatable.

plant_note: Start indoors with warmth; transplant well after frost.
directions: Black Beauty is the classic heirloom eggplant, producing large, glossy deep-purple fruits with tender, mild flesh perfect for grilling and roasting. Start seed indoors 8-10 weeks before the last frost with warm soil, and transplant only after nights reliably stay above 55F. Space plants 18-24 inches apart in full sun and stake them to support the weight of the fruit. Harvest while the skin is firm and glossy, cutting rather than pulling; picking regularly keeps the plants productive.

plant_note: Start indoors 6 to 8 weeks before last frost; transplant once nights stay warm.
directions: Start seed indoors 6 to 8 weeks before the last frost, keeping the mix at 75 to 90 degrees for reliable germination. This standard-setting 1902 heirloom produces large, high-quality fruit with tender skin that needs no peeling, and its lavender blossoms are ornamental. Transplant into warm, well-drained soil after nights stay above 55 degrees. Harvest fruit while the skin is glossy and firm, cutting with pruners since stems are tough, and pick regularly to keep plants productive.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost danger passes and soil has warmed.
directions: Sow seed 1 inch deep in warm, fertile soil once frost danger has passed, thinning to the strongest plants per hill. This highly productive bush-type zucchini from the 1920s takes little space and yields dark green, glossy fruits with tender skin. Harvest fruit young, around 6 to 8 inches, for the best texture and to keep plants producing. Pick every day or two at peak season, as fruit sizes up fast and overgrown squash slows further set.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost when soil is warm.
directions: Black Beauty is a dependable, prolific heirloom zucchini bearing glossy dark-green fruit on compact, bushy plants. Direct-sow two or three seeds per hill after the soil warms to 70F, then thin to the strongest seedling, spacing hills two to three feet apart. Harvest zucchini young at 6-8 inches for the best texture, and pick every day or two at peak season to keep the plant producing. Water evenly at the base and mulch to reduce disease and keep fruit clean.

plant_note: Start indoors, transplant after frost danger passes.
directions: Black Cherry is an indeterminate heirloom producing clusters of round, dark mahogany cherry tomatoes with a sweet, rich, and complex flavor. Start seed indoors 6-8 weeks before the last frost and transplant sturdy seedlings once nights stay above 50F, setting them deep. Give the vigorous vines a tall cage or trellis and prune suckers for airflow, since they can top six feet. Harvest when fruit turns deep purple-brown and gives slightly to the touch; frequent picking keeps the clusters coming all season.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost danger passes and soil has warmed.
directions: Sow seed 1 inch deep in warm soil once frost danger has passed, giving vines room to sprawl. These dramatic, deeply ribbed pumpkins darken as they mature until nearly black and weigh only 1 to 2 pounds, making tidy personal-sized decorations. Group several together for striking fall displays. Leave fruit on the vine until the rind hardens and color fully develops, then cut with a bit of stem attached for the best keeping quality.

plant_note: Sow spring and late summer; very cold-hardy.
directions: Black Knight tatsoi is a heirloom Asian green forming tidy rosettes of dark, spoon-shaped leaves with a sweet, mild mustard flavor. Direct-sow shallowly in early spring and again in late summer, thinning plants to 6-10 inches for full rosettes or leaving them close for baby greens. Harvest outer leaves cut-and-come-again, or cut the whole rosette; the plant tolerates hard frost and sweetens in cold weather. It grows quickly and does well in containers and cold frames for winter harvests.

plant_note: Start indoors 4 to 6 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger passes.
directions: Start seed indoors 4 to 6 weeks before the last frost and transplant deeply once nights stay warm. This Russian heirloom produces baseball-sized, 10 to 12 ounce fruits with reddish-brown flesh and a rich, slightly salty flavor, and it sets fruit well in heat. Provide a sturdy stake or cage for the indeterminate vines and prune suckers for airflow. Water evenly to reduce cracking, and harvest when shoulders take on their characteristic dusky color.

plant_note: Start indoors, transplant after frost danger passes.
directions: Black Krim is a Crimean heirloom beefsteak bearing large, dark purple-brown fruits with a rich, full-bodied, faintly smoky and salty flavor. Start seed indoors 6-8 weeks before the last frost and transplant deeply once nights are reliably warm. Support the indeterminate vines with a strong cage or stake and prune suckers for air movement to reduce cracking in the shoulders. Harvest when the fruit develops its dark color and gives slightly; the color deepens in hot weather and sun.

plant_note: Sow 8–10 weeks before first fall frost; black radish stores well into winter and is best harvested after light frosts improve flavor.
directions: Sow seeds 1/2 inch deep in loose, deeply prepared, fertile soil in late summer for fall harvest; thin seedlings to 6–8 inches to allow the large roots to develop fully. Black radish is an ancient European winter storage root with rough, jet-black skin and crisp, pungent white flesh — significantly hotter than spring radishes and reminiscent of strong horseradish. Harvest after light frost, which mellows the intense pungency; roots can reach 3–4 inches in diameter and 4–6 inches long. The robust flavor is best appreciated when the flesh is grated raw into salads with sour cream, pickled in brine, or cooked in soups where the heat moderates pleasantly.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring and again in late summer for a fall crop.
directions: Sow seed just 1/8 inch deep and barely cover, since lettuce needs light to germinate in cool soil. A garden favorite since 1850, this loose-leaf variety yields full-sized, frilly leaves in just over a month and baby greens in about three weeks. It withstands hot, dry conditions and light frosts better than many other lettuces. Harvest outer leaves as needed for a cut-and-come-again supply, or cut the whole plant before it bolts in heat.

plant_note: Succession sow spring and fall; light aids germination.
directions: Black Seeded Simpson is a fast, reliable heirloom loose-leaf lettuce with tender, light-green ruffled leaves and a mild, crisp flavor. Direct-sow shallowly, barely covering the seed since light aids germination, and thin plants to 6-10 inches apart. Harvest outer leaves cut-and-come-again for a prolonged supply, and succession-sow every two weeks from early spring through fall. Provide afternoon shade in hot weather to delay bolting; it also grows well in containers and hydroponics.

plant_note: Direct sow 1–2 inches deep in well-drained, warm, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun after last frost and when soil reaches 60°F; black turtle beans perform as either bush or climbing types depending on variety. For dry bean production, harvest when 90% of pods have turned brown and dry.
directions: Sow 1–2 inches deep in warm, well-drained soil in full sun; black turtle beans are the standard black bean of Latin American, Caribbean, and American cuisine — the dense, shiny black seeds are rich in protein (15g per cup) and antioxidant anthocyanins, used in black bean soup, rice and beans, burritos, tacos, and countless other dishes. Both bush and pole varieties are available; the most common home-garden varieties include 'Black Valentine' (bush) and 'Black Coco' (bush). Allow pods to dry completely on the plant for dry bean harvest — seeds should rattle in the pod. Thresh by spreading dried plants on a tarp and walking on them, then winnow. In zone 9a, plant in spring for a late-summer dry bean harvest; the plants fix atmospheric nitrogen and improve soil for subsequent crops.

plant_note: Direct sow after soil is warm; thrives in heat.
directions: Black-eyed peas are a heat-tolerant cowpea grown for their nutritious seeds and their ability to fix nitrogen and improve soil. Direct-sow after the soil warms to 70F, planting an inch deep and thinning to 3-6 inches apart, since the plants dislike transplanting. They tolerate heat and drought well, so water moderately and avoid overly rich soil, which favors foliage over pods. Harvest young pods for fresh snap peas or leave them to dry on the plant for shelling and storage.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks after last frost when soil is thoroughly warm; cowpeas are more heat- and drought-tolerant than common beans.
directions: Sow seeds 1 in deep in warm, well-drained soil; black-eyed peas tolerate poor, sandy soils and summer drought better than most vegetables. Avoid over-fertilizing with nitrogen, as they fix their own from the atmosphere. Harvest immature pods at 3–4 in for snap use, or allow pods to swell fully and turn pale tan for fresh shell peas; dry the remaining pods completely for storage as dried beans.

plant_note: Sow early spring and fall; germinates in cool soil, bolts in heat.
directions: Bloomsdale is a cold-hardy heirloom spinach with heavily crinkled, deep-green leaves and a sweet, mild flavor that stands up well in cool weather. Direct-sow in early spring as soon as the soil can be worked and again in late summer, since seed germinates poorly in warm soil. Thin plants to 4-6 inches apart and harvest outer leaves cut-and-come-again, or cut whole plants before hot weather triggers bolting. In mild zones a fall planting under cover can overwinter for an early spring harvest.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring as soon as soil is workable, or again in late summer.
directions: Sow seed 1/2 inch deep in cool soil in early spring or late summer, thinning to give each plant room. This dependable open-pollinated variety from the early 1800s has crinkled, savoyed leaves and can be harvested as tender baby greens in as little as 28 days. It performs well in the ground or in containers and tolerates part shade. Cut outer leaves as needed or harvest whole plants before warm weather triggers bolting.

plant_note: Plant individual cloves in fall a few weeks before the ground freezes.
directions: Separate bulbs into cloves at planting and set each clove pointed end up, 2 inches deep, in fall a few weeks before the ground freezes. This Turban-type hardneck forms large, plump bulbs with violet-streaked wrappers and 8 to 10 easy-peel cloves. Mulch after planting to protect roots over winter, and cut off the curling scapes in early summer to direct energy into the bulbs. Harvest when lower leaves brown, then cure in a dry, airy place before storing.

plant_note: Direct sow after all frost danger has passed and soil has warmed; a drought-tolerant heirloom flour corn.
directions: Sow seeds about 1 inch deep in blocks of at least four short rows rather than a single long row to ensure good wind pollination. This Hopi heirloom tolerates dry, hot conditions better than most corn, so avoid overwatering once established. Allow the ears to dry fully on the stalk before harvesting the deep-blue kernels for grinding into flour or cornmeal.

plant_note: Direct sow into warm soil after frost; an heirloom storage pumpkin with slate blue-gray skin.
directions: Plant seeds an inch deep in hills once the soil is thoroughly warm, thinning to the two strongest vines per hill. Give these sprawling plants plenty of room and a steady supply of water and compost to size up the deeply ribbed fruits. Cure the blue-gray pumpkins in a warm, dry spot after harvest for long winter storage and sweet, dense flesh.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost; a compact bush type producing stringless green beans without support.
directions: Sow seeds about an inch deep directly in the garden once frost has passed, since beans resent transplanting. These compact bushes need no trellis and set a heavy, concentrated crop of straight, tender pods. Pick often while the pods are young and stringless to encourage continued production, and consider a second sowing for a fall harvest.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost danger passes and soil has warmed to at least 65 degrees.
directions: Sow seed 1 inch deep directly in warm soil, since beans dislike transplanting and rot in cold ground. This variety produces a very large concentrated crop of round, 6 inch pods on 16 to 18 inch bushy, disease-resistant plants. Because it crops all at once, sow in successions two weeks apart for a steady supply. Harvest pods while young and tender before the seeds swell, and pick frequently to encourage more pods.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost danger passes and soil has warmed to at least 65 degrees.
directions: Sow seed 1 inch deep at the base of a sturdy trellis or pole in warm soil once frost danger has passed. Vigorous 5 to 8 foot vines are high-yielding and produce a bounty of 5 to 7 inch, white-seeded pods excellent fresh, canned, or frozen. Provide strong support before the vines begin to climb. Harvest pods while young and tender, picking every few days to keep the vines producing over a long season.

plant_note: Direct sow after last frost when soil reaches at least 65 degrees.
directions: Sow seed 1 inch deep in warm soil, planting in blocks of several short rows rather than one long row to ensure good pollination. This sugar-enhanced hybrid produces 8-inch ears packed with 16 rows of juicy golden kernels that hold their sweetness well after harvest. Sturdy stalks carry a strong disease package for consistent yields. Feed with nitrogen and water deeply during tasseling and ear fill, and harvest when silks brown and kernels are plump and milky.

plant_note: Direct sow around last frost date in spring; for fall harvest, sow 6–8 weeks before first frost.
directions: Direct sow seed 1/4 in deep in fertile, moisture-retentive soil and thin to 6–12 in depending on whether you want baby or full-sized heads. Provide consistent moisture and feed lightly with nitrogen; drought stress and heat both trigger premature bolting. Harvest baby bok choy at 4–6 in tall by cutting at the soil line, or allow full-sized types to reach 12–18 in. The whole plant is edible—cut at the base and the plant may re-sprout for a second harvest.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring or summer; this variety tolerates heat for extended sowing.
directions: Sow seed 1/4 inch deep in fertile soil and thin to about 4 inches apart, or grow in containers. This baby bok choy tolerates heat, so you can sow it through summer, harvesting at about 4 inches tall for delicate, crisp, mild-flavored leaves. The uniform green plants are an indispensable stir-fry ingredient and also good fresh or marinated. Keep soil evenly moist to keep growth tender, and harvest whole heads while young for the best texture.

plant_note: Direct sow in warm soil; a vigorous vining gourd best grown on a sturdy trellis.
directions: Soak the hard seeds overnight and sow an inch deep after the soil is thoroughly warm to speed slow germination. Train the aggressive vines up a strong trellis or arbor so the bottle-shaped fruits hang and develop an even shape. Harvest young fruits for cooking, or leave them on the vine to fully mature and dry for crafts and birdhouses.

plant_note: Start indoors 3–4 weeks before last frost and transplant after frost danger passes; direct sowing is also possible once soil is warm.
directions: Sow seeds 1/2 inch deep in warm mix or directly in warm soil; nick the seed coat or soak overnight to speed germination. Bottle gourd is a vigorous, tropical vine that requires a sturdy trellis or fence and very warm conditions to thrive. Harvest immature fruits when they are 6–8 inches long (or the desired shape depending on variety) for vegetable use — they are delicate and best cooked; allow fruits to mature fully and dry on the vine if growing for use as a gourd vessel. Pollination requires both male and female flowers to be open simultaneously; hand-pollinate in the morning if fruit set is poor.

plant_note: Start indoors 4 to 6 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger passes.
directions: Start seed indoors 4 to 6 weeks before the last frost and transplant deeply once nights stay warm. This classic heirloom, thought to date to the late 1800s, has distinctive potato-leaf foliage and produces massive 16 to 24 ounce pink fruits with rich, old-fashioned flavor. The indeterminate vines need a tall, sturdy stake or trellis and benefit from suckering for airflow. Water evenly at the base to reduce cracking, and harvest fruit when fully colored and slightly soft.

common_name: Brandywine Red & Yellow Blend Pole Tomato
plant_note: Start indoors 4 to 6 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger passes.
directions: Start seed indoors 4 to 6 weeks before the last frost and transplant deeply once nights stay warm. This blend pairs Red Brandywine, with its rich, well-balanced flavor, and golden Yellow Brandywine, which is sweet-tart, both producing large fruits often near a pound and sometimes close to two. The indeterminate vines need a tall, sturdy support and benefit from pruning for airflow. Water evenly to prevent cracking, and harvest each color when fully ripe and slightly soft.

plant_note: Root stem cuttings in water or moist potting mix after nighttime temperatures are consistently above 55°F; plants establish rapidly in warm weather.
directions: Plant cuttings in moist, fertile, well-drained soil in partial shade; this low-growing groundcover from Brazil spreads to fill in bare areas beneath shrubs and fruit trees, providing a living mulch while yielding edible leaves. The slightly succulent, wavy-edged leaves are harvested young and used raw in salads or briefly cooked like spinach, with a mild, pleasant flavor free of bitterness. It is extremely productive in the subtropics, rooting wherever stems contact moist soil. Take cuttings in early fall to overwinter indoors in a sunny window, or treat as an annual in zone 8 with fresh transplants each spring.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring and again in late summer for a fall crop.
directions: Sow seed just 1/8 inch deep and barely cover, since lettuce needs light to germinate in cool soil. This variety holds its pretty leaves high above the core for easy cutting and forms compact heads that yield well in a small space. Strong bolt resistance extends the harvest, and it resists downy mildew, lettuce leaf aphid, and Fusarium wilt. Keep soil evenly moist, provide afternoon shade in warm weather, and harvest outer leaves or the whole head as needed.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring in cool soil.
directions: Quinoa is an ancient, nutritious South American grain, and this rainbow blend adds striking seed-head colors of hot pink, burgundy, red, orange, yellow, white, and green. Direct sow in early spring in cool soil, in full sun and well-drained ground. The tender young leaves can be cooked like spinach, while the protein-rich seeds are harvested when the plants dry down. Rinse the harvested seeds well to remove the naturally bitter saponin coating before cooking. Give the tall plants room, as they can reach 4 to 6 feet.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant 2–4 weeks before last frost once seedlings are stocky.
directions: Sow seed 1/4 in deep in starter mix and keep at 70–75°F until germination, then grow on in bright light to prevent leggy transplants. Set out 2–4 weeks before last frost and fertilize with a nitrogen-rich feed at planting and again when heads begin to form. Harvest the central head while florets are still tight and dark green, before any yellow shows. Cut with a few inches of stem to encourage side-shoot production after the main head is removed.

plant_note: One of the easiest microgreens; sow densely on a moist tray and blackout for two to three days.
directions: Broadcast broccoli seed thickly across an evenly moist tray and cover for the first couple of days to encourage strong, uniform germination. Once shoots stand up, uncover and give them bright light so the stems stay short and sturdy. Keep the medium consistently moist and provide gentle airflow to prevent damping-off. Harvest the crisp, mild greens at the cotyledon stage in about a week for the sweetest flavor and best texture.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost for spring crop; sow again in late summer for fall harvest.
directions: Direct sow seed 1/4 in deep and thin to 4–6 in; rapini grows quickly and is best as a cool-season crop because heat intensifies bitterness and accelerates bolting. Side-dress with nitrogen once plants are established to encourage the leafy shoot growth that is the primary harvest. Harvest when flower buds are visible but before they open fully, cutting the top 6–8 in of the shoot with leaves included. Repeated cuts extend the harvest, as side shoots continue to develop after the main stem is removed.

plant_note: Grown indoors year-round in a jar or sprouting tray; not sown in garden soil.
directions: Rinse seeds and soak overnight, then drain and spread in a sprouting jar or tray kept out of direct sun. Rinse and drain two to three times daily to keep sprouts moist but never standing in water. Harvest tangy, nutrient-dense sprouts in four to six days once the first true leaves show and greening begins. Refrigerate harvested sprouts and use within a week for the best crunch and peppery bite.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant out; a cold-hardy drumhead cabbage for fall and winter harvest.
directions: Start seeds indoors about six weeks before setting out sturdy transplants, or direct sow in summer for a fall crop. This heirloom forms dense, flattened drumhead heads that tolerate hard frosts and store well. Keep the soil evenly moist and feed with nitrogen to build large heads, and harvest after cool weather sweetens the leaves.

plant_note: Start indoors 14–18 weeks before first fall frost so plants mature in cool autumn weather.
directions: Sow 1/4 in deep indoors under bright lights; transplant into deeply prepared soil and stake tall plants against wind. When sprouts at the base reach marble size, remove the growing tip (topping) to concentrate energy and speed maturation of the remaining sprouts. Harvest sprouts from the bottom of the stalk upward once they are 1–2 in across and tight. Flavor peaks after exposure to a hard frost.

plant_note: Use unhulled seed and soak overnight; grown indoors on trays year-round.
directions: Soak the unhulled buckwheat seed overnight and rinse it before spreading it densely on a moist tray. Cover for a few days while the heart-shaped cotyledons push up, then expose to bright light and mist as needed. Keep an eye out for seed hulls clinging to the leaves and mist them off before harvest. Cut the tender, lightly nutty greens once the cotyledons have fully opened, generally around a week to ten days.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring after last frost in any well-drained to dry, slightly alkaline to neutral, sandy to rocky soil in full sun; buffalo gourd has a massive perennial taproot that can reach 100+ pounds and regrows aggressively after frost or cutting — it is best suited to large-scale naturalistic plantings where the extensive spread (15–30 feet) is acceptable.
directions: Direct sow in spring after last frost in any well-drained, dry to moderately moist, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun — buffalo gourd is one of the most extraordinarily drought-tolerant plants native to North America, a perennial-rooted annual vine of dry prairies, roadsides, and desert washes from Nebraska to California, extending its large, rough-textured, gray-green leaves on trailing stems up to 30 feet in a single season. The plant has a distinctly unpleasant, musky-fetid odor when handled (hence the species name 'foetidissima' — most stinking). Round, striped, baseball-sized gourds develop in summer that are toxic and extremely bitter when raw (cucurbitacins); these were used by indigenous peoples after processing to remove bitterness, and the seeds are oil-rich and nutritious. The massive taproot (potentially hundreds of pounds on mature plants) stores enormous water reserves. Most appropriate for large-scale wildlife habitat plantings, erosion control on dry slopes, and arid restoration projects rather than conventional garden settings.

plant_note: Plant sets or transplants 2–4 weeks before last frost; onions from sets mature faster and are simpler for home gardeners.
directions: Press sets or transplants 1 in deep with the pointed tip facing up in rich, well-drained, loose soil. Onion bulb size is determined by the number of leaves at the time of bulbing: each leaf corresponds to a ring, so healthy top growth is essential throughout spring. Reduce watering once the necks begin to soften and tops start to fall over naturally. Cure harvested bulbs in a warm, airy spot for 2–3 weeks before moving to dry, cool storage.

plant_note: Sow as soon as soil can be worked; succession sow for continuous greens and roots.
directions: This French heirloom is prized for its deep burgundy, almost black foliage that rivals chard or spinach in the salad bowl. Sow seed directly about half an inch deep and thin seedlings to three to four inches, using the thinnings as baby greens. Harvest tender leaves young for color and flavor, and pull the sweet dark-red roots at one to three inches across. Keep soil evenly moist to prevent woody roots and toughening of the greens.

plant_note: Sow 2–4 weeks before last frost in deep, raised beds or containers; roots can reach 3 feet and demand well-prepared deep soil.
directions: Sow seeds 1/2 inch deep in soil loosened to at least 18 inches; raised beds filled with light, compost-rich mix make harvest far easier than attempting to dig clay or compacted soil. Thin to 4–6 inches once seedlings are established. Gobo roots are harvested at 18–24 inches long and about 3/4 inch in diameter, typically 120 days after sowing; use a spading fork to loosen the soil alongside the root before pulling to prevent breakage. Scrub roots and slice thinly for kinpira and stir-fries; soaking cut pieces in acidulated water prevents browning.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant; also excellent as a fall crop timed to mature in cool weather.
directions: This purple sprouting type forms a modest central head, then rewards patient growers with abundant tender side shoots. Start seed indoors five to six weeks before the last frost and set transplants eighteen to twenty-four inches apart in rich, consistently moist soil. Purple broccolis can be finicky, so keep them well fed and cool for the best flavor and antioxidant-rich color. Harvest the main head first to encourage a long flush of flavorful, tender side shoots.

plant_note: Direct sow 1–2 weeks after the last frost when soil is reliably warm; succession sow every 2–3 weeks for a continuous harvest.
directions: Sow seeds 1 in deep in well-drained soil once nighttime temperatures stay above 50°F. Avoid overhead watering once flowers appear to reduce bean rust and fungal issues. Pick pods when they snap cleanly and the seeds inside are still small; regular harvesting extends production. Inoculating seed with rhizobium before planting boosts nitrogen fixation and yield.

plant_note: Direct sow in warm soil; a giant hard-shelled gourd needing a long season and strong support.
directions: Soak seeds overnight and sow an inch deep once frost danger is well past, giving this long-season gourd every warm day it can get. Provide a very sturdy trellis or let vines run on the ground with straw beneath the developing fruit. The enormous round gourds can reach three to five feet in circumference and dry into durable containers and craft shells.

plant_note: Direct sow or transplant; succession sow every two to three weeks and grow in cool weather to avoid bolting.
directions: A vigorous butterhead beloved since 1963 for its buttery flavor and melt-in-your-mouth texture around a creamy yellow heart. Sow seed shallowly, barely covering it, since lettuce needs light to germinate well. Its compact heads are ideal for containers and single-serve salads, and tolerate a bit more heat than most butterheads. Keep soil evenly moist and provide afternoon shade in warm weather to delay bolting and bitterness.

plant_note: Direct sow in cool weather; an AAS-winning Bibb lettuce with good heat tolerance.
directions: Sow seeds shallowly, barely covering them, and keep the surface moist during cool spring or fall weather. This Bibb-style butterhead forms loose rosettes with a tender, sweet yellow heart and holds up better to heat than most lettuces. Harvest outer leaves as needed or cut the whole head, and succession sow every few weeks for a steady supply.

plant_note: Direct sow into warm soil after frost; a classic tan-skinned winter storage squash.
directions: Sow seeds an inch deep in warm soil, planting in hills and thinning to the strongest plants. Let the long vines sprawl and keep them well watered while the smooth, tan-skinned fruits with deep orange flesh develop. Harvest when the rinds are hard and cure them in a warm spot for a week or two to improve flavor and storage life.

plant_note: Direct sow 1–2 weeks after last frost; count back from first fall frost to ensure enough days to maturity.
directions: Sow 2–3 seeds 1 inch deep per hill in fertile, well-drained soil and thin to the strongest vine. Butternut is a long-season crop, so calculate the days to maturity against your first fall frost date before planting. The vines spread widely; allow 8–10 feet of run per plant. Harvest when the skin has turned uniformly tan and the stem dries out fully; cure for 10–14 days in a warm location before storing.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant 4 weeks before last frost for spring crop, or start midsummer for fall harvest.
directions: Sow seed 1/4 in deep indoors and harden seedlings off gradually before setting out into cool soil. Feed transplants with a balanced fertilizer at planting and side-dress with nitrogen when heads begin to size. Keep soil moisture consistent, as uneven watering causes heads to split. Harvest when the head feels firm and solid when squeezed; a light frost sweetens the flavor.

plant_note: Start indoors early and transplant after the soil warms; a blocky heirloom sweet bell.
directions: Start seeds indoors about eight weeks before the last frost and keep them warm for even germination. Transplant out only after nights stay reliably warm, spacing plants so air moves freely between them. The thick-walled, blocky fruits ripen from green to sweet deep red; harvest at either stage and support heavily laden plants if needed.

plant_note: Start indoors early; transplant only after soil warms and all frost danger passes.
directions: A classic bell pepper since 1928, valued for its large, blocky, uniform fruits that are perfect for stuffing. Start seed indoors eight weeks before the last frost and keep soil warm for reliable germination. Transplant after nights stay above 55 degrees, spacing plants eighteen to twenty-four inches apart. Green peppers left on the plant ripen to sweet red with even higher vitamin C, so harvest at whatever stage you prefer.

plant_note: Sow as early as the ground can be worked in spring; tolerates light frost and thin, dry soils where most oilseeds fail.
directions: Surface-sow tiny seed at 5–8 lb/acre and tamp firmly; camelina requires light for good germination and should not be buried more than 1/4 in deep. It matures in under 100 days and tolerates drought and cold better than any other oilseed crop, making it a fine spring or overwintered autumn oilseed in short-season gardens. Harvest when 80% of the pods are golden and dry — thresh by beating bundled stalks into a bucket, then winnow. The oil is exceptionally high in omega-3 fatty acids and has a pleasant nutty flavor for culinary use.

common_name: Candy Cane Chocolate Cherry Sweet Pepper
plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after frost; excellent in patio containers.
directions: An ornamental edible with variegated foliage and striped fruit that ripens from green-and-white to chocolate-red and cream. Start seed indoors about eight weeks before the last frost and keep warm to encourage germination. Its compact, showy habit makes it a standout in patio containers and balcony gardens. Harvest peppers green for a crisp bite or let them fully stripe and color up for sweeter flavor.

plant_note: Direct sow 1–2 weeks after last frost once soil reaches 65°F; can start indoors 2–3 weeks early.
directions: Sow seeds 1 inch deep in warm, fertile hills after all frost danger has passed, planting 4–5 seeds per hill and thinning to 2. Reduce watering as fruits near maturity to concentrate sugars in the flesh. Ripe cantaloupes emit a sweet fragrance at the blossom end and slip easily from the vine with gentle pressure; this is called full-slip harvest. Avoid overwatering late in the season, which dilutes flavor and can cause splitting.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost when soil warms; succession sow for a long harvest.
directions: A high-yielding French filet-style bush bean producing dark green, stringless pods five to six inches long with excellent flavor. Sow seed directly one inch deep after the soil warms, since beans resent cold, wet ground. Its strong disease resistance and productivity make it a reliable succession crop for a long harvest window. Pick pods regularly while young and slender to keep the plants producing.

plant_note: Direct sow in cool weather; a fast white-stemmed bok choy prone to bolting in heat.
directions: Sow seeds shallowly in cool spring or late summer weather, as heat and stress push this brassica to bolt. Thin plants to give each room to form crisp white stems and tender green leaves. Keep the soil consistently moist for fast, mild growth and harvest whole heads when they reach full size, ideal for stir-fries and soups.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant; also grows well as a fall crop.
directions: A compact, pointed cabbage weighing just one and a half to two pounds, ideal for small households and small gardens. Start seed indoors five to six weeks before the last frost and transplant twelve to eighteen inches apart. Its tightly wrapped, ornamental conical heads shrug off weather and pests while protecting the tender interior. Keep evenly watered and harvest when heads feel firm and full for the sweetest flavor.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after frost; provide sturdy support for this indeterminate vine.
directions: An award-winning heirloom slicer with ten to twelve ounce fruits that are purplish-brown outside and brick-red within. Start seed indoors six weeks before the last frost and transplant after nights warm. As an indeterminate vine it needs a tall stake or cage and regular pruning of suckers for airflow. Water deeply and evenly to prevent cracking, and let the fruit fully color for its rich, complex flavor.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger passes, spacing widely as plants become very large.
directions: Sow seeds 1/2 inch deep in large cells or pots and grow on at 65–70°F; transplant into deeply prepared, fertile soil with plenty of compost. In late summer, blanch the stalks by gathering them upright, wrapping loosely in cardboard or burlap, and tying for 4–6 weeks to produce pale, tender, mildly bitter stalks. Harvest in late autumn by cutting the whole plant at the base, then trim the central stalks for cooking. In Zones 7–10 cardoon is perennial; cut old growth to the ground in early spring and topdress with compost to stimulate regrowth.

plant_note: Direct sow only; carrots do not transplant well. Keep soil surface moist until germination.
directions: A colorful mix of red, purple, yellow, and orange carrots, each carrying its own nutrients and sweet flavor. Sow seed directly a quarter inch deep in loose, stone-free soil and keep the surface consistently moist during the slow germination. Thin seedlings to two to three inches so roots can size up evenly. Harvest young for baby carrots or at full size, and note that colors sweeten with a light frost.

plant_note: Direct sow 3 weeks before to 2 weeks after last frost; succession sow every 3 weeks.
directions: Sow seed 1/4 in deep in loose, stone-free soil and keep the surface consistently moist until sprouting, which is slow. Thin seedlings to 2–3 in so roots can size up. Harvest when shoulders reach desired diameter; flavor sweetens after a light frost.

plant_note: Direct sow as soon as soil can be worked; peas thrive in cool spring weather.
directions: A snap pea with thick, juicy, three-inch pods eaten whole, raw or lightly cooked. Sow seed directly one inch deep as soon as the soil can be worked in early spring. The short, roughly thirty-inch vines are largely self-supporting but benefit from a low trellis. It resists powdery mildew and pea enation, so harvest pods frequently while plump and glossy to keep them coming.

plant_note: Direct sow into warm soil after frost; an heirloom white-skinned pumpkin for decor and baking.
directions: Plant seeds an inch deep in warm soil in hills, thinning to the two best vines each. Give the plants space and steady moisture to develop the smooth, ghostly white rinds and sweet orange flesh. Harvest when the stems harden and cure in the sun to firm the skin for both fall displays and baking.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil, or start indoors a few weeks early in short-season areas.
directions: A petite ornamental pumpkin producing pure-white fruits four to four and a half inches across, perfect for painting and displays. Sow seed directly one inch deep once the soil is warm, or start indoors briefly in short-season climates. Give the vines room to run and keep soil evenly moist while fruit sizes up. Harvest when the skin is hard and the stem corks, leaving a length of stem attached for longer storage.

plant_note: Plant 8–10 inch stem cuttings (stakes) horizontally 4–6 inches deep or at a 45-degree angle after soil warms to 65°F; upright planting produces fewest tubers while horizontal planting maximizes yield.
directions: Plant stem cuttings (stakes) horizontally 4–6 inches deep in well-drained, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; cassava is drought tolerant once established and produces in poor soils where other root crops fail, making it one of the world's most important calorie crops. All varieties contain some cyanogenic glycosides — sweet varieties are low enough for safe eating after cooking, while bitter varieties require extensive washing and fermentation to detoxify. Never eat raw cassava. Harvest tubers when 8–18 months old by digging carefully to avoid breaking the fragile roots; they must be used within a few days of harvest or frozen, as they deteriorate rapidly. In zone 9, the above-ground plant freezes back but roots may survive with heavy mulch.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant for a fall crop; sprouts sweeten after frost.
directions: Start seeds indoors and set out sturdy transplants so the long season finishes as cool fall weather arrives. This 1940s heirloom forms compact, dwarf stalks packed with large, tightly wrapped sprouts. Keep the plants well fed and watered, and wait to harvest until a few frosts have sweetened the sprouts from the bottom of the stalk upward.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant about 4 weeks before last frost.
directions: Sow 1/4 in deep in well-drained starter mix 6–8 weeks before transplanting; cauliflower is sensitive to temperature swings and nutrient deficiencies. When the developing curd is about the size of a golf ball, gather outer leaves over it and secure with a rubber band to blanch for white varieties. Harvest when the head is firm, tight, and 6–8 in across before the curds begin to separate. Consistent soil moisture and boron-sufficient soil prevent hollow stem and poor curd formation.

plant_note: Flavor sweetens after frost; also spring-sow for summer harvest.
directions: Also known as Tuscan or lacinato kale, this Italian heirloom bears puckered blue-black strap leaves used in soups and braises. Start indoors and transplant, or direct sow, spacing plants 15 inches apart in fertile soil. Harvest lower leaves first, working upward and leaving the growing crown intact for continuous production. Cold weather concentrates sugars, so the best-flavored leaves come after the first hard frosts. Watch for cabbage worms in warm spells and cover with row fabric if needed.

plant_note: Start indoors early and transplant after all frost danger passes.
directions: A prolific blend of pencil-thin cayenne peppers used fresh, dried, ground, or strung into a decorative ristra. Start seed indoors about eight weeks before the last frost and keep warm for good germination. Transplant after nights stay above 55 degrees and support heavily laden plants if needed. Let fruits ripen to red for full heat, and dry them whole or grind for homemade red pepper flakes.

plant_note: Start indoors early and transplant after warm-up; a slender hot pepper for drying and sauces.
directions: Start seeds indoors about eight weeks ahead, keeping them warm to germinate reliably. Transplant into a sunny bed once nights are warm, and expect long, slender 5 to 6 inch pods that ripen bright red with lasting heat. Harvest peppers green or red and string the red ones to air-dry for grinding into cayenne powder.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in warm starting mix, maintaining soil temperature above 75°F for prompt sprouting. Set transplants out once the garden is fully warm and side-dress with a balanced fertilizer once plants begin flowering. Thin-walled cayenne fruits dry exceptionally well; allow them to redden fully on the plant before harvesting for drying. String harvested peppers in a warm, airy location or use a dehydrator to preserve them.

plant_note: Direct sow or transplant; tolerates heat better than most greens for a long season.
directions: A rainbow-stemmed chard in red, magenta, orange, pink, yellow, and white beneath glossy dark green leaves. Sow the seed clusters half an inch deep and thin seedlings to six to twelve inches apart. It tolerates both light frost and summer heat, making it productive from spring through fall. Harvest outer leaves regularly and the plant keeps generating new growth from the center all season.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before the last frost; transplant after frost danger has passed when plants are 3–4 in tall.
directions: Sow seeds on the surface of moist starting mix — they need light to germinate — and barely cover with vermiculite; seedling growth is extremely slow for the first 6 weeks. Transplant into rich, consistently moist soil and remove the lower side shoots throughout summer to encourage bulb development. Celeriac bulbs are at their best after light autumn frosts, which deepen the nutty flavor. Harvest before the ground freezes hard; trim roots and leaves and store in slightly moist sand in a cold root cellar.

plant_note: Start indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger passes and soil has warmed slightly.
directions: Surface-sow or barely cover tiny seed with 1/8 in of moist mix; celery germinates slowly and needs consistent warmth and bright light from the start. Transplant into deeply prepared, rich soil and provide consistent, abundant moisture throughout the season, as drought quickly causes tough, stringy stalks. Hill soil around the base or use collars to blanch stalks to a paler color and milder flavor if desired. Harvest entire plants by cutting at soil level when stalks are 12–18 in tall and firm.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost in spring; can also be started indoors 4 weeks early and transplanted out.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep or start indoors and transplant at 4–5 weeks; celtuce is grown primarily for its swollen, asparagus-like central stalk rather than its leaves, though young leaves are also edible. The stalk is ready when it is 1–1.5 inches in diameter and 12–15 inches tall; peel away the outer layer to reveal the crisp, mild interior. Keep soil consistently moist and rich in nitrogen for the fastest stalk development. Harvest before the plant bolts, as heat causes bitterness and pithy texture in the stalk.

plant_note: Direct sow after last frost or start indoors 4 weeks before transplanting in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; soak seeds 24 hours before planting to improve germination. Provide a trellis or other support for climbing.
directions: Direct sow or transplant in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade with a support to climb; Ceylon spinach is the red-stemmed form of Malabar spinach, producing striking bright-red vining stems, dark green to purplish leaves, and decorative dark reddish-purple berries. Like green Malabar spinach (B. alba), the leaves are thick, succulent, and mild-flavored when young, used fresh in salads or cooked as a spinach substitute in stir-fries, soups, and curries throughout tropical Asia. The red-stemmed form is highly ornamental — it can be used as a colorful edible trellis plant or allowed to cascade over a fence. It thrives in heat that wilts true spinach and is particularly productive in Gulf Coast summer gardens. In zone 9–11 it overwinters as a short-lived perennial. Tolerates partial shade better than most summer leafy greens.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after frost; stake or cage this indeterminate vine.
directions: An heirloom indeterminate cherry tomato named for horticulturist Alan Chadwick, bearing heavy trusses of sweet, crack-resistant one to two ounce fruits. Start seed indoors six weeks before the last frost and transplant once nights warm. Provide a tall trellis or cage and prune suckers to keep the vigorous vines manageable. Water evenly and pick the glossy red fruits regularly to encourage continued production.

plant_note: Direct sow in cool weather; a fast heirloom round red radish for spring and fall.
directions: Sow seeds a half inch deep directly in the garden and thin to about an inch apart for well-formed roots. These fast radishes are ready in under a month, producing round, cherry-red roots with crisp, mild white flesh. Keep the soil evenly moist to avoid pithiness and pungency, and sow in succession during the cool ends of the season.

plant_note: Direct sow; a stocky heirloom carrot that performs well in heavy soils and stores long.
directions: Sow seeds shallowly in loose soil and keep the surface consistently moist through the slow germination period. This stocky, tapered variety about four to five inches long tolerates heavier and clay soils better than long carrots. Thin seedlings to prevent crowding and harvest as needed, storing the sweet, red-cored roots in a cool place for winter use.

plant_note: Start indoors two to three weeks early or direct sow in warm soil; melons dislike root disturbance.
directions: A true French cantaloupe too fragile to ship commercially, with smooth skin, light green stripes, and intensely fragrant creamy-orange flesh. Start seed indoors in peat pots two to three weeks before setting out, or direct sow once soil is thoroughly warm. Give vines full sun, warmth, and steady moisture while fruit develops. Harvest at full slip when the fruit detaches easily from the stem and the aroma turns rich and sweet.

plant_note: Needs consistent heat; use black mulch in cooler climates.
directions: This intensely fragrant French melon has smooth gray-green skin and deep orange, aromatic flesh. Start seed indoors three weeks before transplanting to gain a head start in short-season areas, handling the sensitive roots gently. Grow on black plastic mulch with row cover early to trap warmth, removing cover at flowering for pollinators. Reduce watering as fruit ripens to concentrate sugars.
