directions: This German heirloom pattypan bears vibrant yellow, scalloped fruit with creamy yellow and light-green striping. For the best flavor and texture, harvest squash starting at about 2 inches wide up to 4 inches. Pick young and often to keep the plants producing all season. Sow directly into warm, fertile soil in full sun. Water consistently and allow good airflow to limit powdery mildew.

plant_note: Sow after frost in warm soil; long vines need plenty of room.
directions: This Southern heirloom from the 1830s produces oblong, green-striped fruits of 25 to 35 pounds with sweet, rosy-red flesh. Sow seed an inch deep in warm, fertile soil and give the sprawling vines abundant space. Water consistently while fruits swell, then ease off as they near ripeness to concentrate sweetness. Look for a curled, dry tendril near the stem and a creamy ground spot as ripeness cues before cutting the melon.

plant_note: Sow in spring or late summer; flavor improves after fall frost.
directions: This time-tested Southern heirloom yields large, tender, blue-green leaves with a rich, traditional flavor and strong vigor. Sow seed a quarter to half an inch deep and thin plants so the broad leaves have room to develop. Harvest lower leaves as needed for cut-and-come-again picking, leaving the growing tip intact. Highly cold hardy, the greens sweeten noticeably after fall frost and produce reliably in both spring and fall gardens.

plant_note: Start indoors for spring, or sow mid-summer for a fall crop; flavor sweetens after frost.
directions: A Southern staple since the 1800s, this productive collard tolerates both heat and cold, with large leaves for steaming or for use as low-calorie wraps. Flavor sweetens after a light frost. Grow in full sun to partial shade in fertile, moist soil and give plants room since they get tall. Harvest lower leaves as needed and the plant keeps producing from the top. Keep evenly watered for tender, mild greens.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring and again in late summer for fall.
directions: This aptly named radish can be pulled small at quarter size or left to grow as large as a tennis ball while staying crisp and flavorful. It is crack-resistant and holds its texture without turning woody or spongy as it matures. Sow directly in cool weather and thin seedlings to give the large roots room. Keep soil evenly moist for the best flavor. Grows well in spring and fall in full sun to partial shade.

plant_note: Plant cloves in fall a few weeks before the ground freezes.
directions: This porcelain hardneck garlic, also sold as German Extra-Hardy, forms impressive 2 inch or larger bulbs with 4 to 7 fat, easy-peeling cloves and a strong, robust flavor. Plant individual cloves in fall, pointed end up, in fertile, well-drained soil, then mulch for winter protection. Cut the scapes in early summer to channel energy into the bulbs. Harvest when the lower leaves brown and cure in a dry, airy place. It stores well and is very cold-hardy.

plant_note: Start indoors early; the long season and warmth-dependent germination make transplanting after all frost danger essential.
directions: One of the hottest peppers on earth at over one million Scoville units, so wear gloves when handling ripe fruit. Start seeds indoors 8 to 10 weeks before your last frost and provide bottom heat around 85F for reliable germination. Transplant into the warmest spot in the garden after nights stay above 55F. Fruits ripen slowly from green to fiery red; dehydrate and grind them into flakes for a fruity, intense heat.

plant_note: Start indoors 12–14 weeks before last frost to allow enough growing time; transplant only after nights are reliably above 60°F.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep and maintain a constant soil temperature of 85–90°F; germination is slow and irregular, so start more seeds than needed and be patient. Transplant to a sheltered, heat-retaining bed after all frost danger has passed, and protect young plants from cool nights with row cover. Fruits turn from green to red (or yellow in some strains) at full maturity; wear gloves and avoid touching the face when harvesting, as the heat exceeds one million Scoville units. The extremely long growing season makes this pepper best suited to zones 9–12 or greenhouse culture in cooler regions.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in moist, rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; giant cane spreads by rhizome to form extensive canebrakes — use underground barriers if confined space is required.
directions: Plant rhizome divisions in moist, rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; giant cane is the only bamboo native to North America, once forming vast canebrakes across the South and Mississippi Valley that were critical habitat for the now-extinct Carolina parakeet and Bachman's warbler, as well as important forage for Swainson's warbler. The young shoots in spring are edible, similar to commercial bamboo shoots, and were an important food source for Native American peoples. The mature canes were used for tools, weapons, and construction. As a landscape plant it provides fast-growing subtropical screening and naturalistic structure. It spreads aggressively by rhizome and should be managed; use underground root barriers to contain it in smaller spaces. The plant supports several rare specialist moth species.

plant_note: Start indoors early and transplant into deep holes to blanch the stalks.
directions: This AAS-winning heirloom leek produces thick, mild, onion-flavored stalks that store well through cold weather. Start seed indoors about ten weeks before the last frost, then transplant the pencil-thick seedlings into deep dibbled holes to blanch the lower stems. Water steadily and hill soil around the shafts as they grow to lengthen the tender white portion. Harvest as needed through fall and winter, since the hardy plants tolerate hard freezes.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring and again in late summer for a fall crop.
directions: This classic heirloom spinach forms large, deep-green leaves with a mild, tender flavor, spreading widely as it grows. Direct sow seed half an inch deep in cool, fertile soil as early as it can be worked, since spinach bolts quickly in heat. Keep moisture even and thin plants so leaves size up fully. Harvest outer leaves or cut whole plants young, and sow again in late summer for a productive, cold-tolerant fall crop.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring or late summer; heat prompts bolting.
directions: This heirloom mustard combines ornamental burgundy-red leaves brushed with green and a bold, spicy flavor that mellows with cooking. Direct sow seed a quarter to half an inch deep and thin for baby leaves or fuller plants. Harvest young leaves for a milder bite in salads or let them mature for cooking greens. Grow in cool spring and fall weather with steady moisture, since heat pushes plants to bolt and turns the flavor sharp.

plant_note: Start rhizome pieces indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; move outside only after all danger of frost has passed and temperatures are consistently warm.
directions: Break fresh ginger rhizomes into 1–2 in pieces each with a visible growth bud and pre-sprout them in a warm, moist environment for 2–3 weeks before planting 2 in deep in rich, moisture-retentive soil. Ginger thrives in dappled shade and resents direct afternoon sun in hot climates. Keep soil consistently moist and avoid letting it dry out. Harvest young ginger in as little as 4–5 months for milder flavor, or leave in the ground until foliage yellows for full-size, more pungent roots.

plant_note: An early determinate type; start indoors and set out after frost for very early fruit.
directions: A cold-tolerant bush tomato that sets fruit in cooler temperatures than most, giving you some of the earliest ripe tomatoes of summer and a long fall harvest. Start seeds indoors 4 to 6 weeks before the last frost and harden off before transplanting. The compact 30-inch plants stay tidy and do well in containers, though a small cage helps. Pick the 2-inch flavorful slicers as they color up to keep production going.

plant_note: Intermediate-day onion; start indoors early and transplant as soon as soil is workable.
directions: An intermediate-day onion that forms clean, crisp white bulbs 4 to 5 inches across with a mild, sweet flavor good for fresh use. Start seeds indoors 8 to 10 weeks before the last frost and transplant seedlings once the soil can be worked. Keep beds weed-free and evenly moist, tapering off water as the tops fall over near harvest. Cure bulbs in a dry, airy spot before storing.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost when soil has warmed; plant in blocks for pollination.
directions: A stunning ornamental flint corn with kaleidoscopic, translucent rainbow kernels that can also be dried for cornmeal, flour, or popcorn. Direct sow after the last frost in blocks of at least four short rows so wind pollination fills out the ears. Give steady water during tasseling and silking for well-filled cobs. Let ears dry fully on the stalk, then cure indoors before shelling or displaying.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; expose seedlings to cool temperatures (50°F) for 2 weeks before transplanting to vernalize and trigger bud set.
directions: Sow seed 1/4 in deep indoors and expose young plants to 2 weeks of 50°F nights to vernalize them for first-year bud production. Transplant into deeply amended, well-drained soil in a permanent location, as plants are long-lived when winters are mild enough. Harvest the central bud first, while bracts are tight and the stem snaps cleanly, then side buds as they develop. In zones 7–9, cut plants back to 12 in after harvest and mulch heavily for overwintering; divide clumps every 3–4 years to maintain vigor.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost; beans resent transplanting and cold soil.
directions: A golden-yellow wax bean whose bright pods stand out against green foliage, making picking quick and easy. Direct sow after the soil warms past 65F, spacing seeds a few inches apart in rows. This compact, disease-resistant bush type does well in containers and holds quality both on the plant and after harvest. Pick pods young and often to encourage continued production.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant for spring, or sow midsummer for fall heads.
directions: This early heirloom produces compact, round green heads quickly, making it a good fit for small gardens and short seasons. Start seed indoors five to six weeks before transplanting into firm, fertile soil. Water evenly and consistently, as swings in moisture can crack the maturing heads. Harvest as soon as heads feel solid, since the early type is prone to splitting if left too long; it succeeds in both spring and fall.

plant_note: Sow after frost in warm soil; plant in blocks for good pollination.
directions: This historic open-pollinated heirloom brought sweet yellow corn to the home table and still delivers rich, old-fashioned flavor. Sow seed an inch to an inch and a half deep in warm soil, planting in blocks of several short rows so wind pollination fills the ears. Provide plenty of water and nitrogen through tasseling and ear fill. Harvest when the silks brown and kernels are plump and milky, and cook or preserve promptly for the best flavor.

plant_note: Germination can be erratic; sow a bit thicker to compensate.
directions: Golden beets offer a mild, sweet flavor without the earthy note of red types and do not bleed when cut. Because germination is often spotty, presoak seed for a few hours and sow slightly denser than usual. Thin ruthlessly to 3 inches so roots can size up without crowding. Keep beds weed-free and evenly watered to avoid tough, cracked roots. Harvest young for salads or at full size for roasting.

plant_note: Direct sow as soon as soil is workable; succession sow for continuous harvest.
directions: A golden-orange fleshed beet with sweet, mild flavor that won't stain like red types, plus tender edible greens. Direct sow as soon as the soil can be worked, and thin seedlings to about 3 inches since each seed cluster produces several plants. Keep soil evenly moist for smooth, uniform globes and harvest young for the sweetest, most tender roots. The strong upright stems make for attractive bunches.

plant_note: Start indoors with warmth; transplant after nights stay reliably warm.
directions: A blocky sweet bell that starts green and ripens to a sunburst yellow blushed with orange, excellent stuffed, grilled, or stir-fried. Start seeds indoors 6 to 8 weeks before the last frost with bottom heat, then transplant after the soil and nights have warmed. Mulch to keep roots evenly moist and side-dress once fruit sets. Peppers freeze well, so harvest generously as they color up.

plant_note: Sow after frost when soil is warm; succession sow for extended yield.
directions: This classic yellow summer squash offers buttery flavor and tender texture on compact, bush-style plants that suit home gardens. Sow seed an inch deep in warm, fertile soil, spacing plants for good air movement. Keep moisture steady and harvest fruits young, while the curved necks are still small and the skin is tender. Frequent picking keeps the plants producing heavily, since mature fruits left on the plant slow new set.

plant_note: Direct sow into warm soil after frost; squash dislikes root disturbance.
directions: A classic yellow crookneck producing bright, tender fruits best picked at 6 to 8 inches long on compact bushy plants. Direct sow two or three seeds per hill once soil warms past 70F, then thin to the strongest. Keep well watered and harvest every day or two, since regular picking keeps the plants productive all season. The bushy 2-foot habit and 2 to 4 foot spread make it manageable in smaller beds.

plant_note: Direct sow in cool soil as early as it can be worked; provide a tall trellis.
directions: A striking heirloom snow pea with bicolor purple blossoms and flat, lemon-yellow pods that are tender raw, steamed, or stir-fried. Direct sow several weeks before the last frost as soon as the soil is workable, and set up a tall trellis for the 6 to 8 foot vines. Keep the soil cool and moist with mulch, and harvest pods young while flat and crisp. Frequent picking extends the productive window before summer heat.

plant_note: Sow after frost when soil warms; succession sow every few weeks.
directions: This productive yellow wax bean produces bright, stringless pods with tender texture on compact 15 to 20 inch bush plants. Sow seed about an inch deep directly in warm soil, since beans resent root disturbance. Keep moisture even during flowering and pod set, and avoid working among wet plants to limit disease. Pick pods often while young and slender for fresh eating or preserving, which keeps the plants setting new pods.

plant_note: Sow after frost when soil is warm; a single plant is very productive.
directions: This heirloom, open-pollinated variety produces bright yellow, cylindrical fruits with classic zucchini flavor on compact bush plants. Sow seed an inch deep in warm, fertile soil and give each plant room for air circulation. Water consistently at the base and harvest fruits young, around six to eight inches, for the best texture. Pick every day or two during peak season, since fruits enlarge fast and steady harvest keeps production high.

plant_note: Direct sow into warm soil after all frost danger has passed.
directions: A 1973 heirloom zucchini with vivid yellow fruit and creamy-white flesh, most tender when picked at 8 inches or less. Direct sow into warm soil after frost, planting in hills and thinning to the strongest seedlings. Water deeply and consistently to prevent blossom-end rot, and harvest frequently to keep the plant producing. Excellent roasted, grilled, sauteed, or baked.

plant_note: Direct sow as soon as soil is workable; succession sow every few weeks.
directions: A colorful mix of beet varieties including deep-red Detroit Dark Red and candy-striped Chioggia, offering a rainbow of roots and tasty greens. Direct sow as soon as the soil can be worked and thin each seed cluster to a single plant about 3 inches apart. Keep evenly moist for smooth, tender roots and harvest at golf-ball to tennis-ball size. The assorted colors make a beautiful bunch and a striking roasted medley.

plant_note: Needs warm nights above 60 F to set fruit reliably.
directions: This Italian type bears glossy fruit striped in purple and white that fades to solid purple as it matures. Start seed indoors eight weeks before the last frost and transplant only after nights stay reliably above 60 F. Grow in full sun with steady moisture and stake plants that become top-heavy with fruit. Harvest while the skin is still glossy for the mildest, least seedy flesh. Consistent picking encourages the plant to keep flowering and fruiting.

plant_note: Sow after last frost when soil is warm; grain amaranth thrives in hot weather and tolerates drought and poor soil better than most grains.
directions: Surface-sow or barely cover seed 1/8 in deep, then thin to 12–18 in when seedlings are 2 in tall; amaranth seed is tiny and germinates readily in warm soil but competing weeds must be controlled in the first 3 weeks before the plant takes over. The tall, dramatic seed heads ripen from the bottom up — harvest when the top of the panicle begins to dry and seed crumbles easily when pinched. Cut heads into a bag or over a tarp, dry for 1–2 weeks, then thresh and winnow; a single plant can yield 1/4–1/2 lb of seed. Grain amaranth seed is gluten-free and high in lysine, making it nutritionally complementary to corn or wheat.

plant_note: Sow after last frost when soil is reliably above 60°F; grain sorghum needs a long, warm season but withstands drought far better than corn.
directions: Drill seed 1/2 in deep in rows 24–30 in apart, thinning to 6–8 in within the row once plants are established; grain sorghum stalls visibly during drought but resumes growth when moisture returns, unlike corn which suffers permanent yield loss. The compact seed heads (panicles) are ready to harvest when seeds dent and the plant begins to dry down naturally, roughly 30 days after bloom. Cut heads by hand into bags or combine at low cylinder speed to avoid shattering; dry grain to 13% moisture or less before storage. Whole sorghum grain is gluten-free and can be popped like popcorn or ground into flour.

plant_note: Start indoors; transplant after frost and plant at least two for pollination.
directions: A compact tomatillo about 24 inches tall that yields husked green fruit nearly all at once, ideal for canning or freezing salsa verde. Start seeds indoors 4 to 6 weeks before the last frost and transplant after the weather warms. Plant at least two, since tomatillos need cross-pollination to set fruit well. Harvest when the papery husks fill out and split, and stake plants if they get top-heavy.

plant_note: Direct sow in cool soil; this variety tolerates heat and resists bolting.
directions: A crisp iceberg-type lettuce, this 1960s improvement over the original Great Lakes is notably heat tolerant and slow to bolt. Direct sow in cool soil and thin to about 12 inches so heads can fill out fully. Keep consistently watered to avoid bitterness and tip burn, since even moisture builds those firm, crunchy heads. Harvest when heads feel solid, and enjoy the cool crunch in salads and on sandwiches.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after frost once nights stay warm.
directions: This rare heirloom beefsteak produces creamy pale fruits of one to two pounds with sweet, low-acid, almost tropical flavor. Start seed indoors about six weeks before transplanting, then set hardened plants deep into rich, warm soil. As an indeterminate vine, it benefits from sturdy staking or caging and consistent watering to prevent cracking. Harvest when the pale fruits take on a faint blush and yield slightly to gentle pressure.

plant_note: Direct sow as early as soil can be worked; a short trellis helps.
directions: A productive heirloom shelling pea whose sweet green peas fill pods set in pairs atop compact 28-inch plants for easy harvesting. Direct sow several weeks before the last frost as soon as the soil is workable, and give the vines a short trellis or netting. Keep the soil cool and moist and pick pods when plump but still bright green. Excellent for fresh eating and freezing.

plant_note: Easy brassica microgreen; sow densely and blackout two to three days.
directions: Spread cabbage seed thickly over a moist tray and cover for the first two or three days to draw the seedlings up evenly. Uncover once the stems have lengthened and give bright light to keep them stocky and green. Maintain steady moisture and light airflow to fend off damping-off. Harvest the crisp, mildly sweet shoots at the cotyledon stage in eight to ten days.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant while weather is still cool; also good for a fall crop.
directions: A quick-growing miniature cabbage forming 5 to 6 inch heads of 2 to 3 pounds, ready in as little as 50 days. Start seeds indoors 4 to 6 weeks before setting out, then transplant while temperatures remain cool. The small heads suit container and raised-bed gardens; space them a bit tighter than full-size cabbage. Keep evenly watered and harvest promptly once heads firm up to avoid splitting.

plant_note: Start indoors early; chilling young plants encourages first-year budding.
directions: This heirloom perennial grows into a bold 4 to 6 foot plant producing large green buds tipped with light purple. Start seed indoors early and give young plants a cool period, which helps them form buds in the first season. Transplant into rich, well-drained soil with steady moisture and ample space for the architectural foliage. Harvest buds while tight and firm before the scales open; in mild zones the plants return and yield for several years.

plant_note: Start indoors very early; can be grown as an annual with proper vernalization.
directions: An improved Green Globe with more uniform plants, fewer spines, and better production, allowing artichokes even in northern gardens grown as an annual. Start seeds indoors 8 to 10 weeks before the last frost, and expose young plants to a few weeks of cool temperatures to trigger budding. Give each plant plenty of room and rich, well-drained soil. Expect 3 to 5 tender buds per plant; harvest while the scales are still tight.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant, or grow as a fall crop in cool weather.
directions: This rare Italian heirloom forms vivid green curds with a mild, buttery flavor and reliable performance across seasons. Start seed indoors about six weeks ahead, then transplant into rich, evenly moist soil for uninterrupted growth. Consistent feeding and watering prevent stress that leads to loose or off-flavored heads. Cut heads while the green curds are still tight and firm for the best eating quality.

plant_note: Direct sow from 4 weeks before to the time of the last frost; succession sow every 3 weeks throughout the growing season.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep in bands 3–4 in wide rather than single rows to maximize bed productivity. Thin seedlings to 1–2 in apart; the thinnings are already usable as tiny scallions. Water steadily to keep growth rapid and tender; drought causes tough, hot-tasting stems. Pull bunching types when stems reach pencil width, or cut leaves an inch above the base and allow them to regrow for a second harvest.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring and fall; succession sow for a continuous supply.
directions: This AAS award-winning oakleaf-type lettuce produces frilly green leaves that stay sweet and tender even in warm weather. Direct sow seed shallowly and press lightly into moist soil, since lettuce needs light to germinate. Thin plants for full heads or harvest as loose-leaf, cut-and-come-again greens. More heat tolerant and slow to bolt than many lettuces, it extends the salad season with steady moisture and light afternoon shade in summer.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant for spring, or sow midsummer for fall.
directions: This traditional Italian heirloom is the familiar garden broccoli, producing large blue-green central heads followed by abundant side shoots. Start seed indoors about six weeks before transplanting into rich, firm soil. Keep water and nutrients steady, since stress can cause premature buttoning. Cut the main head while the buds are still tight, then keep harvesting the smaller side shoots for weeks of continued production.

plant_note: Start indoors; transplant after frost and provide sturdy support for tall vines.
directions: A gourmet indeterminate tomato bearing smooth, crack-free 2 to 3 inch fruits striped golden green and forest green, with a slight amber blush when ripe. Start seeds indoors 4 to 6 weeks before the last frost and transplant deeply after the weather warms. Stake or cage the vigorous 6-foot-plus vines and prune suckers for airflow. Judge ripeness by softness and blush rather than color, since the fruit stays green when ready.

plant_note: Sow after frost when soil is warm; tolerates heat well.
directions: This heat-tolerant heirloom produces smooth, straight fruits with mottled silver-grey skin on compact, heavy-yielding bush plants. Sow seed an inch deep in warm, fertile soil and space plants for good airflow. Water at the base to keep foliage dry and harvest fruits young, around six to eight inches, for tender flesh. Pick frequently during the peak of summer, as fruits size up quickly and steady harvest sustains production.

plant_note: Start indoors well before the last frost; transplant only after soil has warmed.
directions: This heirloom Physalis produces small golden fruits inside papery, lantern-like husks with a sweet pineapple-vanilla flavor. Start seeds indoors six to eight weeks before your last frost and set out sprawling plants once nights stay warm. Fruits are ripe when the husks turn tan and drop naturally to the ground, so harvest from the soil below the plant. Let them cure a few days in their husks to develop full sweetness for fresh eating or preserves.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed.
directions: Sow seeds shallowly, just 1/8 inch deep, in warm starting mix 6–8 weeks before the last frost. Transplant to the garden once nighttime temperatures stay above 50°F and provide moderate, consistent moisture. Fruits drop to the ground when ripe, so check beneath the plants frequently once husks begin to dry and turn tan. The papery husk protects fallen fruit and allows it to keep for several weeks at room temperature.

plant_note: Direct sow scarified seed in fall in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, loamy to rocky, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun; ground plum has a deep taproot that makes it drought tolerant but very difficult to transplant — sow directly in its permanent location.
directions: Direct sow scarified seed in fall in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun — ground plum is a native sprawling legume of the southern Great Plains and central prairies, historically one of the most important native edible plants of the southern prairie, producing clusters of purple to reddish-purple pea flowers in April–May followed by the remarkable, plum-like fruit pods — green when young, reddish when ripe — that gave the plant its common name. The young, unripe pods have a mild, slightly sweet, pea-like flavor and were eaten raw by Plains peoples as a seasonal vegetable and green snack food on the prairie. The pods are edible raw when fully green and firm, but become papery when ripe. An important nitrogen-fixing prairie legume that improves soil for neighboring plants. Outstanding for prairie restoration plantings in well-drained, alkaline, or rocky soils in zone 3–9.

plant_note: Plant small tubers 2–3 inches deep in spring after last frost; provide a trellis or fence for the twining stems. Tubers from the previous year planted in spring produce the largest harvest in fall.
directions: Plant tubers in moist, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun near a support for the twining stems; this native leguminous vine was a staple food for many Indigenous nations and early European settlers and was considered for commercial cultivation before potatoes became dominant. Harvest the small, walnut-shaped tubers strung along underground rhizomes in fall after frost kills the vines; they taste like a richer, nuttier potato and can be boiled, baked, or roasted. The fragrant reddish-purple flowers in midsummer fix atmospheric nitrogen. Tubers are small in the first year but grow larger each subsequent year — the plant becomes more productive the longer it is left undisturbed.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed into warm, well-drained soil.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in warm starting mix at 80°F and maintain consistent warmth until transplanting time. Plants grow tall and may benefit from staking; set into fertile, well-drained soil after last frost and side-dress with a balanced fertilizer when flowering begins. Long, narrow pods ripen from green to dark glossy red and must be fully dried for authentic guajillo chile flavor — harvest red ripe pods and dry in a food dehydrator or warm oven. The dried pods develop a distinctive tangy, berry-like flavor essential in Mexican mole and enchilada sauces.

plant_note: Start indoors early with strong bottom heat; transplant only after nights are warm.
directions: An intensely hot pepper prized for its fruity aroma as much as its fire, said to be many times hotter than a jalapeno. Start seeds indoors 8 to 10 weeks before the last frost with warmth around 85F for even germination. Transplant into a warm, sunny spot after nights stay above 55F, and mulch to keep roots evenly moist. Wear gloves when picking and processing the ripe orange fruit.

plant_note: Start indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost due to the long growing season; transplant 2 weeks after all frost danger has passed.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep and maintain soil temperature at 85–90°F to achieve reliable germination, as Capsicum chinense species are notably slow to sprout. Transplant into the warmest, most sheltered spot in the garden once conditions are fully settled. Consistent feeding with a potassium-rich fertilizer during fruiting will maximize yields. Harvest when fruits reach their characteristic orange or red color and feel firm; handle with gloves due to extreme capsaicin levels.

plant_note: Direct sow into warm soil after frost, or start indoors briefly to get a jump.
directions: A classic heirloom muskmelon with exceptionally sweet, thick-walled flesh and a small seed cavity, in 3 to 5 pound fruits. Direct sow two or three seeds per hill once soil warms past 70F and thin to the strongest plant. Water deeply while vines grow and fruit sizes up, then ease off as melons near ripeness to concentrate sugars. Harvest when the fruit slips easily from the vine and smells fragrant.

plant_note: Direct sow into warm soil after all frost danger has passed.
directions: Hale's Best is a classic heirloom cantaloupe prized for exceptional sweetness, tender juicy flesh, and a small seed cavity that yields more edible fruit. Sow seeds in warm soil once frost has passed, giving vines several feet to sprawl. Water consistently while fruit develops, then ease off as melons near ripeness to concentrate sugars. Harvest at full slip, when the melon separates cleanly from the vine with a light pull.

plant_note: Direct sow as soon as soil can be worked in spring; the long growing season requires an early start and the slow germination period means pre-soaking seeds is beneficial.
directions: Soak seeds in warm water for 24 hours before sowing to improve germination; sow 1/4 inch deep in deeply loosened, stone-free soil in rows, as seeds germinate slowly and unevenly over 2–4 weeks. Thin seedlings to 6–8 inches apart once established; the flat-leaf parsley-like foliage is edible throughout the season as an herb. The ivory-white, parsnip-like root develops slowly all season and tastes like a concentrated, slightly sweet parsley-parsnip hybrid — exceptional in Central European soups and stews. Harvest in fall after light frost improves sweetness; roots left in the ground over winter in milder climates will re-sprout in spring but roots lose quality as the plant prepares to bolt.

plant_note: Sow 2–4 weeks before last frost for spring crop; sow again in late summer for fall harvest.
directions: Sow seed at 1/8 in depth and thin to 8–12 in once seedlings are 2 in tall; butterhead types form loose, tender heads with buttery inner leaves. Keep soil evenly moist and mulch to maintain cool root temperatures, as heat quickly triggers bolting. Harvest heads by cutting at the base when they feel moderately firm but before the center begins to elongate, which signals imminent bolting. Outer leaves can be removed as the head develops to stretch the harvest period over several weeks.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost into warm soil; suited to shorter seasons.
directions: A productive heirloom muskmelon well suited to short-season climates, bearing round 3-pound fruits with dark orange, firm, aromatic flesh. Direct sow into warmed soil after frost, or start indoors a couple of weeks early in cooler regions. Give vines room to sprawl and keep them evenly watered until the fruit begins to net and ripen. Harvest at full slip when the fruit releases from the vine with gentle pressure.

plant_note: Direct sow after the soil warms and frost risk is gone.
directions: Hearts of Gold is a beloved heirloom cantaloupe with fragrant, honey-sweet orange flesh, usually maturing at two to three pounds. Sow directly into warm, well-drained soil in full sun once frost danger has passed. Keep moisture even during fruit set and growth, then reduce watering as the melons ripen to boost flavor. Harvest when fruit slips easily from the vine and gives off a strong sweet aroma.

plant_note: Direct sow in cool soil; succession sow for a steady supply of mixed leaves.
directions: A blend of four red and green lettuces including bibb, romaine, crisphead, and leaf types for varied color and texture in one bed. Direct sow in cool soil and thin as plants grow, eating the thinnings as baby greens. Keep the soil consistently moist and give afternoon shade in warm spells to slow bolting. Harvest outer leaves for a cut-and-come-again supply or cut whole heads at maturity.

plant_note: Sow at or just after last frost; hemp grows very quickly and tolerates light frost at seedling stage but is damaged by hard freezes.
directions: Drill seed 3/4–1 in deep at 20–30 lb/acre for grain production (denser stands reduce branching and improve yield); hemp requires a federal or state license to grow legally in the United States. Plants are dioecious — female plants produce the seed-bearing flowers; for grain production allow plants to go naturally to seed and harvest when seeds are firm and no longer milky. Thresh and dry seed to below 10% moisture for storage; hulled hemp seed (hearts) is nutritionally rich in protein and balanced omega-3/6 fatty acids. Do not confuse grain hemp with high-THC varieties; certified grain cultivars contain less than 0.3% THC.

plant_note: Sow directly after the soil is reliably warm; lima beans rot in cold ground.
directions: This compact heirloom butterbean, introduced in the late 1800s, is loved for its reliability, mild flavor, and easy sprouting. Sow seeds directly into warm soil, as lima beans germinate poorly in cold or wet ground. The bushy plants need no staking and produce flat pods of small, creamy beans. Pick pods when plump for shelling fresh, or leave them to dry on the plant for storage beans.

plant_note: Direct sow in blocks after frost for good wind pollination.
directions: Hickory King is a historic heirloom white dent corn with unusually large kernels, ideal for grits, hominy, masa, and cornmeal. Sow directly into warm soil in blocks of at least four short rows to ensure good pollination. Give plenty of nitrogen and steady moisture as the tall stalks develop. Let ears dry fully on the stalk before harvesting for grinding or long-term storage.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after the last frost when nights stay above 50F.
directions: This West Virginia heirloom produces huge one-to-two-pound beefsteak fruits with striking yellow-and-red marbled coloring and sweet, low-acid flavor. Start seeds indoors about six weeks before the last frost and transplant once the soil warms. The indeterminate vines can reach five feet or more and need sturdy staking or caging. Support heavy fruit clusters and harvest when the marbled fruits give slightly to gentle pressure.

plant_note: Direct sow seeds in spring after danger of frost passes in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade. Scarify hard seed coats lightly with sandpaper or soak overnight before planting to speed germination. Hog Peanut grows best in light shade and will not thrive in exposed, dry, or heavily fertilized soil.
directions: Hog Peanut is a delicate, twining native legume vine of moist forest floors and shaded stream banks across eastern North America, producing two types of flowers: small lavender blossoms on climbing aerial stems that rarely set fruit, and inconspicuous cleistogamous flowers near the soil surface that produce fleshy, one-seeded pods underground — a remarkable adaptation for seed burial and dispersal. The underground fleshy seeds are rich, sweet, and bean-like in flavor, and were a significant food source for many Native American peoples including the Meskwaki, Cherokee, and Iroquois, who harvested them in fall; settlers called them hog peanuts because pigs eagerly rooted them from the soil. A nitrogen-fixing perennial that returns from taproots each spring in zones 5–9, Hog Peanut adds valuable biodiversity and soil health to shaded native plant gardens and woodland edges while providing food and cover for small wildlife. Direct sow after frost in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; established plants self-seed freely and colonize gradually.

plant_note: Direct sow into warm soil after frost; trellising saves space for the long vines.
directions: A gherkin cucumber producing seedless, bitter-free, uniform fruits perfect for cornichons and sweet pickles, best used from 1.5 to 4 inches long. Direct sow after the soil warms, spacing plants along a trellis for the vines that can reach 6 feet. Keep the soil evenly moist and mulched, since drought stress causes bitterness and misshapen fruit. Harvest small and often to keep the plant setting new cucumbers.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost into warm soil; compact vines suit containers.
directions: A heavy-yielding pickling cucumber ready early, with the ideal interior texture for pickles though also good fresh, harvested anywhere from 1.5 to 6 inches long. Direct sow after frost into warm soil and mulch to hold moisture. The compact 4 to 5 foot vines do well in containers or on a small trellis. Pick frequently and evenly water to keep fruits crisp and the plant productive for canning batches.

plant_note: Direct sow into warm soil after frost; needs a full warm season to mature.
directions: A delicata-type winter squash with rich, golden-orange flesh and a sweet, nutty flavor that is excellent baked and buttered. Direct sow into warm soil after frost, giving the trailing vines plenty of room to run. Water steadily while fruit forms, then let the rinds harden as the plants wind down. Harvest before hard frost when the skin is tough, and cure in a warm spot to deepen the sweetness for storage.

plant_note: Sow directly once frost is past and soil is warm.
directions: Honey Rock, an All-America Selections winner from 1933 also known as Sugar Rock, offers honey-sweet salmon-colored flesh and dependable performance. Direct sow seeds in warm, fertile soil in full sun after frost danger passes. Keep vines evenly watered during growth, then reduce moisture as fruit ripens to intensify sweetness. Harvest at full slip when the melon parts easily from the vine and smells fragrant.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil; plant in blocks for good pollination.
directions: A bicolor sweet corn with a luscious blend of white and yellow kernels bursting with flavor and tenderness far above store-bought. Direct sow after the last frost once soil warms, planting in blocks of at least four rows so wind pollination fills the ears. Feed with nitrogen and keep well watered through tasseling and silking. Harvest at the milk stage when silks brown and kernels squirt milky juice when pressed.

plant_note: Direct sow into warm soil after frost; compact semi-bush habit saves space.
directions: An All-America Selections winning butternut-type hybrid that is very productive, setting numerous 1 to 2 pound fruits on compact 2 to 3 foot vines with some powdery mildew resistance. Direct sow into warm soil after frost, thinning to the strongest seedlings per hill. The semi-bush habit suits smaller gardens; keep evenly watered through fruit set. Harvest when rinds turn tan and hard, then cure before storing.

plant_note: Best in hot-climate gardens; direct sow after frost into warm soil.
directions: Honeydew produces sweet, refreshing melons with crisp pale-green flesh that thrive in hot summer gardens. Sow seeds directly in warm soil and full sun after all frost has passed, giving the long-season vines ample room. Provide steady water during fruit development and a long, warm growing season for the fruit to sweeten fully. Unlike cantaloupe, honeydew does not slip, so harvest when the rind turns creamy and the blossom end softens slightly.

plant_note: Direct sow 1–2 weeks after last frost once soil is warm; in short-season climates, start indoors 3 weeks early.
directions: Sow seeds 1 inch deep in warm, well-drained soil enriched with compost, in hills of 4–5 seeds thinned to 2. Honeydew requires a longer, hotter season than cantaloupe; black plastic mulch is helpful in northern climates to boost soil warmth. Unlike cantaloupes, honeydews do not slip from the vine when ripe, so judge by skin color changing to creamy white, a waxy feel, and a sweet aroma from the blossom end. Cut fruits from the vine with a knife rather than pulling.

plant_note: Direct sow into warm soil after frost; needs a long warm season to ripen fully.
directions: A miniature butternut bred by crossing butternut with buttercup, giving intensely sweet 4 to 5 inch fruits with thin, edible skin that turns from green to terracotta at ripeness. Direct sow into warm soil after frost and let vines sprawl. Keep evenly watered while fruit develops, then ease off near harvest. Cut fruit when the rind is fully terracotta and cure to concentrate the rich, nutty flavor.

plant_note: Plant root cuttings 2–4 weeks before last frost; any root left in the soil will regenerate, so choose the site carefully as it becomes permanent.
directions: Plant 6–8 in root cuttings (thongs) at a 45-degree angle, 2–3 in deep, in deeply tilled, fertile soil; vertical planting produces rougher roots while angled planting encourages smoother, straight taproots. Horseradish is extremely vigorous and should be contained or given a dedicated bed where spreading is acceptable. Harvest main roots in autumn after the first hard frost triggers maximum heat and oil development. Save pencil-thick side roots to replant as next year's cuttings.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost so pumpkins ripen ahead of fall; long vines need space.
directions: The quintessential carving pumpkin developed in 1970s Massachusetts, with bright orange skin, strong handles, and good storage. Direct sow after frost in hills with plenty of room for the sprawling vines. Feed richly and water deeply while fruit sizes up, tucking straw under developing pumpkins to prevent rot. Harvest when the rind is hard and fully colored, leaving a length of stem intact.

plant_note: Direct sow 1–2 weeks after last frost once soil has warmed to 65°F; Hubbard requires a long, warm season so time carefully against fall frost dates.
directions: Plant 3–4 seeds 1 inch deep per hill in deeply worked, fertile soil, thinning to two vines after establishment. Hubbard squash produces very large, warty fruits with a hard blue-gray or orange shell and very sweet, dry, fine-grained flesh ideal for pie and soup. Allow vines to spread freely over 8–12 feet of ground, or train onto a very strong trellis and support individual fruits in slings. Fruits are ripe when the rind resists a fingernail, the skin has turned its characteristic color, and the stem is fully dry; cure for two weeks before storing.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant 1–2 weeks after frost danger passes once nights are consistently above 50°F.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in warm mix maintained at 78–82°F; germination is fastest and most even with bottom heat from a heat mat. Hungarian wax peppers produce thick-walled, banana-shaped fruits that ripen from pale yellow through orange to red; they range from mildly hot (1,000 Scoville) when yellow to moderately hot when fully red. Harvest yellow fruits at 65 days for pickling (the classic use), or allow to ripen through the color stages for fresh eating and sauces. Plants are heavy producers and benefit from a midseason application of balanced fertilizer to sustain yield.

plant_note: Direct sow seeds 1 inch deep after last frost in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun; hyacinth bean is a fast-growing vine requiring a sturdy trellis, fence, or arbor — it can reach 10–15 feet in a single season. Soak seeds in water 8–12 hours before planting to speed germination.
directions: Direct sow after last frost in full sun on a sturdy trellis or fence; hyacinth bean is one of the most ornamental edible vines available — the twining stems, deep purple-flushed leaves, spikes of purple or white flowers, and gleaming metallic-purple flat pods in late summer and fall make it as decorative as any flowering vine while also producing edible beans and pods. The immature green pods are used like snap beans; mature beans must be thoroughly cooked (raw beans contain hydrocyanic acid). In zone 9–11 it is perennial and grows as a woody vine; elsewhere treat as a vigorous warm-season annual that can cover a large arbor in weeks. One of the fastest-growing summer vines for a quick privacy screen or shade structure. Thomas Jefferson grew it at Monticello.

common_name: Ice Plant (Crystal Ice Plant, Common Ice Plant)
plant_note: Direct sow in fall in very well-drained, sandy, slightly alkaline to neutral, lean soil in full sun; ice plant is native to the coastal Mediterranean and South Africa and is entirely intolerant of clay, poor drainage, or humid conditions. In zone 9a, fall and winter culture avoids the summer humidity that causes disease.
directions: Sow in fall in well-drained, sandy, lean, slightly alkaline soil in full sun; the common ice plant is one of the most visually distinctive of all edible plants — the thick, fleshy, spatula-shaped leaves and stems are covered in large, glistening, fluid-filled papillae (water cells) that catch and reflect sunlight like tiny glass beads or ice crystals, giving the plant its enchanting name. The mildly salty, refreshing leaves are edible raw in salads, as a garnish, or as a substitute for spinach, with a texture somewhere between watercress and succulent. The plant is halophytic (salt-tolerant) and is often used to bind coastal sandy soils. It is a cool-season plant that grows through winter in zone 9–11 and dies in summer heat. The species is considered invasive in California coastal areas where it has altered soil chemistry — plant only in maintained garden areas.

plant_note: Direct sow in cool soil; this old variety tolerates both cold and heat.
directions: Also called Reine des Glaces, this 1883 Batavian summer-crisp lettuce is cold tolerant, heat tolerant, and slow to bolt, with frilly emerald leaves and crisp texture. Direct sow in cool soil and thin to give heads room to form. Keep evenly watered to avoid bitterness, and offer light afternoon shade during heat to extend the harvest. Cut whole heads at maturity or harvest outer leaves as needed.

plant_note: Sow in early spring or late summer for a fall crop; heat causes bolting.
directions: This heirloom crisphead forms compact, tight heads about eight inches across with firm, mild, refreshingly crunchy leaves. Sow in cool weather, either early spring or late summer, since heat triggers bolting and bitterness. Thin plants to give each head room to fill out and keep the soil consistently moist. Harvest when heads feel firm and full, cutting at the base in the cool part of the day.

plant_note: Direct sow; carrots resent transplanting. Keep seedbed moist until emergence.
directions: This AAS-winning heirloom yields long, tapered orange roots with fine-grained texture, outstanding sweetness, and thin, easy-to-peel skin. Sow seeds directly into loose, stone-free soil so the long roots can grow straight. Keep the seedbed consistently moist during the slow germination period, then thin seedlings to prevent crowding. Harvest once roots reach full size and sweeten further after a light frost.

plant_note: Plant individual cloves in fall a few weeks before the ground freezes for a summer harvest.
directions: A large, mild softneck garlic with 8 to 20 cloves per bulb and a robust, taste-test-winning flavor, well suited to braiding and long storage. Plant individual cloves pointed end up in fall, a few weeks before the ground freezes, and mulch heavily for winter protection. Keep beds weed-free and watered through spring, then stop watering as the lower leaves brown. Harvest in summer when several lower leaves have yellowed, and cure the bulbs in a dry, airy place.

plant_note: Start indoors; transplant after frost and stake the tall vines.
directions: A deep purple-black cherry tomato bred at Oregon State to be rich in anthocyanin antioxidants, borne in clusters of 6 to 8 two-ounce fruits. Start seeds indoors 4 to 6 weeks before the last frost and transplant deeply once weather warms. Stake or cage the 5-foot indeterminate vines and prune for airflow. Sun exposure deepens the purple color; harvest when the shoulders darken and the undersides turn red.

plant_note: Direct sow as early as soil can be worked; short vines need no support.
directions: A French petit pois variety producing peas half the usual size but packed with sweet flavor, often three dark-green pods per node. Direct sow several weeks before the last frost in cool, workable soil. The productive 28-inch vines are self-supporting but benefit from a low trellis, and the plant offers powdery mildew resistance. Harvest pods when plump and pick often for the tenderest baby peas.

common_name: Italian Red of Florence Bunching Onion
plant_note: Direct sow as soon as soil is workable; harvest young as scallions or let bulbs form.
directions: An Italian heirloom torpedo-shaped red onion used young as a mild scallion or grown on for elongated red bulbs. Direct sow thickly as soon as the soil can be worked, thinning for scallions and eating the thinnings. Keep the bed weed-free and evenly moist for tender stalks. Pull young for fresh use, or let plants mature and cure the bulbs for short-term storage.

plant_note: Start indoors; transplant after frost. Determinate fruit ripens in a concentrated window good for canning.
directions: A classic heirloom paste tomato with firm, meaty, few-seeded 3-inch oblong fruits on compact, very productive determinate vines. Start seeds indoors 4 to 6 weeks before the last frost and transplant deeply after the weather warms. Because most fruit ripens together, it is an excellent choice for canning and sauce. Provide a cage for support, water evenly to prevent blossom-end rot, and harvest when fully colored.

plant_note: Sow in spring or late summer; cool weather mellows the bitterness.
directions: Despite its name, this is an Italian chicory with vivid burgundy stems and deeply lobed green leaves, grown for flavorful bitter greens. Sow directly in cool weather, spring or late summer, when the flavor is at its most balanced. Thin seedlings and keep soil moist to encourage tender leaves. Harvest young leaves for salads or let plants mature for cooking greens with a robust chicory bite.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost into warm soil; compact vines suit large containers.
directions: A tiny ornamental and edible pumpkin, 3 to 4 inches across and 2 inches tall, useful for decorating or as an individual stuffed-and-baked serving. Direct sow after frost into warm soil, with the compact 3 to 5 foot vines producing 6 to 10 fruits each. Keep evenly watered through fruit set and slip straw beneath the little pumpkins. Harvest when fully orange with a hardened rind, leaving a bit of stem for display.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost; count back from Halloween for timing.
directions: This classic heirloom pumpkin has smooth orange skin, dependable size, and the perfect round shape for carving jack-o'-lanterns. Sow directly into warm, rich soil after frost, allowing the sprawling vines plenty of space. Feed and water consistently as fruit sets and swells, tucking a board or straw under each pumpkin to prevent rot. Harvest when rinds harden and turn deep orange, cutting with a length of stem attached for better keeping.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost so pumpkins ripen in time for fall carving.
directions: A classic carving pumpkin since 1956, about 10 inches tall and wide at 10 to 18 pounds, with good keeping quality. Direct sow after frost in hills, giving the vines ample room to run. Feed richly and water deeply through fruit development, tucking straw under the pumpkins to keep rinds clean and rot-free. Harvest when the skin is fully orange and hard, cutting with a length of stem attached.

plant_note: Direct sow into warm soil; excels in hot, dry climates.
directions: This historic dwarf bush butterbean, introduced in 1888, is prized for heat tolerance, resilience, and dependable yields in hot, dry conditions. Sow directly into warm soil after frost, since lima beans will not germinate in cold ground. The compact bush plants need no support and produce speckled, buttery-textured beans. Harvest young pods for fresh shelling or let them dry fully on the plant for storage.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost into warmed soil; succession sow for a long harvest.
directions: A favorite green bean prized for long, straight, stringless dark-green pods that stay tender and sweet, held high on upright plants to avoid tip rot. Direct sow after the soil warms past 65F, spacing seeds a few inches apart. This variety tolerates heat and cold better than most, so it produces reliably across the season. Pick pods young and often to keep the plants setting new beans.

plant_note: Start indoors early; peppers need warm soil and nights above 55F to transplant.
directions: This classic heirloom produces thick-walled, three-to-four-inch fruits with moderate heat and robust flavor for stuffing, slicing, or grilling. Start seeds indoors eight weeks before the last frost, as peppers need a long warm season. Transplant only after nights stay reliably above 55 degrees and the soil has warmed. Pick fruits green for typical jalapeno flavor, or let them ripen red for extra sweetness and heat.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in a warm starting mix and maintain soil temperature above 75°F for best germination. Transplant to the garden once nights are reliably warm and the soil is fully settled. For hotter fruit, allow stress by reducing water slightly as fruits mature. Harvest at 3–4 inches long while still firm and dark green, or leave on the plant until red for a milder, slightly sweeter heat.

plant_note: Earlier and more productive than large globe types in cool summers.
directions: Long, slender, thin-skinned fruit with tender flesh that cooks quickly and rarely needs peeling. Start seed indoors in warmth and transplant into black plastic mulch to boost soil temperature in cooler regions. Feed with a balanced fertilizer and keep evenly watered to prevent bitter, seedy fruit. Cut fruit with pruners while young and glossy, typically at 6 to 8 inches long. These plants often outyield globe eggplants where summers are short or mild.

plant_note: Sow in blocks after frost; isolate from other corn to keep it true.
directions: This compact heirloom reaches about five feet tall, making it well suited to smaller gardens while producing two or three short, full ears per stalk. Sow directly into warm soil in blocks of several rows to ensure good wind pollination. Keep it isolated from sweet or dent corn so the popping quality stays true. Let ears dry fully on the stalk, then cure the tender, low-hull kernels further indoors before popping.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost into warm soil; long vines need generous space.
directions: An Australian heirloom pumpkin with striking silvery blue-grey skin, deep ribbing, and a drum shape, plus sweet, nutty, stringless orange flesh, in 6 to 12 pound fruits. Direct sow after frost into warm soil, giving the long vines plenty of room. Water deeply and feed richly while fruit sizes up, resting pumpkins on straw. Harvest when the rind hardens and coloring is complete, and cure for both decoration and cooking.

plant_note: Direct sow with fresh seed once soil is workable; roots sweeten after fall frost.
directions: A canker-resistant parsnip that holds well in the ground, letting you leave roots to sweeten over a long 110-day season. Direct sow fresh seed shallowly into loose, stone-free soil, keeping the surface moist through the slow germination. Thin seedlings to a few inches apart for long, well-formed roots. Harvest after fall frosts, which convert starches to sugar for the sweetest flavor.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant in early spring; tolerates light frost.
directions: This historic heirloom, dating to the 1840s, forms distinctive pointed heads with sweet flavor and matures early on compact plants ideal for small gardens. Start seeds indoors about six weeks before the last frost and transplant hardened seedlings in early spring. Cabbage tolerates light frost, so it can go out before tender crops. Keep soil evenly moist and harvest the conical heads when firm to avoid splitting.

plant_note: Plant tubers 2–4 weeks before the last frost; Jerusalem artichokes are extremely cold-hardy and will regrow reliably each spring.
directions: Plant small, golf-ball-sized tubers 4 in deep in any well-drained soil; sunchokes tolerate poor, dry conditions and will crowd out weeds once established. Contain plantings with a buried barrier or dedicate a permanent bed, as any tuber left behind will regrow. Harvest tubers after the first hard frost sweetens them by converting inulin to sugars, digging with a fork and leaving the smallest tubers to perpetuate the planting. Store freshly dug tubers in the refrigerator as they do not keep well at room temperature.

plant_note: Start indoors with warmth; transplant well after frost when nights stay warm.
directions: A container-friendly eggplant with a tidy habit and glossy oval purple fruits best picked at 3 to 4.5 inches long. Start seeds indoors 6 to 8 weeks before the last frost with bottom heat, then transplant once nights stay reliably warm. Keep evenly watered and mulched, and harvest regularly from this prolific plant to keep new fruit setting. Pick while the skin is glossy for the firmest, best texture.

plant_note: Needs a long, hot season; start indoors and transplant into warm soil.
directions: Also called Mexican turnip or yam bean, jicama is a warm-season vine grown for crisp, juicy tubers with a lightly sweet, nutty flavor. It needs a long, hot growing season, so start seeds indoors and transplant into warm soil, providing a trellis for the climbing vines. Only the swollen root is edible; the pods, leaves, and seeds are toxic and should not be eaten. Dig the tubers before the first frost, once they have had four to five months to bulk up.

plant_note: Sow 2–4 weeks after last frost into warm soil; jicama needs a long frost-free season of at least 5 months and thrives in zone 9–11 Gulf Coast conditions.
directions: Sow scarified seeds 1 in deep in loose, deep, well-drained soil 2–4 weeks after the last frost once soil is thoroughly warm. Jicama is a vigorous vine requiring a sturdy trellis; pinch flower buds as they appear throughout the season — leaving flowers reduces tuber size dramatically since the plant diverts energy to seed set. Begin harvesting small tubers at about 5 months; larger tubers become more fibrous if left past 9 months. Only the roots are edible — all above-ground parts, including seeds and pods, contain rotenone and are toxic.

plant_note: Sow after last frost when soil is warm; jicama requires a long, frost-free season of at least 5 months to form substantial roots.
directions: Sow seeds 1 in deep in warm, well-drained soil after soaking them overnight to speed germination. Remove all flower buds as they appear throughout the season; allowing flowering diverts energy from root development and shortens the season significantly. Vines can climb and should be trellised or allowed to sprawl; note that all aerial parts of the plant (seeds, pods, leaves) are toxic — only the root is edible. Harvest roots after the first cool weather of autumn when skins turn tan and cork-like.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after all danger of frost has passed and soil has warmed.
directions: Start these Italian heirloom frying peppers indoors 6 to 8 weeks before the last frost, keeping the soil warm at 70 to 90 degrees for reliable germination. Transplant seedlings out only after nights stay above 55 degrees, spacing plants 18 to 24 inches apart in full sun. The long, tapered 6 to 12 inch fruits ripen from green to glossy red and are exceptionally sweet, making them ideal for frying. Provide consistent moisture and a light feeding once fruit sets to keep plants productive through the season.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant 2 weeks after frost danger has passed once soil is warm.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in warm starting mix at 75–80°F and transplant after last frost into fertile, well-drained soil. This Italian heirloom from Basilicata bears long, thin-walled, crinkled fruits that dangle in clusters and turn from green to brilliant red at maturity. The fruits are nearly heat-free and intensely sweet, making them outstanding for frying in olive oil, roasting, or drying. Harvest when fully red for peak sweetness, or dry on a string in a warm, airy location — the thin walls dehydrate rapidly and concentrate flavor.

plant_note: Direct sow 1–2 weeks after last frost once soil temperature reaches 65°F; count back from first fall frost to confirm enough growing days.
directions: Sow 2–3 seeds 1 inch deep per hill in deeply amended, compost-rich soil and thin to the strongest vine after the first true leaves appear. Kabocha is a Japanese winter squash of the Cucurbita maxima species; its flattened, dark-green fruits have dense, dry, sweet orange flesh superior to many other squash types. Harvest when the skin is very hard, the stem corky and dry, and a small section of rind scratched with a fingernail shows no damage. Cure at room temperature for 10–14 days before storing; properly cured kabocha keeps four to six months in a cool, dry room.

plant_note: Indian heirloom; heat-loving and quick to ripen.
directions: A striking Indian heirloom melon striped in green, orange, and cream with sweet, pale flesh. Start seed indoors in warmth and transplant into fertile, well-drained soil once nights stay warm. Train vines with room to sprawl and mulch to keep fruit clean and warm. The dramatic rind color develops as the fruit ripens, and mature melons detach easily from the stem. Cut back on water near harvest to concentrate the sugars in the flesh.

plant_note: Direct sow or transplant 2–4 weeks before last frost in spring; sow again in late summer for a fall and overwintering crop.
directions: Sow seed 1/4 in deep and thin to 12–18 in; kale tolerates crowding better than most brassicas but produces larger leaves with proper spacing. Side-dress with compost or a nitrogen fertilizer midseason to support continued leaf production. Harvest outer leaves first, leaving the growing center intact to keep the plant producing. Flavor becomes noticeably sweeter after the first hard frost, and plants often survive winter in zones 7 and warmer.

plant_note: Siberian kale; reliable brassica microgreen sown densely on trays.
directions: This Siberian kale germinates quickly, so sow it densely on a moist tray and blackout for just a couple of days. Once the stems stretch, uncover and provide bright light to build sturdy, deep-green shoots. Keep the surface evenly moist and give gentle airflow to discourage mold. Cut the tender, mild greens at the cotyledon or first-true-leaf stage after eight to ten days.

plant_note: Propagate by stem cuttings rooted in water or moist medium; transplant to a partially shaded, moist garden location after rooting. Avoid full sun, which bleaches and stresses the foliage in hot climates.
directions: Plant in moist, rich, well-drained soil in partial shade to filtered sun; katuk is a compact tropical shrub of Southeast Asian origin widely grown as one of the most nutritious perennial vegetables for tropical and subtropical gardens. The small, oval leaves have a pleasant, slightly sweet, nutty flavor and are eaten raw in salads, stir-fried, or added to soups; they are exceptionally high in protein (up to 7% fresh weight), Vitamin C, iron, and carotenes. Harvest young shoot tips of 4–6 inches continuously — the plant produces new growth prolifically in warm weather. In zone 9 it may die back in winter but usually re-grows from roots with mulch protection. Important caution: raw katuk should not be consumed in very large quantities daily, as excessive raw consumption has been linked to respiratory issues; normal culinary use is safe.

plant_note: Sow after last frost in warm soil; kenaf grows tall rapidly and is grown for bast fiber, animal fodder, and paper pulp.
directions: Direct-sow seed 1/2–1 in deep at 25–30 lb/acre for fiber production (narrower spacing promotes taller, less branched stems); kenaf is a warm-season annual related to okra that can reach 14 feet in a single season. For fiber, harvest at early flowering before stems become too woody — cut at ground level and ret the stalks in water for 10–20 days to separate the long bast fibers from the woody core. The core material (hurds) is useful as animal bedding or biomass fuel, while the bast fibers make durable rope, sacking, and paper. Young leaves and shoots are edible and nutritious — commonly cooked as a green in West African cuisine.

plant_note: Direct sow into warm soil at the base of a trellis or poles.
directions: This time-tested heirloom snap bean produces heavy yields of long, smooth green pods that are stringless when picked young. Sow directly into warm soil at the base of sturdy poles or a trellis, since the vigorous vines climb six feet or more. Keep the plants evenly watered and pick pods frequently to keep them tender and productive. Regular harvesting encourages the vines to set new flushes of beans all season.

plant_note: Direct sow after all danger of frost has passed and soil has warmed to at least 60 degrees.
directions: Direct sow this vigorous 1800s heirloom pole bean once the soil warms, planting seeds an inch deep at the base of a sturdy trellis or pole 6 to 8 feet tall. Provide support early, as the climbing vines produce heavy crops of 9 inch curved, oval pods that are stringless when young. Pick regularly to encourage continued production and enjoy fresh, or leave pods to dry for shelling beans. Beans dislike root disturbance, so sow directly rather than transplanting and keep evenly moist during flowering and pod set.

plant_note: Start indoors in late winter and transplant seedlings out around the last frost.
directions: Start King Richard leeks indoors 8 to 10 weeks before the last frost, then transplant seedlings the thickness of a pencil into the garden spaced 4 to 6 inches apart. This early variety produces extra-long white stems, so hill or trench the plants and mound soil around the stems as they grow to blanch and lengthen the tender white portion. Leeks tolerate cold well and can be harvested from late summer through fall. Keep the soil consistently moist and weed-free for the best-quality stems.

plant_note: Pickling type; succession sow for a steady supply.
directions: Kirby is the classic short, bumpy, thin-skinned pickling cucumber with crisp, dense flesh. Direct sow after the soil warms, either in hills or along a low trellis for cleaner fruit. Provide abundant, even water since pickling cucumbers are especially prone to bitterness and hollow centers under drought. Pick daily at 3 to 5 inches for pickling, as the vines produce heavily over a short window. Succession sowing every two to three weeks extends the harvest into late summer.

plant_note: Sow directly into warm soil; cowpeas thrive in summer heat.
directions: This productive bush-type cowpea produces plump, succulent peas with a distinctive purple eye inside richly colored purple pods. Sow directly into warm soil, as cowpeas love summer heat and tolerate dry conditions. The compact plants need no staking and set a heavy crop of easy-to-shell pods. Pick pods when the hulls turn purple and the peas inside are full for fresh use, or leave them to dry for storage.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last spring frost, or sow in late summer for a fall crop.
directions: Direct sow seed 1/4 in deep in cool, fertile soil and thin plants to 5–8 in to give the swollen stem room to develop. Keep soil consistently moist, as drought stress causes the bulb to become woody and strongly flavored. Harvest when the swollen stem is 2–3 in across for best tenderness; larger bulbs become fibrous. Pull the whole plant and trim the leaves before storing in the refrigerator.

plant_note: Sow 2–4 weeks before last frost in spring, or 6–8 weeks before first fall frost; succession sow every 2–3 weeks.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep, thinning to 4 inches for baby greens or 8 inches for full-sized plants. Komatsuna is one of the most versatile Asian brassicas — milder than mustard, more heat-tolerant than spinach, and remarkably cold-hardy. Begin harvesting at 30 days for tender salad leaves, or allow plants to reach full size at 8–14 inches for stir-frying and braising. Water consistently to keep leaves mild and succulent.

plant_note: Plant corms 3–4 inches deep in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade after soil warms to 65°F; konjac corms require 2–3 seasons to reach full edible size. In zone 9+ the corm is winter-hardy with mulch; in zone 8 dig and store over winter.
directions: Plant corms in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; konjac is a remarkable shade-tolerant tropical edible related to taro, producing a single enormous, deeply divided compound leaf on a spotted, mottled stem resembling a snake — an unmistakable tropical accent in the shaded garden. The corm is the source of konjac flour (glucomannan), used to produce shirataki noodles, konjac jelly, and low-calorie dietary products throughout East Asia. When flowering (in years without leaves), the plant produces a massive, offensive-smelling inflorescence similar to corpse flower. Before eating, the raw corm must be cooked thoroughly — it contains calcium oxalate crystals that cause intense mouth burning when raw. Established corms reach several pounds over 3–4 seasons. An outstanding tropical foliage plant for shaded Gulf Coast gardens where its bold, architectural leaf is exceptional.

plant_note: Direct sow after the soil has warmed and frost danger has passed.
directions: Direct sow this compact Japanese kabocha after the soil warms, planting seeds an inch deep in hills spaced 3 to 4 feet apart in full sun. Each vine produces small 3 to 4 pound fruits with sweet, fine-grained flesh reminiscent of sweet potato. Allow the squash to mature fully on the vine until the rind hardens and the stem corks, then cure in a warm spot to improve storage and flavor. Give the vines room to sprawl and provide steady moisture, especially while fruit is sizing up.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring or late summer for a fall crop; flavor sweetens after frost.
directions: Sow this 18th-century Tuscan heirloom in early spring for a summer harvest or in mid to late summer for fall and winter greens, as its flavor sweetens after frost. Direct sow a half inch deep and thin seedlings to 12 to 18 inches apart. Harvest tender young leaves as salad greens in about 21 days, or let plants mature for full-size, strap-shaped dark blue-green leaves prized by cooks. Pick outer leaves first to keep plants producing over a long season, and provide steady moisture for tender growth.

plant_note: Sow in spring or late summer; flavor sweetens after frost.
directions: Also known as dinosaur or Tuscan kale, this heirloom features deep blue-green, bumpy-textured leaves with rich, mild flavor. Sow directly in spring or late summer, as it is a cold-hardy crop whose flavor sweetens after a frost. Thin plants to give the tall, upright rosettes room to develop. Harvest outer leaves from the bottom up, letting the center keep producing for a long, continuous cutting season.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost into warm, rich soil.
directions: This heirloom is grown for its unique hulless seeds, which are soft-shelled pepitas that need no peeling for roasting or snacking. Sow directly into warm, rich soil after frost, giving the vines plenty of room to spread. Keep moisture and nutrients steady as fruit develops. Harvest the mature pumpkins, then scoop and dry the green hulless seeds for a homegrown supply of pepitas.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost danger has passed and soil has warmed.
directions: Direct sow this vigorous heirloom squash in warm soil, planting in hills spaced well apart to accommodate 10 to 20 foot vines. Beyond its striking orange and green coloring, Lakota is a superior baking variety with fine-grained, sweet, nutty flesh. Let the pear-shaped fruits ripen fully and the rind harden before harvesting, then cure in a warm, dry spot to improve storage life. Give the sprawling vines full sun and consistent moisture while the fruit develops.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after frost once soil warms.
directions: This classic indeterminate variety yields abundant clusters of bright red cherry tomatoes with fresh, zesty flavor all summer. Start seeds indoors about six weeks before the last frost and transplant once the soil warms. Stake or cage the vigorous vines and remove suckers if you want to manage the sprawl. Pick tomatoes as they ripen to keep the plants setting new fruit continuously through the season.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring as soon as soil can be worked, and again in late summer for fall.
directions: Sow Lavewa spinach directly in early spring as soon as the soil can be worked, and again in late summer for a fall crop, since spinach bolts in heat and long days. This open-pollinated variety offers good heat tolerance and mildew resistance, rivaling hybrids for flavor. Thin seedlings to about 4 to 6 inches apart and keep the soil consistently moist and cool. Harvest outer leaves individually or cut whole plants, and provide light afternoon shade to extend the harvest in warm weather.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost; succession sow every 2 weeks through spring, then again in fall.
directions: Scatter or row-sow seed on the surface or barely cover with 1/8 in of soil, as light aids germination. Thin to 6–10 in and use thinnings as baby greens in salads to avoid waste. Harvest outer leaves continuously once plants are 4–6 in tall, or cut the entire plant an inch above the soil line for a cut-and-come-again harvest. Bolting in warm weather makes leaves bitter; provide shade cloth in late spring to extend the season.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before the last frost; transplant seedlings into deep trenches after the last frost date.
directions: Sow seed 1/4 in deep indoors in flats and thin to 2 in; transplant pencil-thick seedlings into 6-in-deep trenches, dropping each plant into a dibber hole and filling with water rather than soil to encourage a long white shank. Gradually fill trenches and hill up soil around stems as they grow to blanch the shanks. Leeks tolerate hard frost and can be left in the ground well into winter for a late harvest. Dig as needed once shanks reach 1 in or more in diameter.

plant_note: Direct sow into warm soil after frost, or start indoors briefly.
directions: This heirloom produces small, round cucumbers with bright yellow skin and a clean, mild, crisp taste despite the citrusy name. Sow directly into warm soil after frost, or give it a short indoor start in cool climates. Trellis the vines to keep the round fruits clean and easy to spot. Harvest when the skin turns pale yellow and the cucumbers are about the size of an egg for the best flavor and texture.

plant_note: Sow in warm soil; seedlings resent cold, wet conditions.
directions: This heirloom bears round, yellow, lemon-sized fruit with sweet, mild, non-bitter flesh. Sow seeds an inch deep in warm soil and grow on a trellis to keep the round fruit clean and easy to spot. Keep evenly watered to avoid bitterness and misshapen fruit during dry spells. Harvest while the skin is still pale yellow and before it turns deep gold and seedy. Frequent picking keeps the prolific vines producing until frost.

plant_note: Start indoors early; this species is slow to germinate and needs a long, warm season.
directions: Start this Peruvian aji pepper indoors 8 to 10 weeks before the last frost, as baccatum peppers germinate slowly and need a long, warm growing season. Provide bottom heat and warm soil around 80 degrees for best germination, then transplant out well after frost when nights stay warm. The bright yellow, wrinkled pods rate 30,000 to 50,000 Scoville units with a distinct citrus flavor and lemony aroma. Give plants full sun, stake them if they grow tall, and harvest fruits fully colored for the best flavor.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost; lentils prefer cool growing conditions and deteriorate in summer heat.
directions: Sow seeds 1 in deep in rows 18 in apart in well-drained, moderately fertile soil; avoid high-nitrogen fertilizers that delay podding. The slender, branching plants benefit from brushy twigs as supports to keep pods off the ground and improve air circulation. Harvest the entire plant when the majority of lower pods have turned tan but before they shatter. Thresh by beating dried plants on a tarp and winnow lightly to clean seed before storage.

plant_note: Light-sensitive seed germinates best cool; sow shallow on moist medium.
