directions: Sow lettuce seed thinly pressed onto the surface of a moist tray, since it germinates best in cooler conditions and with some light. Keep only a light cover for a day or two, then bring the seedlings into bright light to prevent leggy growth. Mist gently and avoid overwatering, as tender lettuce seedlings rot easily. Harvest the mild, sweet, crunchy greens after about ten days to two weeks once the cotyledons are fully expanded.

plant_note: Sow 2–3 weeks after last frost; lima beans demand warm soil and will rot in cold, wet ground.
directions: Sow seeds 1 to 1.5 in deep in loose, warm soil with the eye of the bean facing downward. Lima beans are sensitive to drought during flowering; irregular moisture can cause blossom drop and reduced pod set. For fresh shell use, harvest pods when they are bright green and plump but before they begin to fade or yellow. Allow pods to dry completely on the vine for dry bean storage.

plant_note: Heat-loving Spanish heirloom; mulch to retain warmth and moisture.
directions: A Spanish heirloom with teardrop fruit striped purple and white and a notably sweet, mild flavor. Give it a long warm season, starting seed indoors early and transplanting into the warmest bed available. Fruit skin is thin and bruises easily, so handle carefully and harvest with shears. Pick when fruit is 5 to 7 inches and still shiny, before seeds harden. Steady watering prevents blossom-end problems and keeps flesh creamy.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring; carrots resent transplanting so always sow in place.
directions: Direct sow this French baby carrot in loose, stone-free soil, since carrots dislike transplanting and forked roots result from compacted ground. Sow seed shallowly and keep the surface consistently moist until germination, which can take two to three weeks. Thin seedlings to about an inch apart so the slender 3 inch roots can size up sweet and crisp. Little Finger is ideal for shallow beds and containers and is best harvested young; succession sow every few weeks for a continuous supply.

plant_note: Direct sow; ideal for containers and shallow beds.
directions: This heirloom baby carrot produces small, vibrant orange roots packed with sweet flavor, bred originally for canning but excellent fresh or in containers. Sow seeds directly into loose soil and keep the bed evenly moist during the slow germination. Thin seedlings so the short roots can size up cleanly. Harvest while the roots are still small and tender for the sweetest, most delicate flavor.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring and succession sow; sow in late summer for a fall crop.
directions: Sow this English heirloom mini-romaine directly in cool weather, barely covering the seed since lettuce needs light to germinate. The petite heads reach just 6 inches tall and 4 inches wide, forming the perfect size for a single-serving salad with tender, sweet, succulent leaves. Thin plants to 6 to 8 inches apart and keep them evenly moist to prevent bitterness and bolting. Succession sow every couple of weeks and give afternoon shade in warm weather to extend the harvest.

plant_note: Direct sow as soon as the soil can be worked in early spring, and again for a fall crop.
directions: Direct sow this compact heirloom shelling pea as soon as the soil can be worked in early spring, since peas thrive in cool weather. The short vines grow only 18 to 24 inches tall and do not require support, making them ideal for small gardens and containers. Plants produce an abundance of 3 to 4 inch pods filled with sweet, tender peas perfect for fresh eating. Keep the soil evenly moist during flowering and pod fill, and harvest promptly to encourage continued production.

plant_note: Sow in spring or late summer; heat causes bolting and bitterness.
directions: This Italian heirloom loose-leaf lettuce forms frilly rosettes of magenta-red and green leaves with mild flavor and striking color. Sow directly in cool weather, spring or late summer, since heat prompts bolting and bitterness. Sow in succession for a continuous supply of tender leaves. Harvest outer leaves as a cut-and-come-again crop, or cut whole rosettes when full, keeping soil evenly moist throughout.

plant_note: Time so the long crop matures into cool fall weather; flavor sweetens after frost.
directions: This time-tested heirloom from the late 1800s is valued for compact plants, dependable yields, and excellent cold tolerance. Start seeds indoors and transplant so the long-season crop matures into cool fall weather, when the sprouts taste sweetest after light frosts. Keep plants well watered and side-dress with nitrogen for steady growth. Harvest the firm sprouts from the bottom of the stalk upward as they reach about an inch across.

plant_note: Start indoors in early to mid summer and transplant for a fall harvest; flavor sweetens after frost.
directions: Start this heirloom Brussels sprout indoors and transplant for a fall harvest, as the sprouts sweeten and firm up after light frosts. Give plants plenty of water, rich soil, and a long season, spacing them 18 to 24 inches apart in full sun. Row covers help protect young plants from cabbage worms and other pests. Harvest sprouts from the bottom of the stalk upward as they reach about an inch in diameter, and top the plants a few weeks before harvest to encourage uniform sizing.

plant_note: Start indoors early; transplant only into warm soil with warm nights.
directions: This prolific Italian-style heirloom produces slender, club-shaped fruits with glossy purple skin and tender white flesh, up to about four per plant. Start seeds indoors eight weeks before the last frost, since eggplant needs a long, warm season. Transplant into warm soil once nights stay reliably above 55 degrees. Harvest the fruits while the skin is firm and glossy for the best mild flavor and texture.

plant_note: Start indoors with warmth and transplant well after frost when soil and nights are warm.
directions: Start this heirloom eggplant indoors 6 to 8 weeks before the last frost, providing bottom heat and warm soil for good germination. Transplant only after nights are reliably warm, as eggplant is very cold sensitive, spacing plants 18 to 24 inches apart in full sun. The long, slender purple fruits are flavorful in countless dishes and are best harvested young when the skin is glossy. Stake taller plants, keep them evenly watered, and pick fruits regularly to encourage continued production.

plant_note: Root 4–6 inch stem cuttings in water or moist mix after last frost; they root in 1–2 weeks and are ready to transplant when roots reach 1 inch.
directions: Root cuttings easily in water and transplant to fertile, well-drained soil in partial shade; the plant sprawls attractively as a groundcover or can be trained onto a low trellis. Harvest leaves and shoot tips regularly; the raw leaves have a crunchy, mild, slightly succulent texture and are popular in Southeast Asian salads and as an anti-diabetic traditional food. Unlike the closely related Okinawa spinach, this species has smooth (not wavy-edged) leaves without the purple underside. Bring cuttings indoors before first frost for overwintering; in frost-free areas it grows year-round and can be lightly sheared to encourage fresh growth.

plant_note: Plant rhizome sections in still or slow-moving water 1–2 feet deep after last frost when water temperature stays above 65°F.
directions: Plant intact rhizome sections (with at least one growing tip) horizontally in the mud bottom of a pond, tub, or container submerged under 12–18 inches of still water; never allow the growing tip to dry out. Full sun is essential — lotus will produce only foliage and few flowers in shade. Harvest rhizomes in fall once foliage dies back by reaching into the mud and carefully pulling out the chain of large, starchy sections; the distinctive lacy, hole-patterned cross-section is a signature feature. Rhizomes must be cooked before eating; they are eaten stir-fried, pickled, or braised throughout East and Southeast Asia and have a crunchy texture even after cooking.

plant_note: Start indoors 3–4 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed. Luffa requires a long, hot season.
directions: Nick or soak seeds for 24 hours before sowing 1/2 inch deep indoors 3–4 weeks before last frost to give this long-season crop a head start. Provide a very sturdy trellis, as vines can reach 15 feet and carry heavy fruit. For eating, harvest young fruits at 4–6 inches before the fibrous skeleton hardens. For sponges, allow fruits to fully mature and dry on the vine until the skin browns, then soak in water, peel, and rinse out the seeds.

plant_note: Needs a long, hot season; start indoors and transplant onto a strong trellis.
directions: This vigorous climbing gourd serves two purposes: harvest young fruits as a tender, okra-like vegetable, or let them mature and dry into natural loofah sponges. It needs a long, hot season, so start seeds indoors and transplant onto a sturdy trellis once soil is warm. The heavy vines produce long, hanging fruits that require strong support. For sponges, leave the gourds on the vine until the skin browns and the interior fibers dry, then peel, shake out the seeds, and rinse.

plant_note: Start indoors early to ensure a long enough season, then transplant after frost.
directions: Luffa needs a long, warm season, so start seeds indoors 4 to 6 weeks before the last frost and transplant after the soil warms. Grow the vigorous vines, which can reach 20 feet, on a sturdy trellis so the 12 to 24 inch gourds hang straight. For edible use, harvest fruits young and tender; for sponges, leave gourds on the vine until the skins turn brown and lightweight, then peel and shake out the seeds. Provide full sun, rich soil, and steady moisture to support the long growing period.

plant_note: Sow 4–6 weeks before first frost for fall and winter harvest; also sow in very early spring as soon as soil can be worked.
directions: Broadcast or row-sow seed at 1/4 in depth in cool soil and keep moist; mache is extraordinarily cold-tolerant and grows slowly through winter in mild climates. Thin to 4–6 in for well-formed rosettes, though closer spacing produces acceptable baby greens. Harvest entire rosettes by cutting at the soil line, or pinch individual rosettes for salad use. Mache does not tolerate heat and will quickly bolt and disappear as spring temperatures rise; enjoy it as a winter green.

plant_note: Plant individual cloves in fall a few weeks before the ground freezes for harvest the following summer.
directions: Plant this Porcelain-type hardneck garlic in fall, a few weeks before the ground freezes, so cloves establish roots before winter. Separate the bulbs into individual cloves just before planting and set them pointed end up, 2 inches deep and 6 to 8 inches apart, then mulch heavily. Each bulb yields 4 to 7 jumbo cloves with an assertive flavor. In early summer, snap off the curling scapes to direct energy into the bulbs, and harvest when the lower leaves brown, then cure in a dry, airy spot.

plant_note: Direct sow after all frost danger has passed and soil has warmed.
directions: Direct sow this AAS-winning purple pole bean once the soil warms, planting seeds an inch deep at the base of a tall trellis or pole. The vigorous climbing vines produce striking 5 to 6 inch pods with rich purple skins and tender green interiors that offer sweet, crisp flavor. The purple color also makes the pods easy to spot at harvest. Pick regularly to keep the vines productive, and provide full sun with consistent moisture during flowering and pod set.

plant_note: Start indoors 4 weeks before last frost; transplant once nights stay above 55°F — the heat-season answer to spinach for zone 8–11 gardens.
directions: Soak seeds overnight before sowing to soften the hard seed coat; germination is slow but improves significantly with scarification and warm soil above 70°F. Provide a trellis or fence — the twining vines reach 6–10 feet and produce heavily when they climb. Harvest young leaves and stem tips frequently; the leaves are mucilaginous (similar to okra) and excellent in stir-fries, soups, and anywhere cooked greens are used. The red-stemmed variety (Basella rubra) is slightly more ornamental and equally productive — both tolerate heat and humidity that quickly kills regular spinach.

plant_note: Plant corms or corm pieces with at least one eye, 4–6 inches deep, after soil warms to 65°F and all frost danger is past. Keeps producing side corms for subsequent harvests.
directions: Plant corms in fertile, loose, consistently moist but well-drained soil in partial shade; malanga grows naturally in Caribbean and South American forest edges and is more tolerant of shade than taro. The arrow-shaped leaves are ornamental as well as edible (properly cooked), and the large starchy corms have a nuttier, creamier flavor than taro. All parts must be thoroughly cooked before eating as raw malanga contains calcium oxalate crystals. Harvest corms when leaves begin to yellow and die back, typically 7–9 months after planting; the central corm and surrounding cormels can all be harvested. In zone 9 store unharvested corms with heavy mulch over winter or dig and store indoors above 50°F.

plant_note: Direct sow as soon as soil can be worked in early spring, and again in late summer for fall.
directions: Direct sow this heirloom snow pea in early spring as soon as the soil can be worked, since peas prefer cool conditions. The vigorous vines reach 5 to 6 feet tall and benefit from a trellis or netting to keep pods clean and easy to pick. Harvest the large, sweet, tender flat pods before the peas swell for the best flavor in stir-fries and fresh eating. Keep the soil evenly moist during flowering and pod development, and pick frequently to prolong the harvest.

plant_note: Sow in mid to late summer for a fall crop; this winter radish needs cool, shortening days to size up.
directions: Sow this Chinese beauty-heart radish in summer or early fall, as it needs cool weather and shortening days to develop its large, mild roots. Direct sow a half inch deep and thin seedlings to 3 to 4 inches apart to give the 3 inch round roots room to swell, some reaching up to a pound. The greenish-white exterior hides a vivid fuchsia interior that is crisp and mildly sweet. Keep the soil evenly moist for tender roots and harvest before hard freezes.

plant_note: Start indoors early; transplant into warm soil after frost with warm nights.
directions: This classic Italian heirloom produces long, slender six-to-eight-inch peppers with thin skin, tender flesh, and sweet mild flavor, excellent roasted or grilled. Start seeds indoors about eight weeks before the last frost and transplant once nights stay warm. Support the plants as the heavy fruits develop. Harvest green for a milder taste, or let the peppers ripen to deep red for their fullest sweetness.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost danger has passed and soil has warmed to at least 65 degrees.
directions: Direct sow this Cornell-bred slicing cucumber after the soil warms, planting seeds an inch deep in full sun. The 4 to 6 foot vines produce dark green, straight fruits best picked at 6 to 8 inches, and they hold their shape well even in poor weather. Grow on a trellis to save space and keep fruit clean, or let vines run over the ground. Keep plants consistently watered, especially during fruiting, and harvest frequently to keep the vines producing and prevent bitterness.

plant_note: Direct sow in warm soil in blocks of several short rows for good pollination.
directions: Direct sow this open-pollinated corn in warm soil, planting in blocks of at least four short rows rather than a single long row to ensure good wind pollination. The purple stalks and husks yield ears with white kernels and a traditional, slightly-less-sweet corn flavor, rich in anthocyanins. Give plants full sun, fertile soil, and steady moisture, especially during tasseling and ear fill. Harvest when the silks brown and the kernels are plump and milky for the best eating.

common_name: Marvel of Four Seasons Butterhead Lettuce
plant_note: Direct sow in early spring and again in late summer; this adaptable variety grows across seasons.
directions: Sow this 1800s French butterhead directly in cool weather, barely covering the seed since it needs light to germinate. The tender, sweet leaves are green at the base and turn a beautiful cranberry-red, forming a crinkly rosette that resembles a flower. Keep plants consistently moist to preserve the coloring and prevent bitterness, and thin to about 8 to 10 inches apart. This adaptable variety performs across the seasons; give it afternoon shade in warm weather to slow bolting.

plant_note: Start seed indoors, transplant crowns after danger of frost; establish in a permanent bed.
directions: Mary Washington is a classic heirloom asparagus producing thick, straight green spears with a faint purple blush at the tips. Start seed indoors early and transplant seedlings or crowns into a deeply worked, well-drained permanent bed. Resist harvesting for the first two seasons so plants build strong root reserves, then cut spears for several weeks each spring. A single planting can yield reliably for fifteen years or more with annual mulching and feeding.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost danger has passed and soil has warmed.
directions: Direct sow this compact white acorn squash after the soil warms, planting in hills spaced a couple of feet apart. Its restricted vines reach only about 45 inches, making it a good choice for smaller gardens, and each plant usually sets three to four fruits. Let the squash mature fully until the rind hardens, then harvest with a bit of stem attached and cure in a warm spot for storage. Provide full sun and steady moisture while the fruits are developing.

plant_note: Plant tubers 3–4 inches deep in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; mashua is a short-day plant that initiates tubers only as days shorten in fall, making it best suited to zones with mild fall weather. Spring through fall is the growing season.
directions: Plant tubers in spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; mashua is one of the 'lost crops of the Andes' — a climbing nasturtium relative that produces edible tubers at the base of its twining stems. It is the most productive of the Andean tuber crops (producing more calories per unit area than potato under appropriate conditions) and is naturally resistant to insects and some nematodes through bitter compounds in its tissues. The tubers have a distinctive sharp, peppery, mustard-like raw flavor that mellows dramatically when cooked (especially by frost exposure) to a sweet, starchy taste similar to parsnip. The attractive red and orange tuberous nasturtium flowers are edible and ornamental. Mashua is a short-day plant that forms tubers only when days shorten in fall — it performs best in zone 7–9 where frost is not severe but fall days are distinctly short.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring as soon as soil can be worked, and again in late summer for fall.
directions: Sow Matador spinach directly in early spring as soon as the soil can be worked, and again in late summer for a fall crop, as spinach bolts quickly in heat. This variety offers good heat tolerance and holds a while before bolting, with smooth, dark green oval leaves suited to salads and cooking. Thin seedlings to 4 to 6 inches apart and keep the soil cool and evenly moist. Harvest baby leaves for salad mixes or let plants mature for cooked dishes like risotto.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost danger has passed and the soil has warmed.
directions: Direct sow this golden zucchini after the soil warms, planting a couple of seeds per hill and thinning to the strongest plant. The long, straight fruits have a small seed cavity and firm, flavorful flesh, and their bright color makes them easy to spot for harvest. Pick fruits young at 6 to 8 inches, checking daily during peak season since summer squash grows fast. Give plants full sun, rich soil, and consistent moisture, and harvest frequently to keep the plant producing.

plant_note: Direct sow after all frost danger has passed and soil has warmed.
directions: Direct sow this French filet bush bean after the soil warms, planting seeds an inch deep in full sun. The compact plants need no support, making them well suited to containers and small beds. Harvest the slender, stringless haricots verts at their peak between 6 and 8 inches long and about a quarter inch wide for the best texture and flavor. Pick every couple of days to keep the plants productive, and sow in succession for a steady supply through the season.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after all danger of frost has passed and soil is warm.
directions: Start this large jalapeno indoors 6 to 8 weeks before the last frost, keeping the soil warm for reliable germination. Transplant seedlings out only after nights stay above 55 degrees, spacing plants 18 to 24 inches apart in full sun. Megatron produces extra-large, thick-walled peppers up to 4.5 inches long and matures earlier than many jalapenos, giving an abundant crop ideal for stuffing. Provide consistent moisture and a light feeding once fruit sets, and harvest green or let peppers ripen red for a sweeter, hotter flavor.

plant_note: Start indoors early since celery is slow to germinate and needs a long season; transplant around last frost.
directions: Start Merengo celery indoors 8 to 10 weeks before the last frost, as the seed germinates slowly and needs a long season; barely cover the tiny seeds since they need light. Transplant hardened seedlings out around the last frost, spacing them 8 to 12 inches apart. Celery is a heavy feeder that demands consistently moist, rich soil to produce tender, flavorful stalks free of stringiness. Water generously and mulch to retain moisture, and harvest whole plants or individual outer stalks as needed.

plant_note: Direct sow after soil warms and all frost has passed.
directions: Midori Giant is a compact heirloom edamame that sets large, plump pods packed with tender, protein-rich beans. Sow seed directly into warm soil in full sun once frost danger has passed, spacing plants a few inches apart in rows. The bushy plants stay tidy and are well suited to smaller gardens and raised beds. Harvest pods when they are bright green and full but before they yellow, then steam or freeze promptly.

plant_note: Sow 4–6 weeks before last frost; it prefers cool, moist conditions and will self-sow prolifically if allowed to set seed.
directions: Broadcast seeds over moist soil and press lightly into contact; no covering is needed as seeds germinate best with light. Miner's lettuce is best sown in partial shade in mild climates, where it will often naturalize and self-sow. The distinctive round, perfoliate leaves (stem appearing to grow through the leaf) are mild and succulent — excellent raw in salads. Harvest whole plants or outer leaves before warm weather triggers bolting, which happens quickly once temperatures exceed 65°F.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall for winter and spring harvest in mild climates; in cold climates, sow in late summer or very early spring as soon as soil can be worked. Seed germinates best in cool, moist soil and will not germinate when temperatures exceed 65°F.
directions: Direct sow thickly on the surface of moist, rich, well-drained soil in partial shade; miner's lettuce is a cool-season annual native to western North America that earned its name from Gold Rush miners who ate it to prevent scurvy. The rounded, succulent leaves are mild, slightly sweet, and excellent in salads or as a wilted green. In mild climates (zones 8–10) it thrives as a winter and early spring salad green, tolerating light frost. Plants mature quickly — begin harvesting whole stems at 40 days — and self-seed prolifically once established, often creating a dependable self-renewing patch. Succession-sow every 3 weeks in fall for continuous harvest through spring; plants bolt and die as temperatures exceed 70°F.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after all danger of frost has passed and soil has warmed.
directions: Start this compact mini bell pepper indoors 6 to 8 weeks before the last frost, keeping the soil warm for reliable germination. The plant stays a tidy 24 inches tall, making it well suited to containers and small spaces. Transplant out after nights warm and space plants 12 to 18 inches apart in full sun. Each little pepper is about 1.5 inches with smooth, thick, juicy flesh; harvest them fully red for peak sweetness, and pick regularly to encourage more fruit.

plant_note: Start indoors early; transplant only after nights stay warm.
directions: This rare Peruvian heirloom produces petite creamy-white pods roughly three-quarters to two inches long with intense, fruity habanero heat. Start seed indoors under warm conditions well before your last frost, as germination is slow and needs consistent warmth. Transplant into full sun once nights are reliably warm, and the ornamental, productive plants thrive in containers. Wear gloves when handling the ripe pods, which are excellent in salsas and hot sauces.

plant_note: Direct sow thickly in early spring or fall in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; minutina germinates rapidly in cool conditions and is one of the most cold-tolerant salad greens available. Make successive sowings every 3 weeks from late winter through early spring, then again from late summer through fall.
directions: Direct sow in cool weather in moist, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; minutina (erba stella, or 'star grass') is one of the most unusual and sought-after Italian salad greens, producing rosettes of narrow, deeply toothed, antler-like leaves that have a pleasantly mild, slightly nutty to salty flavor and a satisfying crunch — a standout texture in any mixed salad. Unlike most salad greens, minutina is very tolerant of light frost and can be harvested almost year-round in mild climates; in the Southeast it is a fall-through-spring crop that withstands temperatures down to the mid-20s°F. The leaves are cut at the base and the plant regrows from the crown for multiple harvests. It tolerates partial shade better than most salad crops, making it productive in spots that receive only 3–4 hours of direct sun. Slow to bolt even in cool, bright conditions. Outstanding in the cut-and-come-again salad garden.

plant_note: Sow in mid to late summer for a fall crop, as daikon needs cool, shortening days.
directions: Sow this Japanese daikon in mid to late summer for a fall harvest, since it is a winter radish that needs cool weather and shortening days to size up. Direct sow in loose, deeply worked soil so the long white roots can grow straight, and thin seedlings to 4 to 6 inches apart. Keep the soil evenly moist for tender, mild roots with a lightly spicy flavor. Harvest before hard freezes; use young roots shredded fresh, or pickle and cook mature roots in stir-fries and soups.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost in spring; make a second sowing 6–8 weeks before first fall frost for autumn harvest.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in rows or broadcast thinly, thinning to 6–9 inches for full heads or leaving dense for cut-and-come-again baby leaves. Mizuna is extremely fast-growing and bolt-tolerant compared to most brassicas, making it one of the most reliable salad greens for both spring and fall. Begin harvesting outer leaves at 21 days or cut the whole plant an inch above the soil line and allow it to regrow. Consistent moisture promotes tender, mild-flavored leaves; drought causes bitterness.

plant_note: Direct sow thickly in early spring or fall in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; mizuna germinates rapidly and is ready for baby-leaf cutting in as few as 21 days. Make successive sowings every 2–3 weeks through cool weather for continuous harvest.
directions: Direct sow in cool weather in moist, well-drained, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; mizuna is one of the most popular Japanese salad greens and one of the fastest, easiest cool-season crops available, producing deeply cut, feathery, pale green leaves with a mild, slightly peppery, refreshing flavor that is pleasantly tender when harvested young. It is one of the most cold tolerant of all the Asian brassicas and continues growing through light freezes, making it an outstanding fall and winter crop in zones 7–9. Begin harvesting as baby leaf at 21 days by cutting the outer leaves; the plant regrows for multiple harvests before bolting in warm weather. Bolt resistance is better than arugula, and the plant handles light shade well, making it productive in partly shaded plots. Red-leaved mizuna cultivars ('Red Kingdom', 'Mikado Red') add color to salad mixes. An outstanding addition to spring and fall salad mixes, stir-fries, and as a garnish in Japanese cuisine.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring; carrots resent transplanting so always sow in place.
directions: Direct sow this Nantes-type hybrid carrot in loose, stone-free soil, since carrots dislike transplanting and rocky ground causes forked roots. Sow shallowly and keep the surface evenly moist until the slow-germinating seeds sprout, then thin seedlings to about an inch apart. Mokum stays sweet even in warmer weather and its slim, nearly coreless roots are crisp whether pulled as baby carrots or grown to a full 6 to 8 inches. Provide steady moisture and full sun for smooth, snappy roots.

plant_note: Heat-loving green; thrives in the hottest part of summer.
directions: Also called Egyptian spinach or jute mallow, molokhia is a heat-loving green that thrives when other leafy crops bolt. Direct sow after the soil is thoroughly warm, since seedlings need heat and stall in cool weather. Pinch the growing tips to encourage bushy branching and a continuous supply of young leaves. Harvest the tender upper leaves and stems, which turn silky and thicken soups and stews when cooked. Keep it well watered through summer, and pick regularly to delay flowering.

plant_note: Start indoors in late winter and transplant seedlings out several weeks before the last frost.
directions: Start Monastrell onions indoors 8 to 10 weeks before the last frost, then transplant the seedlings out several weeks before the last frost since onions are cold tolerant when young. This late-season variety forms uniform, slightly flattened red globes 3.5 to 4 inches across with strong sunscald resistance. Match the variety to your latitude, as onion bulbing is triggered by day length. Keep the bed weed-free and evenly moist, then stop watering once tops fall over and cure the bulbs for storage of up to six months.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after all danger of frost has passed and soil has warmed.
directions: Start this indeterminate heirloom indoors 6 weeks before the last frost, then transplant deeply once nights warm, burying part of the stem to encourage strong roots. Moneymaker earned its name through uniform, reliably heavy yields of medium-sized, meaty, sweet fruits. Provide a tall stake or cage since the vines keep growing all season, and prune suckers to improve airflow. Water consistently at the base to prevent cracking and blossom-end rot, and harvest fruits as they ripen to keep the plant producing.

plant_note: Sow in cool soil; germination fails above 75 F.
directions: This large French heirloom spinach produces enormous smooth leaves and is known for fast, heavy yields. Sow seed half an inch deep in cool soil as early as it can be worked, since heat prevents germination and triggers bolting. Thin to 6 inches and keep beds evenly moist and rich in nitrogen for lush leaves. Harvest whole plants or pick outer leaves for a cut-and-come-again supply. Sow again in late summer for a fall crop that sweetens with frost.

plant_note: Plant individual cloves in fall a few weeks before the ground freezes for harvest the following summer.
directions: Plant this vigorous Porcelain-type hardneck garlic in fall, a few weeks before the ground freezes, so the cloves root before winter. Break the bulbs into individual cloves just before planting and set them pointed end up 2 inches deep and 6 to 8 inches apart, then mulch heavily for winter protection. Each bulb yields 4 to 7 large, pink-wrapped cloves with a buttery, nutty, low-heat flavor. Remove the scapes in early summer to boost bulb size, and harvest when the lower leaves brown, then cure in a dry, airy place.

plant_note: Direct sow in warm soil after frost, or start indoors 3 weeks early.
directions: Moon & Stars is a striking heirloom watermelon named for its deep green rind flecked with golden yellow spots and one or two larger moon-shaped patches. The vigorous vines produce large fruits with sweet, old-fashioned red flesh over a long, warm season. Direct sow into warm, well-drained soil in full sun after all frost danger, giving the sprawling vines plenty of room. Consistent watering while fruit sizes up improves sweetness; ease off as the melons approach ripeness.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after all danger of frost has passed and soil has warmed.
directions: Start this classic indeterminate beefsteak indoors 6 weeks before the last frost, then transplant deeply after frost danger passes, burying part of the stem for strong roots. The Halladay strain produces hefty 1 to 2 pound pink beefsteak fruits with crack-resistant skin on vigorous vines. Provide a sturdy tall cage or stake, prune suckers for airflow, and water consistently at the base to prevent cracking and blossom-end rot. Harvest the big fruits as they ripen to keep the plant setting more.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after frost danger passes.
directions: Mortgage Lifter is a famous indeterminate heirloom beefsteak known for enormous, meaty pink-red fruits that often top two pounds. The rich, mild, low-acid flavor makes it a favorite for slicing and sandwiches. Start seed indoors several weeks before the last frost and transplant into full sun once the soil is warm, burying the stem deeply. Provide sturdy staking or caging, since the tall vines carry very heavy fruit and keep producing until frost.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after all danger of frost has passed; this variety tolerates cool nights.
directions: Start this early Russian indeterminate indoors 6 weeks before the last frost, then transplant deeply after frost danger passes. Bred in the early 1970s near Moscow, Moskvich withstands cool weather and is often the first tomato to ripen, offering round, deep-red fruits with rich flavor. Provide a tall stake or cage since the vines keep growing all season, and prune suckers for better airflow. Water consistently at the base and harvest as fruits color up to encourage continued production.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after all danger of frost has passed and soil has warmed.
directions: Start this determinate slicer indoors 6 weeks before the last frost, then transplant deeply after frost danger passes, burying part of the stem. As a 2014 All-America Selections winner, Mountain Merit offers strong disease resistance including to Fusarium wilt and produces a beautiful red beefsteak on compact plants suited to containers. Its condensed harvest period is handy for preserving a large batch at once. Support the plants with a cage, water consistently at the base, and harvest fruits as they ripen.

plant_note: Sow in warm soil after all danger of frost; needs a long, hot season.
directions: This heirloom yields large oblong melons of 20 to 35 pounds with sweet, juicy yellow flesh inside a dark green striped rind. Give each hill rich, well-drained soil and full sun, and allow long vines plenty of room to sprawl. Water steadily while fruit sets, then ease off as melons ripen to concentrate sugars. Harvest when the tendril nearest the fruit dries and the ground spot turns creamy.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after frost; slow to germinate so give it warmth.
directions: These grape-sized fruits look like miniature watermelons but taste like tart cucumber, perfect for pickling or garnishing drinks. Vines can reach 10 feet, so provide a trellis for tidy growth and easy picking. Harvest fruits when firm and about the size of a grape, before they soften and turn sour. Plants tolerate heat well and produce steadily until frost.

plant_note: Direct sow in warm soil after frost, or start indoors 3 weeks early.
directions: Muncher is a burpless heirloom cucumber prized for mild, low-acid flavor and thin, tender skin that needs no peeling. Harvest fruits young at four to six inches for crisp snacking, or let them size up to eight or nine inches for slicing. Direct sow in warm soil in full sun after frost, or start early indoors, and grow on a trellis to keep fruit straight and clean. Pick regularly and keep the plants evenly watered to prevent bitterness.

plant_note: Direct sow in warm soil once frost has passed.
directions: This mini slicing cucumber produces tender, burpless, thin-skinned fruits with a crisp bite, best picked around 5 inches long. High yields come from steady moisture and full sun, so water consistently to avoid bitterness. Train vines on a trellis to keep fruit straight and clean. Harvest frequently to keep plants productive throughout the season.

plant_note: Direct sow 1–3 weeks after last frost when soil has warmed to at least 65°F; mung bean needs a long, warm, relatively dry summer to set seed.
directions: Sow seeds 1 inch deep in rows, thinning to 3–6 inches; inoculate with cowpea rhizobium inoculant before planting to ensure nitrogen fixation. Pods can be harvested young (50–60 days) for fresh shelling or snap use, or left on the plant to dry for dried beans or sprouting seeds. Pick pods when they turn black and rattle to collect dry beans; harvest in the morning before pods shatter. Mung beans are also a popular choice for home sprouting — the dry seeds germinate in a jar within 2–4 days.

plant_note: Soak seed overnight; often grown as sprouts or in trays with frequent rinsing.
directions: Soak mung beans overnight so they swell and sprout rapidly, then spread them in an even layer on a tray or in a sprouting jar. They germinate within a day or two and thrive in warmth, so keep them covered and rinse or mist frequently to prevent souring. If growing as tray microgreens, give them light once shoots appear to green them up slightly. Harvest the crunchy, protein-rich shoots after roughly a week, or eat earlier as classic bean sprouts.

plant_note: Sprout indoors year-round in a jar; no field planting needed.
directions: Mung beans are prized for quick, protein-rich sprouts used in stir-fries, egg rolls, and salads. Soak seeds overnight, then rinse and drain two to three times daily in a sprouting jar kept out of direct sun. Sprouts are ready to eat in four to seven days once the shoots are an inch or two long. Keep them moist but well-drained to prevent souring.

plant_note: Plant cloves in fall a few weeks before the ground freezes; harvest the following summer.
directions: This porcelain hardneck cultivar produces very large bulbs with 4 to 6 big, easy-to-peel cloves and excellent cold tolerance. Plant individual cloves pointed end up in fall, mulch heavily, and let them overwinter. Snip the curling scapes in early summer to direct energy into the bulbs. Harvest when the lower leaves brown, then cure in a dry, airy spot for storage.

plant_note: Very winter-hardy; can stand in the ground for winter harvest.
directions: This hardy Scottish heirloom leek withstands hard freezes and can be harvested through winter in many climates. Start seed indoors early, then transplant seedlings into deep dibble holes so the shanks blanch white. Hill soil around the stems or drop plants into holes to lengthen the tender white portion. Keep well watered and side-dress with nitrogen midseason for thick shanks. Dig leeks as needed all winter, lifting the last before spring regrowth begins.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost in spring; succession sow every 2–3 weeks and again in early fall.
directions: Broadcast or row-sow seed at 1/4 in depth and thin to 6–12 in; mustard germinates rapidly even in cool soil. Begin harvesting outer leaves when plants are 6–8 in tall for mild flavor, or allow larger leaves to develop for a stronger pungency. Hot weather causes rapid bolting and sharply increases bitterness, so time sowings to mature in cool conditions. Baby leaves can be cut at 3–4 weeks as a spicy salad green.

plant_note: Fast, spicy brassica microgreen; mucilaginous seed sown dry on moist trays.
directions: Sow mustard seed dry and dense on a moist tray, since it develops a gel coat when wet that makes presoaking messy. It sprouts fast, so keep the blackout period short and move it into bright light within a couple of days. Mist to keep the surface moist and provide airflow to keep the fast growth healthy. Harvest the zesty, peppery greens at the cotyledon stage in about a week for maximum heat and aroma.

plant_note: For fall crop, direct sow 8–10 weeks before first frost; for spring, sow as soon as soil can be worked and harvest before summer heat triggers bolting.
directions: Direct sow 1/4 in deep in fertile, well-drained soil and thin to 12–18 in once seedlings are 3–4 in tall. Water regularly, as dry spells encourage premature bolting in warm weather. Fall planting generally produces better-quality heads with fewer pest problems. Harvest when heads feel firm and compact, cutting at the base before temperatures drop below 20°F.

plant_note: Best sown in mid to late summer for a fall harvest to avoid bolting.
directions: This Michihili-type Napa cabbage forms tall, barrel-shaped heads of tightly wrapped, mild, tender leaves ideal for kimchi and stir-fry. It grows best as a fall crop; sow in mid to late summer so heads mature in cool weather, since spring heat and long days trigger bolting. Keep the soil consistently moist and rich in nitrogen for fast, uniform growth. Harvest when heads feel firm and dense, and cut before hard freezes.

plant_note: Direct sow into warm soil once frost has passed.
directions: National Pickling is an open-pollinated heirloom bred for firm, blocky fruits about five to six inches long that are perfect for pickling and canning. The productive vines set fruit heavily and quickly, so pick often to keep new cucumbers coming. Direct sow into warm soil in full sun after frost and keep evenly watered for crisp, uniform fruit. Harvest at the size you want, from small gherkins to larger slicing cucumbers.

plant_note: Sow in warm soil after frost; drought tolerant once established.
directions: This drought-tolerant heirloom, originally grown by the Navajo people, produces 3 to 7 pound melons with sweet orange flesh and a yellow rind. It thrives in poorer soils and hot, dry conditions where other melons struggle. Give it full sun and room to sprawl, watering deeply but infrequently. Harvest when the fruit slips easily from the vine and develops a fragrant aroma.

common_name: Navy Bean (Haricot Bean, Boston Bean, White Pea Bean)
plant_note: Direct sow 1–2 inches deep in well-drained, warm, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun after last frost; navy beans are compact bush types that set pods heavily in a concentrated period. In zone 9a they perform best with spring planting for early summer harvest before peak summer heat.
directions: Sow 1–2 inches deep in warm, well-drained soil in full sun; navy beans are the small, oval, ivory-white dried beans of New England baked beans, Senate bean soup, and countless European cassoulet and bean dishes. The compact bush plants are highly productive and concentrate their pod set for a single, efficient harvest period — more practical for dry bean production than many spreading pole types. Allow pods to turn yellow-brown and papery before harvest; pull the entire plant and dry in a warm, well-ventilated location for 1–2 weeks, then shell. Navy beans are rich in fiber (19g per cup) and folate. In zone 9a, spring plantings avoid the hottest summer weather; flowers drop in temperatures above 90°F, so early planting is key to a full harvest.

plant_note: Direct sow as soon as soil can be worked; sow again in late summer for fall.
directions: This flame-tipped green leaf lettuce holds its flavor even in summer heat and resists downy mildew, bottom rot, and tipburn. Its slow-to-bolt habit lets you harvest over a long window. Sow shallowly in cool soil and keep evenly moist for tender leaves. Pick outer leaves as needed or cut the whole head, and provide light afternoon shade in hot weather to extend the season.

plant_note: Soak seed overnight and direct sow after frost; germination is slow.
directions: New Zealand spinach, also called warrigal greens, is a heat-loving green that thrives through summer when true spinach bolts. The sprawling plants yield tender, mild leaves that can be picked continually as a cut-and-come-again crop. Soak the hard seed overnight and direct sow after frost, since germination is slow and uneven. Harvest the tips regularly to keep growth productive, and give the plants room to spread.

plant_note: Direct sow after last frost when soil has warmed; soak seeds overnight to speed germination.
directions: Nick or soak seed for 24 hours before sowing 1/2 in deep after soil has warmed to at least 60°F; germination is slow and erratic without pre-soaking. Plants spread vigorously and fill space, so allow ample room of 2–3 ft per plant. Harvest by pinching stem tips of 3–4 in including the attached young leaves; regular harvesting keeps growth compact and productive through the entire warm season. Unlike true spinach, New Zealand spinach thrives in heat and is not bothered by long days, making it a valuable summer substitute.

plant_note: Plant bulbs 2 inches deep in fall, 4–6 weeks before ground freezes; alternatively divide established clumps in early spring.
directions: Plant bulbs 2 inches deep in well-drained, average to rocky soil in full sun to partial shade; this tough native wildflower adapts to a wide range of conditions including dry, rocky slopes where little else thrives. The nodding pink flower clusters appear in midsummer and are excellent pollinator magnets, particularly for native bees and butterflies. The entire plant is edible — bulbs, leaves, and flowers all have a mild onion flavor — and it was a widely used food plant for many Indigenous nations. Allow some flower heads to set seed for naturalizing; clumps can also be divided every 3–4 years to maintain vigor.

plant_note: Plant cloves in fall before the ground freezes; harvest the following summer.
directions: This widely adapted softneck heirloom from the San Juan Islands stays productive from wet to arid and cool to hot climates, forming bulbs with 12 to 20 cloves. Plant cloves in fall, mulch, and let them overwinter for a summer harvest. Softneck types store especially well and can be braided. Harvest when the lower leaves yellow, then cure in a shaded, airy place before storing.

plant_note: Detach a mature pad, allow the cut end to callus for one week in dry shade, then plant 2–3 inches deep in sandy soil; water sparingly until roots form (about 4–6 weeks).
directions: Allow a pad cutting to callus (dry) for one week before planting in sandy or rocky, sharply drained soil in full sun; overwatering is the most common cause of failure. Young pads (nopales) are harvested when 6–8 inches long and still tender, using tongs and a sharp knife to remove glochids (small barbed spines) by scraping before cooking. The pads are eaten grilled, in tacos, scrambled with eggs, or in soups, with a mild, slightly mucilaginous texture. The pear-shaped red or yellow fruits (tunas) that ripen in late summer are sweet and edible; wear thick gloves when harvesting as glochids penetrate skin on contact. Established plants are extremely drought and heat tolerant and require virtually no care.

common_name: NuMex Heritage Big Jim Hatch Chile Pepper
plant_note: Start indoors under warmth; transplant after nights stay above 55 F.
directions: This improved Hatch-type chile offers thick flesh, large flavorful pods, and consistent mild-to-medium heat ideal for fire roasting. Start seeds early indoors since peppers need a long warm season, and transplant only after the soil is thoroughly warm. Feed lightly and water evenly to size up the pods. Harvest green for roasting or leave to redden for deeper, sweeter flavor.

common_name: NuMex Joe E. Parker Anaheim Chile Pepper
plant_note: Start indoors under warmth; transplant after frost when soil is warm.
directions: This productive Anaheim-type pepper bears thick-walled, uniform 6 to 8 inch fruits with few seeds, excellent for roasting, stuffing, or canning. Mild heat around 500 to 2,500 Scoville units makes it versatile in the kitchen. Start seeds indoors early and set out sturdy transplants once nights are warm. Peppers ripen from green to red, so pick at whichever stage suits your recipe.

common_name: NuMex Lemon Spice Jalapeno Chile Pepper
plant_note: Start indoors early; transplant after frost when soil warms.
directions: This colorful jalapeno from the Chile Pepper Institute ripens from green to bright yellow with full jalapeno heat and a citrus undertone. Pick pods at either the green or yellow stage for a varied jar of pickled peppers. Start seeds indoors well before your last frost, then transplant into warm, sunny beds. Steady watering and a light feeding keep the compact plants cranking out fruit.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring and again in late summer for fall.
directions: Oakleaf is a classic loose-leaf heirloom lettuce with tender, deeply lobed leaves that resemble oak foliage. It is a reliable cut-and-come-again type, letting you harvest outer leaves while the center keeps growing. Direct sow in early spring and again in late summer, giving it full sun in cool weather or partial shade to slow bolting in heat. Keep soil evenly moist and harvest young for the sweetest flavor.

plant_note: Plant tubers just before to just after last frost — oca needs a long, cool season and tuber formation is triggered by shortening day length in autumn.
directions: Plant small tubers 3 inches deep in loose, well-drained soil in early spring; oca is an Andean crop from the clover family and grows best in cool, moist conditions similar to potato, but requires a very long growing season. The plants produce clover-like foliage and only initiate tuber formation in response to the short days of autumn, so harvesting cannot occur until after the first frosts kill the foliage — typically November. Harvest tubers by digging carefully after frost kills the tops; tubers range from white to yellow, orange, red, and purple depending on the variety. Fresh-dug tubers are acidic and starchy but sweeten after a week of sun exposure that converts starch to sugars; eat roasted, boiled, or raw once sweetened.

plant_note: Direct sow in cool soil as early as it can be worked; sow again for fall.
directions: This spinach yields deep green, easy-to-clean leaves that are ready as baby greens in about 25 days and full size in 40. Sow directly in cool spring or fall soil since heat triggers bolting. Keep the bed evenly moist and thin seedlings for good airflow. Harvest baby leaves with scissors or pull whole plants, and provide afternoon shade to stretch the harvest into warmer weather.

plant_note: Set out rooted cuttings after all frost danger has passed and nighttime temperatures stay above 50°F; in frost-free zones plant any time of year.
directions: Root stem cuttings in water or moist potting mix; they strike quickly at warm temperatures and are ready to transplant in 10–14 days. Plant in fertile, well-drained soil in partial shade — full afternoon shade is ideal in hot climates, where the ornamental two-toned leaves (dark green above, vivid purple beneath) are most striking. Harvest young leaves and stem tips continuously; the raw leaves have a mild, slightly mucilaginous texture and are eaten as salad greens or briefly stir-fried. In zones 9–11 the plant grows as a sprawling perennial; in cooler climates bring cuttings indoors over winter.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks after last frost once soil temperature reaches at least 65°F; okra thrives in heat.
directions: Soak seeds in water for 12–24 hours before sowing 1 inch deep to soften the hard seed coat and speed germination. Plant in the hottest, sunniest spot available, as okra is a true heat-lover and grows slowly in cool conditions. Side-dress with a nitrogen-rich fertilizer when plants reach 12 inches tall to promote vigorous growth. Harvest pods every 2–3 days when they are 3–4 inches long and still tender; pods left to mature become tough and woody and reduce plant productivity.

plant_note: Direct sow in cool soil; best flavor develops in fall.
directions: Also called frisee, this endive forms lacy, finely cut leaves with a mild flavor that dresses up a salad or serves as an ornamental. Sow in cool weather and keep evenly watered to reduce bitterness. Blanch the center by tying up outer leaves a week or two before harvest for a pale, sweeter heart. Harvest before hard frost, cutting whole heads at the base.

common_name: One Kilo Slow Bolt Napa Cabbage
plant_note: Best sown in mid to late summer for a fall crop to avoid bolting.
directions: This napa cabbage forms tight, creamy-yellow heads with a delicate flavor and texture between lettuce and regular cabbage. Its slow-bolt habit lets it hold in the garden longer, and it grows most reliably as a fall crop. Keep the soil consistently moist and fertile for solid heads. Protect young spring plantings from cold snaps, which can trigger premature bolting.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost; seeds need cold stratification or cool soil to germinate well and the crop runs to seed in summer heat.
directions: Press seeds firmly into soil no deeper than 1/4 inch; germination is improved by pre-soaking seeds for a few hours before sowing. Orach grows quickly into an upright plant with red, gold, or green leaves that can substitute for spinach in any cooked preparation. Pinch the central growing tip when the plant is 12 inches tall to encourage bushy side growth and delay bolting. Harvest young leaves frequently; once flower stalks emerge, leaves become less palatable.

plant_note: Start indoors early; transplant after nights stay warm.
directions: This heirloom bell pepper ripens from green to a bright, juicy orange with a crisp, sweet flavor ideal for salads, stuffing, and fresh snacking. Start seed indoors well before the last frost and transplant into full sun once nights are reliably warm. Peppers need steady warmth, even moisture, and moderate feeding to size up well. Allow fruits to fully color on the plant for the sweetest taste, then harvest to encourage continued production.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after frost danger passes.
directions: This indeterminate cherry tomato bears heavy clusters of vibrant golden-orange fruits with a sweet, mild, almost tropical flavor. Start seed indoors several weeks before the last frost and transplant into full sun once soil is warm, setting plants deep. The tall vines need staking or a cage and keep bearing right up to frost. Pick the small fruits as they ripen fully for the best sweetness and to encourage more production.

plant_note: Start indoors early; germination is slow and needs steady warmth.
directions: The classic orange habanero delivers intense heat with a fruity, tropical sweetness that shines in salsas and hot sauces. Start seed indoors well ahead of the last frost, as germination is slow and demands consistent warmth. Transplant into full sun only after nights are reliably warm, and the productive plants do well in containers. Handle the ripe pods with gloves and harvest regularly once they turn deep orange.

plant_note: Start indoors early; transplant after frost when soil is warm.
directions: This large bell pepper ripens from green to glowing orange with thick, sweet, juicy walls. Pick fruit at either stage to boost overall production and add color to meals. Start seeds indoors well ahead of your last frost, then set sturdy transplants into warm soil. Stake heavily laden plants and water evenly to prevent blossom-end rot.

plant_note: Direct sow in warm soil after frost, or start indoors 3 weeks early.
directions: Orangeglo is a beloved heirloom watermelon with brilliant orange flesh and a crisp, exceptionally sweet, tropical flavor. The large oblong fruits ripen on vigorous vines over a long, warm season. Direct sow into warm, well-drained soil in full sun after all frost danger, giving the vines ample room to run. Water consistently while fruit develops, then reduce watering as the melons near ripeness to concentrate sugars.

plant_note: Direct sow in cool soil as soon as it can be worked.
directions: This snow pea produces some of the largest, crispest, sweetest flat pods you will grow, on vines that stay under 30 inches and need little support. It carries strong resistance to powdery mildew, Fusarium wilt, and pea enation mosaic virus. Sow directly in cool spring soil and keep evenly moist. Harvest pods young and flat, before the seeds swell, to keep plants producing.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring as soon as soil can be worked.
directions: Oregon Sugar Pod II is a productive snow pea bearing crisp, sweet, flat pods that are delicious raw or lightly cooked. The compact vines resist common pea diseases and crop heavily over the cool season. Direct sow in early spring as soon as the soil can be worked, and provide short netting or twiggy supports. Pick pods young and often, before the peas inside swell, to keep the plants producing.

common_name: Oregon Sugar Pod II Snow Pea
plant_note: Direct sow in cool soil as soon as it can be worked.
directions: This heavy-yielding snow pea bears large numbers of 4 inch flat pods on manageable 28 inch vines that resist mildew, pea enation virus, and leaf roll. Sow directly in cool spring soil and keep the bed moist. A short trellis or brush keeps vines upright and pods clean. Pick pods while flat and tender, before the peas inside develop, for the best flavor.

common_name: Orient Wonder Pole Yard Long Bean
plant_note: Direct sow in warm soil after frost; loves heat and humidity.
directions: Also called Chinese long beans, this dark green pole variety produces smooth, slender pods that stay tender because the seeds develop slowly. It tolerates weather swings from cool and dry to hot and humid better than common beans. Provide a tall, sturdy trellis for the vigorous vines. Harvest pods young at 12 to 18 inches, picking often to encourage continued production.

plant_note: Start mid to late summer for fall and winter color; frost intensifies the hues.
directions: Ornamental kale, a flowering cabbage, forms compact rosettes of frilly leaves in pink, purple, cream, and white that light up cool-season beds and containers. Start seed in mid to late summer so plants color up as temperatures drop, since the vivid hues develop with cold. Give full sun and steady moisture for full, dense rosettes. Frost only deepens the color, making this a standout for fall and winter displays; the leaves are edible but grown mainly for looks.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after frost danger passes.
directions: Oxheart Pink is a vigorous indeterminate heirloom producing large, heart-shaped fruits with smooth skin, deep pink flesh, and a firm, meaty texture with few seeds. The dense flesh makes it excellent for sauces as well as fresh slicing. Start seed indoors before the last frost and transplant into full sun in warm soil, planting deeply. Provide strong support for the heavy fruit and keep watering even to reduce cracking and blossom-end rot.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after frost when soil is warm.
directions: This Italian heirloom, also known as Cuore di Bue, bears heart-shaped, meaty fruits that are dense with few seeds and cook down into a robust sauce. Being indeterminate, the vines grow tall and need staking or caging. Start seeds indoors, transplant into warm soil, and water evenly to prevent cracking and blossom-end rot. Prune suckers lightly to improve airflow and fruit size.

plant_note: Start indoors early; transplant after frost when soil warms.
directions: This early, thin-skinned Spanish pepper is a classic tapas appetizer, mostly mild but with the occasional fiery pod for a game of Spanish roulette. Pick fruits small at 1 to 1.5 inches for the best texture and flavor. Start seeds indoors early and transplant into warm, sunny beds. Harvest frequently while green, blistering the pods in hot oil for the traditional dish.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant 1–2 weeks after frost danger passes.
directions: Start seeds 1/4 inch deep in warm mix at 78–82°F; transplant into fertile, well-drained soil with consistent moisture. Padrón peppers are the Spanish tapas classic — small, thin-walled, and mostly mild, but a percentage of fruits become significantly hot (especially those left to grow larger or harvested later in the season). Pick pods when they are 1.5–2 inches long and still green for the mildest flavor; fried in olive oil and salted, they are traditionally served as pimientos de Padrón. Consistent watering during fruit set prevents irregular heat and blossom drop.

plant_note: Direct sow in warm soil after frost; plant in blocks for good pollination.
directions: This colorful open-pollinated sweet corn was bred from a cross of Luther Hill sweet corn and Painted Mountain flour corn, giving it kernels in many hues and adaptation to short, cool seasons. Sow in warm soil in blocks of at least four rows so wind pollinates the ears well. Keep evenly watered, especially as tassels and silks form. Harvest in the milk stage when kernels are plump and juicy.

plant_note: Sow in mid to late summer so heads form in cool fall weather.
directions: Palla Rossa is an Italian radicchio-type chicory that forms tight, round heads of deep burgundy leaves with crisp white ribs and a pleasantly bitter flavor. It grows best as a fall crop; the cool weather colors up the heads and mellows their bitterness. Direct sow in mid to late summer in full sun and thin to give each plant room to head up. Harvest when heads feel firm, and a light frost only improves flavor and color.

plant_note: Sow in summer for a fall crop; color and heads develop best in cool weather.
directions: This radicchio forms firm red heads whose color and flavor develop best in cool fall weather, and it overwinters well in zone 4 and warmer. The mildly bitter leaves sweeten after frost. Sow in mid to late summer for heading in autumn, keeping soil evenly moist. Cut heads at the base, and if cut correctly the plants may regrow a second flush.

plant_note: Round roots tolerate heavy or shallow soils where long types fail.
directions: This round French heirloom carrot forms golf-ball-sized roots that thrive in clay or rocky ground where long carrots struggle. Sow seed thinly and shallowly, keeping the surface damp until the slow seedlings emerge. Thin to 2 inches to allow the little globes to plump up evenly. Because roots are short, they mature quickly and are excellent for containers. Harvest young at about 1 to 1.5 inches across for the sweetest, most tender roots.

plant_note: Direct sow in cool soil; sow again in late summer for fall.
directions: This classic romaine, developed by the USDA and Clemson around 1950, forms upright 10 to 12 inch heads with crunchy sweet leaves and a buttery green heart. It shows good heat tolerance and vigor. Sow shallowly in cool soil and keep evenly moist for tender leaves. Harvest whole heads or pick outer leaves, and offer light shade in summer heat to delay bolting.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring and again in late summer for fall.
directions: Parris Island is a classic romaine (cos) heirloom forming large, upright heads up to a foot tall with firm green outer leaves and tender, blanched hearts. It shows good heat resistance for a lettuce, making it dependable into early summer. Direct sow in early spring and again in late summer, keeping soil consistently moist. Give full sun in cool weather, and harvest whole heads when firm or take outer leaves as needed.

plant_note: Sow fresh seed 2–4 weeks before the last frost; parsnip seed has a very short viability window so always use seed from the current season.
directions: Sow 3 seeds per station 1/2 in deep in very deeply dug, stone-free soil and thin to the strongest seedling; long taproots will fork in compacted ground. Parsnip germination is notoriously slow and erratic — sowing radish seed in the same row acts as a quick-germinating row marker. Leave roots in the ground through autumn frosts, which convert starches to sugars for a sweeter harvest. Wear gloves when handling foliage in bright sun to avoid phototoxic sap burns.

plant_note: Start indoors early; transplant after frost when soil warms.
directions: The fresh chilaca pepper dries into the dark, wrinkled pasilla that is essential to traditional mole and adds depth to enchilada sauces and salsas. Heat runs a mild 1,000 to 2,000 Scoville units. Start seeds indoors early and set transplants into warm soil in full sun. Let pods ripen to dark brown for drying, or use them fresh, harvesting regularly to keep plants productive.

common_name: Patio Choice Yellow Bush Cherry Tomato
plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after frost when soil is warm.
directions: This compact determinate bush tomato is ideal for containers and small spaces, yielding abundant sweet half-ounce yellow cherry tomatoes. Its tidy habit needs little staking, making it good for patios and mixed pots. Start seeds indoors, transplant after frost, and water evenly to keep fruit from splitting. Pick ripe tomatoes often to encourage a steady flush.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring as soon as soil can be worked.
directions: Patio Pride is an early, compact heirloom shelling pea that grows just eight to twelve inches tall, making it ideal for containers, window boxes, and small raised beds. Despite its small stature it produces a generous early crop of sweet peas. Direct sow in early spring as soon as the soil can be worked, sowing thickly since the dwarf plants support each other. Harvest promptly once pods fill for the best flavor, and enjoy a quick turnaround for succession planting.

plant_note: Direct sow in cool soil as soon as it can be worked; great for containers.
directions: This early snap pea is a 2017 All-America Selections winner, ready in about 40 days on compact plants perfect for containers. It yields sweet, uniform 2 to 3 inch pods that are tender when picked young. Sow directly in cool soil for a quick spring or fall crop and keep evenly moist. Harvest pods when plump but still crisp to keep the vines producing.

plant_note: Direct sow 1–2 weeks after last frost once soil temperature is at least 65°F.
directions: Sow seeds 1 inch deep in warm, fertile soil in hills of 2–3 seeds, thinning to the strongest plant. Consistent moisture at the root zone keeps the bush compact and productive throughout the season. Pattypan's distinctive flying-saucer shape makes sizing easy. Harvest when fruits are 2–3 inches across for the most tender flesh; over-mature fruits become tough and seedy.

plant_note: Soak peas overnight before sowing thickly; shoots can be cut and regrow once.
directions: Soak the pea seed overnight so it germinates quickly and evenly, then spread it thickly across a moist tray. Keep the seeds covered under weight for a couple of days to encourage strong, upright shoots, then bring them into bright light. Water from below to keep the dense mat hydrated without inviting rot. Harvest the sweet, tender pea shoots at three to six inches tall; many growers get a second cutting from the same tray.

plant_note: Sow thickly in cool soil or trays; harvest young as tender shoots.
directions: Harvested young, pea shoots are a tender, versatile green for salads, sandwiches, soups, and stir-fries. Sow the seeds thickly in a container or garden bed and keep them evenly moist. Snip the shoots with scissors when a few inches tall, cutting above the lowest leaves so plants can regrow for a second cutting. They grow quickly in cool conditions and tolerate part shade.

plant_note: Sow 2–4 weeks after last frost; peanuts need a long, frost-free growing season of at least 120 days.
directions: Shell peanuts and plant seeds 2 in deep in loose, sandy, well-drained soil; heavy clay prevents proper peg penetration and pod development. After flowering, keep the area around plants free of weeds and loosen surface soil lightly so fertilization pegs can easily enter the ground. Harvest when foliage begins to yellow and the inner shell veins turn dark, by lifting the entire plant with a fork. Cure plants upside-down in a warm, airy location for 2–3 weeks before removing pods.

plant_note: Sow thickly and do not thin; crowding keeps bulbs small.
directions: Tiny pearl onions are grown deliberately crowded so each bulb stays small and round for pickling and boiling. Direct sow densely in a wide band and skip thinning, since competition limits bulb size. Keep the bed evenly moist and weed-free during the short growing period. Harvest when tops yellow and fall, then cure the little bulbs before storing or pickling. Blanching the cured bulbs in hot water makes peeling far easier.

plant_note: Direct sow in warm soil after frost; needs a long season to mature.
directions: This Pennsylvania heirloom is a long-necked butternut type with rich, smooth flesh and excellent keeping quality. The elongated necks are nearly solid flesh, ideal for roasting and soups. Give the vigorous 6 to 8 foot vines full sun and plenty of room to sprawl. Cure harvested fruit in a warm, dry spot to deepen flavor and improve storage life.

plant_note: Direct sow at last frost date or start indoors 4–6 weeks earlier; seeds require light to germinate so press onto the soil surface without covering.
directions: Press seeds onto warm, moist soil surface without covering, as light aids germination; cold stratification in the refrigerator for one week before sowing improves germination rates. Perilla is a heat-loving herb-vegetable used extensively in Japanese and Korean cuisines — red types as a colorant and flavoring, green types as a wrap and garnish. Harvest individual large leaves from the base of the plant upward, or cut the whole plant back by half to promote bushy regrowth. Pinch flower spikes as they emerge to extend the leaf harvest and prevent aggressive self-seeding.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring; the biennial keeps producing into fall.
directions: Perpetual spinach is actually a leaf chard grown for tender, mild leaves that eat like true spinach but far more heat- and bolt-resistant. As a cut-and-come-again crop it produces for months, and being biennial it can carry over for a second season in mild areas. Direct sow in spring in full sun to partial shade and keep evenly watered. Harvest outer leaves regularly and the plant will keep pushing out new growth well into fall.

plant_note: Direct sow around last frost; keeps producing through the season.
directions: Despite its name this thin-ribbed Italian heirloom is a Swiss chard grown for its tender, spinach-like leaves, and it is slow to bolt in hot weather. Sow directly and thin seedlings, then harvest outer leaves regularly so the plant keeps producing new growth for months. Keep the soil evenly moist and feed occasionally for lush leaves. It tolerates both spring and fall conditions and mild frost.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–6 weeks after last frost; in zone 9–11 pigeon pea grows as a woody short-lived perennial and may produce for 3–5 years before replanting.
directions: Sow seeds 1 in deep in well-drained, low-fertility soil; pigeon pea is a deep-rooted drought tolerant shrub that fixes nitrogen and does not require fertilization. It grows into a woody perennial in frost-free zones and produces pods over an extended season rather than all at once. Harvest green pods when plump for fresh shelling or allow to dry on the plant for dried dal. Prune back to 2–3 ft after the main harvest season to stimulate a second flush of growth and pods.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in warm starting mix and transplant to fertile, well-drained garden soil once the risk of frost has fully passed. The heart-shaped, thick-walled fruits ripen from green to deep red and are sweeter and more aromatic than most bell peppers, with a rich, almost spicy fragrance that is distinct to the type. Harvest at full red for fresh use, or roast and peel for preserving in oil — pimento is the classic stuffing for green olives. High yields from compact plants make this a productive choice for containers or small beds.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after frost when soil is warm.
directions: This high-yielding beefsteak ripens to yellow-orange marbled with red streaks running through the meaty, low-acid flesh. Being indeterminate, the vines grow tall and need strong staking or caging. Start seeds indoors, transplant into warm soil, and water consistently to reduce cracking on the large fruit. Prune suckers lightly to boost airflow and support ripening of these big tomatoes.

plant_note: Direct sow into warm soil after all frost danger.
directions: Pink Banana is a large heirloom winter squash producing elongated, pink-skinned fruits with sweet, tender orange flesh on vigorous vines that can run fifteen feet. The big fruits store well for months in a cool, dry place. Direct sow into warm, rich soil in full sun after frost, giving the sprawling vines plenty of space. Harvest when the rind hardens and skin turns pink, then cure the squash before storing.

plant_note: Direct sow in warm soil after frost; needs a long warm season.
directions: This heirloom banana-type squash grows 18 to 24 inches long with smooth, velvety skin and sweet flesh good for side dishes and pies. Give the sprawling vines full sun and plenty of space, and sow in warm soil after frost. Water steadily while fruit sizes up, then reduce as the rind hardens. Cure harvested squash in a warm, dry place to sweeten the flesh and extend storage.

plant_note: Direct sow in warm soil after frost; tolerates heat and drier conditions.
directions: These compact 2 foot cowpea plants need no staking and produce prolifically even in drier conditions than most beans. Also called crowder peas, they are nutritious, high in fiber and protein, and pair well with cooked greens. Sow directly in warm soil after frost and give them full sun. Harvest pods when the peas have filled out, shelling for fresh use or leaving to dry for storage.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost; grow to full maturity for dry beans.
directions: Pinto is a heat- and drought-tolerant bush bean grown as a dry bean, producing pods of speckled beans on upright plants with short runners. Direct sow after frost into warm soil in full sun, and let the pods dry fully on the plant. Water moderately during flowering and pod set, then ease off as pods mature. Harvest when pods are brown and brittle, then shell and dry the beans further for storage.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant 2 weeks after frost danger has passed.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in warm starting mix and provide bottom heat around 80°F to speed germination. Transplant to fertile, well-drained soil once nights are consistently above 55°F. Water deeply but infrequently to encourage deep rooting and reduce disease pressure. Harvest dark green fruits at 4–5 inches long for fresh use, or allow to turn red and dry them for ancho chiles.

plant_note: Direct sow in warm soil once frost has passed.
directions: This dependable slicing cucumber yields dark green, straight 7 to 8 inch fruits on plants that resist mildews, anthracnose, scab, and angular leaf spot. Its disease package makes it reliable in humid climates. Sow in warm soil and trellis the vines to keep cucumbers straight and clean. Water consistently to avoid bitterness, and pick often to keep production going through a long season.

plant_note: Sow after last frost when soil is warm; erect a trellis or pole structure before sowing to avoid disturbing roots later.
directions: Plant seeds 1 in deep at the base of a sturdy trellis or teepee of poles set 6–8 ft tall, spacing 3 seeds per pole. Vines twine naturally but may need early guidance toward supports. Harvest pods before seeds bulge prominently for the tenderest texture. Pole beans produce over a longer season than bush types, so keep picking to prevent pod maturation from stopping new flower set.

plant_note: Direct sow at or up to 2 weeks after last frost once soil reaches at least 60°F. Isolate from sweet corn by at least 250 feet or time plantings to avoid simultaneous silking.
directions: Sow seeds 1–2 inches deep in blocks of at least 4 rows for proper wind pollination, isolating from sweet corn to prevent cross-pollination that ruins eating quality. Side-dress with nitrogen at knee-height and again when tassels appear. Leave ears on the stalk past eating stage until husks are completely brown and papery; pull husks back and hang ears in a dry, ventilated spot for 4–6 weeks until kernels release from the cob easily. Kernels are ready to pop when moisture content drops to around 14%.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant after frost when soil is warm.
directions: Bred by Wild Boar Farms, this true-yellow beefsteak has a sweet flavor with citrus notes and slightly flattened 8 to 12 ounce fruits. Being indeterminate, the vines grow tall and benefit from sturdy staking. Start seeds indoors, transplant into warm soil, and water evenly to prevent cracking. Let fruit ripen fully to yellow for the sweetest flavor, picking regularly to sustain production.

plant_note: Plant seed pieces 2–4 weeks before last frost when soil reaches 45°F; in warm climates a second planting is made in late summer for a fall crop.
directions: Plant certified seed potatoes cut to egg-sized pieces (each with 1–2 eyes) 4 in deep in trenches spaced 30 in apart. Hill soil up around the stems as they grow, keeping tubers covered to prevent greening from light exposure. Water consistently and watch for late blight in humid weather — infected foliage should be removed promptly to protect tubers. Harvest new potatoes when plants flower, or cure full-season types for a week at room temperature before long-term storage.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall 4–6 weeks before hard freeze in zones 5–9; in zones 3–4, plant as early as possible in spring.
directions: Plant individual bulbs 1 inch deep in well-drained, fertile soil — large bulbs planted in fall will multiply into a cluster of 8–12 medium onions, while small bulbs tend to produce fewer but larger bulbs. Potato onions are a multiplier onion type that propagates entirely by bulb division, forming a cluster of bulbs in the same way shallots do; no seed is needed or typically produced.
